■ Final Lay English ■ Rule-Based
# Original Summary Rule-Based Simplified Final Indian Lay English Diagnosis Outcome Flesch Ratio
1 male, 42 years old. {organization}. 26/12/2021. stay: 23 h, location: ward, payer: sus. self-referral. emergency. hospital care. [person] with crohn's disease, admitted due to abdominal sepsis associated with acute on chronic kidney disease. [person] with crohn's disease, recently underwent a surgical procedure with ileocolectomy and creation of a new anastomosis after acute perforative abdomen. admitted today to a monitored bed in the red room with hypotension, fever and fainting for 1 day. [person] was recently admitted to [organization] for treatment of intra-abdominal collection, increased urinary output via ileostomy and worsening of renal function, being discharged on 11/15 with a schedule of ertapenem 1 g/day, intramuscularly, for 4 weeks. [person] presents with borderline blood pressure, eupneic on room air. at the time of evaluation, he denies any complaints. he reports maintaining physiological diuresis, without complaints. reports good ostomy function. antibiotic therapy with vancomycin meropenem was started, and [person] was transferred to the intensive care unit of [organization], under the care of the proctology team accepted by [person] from the intensive care unit, {omitted}. meropenem vancomycin replacement of kcl 6% orally continuous medications: loperamide, calcium carbonate, cholestyramine, muciloid. 12/27/2021. follow-up appointment. [person] is discharged on leave to follow-up appointment for hospitalization on 01/22. Male, 42 years old. The hospital. 26/12/2021. Stay: 23 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Self-referral. Emergency. Hospital care. The patient with long-term bowel disease (Crohn's), admitted due to abdominal serious blood infection (sepsis) associated with acute on chronic kidney problem (kidney disease). The patient with long-term bowel disease (Crohn's), recently underwent a surgical procedure with removal of part of bowel (ileocolectomy) and creation of a new surgical joining of bowel ends (anastomosis) after acute perforative abdomen. Admitted today to a monitored bed in the red room with low BP (hypotension), fever and fainting for 1 day. The patient was recently admitted to [organization] for treatment of intra-abdominal collection, increased urinary output via ileostomy and worsening of renal function, being sent home from hospital (discharge)d on 11/15 with a schedule of ertapenem 1 g/day, given as a muscle injection (intramuscular)ly, for 4 weeks. The patient presents with borderline blood pressure, breathing normally (eupneic) on room air. At the time of evaluation, he denies any complaints. He reports maintaining physiological diuresis, without complaints. Reports good ostomy function. Infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) with vancomycin meropenem was started, and the patient was transferred to the ICU (serious care room) of [organization], under the care of the proctology team accepted by the patient from the ICU (serious care room), . Meropenem vancomycin replacement of kcl 6% by mouth (orally) continuous medications: loperamide, calcium carbonate, cholestyramine, muciloid. 12/27/2021. Return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment. The patient is sent home from hospital (discharge)d on leave to return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment for hospitalization on 01/22. Male, 42 years old. The hospital. 26/12/2021. Stay: 23 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Self-referral. Emergency. Hospital care. The patient with long-term bowel disease (Crohn's), admitted due to abdominal serious blood infection (sepsis) associated with acute on chronic kidney problem (kidney disease). The patient was recently admitted to [organization] for treatment of intra-abdominal collection, increased urinary output via ileostomy and worsening of renal function, being sent home from hospital (discharge)d on 11/15 with a schedule of ertapenem 1 g/day, given as a muscle injection (intramuscular)ly, for 4 weeks. The patient presents with borderline blood pressure, breathing normally (eupneic) on room air. At the time of evaluation, he denies any complaints. He reports maintaining physiological diuresis, without complaints. Reports good ostomy function. Infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) with vancomycin meropenem was started, and the patient was transferred to the ICU (serious care room) of [organization], under the care of the proctology team accepted by the patient from the ICU (serious care room), . Meropenem vancomycin replacement of kcl 6% by mouth (orally) continuous medications: loperamide, calcium carbonate, cholestyramine, muciloid. 12/27/2021. Return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment. The patient is sent home from hospital (discharge)d on leave to return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment for hospitalization on 01/22. It's important to make sure that the patient doesn't get any treatment. It'll be done before 01/23.2019.</s> a41. other septicemias. k220. achalasia of the heart. k509. crohn disease of nes localization. n17. acute renal failure. alive 25.3 1.42
2 1. bloodstream infection associated with Schilley catheter in the right femoral vein sac 11/08 2. bacterial endocarditis associated with catheter echocardiogram 11/18: presence of vegetation in the mitral valve 3. pyogenic spondylodiscitis in l3/l4 11/05: low back pain, gait disturbance, computed tomography of the lumbosacral spine 11/12: findings suggestive of a fracture due to bone fragility of the vertebral body of l1, gas foci in the spinal canal at the level of l2l3 that may be related to an extruded disc herniation with degenerative disc disease, and an infectious or inflammatory process cannot be ruled out, lumbar magnetic resonance imaging 25/11/2020: infectious spondylodiscitis with anterior epidural abscess 4. left ileopsoas abscess, magnetic resonance imaging 25/10/2020: intramuscular collection in the ileopsoas left 15 x 2.1 x 1.8 cm with edema and adjacent post-contrast enhancement. admission: 11/08/2020, discharge: 01/02/2021, stay: 01 m 24 d 15 h, location: ward: ward payer: sus. [person] attends hemodialysis session with severe left lower back pain that had started 3 days ago, associated with asthenia, and altered gait. [person] who was accompanying him, denies fever, nausea, and vomiting. Denies other associated symptoms. catheter dressing in the right femoral vein was secreting. [person] evolved during hospitalization with a diagnosis of bloodstream infection by staphylococcus aureus mrsa, complicated by l2l4 spondylodiscitis, mitral valve infective endocarditis, and ileopsoas muscle abscess. Catheter removed on 11/08 and antibiotic therapy guided by vancomycin culture was started on 11/08. Abscess in psoas was not approached due to interposition of bowel loops in an attempt at percutaneous drainage by the {organization} on 12/02. Comparative radiological improvement was observed in axial lesions and conservative treatment was maintained regarding foci of infection identified during hospitalization. [person] remained afebrile with successively negative blood cultures. As a complication associated with immobility, he developed sacral pressure injury, treated with surgical debridement and local measures. Injury care guidelines were provided by the team. He evolved with the need for hemodialysis on Tuesdays, Thursdays and Saturdays due to arteriovenous fistula in the left upper limb, but local stenosis was noted. He is currently on renal replacement therapy. good general condition, pale 1/4, hydrated, acyanotic, anicteric, afebrile to the touch and eupneic in room air. neurology: conscious and oriented in time, space and person. respiratory auscultation: bilateral vesicular breath sounds with discreet bibasilar crackles, no signs of respiratory distress. cardiac auscultation: irregular heart rhythm, with normal sound sounds, no murmurs. abdomen: protuberant, bowel sounds, normotensive, painless on superficial or deep palpation, no signs of peritoneal irritation. warm and well perfused extremities. upper limbs: fistula in the left upper limb with present thrill. lower limbs: presence of discreet edema and anterior tibial ochre dermatitis. nuclear magnetic resonance 25/10/2020: intramuscular collection in the left ileopsoas 15 x 2.1 x 1.8 cm with edema and post-contrast enhancement adjacent cultures: catheter tip 11/08: vancomycin-sensitive staphylococcus aureus blood culture 11/08: staphylococcus aureus sg catheter 11/08 staphylococcus aureus blood culture 11/11: staphylococcus aureus oxa r in 2 samples blood culture 11/21: citrobacter koseri 1 sample staphylococcus aureus 1 sample blood culture 11/24: staphylococcus capitis blood culture 11/28: negative blood culture 12/01: negative blood culture 12/02: negative. attempted percutaneous abscess puncture on 12/02 not performed due to interposition of intestinal loops. nuclear magnetic resonance report of the pelvis performed on 12/16. agreed with [organization] for chronic patients to attend the hemodialysis room at [organization] on Tuesdays, Thursdays and Saturdays, with transportation from the city of origin to the unit carried out by stretcher taxi {omitted}. therefore, we discharge the patient with the following pending issues: observations: administer 0.5 g of vancomycin after each scheduled hemodialysis session antibiotic therapy until 01/09/2021, completing 6 weeks after the last negative blood culture assessment of arteriovenous fistula: phlebography of arteriovenous fistula indicated assess fistulography by brachial to determine the site of stenosis and then perform phlebography or assess pre-procedure with ultrasound in the room. [person] placed on list. [organization] will contact you to schedule a treatment, as recorded in the note by the sacral pressure injury specialist team: [person]'s outpatient follow-up appointment on 16/01/2021 for pressure injury reassessment. Instructions for home care of the injury were provided. Cardiology follow-up for amyloidosis investigation: [person] missed a follow-up appointment appointment on 09/09, scheduled for 01/13 for motor rehabilitation. We are awaiting an appointment by the physiatry department at [organization] requested on 12/27. Check the nuclear magnetic resonance report of the pelvis from 12/16. Previous chronic medications were maintained. Dialysis room Tuesdays, Thursdays, and Saturdays. Amyloidosis cardiology outpatient clinic 01/13. Pressure injury surgery outpatient clinic 01/16. 1. Bloodstream infection associated with schilley catheter in the right femoral vein sac 11/08 2. Bacterial infection of heart valves (endocarditis) associated with catheter heart ultrasound (echocardiogram) 11/18: presence of vegetation in the mitral valve 3. Pyogenic spine bone infection (spondylodiscitis) in l3/l4 11/05: low back pain, gait disturbance, CT scan (computed tomography) of the lumbosacral spine 11/12: findings suggestive of a fracture due to bone fragility of the vertebral body of l1, gas foci in the spinal canal at the level of l2l3 that may be related to an extruded disc herniation with degenerative disc disease, and an infectious or inflammatory process cannot be ruled out, lumbar MRI scan (magnetic resonance) imaging 25/11/2020: infectious spine bone infection (spondylodiscitis) with anterior epidural pus-filled swelling (abscess) 4. Left ileopsoas pus-filled swelling (abscess), MRI scan (magnetic resonance) imaging 25/10/2020: given as a muscle injection (intramuscular) collection in the ileopsoas left 15 x 2.1 x 1.8 cm with swelling (edema) and adjacent post-contrast enhancement. Admission: 11/08/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 01/02/2021, stay: 01 m 24 d 15 h, location: general hospital room (ward): general hospital room (ward) payer: government health scheme. The patient attends kidney dialysis (hemodialysis) session with severe left lower back pain that had started 3 days ago, associated with asthenia, and altered gait. The patient who was accompanying him, denies fever, nausea, and vomiting. Denies other associated symptoms. Catheter dressing in the right femoral vein was secreting. The patient evolved during hospitalization with a diagnosis of bloodstream infection by staphylococcus aureus mrsa, complicated by l2l4 spine bone infection (spondylodiscitis), mitral valve infective infection of heart valves (endocarditis), and ileopsoas muscle pus-filled swelling (abscess). Catheter removed on 11/08 and infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) guided by vancomycin culture was started on 11/08. Pus-filled swelling (abscess) in psoas was not approached due to interposition of bowel loops in an attempt at through the skin (percutaneous) drainage by the the hospital on 12/02. Comparative radiological improvement was observed in axial lesions and conservative treatment was maintained regarding foci of infection identified during hospitalization. The patient remained no fever (afebrile) with successively negative blood test to find infection (blood culture)s. As a complication associated with immobility, he developed sacral pressure injury, treated with surgical surgical wound cleaning (debridement) and local measures. Injury care guidelines were provided by the team. He evolved with the need for kidney dialysis (hemodialysis) on tuesdays, thursdays and saturdays due to dialysis access point on arm (arteriovenous fistula) in the left upper limb, but local stenosis was noted. He is currently on renal replacement therapy. Good general condition, pale 1/4, hydrated, no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no fever (afebrile) to the touch and breathing normally (eupneic) in room air. Neurology: conscious and oriented in time, space and person. Respiratory auscultation: bilateral normal breathing sounds with discreet bibasilar crackles, no signs of respiratory distress. Cardiac auscultation: irregular heart rhythm, with normal sound sounds, no murmurs. Abdomen: protuberant, bowel sounds, normotensive, painless on superficial or deep palpation, no signs of peritoneal irritation. Warm and well perfused extremities. Upper limbs: fistula in the left upper limb with present thrill. Lower limbs: presence of discreet swelling (edema) and anterior tibial ochre dermatitis. Nuclear MRI scan (magnetic resonance) 25/10/2020: given as a muscle injection (intramuscular) collection in the left ileopsoas 15 x 2.1 x 1.8 cm with swelling (edema) and post-contrast enhancement adjacent cultures: catheter tip 11/08: vancomycin-sensitive staphylococcus aureus blood test to find infection (blood culture) 11/08: staphylococcus aureus sg catheter 11/08 staphylococcus aureus blood test to find infection (blood culture) 11/11: staphylococcus aureus oxa r in 2 samples blood test to find infection (blood culture) 11/21: citrobacter koseri 1 sample staphylococcus aureus 1 sample blood test to find infection (blood culture) 11/24: staphylococcus capitis blood test to find infection (blood culture) 11/28: negative blood test to find infection (blood culture) 12/01: negative blood test to find infection (blood culture) 12/02: negative. Attempted through the skin (percutaneous) pus-filled swelling (abscess) puncture on 12/02 not performed due to interposition of intestinal loops. Nuclear MRI scan (magnetic resonance) report of the pelvis performed on 12/16. Agreed with [organization] for chronic patients to attend the kidney dialysis (hemodialysis) room at [organization] on tuesdays, thursdays and saturdays, with transportation from the city of origin to the unit carried out by stretcher taxi . Therefore, we sent home from hospital (discharge) the patient with the following pending issues: observations: give 0.5 g of vancomycin after each scheduled kidney dialysis (hemodialysis) session infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) until 01/09/2021, completing 6 weeks after the last negative blood test to find infection (blood culture) assessment of dialysis access point on arm (arteriovenous fistula): phlebography of dialysis access point on arm (arteriovenous fistula) indicated assess fistulography by brachial to determine the site of stenosis and then perform phlebography or assess pre-procedure with ultrasound in the room. The patient placed on list. [organization] will contact you to schedule a treatment, as recorded in the note by the sacral pressure injury specialist team: the patient's outpatient return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment on 16/01/2021 for pressure injury reassessment. Instructions for home care of the injury were provided. Cardiology return visit to doctor (follow-up) for amyloidosis investigation: the patient missed a return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment appointment on 09/09, scheduled for 01/13 for motor rehabilitation. We are awaiting an appointment by the physiatry department at [organization] requested on 12/27. Check the nuclear MRI scan (magnetic resonance) report of the pelvis from 12/16. 3 1sensitive pulmonary tuberculosis. diagnosis 2019, ripe 07/02/2020 ri 07/04 2 pulmonary complications secondary to tuberculosis: spontaneous pneumothorax on the right chest drainage on the right on 03/02/2020 pleurostomy 03/03/2020 closure of pleurostomy lung collapse, pleural drainage, heimlich valve on 10/03 removal of the heimlich valve from the drain, keeping the drain open for ostomy on 27/03 new pleurotomy 31/03 exploratory thoracotomy on an emergency basis 07/04 due to bleeding in tuberculous caverns significant oozing bleeding 07/04 packing of the cavity with compresses postoperatively 11/04/2020 right pneumonectomy by lateral thoracotomy tracheostomy, without complications chest drain and Tracheostomy removed on 06/06. 3 surgical wound infections. Multidrug-resistant Acinetobacter baumannii treated with vancomycin meropenem amikacin antibiotic therapy suspended on 02/04 after clinical improvement and procalcitonin of 0.09 4 hypovolemic shock due to surgical wound bleeding pleurostomy requiring urgent surgical approach 07/04 5 cardiopulmonary arrest 10/04 in asystole / 4 min secondary to hypoxia. admission: 03/02/2020, discharge: 09/05/2020, stay: 03 m 06 d 07 h, ward bed, payer: sus. [person] referred with a persistent cough for 4 weeks, dry, without expectoration, associated with night sweats. He also reports that 3 days ago he had a fever, not measured, associated with dyspnea, and also reports 1 day ago, sudden pain in the right hemithorax, radiating to the right cervical region, associated with significant and progressive worsening of dyspnea. He denies bloody expectoration or sputum. He reports being in prison for approximately 3 years, for 6 years, and has been asymptomatic since then. He also reports weight loss of approximately 8 kg, not quantified, 1 month ago. He denies urinary or intestinal changes. regular general condition, pale 1/4, hydrated, acyanotic, anicteric, conscious and oriented cardiac auscultation: normal rhythmic sounds, normal S1 and S2, no murmurs, heart rate 85 beats per minute respiratory auscultation vesicular breath sounds present in the left hemithorax, abolished in the right hemithorax, with asymmetry of expansion, no adventitious sounds, saturation 98%. abdomen: flat, bowel sounds, flaccid, no peritonism. extremities: no edema, no signs of deep vein thrombosis, maintained peripheral perfusion. surgical wound in good condition and dry, presence of suture without dehiscence. sensitivity test for mycobacterium tuberculosis material: sputum collection: 02/03/2020 available: 28/02/2020 released: 28/02/2020 method: dna hybridization geno type mtbdr plus rifampin element. sensitive isoniazid. sensitive mycobacteria bacilloscopy tuberculosis material: sputum collection: 02/03/2020 available: 02/07/2020 released: 02/07/2020 method: ziehlnelsen element result. positive: 10 to 99 baar were found in 100 observed fields. right chest drainage on 02/03/2020 pleurostomy 03/03/2020 pleurostomy closure lung collapse pleural drainage Heimlich valve on 03/10 removal of the Heimlich valve from the drain, keeping the drain open for ostomy on 03/27 new pleurotomy 03/31 emergency exploratory thoracotomy 04/07 due to bleeding in tuberculous cavernosal bleeding significant oozing 04/07 packing of the cavity with compresses postoperatively 04/11/2020 right pneumonectomy by lateral thoracotomy tracheostomy, uneventful chest drain and tracheostomy removed on 06/06. only exams for follow-up appointment. I continue treatment with RI. I provide 2 months of prescriptions. I continue oral iron replacement. We hold a meeting with [person], together with the thoracic surgery team, and we clarify any doubts regarding the condition and treatment. [person] understands the guidelines and appreciates the care of the team. I request exams for a follow-up appointment. We schedule a follow-up appointment at [organization] and at the thoracic surgery team. We advise [person] of warning signs and instructed to call with concerns. We provide a report and contact [organization] for assisted treatment. Infectious diseases and thoracic surgery. Good general condition, no complaints. 1sensitive pulmonary TB (tuberculosis). Diagnosis 2019, ripe 07/02/2020 ri 07/04 2 pulmonary complications secondary to TB (tuberculosis): spontaneous air trapped in chest (pneumothorax) on the right chest drainage on the right on 03/02/2020 pleurostomy 03/03/2020 closure of pleurostomy lung collapse, pleural drainage, heimlich valve on 10/03 removal of the heimlich valve from the drain, keeping the drain open for ostomy on 27/03 new pleurotomy 31/03 exploratory chest surgery (thoracotomy) on an emergency basis 07/04 due to bleeding in tuberculous caverns significant oozing bleeding 07/04 packing of the cavity with compresses postoperatively 11/04/2020 right removal of a lung (pneumonectomy) by lateral chest surgery (thoracotomy) breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy), without complications chest drain and breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) removed on 06/06. 3 surgical wound infections. Multidrug-resistant acinetobacter baumannii treated with vancomycin meropenem amikacin infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) government health schemepended on 02/04 after clinical improvement and blood marker for infection (procalcitonin) of 0.09 4 hypovolemic shock due to surgical wound bleeding pleurostomy requiring urgent surgical approach 07/04 5 cardiopulmonary arrest 10/04 in asystole / 4 min secondary to hypoxia. Admission: 03/02/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 09/05/2020, stay: 03 m 06 d 07 h, general hospital room (ward) bed, payer: government health scheme. The patient referred with a persistent cough for 4 weeks, dry, without expectoration, associated with night sweats. He also reports that 3 days ago he had a fever, not measured, associated with difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), and also reports 1 day ago, sudden pain in the right hemithorax, radiating to the right cervical region, associated with significant and progressive worsening of difficulty in breathing (dyspnea). He denies bloody expectoration or sputum. He reports being in prison for approximately 3 years, for 6 years, and systemic arterial high BP (hypertension) been asymptomatic since then. He also reports weight loss of approximately 8 kg, not quantified, 1 month ago. He denies urinary or intestinal changes. Regular general condition, pale 1/4, hydrated, no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), conscious and oriented cardiac auscultation: normal rhythmic sounds, normal s1 and s2, no murmurs, heart rate 85 beats per minute respiratory auscultation normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds) present in the left hemithorax, abolished in the right hemithorax, with asymmetry of expansion, no adventitious sounds, oxygen level in blood (saturation) 98%. Abdomen: flat, bowel sounds, flaccid, no peritonism. Extremities: no swelling (edema), no signs of deep vein blood clot (thrombosis), maintained peripheral perfusion. Surgical wound in good condition and dry, presence of suture without dehiscence. Sensitivity test for mycobacterium TB (tuberculosis) material: sputum collection: 02/03/2020 available: 28/02/2020 released: 28/02/2020 method: dna hybridization geno type mtbdr plus rifampin element. Sensitive isoniazid. Sensitive mycobacteria bacilloscopy TB (tuberculosis) material: sputum collection: 02/03/2020 available: 02/07/2020 released: 02/07/2020 method: ziehlnelsen element result. Positive: 10 to 99 baar were found in 100 observed fields. Only exams for return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment. I continue treatment with ri. I provide 2 months of prescriptions. I continue oral iron replacement. We hold a meeting with the patient, together with the thoracic surgery team, and we clarify any doubts regarding the condition and treatment. The patient understands the guidelines and appreciates the care of the team. I request exams for a return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment. The patient referred with a persistent cough for 4 weeks, dry, without expectoration, associated with night sweats. He also reports that 3 days ago he had a fever, not measured, associated with difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), and also reports 1 day ago, sudden pain in the right hemithorax, radiating to the right cervical region, associated with significant and progressive worsening of difficulty in breathing (dyspnea). He denies bloody expectoration or sputum. He reports being in prison for approximately 3 years, for 6 years, and systemic arterial high BP (hypertension) been asymptomatic since then. He also reports weight loss of approximately 8 kg, not quantified, 1 month ago. He denies urinary or intestinal changes. Regular general condition, pale 1/4, hydrated, no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), conscious and oriented cardiac auscultation: normal rhythmic sounds, normal s1 and s2, no murmurs, heart rate 85 beats per minute respiratory auscultation normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds) present in the left hemithorax, abolished in the right hemithorax, with asymmetry of expansion, no adventitious sounds, oxygen level in blood (saturation) 98%. Abdomen: flat, bowel sounds, flaccid, no peritonism. Extremities: no swelling (edema), no signs of deep vein blood clot (thrombosis), maintained peripheral perfusion. Surgical wound in good condition and dry, presence of suture without dehiscence. 5 1. decreased level of consciousness to be clarified secondary to alcoholic libation? sepsis? status epilepticus? computed tomography of the skull 27/05/2020: signs of cerebral and cerebellar volumetric loss, most notably on the left, failure of continuity of the hard palate with communication of the oral and nasal cavities. cerebrospinal fluid 30/05/2020: clear, colorless appearance 15 cells 95% lymphocytes, 5% neutrophils no bacteria in the gram glucose 52 dextrose 103 protein 36.2 chloride 110.2 2. septic shock from a pulmonary focus bronchoaspiration pneumonia need for vasoactive drug until 05/28 clavulin d0 05/27 clarithromycin 05/27 05/31 3. ciprofloxacin eye drops d0 05/29. admission: 05/28/2020, discharge: 06/01/2020, stay: ward, payer: SUS. [person] reports high alcohol intake yesterday, arriving home at 3am. At 5am, he was found naked in the backyard and taken by his family to the living room, where he was left sleeping. At 10am, [person] was noticed to be unresponsive, vomiting, feces, and urine on the couch. SAMU was called and he was taken to the emergency care unit, where his blood glucose level was 33 mg/dL, with decerebrate posturing and decreased level of consciousness, which persisted after glycemic correction. He was intubated. [person] was brought by SAMU, who reported using etomidate during transport due to agitation. Upon admission, [person] was intubated, unconscious, hemodynamically stable, and had no vasoactive drug. He vomited coffee grounds. Thiamine and glucose serum were started and he was maintained without sedation for a neurological evaluation. well attached to mechanical ventilation, fio2 50% peep 6 vc 380 tinsp 0.9 respiratory rate 16. evolved with improvement in the level of consciousness and respiratory condition, with extubation on 05/31. good general condition, pale /4, hydrated, anicteric, acyanotic, afebrile, eupneic respiratory auscultation reduced vesicular breath sounds bilaterally, discrete crackles in the right base, saturation 98% cardiac auscultation: normal-phonetic, regular heart sounds, normal S1 and S2, no murmurs, heart rate 83 beats per minute abdomen: flat, normotensive, painless on palpation, no palpable masses or visceromegaly warm extremities, no edema, no calf swelling. amputation of left hallux, small healing fcc in 3rd and 4th right toes on the plantar surface neurology: Glasgow coma scale 15, isochoric and photoreactive pupils. computed tomography of the skull 27/05/2020: signs of cerebral and cerebellar volumetric loss, the latter more notably on the left, failure of continuity of the hard palate with communication of the oral and nasal cavities. computed tomography of the chest 27/05/2020: signs suggestive of an inflammatory, infectious process on the right, of probable bronchoaspiration esophagopathy. abdominal computed tomography 27/05/2020: simple renal cysts cerebrospinal fluid 30/05/2020: clear, colorless appearance 15 cells 95% lymphocytes, 5% neutrophils no bacteria in gram glucose 52 dextrose 103 protein 36.2 chloride 110.2. I prescribe clavulin 500 125 mg 8/8 h orally to complete 7 days I prescribe ciprofloxacin eye drops 1 gt in each eye 4/4 h to complete 7 days I maintain prednisone 5 mg/day and advise follow-up appointment follow-up regarding primary adrenal insufficiency I advise maintain regular use of ART and the importance of regular follow-up I advise on cessation of smoking, alcoholism and cocaine use. I make a report requesting follow-up appointment of follow-up at Aena for primary adrenal insufficiency. well, no complaints. 1. Decreased alertness level (level of consciousness) to be clarified secondary to alcoholic libation? Serious blood infection (sepsis)? Prolonged seizure (status epilepticus)? CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 27/05/2020: signs of cerebral and cerebellar volumetric loss, most notably on the left, failure of continuity of the hard palate with communication of the oral and nasal cavities. Cerebrospinal fluid 30/05/2020: clear, colorless appearance 15 cells 95% lymphocytes, 5% neutrophils no bacteria in the gram glucose 52 dextrose 103 protein 36.2 chloride 110.2 2. The patient reports high alcohol intake yesterday, arriving home at 3am. At 5am, he was found naked in the backyard and taken by his family to the living room, where he was left sleeping. At 10am, the patient was noticed to be unresponsive, vomiting, feces, and urine on the couch. Samu was called and he was taken to the emergency care unit, where his blood glucose level was 33 mg/dl, with decerebrate posturing and decreased alertness level, which persisted after glycemic correction. He was intubated. The patient was brought by samu, who reported using etomidate during transport due to agitation. Upon admission, the patient was intubated, unconscious, stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), and had no vasoactive drug. He vomited coffee grounds. Thiamine and glucose serum were started and he was maintained without sedation for a neurological evaluation. Well attached to machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation), fio2 50% peep 6 vc 380 tinsp 0.9 respiratory rate 16. Evolved with improvement in the alertness level (level of consciousness) and respiratory condition, with breathing tube removed (extubation) on 05/31. Good general condition, pale /4, hydrated, no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no fever (afebrile), breathing normally (eupneic) respiratory auscultation reduced normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds) bilaterally, discrete crackles in the right base, oxygen level in blood (saturation) 98% cardiac auscultation: normal-phonetic, regular heart sounds, normal s1 and s2, no murmurs, heart rate 83 beats per minute abdomen: flat, normotensive, painless on palpation, no palpable masses or visceromegaly warm extremities, no swelling (edema), no calf swelling. Amputation of left hallux, small healing fcc in 3rd and 4th right toes on the plantar surface neurology: glasgow coma scale 15, isochoric and photoreactive pupils. CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 27/05/2020: signs of cerebral and cerebellar volumetric loss, the latter more notably on the left, failure of continuity of the hard palate with communication of the oral and nasal cavities. CT scan (computed tomography) of the chest 27/05/2020: signs suggestive of an inflammatory, infectious process on the right, of probable bronchoaspiration esophagopathy. Abdominal CT scan (computed tomography) 27/05/2020: simple renal cysts cerebrospinal fluid 30/05/2020: clear, colorless appearance 15 cells 95% lymphocytes, 5% neutrophils no bacteria in gram glucose 52 dextrose 103 protein 36.2 chloride 110.2. care unit 10/08: electroencephalogram 10/08: neurology suggests performing a computed tomography scan of the skull with venous phase contrast as soon as possible. 09/10: start of sedation weaning 09/10: increase in lamotrigine, uncharacteristic movements 10/10: change of antibiotic to meropenem 10/10: CT Angiography (CTA) of the chest and skull, pulmonary thromboembolism with 25% load, full anticoagulation started with enoxaparin 80 mg 12/12h on 10/11 12/10: hypotension, hypoxia, ETT (Endotracheal Tube) obstruction, start of neuromuscular blockade 12/10: abdominal distension, fasting until further notice, nasogastric tube in drainage, 600 ml in 24h, personal history: human immunodeficiency virus infection, diagnosis in 2000: undetectable CV: August/2020 CD4 904 August/2020 ART: AZT 3TC EFZ 2010. 08/2018 TDF 3tc dol depressive symptoms. neurotoxoplasmosis {omitted} left hemiparesis. sulfa drug use and ceftriaxone. seizures after traumatic brain injury and pneumococcal meningitis in 1998, prior to hcv treatment, it was proposed to change phenobarbital to lamotrigine. lamotrigine protocol opened without success. decided to prescribe clobazam in 2017, in use since consultation in 2017, when he had a seizure. since then he has not had new seizures, phenobarbital was discontinued on account of it. chronic hepatitis c genotype 1a treatment-naïve: ahid follow-up. liver biopsy on 04/15: mild chronic hepatitis metavir a1/f1 rna hcv 2,922,831 august/2019 abdominal ultrasound: 04/18 incipient sonographic signs of chronic liver disease. depression without psychiatric follow-up. renewal of medication asid. continuous use medications valproic acid 250 mg 222 clonazepam 2.5 mg at night tdf 3tc dol sertraline 50 mg/day habits: smoker 1.5 pack/day since 12 years, chronic alcoholic, functionality: independent for basic activities, dependent for instrumental activities rankin 2, devices central venous catheter right internal jugular vein 05/10, orotracheal tube 05/10, indwelling urinary catheter 05/10, nasoenteral tube 05/10, antibiotics: current: meropenem 10/10 previous: ceftriaxone clindamycin d0 05/10 to 10/10 blood cultures 06/10: staphylococcus epidermidis 01 sample exams hospitalization: electroencephalogram 06/10: revealed abnormal, evidencing: moderate to marked diffuse disorganization of the background activity due to low amplitude of tracing rhythms, with a higher content of slow waves in the delta range, lack of physiological graphoelements of sleep, and absence of clinical and electroencephalographic reactivity to painful stimuli, however [person] presented spontaneous superficialization of consciousness during a short stretch of the tracing. electroclinical correlation: the findings are nonspecific and cannot rule out the effect of central nervous system depressant drugs. electroencephalogram 08/10: the marked symmetrical disorganization of the background activity due to diffuse and irregular slowing in the delta range, absence of physiological graphoelements of sleep and absence of electroencephalographic reactivity to painful stimuli noted intermittent modulation by low amplitude sinusoidal rhythms in the alpha/beta range. conclusion and electroclinical correlation: the electroencephalogram is abnormal. the findings are compatible with the effect of central nervous system depressant drugs, and associated encephalopathy cannot be ruled out. Electroclinical correlation: the findings are nonspecific and cannot rule out the effect of central nervous system depressant drugs. Electroencephalogram 08/10: the marked symmetrical disorganization of the background activity due to diffuse and irregular slowing in the delta range, absence of physiological graphoelements of sleep and absence of electroencephalographic reactivity to painful stimuli noted intermittent modulation by low amplitude sinusoidal rhythms in the alpha/beta range. Conclusion and electroclinical correlation: the electroencephalogram is abnormal. The findings are compatible with the effect of central nervous system depressant drugs, and associated brain dysfunction (encephalopathy) cannot be ruled out. away, negative pcr for coronavirus research 06/12/2020 2. primary bloodstream infection, fever, central venous catheter removed on 06/01, blood culture and catheter tip culture 06/01 carbapenemase-resistant klebsiella pneumoniae amikacin 1000 mg 48/48h d0 06/04/2020 polymyxin b 1,000,000 8/8 h d0 06/04/2020 metronidazole 500 mg 8/8h d0 06/06/2020 adjusted for weight and renal function 3. tetraparesis secondary to critical illness myopathy 4. diarrhea after exposure to antibiotics, clostridium toxin a/b research on 06/05/20 still without results, started metronidazole 500 mg 8/8h at midnight on 06/06/20, personal history: recent hospitalization 04/05 23/05: severe acute respiratory syndrome secondary to community-acquired pneumonia? 2 negative coronavirus and influenza pcrs, critically ill myopathy, epigastric cellulitis secondary to resolved proning position, morbid obesity body mass index 54.67 kg/m2 systemic arterial hypertension, congenital divergent strabismus, marijuana user since age 13, social drinker admission weight 210 kg discharge weight 144 kg height 1.96 m. physical examination: good general condition, flushed, hydrated, anicteric, acyanotic, afebrile. lucid, oriented in time and space, glasgow 15 cardiac auscultation: regular double rhythm, normal heart sounds, no murmurs, absence of jugular distension at 45º. respiratory auscultation: symmetrical expansion, vesicular breath sounds present bilaterally, no adventitious sounds, impaired pulmonary auscultation due to prominent adipose tissue; abdomen: globular, no scars, no bulging or retractions, bowel sounds present in all 4 quadrants, tympanic to percussion, painless to palpation, no visceromegaly and no palpable masses, negative sudden decompression throughout the abdomen. extremities: palpable, full and symmetrical pulses in all 4 limbs, no edema, free calves, no signs of deep vein thrombosis. skin: presence of an area of oval hyperchromia in the epigastric region, approximately 10 cm in the largest diameter, with underlying induration. presence of pressure ulcers in the inner regions of both thighs, without necrotic areas and without inflammatory signs. 9 Left-sided pyelonephritis onset on 01/07/20. admission: 01/16/2020, discharge: 01/26/2020, stay: 09 d 21 h, location: ward, payer: sus. [person] with migraine being followed up at acef. came for a consultation today reporting dysuria, fever, and systemic symptoms for 9 days. in {omitted} 2019 she presented with septic shock with a urinary focus due to left-sided obstructive pyelonephritis. she remained hospitalized at [organization] requiring norepinephrine for 2 days, without the need for orotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation. she underwent treatment with a Double-J stent passage to the left, in addition to meropenem for 10 days, with complete response. on that occasion she presented negative urine and blood cultures. was discharged with outpatient reevaluation by a urology team in {omitted} 2019, with scheduled ureterolithotripsy of a 1.2 cm, 600 hu stone in the proximal third of the left ureter in March 2020. At that time, a pre-anesthetic evaluation was requested, and a follow-up visit was scheduled afterwards. Since discharge, the patient has had 3 episodes of lower urinary tract infection, with symptoms of dysuria, pollakiuria, sensation of incomplete emptying, and hypogastric pain, treated respectively with clavulin 875 mg orally 12/12 h for 14 days, a single dose of fosfomycin, and clavulin 875 mg orally 12/12 h for 14 days, all without the need for hospitalization and with improvement of symptoms. On 01/01/20, a new episode of dysuria, pollakiuria, and sensation of incomplete emptying began. The patient reports not having started antibiotics during this period. On 01/07/20, she began to have a fever of 38.5° and 39°, choosing to seek care at [organization]. She underwent a urine test collection 1, checked on 01/08/20, and was then referred to the city's emergency care unit. On 01/09/20, a single dose of fosfomycin was prescribed and released, however, due to continued fever, with the onset of chills and pain in the lower back and left flank, she follow-up appointmented to the emergency care unit, where she was then prescribed ciprofloxacin 500 mg orally 12/12 h. She reports that since 01/11/20 she has been using ciprofloxacin, without fever, but her general condition continues to decline, nausea, chills, with episodes of vomiting on January 14th and 15th, in addition to severe pain in the lower back and left flank, causing her to lie in the left lateral decubitus position. continues to complain of hypogastric pain, dysuria, pollakiuria, sensation of incomplete emptying. Heart rate: 80 beats per minute, blood pressure: 120x70 mmHg, saturation: 98%, room air, respiratory rate: 18 inspirations per minute, obese, good general condition, afebrile, normal color, alert, cooperative. Cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm in 2t, no murmurs auscultation. Respiratory auscultation: vesicular breath sounds without adventitious sounds, no effort, no pain. Abdominal palpation: peripheral perfusion and pulses preserved, no edema. Cultures: urine culture 01/16 70,000 candida albicans multis. Blood culture 01/16 1 sample of micrococcus sp. Urine culture 01/21 negative. Double-J stent exchange on the left 01/18/20. Urine culture for collection 7 days before urological procedure. Lithotripsy to be scheduled by urology team at outpatient follow-up. Follow-up with uro on 03/12/20. Follow-up with cefbot neurocephalea on 05/21/20. Resolution of asymptomatic pyelonephritis. Left-sided pyelonephritis onset on 01/07/20. 12 admission: 04/05/2020, discharge: 22/05/2020, stay: 18 d 06 h, location: ward, payer: SUS. [person] reported that 2 days after admission, he started feeling unwell, headache and mild myalgia, in addition to coughing, reporting reddish secretion, sensation of chills, reporting an unmeasured fever. he developed dyspnea, even at rest, having difficulty sleeping because of it. he reports that the dyspnea progressively worsened during the night, until he sought care on the day of admission. he denied any other symptoms. in the unit of origin, [person] was admitted eupneic, however with sat02 72% room air. and 85 89% with a reservoir mask, with crackles and wheezing, being administered aerosol with salbutamol, in addition to magnesium sulfate and started oseltamivir, according to the transfer report. [person] admitted to a monitored bed in a coronavirus red room, transferred by [organization], accompanied by [person], saturation 89% with reservoir mask, tachydyspneic, respiratory rate 35 breaths per minute, with use of accessory muscles, glasgow 15. it was decided to proceed with orotracheal intubation, [person] with difficult airway, successfully {omitted} by the anesthesiology team, with use of videolaryngoscopy and a 7.5 tube. sedation and neuromuscular blockade were initiated for better coupling to mechanical ventilation. due to suspected diagnosis of pulmonary thromboembolism, unfractionated heparin was started in a pump, already on a therapeutic regimen with ceftriaxone, clarithromycin, and oseltamivir. due to suspected cellulitis in the left lower limb, oxacillin was introduced to the therapeutic regimen on the same day 04/05. After negative PCR for coronavirus and influenza, [person] was transferred to an intensive care unit bed on 05/05, placed in the proning position, with improved Pao2/Fio2 ratio. Attempted again the next day, but without adequate response. Doppler ultrasound of the lower limbs was performed, ruling out the hypothesis of deep vein thrombosis or pulmonary thromboembolism, with transition from heparin to prophylactic. On 07/05, it was decided to exchange the 7.5 tube for a 9 tube with videolaryngoscopy and a changing tube, without complications. Due to difficulty in managing blood pressure levels, Nipride was started on a continuous infusion pump on 05/13, with optimization of oral antihypertensives and successful weaning later. Due to improvement in ventilatory parameters and adequate level of consciousness, extubation was performed on 05/14, successfully. [person] presented bronchospasm after the same, with improvement after clinical measures. After extubation, changes in extrinsic ocular motility were identified, in addition to anisocoria. The hypothesis of pharmacological anisocoria was raised, and a test with 1% atropine was performed, with a response of symmetrical mydriasis and congenital divergent strabismus. In addition, [person] also presents with tetraparesis, secondary to critical patient myopathy. Since 05/15, [person] has been evolving with persistent subfebrile levels 37.5 - 37.7, reaching maximum peaks of 37.9, but continuing to improve clinically. A new infectious screening was performed and central venous access and invasive blood pressure were removed. Due to clinical stability, no use of vasoactive drugs, [person] is comfortable in room air. [person] transferred to [organization] on 05/20. while admitted to the ward, [person] presented a fever of 37.8º C, with no other associated symptoms. A new infectious screening was performed, with no signs of infection in laboratory or radiological tests. Indwelling urinary catheter removed on 05/22. Admission: 04/05/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 22/05/2020, stay: 18 d 06 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme (sus). The patient reported that 2 days after admission, he started feeling unwell, headache and mild myalgia, in addition to coughing, reporting reddish secretion, sensation of chills, reporting an unmeasured fever. He developed difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), even at rest, having difficulty sleeping because of it. He reports that the difficulty in breathing (dyspnea) progressively worsened during the night, until he sought care on the day of admission. He denied any other symptoms. In the unit of origin, the patient was admitted breathing normally (eupneic), however with sat02 72% room air. And 85 89% with a reservoir mask, with crackles and wheezing, being give (administer)ed aerosol with salbutamol, in addition to magnesium sulfate and started oseltamivir, according to the transfer report. The patient admitted to a monitored bed in a coronavirus red room, transferred by [organization], accompanied by the patient, oxygen level in blood (saturation) 89% with reservoir mask, tachydyspneic, respiratory rate 35 breaths per minute, with use of accessory muscles, glasgow 15. It was decided to proceed with orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation), the patient with difficult airway, successfully by the anesthesiology team, with use of videolaryngoscopy and a 7.5 tube. Sedation and neuromuscular blockade were initiated for better coupling to machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation). Due to government health scheme (sus)pected diagnosis of pulmonary thromboblood clot blocking a vessel (embolism), unfractionated heparin was started in a pump, already on a therapeutic regimen with ceftriaxone, clarithromycin, and oseltamivir. Attempted again the next day, but without adequate response. She was taken to the clinical stabilization room. 15 liver cirrhosis. admission: 09/19/2022, discharge: 10/19/2022, stay: 01 m 11 h, location: ward, payer: SUS. 60 years old, female, from [address], incomplete high school education, works as a cleaner. Anthropometry: estimated weight: 60 kg predicted weight: 57 kg height 164 cm, problem: 1 resolved septic shock with pulmonary focus, acute respiratory failure, hypotension decreased level of consciousness, blood culture with growth of staphylococcus aureus oxas, coronavirus infection in march rtpcr 03/15 2 acute kidney injury kdigo 1 resolved pre-renal, 3 cirrhosis pleural effusion need for paracentesis and relief thoracentesis paracentesis 09/19: blood gas: 1.61 albumin: 0.49 serum albumin: 2.1 100 gb glucose: 164 ldh: 72 serum: 314 thoracentesis 09/19: transudate protein: 2 serum: 7.7 ldh: 72 serum: 279 pH: 7.378 albumin: 0.61 serum: 1.9 amylase: 117 glucose: 183 gb: 100, negative culture thoracentesis 09/23/2022: drained 700 ml. transudate thoracentesis 10/06/2022: drained 1000 ml thoracentesis 10/10/2022: drained 1000 ml. transudate thoracentesis 10/15/2022: drained 1000 ml. history of current illness: [person] diagnosed with liver cirrhosis of etiology to be clarified in follow-up with the gastroclinic at [organization]. underwent a consultation on 02/09/2022, when she was advised to increase the furosemide to 40 mg twice daily and spironolactone to 50 mg/d due to massive ascites, dyspnea, and weight gain. Paracentesis was also performed on the same day, with drainage of 4.1 liters of ascitic fluid. She underwent paracentesis again on 13/09, with 1.4 liters of fluid drained. [person] was referred from the emergency care unit with information from a medical report reporting admission on 18/09/2022, with 85% room air desaturation, obtunded, with peripheral cyanosis and livedo reticularis, followed with uneventful orotracheal intubation. {omitted}, reporting onset of dyspnea this morning. 4 ampoules of furosemide were administered, with no urine output, according to the transport team. I admit [person] intubated, sedated with midazolam and fentanyl, blocked with rocuronium, hemodynamically compensated with norepinephrine 20 ml/h. hospitalization summary: [person] initially admitted to {omitted}, where hypotheses of acute respiratory failure secondary to coronavirus, congestion or sepsis of pulmonary focus were considered. upon admission, empirical ceftriaxone was started and diagnostic paracentesis was performed, with no signs of spontaneous bacterial peritonitis. on the same day, thoracentesis was performed with removal of 1000 ml of fluid, suggestive of transudate, and relief paracentesis was performed with removal of 4000 ml, with albumin being administered due to fluid removal. Also on 09/19, a pocus was performed that showed lines and pleural thickening, which, associated with the history of respiratory failure and need for vasoactive drugs, strengthened the hypothesis of sepsis of pulmonary focus as the cause of respiratory failure. Therefore, it was decided to associate clarithromycin with clindamycin on 09/24, after observing growth of Staphylococcus aureus in blood culture, antibiotic therapy was changed from ceftriaxone clarithromycin to levofloxacin oxacillin as directed by the [organization]. On the same day, it was decided to request an echocardiogram to screen for endocarditis, given the growth of this microorganism in blood culture. On the morning of 09/27, it was decided to escalate antibiotic therapy to meropenem oxacillin due to worsening of CRP associated with increased need for vasoactive drugs. evolved with improvement in the ventilatory status, with extubation on 10/03, and withdrawal of supplemental oxygen therapy on 10/13. resolution of the septic condition, with completion of antibiotic therapy on 10/08. remained hospitalized due to the need for 24-hour nursing care, care for pressure injuries, need for oxygen therapy, management of ascites and pleural effusion, without social support for discharge home. personal history: 1 first diagnosis of liver cirrhosis of etiology to be clarified child b7 meld 9 metabolic?, child b8, meld 12 2021, child b8, meld meld 10 2021 under follow-up since 2019 due to alteration in transaminases biopsy on 07/2020 with grade i fibrosis disease progression? sample not significant? 2021 biopsy: chronic liver disease with intense fibrosis and moderate steatosis, favoring the diagnosis of grade 2 steatohepatitis stage 3 complications: ascites, shp with medium-caliber ve, spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, eh, upper gastrointestinal bleeding etiological investigation: 2020 liver biopsy: widened portal space due to mild grade I fibrosis, mild inflammatory infiltrate with mononuclear predominance grade I, minimal hepatitis at the interface, diffuse macro and microgoticular steatosis plus 60% parenchyma grade III, balloon-shaped degeneration grade I, nuclear vacuolization of hepatocytes mild to moderate steatohepatitis nash 6 therapeutic trial for hai with azathioprine and prednisone 11/03 01/14 no satisfactory response drop in tgp, but maintenance of high tgo hai score before treatment 02/2/2/3/1/2/3 9 fan: 1: 350, anti-smooth muscle 1: 80 alteration of canalicular and transaminases alteration international normalized ratio: 06/08: 1.0 / 29/09: 1.9 / 22/10: 4.4 differential diagnosis: fan: 1: 320 homogeneous nuclear anti-mitochondria non-reactive anti-smooth muscle 1: 80 anti-lkm negative ceruloplasmin: 0.305 alpha feto: 6.17 cytomegalovirus: negative epstein bar negative cea: 4.48 n: 4.9 ca 125: 25 n: 21 ca19.9: 27 shp: digestive endoscopy 2021 medium-caliber esophageal varices with no signs of recent bleeding mild hypertensive gastropathy exanthematous bulboduodenitis 2 weight loss to be clarified 10 kg in 9 months malabsorptive syndrome to be clarified folic acid deficiency decompensation diabetes mellitus?? occult neoplasia? depressive disorder? investigation: folic acid 1.29 deficient 0.35 3.37 prot/creat ratio 120 300 immunoglobulin iga dosage: 917 40350 vitamin d 04/01: 34 antitga 04/01: positive 3, recent hospitalization: epistaxis, hematuria and melena coagulopathy due to consumption? progression of liver disease? 12/30 01/01 comorbidities allergy to dipyrone chickenpox and measles in childhood tonsillectomy for hypertrophy of pharyngeal tonsils 15 years ago diabetes mellitus 15 years ago systemic arterial hypertension 10 years ago nontoxic multinodular goiter diagnosed in 2019 anxiety disorder, depression diagnosed 6 years ago spondyloarthrosis spine, low back pain painful nodule in the inferolateral quadrant of the right breast with biopsy performed in November 2019, indicating probable fibroadenoma ex-smoker habits claims to have smoked 1 pack/day for only a few months reports a history of drinking 34 cans of beer per weekend, but denies daily use of the substance claims to have stopped 2 years ago denies use of illicit drugs last sexual activity 1 year ago. Denies sexual activity without a condom Denies use of NSAIDs Reports sporadic use of mate tea and lemon balm Denies tattoos and piercings Medications used prior to hospitalization: Insulin NPH 1008 Glifage XR 500 mg: 2 tablets at lunch and 2 tablets at dinner Simvastatin 20 mg/day Losartan 50 mg: 1 tablet every 12 hours Hydrochlorothiazide: 1x day Sertraline 50 mg: 1 tablet every 12 hours Gabapentin 300 mg/day Calcium and vitamin D Amitriptyline 25 mg Propranolol 10 mg every 12 hours Omeprazole 40 mg Furosemide 40 mg twice a day Spironolactone 50 mg/d External exams: 2020 mammogram: Right retroareolar breast nodule, negative biopsy for neoplasia Right breast: BIRADS 4 Left breast: BIRADS 1 upper digestive endoscopy 2020: distal non-erosive esophagitis, enanthematous pangastritis biopsy: helicobacter pylori antibiotic therapy: current: nothing previous ceftriaxone d0 09/19 09/23 clarithromycin d0 09/20 09/23 levofloxacin d0 09/23 09/26 oxacillin d0 09/24 10/08 meropenem d0 09/27 08/10 devices nasoenteral tube valproic acid previous devices: tot 09/18 09/26 tot 09/26 10/03 central venous access right internal jugular vein 09/19 09/28 indwelling urinary catheter 09/18 03/10 cultures: urine culture 09/19: negative urine culture 09/28: negative blood culture 09/19: staphylococcus aureus in 2 samples blood culture 09/24: staphylococcus epidermidis oxa r blood culture 09/28: staphylococcus capitis oxa r pleural fluid 09/19: negative pleural fluid 09/23: negative pleural fluid 09/24: negative ascitic fluid 09/19: negative catheter tip 09/28: negative tests: tomography 09/21: bilateral pulmonary opacities that may represent edema or inflammatory process. bilateral pleural effusion with atelectatic opacities in the lower lobes. cardiomegaly. transthoracic echocardiography: ejection fraction 59% [person], LVEDD 45. marked dilation of the left atrium. preserved ventricular systolic performance. normal left ventricular segmental mobility. mild mitral and tricuspid valve insufficiency. mild left ventricular diastolic dysfunction. schedule: adjustment of diuretic doses, with active monitoring of renal function; performance of relief paracentesis and thoracentesis; progress in weaning from oxygen therapy; assessment of nasoenteric tube removal; if there is good acceptance of oral diet; care for pressure injuries in sacral and trochanteric regions; scheduled return visit at agaf 18/01, having already spoken with [person] and [person]&#39;s family members about the severity of [person]&#39;s situation as well as prognostic possibilities. family members aware of the situation and a shared decision was made not to perform new invasive measures, {omitted}. Liver liver scarring (cirrhosis). Admission: 09/19/2022, sent home from hospital (discharge): 10/19/2022, stay: 01 m 11 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme (sus). 60 years old, female, from [address], incomplete high school education, works as a cleaner. Anthropometry: estimated weight: 60 kg predicted weight: 57 kg height 164 cm, problem: 1 resolved severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) with pulmonary focus, acute lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure), low BP (hypotension) decreased alertness level (level of consciousness), blood test to find infection (blood culture) with growth of staphylococcus aureus oxas, coronavirus infection in march rtpcr 03/15 2 acute kidney injury kdigo 1 resolved pre-renal, 3 liver scarring (cirrhosis) fluid around the lungs (pleural effusion) need for paracentesis and relief thoracentesis paracentesis 09/19: blood gas: 1.61 albumin: 0.49 serum albumin: 2.1 100 gb glucose: 164 ldh: 72 serum: 314 thoracentesis 09/19: transudate protein: 2 serum: 7.7 ldh: 72 serum: 279 ph: 7.378 albumin: 0.61 serum: 1.9 amylase: 117 glucose: 183 gb: 100, negative culture thoracentesis 09/23/2022: drained 700 ml. Transudate thoracentesis 10/06/2022: drained 1000 ml thoracentesis 10/10/2022: drained 1000 ml. Transudate thoracentesis 10/15/2022: drained 1000 ml. History of current illness: the patient diagnosed with liver liver scarring (cirrhosis) of cause of disease (etiology) to be clarified in return visit to doctor (follow-up) with the gastroclinic at [organization]. Underwent a consultation on 02/09/2022, when she was advised to increase the furosemide to 40 mg twice daily and spironolactone to 50 mg/d due to massive fluid buildup in abdomen (ascites), difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), and weight gain. Paracentesis was also performed on the same day, with drainage of 4.1 liters of ascitic fluid. She underwent paracentesis again on 13/09, with 1.4 liters of fluid drained. The patient was referred from the emergency care unit with information from a medical report reporting admission on 18/09/2022, with 85% room air deoxygen level in blood (saturation), obtunded, with peripheral cyanosis and livedo reticularis, followed with uneventful orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation). , reporting onset of difficulty in breathing (dyspnea) this morning. 4 ampoules of furosemide were give (administer)ed, with no urine output, according to the transport team. I admit the patient intubated, sedated with midazolam and fentanyl, blocked with rocuronium, hemodynamically compensated with norepinephrine 20 ml/h. Hospitalization summary: the patient initially admitted to , where hypotheses of acute lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) secondary to coronavirus, congestion or serious blood infection (sepsis) of pulmonary focus were considered. Upon admission, empirical ceftriaxone was started and diagnostic paracentesis was performed, with no signs of spontaneous bacterial infection inside the abdomen (peritonitis). On the same day, thoracentesis was performed with removal of 1000 ml of fluid, suggestive of transudate, and relief paracentesis was performed with removal of 4000 ml, with albumin being give (administer)ed due to fluid removal. Also on 09/19, a pocus was performed that showed lines and pleural thickening, which, associated with the history of lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) and need for vasoactive drugs, strengthened the hypothesis of serious blood infection (sepsis) of pulmonary focus as the cause of lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure). Therefore, it was decided to associate clarithromycin with clindamycin on 09/24, after observing growth of staphylococcus aureus in blood test to find infection (blood culture), infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) was changed from ceftriaxone clarithromycin to levofloxacin oxacillin as directed by the [organization]. On the same day, it was decided to request an heart ultrasound (echocardiogram) to screen for infection of heart valves (endocarditis), given the growth of this microorganism in blood test to find infection (blood culture). On the morning of 09/27, it was decided to escalate infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) to meropenem oxacillin due to worsening of crp associated with increased need for vasoactive drugs. Evolved with improvement in the ventilatory status, with breathing tube removed (extubation) on 10/03, and withdrawal of supplemental oxygen therapy on 10/13. Resolution of the septic condition, with completion of infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) on 10/08. Remained hospitalized due to the need for 24-hour nursing care, care for pressure injuries, need for oxygen therapy, management of fluid buildup in abdomen (ascites) and fluid around the lungs (pleural effusion), without social support for sent home from hospital (discharge) home. Underwent a consultation on 02/09/2022, when she was advised to increase the furosemide to 40 mg twice daily and spironolactone to 50 mg/d due to massive fluid buildup in abdomen (ascites), difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), and weight gain. Paracentesis was also performed on the same day, with drainage of 4.1 liters of ascitic fluid. She underwent paracentesis again on 13/09, with 1.4 liters of fluid drained. The patient was referred from the emergency care unit with information from a medical report reporting admission on 18/09/2022, with 85% room air deoxygen level in blood (saturation), obtunded, with peripheral cyanosis and livedo reticularis, followed with uneventful orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation). , reporting onset of difficulty in breathing (dyspnea) this morning. 4 ampoules of furosemide were give (administer)ed, with no urine output, according to the transport team. I admit the patient intubated, sedated with midazolam and fentanyl, blocked with rocuronium, hemodynamically compensated with norepinephrine 20 ml/h. Hospitalization summary: the patient initially admitted to , where hypotheses of acute lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) secondary to coronavirus, congestion or serious blood infection (sepsis) of pulmonary focus were considered. Upon admission, empirical ceftriaxone was started and diagnostic paracentesis was performed, with no signs of spontaneous bacterial infection inside the abdomen (peritonitis). On the same day, thoracentesis was performed with removal of 1000 ml of fluid, suggestive of transudate, and relief paracentesis was performed with removal of 4000 ml, with albumin being give (administer)ed due to fluid removal. Also on 09/19, a pocus was performed that showed lines and pleural thickening, which, associated with the history of lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) and need for vasoactive drugs, strengthened the hypothesis of serious blood infection (sepsis) of pulmonary focus as the cause of lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure). Therefore, it was decided to associate clarithromycin with clindamycin on 09/24, after observing growth of staphylococcus aureus in blood test to find infection (blood culture), infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) was changed from ceftriaxone clarithromycin to levofloxacin oxacillin as directed by the [organization]. On the same day, it was decided to request an heart ultrasound (echocardiogram) to screen for infection of heart valves (endocarditis), given the growth of this microorganism in blood test to find infection (blood culture). On the morning of 09/27, it was decided to escalate infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) to meropenem oxacillin due to worsening of crp associated with increased need for vasoactive drugs. Evolved with improvement in the ventilatory status, with breathing tube removed (extubation) on 10/03, and withdrawal of supplemental oxygen therapy on 10/13. Resolution of the septic condition, with completion of infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) on 10/08. Remained hospitalized due to the need for 24-hour nursing care, care for pressure injuries, need for oxygen therapy, management of fluid buildup in abdomen (ascites) and fluid around the lungs (pleural effusion), without social support for sent home from hospital (discharge) home. Personal history: 1 first diagnosis of liver liver scarring (cirrhosis) of cause of disease (etiology) to be clarified child b7 meld 9 metabolic?, child b8, meld 12 2021, child b8, meld meld 10 2021 under return visit to doctor (follow-up) since 2019 due to alteration in transaminases biopsy on 07/2020 with grade i fibrosis disease progression? Sample not significant? 2021 biopsy: chronic liver disease with intense fibrosis and moderate steatosis, favoring the diagnosis of grade 2 steatohepatitis stage 3 complications: fluid buildup in abdomen (ascites), shp with medium-caliber ve, spontaneous bacterial infection inside the abdomen (peritonitis), eh, upper gastrointestinal bleeding etiological investigation: 2020 liver biopsy: widened portal space due to mild grade i fibrosis, mild inflammatory infiltrate with mononuclear predominance grade i, minimal hepatitis at the interface, diffuse macro and microgoticular steatosis plus 60% parenchyma grade iii, balloon-shaped degeneration grade i, nuclear vacuolization of hepatocytes mild to moderate steatohepatitis nash 6 therapeutic trial for hai with azathioprine and prednisone 11/03 01/14 no satisfactory response drop in tgp, but maintenance of high tgo hai score before treatment 02/2/2/3/1/2/3 9 fan: 1: 350, anti-smooth muscle 1: 80 alteration of canalicular and transaminases alteration international normalized ratio: 06/08: 1.0 / 29/09: 1.9 / 22/10: 4.4 differential diagnosis: fan: 1: 320 homogeneous nuclear anti-mitochondria non-reactive anti-smooth muscle 1: 80 anti-lkm negative ceruloplasmin: 0.305 alpha feto: 6.17 cytomegalovirus: negative epstein bar negative cea: 4.48 n: 4.9 ca 125: 25 n: 21 ca19.9: 27 shp: digestive endoscopy 2021 medium-caliber esophageal varices with no signs of recent bleeding mild hypertensive gastropathy exanthematous bulboduodenitis 2 weight loss to be clarified 10 kg in 9 months malabsorptive syndrome to be clarified folic acid deficiency decompensation sugar disease (diabetes)?? Occult neoplasia? Depressive disorder? Investigation: folic acid 1.29 deficient 0.35 3.37 prot/creat ratio 120 300 immunoglobulin iga dosage: 917 40350 vitamin d 04/01: 34 antitga 04/01: positive 3, recent hospitalization: epistaxis, hematuria and melena coagulopathy due to consumption? Progression of liver disease? 12/30 01/01 comorbidities allergy to dipyrone chickenpox and measles in childhood tonsillectomy for hypertrophy of pharyngeal tonsils 15 years ago sugar disease (diabetes) 15 years ago systemic arterial high BP (hypertension) 10 years ago nontoxic multinodular goiter diagnosed in 2019 anxiety disorder, depression diagnosed 6 years ago spondyloarthrosis spine, low back pain painful nodule in the inferolateral quadrant of the right breast with biopsy performed in november 2019, indicating probable fibroadenoma ex-smoker habits claims to have smoked 1 pack/day for only a few months reports a history of drinking 34 cans of beer per weekend, but denies daily use of the substance claims to have stopped 2 years ago denies use of illicit drugs last sexual activity 1 year ago. Denies sexual activity without a condom denies use of nsaids reports sporadic use of mate tea and lemon balm denies tattoos and piercings medications used prior to hospitalization: insulin nph 1008 glifage xr 500 mg: 2 tablets at lunch and 2 tablets at dinner simvastatin 20 mg/day losartan 50 mg: 1 tablet every 12 hours hydrochlorothiazide: 1x day sertraline 50 mg: 1 tablet every 12 hours gabapentin 300 mg/day calcium and vitamin d amitriptyline 25 mg propranolol 10 mg every 12 hours omeprazole 40 mg furosemide 40 mg twice a day spironolactone 50 mg/d external exams: 2020 mammogram: right retroareolar breast nodule, negative biopsy for neoplasia right breast: birads 4 left breast: birads 1 upper digestive endoscopy 2020: distal non-erosive esophagitis, enanthematous pangastritis biopsy: helicobacter pylori infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy): current: nothing previous ceftriaxone d0 09/19 09/23 clarithromycin d0 09/20 09/23 levofloxacin d0 09/23 09/26 oxacillin d0 09/24 10/08 meropenem d0 09/27 08/10 devices nasoenteral tube valproic acid previous devices: tot 09/18 09/26 tot 09/26 10/03 central venous access right internal jugular vein 09/19 09/28 indwelling urinary catheter 09/18 03/10 cultures: urine culture 09/19: negative urine culture 09/28: negative blood test to find infection (blood culture) 09/19: staphylococcus aureus in 2 samples blood test to find infection (blood culture) 09/24: staphylococcus epidermidis oxa r blood test to find infection (blood culture) 09/28: staphylococcus capitis oxa r pleural fluid 09/19: negative pleural fluid 09/23: negative pleural fluid 09/24: negative ascitic fluid 09/19: negative catheter tip 09/28: negative tests: tomography 09/21: bilateral pulmonary opacities that may represent swelling (edema) or inflammatory process. Bilateral fluid around the lungs (pleural effusion) with atelectatic opacities in the lower lobes. Cardiomegaly. Transthoracic echocardiography: ejection fraction 59% the patient, lvedd 45. Marked dilation of the left atrium. Preserved ventricular systolic performance. Normal left ventricular segmental mobility. Mild mitral and tricuspid valve insufficiency. Mild left ventricular diastolic dysfunction. Schedule: adjustment of diuretic doses, with active monitoring of renal function; performance of relief paracentesis and thoracentesis; progress in weaning from oxygen therapy; assessment of nasoenteric tube removal; if there is good acceptance of oral diet; care for pressure injuries in sacral and trochanteric regions; scheduled return visit at agaf 18/01, having already spoken with the patient and the patient&#39;s family members about the severity of the patient&#39;s situation as well as prognostic possibilities. Family members aware of the situation and a shared decision was made not to perform new invasive measures, . d689. coagulation defect n.e. l89. pressure ulcer. a412. staphylococcal septicemia n.e. k746. other forms of liver cirrhosis and associated diseases n.e. m199. osteoarthritis n.e. u071. novel coronavirus infection n.e. e042. multinodular nontoxic goiter n.e. e118. noninsulin-dependent diabetes mellitus with complications n.e. k760. fatty degeneration of liver not classified elsewhere. k766. portal hypertension n.e. k769. liver disease, not otherwise specified. r18. ascites alive 17.2 1.14
16 [person], 34 years old admission to coronavirus red room: 07/26 problem: 1 spontaneous pneumothorax on the left pleuropulmonary fistula secondary to non-bacillary pulmonary tuberculosis drained on 07/22 external drain passed by the surgical clinic: 07/28 removed 07/30 started ceftriaxone in external service for pulmonary focus infection tbtrm 07/30: positive sensitive to rifampicin, sputum culture 07/27: positive for mycobacterium tuberculosis. sputum smear microscopy 07/27: negative, mucosal sputum smear microscopy 07/30: negative mucosal sputum smear microscopy 08/02 negative, start of ripe 07/30, weight at the time: 69 kg positive epidemiology: close contact with individuals with a history of social seclusion. Serologies 07/28: negative anti-hiv elisa, hepatitis and vdrl, 2 septic shock 07/28? pulmonary focus? pleural effusion complicated aspect, q sofa 2, tachypnea and hypotension with mean arterial pressure 50, volume expansion requiring vasoactive drug, ceftriaxone started in an outpatient service for pulmonary focus infection, escalated antibiotic therapy meropenem vancomycin 07/28 08/04 chest drain 22 07/30 reevaluated by the surgical team on 08/02, who did not recommend additional procedures. [person] remained with residual pneumothorax, stable in 3 coronavirus ruled out 2 negative external tests 1 negative test, vaccinated on 06/01 [organization]. admission: 07/26/2020, discharge: 08/09/2020, stay: 13 d 21 h, location: ward, payer: SUS. history and developments during hospitalization: [person] reports that on 07/20, at 10 am, he suddenly presented with left-sided chest pain, ventilator-dependent, severe intensity, accompanied by progressive dyspnea. He sought care and was admitted to [organization] and left the service after some tests were collected. He follow-up appointmented on 07/21, when a chest x-ray was performed, revealing pneumothorax on the left and diffuse interstitial infiltrate. During hospitalization, he developed daily fever. On 07/22, his left chest was drained. [person] reports chronic dry cough since childhood, which is correlated with premature birth. Denies diarrhea, anosmia, ageusia, sore throat, headache, and myalgia. [organization] used ceftriaxone, dexamethasone, rivaroxaban. On admission [person] hemodynamically stable without vasoactive drugs, tachypneic in room air, oxygen saturation 96%. Electrocardiogram tracing due to tachycardia heart rate, 145 sinus rhythm, left ventricular overload. During the night, [person] developed tachycardia and hypotension, suspected septic shock, due to the condition initiated on meropenem and vancomycin, thinking of nosocomial pneumonia with prior use of antibiotic therapy and device and performed volume expansion with improvement of tachycardia and elevation of mean arterial pressure 65. On 07/27, case discussed with [organization] [person] who advised: suspend meropenem and vancomycin and start ceftriaxone and oxacillin, request research for tuberculosis after review of chest tomography and observing an image suggestive of cavern in left parenchyma, request research for fungi and serologies. On 7/28, [person] with borderline mean arterial pressure at the beginning of the shift, developed hypotension during the morning, mean arterial pressure 50, accompanied by sweating and nausea, volume expansion was started with 1,500 ml of saline 0.9% and norepinephrine was turned off on the same day. Later, under the guidance of general surgery, a suction drain was placed in the mariot. As [person] with significant clinical worsening today, with septic shock refractory to volume expansion, and significant worsening of the blood count, leukocytes 16,800 with 37% rods, it was decided to escalate antibiotic therapy to meropenem and vancomycin. On 7/29, [person] was admitted to a monitored isolation bed, hypotensive, saline solution was administered 500 ml with open clamp, but [person] maintained hypotension, low-dose norepinephrine was started at 0.08 mcg/kg/min with gradual weaning throughout the day. On 7/30, in discussion with CLC, discussed with [person] who states that the plan is to remove the drain and request a new x-ray for evaluation. {omitted} [person] is experiencing worsening of the infection with a possible unaddressed focus of empyema. After discussion with {omitted}, it was decided to request a new non-contrast tomography after removal of the drain. [person] referred to the respiratory isolation bed. On 8/4, due to clinical improvement, meropenem and vancomycin were suspended after 7 days of therapy. On 8/5: [person] was admitted to a bed at [organization] to continue tuberculosis treatment and evaluate possible toxicity to RIP. He showed good clinical progress, with improved dyspnea, cessation of fever, and good tolerance to the therapeutic regimen. Therefore, it was decided to be discharged from the hospital on 8/9 and to follow-up appointment to the hospital in 2 weeks. Desaturation, reduced breath sounds in the right hemithorax. 1 chest drainage. 1 continued follow-up with [organization]. follow-up appointment on amin on 08/26. improved.
