17 admission: 03/16/2020, discharge: 04/28/2020, stay: 01 m 11 d 23 h, location: ward, payer: sus. [person] with a history of epilepsy, admitted to the [organization] ward for investigation of generalized weakness, urinary incontinence, and altered mental status with evolution in the last few months, imprecise chronology, evolution initially from December onwards. during hospitalization, non-convulsive status was documented, treatment was carried out with progressive adjustment of antiepileptic drugs until, after a valproate attack on 03/23, the level of consciousness progressively decreased to unresponsiveness, requiring orotracheal intubation and sedation. due to aspiration pneumonia, clavulin 1 g 8/8h was started, then escalated to piperacillin tazobactam as directed by [organization]. [person] transferred to [organization] on 03/27 due to septic shock, progressing to renal failure requiring hemodialysis. Extubated on 04/05, follow-up appointmenting to [organization]. General condition deteriorated and tremors worsened during hospitalization at [organization] for VEEG on 04/14, due to the hypothesis of bacteremia, dialysis catheter removed and meropenem and vancomycin started. Completed 3 doses of vancomycin and 10 days of meropenem, with improvement in the infectious condition and general condition. Also presented improvement in renal function, not requiring hemodialysis in the last days of hospitalization. As [person] is being monitored at [organization], an evaluation by the [organization] team was requested. Due to the hypothesis of mitochondrial disease, an electrocardiogram, echocardiogram, resting and stress lactate, uric acid, creatine phosphokinase, aldolase, ldh, tgo, tgp, calcium, phosphorus, abdominal ultrasound for steatosis?, mitochondrial dna mutation test {omitted} and serum ammonia and phenobarbital collection were recommended. Tests performed, except for cerebrospinal fluid due to clinical complications and [person] refusal. Consider the need for the test and its performance on an outpatient basis. Among the laboratory tests, abnormal resting lactate 3.1 and stress 3.2 were found, however they were collected during an infectious condition. During hospitalization, incomplete horner's syndrome was observed, ptosis and miosis on the left on 10/04, and a chest tomography and brain MRI were performed, which did not explain the deficits. Neck MRI was requested, pending during hospitalization. Although some tests were not performed, we decided to discharge [person] due to improvement in [person]'s clinical condition and risk of infection. We will continue the investigation as an outpatient. Nephrology will perform outpatient follow-up on [person] with diuresis and improvement in renal function, advising the prescription of sodium bicarbonate 1g 12/12h. [organization] aware of the discharge, advised to maintain antiepileptic medications as currently used: phenobarbital 150 mg 12/12h; topiramate 100 mg 12/12h; lamotrigine 200 mg 12/12h. clobazam 20 mg 8/8h. ptosis; miosis on the left; tetraparesis; global ROT G2; rcp in bilateral flexion. maintained DAES as described above. hemodynamically stable, conscious and oriented, without signs of crisis. Admission: 03/16/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 04/28/2020, stay: 01 m 11 d 23 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. The patient with a history of seizure disorder (epilepsy), admitted to the [organization] general hospital room (ward) for investigation of generalized weakness, urinary incontinence, and altered mental status with evolution in the last few months, imprecise chronology, evolution initially from december ongeneral hospital room (ward)s. During hospitalization, non-convulsive status was documented, treatment was carried out with progressive adjustment of antiepileptic drugs until, after a valproate attack on 03/23, the alertness level (level of consciousness) progressively decreased to unresponsiveness, requiring orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) and sedation. Due to lung infection from inhaling food/liquid (aspiration lung infection (pneumonia)), clavulin 1 g 8/8h was started, then escalated to piperacillin tazobactam as directed by [organization]. The patient transferred to [organization] on 03/27 due to severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock), progressing to kidney failure (renal failure) requiring kidney dialysis (hemodialysis). Extubated on 04/05, return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointmenting to [organization]. General condition deteriorated and tremors worsened during hospitalization at [organization] for veeg on 04/14, due to the hypothesis of bacteremia, dialysis catheter removed and meropenem and vancomycin started. Completed 3 doses of vancomycin and 10 days of meropenem, with improvement in the infectious condition and general condition. Also presented improvement in renal function, not requiring kidney dialysis (hemodialysis) in the last days of hospitalization. As the patient is being monitored at [organization], an evaluation by the [organization] team was requested. Due to the hypothesis of mitochondrial disease, an ECG/heart tracing (electrocardiogram), heart ultrasound (echocardiogram), resting and stress lactate, uric acid, creatine phosphokinase, aldolase, ldh, tgo, tgp, calcium, phosphorus, abdominal ultrasound for steatosis?, mitochondrial dna mutation test and serum ammonia and phenobarbital collection were recommended. Tests performed, except for cerebrospinal fluid due to clinical complications and the patient refusal. Consider the need for the test and its performance on an outpatient basis. Among the laboratory tests, abnormal resting lactate 3.1 and stress 3.2 were found, however they were collected during an infectious condition. During hospitalization, incomplete horner's syndrome was observed, ptosis and miosis on the left on 10/04, and a chest tomography and brain mri were performed, which did not explain the deficits. Neck mri was requested, pending during hospitalization. Although some tests were not performed, we decided to sent home from hospital (discharge) the patient due to improvement in the patient's clinical condition and risk of infection. We will continue the investigation as an outpatient. Nephrology will perform outpatient return visit to doctor (follow-up) on the patient with diuresis and improvement in renal function, advising the prescription of sodium bicarbonate 1g 12/12h. [organization] aware of the sent home from hospital (discharge), advised to maintain antiepileptic medications as currently used: phenobarbital 150 mg 12/12h; topiramate 100 mg 12/12h; lamotrigine 200 mg 12/12h. Clobazam 20 mg 8/8h. Ptosis ; miosis on the left; tetraparesis; global rot g2; cardiopulmonary regovernment health scheme (sus)citation in bilateral flexion. Maintained daes as described above. Stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), conscious and oriented, without signs of crisis. Admission: 03/16/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 04/28/2020, stay: 01 m 11 d 23 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. The patient with a history of seizure disorder (epilepsy), admitted to the [organization] general hospital room (ward) for investigation of generalized weakness, urinary incontinence, and altered mental status with evolution in the last few months, imprecise chronology, evolution initially from december ongeneral hospital room (ward)s. During hospitalization, non-convulsive status was documented, treatment was carried out with progressive adjustment of antiepileptic drugs until, after a valproate attack on 03/23, the alertness level (level of consciousness) progressively decreased to unresponsiveness, requiring orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) and sedation. Due to lung infection from inhaling food/liquid (aspiration lung infection (pneumonia)), clavulin 1 g 8/8h was started, then escalated to piperacillin tazobactam as directed by [organization]. The patient transferred to [organization] on 03/27 due to severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock), progressing to kidney failure (renal failure) requiring kidney dialysis (hemodialysis). Extubated on 04/05, return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointmenting to [organization]. General condition deteriorated and tremors worsened during hospitalization at [organization] for veeg on 04/14, due to the hypothesis of bacteremia, dialysis catheter removed and meropenem and vancomycin started. Completed 3 doses of vancomycin and 10 days of meropenem, with improvement in the infectious condition and general condition. Also presented improvement in renal function, not requiring kidney dialysis (hemodialysis) in the last days of hospitalization. As the patient is being monitored at [organization], an evaluation by the [organization] team was requested. Due to the hypothesis of mitochondrial disease, an ECG/heart tracing (electrocardiogram), heart ultrasound (echocardiogram), resting and stress lactate, uric acid, creatine phosphokinase, aldolase, ldh, tgo, tgp, calcium, phosphorus, abdominal ultrasound for steatosis?, mitochondrial dna mutation test and serum ammonia and phenobarbital collection were recommended. Tests performed, except for cerebrospinal fluid due to clinical complications and the patient refusal. Consider the need for the test and its performance on an outpatient basis. Among the laboratory tests, abnormal resting lactate 3.1 and stress 3.2 were found, however they were collected during an infectious condition. During hospitalization, incomplete horner's syndrome was observed, ptosis and miosis on the left on 10/04, and a chest tomography and brain mri were performed, which did not explain the deficits. Neck mri was requested, pending during hospitalization. Although some tests were not performed, we decided to sent home from hospital (discharge) the patient due to improvement in the patient's clinical condition and risk of infection. We will continue the investigation as an outpatient. Nephrology will perform outpatient return visit to doctor (follow-up) on the patient with diuresis and improvement in renal function, advising the prescription of sodium bicarbonate 1g 12/12h. [organization] aware of the sent home from hospital (discharge), advised to maintain antiepileptic medications as currently used: phenobarbital 150 mg 12/12h; topiramate 100 mg 12/12h; lamotrigine 200 mg 12/12h. Clobazam 20 mg 8/8h. Ptosis ; miosis on the left; tetraparesis; global rot g2; cardiopulmonary regovernment health scheme (sus)citation in bilateral flexion. Maintained daes as described above. Stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), conscious and oriented, without signs of crisis. w060. fall from residential bed. z538. procedure not performed for other reasons. s443. axillary nerve injury. s068. other intracranial injuries. z988. other specified postsurgical conditions. n179. acute renal failure nes. s423. fracture of humeral shaft. s099. head injury nes. s500. elbow contusion. a419. septicemia nes. g409. epilepsy unspecified. g250. essential tremor. g401. epilepsies and symptomatic epilepsy syndromes defined by their location. g409. epilepsy unspecified. r270. ataxia nes. alive 18.2 1.18
18 [person], 72 years old, from {city}, widow, problem: 1 septic shock cutaneous focus? cellulitis abscess in left arm with spontaneous drainage ultrasound without collection tazocin vancomycin d0 02/24 blood culture 02/25: awaiting 2 ventilator-associated pneumonia? large amount of thick, yellowish secretion on 02/25 antibiotic therapy started for cutaneous focus has empirical coverage for pulmonary focus tracheal aspirate culture 02/25: awaiting 2 acute on chronic kidney disease metabolic acidosis hyperkalemia renal replacement therapy, started on 02/25. admission: 02/25/2020, discharge: 02/26/2020, stay: 01 d 15 h, location: ward, payer: sus. History of current illness: Patient transferred via zero vacancy from {city}, with the following history reported by [person]: about three weeks ago [person] reported that after applying insulin to her left arm, she noticed that the needle had broken and part of it remained in her arm. In the following days, she developed pain, edema, and heat in the limb. The nursing technician applied a dressing at home. About six days ago, [person] noticed malaise, associated with dyspnea and reduced urinary output, and had no fever until then. When she went to [organization] on Friday, she was medicated with symptomatic medications, urine was collected via a relief catheter, and was discharged with a prescription for an intramuscular antibiotic to be administered for seven days. She was unable to say the name of the prescribed antibiotic therapy. She follow-up appointmented to [organization] on Sunday for the medication to be administered, but was in poor general condition, with dyspnea, oliguria, and mental confusion when she was admitted. According to the information described in the cross-sectional form for the request for a place, they initially raised the hypothesis of a diagnosis of an abscess in the left arm and began treatment with ceftriaxone. Regarding oliguria, 1.5 mg/kg/day of furosemide was administered and an indwelling urinary catheter was inserted, with no response to diuresis, and the patient remained oliguric. According to the form, no volume expansion was administered due to the fact that [person] had heart failure. This morning, [person] developed significant dyspnea, desaturation, and decreased level of consciousness, requiring orotracheal intubation. Upon admission, [person] was in poor general condition, on mechanical ventilation, orotracheal intubation under continuous sedation on an agitation sedation scale of 5, receiving an infusion of norepinephrine at 10 ml/h with a blood pressure of 100/70, 120 beats per minute, and was then taken to bed. Summary of evolution: [person] with grade 3 obesity, difficult-to-control hypertension, [person], with HFpEF, referred due to septic shock with a cutaneous focus who had been taking ceftriaxone, with the presence of a significant abscess in the left upper limb. Intubated at the origin due to dyspnea, desaturation and decreased level of consciousness. [person] admitted, volume expansion performed, antibiotic therapy escalated to tazocin and vancomycin, in addition to initiation of hydrocortisone thinking of refractory septic shock. Three doses of vancomycin administered so far. During hospitalization, [person] was seen with thick, yellowish tracheal secretion in large quantities, however, [person] was unable to perform a tomography due to hemodynamic instability. [person] awaits results of blood and urine cultures. No culture of tracheal aspirate in the system. Furthermore, [person] admitted with acute kidney injury kdigo 3, with metabolic acidosis and hyperkalemia, started renal replacement therapy on 25/02, with DAB and DHE in resolved. She continues to be monitored jointly by nephrology. Medical history: type 2 diabetes mellitus on insulin therapy for 13 years. Hard-to-control arterial hypertension since age 30; HFpEF; left ventricular ejection fraction 65%; renal lithiasis for 30 years. hepatic steatosis obesity grade iii medications for home use: enalapril 20 mg 12/12h amlodipine 10 mg propranolol 80 mg 12/12h acetylsalicylic acid 100 mg clonidine 150mg 12/12h furosemide 1 1 nph insulin 40 40 sertraline 50 mg diazepam 10 mg suspended omeprazole antibiotic therapy: vancomycin d1 02/24/21 dose adjusted for renal function day 02/25 tazocin d0 02/24/21 dose changed for renal function day 02/25 cultures blood culture 02/24: in progress urine culture 02/24: in progress devices: cdl left internal jugular vein 02/24 ultrasound-guided shilley catheter right internal jugular vein 02/25 indwelling urinary catheter 23/02 daily evolution semi-intensive care unit I receive a shift with [person] in very serious general condition, sedated and analgesic with midazolam and fentanyl, in neuromuscular blockade with cisatracurium, hemodynamically unstable despite a moderate dose of vasoactive drug, tachycardia with atrial fibrillation rhythm, presenting ventricular extrasystoles on cardioscopic monitoring, on mechanical ventilation with a 7.5 orotracheal tube, well attached, presenting very high peak and plateau pressure, with hypoxemia refractory to the measures. [person] required orotracheal tube replacement in an attempt to improve ventilation given the small tube for [person]. tube replacement was performed with an exchanger for a 8.5 tube, presenting slight ventilatory improvement, maintaining a low paraf ratio. after tube replacement, [person] presented an episode of tachycardia with widened QRS, with a drop in blood pressure and progressive shock despite an increase in vasoactive drug. {omitted}. [person] evolved with death{omitted}, found with mydriatic and fixed pupils, absence of cough reflex, oculocephalic or corneal-palpebral, without pulse. {omitted} without pulse, mydriatic and fixed pupils, without cough reflex, without oculocephalic or corneal-palpebral reflex. without surgeries performed. discharged dead. 19 history: systemic arterial hypertension, hypothyroidism? chronic liver disease, active significant alcoholism, active smoking 110 pack-years, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, {omitted}. heart failure with reduced ejection fraction stage c chagas serology: negative dilated cardiomyopathy, probable alcoholic etiology, pericardial effusion to be clarified since 2015, mentions brother who died 1 year ago, at age 45, with diagnosis of pulmonary tuberculosis, previous discussion with probable etiology decompensated heart failure, maintaining effusion in the last tomography 2017, chronic kidney disease started hemodialysis in 2019 during hospitalization {organization}. no indication for dialysis during hospitalization in the ward. stopped using all medications 7 months ago. admission: 2021-03-20, discharge: 2021-04-22, stay: 01 m 01 d 20 h, location: ward, payer: sus. patient with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction due to hypertensive dilated cardiomyopathy? alcoholic [person], chronic alcoholic liver disease [person], with acute kidney injury on hemodialysis. {omitted}, on 19/03, transferred from the cardiomyopathy outpatient clinic after hemodialysis in the afternoon due to a report of hypotension 70x40 mmhg, mental confusion and worsening of the general condition. admitted in poor general condition, warm extremities, but capillary refill time of 5 seconds, spontaneous ventilation in room air, with drainage of 5 l after passage of a relief urinary catheter, without auscultation suggestive of pulmonary congestion ica profile l?. due to leukocytosis, elevated C-reactive protein, worsening of perfusion, skin lesion in sacral ulcer with foul-smelling secretion, it was discussed with [organization] and recommended antibiotic treatment with polymyxin, meropenem and vancomycin dated 03/19. [person] evolved in regular general condition, spontaneous ventilation in room air, drowsy, disoriented in time, oriented in space, bradypsychic and non-communicative, responds to commands, verbalizes with laconic speech. hemodynamically unstable using dobutamine at 5.2 mcg/kg/min and norepinephrine 0.11 mcg/kg/min warm extremities with capillary refill time of 4 s. approached on 03/23 by the general surgery team: debridement of sacral ulcer, performed soft tissue ultrasound without collections. administered 750 ml with good tolerability. late afternoon, evolved with slight worsening of pulmonary congestion, limiting volume resuscitation. Hypokalemia: secondary to recovery from obstructive renal injury? Requested for control. Scheduled furosemide was discontinued. Due to a CRL of 10, it was decided not to replace it. Requested transfer to [organization] due to the need for monitoring and intensive care. [person] admitted to [organization] from the coronary care unit on 03/23, used norepinephrine and dobutamine. [person] evolved hemodynamically stable, discontinued dobutamine on 04/01. Using hydralazine 50 mg 8/8h and isosorbide currently at a dose of 20 mg 8/8h. Last increase less than 48 h ago. [person] with a deep sacral ulcer not requiring new surgical debridement, with secretion and fibrin. The nurse advised a collagenase dressing twice a day. [person] oriented in space and person, but disoriented in time, very eager to be discharged and not very cooperative. Reports little appetite, greater acceptance of fruits. Denies dyspnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, orthopnea. No complaints of edema. Denies pain. Presents diuresis present in indwelling urinary catheter, concentrated yellow urine. Progress in the ward [person] evolves stable, in regular general condition, breathing in room air, without use of vasoactive drugs, with improvement in creatinine 04/04: 1.91 and cr 12/04: 1.68 without need for dialysis, without hypotension, without fever, without leukocytosis without use of antibiotic therapy for cutaneous focus, compensated for the cardiovascular part that motivated hospitalization. Increased carvedilol to 12.5 mg 12/12h 2 times a day. transitioned to oral route on 04/05, continues to be treated for the ulcer present in the left gluteus region, assessed by dermatology 04/04: new case scheduled at dtc on 08/01/2021, excision of lesion on the back of the right hand scheduled for 13/08/2021. In the new case at dtc, apply tcle and guidelines regarding the procedure and need for a companion, advised to request plastic surgery evaluation for grade iv sacral ulcer, assessed by plastic surgery 04/08: request pi for general surgery to evaluate wound debridement, refer to cer for ventral decubitus protocol, after, outpatient referral to cpl. assessed by [organization] 04/10: [person] has no indication for surgical debridement, maintain chemical debridement, maintain change of decubitus On 04/09, diarrhea began, 4 episodes. Recent use of 3 antibiotics during hospitalization, thought to be pseudomembranous colitis: metronidazole 400 mg orally 8/8h was started, and toxins A and B were requested in the stool, awaiting results. Since then, the medication has been avoided, waiting for improvement in the diarrheal condition for this purpose. Antibiotic therapy was given for 7 days, with resolution of the diarrheal condition. On 4/21, an indwelling urinary catheter was removed in the morning. At night, [person] developed acute urinary retention and bladder mass at the level of the umbilical scar, requiring a relief bladder catheter, but without symptoms of urinary tract infection at the time. The indwelling urinary catheter was replaced and an opinion from [organization] was requested. [organization] evaluated [person] on 4/22, a priori due to the reserved prognosis of heart failure, with no indication for investigation of prostate cancer. Medical discharge with an indwelling urinary catheter and a follow-up appointment visit to be scheduled for [organization]. General discharge physical examination: regular general condition, conscious, somewhat disoriented, pale /4, hydrated, acyanotic, afebrile to the touch, anicteric, conscious, oriented, pale /4, hydrated, acyanotic, afebrile respiratory auscultation globally reduced vesicular breath sounds, mainly in the bases, without adventitious sounds, without respiratory distress cardiac auscultation: hypophonetic heart sounds 2t, no murmurs. no tj blood pressure: 110x70 mmhg heart rate 64 beats per minute abdomen: protuberant, painful to superficial palpation in the lower abdomen. bladder 1 cm below the umbilical scar. bowel sounds present. extremities: warm, well perfused. no edema, capillary refill time less than 3 s, free calves, grade iv sacral ulcer in the left gluteus region, granulated, with edge detachment, hypersecretive, with improvement in exudate compared to the previous day. electrocardiogram 2016: bradycardic rs, grade 1 bav, anterior wall failure progression, diffuse arv. transthoracic echocardiography {organization} 2016: marked dilation of the right chambers and ae, moderate to marked dilation of the left ventricle, left ventricular ejection fraction 32%, marked depressed rvd, ms: diffuse hypoc, mild dilation of the aortic root, ascending aorta, aortic arch and prothrombin time, mild imi, mild mod iao, marked it, psap 40, moderate pericardial effusion, no signs of tamponade. Transthoracic echocardiography 2016: marked dilatation of the right chambers and atria / moderate to marked dilatation of the left ventricle / left ventricular ejection fraction 32% / markedly depressed RVSD / MS: diffuse hypoc / mild dilatation of the aortic root, ascending aorta, aortic arch and prothrombin time / mild imi / mild ao/ marked it / psap 40 / moderate pericardial effusion, without signs of tamponade. Alcoholic the patient, chronic alcoholic liver disease the patient, with acute kidney injury on kidney dialysis (hemodialysis). , on 19/03, transferred from the cardiomyopathy OPD (Out Patient Department) after kidney dialysis (hemodialysis) in the afternoon due to a report of low BP (hypotension) 70x40 mmhg, mental confusion and worsening of the general condition. Admitted in poor general condition, warm extremities, but capillary refill time of 5 seconds, spontaneous ventilation in room air, with drainage of 5 l after passage of a relief urinary catheter, without auscultation suggestive of pulmonary congestion ica profile l?. Due to high white blood cell count (leukocytosis), elevated c-reactive protein, worsening of perfusion, skin lesion in sacral ulcer with foul-smelling secretion, it was discussed with [organization] and recommended infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment with polymyxin, meropenem and vancomycin dated 03/19. The patient evolved in regular general condition, spontaneous ventilation in room air, drowsy, disoriented in time, oriented in space, bradypsychic and non-communicative, responds to commands, verbalizes with laconic speech. Hemodynamically unstable using dobutamine at 5.2 mcg/kg/min and norepinephrine 0.11 mcg/kg/min warm extremities with capillary refill time of 4 s. Approached on 03/23 by the general surgery team: surgical wound cleaning (debridement) of sacral ulcer, performed soft tissue ultrasound without collections. Give (administer)ed 750 ml with good tolerability. Late afternoon, evolved with slight worsening of pulmonary congestion, limiting volume regovernment health scheme (sus)citation. Hypokalemia: secondary to recovery from obstructive renal injury? Requested for control. Scheduled furosemide was discontinued. Due to a crl of 10, it was decided not to replace it. Requested transfer to [organization] due to the need for monitoring and intensive care. The patient admitted to [organization] from the coronary care unit on 03/23, used norepinephrine and dobutamine. The patient evolved stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), discontinued dobutamine on 04/01. Using hydralazine 50 mg 8/8h and isosorbide currently at a dose of 20 mg 8/8h. Last increase less than 48 h ago. The patient with a deep sacral ulcer not requiring new surgical surgical wound cleaning (debridement), with secretion and fibrin. The nurse advised a collagenase dressing twice a day. The patient oriented in space and person, but disoriented in time, very eager to be sent home from hospital (discharge)d and not very cooperative. Reports little appetite, greater acceptance of fruits. Denies difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), paroxysmal nocturnal difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), orthopnea. No complaints of swelling (edema). Denies pain. Presents diuresis present in indwelling urinary catheter, concentrated yellow urine. Progress in the general hospital room (ward) the patient evolves stable, in regular general condition, breathing in room air, without use of vasoactive drugs, with improvement in kidney function marker (creatinine) 04/04: 1.91 and cr 12/04: 1.68 without need for dialysis, without low BP (hypotension), without fever, without high white blood cell count (leukocytosis) without use of infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) for cutaneous focus, compensated for the cardiovascular part that motivated hospitalization. Increased carvedilol to 12.5 mg 12/12h 2 times a day. Transitioned to oral route on 04/05, continues to be treated for the ulcer present in the left gluteus region, assessed by dermatology 04/04: new case scheduled at dtc on 08/01/2021, excision of lesion on the back of the right hand scheduled for 13/08/2021. In the new case at dtc, apply tcle and guidelines regarding the procedure and need for a companion, advised to request plastic surgery evaluation for grade iv sacral ulcer, assessed by plastic surgery 04/08: request pi for general surgery to evaluate wound surgical wound cleaning (debridement), refer to cer for ventral decubitus protocol, after, outpatient referral to cpl. Assessed by [organization] 04/10: the patient systemic arterial high BP no indication for surgical surgical wound cleaning (debridement), maintain chemical surgical wound cleaning (debridement), maintain change of decubitus on 04/09, diarrhea began, 4 episodes. Recent use of 3 infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic)s during hospitalization, thought to be pseudomembranous colitis: metronidazole 400 mg by mouth (orally) 8/8h was started, and toxins a and b were requested in the stool, awaiting results. Since then, the medication systemic arterial high BP (hypertension) been avoided, waiting for improvement in the diarrheal condition for this purpose. Infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) was given for 7 days, with resolution of the diarrheal condition. On 4/21, an indwelling urinary catheter was removed in the morning. At night, the patient developed acute urinary retention and bladder mass at the level of the umbilical scar, requiring a relief bladder catheter, but without symptoms of urine infection (UTI) at the time. The indwelling urinary catheter was replaced and an opinion from [organization] was requested. [organization] evaluated the patient on 4/22, a priori due to the reserved expected outcome (prognosis) of heart failure, with no indication for investigation of prostate cancer. Medical sent home from hospital (discharge) with an indwelling urinary catheter and a return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment visit to be scheduled for [organization]. General sent home from hospital (discharge) physical examination: regular general condition, conscious, somewhat disoriented, pale /4, hydrated, no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no fever (afebrile) to the touch, no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), conscious, oriented, pale /4, hydrated, no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no fever (afebrile) respiratory auscultation globally reduced normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds), mainly in the bases, without adventitious sounds, without respiratory distress cardiac auscultation: hypophonetic heart sounds 2t, no murmurs. No tj blood pressure: 110x70 mmhg heart rate 64 beats per minute abdomen: protuberant, painful to superficial palpation in the lower abdomen. Bladder 1 cm below the umbilical scar. Bowel sounds present. Extremities: warm, well perfused. No swelling (edema), capillary refill time less than 3 s, free calves, grade iv sacral ulcer in the left gluteus region, granulated, with edge detachment, hypersecretive, with improvement in exudate compared to the previous day. Moderate pericardial effusion, circumferential, predominantly in the inferolateral region of the left ventricle, with anechoic content, without signs of increased intrapericardial pressure. The study suggests that the patient needs to be careful about the symptoms of ulcer. It's important to see if the patient is able to get the best care from the hospital, especially after the patient's first visit to the hospital. They also need to make sure that the hospital is ready to provide the best treatment for the patient. They don't have any problems with the patient, but they need to be prepared for the treatment. This is why we need to focus on the need for more careful care for the patients to be able to make it easier for them to get their patients to take care of the patient to visit the hospital to help them. 20 [person], 52 years old. admission: 02/08/2022, discharge: 09/09/2022, stay: 01 m 07 d 07 h, location: ward, payer: sus. patient admitted for treatment of neurocryptococcosis. good general condition, pale /4, acyanotic, anicteric. no focal deficits. [person], 52 years old, born in {city}, coming from [address] current problem 1 human immunodeficiency virus, neurocryptococcosis under treatment, patient was in a day hospital to receive intravenous medication amphotericin b, lipid complex for treatment of neurocryptococcosis. occurred with seizure, unresponsiveness and hypertonia of the limbs, with cephalic version to the left, lasting between 1 to 2 minutes, being hospitalized for reinvestigation. computed tomography of the skull 06/07: no changes cerebrospinal fluid 06/07: admission pressure 26 gb 15 lm 14 pm 1 glyc 74 biochemical parameters and cellularity improving, in relation to the last fluid 24/06. previous: previous hospitalization to start treatment on 04/25 06/04 ART resumed on 06/03 5 weeks after starting treatment for neurocryptococcosis he presented recurrence of symptoms, being admitted again on 06/16: unprecedented seizure, headache, vomiting, computed tomography of the skull 06/17: no changes in cerebrospinal fluid 06/16: admission pressure 40 cm/h20 / 40 cells / glucose 40 / prot 64 started on dexamethasone 4 mg 6/6h as directed by neurology due to the diagnostic hypothesis of immune reconstitution syndrome. ruled out due to small temporal relationship only 12 days restarted ART previous treatment regimen: amphotericin B 04/27 06/04 used lipid complex, deoxycholate and liposomal. Cumulative dose from 04/25 to 06/05: 4.6 g. Fluconazole 400 mg 12/12h 04/27 06/23 suspended due to hepatotoxicity, zero sensitivity test repeated and confirmed. Amphotericin B restarted on 06/26, lipid complex when fluconazole was suspended. due to recrudescence of symptoms. 2 human immunodeficiency virus since 1998 on salvage ART, poor previous adherence CV 07/05: 64711 CD4 07/05: 18 genotyping 13/10: complete resistance to: 3tc, ftc, idv, rtv, nfv intermediate resistance to: azt, d4t, ddi, abc, sqv, apv, lpv, atv low resistance to: tdf genotyping 22/07: itrn-associated mutations: 41l/ 184v/ 214f/ 215y itrn-associated mutations: 181c ip-associated mutations: 10f/v / 15v/ 24i/ 33f/ 36l/ 46l/ 53l/ 54v/ 58e/ 62v / 63p/ 71v/ 74w/ 82a sensitive: azt 3tc / tdf / tdf 3tc sensitive: drv/r of ip only this one, 3 genital herpes onset of symptoms on 06/27. acyclovir 400 mg 8/8h 06/27 07/07. complete improvement of lesions. history: habits: alcoholism on weekends comorbidities: human immunodeficiency virus, systemic arterial hypertension, mild hepatic steatosis, surgeries: appendectomy, knee fracture vaccinations: ppd: 2009, 2010, and 2012. dt: 1998, 2009 pneumo 23: 1999, 2001, and 2009 flu: 2004, 2007, 2008, 2009, 2010, 2012, and 2020 took the yellow fever vaccine in {city} in 2017 without medical clearance. in this hospitalization: 1. pneumonia due to staphylococcus aureus, staphylococcus under treatment, blood culture isolated staphylococcus aureus oxas 08/05/2022. use of meropenem and vancomycin suspended on 09/04/2022. new negative cultures. 2. neurocryptococcosis patient with previous antifungal test with fluconazole-resistant cryptococci. liposomal amphotericin B scheduled during hospitalization and outpatient care via day hospital. no indication for ventriculoperitoneal shunt by [organization]. re-discuss upon discharge about performing the procedure via outpatient care. fluconazole sensitivity test repeated and confirmed zero. [person] is discharged from the ward, with a schedule of liposomal amphotericin B, 150 mg, 2x per week. 3. human immunodeficiency virus, using antiretroviral therapy during hospitalization with doses adjusted for renal function. administer tenofovir every 3 days, correction for renal function 4. cytomegalovirus infection ruled out, cytomegalovirus pcr 07/16: positive, 11200 copies, log 4 i pcrcmv 07/08: negative [person] without strong evidence of cytomegalovirus disease, in view of other findings suggestive of other infections, it was decided to discontinue therapy with ganciclovir. 5. acute kidney injury kdigo iii secondary to vancomycin in renal replacement therapy. last hemodialysis 08/28/2022. adjust drugs to clcr below 10. maintain hemoglobin 7, mean blood pressure 65, euglycemia and euvolemia. nephrology monitoring together 6. regarding the hematological picture hematology evaluation: [person] with bicytopenia normo/normo thrombocytopenia anemia of probable multifactorial origin, infectious picture, poor control of the human immunodeficiency virus, folic acid deficiency. [person] is responding well to the use of GCSF. I calculate the iron saturation index: 9%, suggesting signs of iron deficiency. I await ferritin dosage. If iron deficiency is confirmed, wait for control of the infectious condition to begin replacement. 7. Regarding the gout, [person] with contraindications to the use of NSAIDs, colchicine, due to changes in renal function, we maintain scheduled analgesia with tramadol 50 mg 8x8 hours, with improvement in the condition. 8. Regarding possible drug eruptions: liposomal amphotericin B does not usually cause drug eruptions. However, up to 25% of [person] may present skin conditions with the infusion of the medication. Therefore, we perform premedication before the infusion of amphotericin with hydrocortisone, antihistamine and paracetamol. I maintain topical hydrocortisone for the site of hives. I maintain the scheduled antihistamine. We are maintaining amphotericin B for now, since [person] has not shown any signs of anaphylaxis. We will observe during the period. On a day hospital basis, maintain premedications before the infusion of amphotericin B. As [person] is showing clinical improvement, with no growth of microorganisms in cultures, afebrile for 48 hours and hemodynamically stable, we have decided to discharge him from this service and continue the treatment of neurocryptococcosis on a day hospital basis. Therefore, [person] will have weekly follow-up appointments twice a week, for administration of liposomal amphotericin B 150 mg. Amphotericin B 150 mg twice a week. Tarv. amphotericin B 150 mg twice a week. The patient, 52 years old. Regarding possible drug eruptions: liposomal amphotericin b does not usually cause drug eruptions. However, up to 25% of the patient may present skin conditions with the infusion of the medication. 21 admission: 2020-06-17, discharge: 2020-07-10, stay: 23 d 01 h, location: ward, payer: sus. [person] 54 years old, no previous comorbidities, admitted to the emergency unit on 2020-06-17 with a report of cough, dyspnea, and myalgia for 5 days after admission. upon arrival at the u.s., [person] wearing a reservoir mask, saturating 80% with a respiratory rate of 36 breaths per minute. orotracheal intubation was performed on arrival, the procedure was uneventful. even during orotracheal intubation, given the difficulty in maintaining adequate saturation, it was decided to start atracurium. upon admission, prophylactic ceftriaxone, clarithromycin, dexamethasone, and enoxaparin were also started. Central venous catheter inserted into the right internal jugular vein on 6/18, without complications. Transferred to a bed in a coronavirus intensive care unit on the evening of 6/18 after a positive PCR result for coronavirus. Developed acute kidney injury, renal replacement therapy was started on 6/19. On 6/22, NB was suspended and infectious screening was requested due to worsening leukocyte count. Developed improved ventilatory parameters and was extubated on 6/24. However, [person] was reintubated on 7/25 due to decreased level of consciousness, tachypnea, and use of accessory muscles. [person] developed fever on 6/26, with no defined focus, empirical meropenem and vancomycin were started and cultures were requested. Antibiotic therapy was suspended on 6/30, seeing growth of coagulase-negative staphylococcus in Shilley catheter culture. New extubation was performed on 7/4, successfully. Last hemodialysis on 07/05. Given clinical stability, [person] transferred to [organization] on 07/06. Upon admission to the ward, given episodes of fever, requested removal of double lumen catheter and Shilley catheter, sent for culture. On 07/09, seen catheter cultures with growth of staphylococci, decided to start vancomycin. On 07/10, seen definitive culture result with coagulase-negative Staphylococcus Oxar and [person] with previous renal dysfunction, discussed with ICC and transitioned from vancomycin to linezolid 600 mg every 12 hours. [person] maintained clinical stability, conscious, accepting oral diet. Needs to remain hospitalized for treatment of catheter-related infection, expected treatment for 7 days and performance of motor physiotherapy. regular general condition, anicteric, acyanotic, pale 1/4, hydrated pas 130x80 heart rate 106 saturation 95% room air respiratory rate 16 breaths per minute, cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm, normal S1 and S2, normal sound sounds, no murmurs. capillary refill time 3 seconds, palpable peripheral pulses. pulmonary auscultation: universally reduced vesicular breath soundss, no adventitious sounds. no signs of respiratory distress in room air. globular, tympanic abdomen, bowel sounds, painless on palpation edematous upper limbs 2/4. edematous lower limbs 2/4. calves without signs of swelling neurological: glasgow coma scale 14 ao4/rv4/rm6, confused. obeys commands. moves 4 limbs when asked. presence of extensive sacral lesion, with no signs of infection. requesting a place in {organization}, place provided by [person], patient outside the isolation period for coronavirus: patient with more than 14 days of onset of symptoms and more than 14 days of positive PCR, improvement in respiratory condition, currently breathing room air. had a fever less than 72 hours ago, but due to a catheter-related infection. already has a negative PCR for coronavirus from 07/01. maintain linezolid 600 mg every 12 hours for 7 days treatment of catheter-related infection by coagulase-negative staphylococcus. motor physiotherapy. [person] maintaining clinical stability, conscious, accepting oral diet. needs to remain hospitalized for treatment of catheter-related infection for 7 days and performance of motor physiotherapy. Vital signs: oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation) 95% on room air; respiratory rate 20; heart rate 85 beats per minute; general condition: good, lucid, oriented in time and space, breathing normally (eupneic), no fever (afebrile), severe malnutrition, pale 1/4, hypohydrated 1/4. Oroscopy with poor dentition. No signs of candidiasis. Respiratory auscultation: bilateral normal breathing sounds, with wheezing, transmission rhonchi and crackles diffusely in the projection of the thorax. No signs of respiratory distress, but with tachypnea. Cardiac auscultation: normal heart sounds, regular rhythm 2 times, no murmurs. No tj. Abdomen: bowel sounds. Slightly distended abdomen, painless on palpation. Presence of pain on palpation of the left flank, reproducible on palpation of the lower rib. Extremities: no swelling (edema). No signs of deep vein blood clot (thrombosis). Capillary refill time less than 3 seconds. hemodialysis and referral to the emergency care unit on 01/23: considering the possibility of bloodstream infection, expanded coverage ceftazidime 2 g 8/8h vancomycin 1 g 12/12h, ultrasound right upper limb 01/23: evaluation of patent radiocephalic AVF, with no signs of complications. soft tissue edema in the distal forearm on 01/25: due to rash, we suspended non-essential medications at the time azathioprine, chloroquine, fluoxetine and changed vancomycin to teicoplanin on 01/28: teicoplanin was suspended after resolution of rash, [person] no signs of cellulitis, 2 catheter-associated fungal bloodstream infection on 01/24: blood culture and catheter blood with growth of yeast-like fungi, start of micafungin and permicath removal, blood culture and catheter tip culture 01/24: candida parapsilosis, blood culture 01/26: negative so far, 4 infective endocarditis: tests to investigate endocarditis: abdominal ultrasound 01/26: signs of bilateral chronic nephropathy. simple renal cyst on the right. fundus 01/25: clear vitreous, no signs of vitritis or snow balls or vasculitis, not stained, well defined, macula without lesions, with shine, retina applied 360 transthoracic echocardiography 01/28: mild left ventricular dilation, mild diffuse left ventricular hypokinesia, mild left ventricular systolic dysfunction ef2d45%. normal right ventricular function. mild mitral, tricuspid and pulmonary valve insufficiency. mild pericardial effusion. transthoracic echocardiogram 02/04: mild left ventricular dilation, eccentric left ventricular hypertrophy, mild depressed left ventricular systolic performance, normal right ventricular systolic performance, mild mitral insufficiency secondary to tricuspid insufficiency. hyperrefringent image on the atrial face of the anterior mitral leaflet, which may correspond to infectious vegetation or Libman-Sacks endocarditis. Sterile vegetation in a patient with systemic lupus erythematosus? transthoracic echocardiography TE 02/18: no evidence of vegetation. 4. Acute generalized exanthematous pustulosis. 24 [person], 88 years old, proc. from {city}. admission: 09/24/2020, discharge: 10/21/2020, stay: 27 days 03 hours, location: ward, payer: SUS. 1 sepsis urinary focus, pulmonary ceftazidime avibactam d0: 10/14 10/21 blood culture 10/14: klebsiella pneumonia in 1 sample, 2 recurrent urinary tract infection meropenem 09/23 10/03 urine culture 09/16/20: klebsiella pneumoniae kpp, citrobacter koseri esbl, urine culture 09/25: klebsiella pneumoniae, 3 anemia to be clarified, upper digestive endoscopy 07/31/20: mild pangastritis colon 10/10/20: diverticulosis of the colon iron deficiency will start replacement 1000 mg in 10 hemodialysis sessions, awaiting discussion on capsule endoscopy 4 decompensated heart failure. History of current illness: Patient with metastatic prostate cancer, undergoing palliative treatment for several years, with stable disease. Has multiple recent hospitalizations for decompensated heart failure or infectious conditions. Was admitted by [organization] from 04 to 13/09/20 for decompensated heart failure and urinary tract infection. Attendance reported a decline in general condition for 1 week, with occasional episodes of fainting. Also reports asthenia and dyspnea on moderate exertion. Reports nausea yesterday, without vomiting. Denies infectious respiratory symptoms, chest pain, palpitations, syncope, or bleeding. Reports cloudy, lumpy urine with an unpleasant odor for 1 week. Reports urine drainage through a neobladder fistula. During hospitalization, 1 red blood cell concentrate was administered due to a decline in general condition and hemoglobin 6.5. Hemoglobin after transfusion of 7.5. He underwent hemodialysis sessions due to pulmonary congestion. He was transferred to nephrology on 09/27, where he finished antibiotic therapy with meropenem until 10/03 and was suspended due to clinical improvement despite carbapenem-resistant Klebsiella. During hospitalization, he underwent a colonoscopy to investigate anemia, having already undergone a previous endoscopy without alterations, which resulted in diverticula, with no signs of bleeding. On 10/14, he developed septic symptoms, and was started on torgena to cover the pulmonary and urinary focus, evolving with a drop in CRP and improvement in the blood count. [person] undergoing hemodialysis with a forecast of maintaining it 2 days a week due to stable uremic toxins. Good general condition, pale 2/4, anicteric, hydrated, conscious and lucid. Respiratory auscultation: reduced vesicular breath sounds in the left base, eupneic on room air. oxygen saturation 98% without respiratory effort, cardiac auscultation: irregular heart rhythm, hypophonetic heart sounds, presence of systolic murmur 2/6 in aortic and tricuspid focus and systolic murmur 1/6 in mitral focus, without irradiation. heart rate 67 beats per minute, good peripheral capillary perfusion. abdomen: bowel sounds, globular, flaccid, painless to palpation, hernia in the right iliac fossa, without phlogistic signs. ostomy without phlogistic signs. scar at the site of a fistula on the right flank, without secretion output and without local phlogistic signs. presence of voluminous inguinal hernia, urine collected in a bag, clear and with lumps. lower limbs: pulse present, free calves, without edema. good general condition, pale 2/4, anicteric, hydrated, conscious and lucid. Hospitalization tests: transthoracic echocardiography 09/06: ejection fraction 63%, moderate severe mitral regurgitation, biological aortic prosthesis TAVI without signs of dysfunction, moderate marked tricuspid regurgitation, moderate atrial dilatation, mild right ventricular dilation, mild to moderate pulmonary hypertension, well-positioned pacemaker, transthoracic echocardiography, upper digestive endoscopy 07/31/20: mild pangastritis, colon 10/10/20: colonic diverticulosis. Hospital discharge prescription: levothyroxine 75 mcg 1xd furosemide 40 mg 110 amiodarone 200 mg apixaban 2.5 mg every 12 hours omeprazole 20 mg/day alfapoietin 1 ampoule 3x/week timolol eye drops 1 drop. follow-up: 10/24 acute care hemodialysis 12/01 ophthalmology 25/01 [organization] 29/03 cardiology angioplasty. upon follow-up appointment: check hb, ht, check clinical signs intravenous iron replacement I will discuss with [organization] team. no complaints. Regarding multiple previous infectious episodes: 3.1 At the moment, since [person] has no symptoms suggestive of new infections, he remains without a prescription for antibiotics. 3.2 Infectious surveillance. 4. 26 id: [person], 59 years old, from {omitted}. weight: 70 kg clinical problem: 1 severe acute respiratory syndrome, coronavirus 19 resolved onset of symptoms: 25/02. worsening of symptoms: 06/03. orotracheal intubation, mechanical ventilation: 11/03. tracheostomy: 01/04. {omitted} 22/04 2 single-episode circumstantial seizure in the context of infectious-metabolic decompensation on 03/04 resolved electroencephalogram 05/04: marked disorganization of baseline activity, characterized by a diffuse burst-attenuation pattern, with bursts dominated by rhythms in the alpha/theta range, in addition to an increase in the content of slow delta waves and absence of physiological graphoelements of sleep. b isolated, occasionally periodic, high-amplitude slow delta wave bursts with diffuse projection and accentuation in the central, parietal region of the midline cz/pz, frequently preceded by bisynchronous epileptiform paroxysms with occipitoparietal projection and low amplitude, occurring in moderate prevalence in the tracing. conclusion and electroclinical correlation: the findings are compatible with the effect of central nervous system depressant medication, possibly associated with diffuse encephalopathy, and with generalized or focal epileptogenesis with secondary bisynchrony. electroencephalogram 08/04: the electroencephalogram was abnormal. the findings are compatible with the effect of sedative medication, possibly associated with diffuse encephalopathy. computed tomography of the head 07/04: normal nuclear magnetic resonance of the brain 12/05: non-localizing for epilepsy. awaiting official report neurology assessment 05/18: considering serum phenytoin level 1.1 in the absence of hypoalbuminemia in a patient with a single concurrent seizure in the context of infectious and metabolic decompensation, we recommend suspension of anticonvulsant without the need for weaning. at the time of hospital discharge, assess referral to secondary neurology for longitudinal follow-up. 3 acute kidney injury kdigo iii in improvement etiology: nephrotoxic sepsis. renal replacement therapy started on 04/21. 4 eosinophilia resolved suspected disseminated strongyloidiasis. {omitted}. 5 severe polyneuropathy of the patient improving 6 sacral pressure injury grade iii daily dressing with 6% papain at the edges surgical debridement performed on 05/27 presence of a focus of necrosis without complications 6 infectious focus of probable cutaneous etiology resolved suspected bacteremia during hemodialysis on 03/20. 17 h, location: ward, payer: sus. vital signs: blood pressure 138/94 mmhg, heart rate 120 beats per minute, respiratory rate 19 inspirations per minute, oxygen saturation 98% regular general condition, pale 1/4, hydrated, afebrile, anicteric, acyanotic respiratory auscultation: reduced murmur in the right base, fine crackles in the right base, no signs of respiratory distress cardiac auscultation: 2 normophonetic rhythmic sounds without murmurs, full and symmetrical pulses, capillary refill time less than 3 seconds abdomen: flat abdomen, normotensive, bowel sounds, painful on deep palpation in the hypogastrium region. Absence of sudden pain upon decompression, palpable masses or visceromegaly. Extremities: Lower limbs without edema or signs of deep vein thrombosis. Ulcer of approximately 2 cm on the right lateral malleolus, without signs of infection. Skin: Sacral lesion with occlusive dressing. Scaly lesions on the feet bilaterally. Schedule outpatient follow-up with the [organization] team for 07/19. I will contact [organization] regarding follow-up appointments after discharge. I am advised by resident [person] to request that [person] come to the acute care room on 06/02 to collect laboratory tests to assess the need for a new hemodialysis cycle. Please, when follow-up appointmenting to [organization], check the requested tests for iron kinetics, vitamins, folic acid, and vitamin B12, since they were requested for 14 days after blood transfusion (01 red blood cell concentrate). I issue medical prescriptions for home use: methadone 5 mg orally every 12 hours gabapentin 300 mg orally daily amitriptyline milk of magnesia 10 ml orally at night clonidine 75 mcg orally every 8 hours dipyrone 1 gram orally up to every 6 hours in case of pain refractory to methadone I ask the nursing team for guidance to [person] regarding pressure injury in the sacral region daily care, dressing I issue a medical report requesting equipment to assist in the daily care of [person], such as a wheelchair and shower chair I advise [person] regarding warning signs that [person] may present worsening of significant pain symptoms, fever, prostration and the need to seek immediate emergency care if these are present, bringing a medical report delivered at the time of hospital discharge. B isolated, occasionally periodic, high-amplitude slow delta wave bursts with diffuse projection and accentuation in the central, parietal region of the midline cz/pz, frequently preceded by bisynchronous epileptiform paroxysms with occipitoparietal projection and low amplitude, occurring in moderate prevalence in the tracing. Conclusion and electroclinical correlation: the findings are compatible with the effect of central nervous system depressant medication, possibly associated with diffuse brain dysfunction (encephalopathy), and with generalized or focal epileptogenesis with secondary bisynchrony. Electroencephalogram 08/04: the electroencephalogram was abnormal. The findings are compatible with the effect of sedative medication, possibly associated with diffuse brain dysfunction (encephalopathy). Breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy): 01/04. 22/04 2 single-episode circumstantial seizure in the context of infectious-metabolic decompensation on 03/04 resolved electroencephalogram 05/04: marked disorganization of baseline activity, characterized by a diffuse burst-attenuation pattern, with bursts dominated by rhythms in the alpha/theta range, in addition to an increase in the content of slow delta waves and absence of physiological graphoelements of sleep. B isolated, occasionally periodic, high-amplitude slow delta wave bursts with diffuse projection and accentuation in the central, parietal region of the midline cz/pz, frequently preceded by bisynchronous epileptiform paroxysms with occipitoparietal projection and low amplitude, occurring in moderate prevalence in the tracing. Conclusion and electroclinical correlation: the findings are compatible with the effect of central nervous system depressant medication, possibly associated with diffuse brain dysfunction (encephalopathy), and with generalized or focal epileptogenesis with secondary bisynchrony. Electroencephalogram 08/04: the electroencephalogram was abnormal. The findings are compatible with the effect of sedative medication, possibly associated with diffuse brain dysfunction (encephalopathy). CT scan (computed tomography) of the head 07/04: normal nuclear MRI scan (magnetic resonance) of the brain 12/05: non-localizing for seizure disorder (epilepsy). Vital signs: blood pressure 138/94 mmhg, heart rate 120 beats per minute, respiratory rate 19 inspirations per minute, oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation) 98% regular general condition, pale 1/4, hydrated, no fever (afebrile), no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no bluish discoloration (acyanotic) respiratory auscultation: reduced murmur in the right base, fine crackles in the right base, no signs of respiratory distress cardiac auscultation: 2 normophonetic rhythmic sounds without murmurs, full and symmetrical pulses, capillary refill time less than 3 seconds abdomen: flat abdomen, normotensive, bowel sounds, painful on deep palpation in the hypogastrium region. Absence of sudden pain upon decompression, palpable masses or visceromegaly. Extremities: lower limbs without swelling (edema) or signs of deep vein blood clot (thrombosis). [person] with history of polyuria, polydipsia, and hyperglycemia hba1c 9.2 4 pulmonary infection clinical and laboratory worsening condensation on the right 06/15 escalated to meropenem pleural effusion with empyema. chest drain on the right from 07/23 to 07/30 piperacillin tazobactam 15 days d0 07/23. [person] remained febrile, but new drainage guided by tomography 08/12 did not identify fluid content pleural thickening secondary to previous drainage trauma? 5 deep vein thrombosis of the right upper limb 06/16 with indication for anticoagulation pulmonary thromboembolism embolic burden of 45% without signs of cor pulmonale 06/20 bilateral with right pulmonary infarction 6 fungemia? resolved candida dubliniensis in central venous catheter 06/21. blood culture without growth of microorganisms fo without fungal ball, abdominal ultrasound without alterations, awaiting transthoracic echocardiography micafungin 06/23 07/04 7 liquid diarrhea started on 07/07 resolved pseudomembranous colitis? used metronidazole and vancomycin 8 sacral pressure ulcer grade iv without signs of infection, antibiotics ceftriaxone 10/06 14/06, meropenem 15/06 27/06 meropenem in: 19/07 micafungin 23/06 04/07 metronidazole 09/07 17/07 and vancomycin in: 19/07 suspended on 22/07 amikacin in: 27/06 12/07 tigecycline in: 27/06 12/07 vancomycin in: 27/06 12/07 piperacillin tazobactam d0 23/07 06/08. personal history: major depression 9 years ago, schizophrenia? hypothesis raised by psychiatry at admission, olanzapine and biperiden were prescribed. admission: 2020-06-11, discharge: 2020-08-13, stay: 02 m 02 d 04 h, location: ward, payer: sus. admission history: [person] brought by usa with a history of having sought emergency care 4 times since 2020-06-08 due to nonspecific malaise and anorexia. The first time a urinary tract infection was diagnosed and treatment with levofloxacin was started. She follow-up appointmented to the emergency care 2 more times, and on 1 of them regular insulin was administered for hyperglycemia, hyponatremia was corrected, and a pcr test for coronavirus was collected. She was discharged after that. On 2020-06-10, she follow-up appointmented to the emergency care unit with a decreased level of consciousness, unresponsive, miotic and photoreactive pupils. Orotracheal intubation was performed at [organization]. In the referral, the escalation of antibiotics to ceftriaxone was described yesterday, with the use of NPH to control hyperglycemia and 3000 ml of 0.9% saline solution infusion. [person] was hemodynamically stable upon admission to the emergency unit using vasoactive drugs, sedated on well-coupled mechanical ventilation, and showed signs of poor perfusion on physical examination. Evolution during hospitalization: [person] developed infectious complications: bacterial pneumonia, fungemia, and acute kidney injury (KADIGO 3) requiring dialysis, as well as thrombotic microangiopathy with distal necrosis of the hallux and second toe on the left and the calcaneus on the right. She was evaluated by the [organization], which did not indicate any intervention due to the absence of infection. [person] presented complicated pneumonia with pleural effusion with characteristics of empyema on the right, and was drained by the surgical team and given 14 days of piperacillin-tazobactam. As [person] remained febrile, a new chest tomography was requested, which showed an image suggestive of persistent empyema and a small lung abscess. However, interventional radiology approached him with insertion of a valved pigtail catheter. However, it was found that there was no fluid in the lesion and the image was attributed to pleural thickening secondary to trauma from previous drainage. Schedule: transfer to [organization] for anticoagulation and adjustment of the international normalized ratio due to pulmonary thromboembolism and deep vein thrombosis. We have not yet resumed anticoagulation due to the approach by RINT today, 08/13, blood glucose control, family training in pressure injury care. Admission: 2020-06-11, sent home from hospital (discharge): 2020-08-13, stay: 02 m 02 d 04 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Admission history: the patient brought by usa with a history of having sought emergency care 4 times since 2020-06-08 due to nonspecific malaise and anorexia. The first time a urine infection (UTI) was diagnosed and treatment with levofloxacin was started. She return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointmented to the emergency care 2 more times, and on 1 of them regular insulin was give (administer)ed for very high blood sugar (hyperglycemia), hyponatremia was corrected, and a pcr test for coronavirus was collected. She was sent home from hospital (discharge)d after that. On 2020-06-10, she return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointmented to the emergency care unit with a decreased alertness level, unresponsive, miotic and photoreactive pupils. Orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) was performed at [organization]. In the referral, the escalation of infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic)s to ceftriaxone was described yesterday, with the use of nph to control very high blood sugar (hyperglycemia) and 3000 ml of 0.9% saline solution infusion. The patient was stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable) upon admission to the emergency unit using vasoactive drugs, sedated on well-coupled machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation), and showed signs of poor perfusion on physical examination. Evolution during hospitalization: the patient developed infectious complications: bacterial lung infection (pneumonia), fungemia, and acute kidney injury (kadigo 3) requiring dialysis, as well as thrombotic microangiopathy with distal tissue death (necrosis) of the hallux and second toe on the left and the calcaneus on the right. She was evaluated by the [organization], which did not indicate any intervention due to the absence of infection. The patient presented complicated lung infection (pneumonia) with fluid around the lungs (pleural effusion) with characteristics of empyema on the right, and was drained by the surgical team and given 14 days of piperacillin-tazobactam. As the patient remained febrile, a new chest tomography was requested, which showed an image suggestive of persistent empyema and a small lung pus-filled swelling. However, interventional radiology approached him with insertion of a valved pigtail catheter. However, it was found that there was no fluid in the lesion and the image was attributed to pleural thickening secondary to trauma from previous drainage. Schedule: transfer to [organization] for anticoagulation and adjustment of the international normalized ratio due to pulmonary thromboblood clot blocking a vessel (embolism) and deep vein blood clot (thrombosis). We have not yet resumed anticoagulation due to the approach by rint today, 08/13, blood glucose control, family training in pressure injury care. The patient was stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable) upon admission to the emergency unit using vasoactive drugs, sedated on well-coupled machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation), and showed signs of poor perfusion on physical examination. Evolution during hospitalization: the patient developed infectious complications: bacterial lung infection (pneumonia), fungemia, and acute kidney injury (kadigo 3) requiring dialysis, as well as thrombotic microangiopathy with distal tissue death (necrosis) of the hallux and second toe on the left and the calcaneus on the right. As the patient remained febrile, a new chest tomography was requested, which showed an image suggestive of persistent empyema and a small lung pus-filled swelling. The radiology team looked at a valved pigtail catheter. However, it found that there was no fluid in the lesion and the image was more likely to get worse. 31 septic shock of pulmonary focus. [person], 66 years old, born and raised in {city}, admitted 03/03/2022, weight: 75 kg. diagnoses: 1 sepsis of pulmonary focus improving 02/28: onset of dyspnea, hyporexia, fever 03/01: in ceftriaxone azithromycin in outpatient service, current antibiotic therapy: cefepime 03/03, vancomycin 03/04 voriconazole in 03/05 chest tomography 03/03: no signs of pulmonary thromboembolism, bilateral consolidative images with air bronchogram suggestive of infectious cause, unable to rule out fungal etiology, 2 progression of chronic lymphocytic leukemia binet b? evans sd? symptoms b increased adenomegaly since January cervical lymph node biopsy 01/16/2022: findings compatible with chronic lymphocytic leukemia bicytopenia anemia thrombocytopenia worsening of leukocytosis at the expense of lymphocytes since March/2022. 3 platelet refractoriness dexamethasone 40 mg/day in 03/07 03/10 human immunoglobulin 0.4g/kg/d 30g/day d1 03/09 scheduled 5 days. 4 anemia to be clarified hemolytic anemia? bleeding from the gastrointestinal tract, melena on 03/13 upper digestive endoscopy 03/14: hemorrhagic pangastritis without active bleeding, 5 dyspnea to be clarified secondary to anemia pneumonia, pleural effusion pulmonary congestion? rtpcr coronavirus 03/10: negative, gastric lavage tuberculosis: suspended, hypothesis of tuberculosis reviewed and ruled out due to unlikely hypothesis, cytomegalovirus pcr: positive in low titer: 146 with no indication for treatment according to ccih. 6 hemorrhagic shock bleeding in the gastrointestinal tract, anterior nasal bleeding, norepinephrine started 03/13. history of current illness 03/03: [person] brought from {city}, already [person] from this service, due to acute lymphocytic leukemia. [person] reports exacerbation of disease since December, presenting with weakness, loss of 15 kg and enlarged cervical lymph nodes. 4 days ago, sought service of origin due to hyporexia, nausea, dyspnea, weakness, sweating and fever. is hemodynamically stable, arrives without oxygen support, with mild dyspnea. is on d2 of ceftriaxone azithromycin, due to the hypothesis of pneumonia raised in that service. [person] also reports the appearance of petechiae, without mucosal bleeding. chronic lymphocytic leukemia treated with rfc between 09/19 and 01/2020 without cytopenias after treatment absence of b symptoms or cytopenias, presence of asymptomatic splenomegaly cervical, axillary and inguinal adenomegaly matutes 4 immunopheno: weak surface ig cd5 cd23 fmc7 cd79b strong? matutes 4 bmo, 09/19/17 examination performed outside the service: cd3, bcl2, cd31, myeloperoxidase positive, cd20, cd23, cyclin d1 cd79a negative. the immunohistochemical findings associated with the morphological picture reveal bone marrow displaying small lymphoid pattern cells, but little reactive for the markers used, possibly associated with exogenous factors. note: at the physician's discretion, it is suggested that the diagnostic investigation continue. Cervical lymph node biopsy 05/27: compatible with small cell lymphocytic lymphoma, chronic lymphocytic leukemia. CD20, CD5, CD23 positivity. CD10, cyclin D1 negativity. Ki67 25%. biopsy of lesion in cavum 16/07/19: mormological and immunohistochemical picture compatible with infiltration of cavum by small cell lymphocytic lymphoma, chronic lymphocytic leukemia, chemotherapies performed: i rfc: 13/08/19 cqtx ii rfc: 09/10/19 cqtx iii rfc: 10/07/19 restaging iv rfc: 11/04/19 cqtx v rfc: 12/04/19 cqtx vi rfc: 01/06/20 cqtx ap: schizophrenia ocd dlp home medications: simvastatin 40 mg 001 gabapentin 300 mg 101 alprazolam 0.5 mg 002 promethazine 25 mg 101 imipramine 25mg 101 quetiapine 200 mg 003 antimicrobials meropenem in: 03/18 gentamicin in: 03/19 vancomycin 03/04 voriconazole in 03/05 previous antibiotic therapy: ceftriaxone azithromycin in 03/01 03/03 external clarithromycin 03/04 to 03/13 cefepime 03/03 to 03/18 blood cultures 03/10: negative urine culture 03/10: nhcb blood culture 03/18: progress uro 03/18: progress tracheal aspirate 03/18: progress devices central venous catheter fd 03/06 indwelling urinary catheter 03/03 transfusions 03/13: 2 packed red blood cells 03/14: 2 packed red blood cells, 2 platelet apheresis, gastrointestinal tract bleeding 03/15: 2 packed red blood cells, 1 platelet apheresis, active nasal bleeding 03/16: 2 packed red blood cells, 1 apheresis, active nasal bleeding, additional tests: chest tomography with contrast 03/13: bilateral pleural effusion, larger on the right, with an 8 cm liquid layer, without septations, displacing contralateral mediastinal structures. 2 cm liquid layer on the left. ground-glass image on the left, cardiomegaly with CTI of 0.55. indirect signs of pulmonary hypertension. tomography of the sinuses 03/13: no changes, upper digestive endoscopy 03/14, upper digestive endoscopy performed in an intensive care unit bed, under conscious sedation with fentanyl and propofol. Procedure without complications. findings: hemorrhagic pangastritis, small amount of hematic stasis fluid, no active bleeding at the time of examination. admission: 03/03/2022, discharge: 07/03/2022, stay: 03 d 19 h, location: ward, payer: sus. Home medications: simvastatin 40 mg 001 gabapentin 300 mg 101 alprazolam 0.5 mg 002 promethazine 25 mg 101 imipramine 25 mg 101 quetiapine 200 mg 003 Admission diagnoses: chronic lymphocytic leukemia relapse with maintenance of chronic phase, sepsis of pulmonary focus, immune refractoriness to platelet transfusion Transfuse only if bleeding, do ig and antimicrobial corticosteroids, start of ceftriaxone azithromycin on 03/01 External start cefepime on 03/03 Start vancomycin on 03/04 Start clarithromycin on 03/04 Associated by interstitial pattern voriconazole in 03/05 Empirical treatment. regular general condition, pale 2/4, hydrated, Glasgow Coma Scale 15, no bleeding; extremities: diffuse petechiae, more noticeable in the lower limbs and right flank, no edema; full pulses; respiratory auscultation: vesicular breath sounds, crackles on the right; cardiac auscultation: normal heart sounds in normal S1 and S2, no murmurs; abdomen: decreased bowel sounds, tympanic to percussion, painless to deep palpation, minor splenomegaly. death Severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) of pulmonary focus. The patient, 66 years old, born and raised in the city, admitted 03/03/2022, weight: 75 kg. bloodstream infection? 05/17 resolved fever, maintaining hypersecretivity no new radiographic image worsening oxygenation requested new tracheal aspirate culture old device, maintained fever performed central venous catheter change associated with vancomycin developed rash eosinophilia performed cholestasis acinetobacter 21 05/31 7 fungal urinary tract infection 05/25 candida albicans resolved fluconazole 05/25 06/04 8 bloodstream infection resolved blood culture 05/25: enterobacter aerogenes multis started ciprofloxacin 05/28 gentamicin 05/29 suspended on 06/04 9 cellulitis right upper limb in focus of htx drainage to and teicopaline started on 05/31 corrected for renal function. suspended on 06/08 due to hypothesis of endocarditis requiring vancomycin. 10th avei probable etiology: septic embolus from vegetation in ad worsening level of consciousness on 06/02 hypodense image on computed tomography performed on 06/02 left aca territory [person] remains with right hemiparesis. study of cervical vessels and transcranial doppler 06/08 within normal limits 11 bacterial endocarditis maintenance of subfebrile peaks isolated enterobacter in blood culture on 05/25 with treatment for 7 days and maintenance of subfebrile and febrile peaks after transthoracic echocardiography 06/08 with evidence of vegetation started cefepi and vancomycin on 06/08 12 status epilepticus on 06/16/2020 presence of myoclonus in the left upper limb accompanied by a vague gaze. assessed by neurology and given diazepam 2 ampoules IV hydantalization 10 mg/kg attack and 5 mg/kg maintenance in 2 daily doses awaiting head tomography. image indicates epileptic picture not compatible with previous stroke secondary to the use of cefipme? 13 new acute kidney injury kdigo iii palliative care: no indication for dialysis. admission: 04/30/2020, discharge: 06/29/2020, stay: 01 m 29 d 06 h, location: ward, payer: sus. hospitalization summary: [person], admitted to the emergency unit due to adult respiratory distress syndrome due to coronavirus, with a diagnosis of pneumothorax in an outpatient service already drained, on mechanical ventilation and neuromuscular blocker. was transferred to intensive care, evolving with a prolonged need for mechanical ventilation and tracheostomy. infectious complications also occurred, requiring broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy guided by cultures. After transfer to semi-intensive care, weaning from invasive support was initiated, however, [person] presented sudden neurological deficit, and an ischemic stroke with a severe neurological prognosis was diagnosed. After investigation by the neurology team, vegetation was seen in the right atrium and a septic embolus was suggested as the etiology of the stroke, and antibiotic therapy was initiated to treat bacterial endocarditis. During hospitalization, close contact was also made with family members, together with the palliative care team, with face-to-face meetings, digital medical bulletin and [phone number]. After transfer to a ward bed, he presented myoclonic seizures, and was diagnosed as status, with improvement after attack phenytoin. He did not present recurrence of seizures. also presented progressive worsening of renal function, of probable intrinsic etiology and related to antibiotic therapy, with a new family meeting being held on 06/25 to strengthen the bond and reinforce the absence of benefit from measures that prolonged suffering, such as hemodialysis. {omitted}. [person] developed a fever, and antipyretics were administered on a schedule to relieve discomfort related to the increase in temperature. on 06/29, with spontaneous eye admission, minimal contact with the team, no signs of pain or discomfort, with semi-thick tracheal secretion of a yellowish color, receiving visits from family members every 48 h {omitted}. general: regular general condition, pale 1/4, hydrated, acyanotic, anicteric, afebrile to the touch oxygen saturation: 91 93% heart rate: 114 respiratory rate: 22 neurology: eyes closed, non-contact, responds to tactile stimulation, closes eyes tightly when trying to examine pupils, does not obey simple commands, isochoric and photoreactive pupils, without muscle twitching ac: normal sound sounds, audible systolic murmur in normal S1 and S2, absence of pathological tvj pulmonary auscultation: presence of diffuse rhonchi, without crackles. abdomen: semiglobular, bowel sounds present, flaccid, painless, no signs of peritoneal irritation, no palpation visceromegaly lower limbs: capillary refill time less than 3 seconds edema of lower limbs 1/4 skin: presence of edema 2/4 in the hands, with marked xerosity presence of edema of upper limbs 1/4. transthoracic echocardiography 08/06: ejection fraction 44% cardiac chambers with normal dimensions mild concentric hypertrophy of the left ventricle mildly depressed left ventricular systolic performance mild diffuse left ventricular hypokinesia aneurysmal interatrial septum presence of a large mass inside the right atrium apparently adhered to the atrial wall protruding into the right ventricle in diastole, measuring 4.5x0.9 cm, which may correspond to vegetation. It should be noted that in the presence of an interatrial septal aneurysm and a mass in the right atrium, a diagnostic suspicion of a possible paradoxical embolism is raised. Electroencephalogram 06/17/2020: a moderate symmetrical disorganization of the background activity due to a diffuse increase in the content of slow waves in the theta and delta bands, with preservation of sections of a dominant posterior wakefulness rhythm in the alpha band up to 9.5 hz. b frequent intermittent bursts of rhythmic slow waves in the delta band, between 1.5 and 2 hz, with diffuse projection and with gross bifrontal accentuation, slightly lateralized to the left. Note: tremors recorded in the left upper limb. Conclusion and electroclinical correlation: Conclusion: the electroencephalogram was abnormal. The background activity findings are compatible with moderate diffuse encephalopathy, of a nonspecific nature; gross can be found in the context of subcortical structural alteration. bubble study 06/18/2020: during a bedside echocardiogram in an attempt to find communication between the right and left chambers in an attempt to explain embolization of vegetation present in the right atrium to the central nervous system. images obtained could not confirm the presence or absence of these alterations. transfer to bed in [organization]. [person] in palliative care {omitted}, vasoactive drug, dialysis prioritization of comfort and dignity measures pressure injury care tracheostomy care ward care. Id: the patient, 54 years old.
