Admission: 04/30/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 06/29/2020, stay: 01 m 29 d 06 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Hospitalization summary: the patient, admitted to the emergency unit due to adult respiratory distress syndrome due to coronavirus, with a diagnosis of air trapped in chest (pneumothorax) in an outpatient service already drained, on machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation) and neuromuscular blocker. Was transferred to intensive care, evolving with a prolonged need for machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation) and breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy). Infectious complications also occurred, requiring broad-spectrum infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) guided by cultures. After transfer to semi-intensive care, weaning from invasive support was initiated, however, the patient presented sudden neurological deficit, and an reduced blood supply (ischemic) stroke with a severe neurological expected outcome (prognosis) was diagnosed. After investigation by the neurology team, vegetation was seen in the right atrium and a septic embolus was suggested as the cause of disease (etiology) of the stroke, and infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) was initiated to treat bacterial infection of heart valves (endocarditis). During hospitalization, close contact was also made with family members, together with the comfort-focused (not curative) care team, with face-to-face meetings, digital medical bulletin and [phone number]. After transfer to a general hospital room (ward) bed, he presented myoclonic seizures, and was diagnosed as status, with improvement after attack phenytoin. He did not present recurrence of seizures. Also presented progressive worsening of renal function, of probable intrinsic cause of disease (etiology) and related to infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy), with a new family meeting being held on 06/25 to strengthen the bond and reinforce the absence of benefit from measures that prolonged suffering, such as kidney dialysis (hemodialysis). . The patient developed a fever, and antipyretics were give (administer)ed on a schedule to relieve discomfort related to the increase in temperature. On 06/29, with spontaneous eye admission, minimal contact with the team, no signs of pain or discomfort, with semi-thick tracheal secretion of a yellowish color, receiving visits from family members every 48 h . General: regular general condition, pale 1/4, hydrated, no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no fever (afebrile) to the touch oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation): 91 93% heart rate: 114 respiratory rate: 22 neurology: eyes closed, non-contact, responds to tactile stimulation, closes eyes tightly when trying to examine pupils, does not obey simple commands, isochoric and photoreactive pupils, without muscle twitching ac: normal sound sounds, audible systolic murmur in normal s1 and s2, absence of pathological tvj pulmonary auscultation: presence of diffuse rhonchi, without crackles. Abdomen: semiglobular, bowel sounds present, flaccid, painless, no signs of peritoneal irritation, no palpation visceromegaly lower limbs: capillary refill time less than 3 seconds swelling (edema) of lower limbs 1/4 skin: presence of swelling (edema) 2/4 in the hands, with marked xerosity presence of swelling (edema) of upper limbs 1/4. Transthoracic echocardiography 08/06: ejection fraction 44% cardiac chambers with normal dimensions mild concentric hypertrophy of the left ventricle mildly depressed left ventricular systolic performance mild diffuse left ventricular hypokinesia aneurysmal interatrial septum presence of a large mass inside the right atrium apparently adhered to the atrial wall protruding into the right ventricle in diastole, measuring 4.5x0.9 cm, which may correspond to vegetation. It should be noted that in the presence of an interatrial septal aneurysm and a mass in the right atrium, a diagnostic government health scheme (sus)picion of a possible paradoxical blood clot blocking a vessel (embolism) is raised. Electroencephalogram 06/17/2020: a moderate symmetrical disorganization of the background activity due to a diffuse increase in the content of slow waves in the theta and delta bands, with preservation of sections of a dominant posterior wakefulness rhythm in the alpha band up to 9.5 hz. B frequent intermittent bursts of rhythmic slow waves in the delta band, between 1.5 and 2 hz, with diffuse projection and with gross bifrontal accentuation, slightly lateralized to the left. Note: tremors recorded in the left upper limb. Conclusion and electroclinical correlation: conclusion: the electroencephalogram was abnormal. The background activity findings are compatible with moderate diffuse brain dysfunction, of a nonspecific nature; gross can be found in the context of subcortical structural alteration. Bubble study 06/18/2020: during a bedside heart ultrasound (echocardiogram) in an attempt to find communication between the right and left chambers in an attempt to explain embolization of vegetation present in the right atrium to the central nervous system. Images obtained could not confirm the presence or absence of these alterations. Transfer to bed in [organization]. Admission: 04/30/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 06/29/2020, stay: 01 m 29 d 06 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Hospitalization summary: the patient, admitted to the emergency unit due to adult respiratory distress syndrome due to coronavirus, with a diagnosis of air trapped in chest (pneumothorax) in an outpatient service already drained, on machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation) and neuromuscular blocker. Was transferred to intensive care, evolving with a prolonged need for machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation) and breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy). Infectious complications also occurred, requiring broad-spectrum infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) guided by cultures. After the surgery, the patient was diagnosed with a heart attack, which caused a stroke, and had a severe neurological problem. After a surgery, he was treated to a general hospital room (ward) bed and a septic embolus was found in the right atrium, which was used to treat bacterial infection of heart valves (endocarditis). During hospitalization, he had a specialized team called the comfort-focused (not curative) care team, with face-to-face meetings, digital medical bulletin and [phone number]. After transfer to a hospital, he presented myoclonic seizures, and was treated as status after attack phenytoin. He did not present any evidence about the condition of the stroke. He also presented progressive worsening of renal functions, including probable intrinsic cause of disease (etiology) and related to infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy), with a new family meeting being held on 06/25 to strengthen the bond of benefit from measures that prolonged suffering from the disease, such as kidney dialysis (hemodialysis).</s> On 06/29, the team looked at a yellowish color, with no signs of pain or discomfort, with semi-thick tracheal secretion, and a red eye, receiving visits from family members every 48 h . General: regular general condition, pale 1/4, hydrated, no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no fever (afebrile) to the eye, isolated eyes: 91 93% heart rate: 114 respiratory rate: 22 eyes closed, non-contact, responds to tactile stimulation, closes eyes tightly when trying to examine pupils, does not obey simple commands, isochoric and photoreactive pupil, without muscle twitching ac: normal sound sounds, audible systolic murmur in normal s1 and s2, absence of pathological tvj pulmonary auscultation: presence of diffuse rhonchi, without crackles.</s> Abdomen is a small part of the body. It's hard to see if the body is tiny, no signs of peritoneal irritation, no palpation, no pain or pain. This is because of the presence of swelling (edema) in the hands, with marked xerosity presence of the upper limbs 1/4 in the eyes. We also need to be sure that this is a good way to understand how well we can handle the body, especially for people with the right right nerv B frequent intermittent bursts of rhythmic slow waves in the delta band, between 1.5 and 2 hz, with diffuse projection and with gross bifrontal accentuation, slightly lateralized to the left. Note: tremors recorded in the left upper limb. Conclusion and electroclinical correlation: conclusion: the electroencephalogram was abnormal. The background activity findings are compatible with moderate diffuse brain dysfunction, of a nonspecific nature; gross can be found in the context of subcortical structural alteration. [person], 67 years old, from {city}. history of current illness: fever for 8 days and asthenia. problem: 01 subacute infective endocarditis of non-native aortic prosthesis to date Duke criteria 1 major and 3 minor persistent fever, predisposing condition bloodstream infection on 24/08/2020 staphylococcus epidermidis in blood culture 1 sample. transthoracic echocardiography 23/09/2020 presence of filamentous image on the atrial face of the aortic prosthesis measuring 6 mm suggestive of vegetation. Blood culture 09/23 Staphylococcus capitis S Vancomycin Urine culture 09/27 Klebsiella aerogenes R: Ampicillin, cefuroxime, ceftazidime, ceftriaxone, pipetazo Antibiotic therapy: Vancomycin Tazocin 09/23 History of current illness: [person] reports feeling feverish for 8 days, daily, starting soon after hospital discharge, only measured yesterday at the emergency care unit 38.1. [person] reports intense asthenia and sporadic precordial pain, of the constant type, lasting more than 20 minutes and less than 1 hour, without triggering factors, with radiation to the upper back region. Denies dyspnea, denies cough or other respiratory symptoms. Admitted to the coronary care unit bed, hemodynamically stable, with no self-referrals. personal history: severe aortic insufficiency postoperative aortic valve replacement with biological aortic valve prosthesis Braille 25 mm 08/11/2020 total cec: 70 min anoxia: 58 min ischemic stroke seizure on 08/11/20 [person] presented with seizure in the context of acute encephalic insult considered as acute symptomatic crisis guided gradual reduction of anticonvulsant phenytoin until its suspension sepsis pulmonary focus on 15/08/20 resolved cefepime 08/15 08/22 acute respiratory failure on 18/08/20 orotracheal intubation 08/18 tracheostomy 08/22 tracheostomy decannulation on 14/09/20 bloodstream infection on 24/08/20 staphylococcus epidermidis in blood culture 1 vancomycin sample 08/24 09/04 infectious worsening fever tachycardia leukocytosis on 31/08/22 without well-defined focus resolved tazocin 08/31 09/12 narrow qrs tachyarrhythmia atrial flutter? atrial tachy? resolved reversion to sinus rhythm after amiodarone, heart rate control with carvedilol two-vessel coronary artery disease catheterization 02/18/20: dominant rc 40% distal 40% ostial and 70% middle third cx 40% middle third 1st mge 80% proximal iao marked diffuse hypokinesia mild depressed global function. revascularization not indicated. systemic arterial hypertension diabetes mellitus continuous-use medications: rivaroxaban 15 mg/day adjusted for renal function atorvastatin 40 mg/day enalapril 10 mg 12/12 h carvedilol 25 mg 12/12 h amlodipine 5 mg 12/12 h hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg/day spironolactone 50 mg/day amiodarone 100 mg/day metformin 500 mg/day omeprazole 20 mg/day. physical examination: heart rate 79 blood pressure 136/95 respiratory rate 17 good general condition, flushed, hydrated, eupneic, capillary refill time 2 seconds. cardiovascular: normal S1 and S2, normal heart sounds, aortic mid-systolic click respiratory auscultation vesicular breath sounds, bilateral reduced at the bases abdomen: flat, flaccid and painless. extremities: absence of edema, free calves neurology: glasgow coma scale 15, Isochoric and photoreactive pupils, no gross deficits. ultrasound with image suggestive of vegetation and non-native valve blood culture with staphylococcus capitis. outpatient follow-up. discharge transfer, antibiotic completion. will be scheduled in the system. 35 [person], 87 years old, admission 01/15, admission: 01/15/2021, discharge: 02/03/2021, stay: 18 d 15 h, location: ward, payer: sus. reason for current hospitalization: 1. acute on chronic kidney disease kdigo iii secondary to an infectious condition, previous chronic kidney disease, start of renal replacement therapy on 01/15/22, will be kept on intermittent dialysis. 2. community-acquired pneumonia, treated, drop in oxygen saturation, productive cough, leukocytosis with left shift. negative external coronavirus test, ceftriaxone, start 01/15, 01/24, azithromycin, start 01/21, 01/26, non-contrast chest tomography 01/20: partial report: pulmonary opacities suggestive of an infectious inflammatory process, with a bronchopneumonia pattern. bilateral laminar pleural effusion. scattered, nonspecific pulmonary micronodules. Follow-up is suggested. ascending aortic ectasia. 3. frailty syndrome in the elderly. history of current illness: [person] reports that, 14 days ago, [person] presented with flu-like symptoms, with cough, fever in 1 measurement, dyspnea, and decline in general condition. She was treated at an emergency care unit, with a coronavirus test (she cannot say which one was negative), and was discharged with only symptoms. [person] reports that, approximately 5 days later, [person] began to complain of significant bilateral low back pain, maintaining a productive cough. In the following days, she developed progressive asthenia, headache, myalgia, worsening of low back pain, nausea, and vomiting of food contents. She denies fever during this period. 3 days ago, [person] presented a decrease in the level of consciousness, remaining moaning, refusing any oral intake, and reducing the volume of diuresis. [person] took her to the emergency care unit, where initial tests were performed, which showed acute kidney injury with uremia UR 200. [person] was then referred to this service. Upon admission, she presented with a Glasgow Coma Scale of 10, moaning, hypertensive, respiratory discomfort, with adequate saturation in CNO2 at 3 L/min. History: non-insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus II, systemic arterial hypertension, blood cultures and urine cultures 01/16: negative. None. No pending tests. Keep using insulin NPH 880 for home use and suspend the use of antihypertensives, I request follow-up with speech therapist and nutritionist. Family already advised about diet. [person] dependent on oxygen arterial blood gas analysis 01/25 with PO2: 53.4 and oxygen saturation: 86%. Already with home oxygen installed. I request a wheelchair, bath chair and diapers. I request follow-up at a basic health unit for comorbidities. by nephrology: I have seen the discharge schedule, as previously discussed, [person] with criteria for performing incremental dialysis, therefore, I schedule dialysis using low-flow oxygen via nasal catheter, stable, without signs of infection, without dialysis urgency. [registration number]. admission to surgery: 09/10 referred from {organization} problem 1 upper gastrointestinal bleeding, upper gastrointestinal endoscopy of origin with duodenal ulcer forrest 2b, without adequate hemostasis transfusion 6 packed red blood cells 700 ml pfc admission 4 packed red blood cells 11/10 no criteria for disseminated intravascular coagulation, on admission arteriography with successful embolization in {omitted} 2 ecchymoses and blood bullae, fixed cyanosis? in extremities paraneoplastic leukocytoclastic vasculitis? septic embolus? 3 clinical decompensation secondary to shock of mixed etiology? hypovolemic shock, upper gastrointestinal bleeding secondary to duodenal varices, refractory anemia, septic shock infective endocarditis? pulmonary focus? 4 pulmonary nodules and cavitations to be clarified, metastasis of neoplasia from site to be clarified? primary lung neoplasm? chest tomography performed at {organization} 10/07: lung masses probably primary neoplastic with pulmonary nodules suspicious for secondary involvement. pneumonia? tuberculosis? granulomatosis with polyangiitis? 5 septic hemorrhagic shock pulmonary focus? abdominal? endocarditis? 6 acute kidney injury kdigo 3 secondary to sepsis 7 hypernatremia secondary to dehydration?. admission: 10/09/2020, discharge: 14/10/2020, stay: 04 d 14 h, location: ward, payer: sus. i speak to [person] patient [organization] at the time of the evaluation, who reports that the patient had been complaining of abdominal pain for about 2 months, in addition to nonspecific pain in the lower limbs. in addition, she reports that 1 month ago there was the appearance of darkened skin spots on the extremities. Reports that [person] sought care, and tests were requested that were not performed. In addition, {person} reports that [person] has lost an estimated 10 kg of weight in the last 2 months. [person] denies that [person] has had fever, respiratory symptoms such as cough or hemoptysis, joint pain, or altered level of consciousness. Reports bowel habits with baseline constipation. Reports that 1 week ago he sought emergency care due to worsening abdominal pain, in addition to skin lesions, which revealed anemia, and was admitted and referred to {organization}. At {organization}, he presented melena, and an upper gastrointestinal endoscopy was performed, which revealed duodenal ulcers, which could not be resolved endoscopically, and he was referred to surgery at {organization}. Upon admission, he received a transfusion of 6 red blood cell concentrates and 700 ml of plasma. A medical clinic consultation was requested to assess skin lesions, cyanosis, and blood bubbles, and an arteriography was requested. 2 days after admission, he developed a new drop in hemoglobin, hemodynamic instability, and altered level of consciousness. The surgical team performed orotracheal intubation. The hemoglobin level was 4.9, and he was transfused with 4 packed red blood cells. His general condition worsened progressively throughout his stay, and he developed hemodynamic instability that was refractory to the prescribed measures. He was given a double dose of vasoactive drugs, but there was no response. He presented with fever and left lung consolidation. He was given antibiotics, but there was no clinical improvement. At {omitted} 10/21, [person] was evaluated. He was sedated and taking a neuromuscular blocker, with a monitor showing a heart rate of 69, but no peripheral oxygen saturation or blood pressure measurements. During clinical evaluation, [person] was found to have non-photoreactive mydriatic pupils. Absence of brainstem reflexes. Absence of palpable central pulses. CT scan of the skull: no acute intracranial traumatic findings. Changes suggestive of discrete cerebral edema. CT angiography: no active bleeding points identified. Pulmonary lesions, some cavitated, the largest in the left upper lobe, of unclear etiology. Possibilities include granulomatous process, vasculitis or neoplasia. Signs of intestinal subocclusion. 11/10. Arteriography of visceral organs. Embolization of gastroduodenal artery with Concerto coil 5x15 microparticles. Chest X-ray. 13/10. Presence of pulmonary cavitations. Consolidation at the base of the left hemithorax. Arteriography with successful embolization on 11/10. antibiotic therapy ceftriaxone d1 11/10 oxacillin d0 11/10 metronidazole 11/10 12/10 transfusion 6 packed red blood cells, 700 ml pfc admission 4 packed red blood cells 11/10. 1 death certificate at {omitted} 2 I fill out the death certificate 3 I call family members. [person] evolves with death at {omitted}. The patient, 57 years old. [registration number]. In extremities paraneoplastic leukocytoclastic vasculitis? Septic embolus? 3 clinical decompensation secondary to shock of mixed cause of disease (etiology)? Hypovolemic shock, upper gastrointestinal bleeding secondary to duodenal varices, refractory low blood (anemia), severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) infective infection of heart valves (endocarditis)? Pulmonary focus? 4 pulmonary nodules and cavitations to be clarified, metastasis of neoplasia from site to be clarified? Primary lung neoplasm? Chest tomography performed at the hospital 10/07: lung masses probably primary neoplastic with pulmonary nodules government health schemepicious for secondary involvement. Lung infection (pneumonia)? TB (tuberculosis)? Granulomatosis with polyangiitis? 5 septic hemorrhagic shock pulmonary focus? Abdominal? Infection of heart valves (endocarditis)? 6 acute kidney injury kdigo 3 secondary to serious blood infection (sepsis) 7 hypernatremia secondary to dehydration?. Admission: 10/09/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 14/10/2020, stay: 04 d 14 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. I speak to the patient patient [organization] at the time of the evaluation, who reports that the patient had been complaining of abdominal pain for about 2 months, in addition to nonspecific pain in the lower limbs. In addition, she reports that 1 month ago there was the appearance of darkened skin spots on the extremities. Reports that the patient sought care, and tests were requested that were not performed. In addition, {person} reports that the patient systemic arterial high BP (hypertension) lost an estimated 10 kg of weight in the last 2 months. The patient denies that the patient systemic arterial high BP had fever, respiratory symptoms such as cough or hemoptysis, joint pain, or altered alertness level (level of consciousness). Reports bowel habits with baseline constipation. Reports that 1 week ago he sought emergency care due to worsening abdominal pain, in addition to skin lesions, which revealed low blood (anemia), and was admitted and referred to the hospital. At the hospital, he presented melena, and an upper gastrointestinal endoscopy was performed, which revealed duodenal ulcers, which could not be resolved endoscopically, and he was referred to surgery at the hospital. Upon admission, he received a transfusion of 6 red blood cell concentrates and 700 ml of plasma. A medical clinic consultation was requested to assess skin lesions, cyanosis, and blood bubbles, and an arteriography was requested. 2 days after admission, he developed a new drop in blood count (Hb), hemodynamic instability, and altered alertness level (level of consciousness). The surgical team performed orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation). The blood count (Hb) level was 4.9, and he was transfused with 4 packed red blood cells. His general condition worsened progressively throughout his stay, and he developed hemodynamic instability that was refractory to the prescribed measures. He was given a double dose of vasoactive drugs, but there was no response. He presented with fever and left lung consolidation. He was given infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic)s, but there was no clinical improvement. At 10/21, the patient was evaluated. He was sedated and taking a neuromuscular blocker, with a monitor showing a heart rate of 69, but no peripheral oxygen oxygen level in blood or blood pressure measurements. During clinical evaluation, the patient was found to have non-photoreactive mydriatic pupils. Absence of brainstem reflexes. Absence of palpable central pulses. Ct scan of the skull: no acute intracranial traumatic findings. Changes suggestive of discrete cerebral swelling (edema). Ct angiography: no active bleeding points identified. Pulmonary lesions, some cavitated, the largest in the left upper lobe, of unclear cause of disease (etiology). Possibilities include granulomatous process, vasculitis or neoplasia. Signs of intestinal subocclusion. 11/10. Arteriography of visceral organs. Embolization of gastroduodenal artery with concerto coil 5x15 microparticles. Chest x-ray. 13/10. Presence of pulmonary cavitations. Consolidation at the base of the left hemithorax. Arteriography with successful embolization on 11/10. 40 1 severe traumatic brain injury, trauma mechanism: [person] found lying next to his motorcycle, on an urban road, around {omitted}. He was wearing a helmet. There was no report of Glasgow Coma Scale at the scene. Glasgow Coma Scale 7 on admission at the origin, and orotracheal intubation was performed. Report of right otorrhagia. 09/01: Neuroprotective measures and prophylactic pht were initiated. Central invasive blood pressure was inserted. Amoxicillin-clavulanate was started due to sinus hemorrhage. 10/01: [organization] advises to contact us when level of consciousness improves to assess removal of collar. 13/01: Attempt to wean from sedation. Presented increased central invasive blood pressure, with need to optimize sedation and analgesia again. 15/01: Increased central invasive blood pressure. Increased sedation and mannitol and hypertonic saline were administered. 01/16: increased central invasive blood pressure. Administration of mannitol and intermittent hypertonic saline was required again. 01/17: increased central invasive blood pressure. Hypertonic saline was administered and neuromuscular blockade was initiated. 01/18: neuromuscular blockade was suspended and sedation/analgesia was weaned. Central invasive blood pressure was removed. 01/19: CTD was performed, with normal pulsatility index and bilaterally increased BNO. 01/26: electroencephalogram showed marked encephalopathy with predominance of the left temporal region, of a structural nature. Phenytoin weaning was initiated. 01/28: phenytoin was suspended. 02/06: vancomycin was suspended. 02/07: tracheostomy decannulation, 2nd focus of infection, pulmonary artery. 01/18: worsening of radiological findings on chest x-ray. Antibiotic therapy was escalated from amoxicillin (clavulanate) to piperacillin (tazobactam). growth of klebsiella oxytoca multis, proteus multis in tracheal aspirate. 01/20: worsening of pulmonary secretion, worsening of leukocytosis and fever plateau. associated vancomycin. 3 central nervous system infection? 01/26: fever plateau. performed lumbar puncture: 11 cells 7% neutrophils 93% lymphocytes protein: 54.4. 01/26: replaced piperacillin tazobactam by meropenem. 02/08 end of antibiotic therapy on d13 due to pharmacodermia 4 pseudomembranous colitis? 01/26: metronidazole started due to diarrhea, fever, elevated cpr. 02/08 end of antibiotic therapy on d13 due to pharmacodermia 5 pharmakodermia to be clarified dress? record 34 secondary to haldol phenergan? dermatology evaluation 02/08 clinical, systemic surveillance and use of antihistamines dermatology evaluation 02/08 pharmacodermia secondary to haldol and/or phenergan? i maintain clinical and laboratory surveillance. i prescribe antihistamine. admission: 01/09/2020, discharge: 02/22/2020, stay: 01 m 13 d 08 h, location: ward, payer: sus. physical examination upon discharge: regular general condition, afebrile, acyanotic, anicteric, pale 1/4, hydrated, eupneic skin: peeling on the face, trunk, back and limbs. Cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm in two beats, normal heart sounds, no murmurs. Pulmonary auscultation: vesicular breath sounds, no adventitious sounds. Abdomen: flat, bowel sounds, flaccid and depressible, no masses or megalia, painless to palpation. Extremities: perfused, no edema or cyanosis. Free calves, no swelling. Erythema in the left upper limb, close to the site of a previous peripherally inserted central catheter. Neurological: Glasgow coma scale 14.04.04.06.06. Disoriented in time, space, and person. Isochoric and photoreactive pupils, restricted adduction of the left eye. Mobilizes all 4 limbs. Hoffman and Tromner are absent bilaterally. Bilateral indifferent CPR, no clinical stigmata of crisis. Computerized tomography of the skull 09/01: hemorrhagic contusions in the bilateral frontotemporal region, with greater volume on the left. Frontotemporal laminar HSD on the left, with a maximum thickness of 3 mm. Fracture line extending from the right parietal bone to the temporal bone and its petrous part to the sphenoid bone, mastoid and middle ear on the right. Hemotympanum on the right. Hemosinus. Carotid atheromatosis. Computerized tomography of the skull 10/01: increased bilateral frontotemporal hemorrhagic contusions, especially perilesional edema, exerting a mass effect. Small volume pneumonecephalus in the right frontal region. Frontotemporal laminar HSD on the left. Midline deviation of 5 mm. Peripherally inserted central catheter well positioned. computed tomography of the skull 01/14: maintenance of bilateral frontotemporal hemorrhagic contusions and 5 mm midline shift computed tomography of the skull 01/15: maintenance of bilateral frontotemporal hemorrhagic contusions and 5 mm midline shift computed tomography of the skull 01/17: maintenance of bilateral frontotemporal hemorrhagic contusions and 5 mm midline shift. computed tomography of the skull 01/23 [no report]: no worsening of previous lesions. areas of bleeding persist. computed tomography of the skull 01/26 {omitted}: control tomography of the skull 02/03: greater absorption of previous contusions computed tomography of the skull 02/08: better definition of left temporal hypodensity tcd 01/19: normal pulsatility index. Bilaterally enlarged optic nerve sheath. Cervical spine CT scan 09/01: no evidence of acute traumatic injuries. Slight reduction of the disc space and intervertebral foramina of c5c6, notably on the right. No evidence of root or spinal compression. Chest CT scan 09/01: signs suggestive of bronchoaspiration. No evidence of acute traumatic injuries. Abdominal CT scan 09/01: no evidence of acute traumatic injuries. Pelvic CT scan 09/01: no evidence of acute traumatic injuries. Electroencephalogram 26/01: markedly disorganized baseline activity due to diffuse increase in the content of slow waves in the theta and delta bands, lack and depression of fast rhythms, and absence of physiological graphoelements of sleep. There is a slight clinical and electroencephalographic reactivity after painful stimulation, with desynchronization and the emergence of a very low voltage beta rhythm. There is a slight asymmetry with greater attenuation of amplitudes in the posterior medial temporal areas of the left cerebral hemisphere. B. Slow wave bursts in the bifrontal projections, delta and theta, polymorphic or monorhythmic, of intermittent occurrence. Conclusion and electroclinical correlation: The electroencephalogram was abnormal. The findings are compatible with marked encephalopathy, with left temporal predominance, of a structural nature. cerebrospinal fluid 01/26: cytology: 11 cells 7% neutrophils 93% lymphocytes 1 red blood cell biochemistry: glucose: 68.2 57% protein: 54.4 chloride: 121 lactate: 3.1 antibiotic therapy: current: ø previous: amoxicillin aclavulanate 01/09 01/18 piperacillin tazobactam 01/18 01/26 vancomycin 01/20 02/06 meropenem 01/26 02/08 metronidazole 01/26 02/08 cultures: blood culture: 01/30: negative so far 01/27: negative so far 01/26: negative so far 01/20: negative 01/17: negative 01/14: negative 02/08: absence of growth after 7 days tracheal aspirate 01/14: proteus multis and klebsiella oxytoca multis urine culture: 01/26: negative 02/11: 80000 cfu/ml klebsiella pneumoniae carbapenemase sensitive to amikacin, proteus mirabilis multis intravenous catheter: 01/17: negative 01/20: negative 01/27: klebsiella pneumoniae carbapenemase proteus multi s. 02/02: negative cerebrospinal fluid 01/26: negative. counter-reference to [organization] for behavioral management and post-dress surveillance clin follow-up appointment 06/08/2020. stable. 1 severe traumatic brain injury, trauma mechanism: the patient found lying next to his motorcycle, on an urban road, around . Admission: 01/09/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 02/22/2020, stay: 01 m 13 d 08 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Physical examination upon sent home from hospital (discharge): regular general condition, no fever (afebrile), no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), pale 1/4, hydrated, breathing normally (eupneic) skin: peeling on the face, trunk, back and limbs. Cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm in two beats, normal heart sounds, no murmurs. Pulmonary auscultation: normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds), no adventitious sounds. Abdomen: flat, bowel sounds, flaccid and depressible, no masses or megalia, painless to palpation. Extremities: perfused, no swelling (edema) or cyanosis. Free calves, no swelling. Erythema in the left upper limb, close to the site of a previous peripherally inserted central catheter. Neurological: glasgow coma scale 14.04.04.06.06. Disoriented in time, space, and person. Isochoric and photoreactive pupils, restricted adduction of the left eye. Mobilizes all 4 limbs. Hoffman and tromner are absent bilaterally. Bilateral indifferent cpr, no clinical stigmata of crisis. Computerized tomography of the skull 09/01: hemorrhagic contusions in the bilateral frontotemporal region, with greater volume on the left. Frontotemporal laminar hsd on the left, with a maximum thickness of 3 mm. Fracture line extending from the right parietal bone to the temporal bone and its petrous part to the sphenoid bone, mastoid and middle ear on the right. Hemotympanum on the right. Hemosinus. Carotid atheromatosis. Computerized tomography of the skull 10/01: increased bilateral frontotemporal hemorrhagic contusions, especially perilesional swelling (edema), exerting a mass effect. Small volume pneumonecephalus in the right frontal region. Frontotemporal laminar hsd on the left. Midline deviation of 5 mm. Peripherally inserted central catheter well positioned. CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 01/14: maintenance of bilateral frontotemporal hemorrhagic contusions and 5 mm midline shift CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 01/15: maintenance of bilateral frontotemporal hemorrhagic contusions and 5 mm midline shift CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 01/17: maintenance of bilateral frontotemporal hemorrhagic contusions and 5 mm midline shift. CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 01/23 [no report]: no worsening of previous lesions. Areas of bleeding persist. CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 01/26 : control tomography of the skull 02/03: greater absorption of previous contusions CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 02/08: better definition of left temporal hypodensity tcd 01/19: normal pulsatility index. Bilaterally enlarged optic nerve sheath. Cervical spine ct scan 09/01: no evidence of acute traumatic injuries. Slight reduction of the disc space and intervertebral foramina of c5c6, notably on the right. No evidence of root or spinal compression. Chest ct scan 09/01: signs suggestive of bronchoaspiration. No evidence of acute traumatic injuries. Abdominal ct scan 09/01: no evidence of acute traumatic injuries. Pelvic ct scan 09/01: no evidence of acute traumatic injuries. Electroencephalogram 26/01: markedly disorganized baseline activity due to diffuse increase in the content of slow waves in the theta and delta bands, lack and depression of fast rhythms, and absence of physiological graphoelements of sleep. There is a slight clinical and electroencephalographic reactivity after painful stimulation, with desynchronization and the emergence of a very low voltage beta rhythm. There is a slight asymmetry with greater attenuation of amplitudes in the posterior medial temporal areas of the left cerebral hemisphere. B. Slow wave bursts in the bifrontal projections, delta and theta, polymorphic or monorhythmic, of intermittent occurrence. Conclusion and electroclinical correlation: the electroencephalogram was abnormal. The findings are compatible with marked brain dysfunction (encephalopathy), with left temporal predominance, of a structural nature. Growth of klebsiella oxytoca multis, proteus multis in tracheal aspirate. 01/20: worsening of pulmonary secretion, worsening of high white blood cell count (leukocytosis) and fever plateau. Associated vancomycin. 3 central nervous system infection? 01/26: fever plateau. Performed lumbar puncture: 11 cells 7% neutrophils 93% lymphocytes protein: 54.4. 01/26: replaced piperacillin tazobactam by meropenem. 02/08 end of infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) on d13 due to pharmacodermia 4 pseudomembranous colitis? 01/26: metronidazole started due to diarrhea, fever, elevated cpr. 02/08 end of infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) on d13 due to pharmacodermia 5 pharmakodermia to be clarified dress? Record 34 secondary to haldol phenergan? Dermatology evaluation 02/08 clinical, systemic surveillance and use of antihistamines dermatology evaluation 02/08 pharmacodermia secondary to haldol and/or phenergan? Admission: 01/09/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 02/22/2020, stay: 01 m 13 d 08 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Physical examination upon sent home from hospital (discharge): regular general condition, no fever (afebrile), no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), pale 1/4, hydrated, breathing normally (eupneic) skin: peeling on the face, trunk, back and limbs. Cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm in two beats, normal heart sounds, no murmurs. Pulmonary auscultation: normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds), no adventitious sounds. Abdomen: flat, bowel sounds, flaccid and depressible, no masses or megalia, painless to palpation. Extremities: perfused, no swelling (edema) or cyanosis. Free calves, no swelling. Erythema in the left upper limb, close to the site of a previous peripherally inserted central catheter. Neurological: glasgow coma scale 14.04.04.06.06. Disoriented in time, space, and person. Isochoric and photoreactive pupils, restricted adduction of the left eye. Mobilizes all 4 limbs. Hoffman and tromner are absent bilaterally. Bilateral indifferent cpr, no clinical stigmata of crisis. Computerized tomography of the skull 09/01: hemorrhagic contusions in the bilateral frontotemporal region, with greater volume on the left. Frontotemporal laminar hsd on the left, with a maximum thickness of 3 mm. Fracture line extending from the right parietal bone to the temporal bone and its petrous part to the sphenoid bone, mastoid and middle ear on the right. Hemotympanum on the right. Hemosinus. Carotid atheromatosis. Computerized tomography of the skull 10/01: increased bilateral frontotemporal hemorrhagic contusions, especially perilesional swelling (edema), exerting a mass effect. Small volume pneumonecephalus in the right frontal region. Frontotemporal laminar hsd on the left. Midline deviation of 5 mm. Peripherally inserted central catheter well positioned. CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 01/14: maintenance of bilateral frontotemporal hemorrhagic contusions and 5 mm midline shift CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 01/15: maintenance of bilateral frontotemporal hemorrhagic contusions and 5 mm midline shift CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 01/17: maintenance of bilateral frontotemporal hemorrhagic contusions and 5 mm midline shift. CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 01/23 [no report]: no worsening of previous lesions. Areas of bleeding persist. CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 01/26 : control tomography of the skull 02/03: greater absorption of previous contusions CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 02/08: better definition of left temporal hypodensity tcd 01/19: normal pulsatility index. Bilaterally enlarged optic nerve sheath. Cervical spine ct scan 09/01: no evidence of acute traumatic injuries. Slight reduction of the disc space and intervertebral foramina of c5c6, notably on the right. No evidence of root or spinal compression. Chest ct scan 09/01: signs suggestive of bronchoaspiration. No evidence of acute traumatic injuries. Abdominal ct scan 09/01: no evidence of acute traumatic injuries. Pelvic ct scan 09/01: no evidence of acute traumatic injuries. Electroencephalogram 26/01: markedly disorganized baseline activity due to diffuse increase in the content of slow waves in the theta and delta bands, lack and depression of fast rhythms, and absence of physiological graphoelements of sleep. There is a slight clinical and electroencephalographic reactivity after painful stimulation, with desynchronization and the emergence of a very low voltage beta rhythm. There is a slight asymmetry with greater attenuation of amplitudes in the posterior medial temporal areas of the left cerebral hemisphere. B. Slow wave bursts in the bifrontal projections, delta and theta, polymorphic or monorhythmic, of intermittent occurrence. Conclusion and electroclinical correlation: the electroencephalogram was abnormal. The findings are compatible with marked brain dysfunction (encephalopathy), with left temporal predominance, of a structural nature. Cerebrospinal fluid 01/26: cytology: 11 cells 7% neutrophils 93% lymphocytes 1 red blood cell biochemistry: glucose: 68.2 57% protein: 54.4 chloride: 121 lactate: 3.1 infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy): current: ø previous: amoxicillin aclavulanate 01/09 01/18 piperacillin tazobactam 01/18 01/26 vancomycin 01/20 02/06 meropenem 01/26 02/08 metronidazole 01/26 02/08 cultures: blood test to find infection (blood culture): 01/30: negative so far 01/27: negative so far 01/26: negative so far 01/20: negative 01/17: negative 01/14: negative 02/08: absence of growth after 7 days tracheal aspirate 01/14: proteus multis and klebsiella oxytoca multis urine culture: 01/26: negative 02/11: 80000 cfu/ml klebsiella lung infection (pneumonia)e carbapenemase sensitive to amikacin, proteus mirabilis multis given through a vein (drip/IV) catheter: 01/17: negative 01/20: negative 01/27: klebsiella lung infection (pneumonia)e carbapenemase proteus multi s. 02/02: negative cerebrospinal fluid 01/26: negative. [person] reports that, two weeks ago, he began experiencing tight precordial pain, lasting a few seconds, starting with exertion and relieved with rest, without radiation and without associated vegetative symptoms, along with significant edema of the lower limbs, worsening of nocturnal cough, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, and dyspnea on exertion, with loss of functional capacity. He was undergoing rehabilitation with physical therapy after hospitalization for coronavirus. denies fever. denies other upper airway symptoms. denies dysuria. denies palpitations. [person]. sought [organization], where 40 mg of intravenous furosemide was administered and tests were requested, and the patient was referred to this service. Fever: no fever since admission, anemia: no anemia, infection: no leukocytosis, no progression of white blood cells. Growth of Klebsiella pneumoniae in culture on 05/29 treated with gentamicin for 7 days, renal function: stable, hydroelectrolytic disturbances: no hydroelectrolytic disturbances, constipation: absent daily bowel movements. Pain or dyspnea: absent. Electrocardiogram on 05/24 provisional report: regular rhythm, sinus. Left atrial overload, PR < 200 ms. QRS: globally reduced amplitude, no overload, no Q wave. Repolarization: no supra, flattening of T waves between v46. Echocardiogram definitive report, 05/25: left ventricular ejection fraction 14%, left atrium 35, moderate dilation of the left chambers and mild dilation of the right chambers. Segmental mobility of the left ventricle showed diffuse hypokinesia of marked degree. Moderate to marked mitral insufficiency. PSAP 49 mmHg underestimated. Chest tomography 05/24: cardiomegaly with signs of pulmonary edema. Large bilateral pleural effusion. LSE pulmonary nodule, stable. Diagnostic thoracentesis in the right hemithorax, liver biopsy. Biopsy results and oncotic cytology. Medical discharge with nitrate 10 mg 8/8h, hydralazine 25 mg 8/8h, furosemide 80 mg 2x day. Geriatric outpatient consultation 06/22: Attention: check the results of the liver biopsy, oncotic cytology and pelural effusion cultures. 43 refractory shock. admission: 01/08/2020, discharge: 03/08/2020, stay: 01 d 19 h, bed: ue ward, payer: sus. [person] referred to zero vacancy, coming from {omitted}, in an advanced ambulance, accompanied by [person], who reports that [person] was evaluated approximately 20 days ago at an emergency care unit in {omitted}, being diagnosed with acute myocardial infarction and released on the same day, sic. reports that [person] was admitted on 01/08 due to severe epigastrium pain, with no improvement with the use of analgesics, and investigation was carried out with electrocardiogram, laboratory tests and chest x-ray. reports that the x-ray showed mediastinal widening compatible with thoracoabdominal aortic widening. the image is not included in the exams brought by [person]. It also states that [person] was admitted with blood pressure of 180x100 mmHg, showing regression to levels of 130x80 mmHg. When questioned, [person] denies precordialgia, denies irradiating abdominal pain, denies cervical pain, denies pain in the upper limbs, denies nausea, denies vomiting, denies diarrhea or other symptoms. Evaluated with an imaging exam, which ruled out the diagnostic hypothesis of referral, it was decided to maintain hospitalization for further investigation. He evolved with clinical worsening during hospitalization, being collected infectious screening, initiated empirical antibiotic therapy with ceftriaxone and metronidazole, volume expansion and opted for serial physical examination. In the early morning of 03/08 he evolved with shock, requiring orotracheal intubation, mechanical ventilation and initiation of vasoactive drugs. Progression to refractory shock measures was necessary. Antibiotic therapy was escalated on 03/08 given clinical worsening. [person] evolved with refractoriness to the instituted clinical measures, evolving to death in {omitted}. physical examination upon admission: [person] in good general condition, lucid, oriented, communicative and collaborative vital signs: saturation 93% on room air heart rate 104 right upper limb blood pressure 125x88 left upper limb blood pressure 121x87. flat abdomen, bowel sounds, flaccid, painful on deep palpation in the epigastrium and flanks, voluntary defense, no palpable pulsatile masses, negative dB. extremities: upper limbs: symmetrical, preserved and full pulses lower limbs: left: fe / pop / ta / tp right: fe / póp / ta / tp preserved capillary refill time. 03/08: 01h: lactate: 3.4 hb: 14.5 gb: 5.7 30% sticks pqt: 203 ckmb: 28 amylase: 288 trope 21 gas: ph 7.11 po2: 265 pco2: 45 hco3: 14.3 be 14 falls: 1.15 k: 4.8 na: 136 05h: lactate: 3.9 02/08 2pm: lactate: 4.1 u: 76 cr: 2.65 tgo: 39 tgp: 16 bt: 1.1 hb: 14.3 ht: 44 pqt: 203 gb: 3.9 intravenous gas: ph: 7.19 po2: 20.9 pco3: 46.5 hco3: 17.6 be: 10 na: 137 k: 3.8 pcr: 26.4 amylase: 299 urinalysis: cloudy, bilirubin, ptn, nitrite, heme epithelial cells various intense bacteriuria 08/01 hb 14.9 / gb 6.6 / platelets 189 / na 138 / k 4.2 / ur 51 / cr 1.34 / prothrombin time 1.18 / aptd 0.96 definitive angiotc 08/01 without signs of arterial dissection. mediastinal, abdominal and retroperitoneal lymphonopathy. consolidation in the left lower lobe associated with cavities, the possibility of neoplasia or infectious/inflammatory process should be considered. small volume ascites.. 03/08: 01h: lactate: 3.4 hb: 14.5 gb: 5.7 30% pqt rods: 203 ckmb: 28 amylase: 288 tropo 21 gas: ph 7.11 po2: 265 pco2: 45 hco3: 14.3 be 14 cai: 1.15 k: 4.8 na: 136 05h: lactate: 3.9 02/08 14h: lactate: 4.1 u: 76 cr: 2.65 tgo: 39 tgp: 16 bt: 1.1 hb: 14.3 ht: 44 pqt: 203 gb: 3.9 venous gas: ph: 7.19 po2: 20.9 pco3: 46.5 hco3: 17.6 be: 10 na: 137 k: 3.8 pcr: 26.4 amylase: 299 urinalysis: cloudy, bilirubin, ptn, Refractory shock. Admission: 01/08/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 03/08/2020, stay: 01 d 19 h, bed: ue general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. The patient referred to zero vacancy, coming from , in an advanced ambulance, accompanied by the patient, who reports that the patient was evaluated approximately 20 days ago at an emergency care unit in , being diagnosed with acute heart attack (myocardial infarction) and released on the same day, sic. Reports that the patient was admitted on 01/08 due to severe epigastrium pain, with no improvement with the use of analgesics, and investigation was carried out with ECG/heart tracing (electrocardiogram), laboratory tests and chest x-ray. Reports that the x-ray showed mediastinal widening compatible with thoracoabdominal aortic widening. The image is not included in the exams brought by the patient. It also states that the patient was admitted with blood pressure of 180x100 mmhg, showing regression to levels of 130x80 mmhg. When questioned, the patient denies precordialgia, denies irradiating abdominal pain, denies cervical pain, denies pain in the upper limbs, denies nausea, denies vomiting, denies diarrhea or other symptoms. Evaluated with an imaging exam, which ruled out the diagnostic hypothesis of referral, it was decided to maintain hospitalization for further investigation. He evolved with clinical worsening during hospitalization, being collected infectious screening, initiated empirical infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) with ceftriaxone and metronidazole, volume expansion and opted for serial physical examination. In the early morning of 03/08 he evolved with shock, requiring orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation), machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation) and initiation of vasoactive drugs. Progression to refractory shock measures was necessary. Infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) was escalated on 03/08 given clinical worsening. The patient evolved with refractoriness to the instituted clinical measures, evolving to death in . Physical examination upon admission: the patient in good general condition, lucid, oriented, communicative and collaborative vital signs: oxygen level in blood (saturation) 93% on room air heart rate 104 right upper limb blood pressure 125x88 left upper limb blood pressure 121x87. Flat abdomen, bowel sounds, flaccid, painful on deep palpation in the epigastrium and flanks, voluntary defense, no palpable pulsatile masses, negative db. Extremities: upper limbs: symmetrical, preserved and full pulses lower limbs: left: fe / pop / ta / prothrombin time right: fe / póp / ta / prothrombin time preserved capillary refill time. 03/08: 01h: lactate: 3.4 hb: 14.5 gb: 5.7 30% sticks pqt: 203 ckmb: 28 amylase: 288 trope 21 gas: ph 7.11 po2: 265 pco2: 45 hco3: 14.3 be 14 falls: 1.15 k: 4.8 na: 136 05h: lactate: 3.9 02/08 2pm: lactate: 4.1 u: 76 cr: 2.65 tgo: 39 tgp: 16 bt: 1.1 hb: 14.3 ht: 44 pqt: 203 gb: 3.9 given through a vein (drip/IV) gas: ph: 7.19 po2: 20.9 pco3: 46.5 hco3: 17.6 be: 10 na: 137 k: 3.8 pcr: 26.4 amylase: 299 urinalysis: cloudy, liver function marker (bilirubin), ptn, nitrite, heme epithelial cells various intense bacteriuria 08/01 hb 14.9 / gb 6.6 / platelets 189 / na 138 / k 4.2 / ur 51 / cr 1.34 / prothrombin time 1.18 / aptd 0.96 definitive angiotc 08/01 without signs of arterial dissection. Mediastinal, abdominal and retroperitoneal lymphonopathy. Consolidation in the left lower lobe associated with cavities, the possibility of neoplasia or infectious/inflammatory process should be considered. Small volume fluid buildup in abdomen (ascites).. 03/08: 01h: lactate: 3.4 hb: 14.5 gb: 5.7 30% pqt rods: 203 ckmb: 28 amylase: 288 tropo 21 gas: ph 7.11 po2: 265 pco2: 45 hco3: 14.3 be 14 cai: 1.15 k: 4.8 na: 136 05h: lactate: 3.9 02/08 14h: lactate: 4.1 u: 76 cr: 2.65 tgo: 39 tgp: 16 bt: 1.1 hb: 14.3 ht: 44 pqt: 203 gb: 3.9 venous gas: ph: 7.19 po2: 20.9 pco3: 46.5 hco3: 17.6 be: 10 na: 137 k: 3.8 pcr: 26.4 amylase: 299 urinalysis: cloudy, liver function marker (bilirubin), ptn, nitrite, heme epithelial cells various intense bacteriuria 01/08 hb 14.9 / gb 6.6 / platelets 189 / na 138 / k 4.2 / ur 51 / cr 1.34 / prothrombin time 1.18 / apt_pt 0.96 definitive angiotc 01/08 without signs of arterial dissection. Consolidation in the left lower lobe associated with cavities, the possibility of neoplasia or infectious/inflammatory process should be considered. Small volume fluid buildup in abdomen (ascites). 44 pneumonia. septic shock of pulmonary focus. admission: 2020-04-28, discharge: 2020-05-10, stay: 12 d 03 h, location: ward, payer: sus. patient develops shock of pulmonary focus, loss of access, it was decided to suspend invasive measures and prioritize palliative care. 85 years old; family members report being 90 years old, coming from [address]; admitted coronavirus red room: 2020-04-28, admitted to intensive care unit: 28/04, semi-intensive admission on: 09/05. problem: pulmonary sepsis due to exacerbated chronic obstructive pulmonary disease? 1 acute respiratory failure, negative coronavirus, methylprednisolone 40 mg/day for 5 days, amoxicillin, clavulin, clarithromycin, oseltamivir, started 04/28 oxacillin 05/02 growth of oxasensitive staphylococcus aureus in tracheal secretion culture, pulmonary thromboembolism ruled out, 2 left femur fracture fall from standing height on 04/24, fracture diagnosis made on 04/27, after onset of dyspnea, surgical approach scheduled, 3 acute kidney injury kdigo 2 prerenal? clcr 04/28: 22.24 ml/min/1.73 m² conservative treatment, 4 new left occipital ischemic stroke 5 previous ischemic stroke 6 heart failure with normal ejection fraction, transthoracic echocardiography showing significant left atrial dilation, marked diastolic dysfunction 7 palliative care defined by the palliative care team at a family meeting on 05/04, personal history: chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, bilateral hearing impairment, right lower limb fracture in a car accident 15 years ago, case summary: [person] already in proportional palliative care, however without the possibility of being transferred to a ward bed due to the use of norepinephrine. admitted to {organization} on 04/28 with orotracheal intubation due to probable pulmonary sepsis, by [organization]. sought [organization] after falling from his own height. external investigation revealed pulmonary infection. exacerbated chronic obstructive pulmonary disease? with respiratory failure and need for intubation. Regulated to {organization}, admitted to [organization] where he was started on ceftriaxone, clarithomycin, oseltamivir and acute respiratory failure protocol. Coronavirus test on 04/28 negative. Antibiotic therapy was transitioned to clavulin. Unfractionated heparin was started due to suspicion of pulmonary thromboembolism due to report of possible fracture in the left femur and renal failure. CT Angiography (CTA) was performed on 04/29 which ruled out pulmonary thromboembolism. Computerized tomography of the skull was performed on the same day which showed recent left occipital cerebral infarction. Due to growth of oxa-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus in tracheal aspirate, antibiotic therapy was changed to oxacillin on 05/02. held family meeting by [organization] on 04/05 aligned with family about limiting additional advanced life support measures {omitted}. poor condition, poorly perfused. [person] died {omitted} Lung infection (pneumonia). Severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) of pulmonary focus. Admission: 2020-04-28, sent home from hospital (discharge): 2020-05-10, stay: 12 d 03 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Clcr 04/28: 22.24 ml/min/1.73 m² conservative treatment, 4 new left occipital reduced blood supply (ischemic) stroke 5 previous reduced blood supply (ischemic) stroke 6 heart failure with normal ejection fraction, transthoracic echocardiography showing significant left atrial dilation, marked diastolic dysfunction 7 comfort-focused (not curative) care defined by the comfort-focused (not curative) care team at a family meeting on 05/04, personal history: chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, bilateral hearing impairment, right lower limb fracture in a car accident 15 years ago, case summary: the patient already in proportional comfort-focused (not curative) care, however without the possibility of being transferred to a general hospital room (ward) bed due to the use of norepinephrine. Admitted to the hospital on 04/28 with orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) due to probable pulmonary serious blood infection (sepsis), by [organization]. Sought [organization] after falling from his own height. External investigation revealed pulmonary infection. Exacerbated chronic obstructive pulmonary disease? With lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) and need for breathing tube placed in throat (intubation). Regulated to the hospital, admitted to [organization] where he was started on ceftriaxone, clarithomycin, oseltamivir and acute lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) protocol. Coronavirus test on 04/28 negative. Infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) was transitioned to clavulin. Held family meeting by [organization] on 04/05 aligned with family about limiting additional advanced life support measures . Poor condition, poorly perfused. The patient died Lung infection (pneumonia) is a serious condition that causes pulmonary focus. Admission: 2020-04-28, sent home from hospital (discharge): 2020-10-10, stay: 12 d 03 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. When a patient develops shock and loss of access, it was decided to government health schemepend invasive measures and prioritize comfort-focused (not curative) care. 45 [person] 87 years old. admission: 2020-12-03, discharge: 2021-01-07, stay: 01 m 04 d 03 h, location: ward, payer: sus. patient with onset of coronavirus symptoms and positive pcr on {omitted} december/2020, with sepsis of pulmonary focus and acute on chronic kidney injury, hepatitis to be clarified with significant increase in bilirubin and worsening of liver function. in refractory septic shock, died on {omitted}. patient in serious general condition, jaundiced /4, hydrated, acyanotic, hypothermic using a thermal blanket, on mechanical ventilation, using high doses of vasoactive drugs, afebrile, pulmonary auscultation: bilateral vesicular breath sounds with diffuse rhonchi; cardiac auscultation: normal rhythmic heart sounds without murmurs or es; abdomen: protuberant, distended, palpable mass in the supraumbilical region, umbilical hernia; lower limbs: edema of /4, free calves, upper limbs edema of /4. [person] with worsening of blood gas analysis with metabolic and respiratory acidosis, prolonged prothrombin time and altered liver function. [person] underwent ultrasound to evaluate the abdomen. awaiting compensation for hemodynamic instability and completion of antibiotic therapy. antibiotic therapy and reversal of septic shock of pulmonary focus and resolution of hepatitis to be clarified. death. 46 sacral pressure ulcer sacral osteomyelitis. admission: 05/01/2021, discharge: 02/19/2021, stay: 01 m 13 d 15 h, location: ward payer: sus. personal history: myelomeningocele with neurological complications: hydrocephalus requiring ventriculoperitoneal shunt paraplegia neurogenic bladder requiring cystostomy lower limb lymphedema grade 3 obesity pulmonary thromboembolism: low embolic burden 10% in 2019 previous hospitalization at this institution 10/31/2020 12/05/2020, including the need for admission to an intensive care unit due to respiratory failure septic shock with urinary and cutaneous focus sacral pressure injury grade 4. continuous-use medications: warfarin 1 tablet daily. Case report: Reports that 2 weeks after admission, he did not perform wound care because [person] was not present and noticed a necrotic point in the ulcer and yellowish secretion, without foul odor, in addition to fever spikes measured 1 day ago. Denies other infectious symptoms. Denies burning when urinating or change in urine color and odor. Reports preserved bowel habits, however. Reports self-limiting diarrhea for 2 weeks. Denies bleeding. Denies cough, dyspnea. Reports pain in the sacral region of mild intensity. Denies other complaints. Diagnostic hypotheses: 1 Sacral ulcer infection: [person] upon admission presented with fever spikes, elevated CRP and leukocytosis, with ulcer presenting yellowish and foul-smelling secretion. 2 Infectious diarrhea?: [person] presented diarrhea again at the beginning of hospitalization, 3 Osteomyelitis, 4 Anticoagulation due to previous pulmonary thromboembolism. antibiotic therapy: oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg 8/8 hourly d0: 01/27 oral clindamycin 600 mg 8/8 hourly d0: 01/27 introduced for empirical treatment of osteomyelitis cefepime vancomycin 01/05 01/27: started due to an infected sacral ulcer. considering recent prolonged hospitalization in this service, broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy was chosen. this was maintained for 3 weeks due to the subsequent finding of associated osteomyelitis metronidazole 01/05 01/12: infectious diarrhea complete resolution. cultures: blood culture 01/06: negative urine culture 01/06: negative relevant tests: computed tomography of the pelvis 01/10: signs of osteomyelitis of the coccygeal bones magnetic resonance imaging of the lumbar spine and pelvis: not possible to be performed due to the [person]'s abdominal circumference. Sacral bone biopsy 12/02: dense connective tissue with exudative chronic inflammatory process, fibrosis, reactive capillary proliferation and foci of osteocartilaginous metaplasia. good general condition, flushed, hydrated, anicteric, acyanotic. afebrile. eupneic. alert, oriented. blood pressure 110x70 mmHg heart rate 8normal S1 and S2 per minute SO2 97% room air respiratory auscultation: bilateral vesicular breath sounds present without adventitious sounds, no signs of respiratory distress cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm in two beats normal phonetic sounds, no audible murmurs. abdomen: globular, normotensive, bowel sounds, painless on palpation, no masses ext: lymphatic edema 4/4 of lower limbs, no signs of deep vein thrombosis. Adequate peripheral perfusion. Grade 4 sacral ulcer, with no secretion output and apparent bone exposure, gradually improving. Sacral biopsy. Osteomyelitis: Discussed with [person] from [organization] who advises: Continue treatment with oral antibiotics for osteomyelitis (ciprofloxacin and clindamycin) until 8 weeks, which can be performed at home. Refer to [organization] for ulcer follow-up and dressings. Discussed new cultures from 02/11. We consider it as contamination. Given the clinical and laboratory improvement, demonstrating the effectiveness of the current treatment, we opted to maintain the established antibiotic therapy schedule. [person] in conditions for hospital discharge. We plan to finish oral antibiotic therapy at home until 8 weeks. 02/23. I prepare a report for follow-up by the [organization] team and [organization] for periodic evaluation. Check sacral bone biopsy. Pressure ulcer. Change dressing on sacral lesion twice a day. Clinical and infectious surveillance. We advise local care. I request follow-up with the multidisciplinary team at [organization] for anticoagulation for pulmonary thromboembolism. I am maintaining the dose of warfarin at 5 mg/day, since it was already the previous dose and good controls during hospitalization. [person] is able to suspend enoxaparin and discharge from hospital. I request follow-up for anticoagulation adjustment and prothrombin time measurement at [organization]. I am reporting to the team. Follow-up with the team at [organization] and [organization]. I have scheduled a follow-up appointment at [organization]. I will provide guidance on ulcer care and dressing. I will complete oral antibiotic therapy for 8 weeks. I will provide guidance on ulcer care and dressing. I will complete oral antibiotic therapy for 8 weeks. In good general condition, stable, ulcer is progressing well. Sacral pressure ulcer sacral bone infection (osteomyelitis). Admission: 05/01/2021, sent home from hospital (discharge): 02/19/2021, stay: 01 m 13 d 15 h, location: general hospital room (ward) payer: government health scheme. Personal history: myelomeningocele with neurological complications: hydrocephalus requiring ventriculoperitoneal shunt paraplegia neurogenic bladder requiring cystostomy lower limb lymphswelling grade 3 obesity pulmonary thromboblood clot blocking a vessel (embolism): low embolic burden 10% in 2019 previous hospitalization at this institution 10/31/2020 12/05/2020, including the need for admission to an ICU (serious care room) due to lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) with urinary and cutaneous focus sacral pressure injury grade 4. Continuous-use medications: warfarin 1 tablet daily. Case report: reports that 2 weeks after admission, he did not perform wound care because the patient was not present and noticed a necrotic point in the ulcer and yellowish secretion, without foul odor, in addition to fever spikes measured 1 day ago. Denies other infectious symptoms. Denies burning when urinating or change in urine color and odor. Reports preserved bowel habits, however. Reports self-limiting diarrhea for 2 weeks. Denies bleeding. Denies cough, difficulty in breathing (dyspnea). Reports pain in the sacral region of mild intensity. Denies other complaints. Diagnostic hypotheses: 1 sacral ulcer infection: the patient upon admission presented with fever spikes, elevated crp and high white blood cell count (leukocytosis), with ulcer presenting yellowish and foul-smelling secretion. 2 infectious diarrhea?: the patient presented diarrhea again at the beginning of hospitalization, 3 bone infection (osteomyelitis), 4 anticoagulation due to previous pulmonary thromboblood clot blocking a vessel (embolism). Infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy): oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg 8/8 hourly d0: 01/27 oral clindamycin 600 mg 8/8 hourly d0: 01/27 introduced for empirical treatment of bone infection (osteomyelitis) cefepime vancomycin 01/05 01/27: started due to an infected sacral ulcer. Considering recent prolonged hospitalization in this service, broad-spectrum infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) was chosen. This was maintained for 3 weeks due to the subsequent finding of associated bone infection (osteomyelitis) metronidazole 01/05 01/12: infectious diarrhea complete resolution. Cultures: blood test to find infection (blood culture) 01/06: negative urine culture 01/06: negative relevant tests: CT scan (computed tomography) of the pelvis 01/10: signs of bone infection (osteomyelitis) of the coccygeal bones MRI scan (magnetic resonance) imaging of the lumbar spine and pelvis: not possible to be performed due to the the patient's abdominal circumference. Sacral bone biopsy 12/02: dense connective tissue with exudative chronic inflammatory process, fibrosis, reactive capillary proliferation and foci of osteocartilaginous metaplasia. Good general condition, flushed, hydrated, no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no bluish discoloration (acyanotic). No fever (afebrile). Breathing normally (eupneic). Alert, oriented. Blood pressure 110x70 mmhg heart rate 8normal s1 and s2 per minute so2 97% room air respiratory auscultation: bilateral normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds) present without adventitious sounds, no signs of respiratory distress cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm in two beats normal phonetic sounds, no audible murmurs. Abdomen: globular, normotensive, bowel sounds, painless on palpation, no masses ext: lymphatic swelling (edema) 4/4 of lower limbs, no signs of deep vein blood clot (thrombosis). Adequate peripheral perfusion. Grade 4 sacral ulcer, with no secretion output and apparent bone exposure, gradually improving. Sacral biopsy. Bone infection (osteomyelitis): discussed with the patient from [organization] who advises: continue treatment with oral infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic)s for bone infection (osteomyelitis) (ciprofloxacin and clindamycin) until 8 weeks, which can be performed at home. Refer to [organization] for ulcer return visit to doctor (follow-up) and dressings. Discussed new cultures from 02/11. We consider it as contamination. Given the clinical and laboratory improvement, demonstrating the effectiveness of the current treatment, we opted to maintain the established infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) schedule. The patient in conditions for hospital sent home from hospital (discharge). We plan to finish oral infection-fighting medicine treatment at home until 8 weeks. 02/23. I prepare a report for return visit to doctor (follow-up) by the [organization] team and [organization] for periodic evaluation. Check sacral bone biopsy. Pressure ulcer. Change dressing on sacral lesion twice a day. Clinical and infectious surveillance. We advise local care. I request return visit to doctor (follow-up) with the multidisciplinary team at [organization] for anticoagulation for pulmonary thromboblood clot blocking a vessel (embolism). I am maintaining the dose of warfarin at 5 mg/day, since it was already the previous dose and good controls during hospitalization. The patient is able to government health schemepend enoxaparin and sent home from hospital (discharge) from hospital. I request return visit to doctor (follow-up) for anticoagulation adjustment and prothrombin time measurement at [organization]. I am reporting to the team. Return visit to doctor (follow-up) with the team at [organization] and [organization]. I have scheduled a return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment at [organization]. I will provide guidance on ulcer care and dressing. I will complete oral infection-fighting medicine treatment for 8 weeks. I will provide guidance on ulcer care and dressing. I will complete oral infection-fighting medicine treatment for 8 weeks. In good general condition, stable, ulcer is progressing well. Sacral pressure ulcer sacral bone infection (osteomyelitis). Admission: 05/01/2021, sent home from hospital (discharge): 02/19/2021, stay: 01 m 13 d 15 h, location: general hospital room (ward) payer: government health scheme. Personal history: myelomeningocele with neurological complications: hydrocephalus requiring ventriculoperitoneal shunt paraplegia neurogenic bladder requiring cystostomy lower limb lymphswelling grade 3 obesity pulmonary thromboblood clot blocking a vessel (embolism): low embolic burden 10% in 2019 previous hospitalization at this institution 10/31/2020 12/05/2020, including the need for admission to an ICU (serious care room) due to lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) with urinary and cutaneous focus sacral pressure injury grade 4. Continuous-use medications: warfarin 1 tablet daily. Case report: reports that 2 weeks after admission, he did not perform wound care because the patient was not present and noticed a necrotic point in the ulcer and yellowish secretion, without foul odor, in addition to fever spikes measured 1 day ago. Denies other infectious symptoms. Denies burning when urinating or change in urine color and odor. Reports preserved bowel habits, however. Reports self-limiting diarrhea for 2 weeks. Denies bleeding. Denies cough, difficulty in breathing (dyspnea). Reports pain in the sacral region of mild intensity. Denies other complaints. Diagnostic hypotheses: 1 sacral ulcer infection: the patient upon admission presented with fever spikes, elevated crp and high white blood cell count (leukocytosis), with ulcer presenting yellowish and foul-smelling secretion. 2 infectious diarrhea?: the patient presented diarrhea again at the beginning of hospitalization, 3 bone infection (osteomyelitis), 4 anticoagulation due to previous pulmonary thromboblood clot blocking a vessel (embolism). Infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy): oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg 8/8 hourly d0: 01/27 oral clindamycin 600 mg 8/8 hourly d0: 01/27 introduced for empirical treatment of bone infection (osteomyelitis) cefepime vancomycin 01/05 01/27: started due to an infected sacral ulcer. Considering recent prolonged hospitalization in this service, broad-spectrum infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) was chosen. This was maintained for 3 weeks due to the subsequent finding of associated bone infection (osteomyelitis) metronidazole 01/05 01/12: infectious diarrhea complete resolution. Cultures: blood test to find infection (blood culture) 01/06: negative urine culture 01/06: negative relevant tests: CT scan (computed tomography) of the pelvis 01/10: signs of bone infection (osteomyelitis) of the coccygeal bones MRI scan (magnetic resonance) imaging of the lumbar spine and pelvis: not possible to be performed due to the the patient's abdominal circumference. Sacral bone biopsy 12/02: dense connective tissue with exudative chronic inflammatory process, fibrosis, reactive capillary proliferation and foci of osteocartilaginous metaplasia. Good general condition, flushed, hydrated, no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no bluish discoloration (acyanotic). No fever (afebrile). Breathing normally (eupneic). Alert, oriented. Abdomen: globular, normotensive, bowel sounds, painless on palpation, no masses ext: lymphatic swelling (edema) 4/4 of lower limbs, no signs of deep vein blood clot (thrombosis). 48 [person], 59 years old, admission: 02/08/2020, discharge: 05/08/2020, stay: 03 d 11 h, location: ward, payer: SUS. problem: 1. obstructive acute abdomen abdominal tomography: distention of small bowel loops by fluid content and colon by fecal content, without obstructive points identified by the stoma with resolution of the condition, 2. sepsis without a defined focus, probable pulmonary focus 08/03/2020: patient developed hypotension, slowing, requiring initiation of vasoactive drug norepinephrine, electrocardiogram sinus tachycardia, volume expansion was performed 1000 ml ringer lactate and antibiotic therapy was started, chest x-ray showed consolidation in the left base 08/03/2020: blood culture: awaiting results, antibiotic therapy: ceftriaxone 08/03/2020 and metronidazole 08/03/2020 antibiotic therapy: ceftriaxone in 08/03 metronidazole in 08/03 cultures: blood culture 08/03: negative until current, devices: central venous catheter left internal jugular vein 08/03 08/05, history of current illness, reports nausea and vomiting with food debris that started 2 days ago, associated with cessation of stool and flatus elimination through the colostomy 1 day ago and diffuse abdominal discomfort, mainly in the epigastrium. does not urinate, [person] chronic kidney disease dialysis. has not eaten since yesterday. denies fever, comorbidities systemic arterial hypertension, chronic kidney disease dialysis 3 weeks since 2011, human immunodeficiency virus, previous surgeries: rectosigmoidectomy colostomy Haartman for ischemic colitis 2010, surgical drainage of periostomy collection 2019. hospitalization exams: abdominal and pelvic tomography 08/02/2020: post-Hartmann colectomy status with large parasternal hernia. distension of small bowel loops predominantly in the left hemiabdomen, but without identified transition points. signs of an inflammatory process in the mesentery at the level of the mesogastrium, nonspecific, characterized by blurring of the adipose planes. signs of chronic nephropathy on the right. absence of the left kidney. atheromatosis of the aorta and its main branches. post-cholecystectomy status. previous surgeries: rectosigmoidectomy, colostomy, Haartman's test for ischemic colitis 2010; surgical drainage of periostomy collection 2019. as defined: [person] presenting significant improvement in the condition, for more than 24 hours without vasoactive drugs and eupneic in room air without the need for supplemental oxygen therapy. I deescalate ceftriaxone to oral clavulin and maintain oral metronidazole to complete the antibiotic therapy regimen at home. seen {omitted} and improvement in [person] clinical condition, I discharge him from hospital with a follow-up appointment visit scheduled for 08/28 at ASID. [person] is already being monitored at an external hemodialysis clinic with an appointment for Monday. I am maintaining continuous use of medications. hospital discharge. I am deescalating ceftriaxone to oral clavulin and maintaining oral metronidazole to complete the antibiotic therapy regimen at home until completing d7, already corrected for renal function with a dose after hemodialysis. improved.
