Obstructive acute abdomen abdominal tomography: distention of small bowel loops by fluid content and colon by fecal content, without obstructive points identified by the stoma with resolution of the condition, 2. Serious blood infection (sepsis) without a defined focus, probable pulmonary focus 08/03/2020: patient developed low BP (hypotension), slowing, requiring initiation of vasoactive drug norepinephrine, ECG/heart tracing (electrocardiogram) sinus fast heartbeat (tachycardia), volume expansion was performed 1000 ml ringer lactate and infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) was started, chest x-ray showed consolidation in the left base 08/03/2020: blood test to find infection (blood culture): awaiting results, infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy): ceftriaxone 08/03/2020 and metronidazole 08/03/2020 infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy): ceftriaxone in 08/03 metronidazole in 08/03 cultures: blood test to find infection (blood culture) 08/03: negative until current, devices: central venous catheter left internal jugular vein 08/03 08/05, history of current illness, reports nausea and vomiting with food debris that started 2 days ago, associated with cessation of stool and flatus elimination through the colostomy 1 day ago and diffuse abdominal discomfort, mainly in the epigastrium. Does not urinate, the patient chronic kidney problem (kidney disease) dialysis. Systemic arterial high BP not eaten since yesterday. Denies fever, comorbidities systemic arterial high BP (hypertension), chronic kidney problem (kidney disease) dialysis 3 weeks since 2011, human immunodeficiency virus, previous surgeries: rectosigmoidectomy colostomy haartman for reduced blood supply (ischemic) colitis 2010, surgical drainage of periostomy collection 2019. Hospitalization exams: abdominal and pelvic tomography 08/02/2020: post-hartmann colectomy status with large parasternal hernia. Distension of small bowel loops predominantly in the left hemiabdomen, but without identified transition points. Signs of an inflammatory process in the mesentery at the level of the mesogastrium, nonspecific, characterized by blurring of the adipose planes. Signs of chronic nephropathy on the right. Absence of the left kidney. Atheromatosis of the aorta and its main branches. Post-cholecystectomy status. Previous surgeries: rectosigmoidectomy, colostomy, haartman's test for reduced blood supply (ischemic) colitis 2010; surgical drainage of periostomy collection 2019. As defined: the patient presenting significant improvement in the condition, for more than 24 hours without vasoactive drugs and breathing normally (eupneic) in room air without the need for supplemental oxygen therapy. I deescalate ceftriaxone to oral clavulin and maintain oral metronidazole to complete the infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) regimen at home. Seen and improvement in the patient clinical condition, i sent home from hospital (discharge) him from hospital with a return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment visit scheduled for 08/28 at asid. The patient is already being monitored at an external kidney dialysis (hemodialysis) clinic with an appointment for monday. I am maintaining continuous use of medications. Hospital sent home from hospital (discharge). Hospitalization exams: abdominal and pelvic tomography 08/02/2020: post-hartmann colectomy status with large parasternal hernia. Distension of small bowel loops predominantly in the left hemiabdomen, but without identified transition points. Signs of an inflammatory process in the mesentery at the level of the mesogastrium, nonspecific, characterized by blurring of the adipose planes. Signs of chronic nephropathy on the right. Absence of the left kidney. Atheromatosis of the aorta and its main branches. Post-cholecystectomy status. Previous surgeries: rectosigmoidectomy, colostomy, haartman's test for reduced blood supply (ischemic) colitis 2010; surgical drainage of periostomy collection 2019. As defined: the patient presenting significant improvement in the condition, for more than 24 hours without vasoactive drugs and breathing normally (eupneic) in room air without the need for supplemental oxygen therapy. I deescalate ceftriaxone to oral clavulin and maintain oral metronidazole to complete the infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) regimen at home. I am maintaining continuous use of medications and maintaining a healthy lifestyle. This means that it's important to keep up with the patient's medication. Left leg: skin with morphological and immunohistochemical findings compatible with leiomyosarcoma associated with superficial and deep chronic dermatitis, extensively ulcerated with fibroblastic proliferation, capillaries, thickening and hyalinization of collagen fibers. Resuscitation protocol initiated, arrest team arrives on site with procedure in progress, approximately 1 minute later. Compressions maintained and arrest rhythm measured as per protocol. Blood culture positive for Staphylococcus aureus on 05/14. Admission February, March. Pulse CYC 02/26. Methylprednisolone 1 g on 02/23, 24, and 25. Prednisone 1 mg/kg/day on 02/20, reduced to 0.4 mg/kg/day on 03/02 due to positive blood culture 03/02. Weekly vitamin B12 replacement 5000 IU IM on Saturdays, beginning 02/26. 03/02: fever spikes and positive blood culture with [person]. Septic arthritis in left elbow and left ankle. Human immunoglobulin beginning first dose 03/04 03/08, total 140 g. Second dose 03/30 03/31, total 140 g. Aiming to control hemolytic anemia with the least possible immunosuppression, given the infection that started on 03/02. Transfusion of packed red blood cells Days: 27/02 09/03 10/03 CCIH guidance 23/03: need to use intravenous daptomycin for 14 days after the first negative blood culture 16/03. After that, considering septic arthritis, treatment can be done with oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for another 14 days. Levofloxacin can be discontinued, since the doses for treatment of pneumonia have already been completed. tenosynovitis of the extensor digitorum longus non-insertional Achilles tendinopathy. shoulder MRI 03/18: findings compatible with the hypothesis of osteomyelitis, glenohumeral septic arthritis. signs of rotator cuff tendinopathy. associated foci of intrasubstantial rupture in the supraspinatus and subscapularis tendons. hospitalization in May: edema hyperemia on the lateral side of the right lower limb asthenia fever arthralgia in the shoulder and signs of thrombophlebitis on ultrasound cellulitis? reactivation of septic arthritis? osteomyelitis? vancomycin started on 04/28 {omitted} blood culture on 05/14 positive for staphylococcus aureus oxar complications during hospitalization: acute on chronic kidney disease, requiring dialysis currently stable hit depressive symptoms adjustment disorder herpes esophagitis treated with 20 days of intravenous acyclovir 350 mg/day pellagra? scaly lesions on lower limbs confusional state improvement after nicotinic acid? magnetic resonance imaging on 05/21 showing signs of glenohumeral synovitis with bone extension osteomyelitis surgical approach by orthopedics on 06/01, evolving with improvement in pain and without fever spikes, summary antibiotic therapy in this hospitalization: vancomycin d0 04/28 06/07 teicoplanin d0 06/07 do until 06/29 therapeutic schedule for osteomyelitis: [organization] [person] advises: [person] presented nephrotoxicity due to vancomycin, which was replaced by teicoplanin, which should be maintained until 4 weeks after surgical debridement. [person] remained afebrile with teicoplanin, with improving renal function, reaching creatinine clearance 50. therefore, I request special medication of teicoplanin for dehospitalization, to be administered in a basic health unit, at a dose of 400 mg 1x/day IV until 06/29. 53 [person], 62 years old. admission: 07/12/2020, discharge: 14/08/2020, stay: 01 m 01 d 12 h, location: ward, payer: healthcare system. problem: 1. severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus - associated pneumonia? onset of symptoms: 07/03 worsening: 07/09 orotracheal intubation, mechanical ventilation initiated: 07/12 proning: 07/15 2x, 3h interval 07/15: post-proning extubation reintubation without complications 07/22: new proning orotracheal tube exchange on 07/24 for tracheostomy stopper on 07/28 massive bleeding on 07/30 assessed by head and neck surgery, compressive dressing performed 2. intergluteal cellulitis with intense inflamation without collection on ultrasound of the gluteal region 07/16 3. pneumonia associated with mechanical ventilation: worsening of the ventilatory pattern and tracheal secretion, tracheal aspirate on 07/18 with acinetobacter multir, antibiotic therapy escalated to polymyxin and gentamicin, severe bronchospasm on 07/22 with recurrence on 08/03, weaning from mechanical ventilation to tracheal mask on 08/07 4. acute kidney injury kadigo 3 07/27 nephrotoxicity? infection? follow-up with nephrology start of hemodialysis on 07/28 5. abdominal distension 07/29 simethicone and laxatives, nasogastric tube in drainage intestinal lavage on 08/11 with success. poor general condition, severe hypotension, blood pressure 6x4 mmHg, with high dose of norepinephrine, vesicular breath sounds with sparse rhonchi, under mechanical ventilation, globular abdomen, distended nervous system: sedation agitation scale 5. cultures: blood cultures: 08/06: staphylococcus epidermidis oxar 07/12: negative 07/22: negative, tracheal aspirate 08/07: negative 07/22: negative 07/16: acinetobacter multir, sensitive to gentamicin and amikacin urine culture 08/06: pseudomonas multis 07/28: candida tropicalis 07/31: candida tropicalis. tracheostomy on 07/28, massive bleeding on 07/30, assessed by head and neck surgery, compressive dressing applied. death. evolves with refractory septic shock. 54 [person], 45 years old, from [address] estimated weight kg: 120 kg predicted weight kg: 80 kg height cm: 180 cm vt 4 to 6 ml/kg: 300 to 450, admission: 06/17/2020, discharge: 07/01/2020, stay: 14 d 13 h, location: ward, payer: sus. problem: 1 acute respiratory syndrome secondary to coronavirus, onset of symptoms 06/03: myalgia, unmeasured fever, headache, dyspnea, persistence of symptoms 06/13: previous dysuria, hematuria, nausea and vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhea, worsening of symptoms 06/16: worsening of dyspnea and start of oxygen therapy, orotracheal intubation for acute respiratory failure 06/17 06/24, rtpcr coronavirus 06/12: positive rtpcr coronavirus [organization] 06/17: positive rtpcr influenza [organization] 06/17: negative, history of current illness: [person], with no other previous diagnoses, reports that he started with unmeasured fever, myalgia, headache, and mild dyspnea approximately 15 days after admission. evolved after 05 days with abdominal discomfort, nausea and vomiting, diarrhea, in addition to dysuria and hematuria, associated with the persistence of the previous symptoms, which led to seeking medical attention on 06/13, when a positive RT-PCR test for coronavirus was collected and released for home isolation. reports that he presented significant worsening of dyspnea the following day, leading him to seek medical attention again, when it was noted that he needed oxygen therapy, saturating 80% in room air and 96% in a mask with a reservoir at 15 l/min, and was then referred to [organization]. upon admission to [organization], [person] was hemodynamically stable, tachypneic, using a 15 l/min reservoir mask, without the use of accessory muscles. medical history: obesity, denied other comorbidities, medical history: denied previous surgeries, denied drug allergies, denied use of medications, lifestyle habits: denied smoking and alcoholism; evolution during hospitalization: [person] admitted to [organization] on 06/17, started antibiotic therapy with ceftriaxone and clarithromycin, methylprednisolone and unfractionated heparin. evolved with hemodynamic instability and maintenance of tachydyspnea and need for supplemental oxygen, having been made central venous access to start vasoactive drug and orotracheal intubation performed and later referred to the intensive care unit. upon admission to the intensive care unit, [person] was hemodynamically stable, vasoactive drug was discontinued on the same day and proning position was started. in the intensive care unit, presented hypernatremia and prerenal azotemia, which were managed and resolved. The first attempt at extubation was made on 04/23, however [person] developed significant psychomotor agitation and was only extubated on 04/24. He was kept on supplemental oxygen with a nasal catheter for 48 h and was admitted to [organization] on 04/26. Weaning of drugs initiated due to psychomotor agitation presented in the intensive care unit was initiated, with [person] evolving with significant clinical improvement, improvement in drowsiness and dyspnea. At the moment, [person] is clinically and hemodynamically stable, continuing to require supplemental oxygen with a nasal catheter 1 L/min with saturation 95%, maintaining mild drowsiness, admission his eyes only when called, without complaints. current antimicrobials: no previous antimicrobial use: acyclovir for genital herpes d1 06/21 d7 06/27 ceftriaxone d1 06/17 to 06/25 clarithromycin d1 06/17 to 06/25 coronavirus protocol: methylprednisolone 40 mg 12/12 h 06/17 06/19 unfractionated heparin 7500iu 08/08 h {omitted}: no previous devices: central venous catheter subclavian vein 06/17 nasoenteric tube 06/17. physical examination: good general condition, flushed, hydrated, akinetic, anicteric, afebrile to the touch neurology: vigilant, lucid and oriented in time and space, logical and coherent speech, isochoric and photoreactive pupils, preserved extrinsic ocular motricity, strength in the 4 limbs apparently preserved, [person] restrained in bed, which hinders a better neurological examination respiratory auscultation: symmetrical expansibility, vesicular breath sounds present and symmetrical, reduced in both bases, no adventitious sounds abdomen: globular, normotensive, depressed, bowel sounds present and normal, tympanic to percussion, painless to palpation, no pain on sudden decompression, no masses or visceromegaly palpable lower limbs: warm, capillary refill time less than 3s, palpable and symmetrical pulses, no edema and no signs of deep vein thrombosis. cultures: blood culture 06/17: negative urine culture 06/17: negative tracheal aspirate 06/17: negative. central venous access orotracheal intubation. regarding hyperglycemia: [person] without a previous diagnosis of diabetes mellitus, however, maintaining hyperglycemia during the hospitalization period, refer [person] for outpatient diagnostic clarification. Regarding respiratory status: [person] was kept with a nasal catheter at 1 l/min, given the drop in saturation after an attempt to remove it, he was kept without antibiotics or corticosteroids, and he was prescribed unfractionated heparin 7,500 IU every 8 hours. Regarding drowsiness: a head tomography scan showed no signs of acute bleeding, awaiting a definitive report, and drowsiness was considered secondary to the effects of the prescribed medications, already being weaned, since [person] did not present any other neurological findings. [person] was taking methadone, quetiapine, and lorazepam. The methadone dose was reduced to 5 mg every 12 hours. Suspension should be reassessed tomorrow. Low dose of quetiapine 25 mg once a day at night. Haldol should be maintained only if necessary. Vitamin B1 replacement should be suspended. After 7 days of replacement, schedule an outpatient follow-up appointment. [person] will be transferred to the hospital after discharge. [person] clinically and hemodynamically stable, using a 1 l/min nasal oxygen catheter, saturating 95%, in good condition for transfer to [organization] for weaning from oxygen therapy and medications. The patient, 45 years old, from [address] estimated weight kg: 120 kg predicted weight kg: 80 kg height cm: 180 cm vt 4 to 6 ml/kg: 300 to 450, admission: 06/17/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 07/01/2020, stay: 14 d 13 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Problem: 1 acute respiratory syndrome secondary to coronavirus, onset of symptoms 06/03: myalgia, unmeasured fever, headache, difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), persistence of symptoms 06/13: previous dysuria, hematuria, nausea and vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhea, worsening of symptoms 06/16: worsening of difficulty in breathing (dyspnea) and start of oxygen therapy, orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) for acute lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) 06/17 06/24, rtpcr coronavirus 06/12: positive rtpcr coronavirus [organization] 06/17: positive rtpcr influenza [organization] 06/17: negative, history of current illness: the patient, with no other previous diagnoses, reports that he started with unmeasured fever, myalgia, headache, and mild difficulty in breathing (dyspnea) approximately 15 days after admission. Evolved after 05 days with abdominal discomfort, nausea and vomiting, diarrhea, in addition to dysuria and hematuria, associated with the persistence of the previous symptoms, which led to seeking medical attention on 06/13, when a positive rt-pcr test for coronavirus was collected and released for home isolation. Reports that he presented significant worsening of difficulty in breathing (dyspnea) the following day, leading him to seek medical attention again, when it was noted that he needed oxygen therapy, saturating 80% in room air and 96% in a mask with a reservoir at 15 l/min, and was then referred to [organization]. Upon admission to [organization], the patient was stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), tachypneic, using a 15 l/min reservoir mask, without the use of accessory muscles. Medical history: obesity, denied other comorbidities, medical history: denied previous surgeries, denied drug allergies, denied use of medications, lifestyle habits: denied smoking and alcoholism; evolution during hospitalization: the patient admitted to [organization] on 06/17, started infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) with ceftriaxone and clarithromycin, methylprednisolone and unfractionated heparin. Evolved with hemodynamic instability and maintenance of tachydifficulty in breathing (dyspnea) and need for supplemental oxygen, having been made central venous access to start vasoactive drug and orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) performed and later referred to the ICU (serious care room). Upon admission to the ICU (serious care room), the patient was stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), vasoactive drug was discontinued on the same day and proning position was started. In the ICU (serious care room), presented hypernatremia and prerenal azotemia, which were managed and resolved. The first attempt at breathing tube removed (extubation) was made on 04/23, however the patient developed significant psychomotor agitation and was only extubated on 04/24. He was kept on supplemental oxygen with a nasal catheter for 48 h and was admitted to [organization] on 04/26. Weaning of drugs initiated due to psychomotor agitation presented in the ICU (serious care room) was initiated, with the patient evolving with significant clinical improvement, improvement in drowsiness and difficulty in breathing (dyspnea). At the moment, the patient is clinically and stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), continuing to require supplemental oxygen with a nasal catheter 1 l/min with oxygen level in blood (saturation) 95%, maintaining mild drowsiness, admission his eyes only when called, without complaints. Current antimicrobials: no previous antimicrobial use: acyclovir for genital herpes d1 06/21 d7 06/27 ceftriaxone d1 06/17 to 06/25 clarithromycin d1 06/17 to 06/25 coronavirus protocol: methylprednisolone 40 mg 12/12 h 06/17 06/19 unfractionated heparin 7500iu 08/08 h : no previous devices: central venous catheter subclavian vein 06/17 nasoenteric tube 06/17. Physical examination: good general condition, flushed, hydrated, akinetic, no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no fever (afebrile) to the touch neurology: vigilant, lucid and oriented in time and space, logical and coherent speech, isochoric and photoreactive pupils, preserved extrinsic ocular motricity, strength in the 4 limbs apparently preserved, the patient restrained in bed, which hinders a better neurological examination respiratory auscultation: symmetrical expansibility, normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds) present and symmetrical, reduced in both bases, no adventitious sounds abdomen: globular, normotensive, depressed, bowel sounds present and normal, tympanic to percussion, painless to palpation, no pain on sudden decompression, no masses or visceromegaly palpable lower limbs: warm, capillary refill time less than 3s, palpable and symmetrical pulses, no swelling (edema) and no signs of deep vein blood clot (thrombosis). Cultures: blood test to find infection (blood culture) 06/17: negative urine culture 06/17: negative tracheal aspirate 06/17: negative. Central venous access orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation). Regarding very high blood sugar (hyperglycemia): the patient without a previous diagnosis of sugar disease (diabetes), however, maintaining very high blood sugar (hyperglycemia) during the hospitalization period, refer the patient for outpatient diagnostic clarification. Regarding respiratory status: the patient was kept with a nasal catheter at 1 l/min, given the drop in oxygen level in blood (saturation) after an attempt to remove it, he was kept without infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic)s or corticosteroids, and he was prescribed unfractionated heparin 7,500 iu every 8 hours. Regarding drowsiness: a head tomography scan showed no signs of acute bleeding, awaiting a definitive report, and drowsiness was considered secondary to the effects of the prescribed medications, already being weaned, since the patient did not present any other neurological findings. The patient was taking methadone, quetiapine, and lorazepam. The methadone dose was reduced to 5 mg every 12 hours. Government health scheme (sus)pension should be reassessed tomorrow. Low dose of quetiapine 25 mg once a day at night. Haldol should be maintained only if necessary. Vitamin b1 replacement should be government health schemepended. After 7 days of replacement, schedule an outpatient return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment. The patient will be transferred to the hospital after sent home from hospital (discharge). The patient clinically and stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), using a 1 l/min nasal oxygen catheter, saturating 95%, in good condition for transfer to [organization] for weaning from oxygen therapy and medications. In the ICU (serious care room), presented hypernatremia and prerenal azotemia, which were managed and resolved. The first attempt at breathing tube removed (extubation) was made on 04/23, however the patient developed significant psychomotor agitation and was only extubated on 04/24. He was kept on supplemental oxygen with a nasal catheter for 48 h and was admitted to [organization] on 04/26. Weaning of drugs initiated due to psychomotor agitation presented in the ICU (serious care room) was initiated, with the patient evolving with significant clinical improvement, improvement in drowsiness and difficulty in breathing (dyspnea). 55 problem: 1 Severe acute respiratory syndrome paraf 60 evolution in 7 days. Orotracheal intubation on 04/12 04/24. 2 Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, exacerbated septic shock of pulmonary focus. Active smoker, increased cough and change in sputum characteristics for 7 days, episodes of bronchospasm during hospitalization. 3 Contextual atrial flutter Electrical cardioversion 04/12 and 04/13 After orotracheal intubation and tube replacement, respectively, sinus rhythm maintained, with atrial extrasystoles, amiodarone accumulated dose 10.2 g on 05/05 maintained maintenance 200 mg/day. 4. Acute kidney injury Kdigo 3 secondary to sepsis of pulmonary focus Urinary tract infection Intermittent hemodialysis since 04/15. 5. Upper gastrointestinal bleeding due to duodenal ulcer Forrest IIB Local injection of adrenaline performed. 01/05 omeprazole 40 mg IV 12/12h. 6. intestinal constipation. non-contrast abdominal computed tomography: distension of intestinal loops without obstructive factor requiring multiple intestinal lavages. contrast-enhanced abdominal tomography 03/05: slight distension of colonic loops, presence of fecal content in the small intestine. no obstructive factor visible. small volume of free intra-abdominal fluid. findings suggest reduced intestinal motility {omitted}. 7. urinary tract infection, urine with pyuria-like appearance episodes of urinary retention urine culture 23/04: polymyxin-resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae in ceftazidime avibctam 01/05. 8. decreased level of consciousness to be clarified infectious, metabolic? encephalopathy due to ceftazidime? on 05/05 with progressive worsening of oromandibular automatism, myoclonus in the left side of the body focal seizure? myoclonus of metabolic etiology? computed tomography of the skull 07/05: subcortical hypodensities, moderate microangiopathy, without signs of acute ischemia and without signs of hemorrhage. calcifications in the basal ganglia, which may not have clinical significance. electroencephalogram 08/05: the electroencephalogram was abnormal. the findings of basal activity are compatible with marked diffuse encephalopathy, being more suggestive of toxic, metabolic etiology. by neurology: hypothesis of association of ceftazidime in the presence of acute renal failure as possible etiology. 9. infectious worsening during antibiotic therapy fungal urinary tract infection? bloodstream infection related to the catheter? deviation to the left, subfebrile peaks, decreased level of consciousness on 05/06 in vancomycin 05/07 catheter tip culture 04/26 with growth of staphylococcus aureus we considered the finding as colonizing, however in view of clinical worsening we opted for urinalysis coverage: field taken for leukocytes, urine culture: candida albicans. admission: 13/04/2020, discharge: 13/05/2020, stay: 01 m 04 h, location: ward, payer: sus. history of current illness: [person] brought to this service by [organization], sedated, orotracheal intubation mechanical ventilation agitation scale sedation 5. according to report by the [organization] team and later by family members present, [person] has presented prostration, fever, myalgia and dyspnea on exertion for approximately 07 days. [person] had a long-standing history of worsening productive cough with mucopurulent expectoration in association with general symptoms. He sought care at [organization], where he remained for 2 days for further investigation. This morning, still at [organization], after reversal of atrial flutter by 100J electrical cardioversion, he developed acute respiratory failure and was then subjected to orotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation at the service of origin. Given the associated hemodynamic instability, vasoactive drugs were started, discontinued after a short period, after which [person] began to present arterial hypertension. He continued to show no therapeutic response and died today at {omitted}. Absence of central pulses, silent chest, apnea, absence of pupillary reflexes. Death. Problem: 1 severe acute respiratory syndrome paraf 60 evolution in 7 days. Orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) on 04/12 04/24. 2 chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, exacerbated severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) of pulmonary focus. Active smoker, increased cough and change in sputum characteristics for 7 days, episodes of bronchospasm during hospitalization. 3 contextual atrial flutter electrical cardioversion 04/12 and 04/13 after orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) and tube replacement, respectively, sinus rhythm maintained, with atrial extrasystoles, amiodarone accumulated dose 10.2 g on 05/05 maintained maintenance 200 mg/day. 4. Acute kidney injury kdigo 3 secondary to serious blood infection (sepsis) of pulmonary focus urine infection (UTI) intermittent kidney dialysis (hemodialysis) since 04/15. 5. Upper gastrointestinal bleeding due to duodenal ulcer forrest iib local injection of adrenaline performed. 01/05 omeprazole 40 mg iv 12/12h. 6. Intestinal constipation. Non-contrast abdominal CT scan: distension of intestinal loops without obstructive factor requiring multiple intestinal lavages. Contrast-enhanced abdominal tomography 03/05: slight distension of colonic loops, presence of fecal content in the small intestine. No obstructive factor visible. Small volume of free intra-abdominal fluid. Findings suggest reduced intestinal motility . 7. Urine infection (UTI), urine with pyuria-like appearance episodes of urinary retention urine culture 23/04: polymyxin-resistant klebsiella lung infection (pneumonia)e in ceftazidime avibctam 01/05. 8. Decreased alertness level (level of consciousness) to be clarified infectious, metabolic? Brain dysfunction (encephalopathy) due to ceftazidime? On 05/05 with progressive worsening of oromandibular automatism, myoclonus in the left side of the body focal seizure? Myoclonus of metabolic cause of disease (etiology)? CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 07/05: subcortical hypodensities, moderate microangiopathy, without signs of acute ischemia and without signs of hemorrhage. Calcifications in the basal ganglia, which may not have clinical significance. Electroencephalogram 08/05: the electroencephalogram was abnormal. The findings of basal activity are compatible with marked diffuse brain dysfunction, being more suggestive of toxic, metabolic cause of disease (etiology). By neurology: hypothesis of association of ceftazidime in the presence of sudden kidney failure (acute kidney failure (renal failure)) as possible cause of disease (etiology). 9. Infectious worsening during infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) fungal urine infection (UTI)? Bloodstream infection related to the catheter? Deviation to the left, subfebrile peaks, decreased alertness level (level of consciousness) on 05/06 in vancomycin 05/07 catheter tip culture 04/26 with growth of staphylococcus aureus we considered the finding as colonizing, however in view of clinical worsening we opted for urinalysis coverage: field taken for leukocytes, urine culture: candida albicans. The patient had a long-standing history of worsening productive cough with mucopurulent expectoration in association with general symptoms. He sought care at [organization], where he remained for 2 days for further investigation. This morning, still at [organization], after reversal of atrial flutter by 100j electrical cardioversion, he developed acute lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) and was then subjected to orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) and machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation) at the service of origin. Given the associated hemodynamic instability, vasoactive drugs were started, discontinued after a short period, after which the patient began to present arterial high BP (hypertension). He continued to show no therapeutic response and died today at . Absence of central pulses, silent chest, apnea, absence of pupillary reflexes. Death. 57 diabetic ketoacidosis. admission: 04/08/2020, discharge: 05/01/2020, stay: 22 d 21 h, location: ward, payer: sus. problem: immediate postoperative period of suprapatellar amputation of the left lower limb. septic shock 03/25: admission to an outpatient service due to diabetic ketoacidosis, probable cause: septic shock of pulmonary focus. onset of general symptoms since 03/21: fatigue, shortness of breath 03/25: hyperglycemia, general worsening, and diagnosis of diabetic ketoacidosis. 03/26: clindamycin ceftriaxone started. 03/26: decreased level of consciousness, shock, need for orotracheal intubation, sedation, and mechanical ventilation. report of left femoral artery puncture on 03/26. evolved with coldness of the left lower limb and decreased pulse 04/04: Doppler ultrasound of the left lower limb: signs of occlusion of the posterior tibial and anterior tibial arteries 06/04: antibiotic changed to PPE/TAZO until 12/04 08/04: transferred to [organization]. upon admission: use of the vasoactive drug norepinephrine 3 ml/h and mechanical ventilation, with bandaged left lower limb. 12/04: antibiotic changed to meropenm, clindamycin, adjusted vancomycin, oral metronidazole diarrhea, colitis? 12/04 CT scan of the skull due to anisocoria on examination: discussed with radiology: no bleeding, no signs of intracranial hypertension, no signs of herniation. awaiting stability and delimitation of ischemic areas for amputation level. 04/15: underwent left suprapatellar amputation, without complications; surgical findings: presence of fixed cyanosis throughout the foot and distal third of the leg, with an area of non-fixed cyanosis in the anterolateral portion of the middle third of the leg. admitted to the intensive care unit, sedated, immediate postoperative, orotracheal intubation on mechanical ventilation, hemodynamic stability maintained with norepinephrine. 04/16: acute respiratory distress syndrome secondary to pneumonia associated with mechanical ventilation? acinetobacter multis in tracheal aspirate on 04/08 staphylococcus epidermidis and haemolyticus in central venous catheter tip 04/15, vancomycin maintained, PCR for coronavirus on 04/11: negative, gentamicin started on 04/16, empirical proning: 04/17 at 01:40 hours r paraf142 post proning 238 supine at 00:30 hours 04/18 04/23: fluconazole suspended after 10 days of use for treatment of fungal urinary tract infection 04/24: fever plateau new cultures 04/25: fever maintenance, central venous catheter change and sending tip for negative culture 04/25: portex tracheal cannula change 04/25: nephrology evaluation conservative management at the moment 04/26: clinical worsening, fever plateau, worsening laboratory results discussed with ccih and antibiotic therapy escalated to tigecycline, polymyxin and vancomycin 04/27: start of hemodialysis instability during session, increase in vasoactive drugs 04/28: meeting between [organization] team and family members defined palliative care, {omitted}. left suprapatellar amputation due to acute arterial obstruction. death confirmed as {omitted}. Diabetic ketoacidosis. Admission: 04/08/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 05/01/2020, stay: 22 d 21 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Problem: immediate postoperative period of suprapatellar amputation of the left lower limb. Severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) 03/25: admission to an outpatient service due to diabetic ketoacidosis, probable cause: severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) of pulmonary focus. Onset of general symptoms since 03/21: fatigue, shortness of breath 03/25: very high blood sugar (hyperglycemia), general worsening, and diagnosis of diabetic ketoacidosis. 03/26: clindamycin ceftriaxone started. 03/26: decreased alertness level, shock, need for orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation), sedation, and machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation). Report of left femoral artery puncture on 03/26. Evolved with coldness of the left lower limb and decreased pulse 04/04: doppler ultrasound of the left lower limb: signs of occlusion of the posterior tibial and anterior tibial arteries 06/04: infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) changed to ppe/tazo until 12/04 08/04: transferred to [organization]. Upon admission: use of the vasoactive drug norepinephrine 3 ml/h and machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation), with bandaged left lower limb. 12/04: infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) changed to meropenm, clindamycin, adjusted vancomycin, oral metronidazole diarrhea, colitis? 12/04 ct scan of the skull due to anisocoria on examination: discussed with radiology: no bleeding, no signs of intracranial high BP (hypertension), no signs of herniation. Awaiting stability and delimitation of reduced blood supply (ischemic) areas for amputation level. 04/15: underwent left suprapatellar amputation, without complications; surgical findings: presence of fixed cyanosis throughout the foot and distal third of the leg, with an area of non-fixed cyanosis in the anterolateral portion of the middle third of the leg. Admitted to the ICU (serious care room), sedated, immediate postoperative, orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) on machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation), hemodynamic stability maintained with norepinephrine. 04/16: acute respiratory distress syndrome secondary to lung infection (pneumonia) associated with machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation)? Acinetobacter multis in tracheal aspirate on 04/08 staphylococcus epidermidis and haemolyticus in central venous catheter tip 04/15, vancomycin maintained, pcr for coronavirus on 04/11: negative, gentamicin started on 04/16, empirical proning: 04/17 at 01:40 hours r paraf142 post proning 238 supine at 00:30 hours 04/18 04/23: fluconazole government health schemepended after 10 days of use for treatment of fungal urine infection (UTI) 04/24: fever plateau new cultures 04/25: fever maintenance, central venous catheter change and sending tip for negative culture 04/25: portex tracheal cannula change 04/25: nephrology evaluation conservative management at the moment 04/26: clinical worsening, fever plateau, worsening laboratory results discussed with ccih and infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) escalated to tigecycline, polymyxin and vancomycin 04/27: start of kidney dialysis (hemodialysis) instability during session, increase in vasoactive drugs 04/28: meeting between [organization] team and family members defined comfort-focused (not curative) care, . Left suprapatellar amputation due to acute arterial obstruction. Death confirmed as . Diabetic ketoacidosis. Admission: 04/08/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 05/01/2020, stay: 22 d 21 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Problem: immediate postoperative period of suprapatellar amputation of the left lower limb. Severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) 03/25: admission to an outpatient service due to diabetic ketoacidosis, probable cause: severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) of pulmonary focus. Onset of general symptoms since 03/21: fatigue, shortness of breath 03/25: very high blood sugar (hyperglycemia), general worsening, and diagnosis of diabetic ketoacidosis. 03/26: clindamycin ceftriaxone started. 03/26: decreased alertness level, shock, need for orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation), sedation, and machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation). Report of left femoral artery puncture on 03/26. Evolved with coldness of the left lower limb and decreased pulse 04/04: doppler ultrasound of the left lower limb: signs of occlusion of the posterior tibial and anterior tibial arteries 06/04: infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) changed to ppe/tazo until 12/04 08/04: transferred to [organization]. Upon admission: use of the vasoactive drug norepinephrine 3 ml/h and machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation), with bandaged left lower limb. 12/04: infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) changed to meropenm, clindamycin, adjusted vancomycin, oral metronidazole diarrhea, colitis? 12/04 ct scan of the skull due to anisocoria on examination: discussed with radiology: no bleeding, no signs of intracranial high BP (hypertension), no signs of herniation. Awaiting stability and delimitation of reduced blood supply (ischemic) areas for amputation level. 04/15: after surgery, he was able to get suprapatellar amputation throughout the foot and distal third of the leg. The doctor found that he had a problem in the leg and had a weakness in the foot. The patient was admitted to the ICU (serious care room), sedated, immediate postoperative, orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) on machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation), and hemodynamic stability maintained with norepinephrine. The patient had no problem with the leg, but he had no problems with the back and shoulder.it's important to keep the leg healthy and maintain the back of his leg. The surgery was done with a specialized surgery unit, which was done in the back, but it's a good way to get back to the legs.</s> 04/16: acute respiratory distress syndrome secondary to lung infection (pneumonia) associated with machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation)? 59 admission: 07/05/2020, discharge: 07/16/2020, stay: 11 d 08 h, location: ward, payer: sus. hospital discharge report [person] 34 years old weight: 52 kg, height: 161 cm saps 3: 63 postoperative: 55%, current: septic shock of pulmonary focus impression 1 septic shock of pulmonary focus, aspiration pneumonia, antibiotic therapy suspended on d7 07/11 extubation on 07/11 2 substance use disorder 3 hydroelectrolytic disorders resolved, history of current illness, patient heavy substance use disorder, cocaine metabolite positive upon hospital admission, admitted on 07/04, by [organization], with abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting that started 2 days before. evolved on the day of admission with obtundation, decreased level of consciousness, acute respiratory failure, orotracheal intubation. Given the diagnostic hypothesis of mesenteric ischemia, an abdominal CT Angiography (CTA) was performed, provisional report without evidence of acute vascular abdomen and without pancreatitis, but with bibasal pulmonary consolidations suggestive of bronchoaspiration. also presented significant leukocytosis, with left shift, empirical antibiotic therapy with ceftriaxone, metronidazole and oxacillin was started, used for 7 days, suspended on 07/12 by guidance of the [organization]. transferred to the intensive care unit on 07/05, evolved with circulatory shock, severe metabolic acidosis and acute kidney injury secondary to sepsis, with good response to volume expansion. presented progressive improvement, sedation was suspended on 07/09 and extubated on 07/11. presented initial psychomotor agitation, with improvement on 07/12. On 07/13, an oral diet was prescribed, and compliance with removal of the nasoenteral tube was observed. [person] evolved with good acceptance of the oral diet and complete improvement of the clinical condition. [organization] evaluated [person] and referred him for follow-up with a psychiatrist in {city}, since [person] wishes to remain abstinent. In a review of exams, an altered lipid profile was observed: ct400 / ldl: indeterminate / hdl < 30 / tg 2500 and then bezafibrate 400 mg/day was started. as [person] cocaine user and transthoracic echocardiography on 7/11 showing EF 40%, left ventricular systolic performance depressed to a mild/moderate degree, left ventricle with diffuse hypokinesia, moderate left ventricular dilation and mild left atrial dilation, eccentric left ventricular hypertrophy: we started beta-blocker carvedilol 3.125 12/12h and ACE inhibitor enalapril 5mg 12/12h and scheduled an appointment at [organization] for follow-up. Ward Upon admission to the ward: good general condition, acyanotic, anicteric, afebrile, normal color and hydrated, capillary refill time 3 seconds, bpp, no edema, vital signs heart rate: 80 beats per minute respiratory rate: 18 inspirations per minute, oxygen saturation: 97% room air blood pressure: 140x80 mmhg cardiac auscultation: normal rhythmic sounds in 2t without murmurs, without tjp respiratory auscultation: normally distributed vesicular breath sounds without adventitious sounds abdomen: bowel sounds present, nt/nt, painless, without visceromegaly, free traube lower limbs without edema, without calf swelling nervous system glasgow coma scale 15, conscious and oriented in time and space. comorbidities ovarian carcinoma, pending start of follow-up, scheduled appointment at the procto-oncology outpatient clinic, [person] was absent, reports having stopped using, denies other comorbidities, previous exams brought by the family, emergency abdominal tomography 05/19: hepatomegaly associated with signs of marked fatty disease cystic formation in the right adnexa, nonspecific, measuring 6.8 cm in longest axis retroperitoneal nodular formation with soft tissue density near the iliac vessels on the right, measuring 4.2 x 5.1 cm nature to clarify another lesion near the iliac vessels on the right peritoneal implants? transvaginal ultrasound 06/19: right ovary with preserved morphology, regular contours, with an anechoic oval image measuring approximately 6.7x6.5x5.7 cm, compatible with dysfunctional cyst?. free posterior cul-de-sac. normal uterus and left ovary. tests during admission abdominal tomography 08/07: moderate amount of intra-abdominal free fluid. reduction in hepatic attenuation coefficient, suggestive of fatty disease. image with fluid density anterior to the third duodenal portion, which may represent diverticulum. previously used medications denied, tests upon admission, blood culture 07/05 4 samples: no growth after 7 days urine culture 07/05: negative after 48 hours urine culture 07/10: negative after 48 hours antibiotic therapy ceftriaxone 1g 12/12h, d0 07/04 suspended on 07/11 oxacillin 2g 4/4h, d0 07/04 suspended on 07/11 metronidazole 500mg 8/8h, d0 07/04, suspended on 07/11, [person] is discharged in good health, walking, breathing room air. altered lipid profile ct400 / ldl: indeterminate / hdl < 30 / tg2500 transthoracic echocardiography on 07/11 showing ef 40%, mildly/moderately depressed left ventricular systolic performance, left ventricle with diffuse hypokinesia, moderate left ventricular dilation and mild left atrial dilation, eccentric left ventricular hypertrophy. none. outpatient laboratory tests requested. follow-up appointment in cardio blood pressure started bezafibrate 400 mg/day. as [person] and transthoracic echocardiography on 07/11 showing ef 40%, mild/moderate depressed left ventricular systolic performance, left ventricle with diffuse hypokinesia, moderate LV dilation and mild ae, eccentric LV hypertrophy: we started beta-blocker carvedilol 3.125 12/12h and ACE inhibitor enalapril 5mg 12/12h and scheduled an appointment at [organization] for follow-up. [person] was discharged in good health, walking, ventilated with room air. Admission: 07/05/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 07/16/2020, stay: 11 d 08 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Hospital sent home from hospital (discharge) report the patient 34 years old weight: 52 kg, height: 161 cm saps 3: 63 postoperative: 55%, current: severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) of pulmonary focus impression 1 severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) of pulmonary focus, lung infection from inhaling food/liquid (aspiration lung infection (pneumonia)), infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) government health schemepended on d7 07/11 breathing tube removed (extubation) on 07/11 2 substance use disorder 3 hydroelectrolytic disorders resolved, history of current illness, patient heavy substance use disorder, cocaine metabolite positive upon hospital admission, admitted on 07/04, by [organization], with abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting that started 2 days before. Evolved on the day of admission with obtundation, decreased alertness level, acute lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure), orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation). Given the diagnostic hypothesis of mesenteric ischemia, an abdominal ct angiography (cta) was performed, provisional report without evidence of acute vascular abdomen and without pancreatitis, but with bibasal pulmonary consolidations suggestive of bronchoaspiration. Also presented significant high white blood cell count (leukocytosis), with left shift, empirical infection-fighting medicine treatment with ceftriaxone, metronidazole and oxacillin was started, used for 7 days, government health schemepended on 07/12 by guidance of the [organization]. Transferred to the ICU (serious care room) on 07/05, evolved with circulatory shock, severe metabolic acidosis and acute kidney injury secondary to serious blood infection (sepsis), with good response to volume expansion. Presented progressive improvement, sedation was government health schemepended on 07/09 and extubated on 07/11. Presented initial psychomotor agitation, with improvement on 07/12. On 07/13, an oral diet was prescribed, and compliance with removal of the nasoenteral tube was observed. The patient evolved with good acceptance of the oral diet and complete improvement of the clinical condition. [organization] evaluated the patient and referred him for return visit to doctor (follow-up) with a psychiatrist in the city, since the patient wishes to remain abstinent. In a review of exams, an altered lipid profile was observed: ct400 / ldl: indeterminate / hdl < 30 / tg 2500 and then bezafibrate 400 mg/day was started. As the patient cocaine user and transthoracic echocardiography on 7/11 showing general hospital room (ward) 40%, left ventricular systolic performance depressed to a mild/moderate degree, left ventricle with diffuse hypokinesia, moderate left ventricular dilation and mild left atrial dilation, eccentric left ventricular hypertrophy: we started beta-blocker carvedilol 3.125 12/12h and ace inhibitor enalapril 5mg 12/12h and scheduled an appointment at [organization] for return visit to doctor (follow-up). General hospital room (ward) upon admission to the general hospital room (ward): good general condition, no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no fever (afebrile), normal color and hydrated, capillary refill time 3 seconds, bpp, no swelling (edema), vital signs heart rate: 80 beats per minute respiratory rate: 18 inspirations per minute, oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation): 97% room air blood pressure: 140x80 mmhg cardiac auscultation: normal rhythmic sounds in 2t without murmurs, without tjp respiratory auscultation: normally distributed normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds) without adventitious sounds abdomen: bowel sounds present, nt/nt, painless, without visceromegaly, free traube lower limbs without swelling (edema), without calf swelling nervous system glasgow coma scale 15, conscious and oriented in time and space. Comorbidities ovarian carcinoma, pending start of return visit to doctor (follow-up), scheduled appointment at the procto-oncology OPD (Out Patient Department), the patient was absent, reports having stopped using, denies other comorbidities, previous exams brought by the family, emergency abdominal tomography 05/19: hepatomegaly associated with signs of marked fatty disease cystic formation in the right adnexa, nonspecific, measuring 6.8 cm in longest axis retroperitoneal nodular formation with soft tissue density near the iliac vessels on the right, measuring 4.2 x 5.1 cm nature to clarify another lesion near the iliac vessels on the right peritoneal implants? Transvaginal ultrasound 06/19: right ovary with preserved morphology, regular contours, with an anechoic oval image measuring approximately 6.7x6.5x5.7 cm, compatible with dysfunctional cyst?. Free posterior cul-de-sac. Normal uterus and left ovary. Tests during admission abdominal tomography 08/07: moderate amount of intra-abdominal free fluid. Reduction in hepatic attenuation coefficient, suggestive of fatty disease. Image with fluid density anterior to the third duodenal portion, which may represent diverticulum. Previously used medications denied, tests upon admission, blood test to find infection (blood culture) 07/05 4 samples: no growth after 7 days urine culture 07/05: negative after 48 hours urine culture 07/10: negative after 48 hours infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) ceftriaxone 1g 12/12h, d0 07/04 government health schemepended on 07/11 oxacillin 2g 4/4h, d0 07/04 government health schemepended on 07/11 metronidazole 500mg 8/8h, d0 07/04, government health schemepended on 07/11, the patient is sent home from hospital (discharge)d in good health, walking, breathing room air. Altered lipid profile ct400 / ldl: indeterminate / hdl < 30 / tg2500 transthoracic echocardiography on 07/11 showing general hospital room (ward) 40%, mildly/moderately depressed left ventricular systolic performance, left ventricle with diffuse hypokinesia, moderate left ventricular dilation and mild left atrial dilation, eccentric left ventricular hypertrophy. 61 admission: 2020-01-24, discharge: 2020-02-24, stay: 07 d 14 h, location: ward, payer: sus. discharge report [person], 44 years old human immunodeficiency virus diagnosis {omitted} treatment abandonment cd4 14 viral load 1911682 september/2016 past history of abdominal tuberculosis late latent syphilis treated on {omitted} alcoholism smoking 23 pack-day substance use disorder crack use patient was admitted on 23/01 to [organization] with a report that two months ago he started a progressively worsening productive cough associated with ventilator-dependent chest pain, with purulent secretion discharge and atypical precordial pain. 20 days after admission, he started to have daily fever and lost 20 kg in one month. he continued to have a loss of appetite also associated with excessive substance use disorder. He denied hemoptysis, diarrhea, syncope, palpitations, vomiting, dyspnea, or dysuria. He denied bleeding, skin lesions, nodules, or rash on the body. [person] was admitted to [organization] for investigation of atypical precordial pain, weight loss, weakness, and cough. He remained in a respiratory isolation bed and was ordered to undergo an echocardiogram, myocardial necrosis markers, upper digestive endoscopy, and PCR for cytomegalovirus for investigation. TDF 3TC efz. The patient was restarted with negative troponin, an unchanged electrocardiogram, and transthoracic echocardiography, which showed an improvement in the left ventricular ejection fraction (56%) compared to the previous one, normal segmental movement, normal right ventricular systolic performance, mild mitral insufficiency, and mild ae dilation. He also underwent cardiac magnetic resonance imaging, which ruled out pericarditis and showed complete recovery of myocardial function. However, he continued to complain of moderate, stabbing right-sided chest pain that worsened with food intake and improved with common analgesics. He developed sepsis of pulmonary focus and was treated with cefepime and clarithromycin. He finished treatment with ceftazidime and had hospital pneumonia for 14 days due to growth of Pseudomonas spp in blood culture. He underwent upper digestive endoscopy on 01/31, which revealed esophageal ulcers and collected a biopsy of the same. He was removed from respiratory isolation, seeing negative bacilloscopy results. He started empirical ganciclovir on 02/01 due to a diagnosis of esophageal ulcers in an immunosuppressed patient, despite a negative PCR for cytomegalovirus. underwent a new upper gastrointestinal endoscopy on 02/14 with an increase in the size of the ulcers, however as [person] with complete symptomatic improvement of chest pain and odynophagia, it was decided to maintain ganciclovir for 21 days and repeat a new upper gastrointestinal endoscopy on an outpatient basis, having completed the treatment on 02/22. He presented with toothache at the end of the hospitalization, being evaluated by the dentistry, without the need for other treatments. Previous antihypertensives enalapril and carvedilol were reintroduced, however with poor blood pressure control, and hydrochlorothiazide was started on an outpatient basis. He was discharged in good general condition, without complaints of pain, without odynophagia or dysphagia, with outpatient follow-up schedule, new upper gastrointestinal endoscopy in March and maintenance of use. Admission: 2020-01-24, sent home from hospital (discharge): 2020-02-24, stay: 07 d 14 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. He continued to have a loss of appetite also associated with excessive substance use disorder. He denied hemoptysis, diarrhea, syncope, palpitations, vomiting, difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), or dysuria. He denied bleeding, skin lesions, nodules, or rash on the body. The patient was admitted to [organization] for investigation of atypical precordial pain, weight loss, weakness, and cough. He remained in a respiratory isolation bed and was ordered to undergo an heart ultrasound (echocardiogram), myocardial tissue death (necrosis) markers, upper digestive endoscopy, and pcr for cytomegalovirus for investigation. Tdf 3tc efz. The patient was restarted with negative troponin, an unchanged ECG/heart tracing (electrocardiogram), and transthoracic echocardiography, which showed an improvement in the left ventricular ejection fraction (56%) compared to the previous one, normal segmental movement, normal right ventricular systolic performance, mild mitral insufficiency, and mild ae dilation. He also underwent cardiac MRI scan (magnetic resonance) imaging, which ruled out pericarditis and showed complete recovery of myocardial function. However, he continued to complain of moderate, stabbing right-sided chest pain that worsened with food intake and improved with common analgesics. He developed serious blood infection (sepsis) of pulmonary focus and was treated with cefepime and clarithromycin. He finished treatment with ceftazidime and had hospital lung infection (pneumonia) for 14 days due to growth of pseudomonas spp in blood test to find infection (blood culture). He underwent upper digestive endoscopy on 01/31, which revealed esophageal ulcers and collected a biopsy of the same. He was removed from respiratory isolation, seeing negative bacilloscopy results. He started empirical ganciclovir on 02/01 due to a diagnosis of esophageal ulcers in an immunosuppressed patient, despite a negative pcr for cytomegalovirus. Underwent a new upper gastrointestinal endoscopy on 02/14 with an increase in the size of the ulcers, however as the patient with complete symptomatic improvement of chest pain and odynophagia, it was decided to maintain ganciclovir for 21 days and repeat a new upper gastrointestinal endoscopy on an outpatient basis, having completed the treatment on 02/22. He presented with toothache at the end of the hospitalization, being evaluated by the dentistry, without the need for other treatments. Previous antihypertensives enalapril and carvedilol were reintroduced, however with poor blood pressure control, and hydrochlorothiazide was started on an outpatient basis. He was sent home from hospital (discharge)d in good general condition, without complaints of pain, without odynophagia or dysphagia, with outpatient return visit to doctor (follow-up) schedule, new upper gastrointestinal endoscopy in march and maintenance of use. Healed gastric antrum ulcer sakita s2 ap without malignancy, helicobacter pylori, abdominal computed tomography February: diffuse parietal thickening of the stomach and small intestine loops, a finding that may be related to an inflammatory process. Pancreas with normal contours and dimensions, tiny calcifications in the head and body with a residual appearance. Partial deep vein thrombosis of the common and external iliac veins on the left. Fatty liver disease. Heterogeneous opacity with a nodular appearance with air bronchograms and architectural distortion in the middle lobe. Pleural effusion on the right. abdominal ultrasound 01/08/2020: increased liver echogenicity, a finding most commonly associated with fatty liver disease, biliary sludge chest computed tomography 03/20: acute right-sided pulmonary thromboembolism without cor pulmonale, opacity in the left middle and lower lobes that should represent pulmonary infarctions, not being able to rule out a granulomatous infectious inflammatory process on the left, pleural effusion on the left, moderate pericardial effusion, compatible with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, predominantly emphysematous, electrocardiogram 03/22: generalized low-voltage sinus tachycardia, with right branch conduction delay, absence of changes suggestive of ischemia. calprotectin 03/22: 5,800 cie fungi 03/23: nonreactive transthoracic echocardiography 03/23: cardiac chambers with normal dimensions, preserved systolic performance of the ventricles, normal LV segmental mobility, moderate to marked pericardial effusion with no signs of increased intrapericardial pressure, image of a mass adhered to the visceral pericardium, which may correspond to a metastatic implant or pericardial fat. [person] 03/21: atrophic gastritis of the deep body, healed ulcer. biopsies: mild chronic duodenitis, absence of villous atrophy, lymphocytosis or hyperplasia. absence of signs of malignancy. mild chronic gastritis, with moderate glandular atrophy and foci of intestinal metaplasia. colonoscopy 03/29: sparse hypertonic diverticula of the left and transverse colon, with no signs of inflammation, bleeding or impacted fecalith. In the ascending colon, there are areas of whitish mucosa with a scarred appearance. Sessile polyp of the sigmoid colon 6 mm and of the rectum 7 mm. Cold loop polypectomies were performed without complications. There are no inflammatory or neoplastic lesions in the present examination. Serial biopsies were performed in the terminal ileum, ascending colon, transverse colon, sigmoid descending colon, and rectum. Petscan 20/04: no lesions suggestive of neoplasia or metastases. Death. 64 seizure status right bronchus injury pneumothorax pneumonia bloodstream infection. admission: 14/05/2020, discharge: 06/05/2020, stay: 22 days 04 hours, location: ward, payer: sus. history of current illness informant entry: [person]: patient last seen well on 10/05/2020, around 9:00 pm. the next day, she was found at 7:30 am by neighbors unconscious, with her tongue bitten off and with urine on her clothes and on the floor. she was taken to the emergency care unit by [organization]. there, reentrant seizures were reported that did not cease even after diazepam and hydantoin. she was intubated due to a decrease in her level of consciousness. after sedation with midazolam and bic fentanyl, the seizures ceased. she developed acute kidney injury, presenting oliguria and dark urine. Report of attempts to wean from sedation, but [person] follow-up appointmented to present signs of a crisis, requiring sedation to be resumed. Due to a fever spike, on 05/11 ceftriaxone was started due to the diagnostic hypothesis of meningitis causing CC and lowering of the level of consciousness. In external examinations, he presented significant thrombocytopenia. [person] has been drinking 1 l of distilled spirits per day for 20 years. [person] reports that for about 5 years he has been presenting memory alteration, with episodes of leaving the stove on and confusing people, with progressive worsening. He has also been presenting falls from his own height, one of the times with traumatic brain injury requiring hospitalization. He does not know whether he required ICU, but does not think he required orotracheal intubation, for about 3 years. He has also been presenting convulsive crises for about 3 years. [person] does not remember whether they started before or after the [organization]. A month ago, [person] presented a similar picture of seizures refractory to medication and also required orotracheal intubation. She was discharged with a prescription for antiepileptics carbamazepine 200 mg 8/8h clonazepam 20 gts/night, but was not taking them. [person] was admitted to the emergency unit, brought by [organization] already intubated with TOT 7.5 fixed at the 25cm rim, and was positioned on the respiratory isolation bed for monitoring. She was admitted in poor general condition, sedated, Glasgow 3T, mydriatic, isochoric, bradyreactive pupils. In orotracheal intubation, ventilation was difficult in the AMB, with tongue edema. After measures for bronchospasm and aspiration of the mucus plug in TOT, there was an improvement in the ventilatory parameters. She presented with shock, requiring norepinephrine, which was weaned on the same day, maintaining a mean arterial pressure of 70. Evolution during hospitalization [person] was admitted to the isolation bed in poor general condition, hypotensive, with a silent chest. She underwent norepinephrine volume replacement, measures for bronchospasm, and airway aspiration with improvement in ventilatory parameters and improvement in hypotension. She underwent laboratory tests that showed acute kidney injury and rhabdomyolysis, and hyperhydration was initiated. A cranial computed tomography scan and cerebrospinal fluid puncture were requested, but due to the platelet count of 9000, cerebrospinal fluid was collected on 05/14 after transfusion of 1 platelet apheresis. A cranial computed tomography scan showed no changes and the cerebrospinal fluid was normal, so [person] was taken to the ER and had her ceftriaxone dose reduced to 1 g 12/12h. Phenobarbital was started on May 15, under the guidance of the neurology department, due to a previous history of seizures. [person] presented high blood pressure levels, requiring the introduction of nitroprusside on May 15. An attempt was made to wean her from sedation for extubation, but [person] presented significant agitation even with considerable doses of midazolam and Precedex. She was admitted to the semi-intensive care unit at night on May 16. On May 17, due to a history of significant alcoholism, it was decided to start lorazepam to manage a possible alcohol withdrawal syndrome, and ceftriaxone was maintained and clindamycin was associated with her on May 17 due to the possibility of aspiration pneumonia [person] with leukocytosis and fever spikes even while taking ceftriaxone. On May 19, it was decided to perform extubation, since [person] was conscious, apparently cooperative, without bronchospasm, on mechanical ventilation by PSV, and on methylprednisolone for several days. However, [person] presented tachypnea and respiratory effort even with the use of a mask with a 10 L/min reservoir. He presented wheezing and mainly diffuse rhonchi, with cervical auscultation suggestive of stridor. Given the extubation failure, it was decided to perform orotracheal intubation to ensure adequate ventilation. [person] underwent intubation with a bougie, but [person] maintained desaturation and evolved with pulseless electrical activity. Cardiopulmonary resuscitation maneuvers were initiated, and 4 cycles were performed with administration of 2 ampoules of adrenaline. [person] follow-up appointmented to spontaneous circulation after 4 cycles, with palpable central pulses, but maintained desaturation. The presence of tension pneumothorax was verified on the right, with improvement in ventilatory parameters after relief thoracentesis and placement of a chest tube. underwent computed tomography of the skull, neck, and chest on 05/20, which showed massive subcutaneous emphysema and nonspecific imaging in the right main bronchus. [person] required chest drain replacement on 05/20 due to poor positioning and lack of oscillation. The chest drain remained in place until 05/22, when it was removed by the surgical team. There was no decrease in auscultation or worsening of ventilatory parameters after that. [person] presented worsening of leukocytosis and fever plateau. New cultures were collected and antibiotic therapy was escalated to meropenem vancomycin on 05/21. The patient progressed well with ventilatory weaning. She was maintained on macronebulization on 05/28 without complications. She had 1 fever spike on 05/27. We removed the indwelling urinary catheter and requested infection screening with blood culture and chest X-ray. Although fever did not recur, worsening of the leukogram was observed. On 05/28, we decided to remove the central venous access in the right subclavian vein with a catheter tip culture. We performed a new puncture of the central venous access in the right femoral vein due to peripheral access failure. [person] was progressing well, without sedation since the morning of 05/27. He presented with hyperactive delirium, which is already improving. He responds well to verbal management with quetiapine. On 06/02, the tracheostomy was replaced with a metal one, without complications. [person] maintained moderate secretion volume via the tracheostomy. On 06/03, he was evaluated by [organization], performed a PAN, and was advised to continue follow-up with speech therapist, and a new case was scheduled for ELT on 08/13. regurgitation and moderate mitral stenosis, planimetry 1.35, gradient: 5.5, severe tricuspid regurgitation, leaflet prolapse, psap 39, mild aortic insufficiency. marked dilatation of ae, moderately depressed LVSD, LVMS: akinesia of the mid-anterior septum, apical septum, basal and mid-inferior septum, inferoapical, hypokinesia of the mid-inferolateral, basal and mid-inferior, mid-anterior, aneroapical, lateroapical, other normal mobility. normal LVSD. electrocardiogram 06/24/2020: atrial fibrillation rhythm and septal negative electrical zone. hemodynamic echocardiogram 06/30/2020 without improvement in cardiac output after expansion of 500 ml of 0.9% saline solution. tracheostomy. discharge death. 1 acute lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) acute pulmonary swelling (edema) acute myocardial injury acs stemi troponin 4381 secondary to decompensated mitral stenosis? Report of negative rapid test for coronavirus performed today. Denies contact with positive or government health scheme (sus)pected cases. Upon admission the patient tachydyspneic, desaturating 84% on reservoir mask, with use of accessory muscles. Denies nausea or vomiting. Reports episode of retrosternal, epigastric pain with radiation to the back, associated with fatigue for 12 hours prior to admission. Reports concomitant palpitation and difficulty in breathing (dyspnea). Denies cough or other flu-like symptoms. Denies changes in urinary or bowel habits. Sent home from hospital (discharge)d, deceased. Transthoracic echocardiography 03/17/2020 : moderate to severe mitral stenosis, hap, psap 56 mmhg, valve area 1.2 and av gradient 8 mmhg, mild to moderate aoi, left ventricular ejection fraction 64% transthoracic echocardiography 06/23/2020: ao: 30, ae: 41, lvedd: 48, septum 9.5, lvepp: 9, left ventricular ejection fraction: 34%, moderate mitral regurgitation and moderate mitral stenosis, planimetry 1.35, gradient: 5.5, severe tricuspid regurgitation, leaflet prolapse, psap 39, mild aortic insufficiency. Marked dilatation of ae, moderately depressed lvsd, lvms: akinesia of the mid-anterior septum, apical septum, basal and mid-inferior septum, inferoapical, hypokinesia of the mid-inferolateral, basal and mid-inferior, mid-anterior, aneroapical, lateroapical, other normal mobility. Normal lvsd. ECG/heart tracing (electrocardiogram) 06/24/2020: irregular heartbeat (atrial fibrillation) rhythm and septal negative electrical zone. Hemodynamic heart ultrasound (echocardiogram) 06/30/2020 without improvement in cardiac output after expansion of 500 ml of 0.9% saline solution. Breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy). Sent home from hospital (discharge) death. a41. other septicemias. e108. insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus with complications n.e. i21. acute myocardial infarction. i50. heart failure. b342. coronavirus infection, n.e. death 17.1 1.15
66 [person], 69 years old. admission: 07/01/2020, discharge: 12/02/2020, stay: 01 m 05 d 06 h, location: ward, payer: sus. patient admitted to this service with acute abdomen on 07/01/2020. underwent exploratory laparotomy that revealed intrathoracic gastric volvulus, gastric secretion in the thoracic cavity, lesser curvature ischemia with perforation of approximately 2 centimeters. total gastrectomy, end-to-side esophagojejunal anastomosis, roux-en-y reconstruction were performed. and remained under peritoneostomy. evolution with refractory abdominal septic shock, acute kidney injury, and disseminated intravascular coagulation. need for multiple surgical reapproaches due to duodenal fistula. During hospitalization in the intensive care unit, he received broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy, including polymyxin B, meropenem, micafungin, and levofloxacin. An evaluation by the palliative care team was requested, and then. Abdominal pain that had been present for 4 days prior to hospital admission. Blood culture, ascitic fluid culture, and tracheal aspirate were positive for multidrug-resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae. Stomach necrosis culture was positive for Candida tropicalis. Tracheal aspirate culture was positive for Stenotrophomonas. Ascitic fluid culture was positive for Proteus mirabilis. On January 7, total gastrectomy was performed, esophagojejunal anastomosis was performed at the end of the lateral esophagus, and Roux-en-Y reconstruction was performed. He remained with peritoneostomy. On January 21, tracheostomy was performed, and multiple revisions of the peritoneostomy were performed. [person] developed cardiorespiratory arrest in asystole and death was confirmed at {omitted}, death certificate issued, family welcomed. The patient, 69 years old. Admission: 07/01/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 12/02/2020, stay: 01 m 05 d 06 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Patient admitted to this service with acute abdomen on 07/01/2020. Underwent exploratory laparotomy that revealed intrathoracic gastric volvulus, gastric secretion in the thoracic cavity, lesser curvature ischemia with perforation of approximately 2 centimeters. Total gastrectomy, end-to-side esophagojejunal surgical joining of bowel ends (anastomosis), roux-en-y reconstruction were performed. And remained under peritoneostomy. Evolution with refractory abdominal severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock), acute kidney injury, and disseminated intravascular coagulation. Need for multiple surgical reapproaches due to duodenal fistula. During hospitalization in the ICU (serious care room), he received broad-spectrum infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy), including polymyxin b, meropenem, micafungin, and levofloxacin. An evaluation by the comfort-focused (not curative) care team was requested, and then. Abdominal pain that had been present for 4 days prior to hospital admission. Blood test to find infection (blood culture), ascitic fluid culture, and tracheal aspirate were positive for multidrug-resistant klebsiella lung infection (pneumonia)e. Stomach tissue death (necrosis) culture was positive for candida tropicalis. Tracheal aspirate culture was positive for stenotrophomonas. Ascitic fluid culture was positive for proteus mirabilis. On january 7, total gastrectomy was performed, esophagojejunal surgical joining of bowel ends (anastomosis) was performed at the end of the lateral esophagus, and roux-en-y reconstruction was performed. He remained with peritoneostomy. On january 21, breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) was performed, and multiple revisions of the peritoneostomy were performed. The patient developed cardiorespiratory arrest in asystole and death was confirmed at , death certificate issued, family welcomed. The patient, 69 years old. Admission: 07/01/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 12/02/2020, stay: 01 m 05 d 06 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Evolution with refractory abdominal severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock), acute kidney injury, and disseminated intravascular coagulation. Need for multiple surgical reapproaches due to duodenal fistula. During hospitalization in the ICU (serious care room), he received broad-spectrum infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy), including polymyxin b, meropenem, micafungin, and levofloxacin. An evaluation by the comfort-focused (not curative) care team was requested, and then. Abdominal pain that had been present for 4 days prior to hospital admission.
