On january 7, total gastrectomy was performed, esophagojejunal surgical joining of bowel ends (anastomosis) was performed at the end of the lateral esophagus, and roux-en-y reconstruction was performed. He remained with peritoneostomy. On january 21, breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) was performed, and multiple revisions of the peritoneostomy were performed. The patient developed cardiorespiratory arrest in asystole and death was confirmed at , death certificate issued, family welcomed. n179. acute renal failure ne. r100. acute abdomen. r579. shock ne. e10. insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus. r13. dysphagia. k44. diaphragmatic hernia. k650. acute peritonitis. a419. septicemia ne. death 13.1 1.22
67 sepsis of cutaneous focus erysipelas, cellulitis left lower limb?. admission: 01/30/2020, discharge: 03/08/2020, stay: 01 m 08 d 10 h, location: ward, payer: sus. [person] referred from {city}, with report of septic shock of cutaneous focus. report of liquid diarrhea, edema and hyperemia in the left foot and ankle for 1 week, with progressive worsening, in the last 3 days, extending throughout the left lower limb, genital region and abdomen. previous history of recurrent erysipelas for years and deep vein thrombosis. [person] obese grade 3, hypertensive, with irregular use of medications. [person] with confused speech, reports being institutionalized in a nursing home, and states that the condition began with pain in the left leg, with lesions that worsened and evolved even after hospitalization. denies being hypertensive, reports taking sedatives. [person] from the nursing home accompanies [person] and is unable to report the medications she uses. The referral report is that [person] has been taking ciprofloxacin and clindamycin for 4 days, and has used NSAIDs, analgesics, and hydration at the beginning. On 01/28, in addition to the progression of lesions, she also had fever spikes of 38 to 39.6°C and began to have episodes of hypotension. For this reason, oxacillin was started. Upon admission, [person] was hypertensive with blood pressure of 200/110 mmHg, presenting audible wheezing even without a stethoscope, but which is interrupted during speech due to respiratory effort?, saturation of 95 to 96% on room air, tachypneic. She denied flu-like symptoms. A hypothesis of cardiac asthma was raised, a transthoracic echocardiogram was performed showing a left ventricular ejection fraction of 48%, vasodilation was started, and pulmonary congestion improved. fair general condition, pale /4, hydrated, anicteric. heart rate: 80 beats per minute; respiratory rate: 20 inspirations per minute; oxygen saturation: 94%; ectoscopy: extensive hyperemic lesion on flanks, extending to hips and buttocks, with presence of pustules. diffuse fine scaly lesion on trunk with beginning of grade 1 pressure ulceration, superficial; significant diffuse scaly on plantar and dorsal surfaces of feet. neurology: Isochoric and photoreactive pupils, glasgow 15, communicating, active and reactive, oriented in time, person and space; respiratory auscultation: no signs of respiratory distress. vesicular breath sounds present and symmetrical, without adventitious sounds. cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm in 2t, normal sound sounds, no murmurs, full and symmetrical radial pulses, capillary refill time 3s. Abdomen: globular, normal bowel sounds, difficult to palpate, painless on deep palpation. No signs of peritonitis. Diffuse fine scaly lesions in the abdomen, without hyperemia, no other inflammatory signs. Extremities: warm extremities, broad and symmetrical pulses. Right upper limb: no edema, no difficulty in mobilizing the limb or pain on palpation. Left upper limb: no edema, no difficulty in mobilizing the limb or pain on palpation. Right lower limb: 2/4 edema. Presence of hypochromic scarring lesions in the perimalleolar region. Previous ulcer with no other lesions. Free and painless calves. Left lower limb: 4/4 edema. Ulcer in the medial malleolar region measuring approximately 2x3 cm, with a clean base, with fibrin and a scarred appearance. On the posterior portion of the leg, there are 2 grade 2 pressure injuries, with a fibrin cap, no signs of infection. free and painless calf sacral region: grade 2 ulcers on both buttocks, approximately 6 cm long each, with no signs of infection. exams: ultrasound of lower limbs 01/29 no thrombosis in the studied segment enlarged lymph nodes, with a reactive appearance, in the left inguinal portion chest tomography 02/01: cardiomegaly with signs of pulmonary congestion, reticular opacities in the right upper lobe with a sequelae appearance transthoracic echocardiography 02/02: concentric remodeling of the LV mild dilation of the right ventricle septal dyssynergia and hypokinesia of the basal inferior and basal inferolateral and middle segments mildly depressed LVSD left ventricular ejection fraction 45% normal RVSD no signs of LV diastolic dysfunction mild insufficiency of the mitral, tricuspid and aortic valves ultrasound of the right lower limb and right upper limb 02/16 no signs of venous thrombosis in the studied segment ultrasound of the kidneys and urinary tract 02/22: within normal limits, abdominal ultrasound 02/03: slight increase in liver echogenicity, possibly corresponding to hepatic steatosis. Cholelithiasis. Clinical management. Laboratory tests and cultures. End of antibiotic therapy. Transfer to [organization] for end of antibiotic therapy. [person] able to be transferred to another hospital. Serious blood infection (sepsis) of cutaneous focus erysipelas, skin infection (cellulitis) left lower limb?. Admission: 01/30/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 03/08/2020, stay: 01 m 08 d 10 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. The patient referred from the city, with report of severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) of cutaneous focus. Report of liquid diarrhea, swelling (edema) and hyperemia in the left foot and ankle for 1 week, with progressive worsening, in the last 3 days, extending throughout the left lower limb, genital region and abdomen. Previous history of recurrent erysipelas for years and deep vein blood clot (thrombosis). The patient obese grade 3, hypertensive, with irregular use of medications. The patient with confused speech, reports being institutionalized in a nursing home, and states that the condition began with pain in the left leg, with lesions that worsened and evolved even after hospitalization. Denies being hypertensive, reports taking sedatives. The patient from the nursing home accompanies the patient and is unable to report the medications she uses. The referral report is that the patient systemic arterial high BP (hypertension) been taking ciprofloxacin and clindamycin for 4 days, and systemic arterial high BP (hypertension) used nsaids, analgesics, and hydration at the beginning. On 01/28, in addition to the progression of lesions, she also had fever spikes of 38 to 39.6°c and began to have episodes of low BP (hypotension). For this reason, oxacillin was started. Upon admission, the patient was hypertensive with blood pressure of 200/110 mmhg, presenting audible wheezing even without a stethoscope, but which is interrupted during speech due to respiratory effort?, oxygen level in blood (saturation) of 95 to 96% on room air, tachypneic. She denied flu-like symptoms. A hypothesis of cardiac asthma was raised, a transthoracic heart ultrasound (echocardiogram) was performed showing a left ventricular ejection fraction of 48%, vasodilation was started, and pulmonary congestion improved. Fair general condition, pale /4, hydrated, no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric). Heart rate: 80 beats per minute; respiratory rate: 20 inspirations per minute; oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation): 94%; ectoscopy: extensive hyperemic lesion on flanks, extending to hips and buttocks, with presence of pustules. Diffuse fine scaly lesion on trunk with beginning of grade 1 pressure ulceration, superficial; significant diffuse scaly on plantar and dorsal surfaces of feet. Neurology: isochoric and photoreactive pupils, glasgow 15, communicating, active and reactive, oriented in time, person and space; respiratory auscultation: no signs of respiratory distress. Normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds) present and symmetrical, without adventitious sounds. Cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm in 2t, normal sound sounds, no murmurs, full and symmetrical radial pulses, capillary refill time 3s. Abdomen: globular, normal bowel sounds, difficult to palpate, painless on deep palpation. No signs of infection inside the abdomen (peritonitis). Diffuse fine scaly lesions in the abdomen, without hyperemia, no other inflammatory signs. Extremities: warm extremities, broad and symmetrical pulses. Right upper limb: no swelling (edema), no difficulty in mobilizing the limb or pain on palpation. Left upper limb: no swelling (edema), no difficulty in mobilizing the limb or pain on palpation. Right lower limb: 2/4 swelling (edema). Presence of hypochromic scarring lesions in the perimalleolar region. Previous ulcer with no other lesions. Free and painless calves. Left lower limb: 4/4 swelling (edema). Ulcer in the medial malleolar region measuring approximately 2x3 cm, with a clean base, with fibrin and a scarred appearance. On the posterior portion of the leg, there are 2 grade 2 pressure injuries, with a fibrin cap, no signs of infection. Free and painless calf sacral region: grade 2 ulcers on both buttocks, approximately 6 cm long each, with no signs of infection. Exams: ultrasound of lower limbs 01/29 no blood clot (thrombosis) in the studied segment enlarged lymph nodes, with a reactive appearance, in the left inguinal portion chest tomography 02/01: cardiomegaly with signs of pulmonary congestion, reticular opacities in the right upper lobe with a sequelae appearance transthoracic echocardiography 02/02: concentric remodeling of the lv mild dilation of the right ventricle septal dyssynergia and hypokinesia of the basal inferior and basal inferolateral and middle segments mildly depressed lvsd left ventricular ejection fraction 45% normal rvsd no signs of lv diastolic dysfunction mild insufficiency of the mitral, tricuspid and aortic valves ultrasound of the right lower limb and right upper limb 02/16 no signs of venous blood clot (thrombosis) in the studied segment ultrasound of the kidneys and urinary tract 02/22: within normal limits, abdominal ultrasound 02/03: slight increase in liver echogenicity, possibly corresponding to hepatic steatosis. Cholelithiasis. Clinical management. Laboratory tests and cultures. End of infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy). Transfer to [organization] for end of infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy). The patient able to be transferred to another hospital. Serious blood infection (sepsis) of cutaneous focus erysipelas, skin infection (cellulitis) left lower limb?. 68 problem: high-risk burkitt-like lymphoma stage iv infiltration mo diagnosis 2020 right neck mass nasopharyngeal renal lesions, cervical lymph node biopsy 05/22: morphological and immunohistochemical picture of b-cell lymphoma, with small, intermediate-sized cells and high proliferative index. immunohistochemistry: positivity for cd20, pax5, weak cd10, bcl6. negativity for cd34, tdt, bcl2, cmyc, ki67 of 95 100%. cd3 evidenced the population of reactive t lymphocytes. cmyc negativity rules out the diagnosis of burkitt's lymphoma. the present case may correspond to burkittlike lymphoma with 11q aberration nasopharyngeal mass biopsy 05/26: b lymphoma with high mitotic index cerebrospinal fluid puncture 06/05: no bmo infiltration 06/03/2020: diffusely infiltrated by b lymphoma with high mitotic index. cd19 / cd 20 / cd10 / bcl 6 if de mo 06/04: cd 19 / cd 20 / cd10 / cd 43 / cd 81 / lambda collection of material for semen cryopreservation 06/05/2020 ecog 0. admission: 13/08/2020, discharge: 08/21/2020, stay: 07 d 22 h, location: ward, payer: sus. [person] diagnosed with Burkitt's lymphoma was admitted to the hospital for a second cycle of Rivac D1: 14/08/2020. On 08/16, D3 of the second Rivac, he had a fever peak of 38.1 °C, which lasted for about 1 hour. He reported nausea and had had 3 episodes of bowel movements with softer stools on the day in question. He also had a blood count on the day with 2,000 neutrophils, but already falling, with a tendency towards neutropenia. Given this, the condition was managed as febrile neutropenia, with a probable abdominal focus of infection. It was decided to start cefepime and metronidazole. He underwent a chest CT scan without changes, and a CT scan of the sinuses with evidence of content in the frontal sinus and mastoid. An endoscopic evaluation of the sinuses was performed by [organization], with no evidence of invasive fungal infection. remained on intravenous antibiotic therapy until 08/20, when antibiotic therapy was discussed with [organization], and it was decided to transition to oral clavulin ciprofloxacin. In addition to this, he underwent chemotherapy to date without other complications. He required a transfusion of 1 red blood cell concentrate on 08/19, without complications. Normal physical examination upon discharge. No procedures were performed. Diagnosis already established. 1. I provide folinic acid 15 mg 1 tablet, to be taken on 09/01/20 to be taken 24 h after intrathecal chemotherapy 2. I prescribe amoxicillin clavulanate 875 125 mg and ciprofloxacin 500 mg, to be used every 12/12 h, until 8 am on 08/31 3. I prescribe filgrastim for use of 600 mg 2 ampoules in the morning and 300 mg 1 ampoule at night, via subcutaneous application, every day, until the follow-up appointment on 08/27 4. I prescribe prophylaxis: acyclovir 200 mg 2 tablets every 12/12 h sulfamethoxazole trimethoprim 400 80 mg 1 tablet every 12/12 h on Mondays, Wednesdays and Fridays omeprazole 20 mg 1 capsule on an empty stomach 5. I prescribe, for use in case of constipation and no bowel movement: lactulose syrup 15 ml every 8 hours, and bisacodyl 5 mg 1 tablet at night. 6. I prescribe ondansetron, in case of nausea or vomiting. 08/25 blood center transfusion room 08/27 outside hematology daytime room 10 counter 6 HC 08/31 madit outside hematology daytime room 10 counter 6 HC 09/15 [organization] safety follow-up appointment for clinical reevaluation and check PETCT scheduling counter 6 HC. Good. Problem: high-risk burkitt-like lymphoma stage iv infiltration mo diagnosis 2020 right neck mass nasopharyngeal renal lesions, cervical lymph node biopsy 05/22: morphological and immunohistochemical picture of b-cell lymphoma, with small, intermediate-sized cells and high proliferative index. Immunohistochemistry: positivity for cd20, pax5, weak cd10, bcl6. Negativity for cd34, tdt, bcl2, cmyc, ki67 of 95 100%. Cd3 evidenced the population of reactive t lymphocytes. Cmyc negativity rules out the diagnosis of burkitt's lymphoma. The present case may correspond to burkittlike lymphoma with 11q aberration nasopharyngeal mass biopsy 05/26: b lymphoma with high mitotic index cerebrospinal fluid puncture 06/05: no bmo infiltration 06/03/2020: diffusely infiltrated by b lymphoma with high mitotic index. Cd19 / cd 20 / cd10 / bcl 6 if de mo 06/04: cd 19 / cd 20 / cd10 / cd 43 / cd 81 / lambda collection of material for semen cryopreservation 06/05/2020 ecog 0. An endoscopic evaluation of the sinuses was performed by [organization], with no evidence of invasive fungal infection. In addition to this, he underwent chemotherapy to date without other complications. He required a transfusion of 1 red blood cell concentrate on 08/19, without complications. I prescribe prophylaxis: acyclovir 200 mg 2 tablets every 12/12 h sulfamethoxazole trimethoprim 400 80 mg 1 tablet every 12/12 h on mondays, wednesdays and fridays omeprazole 20 mg 1 capsule on an empty stomach 5. I prescribe, for use in case of constipation and no bowel movement: lactulose syrup 15 ml every 8 hours, and bisacodyl 5 mg 1 tablet at night. 6. I prescribe ondansetron, in case of nausea or vomiting. 08/25 blood center transfusion room 08/27 outside hematology daytime room 10 counter 6 hc 08/31 madit outside hematology daytime room 10 counter 6 hc 09/15 [organization] safety return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment for clinical reevaluation and check petct scheduling counter 6 hc. Admission: 13/08/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 08/21/2020, stay: 07 d 22 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. The patient diagnosed with burkitt's lymphoma was admitted to the hospital for a second cycle of rivac d1: 14/08/2020. On 08/16, d3 of the second rivac, he had a fever peak of 38.1 °c, which lasted for about 1 hour. He reported nausea on the day of the day. He also had a blood count of 2,000 neutrophils, but had a tendency togeneral hospital rooms neutropenia. Given this, he had a probable abdominal focus on ciprofloxacin and metronidazole. He underwent a chest ct scan without changes in the sinuses. He found that a lot of blood samples were found in the frontal sinus and mastoid cells. However, there was no evidence of invasive fungal infection. He didn't have a specific infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) until 08/20, when he was diagnosed with the disease. It was decided to make sure that he was able to get a blood test without changes. This means that he could be able to see if he had had a problem with the bloodstream. This could lead to more complications, especially if he was unable to get the test done. This can be a good way to prevent the infection, but it's important to keep it safe for everyone to get it done.</s> I prescribe amoxicillin clavulanate 875 125 mg and ciprofloxacin 500 mg, to be used every 12/12 h, until 8 am on 08/31 3. I prescribe filgrastim for use of 600 mg 2 ampoules in the morning and 300 mg 1 ampoule at night, via subcutaneous application, every day, until the return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment on 08/27 4. I prescribe prophylaxis: acyclovir 200 mg 2 tablets every 12/12 h sulfamethoxazole trimethoprim 400 80 mg 1 tablet every 12/12 h on mondays, wednesdays and fridays omeprazole 20 mg 1 capsule on an empty stomach 5. I prescribe, for use in case of constipation and no bowel movement: lactulose syrup 15 ml every 8 hours, and bisacodyl 5 mg 1 tablet at night. 6. I prescribe ondansetron, in case of nausea or vomiting. 08/25 blood center transfusion room 08/27 outside hematology daytime room 10 counter 6 hc 08/31 madit outside hematology daytime room 10 counter 6 hc 09/15 [organization] safety return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment for clinical reevaluation and check petct scheduling counter 6 hc. Good. r599. adenomegaly or enlarged lymph nodes, nes. a419. septicemia nes. b965. pseudomonas aeruginosa mallei pseudomallei, as a cause of classifed diseases. c851. b-cell lymphoma, unspecified. d180. hemangioma of any location. d479. neoplasm of uncertain or unknown behavior of lymphatic or hematopoietic tissue. k053. chronic periodontitis. r220. localized swelling, mass, or tumorization of head. u071. novel coronavirus infection. d70. agranulocytosis. c837. burkitt's tumor. alive 45.0 1.00
69 traumatic brain injury, meningitis. admission: 2020-01-08, discharge: 2020-02-17, stay: 01 m 09 d 13 h, location: ward, payer: sus. clinical summary: [person] admitted to [organization] on 2020-01-08 due to mental confusion, dysphagia, dyspnea, inability to walk, dysarthria, and hearing loss in the left ear. he fell off a bicycle in december 2019, presenting with mild head trauma and exposed skull fracture treated clinically with an empirical antibiotic regimen. he was discharged at that time {omitted}. on 2020-01-08, upon readmission to {organization}, he underwent a cranial computed tomography scan, which revealed hydrocephalus. an indication for an external ventricular shunt was given. During surgery, an infectious aspect of the cerebrospinal fluid was noted and drug treatment for meningitis and ventriculitis was initiated. Culture tests revealed pneumococcal infection and treatment directed at the bacteria was initiated, while the patient was in an intensive care unit bed. During hospitalization, [person] developed significant neurological deterioration related to a serious infection of the central nervous system. He also presented bullous lesions in the thighs and chest, with a hypothesis of drug eruption. At the moment, [person] remains hospitalized, under the care of [organization], still in a serious neurological condition, with improvement in skin lesions, and the current condition has not changed for about 3 weeks. physical and neurological examination: eupneic, with tracheostomy and macronebulization, normal color; cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm in two beats, normal heart sounds without murmurs; respiratory auscultation: vesicular breath sounds without adventitious sounds; glasgow 5t; anisochoric pupils, left larger than right and reactant; spontaneous breathing; sporadic Cheyne Stokes rhythm; ventriculoperitoneal shunt inserted on 02/06; clean and dry cranial and abdominal surgical wounds; traumatic brain injury; traumatic brain injury - meningitis; ventriculoperitoneal shunt; transfer: given the poor neurological prognosis and the absence of new neurosurgical measures to be undertaken at this time, I request a backup bed {omitted}; [person]. is not currently taking antibiotics and has no signs of an ongoing infectious process. follow-up: consultation [organization] on 23/02, consultation [organization] on 22/06. follow-up: consultation {organization} on 23/02 consultation {organization} neca outpatient clinic 22/06/20 8h. clinically stable. Traumatic brain injury, brain membrane infection (meningitis). Admission: 2020-01-08, sent home from hospital (discharge): 2020-02-17, stay: 01 m 09 d 13 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Clinical summary: the patient admitted to [organization] on 2020-01-08 due to mental confusion, dysphagia, difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), inability to walk, dysarthria, and hearing loss in the left ear. He fell off a bicycle in december 2019, presenting with mild head trauma and exposed skull fracture treated clinically with an empirical infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) regimen. He was sent home from hospital (discharge)d at that time . On 2020-01-08, upon readmission to the hospital, he underwent a cranial CT scan (computed tomography) scan, which revealed hydrocephalus. An indication for an external ventricular shunt was given. During surgery, an infectious aspect of the cerebrospinal fluid was noted and drug treatment for brain membrane infection (meningitis) and ventriculitis was initiated. Culture tests revealed pneumococcal infection and treatment directed at the bacteria was initiated, while the patient was in an ICU (serious care room) bed. During hospitalization, the patient developed significant neurological deterioration related to a serious infection of the central nervous system. He also presented bullous lesions in the thighs and chest, with a hypothesis of drug eruption. At the moment, the patient remains hospitalized, under the care of [organization], still in a serious neurological condition, with improvement in skin lesions, and the current condition systemic arterial high BP (hypertension) not changed for about 3 weeks. Physical and neurological examination: breathing normally (eupneic), with breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) and macronebulization, normal color; cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm in two beats, normal heart sounds without murmurs; respiratory auscultation: normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds) without adventitious sounds; glasgow 5t; anisochoric pupils, left larger than right and reactant; spontaneous breathing; sporadic cheyne stokes rhythm; ventriculoperitoneal shunt inserted on 02/06; clean and dry cranial and abdominal surgical wounds; traumatic brain injury; traumatic brain injury - brain membrane infection (meningitis); ventriculoperitoneal shunt; transfer: given the poor neurological expected outcome (prognosis) and the absence of new neurosurgical measures to be undertaken at this time, i request a backup bed ; the patient. Is not currently taking infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic)s and systemic arterial high BP (hypertension) no signs of an ongoing infectious process. Return visit to doctor (follow-up): consultation [organization] on 23/02, consultation [organization] on 22/06. Return visit to doctor (follow-up): consultation the hospital on 23/02 consultation the hospital neca OPD (Out Patient Department) 22/06/20 8h. Clinically stable. During hospitalization, the patient developed significant neurological deterioration related to a serious infection of the central nervous system. He also presented bullous lesions in the thighs and chest, with a hypothesis of drug eruption. At the moment, the patient remains hospitalized, under the care of [organization], still in a serious neurological condition, with improvement in skin lesions, and the current condition systemic arterial high BP (hypertension) not changed for about 3 weeks. 70 admission: 10/06/2020, discharge: 11/20/2020, stay: 01 m 13 d 19 h, location: ward, payer: sus. discharge report patient [person], 54 years old, single, from [address], receives retirement benefit, admitted to the emergency unit of [organization] on 10/06 with dyspnea 8 days after admission, fever not measured and cough with greenish sputum. on 10/08, he presented respiratory failure, requiring orotracheal intubation and high oxygen concentrations pao2/fio2 ratio: 104, with neuromuscular blockade for ventilation for sara. On 10/9, due to oliguria in the last 24 hours, however, maintaining a positive fluid balance, he was transferred to the semi-intensive care unit without the use of vasoactive drugs due to the hypothesis of nephrotoxicity. On 10/10, vasoactive drugs were restarted due to poor peripheral perfusion. Neuromuscular blockade with cisatracurium was suspended. [person] developed abdominal distension, with suspension of the diet. On 10/14, an abdominal ultrasound was performed with no signs of ascites or gaseous distension. On 10/15, sedation was suspended. On the same day, [person] presented with a deviation of the fixed gaze to the right, being evaluated by neurology and, after a computed tomography scan of the skull and electroencephalogram, without new alterations that would justify the condition, vitamin B1 replacement was indicated. On 10/19, a thoracic ultrasound was performed, showing evidence of pleural effusion on the left, measuring approximately 3.3 cm. Diagnostic thoracentesis was performed, with negative transudate culture and bacilloscopy, but with positive TBTRM on 10/22. At the time, a case of ICC was discussed, and as [person] did not present a picture suggestive of pulmonary tuberculosis upon admission, with no characteristic image, remaining afebrile throughout hospitalization, with progressive improvement of the pulmonary condition with Bactrim, in addition to 3 negative gastric lavage bacilloscopy samples in August of that year, it was decided not to isolate [person] and not to collect bacilloscopy again, since the diagnosis of pulmonary tuberculosis was very unlikely. On 10/22, extubation was performed, without complications. On 10/23, [person] presented diarrhea, with liquid stools, no pathological products and no other systemic symptoms. On 10/25, he started a soft oral diet with good acceptance, and the nasogastric tube was removed and treatment for pleural tuberculosis was initiated by the attending medical team. On 10/26, [person] was admitted to a bed in the [organization] ward and, on 10/29, transferred to a bed in the [organization] ward, under the care of the infectious disease team. On 10/28, treatment for pneumocystosis with Bactrim D21/D21 was suspended and corticosteroid therapy was suspended, with Bactrim being resumed at a prophylactic dose for neurotoxoplasmosis and pneumocystosis. During hospitalization, [person] presented persistent thrombocytopenia while on prophylactic anticoagulation. Hematology assessed him and indicated the start of full-dose subcutaneous fondaparinoux 7.5 mg. On 11/3, with a platelet count above 150,000, dual anticoagulation with warfarin 5 mg/day was initiated. maintained until 11/08, with warfarin adjustment according to prothrombin time, due to anticoagulation for 4 weeks. In addition, [person] presented anemia probably secondary to azathioprine myelotoxicity and vitamin B12 deficiency. Vitamin B12 replacement was started, 1 vial of 5000 mcg 1x per week. Upon admission to the [organization] ward, it was decided to suspend the RIP regimen due to an acute clinical laboratory picture not compatible with pulmonary or pleural tuberculosis. [person] presented infectious urinalysis culture positive for Klebsiella pneumoniae carbapenemase, but as he remained asymptomatic and with good renal function, it was decided not to treat it, considering contamination. [person] is currently fit for hospital discharge, being on room air, with no signs of discomfort, hemodynamically stable, not using vasoactive drugs, and with good acceptance of oral diet, assisted only by the risk of bronchoaspiration, with international normalized ratio in the range of 2.9 with 5 mg of warfarin/day. Current problem 1. Nosocomial pneumonia? Recent admission to [organization] 08/14 09/25 Community-acquired pneumonia treated with clavulin, pcp Presumed hepatotoxicity due to bactrin Nosocomial pneumonia treated with cefepime 2. Pneumocystosis? 3. Normochromic macrocytic anemia to be clarified secondary to bactrim? Secondary to zidovudine? 4. Thrombocytopenia to be clarified Thrombocytopenia induced by prophylactic heparin? Tests 1. Transthoracic echocardiography 08/17: cardiac chambers with normal dimensions. left ventricular systolic performance and segmental mobility were normal. 2. abdominal ultrasound 8/17: ultrasound signs that may correspond to liver disease, with no evidence of suspicious focal lesions. absence of free intra-abdominal fluid. problem: 1. human immunodeficiency virus c3 diagnosed in 2004 8/17: cd4 135/cv: undetectable 2. neurotoxoplasmosis with motor sequelae in the right upper limb 3. anti hcv / hcv pcr negative 4. neurological disorder to be clarified wernicke's encephalopathy? psychiatric disorder to be clarified 5. chronic liver disease diagnosed in 2016 6. presumed chronic obstructive pulmonary disease medications in use risperidone 2 mg at night warfarin 5 mg/day until 12/01 discontinue after antiretroviral therapy: tenofovir 300 mg lamivudine 300 mg 1 tablet early darunavir 600 mg 1 tablet 12/12h ritonavir 100 mg 1 tablet 12/12h sulfamethoxazole trimethoprim 400 80 mg 1 tablet 12/12h pcp prophylaxis and neurotoxicity folinic acid 15 mg 1 tablet/day. no changes.. programming we consider anemia secondary to probable myelotoxicity due to antiretroviral therapy AZT being replaced by tenofovir on 11/09. I maintain prophylaxis for PCP and neurotox with Bactrim, maintain folinic acid replacement 15 mg/day until follow-up appointment, decided to suspend vitamin B12 replacement, given 2 recent dosages with normal vitamin B12, maintain anticoagulation with warfarin started on 11/03 for 4 weeks until 12/01. follow-up appointment to the outpatient clinic, request tests for follow-up appointment. Good. Admission: 10/06/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 11/20/2020, stay: 01 m 13 d 19 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. On 10/08, he presented lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure), requiring orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) and high oxygen concentrations pao2/fio2 ratio: 104, with neuromuscular blockade for ventilation for sara. On 10/9, due to oliguria in the last 24 hours, however, maintaining a positive fluid balance, he was transferred to the semi-ICU (serious care room) without the use of vasoactive drugs due to the hypothesis of nephrotoxicity. On 10/19, a thoracic ultrasound was performed, showing evidence of fluid around the lungs (pleural effusion) on the left, measuring approximately 3.3 cm. Diagnostic thoracentesis was performed, with negative transudate culture and bacilloscopy, but with positive tbtrm on 10/22. At the time, a case of heart failure was discussed, and as the patient did not present a picture suggestive of pulmonary TB (tuberculosis) upon admission, with no characteristic image, remaining no fever (afebrile) throughout hospitalization, with progressive improvement of the pulmonary condition with bactrim, in addition to 3 negative gastric lavage bacilloscopy samples in august of that year, it was decided not to isolate the patient and not to collect bacilloscopy again, since the diagnosis of pulmonary TB (tuberculosis) was very unlikely. On 10/22, breathing tube removed (extubation) was performed, without complications. On 10/23, the patient presented diarrhea, with liquid stools, no pathological products and no other systemic symptoms. On 10/25, he started a soft oral diet with good acceptance, and the nasogastric tube was removed and treatment for pleural TB (tuberculosis) was initiated by the attending medical team. On 10/26, the patient was admitted to a bed in the [organization] general hospital room (ward) and, on 10/29, transferred to a bed in the [organization] general hospital room (ward), under the care of the infectious disease team. During hospitalization, the patient presented persistent low platelet count while on prophylactic anticoagulation. Hematology assessed him and indicated the start of full-dose subcutaneous fondaparinoux 7.5 mg. On 11/3, with a platelet count above 150,000, dual anticoagulation with warfarin 5 mg/day was initiated. Maintained until 11/08, with warfarin adjustment according to prothrombin time, due to anticoagulation for 4 weeks. In addition, the patient presented low blood (anemia) probably secondary to azathioprine myelotoxicity and vitamin b12 deficiency. Vitamin b12 replacement was started, 1 vial of 5000 mcg 1x per week. Upon admission to the [organization] general hospital room (ward), it was decided to government health schemepend the rip regimen due to an acute clinical laboratory picture not compatible with pulmonary or pleural TB (tuberculosis). The patient presented infectious urinalysis culture positive for klebsiella lung infection (pneumonia)e carbapenemase, but as he remained asymptomatic and with good renal function, it was decided not to treat it, considering contamination. The patient is currently fit for hospital sent home from hospital (discharge), being on room air, with no signs of discomfort, stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), not using vasoactive drugs, and with good acceptance of oral diet, assisted only by the risk of bronchoaspiration, with international normalized ratio in the range of 2.9 with 5 mg of warfarin/day. 71 disseminated histoplasmosis. admission: 10/25/2022, discharge: 11/08/2022, stay: 14 d 02 h, location: ward, payer: sus. problem: disseminated pulmonary and cutaneous histoplasmosis cie 1/16 histoplasmosis, x-ray with infiltrate in the left apex biopsy of compatible skin lesion. previous hospitalization mi: 09/20 10/09 reason: worsening of renal function urinary tract infection, previous use of indwelling bladder catheter, with urine culture for pseudomonas aeruginosa 09/21 and 09/30 14 days of meropenem 09/22 to 10/06 skin lesion on the back proteus culture 8 days of vancomycin 09/30 to 10/08 investigation pulmonary condition: chest tomography 06/21/2022 bilateral centrilobular masses and opacities, compatible with granulomatous tuberculosis or fungal infection. remote differential diagnosis with multifocal neoplasia is made. bronchopathy. histoplasmosis 1/16 bacilloscopy and culture of 27/06 saliva without baar bacilloscopy 29/06 negative induced sputum 2 samples tb trm induced sputum 29/06 negative 2 samples investigation skin condition: skin biopsy 22/06: deep granulomatous cutaneous mycosis. the characteristics of the yeasts are suggestive of histoplasma capsulatum, however it is not possible to exclude paracoccidioidomycosis with small yeasts or even other fungal species culture growth of pseudomonas multi s tbtrm negative baar negative direct mycological negative culture for fungi with growth of bacteria antecedents: 1. kidney transplant 2004 {omitted} 2. iamssst in 2012 treatment with revascularization 3. ischemic stroke in 2016 with left hemiparesis 4. efs 5. sebaceous carcinoma in the left breast in 2008 exeresis of the lesion with reapproach to deepen margins without follow-up after previous exams: skin biopsy 10/2021: deep mycosis with lateral sporulation, without identification did not have adequate treatment exeresis of skin lesion in the left anterior chest 05/19/2021: sebaceous carcinoma without vascular infiltration, margin free lateral, free deep margin, but narrow, in the sections analyzed. medications in use: prednisone 5 mg/day acetylsalicylic acid 100 mg/day omeprazole 20 mg/day mycophenolate 360 101 atorvastatin 40 mg amiodarone 200 mg/day sertraline 25 mg/day clobazan lamotrigine amitriptyline, reason for hospitalization: aspiration pneumonia, with worsening of renal function and elevation of transaminases on use of fluconazole. [person] under follow-up of disseminated histoplasmosis, cutaneous and pulmonary, came for an outpatient follow-up consultation after hospital discharge, on 10/25, maintaining treatment with fluconazole at reduced doses of 150 mg/day. Asymptomatic and with no reports of new skin lesions, but reporting a 3-day history of daily fever spikes, predominantly in the afternoon, associated with cough, sometimes productive, sometimes dry. In laboratory evaluation, elevated transaminases and canaliculars were seen (tgo: 133 / tgp: 182 / atrial fibrillation: 1315), associated with worsening renal function (urea: 82 / creatinine: 1.35). HB drop to be clarified during hospitalization iron 15.5, tibic 154, ferritin 486 ist 10% folic acid 1.61 b12 1060 without exteriorization, omeprazole full dose 01/05 7. infiltrate in the base of the right hemithorax community-acquired pneumonia? aspiration pneumonia in the context of seizure crisis and orotracheal intubation? treated ceftriaxone clindamycin d7/d7 8. heart failure profile c cardiogenic shock resolved dobutamine 02/01 05/01 9. pneumothorax {omitted} 02/01 resolved chest drain 02/01 04/01. admission: 01/01/2020, discharge: 01/20/2020, stay: 19 d, location: ward, payer: sus. history summary: alcoholic libation on 12/31. he presented to the emergency room confused, hypoglycemia was observed. report of having been administered glucose solution and thiamine, with partial improvement in the level of consciousness, but with subsequent evolution of generalized tonic-clonic seizures according to referral. after this event, he was intubated decreased level of consciousness? irpa?. personal history: heart failure with reduced ejection fraction left ventricular ejection fraction 22%, dilated ventricular septal defect possibly ischemic coronary artery disease bilateral submassive pulmonary thromboembolism in October/2019 started rivaroxaban current smoking 1 pack/day 56 years/pack active alcoholic drinker use of spirits nodular prostatic hyperplasia home medications: acetylsalicylic acid 100 mg/day enalapril 10 mg 12/12 hours carvedilol 6.25 12/12 hours spironolactone 25 mg/day atorvastatone 40 mg/day rivaroxaban 20 mg/day complementary tests current hospitalization computed tomography of the skull 01/01/22: diffuse global atrophy, no bleeding CT Angiography (CTA) 01/01: no occlusion of large vessels hospitalization 10/22 transthoracic echocardiography 06/10: ao: 29/ ae: 31/ fe: 22%. mild im. mild iao. mild dilatation of ae and moderate of ve. septal dyssynergia. akinesia of the entire septum and inferior wall. anteroapical hypokinesia, am, alm, alp. eccentric hypertrophy of ve. catheterization 10/10: cd 80% lesion in the middle segment of the 40 50% moderate in the middle segment cx 30% in the middle segment 1st mg 40% proximal segment devices femoral central venous catheter 13/01 previous tot no. 7.5 rima 23 01/01 04/01 right chest drain 02/01 04/01 central venous catheter right internal jugular vein 02/01 09/01 negative antibiotic therapy current: meropenem vancomycin 13/01 previous: ceftriaxone 02/01 10/01 clindamycin in 03/01 10/01 piperacillin tazobactan. antibiotic therapy: meropenem vancomycin d0 13/01 cultures: urine culture 13/01: negative blood culture 02/01: staphylococcus haemolyticus blood culture 12/01: negative blood culture 13/01: negative. sepsis of pulmonary focus, urinary in resolution as to infectious condition: maintained meropenem vancomycin d7/d10 as to heart failure: introduced enalapril 2.5 mg 12/12h consider starting beta-blocker after completion of antibiotic therapy general: dextrose measurement only if signs of hypoglycemia oral diet seen good acceptance awaiting psa result awaiting definitive ultrasound report of kidneys and urinary tract provisional without changes seen hospitalization to complete antibiotic therapy and introduction of medications for heart failure. 73 [person], 72 years old, born in {city}, from {city} weight 85 kg height 170 cm bmi: 29.4 kg/m2 pp: 66 kg vc 68ml/kg: 396/462/528 ml basal energy expenditure 2125 kcal/day kcal/kg proteins: 100g 1.5 g/kg of maximum ideal weight, admission: 08/19/2020, discharge: 09/18/2020, stay: 30 d 14 h, location: ward, payer: sus. 1 severe acute respiratory syndrome secondary to coronavirus, resolved onset of symptoms: 08/10 dry cough and fever worsening of symptoms: 08/19 dyspnea with minimal effort and ventilatory pain, dependent on orotracheal intubation 08/22 08/31 proning 08/22 and 08/25 oxygen turned off 09/18/20 at 10 am 2 chest tomography 09/11/2020: changes compatible with the evolution of severe viral pneumonia, organizing pneumonia pattern with important areas of fibrorepair. started methylprednisone 40 mg 12/12h day 09/11 09/18 transitioned to prednisone 40 mg/day on 09/18 schedule: 3 days and discontinue, 3 tracheobronchitis, pneumonia associated with mechanical ventilation treated, used cefepime vancomycin 09/01 09/09 previous antibiotic therapy: clavulin 08/15 08/21 external service ceftriaxone 08/21 08/26 clarithromycin 08/19 08/24 oxacillin 08/21 08/26 meropenem 08/27 09/01 cefepime: 09/02 09/09 vancomycin: 09/01 09/09 corticosteroid therapy: dexamethasone 6 mg/day 08/19 08/28 methylprednisone 40 mg 12/12h 09/11 09/18 prednisone 40 mg/day 09/18 cultures: tracheal aspirate culture 08/22 positive for Enterobacter cloacae multi s urine culture of 08/30 positive for fungi awaiting identification, blood culture 08/27 with growth of Staphylococcus capitis in both samples and of 08/20 of Staphylococcus hominis. history: diabetes mellitus diagnosed 20 years ago, insulin dependent for some years systemic arterial hypertension, benign prostatic hyperplasia, ex-smoker 18 years/pack medications previous use: NPH insulin? doxazosin finasteride suspended during hospitalization, maintaining spontaneous diuresis in diaper, acetylsalicylic acid 100 mg/day suspended during hospitalization primary prophylaxis patient convalescing from coronavirus infection, not in respiratory isolation, without antibiotic therapy, on corticosteroid therapy for weaning from oxygen and treatment of organizing pneumonia. turned off oxygen on 09/18. will be transferred to {organization} to end corticosteroid therapy and observation without oxygen for 48 hours. presented desaturation yesterday, now resolved, having undergone chest CT Angiography (CTA) that ruled out pulmonary thromboembolism and showed viral pneumonia in resolution. performed serum therapy for prophylaxis of contrast nephropathy, suspended today. good general condition, flushed, hydrated, acyanotic, anicteric, afebrile, eupneic vital signs: heart rate 89 beats per minute, respiratory rate 24 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation 91% on nasal catheter 4 l oxygen/min, 92% on room air cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm, no murmurs auscultated. capillary refill time less than 3 seconds respiratory auscultation vesicular breath sounds, decreased at the bases, more important at the left base. abdomen: globular, bowel sounds in all quadrants, flaccid, painless on superficial and deep palpation, no masses palpated, no visceromegaly palpated. lower limbs: no edema, free calves, no signs of deep vein thrombosis. Chest tomography 09/11: changes compatible with the progression of severe vital pneumonia, organizing pneumonia pattern with important areas of fibrorepair. Therapeutic schedule: end of corticosteroid therapy transitioned from methylprednisolone to prednisone 40 mg/day today for use for 3 days until d10 with complete suspension afterwards. Monitoring of renal function after computed tomography angiography performed on 09/17/20. Monitoring of ventilatory pattern for 48 hours after suspension of oxygen therapy. Follow-up of systemic arterial hypertension, diabetes mellitus and BPH in a basic health unit. follow-up appointment to AMINC on 11/12 with protocol exams for reevaluation. Turned off oxygen on 09/18. Will be transferred to the hospital to end corticosteroid therapy and observation without oxygen for 48 hours. Presented deoxygen level in blood (saturation) yesterday, now resolved, having undergone chest ct angiography (cta) that ruled out pulmonary thromboblood clot blocking a vessel (embolism) and showed viral lung infection (pneumonia) in resolution. Performed serum therapy for prophylaxis of contrast nephropathy, government health schemepended today. Good general condition, flushed, hydrated, no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no fever (afebrile), breathing normally (eupneic) vital signs: heart rate 89 beats per minute, respiratory rate 24 breaths per minute, oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation) 91% on nasal catheter 4 l oxygen/min, 92% on room air cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm, no murmurs auscultated. Capillary refill time less than 3 seconds respiratory auscultation normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds), decreased at the bases, more important at the left base. Abdomen: globular, bowel sounds in all quadrants, flaccid, painless on superficial and deep palpation, no masses palpated, no visceromegaly palpated. Lower limbs: no swelling (edema), free calves, no signs of deep vein blood clot (thrombosis). Chest tomography 09/11: changes compatible with the progression of severe vital lung infection (pneumonia), organizing lung infection (pneumonia) pattern with important areas of fibrorepair. Monitoring of ventilatory pattern for 48 hours after government health scheme (sus)pension of oxygen therapy. The patient, 72 years old, born in the city, was born in a small town, with a weight of about 30 kg, weighed about 15 cm bmi: 29.4 kg/m2 pp: 66ml/kg. The patient's body weight is about 30 cm, and it's hard to get enough energy. The doctor said that the condition of the patient was worse than the patient. He said that it was a serious condition and had a lot of hard work, but it was harder to get it to the doctor's office. He also said that there were many problems with the symptoms of severe viral lung infection (pneumonia), such as difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), chest pain, orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation), and other problems. However, the doctor didn't agree with the changes in the treatment. The hospital's findings show that the patient had a severe respiratory syndrome in the area of fibrorepair. The study suggests that people with severe viral fever are more likely to experience more pain and ventilatory pain, depending on how long it is going to be, and how much it can be used to treat it. The researchers also found that these changes are related to severe viral infections, especially in the areas of chest tomography, like the bacteria and other diseases, like cancer, and infections. 10/26: electroencephalogram with no status 10/26: circulatory shock to be clarified 10/27: transferred to intensive care unit, right-sided tension pneumothorax, drained by general surgery 10/27: acute kidney injury, still without evaluation by nephrology until definition of conduct by neurology, 10/27: transthoracic echocardiography: left ventricular ejection fraction 66%, ae: 53 ml/ae indexed: 35 ml/m²/systolic performance and left ventricular segmental mobility normal left atrial dilation, left ventricular remodeling, tricuspid insufficiency and moderate aortic insufficiency, mild mitral insufficiency 10/29: total sedation suspended 10/30: acute kidney injury in resolution 10/30: chest tube removed by general surgery 10/31: tracheostomy requested, tcle issued 11/02: access change 11/03: Glasgow coma scale: 9t 11/03: suap electroencephalogram, ade adjustment, fnt suspended, phenobarbital maintained and lamotrigine associated 11/04: septic shock with no defined focus meropenem and vancomycin empirical treatment infection with no defined focus vancocinemia 11/07 23.5. next dose in 24 h after new vancocinemia 11/07 presented a period of fatigability in macronebulization. follow-up appointment to invasive mechanical ventilation on the night shift. chest x-ray with maintained pattern atelectatic beam in the right base maintained in psv ps 5 peep 08 fio2 35% oxygen saturation 97% respiratory rate 28 mechanical ventilation 9.7 vc 340, well attached to a ventilatory prosthesis. History of present illness according to [person] presented nonspecific malaise this morning seeking medical care, being admitted to [organization] with a hypertensive peak, being medicated and discharged. At 12:00 p.m. she presented sudden bilateral blindness, lasting about a few minutes and without precision of time, with complete improvement afterwards. On {omitted} 10/22/2020 she presented labial rhyme deviation and dysarthria. There was no perception of paresis, stigmata of crisis or lowered level of consciousness. Functionality: partially dependent for ivds. Difficulty dealing with money and forgets some events. mrs 3. neurological: glasgow coma scale 9t to 2 / rv 1t / rm 4 nihss 25 1a.1 / 1b.2 / 1c.1 / 5a.4 / 5b.3 / 6a.4 / 6b.4 / 7.nt / 8.1 / 9.3 / 10.2 / 11.nt. regular general condition, anicteric, acyanotic, normal color, hydrated, eupneic in room air, confused, frequent left hemifacial spasms and bilateral eye blinking, respiratory system: universally audible vesicular breath soundss, no adventitious sounds; cardiovascular system: regular heart rhythm in normal S1 and S2, normal heart sounds, no murmurs; abdominal: atypical, peristaltic, flaccid, painless on palpation, I do not palpate masses or visceromegaly; lower limbs: no edema, calves without signs of deep vein thrombosis, preserved peripheral capillary perfusion of less than 3 seconds; neurological: Glasgow 13 O4 / V3 / M6, NIHSS 6 1b.1 / 5.e1 / 6.e1 / 10.2 / 11.1, isochoric and photoreactive pupils. CT scan of the skull on admission: multiple frontal and parietal malacea on the right, posterior watershed pattern on the right, global atrophy, ventriculomegaly exvacuum angio CT scan of the skull and cervical vessels on admission: heterogeneous plaques in the 4 mm aortic arch and left subclavian, bilateral M1 and left M2 stenoses, fetal pattern on the left, stenosis in left V0 due to calcified plaque, dominant left vertebral, atheromatosis in carotid siphon worse on the right CT scan of the skull control after worsening of the deficit 13 h: maintained CT scan of the skull ctrl 30/10: formation of hypodensity in the insula and right temporal. Probable low-flow mechanism seen stenosis in the right M1 MCA. Cranial computed tomography control 10/11: findings from the last computed tomography scan 10/30 maintained, hypodensity in the right insula and findings already present in the admission cranial Conduct: Regarding neurological: maintains decreased level of consciousness, low cerebral reserve due to the presence of multiple lesions secondary to ischemic events and possible previous dementia: palliative care. Regarding AEDs: maintain progression of lamotrigine current dose 25 mg 12/12h next increase of 25 mg on 11/17 verify phenobarbital dosage on 11/10: 24.2. Schedule to gradually cease after reaching target dose of lamotrigine verify electroencephalogram on 11/08. I maintain AEDs Regarding proportional care: anesthesia with morphine 3 mg 8/8 hours and 4 mg acm. Today I contact the patient's daughter [person], I explain about the irreversibility of the condition and the harms of invasive measures. I ask about the possibility of home care, but [person] says he is unable to. I offer a long-term bed, which she accepts. Although the etfg is only an estimate of the glomerular filtration rate, it provides the clinician with a better assessment of renal function than serum kidney function marker (creatinine) alone. Reference values and staging, according to kdigo 2013, for adults: normal: 90 ml/min/1.73 m² stage g1, slight reduction: 89 60 ml/min/1.73 m² stage g2, slight-moderate reduction: 59 45 ml/min/1.73 m² stage g3a, moderate-severe reduction: 44 30 ml/min/1.73 m² stage g3b, severe reduction: 29 15 ml/min/1.73 m² stage g4, kidney failure (renal failure): < 15 ml/min/1.73 m² stage g5. 75 reason for admission: 01 decompensation of pulmonary arterial hypertension, due to: a septic condition of pulmonary focus symptoms on admission: worsening of dyspnea, productive cough, fever, diarrhea, desaturation initial tests with metabolic acidosis, elevated lactate and cpr, pre-renal acute kidney injury started ceftriaxone clarithromycin 01/24 negative blood and stool cultures b decompensated cor pulmonale heart failure profile b 02 anemia to be clarified activity of rheumatological disease? 03 resolved diarrhea 04 resolved pre-renal acute kidney injury kdigo 1 05 resolved coumarin poisoning secondary to resolved sepsis. admission: 01/24, discharge: 02/02, stay: 08 d 17 h, location: ward, payer: sus. [person] admitted to the pulmonology ward on 01/24/2020 with severe acute respiratory failure requiring oxygen therapy via nasal catheter O2 5 l/min. secondary to sepsis of pulmonary focus and due to decompensation of heart failure, previous cor pulmonale. [person] demonstrated multiple signs of circulatory congestion upon admission. To approach the condition, we started intravenous antibiotic therapy with ceftriaxone clarithromycin, maintained respiratory support, and prescribed intravenous diuretic therapy. [person] evolved with progressive improvement of metabolic acidosis, drop in serum lactate, and normalization of renal dysfunction. Since 01/30, he no longer required oxygen therapy, maintaining stable saturation in room air. On 02/01, we transitioned all medications to oral use. Considering that [person] had not required oxygen therapy for more than 72 hours, had taken all medications orally, and was stable in clinical and laboratory tests. We decided to discharge the patient during the morning visit on February 2. Vital signs: blood pressure 98 x 60 mmHg, heart rate 86, oxygen saturation 94% on room air. Good general condition, pale 2/4, hydrated, acyanotic, anicteric, and afebrile to the touch. Respiratory auscultation: Universally audible vesicular breath sounds, symmetrical, with crackles in both basal regions. Eupneic on room air, with no signs of accessory muscle use. Cardiac auscultation: Regular heart rhythm in normal S1 and S2, normal heart sounds, 3/6 diastolic murmur in the pulmonary area. Capillary refill time 2 seconds, no TJP at 90°. Abdomen: globular, normotensive, bowel sounds in the abdomen, painless on palpation. No masses or visceromegaly, negative abrupt decompression. Extremities: warm and well perfused, no edema, no signs of deep vein thrombosis. Investigation into the etiology of associated anemia. Previous medications maintained. Pulmonary rehabilitation proposal for pulse therapy by the [organization] team. We reiterate the need for hydrosaline restriction due to the heart failure and cor pulmonale. Follow-up visits: 02/14, infectious diseases, SID 02/23, pulmonology outpatient clinic, international normalized ratio 03/07, pulmonary hypertension outpatient clinic 04/14, neurology, pending follow-up visits: tests requested to investigate the anemia presented by [person]. Discuss and discuss with [organization] during the follow-up visit regarding the proposal for pulse therapy for [person]. reassess the diagnosis of asthma questioned after the last spirometry when follow-up appointmenting to pulmonology. Clinical improvement, follow-up scheduled with the assisting specialties. Reason for admission: 01 decompensation of pulmonary arterial high BP (hypertension), due to: a septic condition of pulmonary focus symptoms on admission: worsening of difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), productive cough, fever, diarrhea, deoxygen level in blood (saturation) initial tests with metabolic acidosis, elevated lactate and cpr, pre-renal acute kidney injury started ceftriaxone clarithromycin 01/24 negative blood and stool cultures b decompensated cor pulmonale heart failure profile b 02 low blood (anemia) to be clarified activity of rheumatological disease? 03 resolved diarrhea 04 resolved pre-renal acute kidney injury kdigo 1 05 resolved coumarin poisoning secondary to resolved serious blood infection (sepsis). Admission: 01/24, sent home from hospital (discharge): 02/02, stay: 08 d 17 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. The patient admitted to the pulmonology general hospital room on 01/24/2020 with severe acute lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) requiring oxygen therapy via nasal catheter oxygen 5 l/min. Secondary to serious blood infection (sepsis) of pulmonary focus and due to decompensation of heart failure, previous cor pulmonale. The patient demonstrated multiple signs of circulatory congestion upon admission. To approach the condition, we started given through a vein (drip/IV) infection-fighting medicine treatment with ceftriaxone clarithromycin, maintained respiratory support, and prescribed given through a vein (drip/IV) diuretic therapy. The patient evolved with progressive improvement of metabolic acidosis, drop in serum lactate, and normalization of renal dysfunction. Since 01/30, he no longer required oxygen therapy, maintaining stable oxygen level in blood (saturation) in room air. On 02/01, we transitioned all medications to oral use. Considering that the patient had not required oxygen therapy for more than 72 hours, had taken all medications by mouth (orally), and was stable in clinical and laboratory tests. We decided to sent home from hospital (discharge) the patient during the morning visit on february 2. Vital signs: blood pressure 98 x 60 mmhg, heart rate 86, oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation) 94% on room air. Good general condition, pale 2/4, hydrated, no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), and no fever (afebrile) to the touch. Respiratory auscultation: universally audible normal breathing sounds, symmetrical, with crackles in both basal regions. Breathing normally (eupneic) on room air, with no signs of accessory muscle use. Cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm in normal s1 and s2, normal heart sounds, 3/6 diastolic murmur in the pulmonary area. Capillary refill time 2 seconds, no tjp at 90°. Abdomen: globular, normotensive, bowel sounds in the abdomen, painless on palpation. No masses or visceromegaly, negative abrupt decompression. Extremities: warm and well perfused, no swelling (edema), no signs of deep vein blood clot (thrombosis). Investigation into the cause of disease (etiology) of associated low blood (anemia). Previous medications maintained. Pulmonary rehabilitation proposal for pulse therapy by the [organization] team. We reiterate the need for hydrosaline restriction due to the heart failure and cor pulmonale. Return visit to doctor (follow-up) visits: 02/14, infectious diseases, sid 02/23, pulmonology OPD (Out Patient Department), international normalized ratio 03/07, pulmonary high BP (hypertension) OPD (Out Patient Department) 04/14, neurology, pending return visit to doctor (follow-up) visits: tests requested to investigate the low blood (anemia) presented by the patient. Discuss and discuss with [organization] during the return visit to doctor (follow-up) visit regarding the proposal for pulse therapy for the patient. Reassess the diagnosis of asthma questioned after the last spirometry when return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointmenting to pulmonology. Clinical improvement, return visit to doctor (follow-up) scheduled with the assisting specialties. 78 1 acute respiratory distress syndrome secondary to coronavirus 19 confirmed on 05/21. 2 treated bloodstream infection. admission: 05/20/2020, discharge: 06/22/2020, stay: 01 m 02 d, location: ward, payer: SUS. [person] reports that 8 days after admission he started having a dry cough and fever, which progressively worsened with the onset of dyspnea and worsening of the condition 1 day ago. He sought emergency care and was treated with suspected coronavirus infection. [person] had a swab collected during care and was medicated with ceftriaxone and furosemide and referred to [organization] due to suspicion. Admitted tachypneic, requiring high-flow supplemental oxygen and desaturation. He progressed to orotracheal intubation on 05/20 and was then transferred to [organization]. He was started on methylprednisone, ceftriaxone, and clarithromycin for 7 days. During hospitalization, he underwent treatment for bloodstream infection with meropenm and vancomycin. He was extubated on 03/06 and remained in a ward bed for management of hypoactive delirium and weaning from supplemental oxygen. He has been on room air for over 72 hours. He is tearful and wishes to be discharged. He is hemodynamically stable, without need for vasoactive drugs. He has diuresis and spontaneous bowel movements. He is improving clinically and laboratory tests. His general condition is fair, flushed and hydrated, anicteric, acyanotic, afebrile, and eupneic. He is alert and disoriented in time and space. On respiratory auscultation, he has bilateral vesicular breath soundss and discreet bile crackles. Respiratory rate: 18 breaths per minute and oxygen saturation: 95% on room air. Cardiac auscultation: normal rhythmic heart sounds in normal S1 and S2, no murmurs. Heart rate: 87 beats per minute. Blood pressure: 125 x 90 mmHg. Abdomen: globular, flaccid, bowel sounds, painless to palpation, no visceromegaly or palpable masses. Extremities: well perfused, capillary refill time of less than 3 seconds, no edema in the lower limbs, no signs of deep vein thrombosis. Concentrated urine in collector. Daily laboratory tests. Use of broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy during hospitalization. Schedule a follow-up appointment visit to [organization] on July 9 in the morning. Request protocol exams after coronavirus. Optimized. 1 acute respiratory distress syndrome secondary to coronavirus 19 confirmed on 05/21. 2 treated bloodstream infection. 79 id: [person], 51 years old, from [address]. admission: 06/08/2020, discharge: 24/08/2020, stay: 17 d 14 h, location: ward: [organization] of [organization], service: ward [organization], payer: sus. case report: 1 septic shock with abdominal focus, cholecystitis, acute cholangitis, {omitted}, percutaneous cholecystostomy rint on 08/06 with 60 ml of purulent output orotracheal intubation for acute hypoxemic respiratory failure on 08/06 extubation on 08/15 antibiotic therapy: ceftriaxone metronidazole in 08/06 2 acute kidney injury kdigo 3 nta due to sepsis, post-contrast nephropathy basal cr 1.5 cr hospitalization 5.5 hemodialysis started on 08/07 discharged from nephrology due to resolution of acute kidney injury on 08/22 history of present illness: patient evaluated at [organization] on 08/05 due to complaint of abdominal pain and abdominal distension for 2 days, discharged in the early morning of 08/06 with no laboratory alterations, with guidance on measures for dyspepsia and counter-referral for endoscopy. That same morning, [person] sought emergency care due to worsening abdominal pain associated with sweating, tachycardia, and respiratory distress. He was transferred back to [organization], presenting with respiratory distress while using MR 15 L/min and hypotension with blood pressure 60x30. He was intubated shortly after admission and required initiation of vasoactive drugs. A computed tomography scan of the abdomen revealed acute cholangitis, and he then underwent percutaneous cholecystostomy via RINT on 06/08, with the output of 60 ml of purulent fluid. Personal history: systemic arterial hypertension, dyslipidemia, alcoholism (6 cans of beer/day); antibiotic therapy: ceftriaxone on 06/08 and 24/08; metronidazole on 06/08; ciprofloxacin on 24/08. good general condition, flushed, hydrated, anicteric, afebrile; cardiac auscultation: 2 rhythmic normal heart sounds, no murmurs; respiratory auscultation: vesicular breath sounds, no adventitious sounds; abdomen: flaccid, painless to superficial and deep palpation; wound in good appearance; sudden decompression negative; extremities: no edema, no clinical signs of deep vein thrombosis; capillary filling time less than 3 seconds; complementary exams: computed tomography of the abdomen 06/08: changes in the bile ducts suspicious for cholangitis, acute cholecystopathy, with liver image that may correspond to small abscesses; hepatomegaly associated with perfusion disorder, periportal edema, suggesting acute reactive liver disease; ultrasound of the abdomen 06/08: cholelithiasis and biliary sludge; thickening of the gallbladder wall with a nonspecific appearance. cultures cholecystostomy material 08/06: escherichia coli sa carbapenems, ciprofloxacin, cefepime blood culture 08/06: escherichia coli sa carbapenems, ciprofloxacin, cefepime, blood culture 08/13: nhcb blood culture 08/16: nhcb abscess culture 08/20: negative. [person] in significant clinical improvement, without need for vasoactive drugs and supplemental oxygen, afebrile for 5 days and asymptomatic. discussed with [organization]: we transitioned antibiotic therapy to oral ciprofloxacin metronidazole for another 2 weeks and monitored afterwards with a new imaging exam. follow-up appointment to the INT emergency unit on 08/31. In case of worsening of general condition, fever, chills, pain that does not improve with analgesia, seek urgent care. take medications as prescribed schedule surgery after improvement. good. That same morning, the patient sought emergency care due to worsening abdominal pain associated with sweating, fast heartbeat (tachycardia), and respiratory distress. He was transferred back to [organization], presenting with respiratory distress while using mr 15 l/min and low BP (hypotension) with blood pressure 60x30. He was intubated shortly after admission and required initiation of vasoactive drugs. A CT scan (computed tomography) scan of the abdomen revealed acute cholangitis, and he then underwent through the skin (percutaneous) cholecystostomy via rint on 06/08, with the output of 60 ml of purulent fluid. Personal history: systemic arterial high BP (hypertension), dyslipidemia, alcoholism (6 cans of beer/day); infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy): ceftriaxone on 06/08 and 24/08; metronidazole on 06/08; ciprofloxacin on 24/08. Good general condition, flushed, hydrated, no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no fever (afebrile); cardiac auscultation: 2 rhythmic normal heart sounds, no murmurs; respiratory auscultation: normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds), no adventitious sounds; abdomen: flaccid, painless to superficial and deep palpation; wound in good appearance; sudden decompression negative; extremities: no swelling (edema), no clinical signs of deep vein blood clot (thrombosis); capillary filling time less than 3 seconds; complementary exams: CT scan (computed tomography) of the abdomen 06/08: changes in the bile ducts government health schemepicious for cholangitis, acute cholecystopathy, with liver image that may correspond to small pus-filled swelling (abscess)es; hepatomegaly associated with perfusion disorder, periportal swelling (edema), suggesting acute reactive liver disease; ultrasound of the abdomen 06/08: cholelithiasis and biliary sludge; thickening of the gallbladder wall with a nonspecific appearance. Cultures cholecystostomy material 08/06: escherichia coli sa carbapenems, ciprofloxacin, cefepime blood test to find infection (blood culture) 08/06: escherichia coli sa carbapenems, ciprofloxacin, cefepime, blood test to find infection (blood culture) 08/13: nhcb blood test to find infection (blood culture) 08/16: nhcb pus-filled swelling (abscess) culture 08/20: negative. The patient in significant clinical improvement, without need for vasoactive drugs and supplemental oxygen, no fever (afebrile) for 5 days and asymptomatic. Discussed with [organization]: we transitioned infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) to oral ciprofloxacin metronidazole for another 2 weeks and monitored aftergeneral hospital rooms with a new imaging exam. Return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment to the int emergency unit on 08/31. In case of worsening of general condition, fever, chills, pain that does not improve with analgesia, seek urgent care. Take medications as prescribed schedule surgery after improvement. Good. 80 [person] with a previous history of ischemic stroke in 2020, progressing with slowed speech and left hemiplegia, had an unprecedented episode of seizure approximately 30 days ago, having been introduced by [organization] clobazan and lamotrigine. [person] after starting anticonvulsants, she observed the appearance of an erythematous rash, but as she evolved with septic shock with a urinary focus, she discontinued medications, having been hospitalized in {organization} for septic shock, using ceftriaxone until d14. After discharge, [person] follow-up appointmented to anticonvulsants, observing the follow-up appointment of erythematous rash, associated with worsening of lesions in the intergluteal region and in the medial region of the thigh, and diarrhea. Hospital admission was chosen due to pre-renal acute renal failure (KD 2). Antibiotic therapy with clindamycin and ciprofloxacin was started, in addition to intravenous hydration. Neurology re-evaluated the condition, and clobazan was maintained and lamotrigine was discontinued, in addition to topiramate being introduced, according to prescriptions prepared by neurology, with family members already admitted. [person] underwent pelvic tomography with no signs of infection, in addition to ultrasound of the lower limbs due to asymmetric edema, with ultrasound showing no signs of venous thrombosis. Hospital discharge was chosen on 8/24 {omitted}, with instructions to increase fluid intake at home due to improved renal function but not yet back to pre-admission levels. The patient was discharged with current antibiotic therapy (ciprofloxacin and oral clindamycin). 81 identification: [person], 38 years old, from [address] admission to [organization] on 04/08/2020 hospital discharge on 12/09/2020 problem: lung abscess, spontaneous pneumothorax on 04/08/2020. admission: 04/08/2020, discharge: 10/09/2020, stay: 01 m 05 d 19 h, location: ward payer: sus. patient after coronavirus infection, current: sacral abscess requiring drainage. physical examination: good general condition, flushed, hydrated, anicteric, acyanotic, afebrile neurology: isochoric pupils, photoreactive with no focal signs, respiratory auscultation vesicular breath sounds present bilaterally, slightly decreased in the right hemithorax, no adventitious sounds cardiac auscultation: normal-phonetic heart sounds, regular with no murmurs. abdomen: globular, bowel sounds, not painful, no visceromegaly, negative sudden decompression, 1/4 edema bilaterally, no calves swelling. chest computed tomography 08/08: appearance of lung abscess, {omitted} started on meropenem vancomycin on 07/08/2020 suspended on 14/08/2020, blood culture from 06/08 first febrile episode negative. Lung abscess secretion culture 08/13: negative Chest tomography 08/24: improvement in the radiological pattern, reduction of emphysema and hydropneumothorax on the right, right VI bronchopleural fistula, interfissural fluid collection. Drainage on 08/04/2020. Thoracoscopy pleural lavage 2 drains 08/12 Lung abscess secretion culture 08/13: negative Transitioned to polymyxin tigecycline regimen on 14/08/2020 due to fever spikes. Desaturation, collection reformation. Thoracotomy, resection of lung segment with pleuropulmonary fistula 08/27, uneventful, drain removed on 08/30. Control chest x-ray in 2 weeks. Maintain antibiotic therapy for 14 days. 23/09/2020 [organization] 29/09/2020 [organization] 27/10/2020 speech therapy. [person] evolved well after the procedure. I evaluate [person] in a ward bed. Hemodynamically stable, conscious and oriented, reporting that he is well. Ventilating spontaneously in room air, without complaints. Denies dyspnea, chest or abdominal pain. Denies nausea and vomiting, with good acceptance of the diet. Preserved diuresis, without complaints. No other complications. 82 1 severe acute respiratory syndrome associated with coronavirus 19 infection 2 nosocomial infection by acinetobacter baumanii. 3 critically ill neuropathy. admission: 05/28, discharge: 07/06, stay: 01 m 08 d 12 h, ward bed, payer: sus. [person] admitted with a history of dry cough, dyspnea, and myalgia progressive for 9 days after admission. at the time, she was using azithromycin for pneumonia, but without improvement, with no description of the duration of azithromycin. upon admission, [person] with intense tachydyspnea on admission, with a respiratory rate of 52 breaths per minute under a mask with a 6 l/min reservoir and saturation 94%. due to the condition of acute respiratory failure, po2/fio2 ratio: 64, orotracheal intubation was chosen. on 05/28, she tested positive for pcr for coronavirus. institutional protocol maintained with ceftriaxone clarithromycin. {omitted}. 05/29 [person] transferred to [organization] for intensive therapy. underwent tracheostomy on 06/18. on 07/02, [person] started having fever spikes and an increase in leukogram, and an infectious screening was collected on 07/04 and on 06/06, due to the persistence of fever spikes and worsening of markers of inflammatory activity, it was decided to introduce vancomycin, given the growth of staphylococcus aureus in blood culture. as [person] continued to have fever spikes, with growth of gram negative in tracheal aspirate on 06/12, it was decided to start meropenem polymyxin given the possibility of acinetobacter sp. despite expanded coverage for germs, [person] continued to have recurrent fever spikes, intermittently. The catheter was removed and the tip sent for culture. A hypothesis of infective endocarditis was raised. A transthoracic echocardiogram was requested on 06/26, which did not reveal any vegetations. The catheter culture was positive for Acinetobacter sp. The case was discussed again with [organization]: given the persistent fever, we decided to expand coverage for probable Acinetobacter sp., associating amikacin with the therapeutic regimen. Furthermore, considering that [person] had a sacral ulcer, vancomycin was reintroduced. On 06/03, also on the advice of [organization], we restarted treatment with meropenem to cover gram-negative bacteria. After adjusting the antibiotic therapy, [person] showed a good clinical response, with improvement in his general condition, remission of the fever, and a progressive drop in CRP. [person] developed progressive improvement in the infectious condition, but still required a motor rehabilitation program, given the after-effects of the prolonged hospitalization. poor general condition, tachydyspnea, room air desaturation, acute respiratory failure on admission. quantification of fibrin d dimers material: blood collection: 07/06 entry: 07/06 available: 07/06 result to be
