potentiometry element result reference unit value. 141.00 vn: 135.0 145.0 mmol/l [organization]. 1 orotracheal intubation 2 tracheostomy. 1 collection of general exams in the coronavirus protocol for cr follow-up appointment. urea, tgo, tgp, fibrinogen, ferritin, d-dimers, complete blood count. transfer to rear bed [organization]. good general condition, flushed, hydrated, acyanotic, anicteric, in room air, saturating 94%, tracheostomy occluded. regular heart rhythm, normal S1 and S2, rhythmic and normal-phonetic heart sounds, without audible murmurs. 1 severe acute respiratory syndrome associated with coronavirus 19 infection 2 hospital-acquired (nosocomial) infection by acinetobacter baumanii. 3 critically ill neuropathy. Admission: 05/28, sent home from hospital (discharge): 07/06, stay: 01 m 08 d 12 h, general hospital room (ward) bed, payer: government health scheme. The patient admitted with a history of dry cough, difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), and myalgia progressive for 9 days after admission. At the time, she was using azithromycin for lung infection (pneumonia), but without improvement, with no description of the duration of azithromycin. Upon admission, the patient with intense tachydifficulty in breathing (dyspnea) on admission, with a respiratory rate of 52 breaths per minute under a mask with a 6 l/min reservoir and oxygen level in blood (saturation) 94%. Due to the condition of acute lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure), po2/fio2 ratio: 64, orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) was chosen. On 05/28, she tested positive for pcr for coronavirus. Institutional protocol maintained with ceftriaxone clarithromycin. . 05/29 the patient transferred to [organization] for intensive therapy. Underwent breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) on 06/18. On 07/02, the patient started having fever spikes and an increase in leukogram, and an infectious screening was collected on 07/04 and on 06/06, due to the persistence of fever spikes and worsening of markers of inflammatory activity, it was decided to introduce vancomycin, given the growth of staphylococcus aureus in blood test to find infection (blood culture). As the patient continued to have fever spikes, with growth of gram negative in tracheal aspirate on 06/12, it was decided to start meropenem polymyxin given the possibility of acinetobacter sp. Despite expanded coverage for germs, the patient continued to have recurrent fever spikes, intermittently. The catheter was removed and the tip sent for culture. A hypothesis of infective infection of heart valves (endocarditis) was raised. A transthoracic heart ultrasound (echocardiogram) was requested on 06/26, which did not reveal any vegetations. The catheter culture was positive for acinetobacter sp. The case was discussed again with [organization]: given the persistent fever, we decided to expand coverage for probable acinetobacter sp., associating amikacin with the therapeutic regimen. Furthermore, considering that the patient had a sacral ulcer, vancomycin was reintroduced. On 06/03, also on the advice of [organization], we restarted treatment with meropenem to cover gram-negative bacteria. After adjusting the infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy), the patient showed a good clinical response, with improvement in his general condition, remission of the fever, and a progressive drop in crp. The patient developed progressive improvement in the infectious condition, but still required a motor rehabilitation program, given the after-effects of the prolonged hospitalization. Poor general condition, tachydifficulty in breathing (dyspnea), room air deoxygen level in blood (saturation), acute lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) on admission. Quantification of fibrin d dimers material: blood collection: 07/06 entry: 07/06 available: 07/06 result to be released method: immunoturbidimetry element result reference unit value. 4.89 vn: < or 0.5 ug/ml, fibrinogen material: blood collection: 07/06 entry: 07/06 available: 07/06 result to be released method: clauss method element result reference unit value. 764.0 vn: 200 400 mg/dl, c-reactive protein material: blood collection: 07/06 entry: 07/06 available: 07/06 result to be released method: turbidimetry element result reference unit value. 2.90 vn: < 1.0 mg/dl, urea material: blood collection: 07/06 entry: 07/06 available: 07/06 result to release method: enzymatic element result reference unit value. 61.42 vn: 19 to 49 mg/dl, tgp / alt alanine aminotransferase material: blood collection: 07/06 entry: 07/06 available: 07/06 result to release method: enzymatic element result reference unit value. 48.40 vn: 10 to 49 u/l, tgo / ast glutamic oxaloacetic transaminase material: blood collection: 07/06 entry: 07/06 available: 07/06 result to release method: enzymatic element result reference unit value. 28.90 vn: up to 32.0 u/l, potassium material: blood collection: 07/06 entry: 07/06 available: 07/06 result to be released method: integrated multisensor potentiometry element result reference unit value. 4.05 vn: 3.5 to 5.1 mmol/l, sodium material: blood collection: 07/06 entry: 07/06 available: 07/06 result to be released method: integrated multisensor potentiometry element result reference unit value. 141.00 vn: 135.0 145.0 mmol/l [organization]. 1 orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) 2 breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy). 1 collection of general exams in the coronavirus protocol for cr return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment. Urea, tgo, tgp, fibrinogen, ferritin, d-dimers, complete blood count. Transfer to rear bed [organization]. Good general condition, flushed, hydrated, no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), in room air, saturating 94%, breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) occluded. Regular heart rhythm, normal s1 and s2, rhythmic and normal-phonetic heart sounds, without audible murmurs. 1 severe acute respiratory syndrome associated with coronavirus 19 infection 2 hospital-acquired (nosocomial) infection by acinetobacter baumanii. 3 critically ill neuropathy. Admission: 05/28, sent home from hospital (discharge): 07/06, stay: 01 m 08 d 12 h, general hospital room (ward) bed, payer: government health scheme. The patient admitted with a history of dry cough, difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), and myalgia progressive for 9 days after admission. At the time, she was using azithromycin for lung infection (pneumonia), but without improvement, with no description of the duration of azithromycin. Upon admission, the patient with intense tachydifficulty in breathing (dyspnea) on admission, with a respiratory rate of 52 breaths per minute under a mask with a 6 l/min reservoir and oxygen level in blood (saturation) 94%. Due to the condition of acute lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure), po2/fio2 ratio: 64, orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) was chosen. On 05/28, she tested positive for pcr for coronavirus. Institutional protocol maintained with ceftriaxone clarithromycin. . 05/29 the patient transferred to [organization] for intensive therapy. Underwent breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) on 06/18. On 07/02, the patient started having fever spikes and an increase in leukogram, and an infectious screening was collected on 07/04 and on 06/06, due to the persistence of fever spikes and worsening of markers of inflammatory activity, it was decided to introduce vancomycin, given the growth of staphylococcus aureus in blood test to find infection (blood culture). As the patient continued to have fever spikes, with growth of gram negative in tracheal aspirate on 06/12, it was decided to start meropenem polymyxin given the possibility of acinetobacter sp. Despite expanded coverage for germs, the patient continued to have recurrent fever spikes, intermittently. The catheter was removed and the tip sent for culture. A hypothesis of infective infection of heart valves (endocarditis) was raised. A transthoracic heart ultrasound (echocardiogram) was requested on 06/26, which did not reveal any vegetations. The catheter culture was positive for acinetobacter sp. The case was discussed again with [organization]: given the persistent fever, we decided to expand coverage for probable acinetobacter sp., associating amikacin with the therapeutic regimen. Furthermore, considering that the patient had a sacral ulcer, vancomycin was reintroduced. On 06/03, also on the advice of [organization], we restarted treatment with meropenem to cover gram-negative bacteria. After adjusting the infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy), the patient showed a good clinical response, with improvement in his general condition, remission of the fever, and a progressive drop in crp. The patient developed progressive improvement in the infectious condition, but still required a motor rehabilitation program, given the after-effects of the prolonged hospitalization. Poor general condition, tachydifficulty in breathing (dyspnea), room air deoxygen level in blood (saturation), acute lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) on admission. Urea, tgo, tgp, fibrinogen, ferritin, d-dimers, complete blood count. Transfer to rear bed [organization]. Good general condition, flushed, hydrated, no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), in room air, saturating 94%, breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) occluded. Regular heart rhythm, normal s1 and s2, rhythmic and normal-phonetic heart sounds, without audible murmurs. a419. septicemia unclass. r57. shock unclass. elsewhere. z991. respirator dependence. b342. coronavirus infection, unclass. u071. novel coronavirus infection coronavirus alive {transfer} 25.9 1.15
83 [person] admitted by [organization] in the context of rhabdomyolysis to clarify uremic encephalopathy, KCO 3, requiring intermittent dialysis. admission: 24/02/2020, discharge: 21/03/2020, stay: 24 d 15 h, location: ward, payer: SUS. History of current illness: [person] has little contact, history collected from cross-examination form, transport team and [person] admission: [person] brought by SAMU with a history of having presented mental confusion for 1 day, changes in behavior and fluctuating level of consciousness between drowsiness and wakefulness, without moments of agitation. The team states that [person] has been an alcoholic for a long time and that until yesterday, during the afternoon, he had not presented any changes in behavior. A family member states that [person] went to a bar, drank less than usual and, afterwards, sat for hours without communicating with others. [person] noticed a change and contacted [organization]. [person] was admitted to [organization] with the same signs and symptoms. Laboratory tests were requested, which showed acute kidney injury, elevated creatine phosphokinase, and leukocytosis. 500 ml of Ringer's lactate and 2,000 ml of 0.9% saline solution were administered at the origin. Report of indwelling urinary catheter placement at the origin, with minimal diuresis. Family and team reports conflicting regarding the presence or absence of falls. There are no reports of witnessed seizures, current or previous. I admit [person] hemodynamically stable without vasoactive drugs, breathing spontaneously in room air, with no signs of discomfort. Confused and barely able to communicate, with slowed speech and slight tremor in the right upper limb. Poor general condition, flushed, hydrated, anicteric, acyanotic, afebrile to the touch, conscious eupneic, alert, but due to catatonia, assessment regarding degree of orientation, he is impaired. Cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm in two beats, normal heart sounds, no murmurs, wide and symmetrical pulses. Respiratory auscultation: present and symmetrical vesicular breath sounds, without adventitious sounds. No signs of discomfort. Oxygen saturation: 94% in room air. Abdomen: globular, without visible lesions, normotensive, diffuse tympanism. Painless to palpation. No palpable masses. Absence of peritonism. Lower limbs: good peripheral perfusion, without edema, without swelling. External rotation of the left lower limb. Presence of edema in the right ankle, with previous surgical scar. Intermittent hemodialysis. Regarding kidney injury: under joint follow-up with nephrology. Hemodialysis was performed yesterday considering episodes of instability to volume removal in hemodialysis, we maintain for now without adding new antihistamines. I request new tests for routine nephrology reassessment regarding infectious condition: meropenem ends on 03/17. We will not start a new antibiotic therapy trial, given the current situation, with no probability of reversal of the condition. Regarding the neurological condition: assessed by [organization], which attributed it to an infectious-metabolic context, making itself available in case of new changes. CT scan of the skull showed no changes relative to the previous CT scan and nonspecific electroencephalogram. Given the history of alcoholism, I maintain thiamine 100 mg/day. Given the history of alcoholism and deficit, I maintain vitb12 5000 mcg/week for 1 month. Regarding the pain in the left hip: by [organization] 02/26: no evidence of fracture of the proximal femur, only chronic changes in osteoarthritis of the hip. I maintain dipyrone on a schedule and I stop taking tramadol 50 mg if necessary to avoid drowsiness. [person] is comfortable with analgesia. Regarding catatonia: according to [organization]'s assessment, lorazepam 3333 mg was maintained. [organization] continued to be followed and advised not to administer antipsychotics if [person] was agitated due to the risk of neuroleptic malignant syndrome. Benzodiazepines were administered until sedation was achieved and, if necessary, a beeper was administered to [organization]. Report was prepared by the [organization] team aiming to continue bedside treatment at {organization}. Regarding diet: lactulose 20 ml was maintained once a day and bisacodyl was administered due to [person] having a more constipated habit. If diarrhea was suspended, oral diet was suspended due to [person] not being able to swallow, diet was optimized via nasoenteric tube, and hydration was maintained via nasoenteric tube. Other: vacancy accepted in {city}. [organization] and [organization] already prepared medical reports, awaiting transfer, scheduled for today I mobilize [organization] to inform family members about the transfer of [person] who were previously aware of the transfer. {omitted}. maintained with intravenous omeprazole and suspension of unfractionated heparin due to history of decreased hemoglobin, hematocrit still being evaluated as per ophthalmology guidance, we prescribe cleaning with cold saline solution, if secretion maintenance of lbf 5x a day nocturnal regencel for comfort. we maintain amlodipine 5 mg 12/12 h. pay attention to the risk of hypotension due to volume removal in dialysis. we request motor physiotherapy to stimulate rehabilitation, however [person] is not cooperative at the moment. [person] with bed in backup provided {city}. awaiting psychiatric opinion for follow-up/report. from the clinical part, no contraindication for transfer to backup bed. social: talked to [person] [person] about [person] uncertain prognosis. She understands the seriousness of the situation and claims that [person] has no other relatives and that she does not have the possibility of residential care, if necessary. talked to [person] [person] says that she and other family members agree to transfer [person] to a backup bed at [organization], given that she believes they will not be able to offer home care to [person], but will try together to get a place in a long-term care facility. The patient admitted by [organization] in the context of rhabdomyolysis to clarify uremic brain dysfunction (encephalopathy), kco 3, requiring intermittent dialysis. Admission: 24/02/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 21/03/2020, stay: 24 d 15 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme (sus). History of current illness: the patient systemic arterial high BP little contact, history collected from cross-examination form, transport team and the patient admission: the patient brought by samu with a history of having presented mental confusion for 1 day, changes in behavior and fluctuating alertness level (level of consciousness) between drowsiness and wakefulness, without moments of agitation. The team states that the patient systemic arterial high BP been an alcoholic for a long time and that until yesterday, during the afternoon, he had not presented any changes in behavior. A family member states that the patient went to a bar, drank less than usual and, aftergeneral hospital room (ward)s, oxygen level in blood (saturation) for hours without communicating with others. The patient noticed a change and contacted [organization]. The patient was admitted to [organization] with the same signs and symptoms. Laboratory tests were requested, which showed acute kidney injury, elevated creatine phosphokinase, and high white blood cell count (leukocytosis). 500 ml of ringer's lactate and 2,000 ml of 0.9% saline solution were give (administer)ed at the origin. Report of indwelling urinary catheter placement at the origin, with minimal diuresis. Family and team reports conflicting regarding the presence or absence of falls. There are no reports of witnessed seizures, current or previous. I admit the patient stable blood pressure and heart rate without vasoactive drugs, breathing spontaneously in room air, with no signs of discomfort. Confused and barely able to communicate, with slowed speech and slight tremor in the right upper limb. Poor general condition, flushed, hydrated, no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no fever (afebrile) to the touch, conscious breathing normally (eupneic), alert, but due to catatonia, assessment regarding degree of orientation, he is impaired. Cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm in two beats, normal heart sounds, no murmurs, wide and symmetrical pulses. Respiratory auscultation: present and symmetrical normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds), without adventitious sounds. No signs of discomfort. Oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation): 94% in room air. Abdomen: globular, without visible lesions, normotensive, diffuse tympanism. Painless to palpation. No palpable masses. Absence of peritonism. Lower limbs: good peripheral perfusion, without swelling (edema), without swelling. External rotation of the left lower limb. Presence of swelling (edema) in the right ankle, with previous surgical scar. Intermittent kidney dialysis (hemodialysis). Regarding kidney injury: under joint return visit to doctor (follow-up) with nephrology. Kidney dialysis (hemodialysis) was performed yesterday considering episodes of instability to volume removal in kidney dialysis (hemodialysis), we maintain for now without adding new antihistamines. I request new tests for routine nephrology reassessment regarding infectious condition: meropenem ends on 03/17. We will not start a new infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) trial, given the current situation, with no probability of reversal of the condition. Regarding the neurological condition: assessed by [organization], which attributed it to an infectious-metabolic context, making itself available in case of new changes. Ct scan of the skull showed no changes relative to the previous ct scan and nonspecific electroencephalogram. Given the history of alcoholism, i maintain thiamine 100 mg/day. Given the history of alcoholism and deficit, i maintain vitb12 5000 mcg/week for 1 month. Regarding the pain in the left intraparenchymal hemorrhage: by [organization] 02/26: no evidence of fracture of the proximal femur, only chronic changes in osteoarthritis of the intraparenchymal hemorrhage. I maintain dipyrone on a schedule and i stop taking tramadol 50 mg if necessary to avoid drowsiness. The patient is comfortable with analgesia. Regarding catatonia: according to [organization]'s assessment, lorazepam 3333 mg was maintained. [organization] continued to be followed and advised not to give antipsychotics if the patient was agitated due to the risk of neuroleptic malignant syndrome. Benzodiazepines were give (administer)ed until sedation was achieved and, if necessary, a beeper was give (administer)ed to [organization]. Report was prepared by the [organization] team aiming to continue bedside treatment at the hospital. Regarding diet: lactulose 20 ml was maintained once a day and bisacodyl was give (administer)ed due to the patient having a more constipated habit. If diarrhea was government health schemepended, oral diet was government health schemepended due to the patient not being able to swallow, diet was optimized via nasoenteric tube, and hydration was maintained via nasoenteric tube. Other: vacancy accepted in the city. [organization] and [organization] already prepared medical reports, awaiting transfer, scheduled for today i mobilize [organization] to inform family members about the transfer of the patient who were previously aware of the transfer. . Maintained with given through a vein (drip/IV) omeprazole and government health scheme (sus)pension of unfractionated heparin due to history of decreased blood count (Hb), hematocrit still being evaluated as per ophthalmology guidance, we prescribe cleaning with cold saline solution, if secretion maintenance of lbf 5x a day nocturnal regencel for comfort. We maintain amlodipine 5 mg 12/12 h. Pay attention to the risk of low BP (hypotension) due to volume removal in dialysis. We request motor physiotherapy to stimulate rehabilitation, however the patient is not cooperative at the moment. The patient with bed in backup provided the city. Awaiting psychiatric opinion for return visit to doctor (follow-up)/report. From the clinical part, no contraindication for transfer to backup bed. Social: talked to the patient the patient about the patient uncertain expected outcome (prognosis). She understands the seriousness of the situation and claims that the patient systemic arterial high BP no other relatives and that she does not have the possibility of residential care, if necessary. Talked to the patient the patient says that she and other family members agree to transfer the patient to a backup bed at [organization], given that she believes they will not be able to offer home care to the patient, but will try together to get a place in a long-term care facility. The patient admitted by [organization] in the context of rhabdomyolysis to clarify uremic brain dysfunction (encephalopathy), kco 3, requiring intermittent dialysis. Admission: 24/02/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 21/03/2020, stay: 24 d 15 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme (sus). History of current illness: the patient systemic arterial high BP little contact, history collected from cross-examination form, transport team and the patient admission: the patient brought by samu with a history of having presented mental confusion for 1 day, changes in behavior and fluctuating alertness level (level of consciousness) between drowsiness and wakefulness, without moments of agitation. The team states that the patient systemic arterial high BP been an alcoholic for a long time and that until yesterday, during the afternoon, he had not presented any changes in behavior. A family member states that the patient went to a bar, drank less than usual and, aftergeneral hospital room (ward)s, oxygen level in blood (saturation) for hours without communicating with others. The patient noticed a change and contacted [organization]. The patient was admitted to [organization] with the same signs and symptoms. Laboratory tests were requested, which showed acute kidney injury, elevated creatine phosphokinase, and high white blood cell count (leukocytosis). 500 ml of ringer's lactate and 2,000 ml of 0.9% saline solution were give (administer)ed at the origin. Report of indwelling urinary catheter placement at the origin, with minimal diuresis. Family and team reports conflicting regarding the presence or absence of falls. There are no reports of witnessed seizures, current or previous. I admit the patient stable blood pressure and heart rate without vasoactive drugs, breathing spontaneously in room air, with no signs of discomfort. Confused and barely able to communicate, with slowed speech and slight tremor in the right upper limb. Poor general condition, flushed, hydrated, no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no fever (afebrile) to the touch, conscious breathing normally (eupneic), alert, but due to catatonia, assessment regarding degree of orientation, he is impaired. Cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm in two beats, normal heart sounds, no murmurs, wide and symmetrical pulses. Respiratory auscultation: present and symmetrical normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds), without adventitious sounds. No signs of discomfort. Oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation): 94% in room air. Abdomen: globular, without visible lesions, normotensive, diffuse tympanism. Painless to palpation. No palpable masses. Absence of peritonism. Lower limbs: good peripheral perfusion, without swelling (edema), without swelling. External rotation of the left lower limb. Presence of swelling (edema) in the right ankle, with previous surgical scar. Intermittent kidney dialysis (hemodialysis). Regarding kidney injury: under joint return visit to doctor (follow-up) with nephrology. Kidney dialysis (hemodialysis) was performed yesterday considering episodes of instability to volume removal in kidney dialysis (hemodialysis), we maintain for now without adding new antihistamines. I request new tests for routine nephrology reassessment regarding infectious condition: meropenem ends on 03/17. We will not start a new infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) trial, given the current situation, with no probability of reversal of the condition. Regarding the neurological condition: assessed by [organization], which attributed it to an infectious-metabolic context, making itself available in case of new changes. Ct scan of the skull showed no changes relative to the previous ct scan and nonspecific electroencephalogram. Given the history of alcoholism, i maintain thiamine 100 mg/day. Given the history of alcoholism and deficit, i maintain vitb12 5000 mcg/week for 1 month. Regarding the pain in the left intraparenchymal hemorrhage: by [organization] 02/26: no evidence of fracture of the proximal femur, only chronic changes in osteoarthritis of the intraparenchymal hemorrhage. I maintain dipyrone on a schedule and i stop taking tramadol 50 mg if necessary to avoid drowsiness. The patient is comfortable with analgesia. Regarding catatonia: according to [organization]'s assessment, lorazepam 3333 mg was maintained. [organization] continued to be followed and advised not to give antipsychotics if the patient was agitated due to the risk of neuroleptic malignant syndrome. Benzodiazepines were give (administer)ed until sedation was achieved and, if necessary, a beeper was give (administer)ed to [organization]. Report was prepared by the [organization] team aiming to continue bedside treatment at the hospital. Regarding diet: lactulose 20 ml was maintained once a day and bisacodyl was give (administer)ed due to the patient having a more constipated habit. If diarrhea was government health schemepended, oral diet was government health schemepended due to the patient not being able to swallow, diet was optimized via nasoenteric tube, and hydration was maintained via nasoenteric tube. Other: vacancy accepted in the city. [organization] and [organization] already prepared medical reports, awaiting transfer, scheduled for today i mobilize [organization] to inform family members about the transfer of the patient who were previously aware of the transfer. . Maintained with given through a vein (drip/IV) omeprazole and government health scheme (sus)pension of unfractionated heparin due to history of decreased blood count (Hb), hematocrit still being evaluated as per ophthalmology guidance, we prescribe cleaning with cold saline solution, if secretion maintenance of lbf 5x a day nocturnal regencel for comfort. We need amlodipine 5 mg 12/12 h. We need to be careful about the risk of low BP (hypotension) due to volume removal in dialysis. We request motor physiotherapy to stimulate rehabilitation, however the patient is not cooperative at the moment. The patient with bed in backup provided the doctor with a psychiatric opinion for return visit to doctor (follow-up)/report. From the clinical part, there's no contraindication for transfer to backup bed. Social media users talked to the patient about the patient uncertain expected outcome (prognosis) and the seriousness of the situation and claims that the patient doesn't have enough time to get back to the hospital. She also talked to her family members about the condition of the patient and said that she has no plans to take care of it. She believes they will not be able to offer home care to the patients, but will try together to get a place in a long-term care facility. She feels she's very happy with the situation. She says she is happy to see the patient in the hospital and hoped that the hospital will get a better place for the patient. Her family will continue to work with the patient at the hospital, and she believes that it's important for everyone in the family to get the best possible way to get home care.</s> z010. examination of eyes and vision. f061. organic catatonic state. f10. mental and behavioral disorders due to alcohol use. m999. biomechanical injury nes. n17. acute renal failure. a419. septicemia nes. alive {transfer} 31.2 1.21

[person] admitted to the hc complex since 08/08 due to coronavirus infection. he was admitted to an intensive care unit bed, being managed with complications related to prolonged hospitalization difficulty in weaning from ventilation, need for vasoactive drugs, nosocomial infections, need for hemodialysis. he presented septic shock since 09/23 with positive blood culture for multidrug-resistant acinetobacter. on 09/28, it was decided to start tigecycline and meropenem at a double dose, in addition to maintaining polymyxin started on 09/26. meropenem was suspended on 09/29 after an antibiogram result showed resistance to carbapenems. [person] with significant clinical deterioration and no response to instituted measures, with increased need for vasoactive drugs, worsening of ventilatory mixed acidosis in blood gas analysis, with pH 7.1 and great difficulty in ventilatory management due to co2 retention, worsening of laboratory tests (increased lactate), worsening of leukocytosis and c-reactive protein. considered septic shock with infection refractory to antimicrobials, and it was decided to prioritize comfort measures. evolved with asystole at {omitted}. blood culture material: blood collection: 2020-09-23 made available: 2020-09-29 released: 2020-10-01 method: automated culture sample element a entry. 2020-09-23 result. isolation of gram-negative bacteria after 11h 39min of incubation. await identification. identification of acinetobacter baumannii sample b entry. 09/23/2020 result. no growth after 7 days of incubation. observation polymyxin mic 2 mm. tigecycline mic 0.5 mm. ampicillin subactam mic greater than 32 mm. to interpret the micm minimum inhibitory concentrations described in the result, please consult the ccih antibiogram acinetobacter baumannii mic sens amikacin. 32 r gentamicin. 8 r imipenem. 16 r meropenem. 16 r interpretation: sensitive intermediate resistant micm minimum inhibitory concentration. see medical record data. death 1 severe acute respiratory syndrome due to Cloudy urine fever on 09/20 urine culture 09/20 and 09/22: 100,000 cfu candida tropicalis multis started fluconazole 200 mg on 09/28 9 severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) 09/23 blood test to find infection (blood culture) 09/23: multidrug-resistant acinetobacter resistant to aminoglycosides and carbapenems started with polymyxin on 09/26 associated with tigecycline and meropenem double dose on 09/28 government health scheme (sus)pended meropenem on 09/29 decided to perform infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) trial for 48 72 h multidrug-resistant acinetobacter in blood test to find infection (blood culture), with high mic of polymyxin, if the patient does not present a response, consider refractory infection and prioritize comfort and care measures. Admission: 08/08/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 02/10/2020, stay: 01 m 24 d 11 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. The patient admitted to the hc complex since 08/08 due to coronavirus infection. He was admitted to an ICU (serious care room) bed, being managed with complications related to prolonged hospitalization difficulty in weaning from ventilation, need for vasoactive drugs, hospital-acquired (nosocomial) infections, need for kidney dialysis (hemodialysis). He presented severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) since 09/23 with positive blood test to find infection (blood culture) for multidrug-resistant acinetobacter. On 09/28, it was decided to start tigecycline and meropenem at a double dose, in addition to maintaining polymyxin started on 09/26. Meropenem was government health schemepended on 09/29 after an antibiogram result showed resistance to carbapenems. The patient with significant clinical deterioration and no response to instituted measures, with increased need for vasoactive drugs, worsening of ventilatory mixed acidosis in blood gas analysis, with ph 7.1 and great difficulty in ventilatory management due to co2 retention, worsening of laboratory tests (increased lactate), worsening of high white blood cell count (leukocytosis) and c-reactive protein. Considered severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) with infection refractory to antimicrobials, and it was decided to prioritize comfort measures. Evolved with asystole at . Blood test to find infection (blood culture) material: blood collection: 2020-09-23 made available: 2020-09-29 released: 2020-10-01 method: automated culture sample element a entry. 2020-09-23 result. Isolation of gram-negative bacteria after 11h 39min of incubation. Await identification. Identification of acinetobacter baumannii sample b entry. 09/23/2020 result. No growth after 7 days of incubation. Observation polymyxin mic 2 mm. Tigecycline mic 0.5 mm. Ampicillin subactam mic greater than 32 mm. To interpret the micm minimum inhibitory concentrations described in the result, please consult the ccih antibiogram acinetobacter baumannii mic sens amikacin. 32 r gentamicin. 8 r imipenem. 16 r meropenem. 16 r interpretation: sensitive intermediate resistant micm minimum inhibitory concentration. See medical record data. Death 1 severe acute respiratory syndrome due to coronavirus on 07/28 worsening of symptoms on 08/06 orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) due to a heart failure on 08/10 breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) on 09/04 2 high-response irregular heartbeat (atrial fibrillation) cves were found to be a major cause of heart failure. This led to a new episode of high-respiratory problems, 09/06 transitioned to enteral amiodarone sinus rhythm new high, but it didn't work well. However, the interconsultation request for cardiology 09/25: if an active infectious process is ruled out, start metoprolol tartrate 25 mg 12/12h will be sent to the hospital.</s> Target heart rate: 80-110 beats per minute no difference between strict and lenient heart rate control maintain amiodarone 200 mg 1x/day after improvement in clinical decompensations, request new evaluation to discuss rhythm control 3 bilateral pulmonary thromboblood clot blocking a vessel with high embolic burden diagnosed on 08/20 using apixaban since 09/19 due to worsening renal function 4 heparin-induced low platelet count (thrombocytopenia) on d15 of lmwh use hit score: 4t intermediate risk 1 platelet apheresis on 08/28 5 severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) with pulmonary focus resolved growth of klebsiella lung infection (pneumonia)e carbapenemase in tracheal aspirate and staphylococcus in blood test to find infection (blood culture): poly amica vancomycin 6 fungal urine infection (UTI) treated 08/31 09/13 micafungin candida albicans in urine culture 7 severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) 09/09 pulmonary focus? Admission: 08/08/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 02/10/2020, stay: 01 m 24 d 11 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. The patient admitted to the hc complex since 08/08 due to coronavirus infection. He was admitted to an ICU (serious care room) bed, being managed with complications related to prolonged hospitalization difficulty in weaning from ventilation, need for vasoactive drugs, hospital-acquired (nosocomial) infections, need for kidney dialysis (hemodialysis). He presented severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) since 09/23 with positive blood test to find infection (blood culture) for multidrug-resistant acinetobacter. On 09/28, it was decided to start tigecycline and meropenem at a double dose, in addition to maintaining polymyxin started on 09/26. Meropenem was government health schemepended on 09/29 after an antibiogram result showed resistance to carbapenems. The patient with significant clinical deterioration and no response to instituted measures, with increased need for vasoactive drugs, worsening of ventilatory mixed acidosis in blood gas analysis, with ph 7.1 and great difficulty in ventilatory management due to co2 retention, worsening of laboratory tests (increased lactate), worsening of high white blood cell count (leukocytosis) and c-reactive protein. Considered severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) with infection refractory to antimicrobials, and it was decided to prioritize comfort measures. Evolved with asystole at . Blood test to find infection (blood culture) material: blood collection: 2020-09-23 made available: 2020-09-29 released: 2020-10-01 method: automated culture sample element a entry. 2020-09-23 result. The bacteria after 11h 39min of incubation are found in the gram-negative mic. Identification of acinetobacter baumannii sample b entry. It's a good time to see if they're getting acubated after 7 days. Observation polymyxin mic 2 mm, tigecycline mic 0.5 mm, and ampicillin subactam mic greater than 32 mm. 85 pulmonary tuberculosis and disseminated nontuberculous mycobacteriosis septicemia of pulmonary focus. admission: 02/04/2021, discharge: 13/03/2021, stay: 01 m 08 d 23 h, location: ward, payer: sus. [person], 40 years old, from [address], was admitted to [organization] on 02/04 due to productive cough and right-sided cervical swelling for 1 month. patient with a previous diagnosis of tuberculosis, with 2 previous treatment attempts that were interrupted by the patient herself. a neck ultrasound was performed, which showed abscessed lymphadenopathy suggestive of lymph node tuberculosis. treatment was restarted, initially with rip, however, in tbtrm performed on the sputum, resistance to rifampicin was shown. alternative treatment with capreomycin, ethambutol, levofloxacin, pyrazinamide and ethionamide was then started on 02/09. on 03/03 ethionamide was replaced by terizidone, since this is the drug of choice, {omitted}. [person] presented sepsis of pulmonary focus during hospitalization, being treated with clavulin and later transitioned to clarithromycin. also presented oral moniliasis, being treated with fluconazole. presented progressive clinical improvement, becoming asymptomatic of the pulmonary condition. cervical adenomegaly remains on the left, but it is decreasing and currently painless. attempted hospitalization for treatment in [organization] without response in bed, in addition to [person] stating on several occasions that she does not accept being transferred from the institution. talked to [person]'s husband, in her presence, and he agrees to take her to [organization] 3 times a week on Monday, Wednesday and Friday for the application of capreocin and supervised intake of other medications. in addition, talked to the head nurse of {city} surveillance, [person], who agrees to make home visits on Tuesdays and Thursdays for supervised treatment of tuberculosis. opted for hospital discharge, with a schedule of at least 6 months of treatment in the currently proposed regimen. restarted ART on 03/11. history: human immunodeficiency virus, diagnosis in {omitted} cd4 10% hp: 134,751 2017 tdf 3tc nvp, prescribed in 2016 restarted in 2017 disseminated tuberculosis on irregular treatment ripe started in 2016, restarted in 08/2016 transitioned to ir in 10/2016 without adherence 2 tbtrm with rifampicin resistance, chose to start treatment for tbmr and wait for the isoniazid sensitivity test, nontuberculous mycobacteria isolated in sputum cultures in 2012 and 2014 mycobacteria intracellulare and untreated chimaera, thyroid nodule under investigation, smoking 1 pack/day, previous substance use disorder ceased 1 month ago. good general condition, flushed, hydrated, anicteric, acyanotic, afebrile, conscious and oriented in time and space presence of a palpable ganglion in the upper left anterior cervical chain, measuring 6 cm, painless, parenchymal, mobile respiratory auscultation vesicular breath sounds without adventitious sounds. eupneic in room air cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm in normal S1 and S2, normal sound sounds, no murmurs. heart rate: 84 beats per minute, blood pressure: 100x80 mmHg abdomen: protuberant, normal bowel sounds, normotensive, painless, palpable liver 4 cm from the right costal margin, no masses extremities: good perfusion, no edema, free calves, broad and symmetrical distal pulses. mycobacteria bacilloscopy tuberculosis material: sputum collection: 02/24 available: 02/24 released: 02/24 method: ziehlnelsen result no acid-fast bacilli were found, mycobacteria bacilloscopy tuberculosis material: sputum collection: 02/08 available: 02/08 released: 02/08 method: ziehlnelsen positive result: more than 10 acid-fast bacilli were found per field in the first 20 fields observed. awaiting sputum bacilloscopy from 03/06. discharged using: ethambutol 1200 mg/day starting on 02/09 pyrazinamide 1500 mg/day starting on 02/09 levofloxacin 750 mg/day starting on 02/09 terizidone 750 mg/day starting on 03/03 previously using ethionamide capreomycin 1000 mg 3xweek Monday, Wednesday and Friday starting on 02/09 sulfamethoxazole 400mg trimethoprim 80 mg 1 tablet every 12h furosemide 40mg/day chlorpromazine 25 mg every 12h clonazepam 10 gts/night antirv: tenofovir lamivudine nevirapine restart on 0/10. [person] schedules follow-up appointment appointment at [organization] on 03/16, schedules increase in nevirapine to full dose on 03/24, 14 days after start, follow-up appointments to the tuberculosis outpatient clinic, tests on 03/23, reinforces with [person] the importance of finishing treatment and reinforces the risk of death involved. [person] understands and reports that he/she is now motivated to treat until the end of the supervised tuberculosis treatment twice a week and has been to [organization] 3 times a week for application of capreomycin and supervision of taking the medication. stable, with clinical improvement, maintaining drug treatment. Pulmonary TB (tuberculosis) and disseminated nontuberculous mycobacteriosis blood infection (serious blood infection) of pulmonary focus. Admission: 02/04/2021, sent home from hospital (discharge): 13/03/2021, stay: 01 m 08 d 23 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. The patient, 40 years old, from [address], was admitted to [organization] on 02/04 due to productive cough and right-sided cervical swelling for 1 month. Patient with a previous diagnosis of TB (tuberculosis), with 2 previous treatment attempts that were interrupted by the patient herself. A neck ultrasound was performed, which showed pus-filled swelling (abscess)ed lymphadenopathy suggestive of lymph node TB (tuberculosis). Treatment was restarted, initially with rip, however, in tbtrm performed on the sputum, resistance to rifampicin was shown. Alternative treatment with capreomycin, ethambutol, levofloxacin, pyrazinamide and ethionamide was then started on 02/09. On 03/03 ethionamide was replaced by terizidone, since this is the drug of choice, . The patient presented serious blood infection (sepsis) of pulmonary focus during hospitalization, being treated with clavulin and later transitioned to clarithromycin. Also presented oral moniliasis, being treated with fluconazole. Presented progressive clinical improvement, becoming asymptomatic of the pulmonary condition. Cervical adenomegaly remains on the left, but it is decreasing and currently painless. Attempted hospitalization for treatment in [organization] without response in bed, in addition to the patient stating on several occasions that she does not accept being transferred from the institution. Talked to the patient's husband, in her presence, and he agrees to take her to [organization] 3 times a week on monday, wednesday and friday for the application of capreocin and supervised intake of other medications. In addition, talked to the head nurse of the city surveillance, the patient, who agrees to make home visits on tuesdays and thursdays for supervised treatment of TB (tuberculosis). Opted for hospital sent home from hospital (discharge), with a schedule of at least 6 months of treatment in the currently proposed regimen. Restarted art on 03/11. History: human immunodeficiency virus, diagnosis in cd4 10% hp: 134,751 2017 tdf 3tc nvp, prescribed in 2016 restarted in 2017 disseminated TB (tuberculosis) on irregular treatment ripe started in 2016, restarted in 08/2016 transitioned to ir in 10/2016 without adherence 2 tbtrm with rifampicin resistance, chose to start treatment for tbmr and wait for the isoniazid sensitivity test, nontuberculous mycobacteria isolated in sputum cultures in 2012 and 2014 mycobacteria intracellulare and untreated chimaera, thyroid nodule under investigation, smoking 1 pack/day, previous substance use disorder ceased 1 month ago. 86 1 severe acute respiratory syndrome due to coronavirus: onset of symptoms: 12/10. worsening of symptoms: 12/19. positive rt pcr on 12/14. orotracheal intubation: 12/20 extubation failure: 1/10 secretion? edema? 2 acute kidney injury kdigo3 oliguric dialysis creatinine 1.48 2.48 4.5. hemodialysis started: 12/22 last hemodialysis on 01/27, showing progressive improvement and no new dialysis emergencies. 3 fever, worsening of secretion: 01/03 pulmonary focus? ciprofloxacin started: 01/07 ceftazidime: 01/08. 4 herpes zoster on the back: acyclovir di 01/04. 5 faar: 01/03. impregnation with amiodarone started. 6 septic shock with focus to be clarified: 01/17. vancomycin, meropenem, and gentamicin started. 7 deep vein thrombosis of the right upper limb 01/19: heparin pump. warfarin started: 01/25. 8 isolated fever on 02/31: catheter-related infection? improvement of thermal curve after cdl removal 9 peripheral facial paralysis herpes zoster: valacyclovir 01/29 02/08 prednisone 60 mg/day 01/29 02/08. admission: 12/20/2020, discharge: 02/10/2021, stay: 01 m 20 d 17 h, location: ward, payer: sus. [person] previously hypertensive, diabetic, and obese with prolonged hospitalization and multiple complications secondary to severe coronavirus. Reports onset of symptoms 10 days after admission, with fever, dry cough, and odynophagia. Symptoms worsened 1 day ago, with onset of dyspnea. Has a positive RT-PCR test on paper records from 12/14. [person] admitted with a mask with a reservoir at 15 L/min, severely tachydyspneic with respiratory rate of 40 breaths per minute, hypoxemic with oxygen saturation of 85% with Fio2 100%, hemodynamically stable, conscious, and oriented. Upon admission, an attempt at noninvasive ventilation was made, but [person] did not tolerate noninvasive ventilation, with worsening respiratory distress, and orotracheal intubation was performed. Transferred to [organization] where he remained intubated until 10/01, when extubation failed, and a tracheostomy was performed on 12/01. Had 2 nosocomial infections that were reversed. evolved with need for renal replacement therapy from 12/22 to 01/27, with stability of nitrogenous wastes after. also presented high-response atrial fibrillation and is currently on amiodarone 200 mg/day. On 01/19 he had thrombosis of the left upper limb, requiring a heparin pump and is currently on warfarin with an international normalized target ratio of 23. On 01/29 he presented peripheral facial paralysis and was treated with valacyclovir and prednisone. tracheostomy was decannulated on 02/07, is without invasive devices, accepting an oral diet, clinically stable, in condition to be discharged from the hospital on 02/10, with family support and rehabilitation care after prolonged hospitalization. eg, pale 1, hydrated, acyanotic, anicteric and afebrile regular heart rhythm in two beats normal heart sounds without murmurs vesicular breath sounds present bilaterally without adventitious sounds, good respiratory pattern in room air. capped tracheostomy. globular abdomen, normotensive, normal bowel sounds, tympanic, painless central nervous system: conscious, contacting, Glasgow coma scale 15, isochoric and photoreactive pupils. reduced muscle strength globally, symmetrical. facial paralysis of peripheral nature in improvement. medications at hospital discharge: omeprazole 20 mg sos gliclazide 60 mg/day. metformin 1500 mg/day. insulin bt 10 iu according to glycemic control warfarin 5 mg: 10 mg Mon/Wed/Fri 7.5 mg on other days amiodarone 200 mg/day amlodipine 5 mg 12/12h clonidine 150 mcg 12/12h simvastatin 40mg/day. follow-up appointments at [organization] with: nephrology on 03/16, with exams already requested infectology in outpatient post-coronavirus clinic on 03/18 check current exams and evaluate rehabilitation and glycemic control vascular surgery for international normalized ratio follow-up follow-up appointment in. optimized conditions. 1 severe acute respiratory syndrome due to coronavirus: onset of symptoms: 12/10. Worsening of symptoms: 12/19. Positive rt pcr on 12/14. Orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation): 12/20 breathing tube removed (extubation) failure: 1/10 secretion? Swelling (edema)? 2 acute kidney injury kdigo3 oliguric dialysis kidney function marker 1.48 2.48 4.5. Kidney dialysis (hemodialysis) started: 12/22 last kidney dialysis (hemodialysis) on 01/27, showing progressive improvement and no new dialysis emergencies. 3 fever, worsening of secretion: 01/03 pulmonary focus? Ciprofloxacin started: 01/07 ceftazidime: 01/08. 4 herpes zoster on the back: acyclovir di 01/04. 5 faar: 01/03. Impregnation with amiodarone started. 6 severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) with focus to be clarified: 01/17. Vancomycin, meropenem, and gentamicin started. 7 deep vein blood clot (thrombosis) of the right upper limb 01/19: heparin pump. Warfarin started: 01/25. 8 isolated fever on 02/31: catheter-related infection? Admission: 12/20/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 02/10/2021, stay: 01 m 20 d 17 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. The patient previously hypertensive, diabetic, and obese with prolonged hospitalization and multiple complications secondary to severe coronavirus. Reports onset of symptoms 10 days after admission, with fever, dry cough, and odynophagia. Symptoms worsened 1 day ago, with onset of difficulty in breathing (dyspnea). Systemic arterial high BP (hypertension) a positive rt-pcr test on paper records from 12/14. The patient admitted with a mask with a reservoir at 15 l/min, severely tachydyspneic with respiratory rate of 40 breaths per minute, hypoxemic with oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation) of 85% with fio2 100%, stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), conscious, and oriented. Upon admission, an attempt at noninvasive ventilation was made, but the patient did not tolerate noninvasive ventilation, with worsening respiratory distress, and orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) was performed. Transferred to [organization] where he remained intubated until 10/01, when breathing tube removed (extubation) failed, and a breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) was performed on 12/01. Had 2 hospital-acquired (nosocomial) infections that were reversed. Evolved with need for renal replacement therapy from 12/22 to 01/27, with stability of nitrogenous wastes after. Also presented high-response irregular heartbeat (atrial fibrillation) and is currently on amiodarone 200 mg/day. On 01/19 he had blood clot (thrombosis) of the left upper limb, requiring a heparin pump and is currently on warfarin with an international normalized target ratio of 23. On 01/29 he presented peripheral facial paralysis and was treated with valacyclovir and prednisone. Breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) was decannulated on 02/07, is without invasive devices, accepting an oral diet, clinically stable, in condition to be sent home from hospital (discharge)d from the hospital on 02/10, with family support and rehabilitation care after prolonged hospitalization. Eg, pale 1, hydrated, no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric) and no fever (afebrile) regular heart rhythm in two beats normal heart sounds without murmurs normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds) present bilaterally without adventitious sounds, good respiratory pattern in room air. Capped breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy). Globular abdomen, normotensive, normal bowel sounds, tympanic, painless central nervous system: conscious, contacting, glasgow coma scale 15, isochoric and photoreactive pupils. Reduced muscle strength globally, symmetrical. Facial paralysis of peripheral nature in improvement. Medications at hospital sent home from hospital (discharge): omeprazole 20 mg sos gliclazide 60 mg/day. Metformin 1500 mg/day. Insulin bt 10 iu according to glycemic control warfarin 5 mg: 10 mg mon/wed/fri 7.5 mg on other days amiodarone 200 mg/day amlodipine 5 mg 12/12h clonidine 150 mcg 12/12h simvastatin 40mg/day. Return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointments at [organization] with: nephrology on 03/16, with exams already requested infectology in outpatient post-coronavirus clinic on 03/18 check current exams and evaluate rehabilitation and glycemic control vascular surgery for international normalized ratio return visit to doctor (follow-up) return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment in. Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy 12/28: sliding hiatal hernia measuring 4 cm, scar area in the anterior wall of the duodenal bulb, causing retraction and deformity of the pylorus. Heart ultrasound (echocardiogram), cardiology advised not to increase the dose of vasodilators, since the patient had a condition compatible with aortic insufficiency. Increased vasodilators may underestimate the degree of valve injury. The patient had improved renal function, kidney function marker (creatinine) 1.7, and no recurrence of hyperkalemia today. Also no evidence of congestion. We chose to keep him off furosemide. The patient had no congestive symptoms, improved renal function, and no hydroelectrolytic disturbances. This is a serious condition, with no life-threatening repercussions at this time. The patient was not fit for hospital sent home from hospital (discharge). 88 coronavirus, hypertension. admission: 03/04/2020, discharge: 03/26/2020, stay: 21 d 15 h, location: ward, payer: sus. case summary: [person] with onset of flu-like symptoms on 02/23, with myalgia, runny nose, headache, and malaise. evolved with dry cough for 4 days and asthenia for 2 days after admission. sought emergency care on 03/04 with oxygen saturation: 85%, showing improvement with nasal catheter, but maintaining respiratory discomfort. remained with reservoir mask, being refractory. was continued with orotracheal intubation, without complications. used ceftriaxone, clarithromycin, dexamethasone, ivermectin, colchicine, vitamin D and other symptomatic. admitted on 03/05 to {organization}, hemodynamically stable without use of vasoactive drugs, on mechanical ventilation, sedated on sedation agitation scale 4, well coupled. admitted to [organization] on 03/05, intubated, sedated, using midazolam, fentanyl and propofol, without neuromuscular blocker, poorly adapted to mechanical ventilation. without vasoactive drugs, hemodynamically stable. febrile, tachypneic and tachycardic. using an indwelling urinary catheter. central venous catheter in the right jugular vein. developed acute kidney injury secondary to coronavirus? pre-renal? already resolved. institutional protocol performed, corticotherapy and prophylactic enoxaparin instituted. presented bloodstream infection by acinetobacter baumanii, resolved after removal of the device. used antibiotic therapy see above, guided by cultures, [person] progressed well, with weaning from sedation and ventilation, transferred to the care of the ward at [organization] on 03/22, in room air, saturation 97%, no signs of respiratory distress, fit for discharge on 03/26, 48 hours without the need for oxygen support. good general condition, flushed, hydrated, anicteric, acyanotic, afebrile, lucid, oriented in time and space, eupneic in room air. blood culture material: blood collection: 03/24 result to be validated result to be released method: automated culture element sample a entry. 03/24 result. negative culture so far. sample b entry. 03/24 result. negative culture so far. weaning from corticosteroids, antihypertensives, medical advice. follow-up at a basic health unit. more than 48 hours in room air. Coronavirus, high BP (hypertension). Admission: 03/04/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 03/26/2020, stay: 21 d 15 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Case summary: the patient with onset of flu-like symptoms on 02/23, with myalgia, runny nose, headache, and malaise. Evolved with dry cough for 4 days and asthenia for 2 days after admission. Sought emergency care on 03/04 with oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation): 85%, showing improvement with nasal catheter, but maintaining respiratory discomfort. Remained with reservoir mask, being refractory. Was continued with orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation), without complications. Used ceftriaxone, clarithromycin, dexamethasone, ivermectin, colchicine, vitamin d and other symptomatic. Admitted on 03/05 to the hospital, stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable) without use of vasoactive drugs, on machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation), sedated on sedation agitation scale 4, well coupled. Admitted to [organization] on 03/05, intubated, sedated, using midazolam, fentanyl and propofol, without neuromuscular blocker, poorly adapted to machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation). Without vasoactive drugs, stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable). Febrile, tachypneic and tachycardic. Using an indwelling urinary catheter. Central venous catheter in the right jugular vein. Developed acute kidney injury secondary to coronavirus? Pre-renal? Already resolved. Institutional protocol performed, corticotherapy and prophylactic enoxaparin instituted. Presented bloodstream infection by acinetobacter baumanii, resolved after removal of the device. Used infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) see above, guided by cultures, the patient progressed well, with weaning from sedation and ventilation, transferred to the care of the general hospital room (ward) at [organization] on 03/22, in room air, oxygen level in blood (saturation) 97%, no signs of respiratory distress, fit for sent home from hospital (discharge) on 03/26, 48 hours without the need for oxygen support. Good general condition, flushed, hydrated, no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no fever (afebrile), lucid, oriented in time and space, breathing normally (eupneic) in room air. Blood test to find infection (blood culture) material: blood collection: 03/24 result to be validated result to be released method: automated culture element sample a entry. 03/24 result. Negative culture so far. Sample b entry. 03/24 result. Negative culture so far. Weaning from corticosteroids, antihypertensives, medical advice. Return visit to doctor (follow-up) at a basic health unit. More than 48 hours in room air. Coronavirus, high BP (hypertension). Progression with severity, requiring orotracheal intubation and prolonged intensive support. Referred to our service on 09/24 due to inflammatory bulging in the right clavicular region. Denies fever, denies spontaneous drainage, denies arthritis or arthralgia. During the current hospitalization, she was approached by [organization], with abscess drainage showing growth of Staphylococcus aureus and MRSA, as well as a diagnosis of acute osteomyelitis. She underwent intravenous treatment with linezolid for 2 weeks and due to clinical improvement, it was decided to consolidate treatment with oral clindamycin for another 4 weeks, according to clinical evolution. She is currently clinically stable and in condition to be discharged from hospital. 90 [person], 21 years old, admission: 14/05/2020, discharge: 21/05/2020, stay: 07 d 07 h, location: ward, payer: sus. problem: recent admission to the intensive care unit of [organization] due to septic shock of pulmonary focus, influenza, discharge with tracheostomy referred with report of acute respiratory failure, productive cough and desaturation on admission, orotracheal intubation performed with difficult airway and difficult ventilation due to probable stenosis, meropenem and vasoactive drug started due to circulatory shock. patient underwent tracheostomy to ensure a definitive airway on 05/14, with evidence of 60% tracheostomy stenosis at 3 cm from the carina, the patient improved clinically, was weaned off vasoactive drugs in the postoperative period, antibiotic therapy was suspended due to the absence of evidence of infection and ventilatory support was weaned, remaining comfortably on macronebulization, tracheal dilation was performed and the cannula was changed to a long port no. 8, family training for home care was performed, history of current illness: [person] with previous hospitalization in [organization], in March, caused by septic shock of pulmonary focus, being managed in parallel for influenza, complete treatment with tamiflu with negative sputum, remained intubated on mechanical ventilation, and prolonged hospitalization, subsequently requiring tracheostomy, the day of occlusion was not reported, with compensation of the clinical condition later, was discharged 7 days ago. Yesterday, he was admitted to this service, referred from [organization] due to respiratory failure, cough and presence of desaturation in room air, requiring the use of a Venturi mask, family history of bronchoaspiration. He presented respiratory failure with report of laryngeal stridor, requiring orotracheal intubation, difficult airway due to possible tracheal obstruction. He was administered 3 ampoules of pancuronium. He presented hemodynamic instability, despite expansion with 1000 ml of crystalloid. Meropenem 1 g 8/8h was started on 05/13. Norepinephrine 5 ml/h was started and quickly discontinued. A tracheostomy was performed on 05/14, without complications, and he was transferred to a semi-intensive care bed, where he remained hemodynamically stable, ventilating spontaneously with macronebulization, asymptomatic. On May 16, the patient underwent stenosis dilation by the [organization] team. The procedure was uneventful and the cannula was changed to a long size 8 port. [person] progressed well clinically, was asymptomatic, ventilated spontaneously, had a comfortable respiratory pattern, maintained good saturation in room air, and had no evidence of infection. Family training was provided on how to use an aspirator if necessary. A head tomography scan was performed to assess changes that would justify continued use of phenytoin, which was started after a seizure during a previous hospitalization for treatment of a lung infection. The exam showed no changes, and it was decided to gradually discontinue phenytoin. The patient was discharged in good general condition, with a follow-up appointment and schedule listed below. personal history: down syndrome, heart surgery at 1 year and 8 months, [person] unable to report underlying heart disease, previous use of: risperidone 1 mg at night phenytoin 100 mg every 8 hours due to seizure, hospitalized in the ICU, prednisone weaning 1/4 in the morning for 5 days transitioned to methylprednisolone previous cultures in external exams: central catheter and secretion on 02/04: acinetobacter sensitive to tigecycline blood culture 3 samples on 09/04: klebsiella pneumoniae sensitive to amikacin, cefoxime, imipenem, ertapenem, meropenem urine culture on 09/04: psudomonas sensitive to amikacin, cefepime, gentamicin. resistant to ciprofloxacin, imipenem, levofloxacin previous antibiotics: amikacin d13 clarithromycin d1 oseltamevir d5 levofloxacin d10 cefepime d14 tigecycline d7. tests performed during admission: computed tomography 05/14: apparent reduction in tracheal caliber immediately after the endotracheal tube, with a lumen diameter of 4 mm, approximately 7 mm in length, approximately 8.0 cm from the tracheostomy orifice. tests performed during admission: computed tomography 05/14: apparent reduction in tracheal caliber immediately after the endotracheal tube, with a lumen diameter of 4 mm, approximately 7 mm in length, approximately 8.0 cm from the tracheostomy orifice. tracheostomy and dilation of tracheal sternotomy. Case discussed with [person]: Impression: Upper airway obstruction Conduct: [person] Clinically stable, ventilating spontaneously with macronebulization, with no evidence of respiratory distress, asymptomatic. [person] Evaluated by neurology, with CT radiological image of the skull without alterations and guidance to suspend phenytoin with gradual weaning in view of situational seizure. We maintain prednisone weaning schedule on 05/19 reduced from 40 to 30 mg/day. We recommend home nebulization SOS. Outpatient follow-up scheduled: head and neck surgery: ELT on 05/23, thoracic surgery: ACT on 05/23, for joint case follow-up due to the possibility of a joint approach thorax head and neck surgery, speech therapy: for swallowing assessment in the outpatient clinic on 05/28, I clarify [person]'s doubts regarding home care and the current indication for discharge with the use of a tracheal cannula. I inform that surgical programming and approaches must be resolved with teams specialized in outpatient follow-up. ward care. [person]. is discharged in good general condition, with follow-up and schedule listed below. The patient, 21 years old, admission: 14/05/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 21/05/2020, stay: 07 d 07 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Problem: recent admission to the ICU (serious care room) of [organization] due to severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) of pulmonary focus, influenza, sent home from hospital (discharge) with breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) referred with report of acute lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure), productive cough and deoxygen level in blood (saturation) on admission, orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) performed with difficult airway and difficult ventilation due to probable stenosis, meropenem and vasoactive drug started due to circulatory shock. Patient underwent breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) to ensure a definitive airway on 05/14, with evidence of 60% breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) stenosis at 3 cm from the carina, the patient improved clinically, was weaned off vasoactive drugs in the postoperative period, infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) was government health schemepended due to the absence of evidence of infection and ventilatory support was weaned, remaining comfortably on macronebulization, tracheal dilation was performed and the cannula was changed to a long port no. 8, family training for home care was performed, history of current illness: the patient with previous hospitalization in [organization], in march, caused by severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) of pulmonary focus, being managed in parallel for influenza, complete treatment with tamiflu with negative sputum, remained intubated on machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation), and prolonged hospitalization, subsequently requiring breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy), the day of occlusion was not reported, with compensation of the clinical condition later, was sent home from hospitald 7 days ago. Yesterday, he was admitted to this service, referred from [organization] due to lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure), cough and presence of deoxygen level in blood (saturation) in room air, requiring the use of a venturi mask, family history of bronchoaspiration. He presented lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) with report of laryngeal stridor, requiring orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation), difficult airway due to possible tracheal obstruction. He was give (administer)ed 3 ampoules of pancuronium. He presented hemodynamic instability, despite expansion with 1000 ml of crystalloid. Meropenem 1 g 8/8h was started on 05/13. Norepinephrine 5 ml/h was started and quickly discontinued. A breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) was performed on 05/14, without complications, and he was transferred to a semi-intensive care bed, where he remained stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), ventilating spontaneously with macronebulization, asymptomatic. On may 16, the patient underwent stenosis dilation by the [organization] team. The procedure was uneventful and the cannula was changed to a long size 8 port. The patient progressed well clinically, was asymptomatic, ventilated spontaneously, had a comfortable respiratory pattern, maintained good oxygen level in blood (saturation) in room air, and had no evidence of infection. Family training was provided on how to use an aspirator if necessary. A head tomography scan was performed to assess changes that would justify continued use of phenytoin, which was started after a seizure during a previous hospitalization for treatment of a lung infection. The exam showed no changes, and it was decided to gradually discontinue phenytoin. The patient was sent home from hospital (discharge)d in good general condition, with a return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment and schedule listed below. Personal history: down syndrome, heart surgery at 1 year and 8 months, the patient unable to report underlying heart disease, previous use of: risperidone 1 mg at night phenytoin 100 mg every 8 hours due to seizure, hospitalized in the icu, prednisone weaning 1/4 in the morning for 5 days transitioned to methylprednisolone previous cultures in external exams: central catheter and secretion on 02/04: acinetobacter sensitive to tigecycline blood test to find infection (blood culture) 3 samples on 09/04: klebsiella lung infection (pneumonia)e sensitive to amikacin, cefoxime, imipenem, ertapenem, meropenem urine culture on 09/04: psudomonas sensitive to amikacin, cefepime, gentamicin. Resistant to ciprofloxacin, imipenem, levofloxacin previous infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic)s: amikacin d13 clarithromycin d1 oseltamevir d5 levofloxacin d10 cefepime d14 tigecycline d7. Tests performed during admission: CT scan (computed tomography) 05/14: apparent reduction in tracheal caliber immediately after the endotracheal tube, with a lumen diameter of 4 mm, approximately 7 mm in length, approximately 8.0 cm from the breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) orifice. Tests performed during admission: CT scan (computed tomography) 05/14: apparent reduction in tracheal caliber immediately after the endotracheal tube, with a lumen diameter of 4 mm, approximately 7 mm in length, approximately 8.0 cm from the breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) orifice. Breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) and dilation of tracheal sternotomy. Case discussed with the patient: impression: upper airway obstruction conduct: the patient clinically stable, ventilating spontaneously with macronebulization, with no evidence of respiratory distress, asymptomatic. The patient evaluated by neurology, with ct radiological image of the skull without alterations and guidance to government health schemepend phenytoin with gradual weaning in view of situational seizure. We maintain prednisone weaning schedule on 05/19 reduced from 40 to 30 mg/day. We recommend home nebulization sos. 91 [person], 42 years old, history of neuromyelitis optica since {omitted}. was bedridden most of the time. used a wheelchair since 2005. admission: 04/06/2020, discharge: 06/06/2020, stay: 02 d 20 h, location: ward, payer: sus. history of dyspnea 3 days before admission to [organization]. verbal report of negative rapid test for coronavirus on 03/06. chest tomography with extensive consolidation in the right lung compatible with pneumonia, areas of ground-glass, residual? had coronavirus 3 months ago. started treatment with piperacillin tazobactam on 03/06. was using a high-flow nasal catheter, cnaf. transferred to the emergency unit on 04/06: evolution with respiratory failure requiring orotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation, as well as worsening hemodynamics requiring vasoactive drug. empirical antibiotic escalation, meropenem and vancomycin therapy, once evolution to septic shock of pulmonary focus. transfer to intensive care unit around {omitted} on 04/06 evolution with refractory septic shock requiring high dose of norepinephrine and vasopressin, associated with gentamicin on 06/06. evolution with multiple organ dysfunction and cardiorespiratory arrest in asystole, with death confirmed at {omitted}. cold extremities with slowed capillary refill. evolution with multiple organ dysfunction: refractory septic shock, pulmonary focus, acute kidney injury with refractory metabolic acidosis, disseminated intravascular coagulation, disseminated intravascular coagulation, thrombocytopenia, coagulation disorder. orotracheal tube no. 7.5 access in right internal jugular vein, invasive blood pressure in left radial artery, nasoenteric tube, indwelling urinary catheter. cardiorespiratory arrest in asystole with death confirmed {omitted}. Empirical infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) escalation, meropenem and vancomycin therapy, once evolution to severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) of pulmonary focus. Transfer to ICU (serious care room) around on 04/06 evolution with refractory severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) requiring high dose of norepinephrine and vasopressin, associated with gentamicin on 06/06. Evolution with multiple organ dysfunction and cardiorespiratory arrest in asystole, with death confirmed at . Cold extremities with slowed capillary refill. Evolution with multiple organ dysfunction: refractory severe infection causing dangerously low BP, pulmonary focus, acute kidney injury with refractory metabolic acidosis, disseminated intravascular coagulation, disseminated intravascular coagulation, low platelet count (thrombocytopenia), coagulation disorder. Orotracheal tube no. 7.5 access in right internal jugular vein, invasive blood pressure in left radial artery, nasoenteric tube, indwelling urinary catheter.
