92 sca ssst hda septic shock with pulmonary focus. admission: 01/08/2020, discharge: 14/08/2020, stay: 13 d, location: ward, payer: sus. hospital discharge report: [person], 72 years old, from {city} date of admission: 01/08/2020 problem: 1 septic shock with pulmonary focus chest tomography 02/08: visible consolidations with appearance of bronchoaspiration. 2 decreased level of consciousness to be clarified, intubation at the source due to decreased level of consciousness. computed tomography of the skull 02/08: foci of pneumocephalus in the right middle fossa. computed tomography of the skull 10/08: complete regression of foci of pneumocephalus. talked to {person} who denies history of traumatic brain injury. cerebrospinal fluid 08/03: normal. 3 acute kidney injury probably pre-renal resolved acute on chronic kidney disease. probable pre-renal etiology secondary to septic shock nephrological evaluation 08/02/20: no need for urgent dialysis, conservative treatment with hydration 4 upper gastrointestinal bleeding??? history of massive hematemesis, 3-point drop in hemoglobin, no exteriorization witnessed in the service. abdominal ultrasound showing isolated hepatomegaly, no signs of chronic liver disease, no signs of portal hypertension. laboratory tests showed no alterations suggestive of liver disease. upper gastrointestinal endoscopy 08/03: small-caliber varices, no evidence of bleeding. 5 type 2 acute myocardial infarction probably secondary to septic shock: troponin 01/08: 2082, no signs of ischemia on admission electrocardiogram reported normal transthoracic echocardiography on 04/08 follow-up appointment of measurements for sca on 09/08 request catheterization when neurological level permissive catheterization 14/08: no major lesions, right coronary artery with discrete intraluminal irregularities. personal history: pathologies: verify glaucoma in electronic medical record with no other reports. medications in use: eye drops for verify glaucoma in electronic medical record allergies: no record. surgeries: glaucoma 2020, hemorrhoids 2007. habits: long-term alcoholism, described by [person] as high volume. evolution: during hospitalization, [person] evolved with improvement from septic shock of pulmonary focus after instituted antibiotic therapy, with satisfactory ventilatory weaning and good recovery of level of consciousness, having [person] been extubated on 08/10, without complications. Investigation for possible NSTE-ACS was performed, however, catheterization without significant alterations, initial hypothesis of type 2 acute myocardial infarction maintained. [person] stable, without other alterations. antibiotic therapy: current: meropenem d0: 08/07 previous: ceftriaxone d0: 01 08/06 clindamycin d0: 02 08/06 clarithromycin d0: 08/01 08/02 tests during this hospitalization: upper gastrointestinal endoscopy 08/03: varicose stump in the distal portion of the esophagus, small caliber, no signs of recent bleeding and pangastritis note, still without report. computed tomography of the skull 02/08: foci of pneumocephalus in the middle fossa on the right. diffuse and proportionate volumetric reduction. signs of white matter microangiopathy. intracranial carotid atheromatosis. chest tomography 02/08: signs of bilateral pulmonary infectious/inflammatory process, aspiration etiology should be considered; consolidations with air bronchogram signal in the posterior segments of the lower lobes, more important on the right; faint central peribronchovascular ground-glass opacities in the left lumbar spine. ectatic bronchi with irregular contours. mild emphysema and bronchopathy. coronary atheromatosis. hepatic steatosis. abdominal ultrasound 01/08: hepatomegaly, without clear evidence of liver disease; provisional report. echocardiogram 04/08: left ventricular ejection fraction: 65%. chambers with normal dimensions. Normal left ventricular systolic performance and segmental mobility. Preserved right ventricular systolic performance. Catheterization 08/14: no major lesions, right coronary artery with slight intraluminal irregularities. Medications for continuous use at discharge: acetylsalicylic acid 100 mg/day, losartan 50 mg every 12 hours, atorvastatin 40 mg/day. Good general condition, flushed, hydrated, anicteric, acyanotic, afebrile. Nothing worthy of note. Schedule: [person] in significant clinical improvement, with no new episodes of intestinal bleeding, discharge from this ward was chosen. I prescribe for continuous use, given systemic arterial hypertension with high cardiovascular risk: acetylsalicylic acid 100 mg/day, atorvastatin 40 mg/day, losartan 50 mg/day. Please request tests for the high-cost medication process for atorvastatin, via the basic health unit. [person] with small-caliber varicose veins, without other signs of liver disease, I suggest observing liver function, and if respiratory rate worsens, refer for follow-up in gastroenterology. CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 02/08: foci of pneumocephalus in the right middle fossa. CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 10/08: complete regression of foci of pneumocephalus. Evolution: during hospitalization, the patient evolved with improvement from severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) of pulmonary focus after instituted infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy), with satisfactory ventilatory weaning and good recovery of alertness level (level of consciousness), having the patient been extubated on 08/10, without complications. Investigation for possible nste-acs was performed, however, catheterization without significant alterations, initial hypothesis of type 2 acute heart attack (myocardial infarction) maintained. The patient stable, without other alterations. Intracranial carotid atheromatosis. Chest tomography 02/08: signs of bilateral pulmonary infectious/inflammatory process, aspiration cause of disease (etiology) should be considered; consolidations with air bronchogram signal in the posterior segments of the lower lobes, more important on the right; faint central peribronchovascular ground-glass opacities in the left lumbar spine. Ectatic bronchi with irregular contours. Mild emphysema and bronchopathy. Coronary atheromatosis. Hepatic steatosis. Abdominal ultrasound 01/08: hepatomegaly, without clear evidence of liver disease; provisional report. Heart ultrasound (echocardiogram) 04/08: left ventricular ejection fraction: 65%. Chambers with normal dimensions. Normal left ventricular systolic performance and segmental mobility. Preserved right ventricular systolic performance. Catheterization 08/14: no major lesions, right coronary artery with slight intraluminal irregularities. Medications for continuous use at sent home from hospital (discharge): acetylsalicylic acid 100 mg/day, losartan 50 mg every 12 hours, atorvastatin 40 mg/day. Intracranial carotid atheromatosis. Chest tomography 02/08: signs of bilateral pulmonary infectious/inflammatory process, aspiration cause of disease (etiology) should be considered; consolidations with air bronchogram signal in the posterior segments of the lower lobes, more important on the right; faint central peribronchovascular ground-glass opacities in the left lumbar spine. Ectatic bronchi with irregular contours. Mild emphysema and bronchopathy. Coronary atheromatosis. Hepatic steatosis. Abdominal ultrasound 01/08: hepatomegaly, without clear evidence of liver disease; provisional report. Heart ultrasound (echocardiogram) 04/08: left ventricular ejection fraction: 65%. Chambers with normal dimensions. Normal left ventricular systolic performance and segmental mobility. Preserved right ventricular systolic performance. Catheterization 08/14: no major lesions, right coronary artery with slight intraluminal irregularities. Medications for continuous use at sent home from hospital (discharge): acetylsalicylic acid 100 mg/day, losartan 50 mg every 12 hours, atorvastatin 40 mg/day. 93 hydrocephalus, ventriculitis. admission: 2020-01-19, discharge: 2020-03-17, stay: 01 m 26 d 01 h, location: ward, payer: sus. [person] was admitted to the emergency unit of [organization] with fever, vomiting shortly after eating but maintaining normal neurological status. was admitted for laboratory and imaging tests and was admitted to glasgow 9t rm4/ao4/rv1 with possible ventriculitis due to persistent fever. underwent tests that confirmed the diagnostic hypothesis. on 2020-02-05 an external ventricular shunt procedure was performed, cerebrospinal fluid tests showed an infectious nature. After prolonged antibiotic therapy with meropenem and vancomycin, improvement in the cerebrospinal fluid was observed, with the possibility of performing a ventriculoperitoneal shunt with a low-pressure valve on 03/12/2020, without complications. Personal history: intraparenchymal hematoma after traumatic brain injury on 07/12. During hospitalization, the patient developed multiple infections and was discharged on 09/21 after refractory to infectious treatment. The patient was discharged on 03/17/2020 in good general condition, with no signs of dehiscence or secretion discharge from the surgical wound, and no new apparent deficits. The patient had no seizures, was afebrile, and accepted enteral nutrition well, with normal neurological status. good general condition, no painful features, eupneic in macronebulization with stable tracheostomy without active drug vaso glasgow 9t ao4 rm4 rv1t, nonspecific withdrawal pupils: anisochoric ed, right pupil with bradyreactive photomotor reflex, mydriatic reactive left pupil, previous cardiopulmonary resuscitation: indifferent cranial surgical wound without dehiscence or secretions, without phlogistic signs abdominal surgical wound good appearance, without secretions or phlogosis. 02/05/2020: installation of external ventricular shunt 03/12/2020: installation of ventriculoperitoneal shunt. should follow-up appointment to the outpatient clinic on 03/22/2020. must follow-up appointment to {organization} on 07/12/2020 anec outpatient clinic, must follow-up appointment to {organization} on 08/04/2020 [organization]. I advise family members of warning signs and seek immediate emergency care if they appear. good clinical and neurological conditions. Hydrocephalus, ventriculitis. 2 syncope? high-risk csrs electrocardiogram with bre bav 1st degree negative troponin, history of present illness: [person] reports that the patient presented weakness in both legs at 10 h after bathing. followed by transient unresponsiveness for 20-25 minutes, without sphincter release or other signs of crisis, denies dilated alteration, denies traumatic brain injury. also reports that in the emergency room she presented blood pressure of 80 x 60. in time, she reports a fall from her own height 9 days ago and on Monday, with traumatic brain injury in the frontal region. admitted with limited movement of the right lower limb due to pain, somewhat confused, without aphasia, hemiparesis or sensory deficits. personal history: rankin 4 walked with assistance, diabetes mellitus, systemic arterial hypertension, dementia to be clarified, continuous use medication: carverdilol 3.125 mg 12/12h losartan 50 mg 12/12h, habits: smoker for around 60 years x pack, relevant information from hospital admission: additional tests: head tomography 02/06/2020: aspects 10, diffuse atrophy and microangiopathy, no ischemic lesions CT Angiography (CTA) 02/06/2020: no occlusions or proximal stenoses, hospital admission history: [person] was admitted by [organization], who investigated the condition with imaging tests and ruled out the hypothesis of a cerebrovascular event. due to the presence of left bundle branch block and first-degree atrioventricular block visualized on electrocardiogram, requested evaluation of the case by [organization]. [organization] assumed care, with the main hypothesis for the condition being a decrease in consciousness secondary to sepsis, decrease in the level of consciousness hypotension, sofa score 2, being questioned as to urinary focus, urinalysis test with leukocyturia and bacteriuria, cutaneous [person] with sacral and abdominal pressure injury, onset of diarrhea during hospitalization. Antibiotic therapy with ceftriaxone and metronidazole was started on 06/03/2020 to cover the infectious condition, in addition to intravenous hydration with ringer lactate. [person] received clindamycin for one day, but suspended due to the previously mentioned regimen already offering coverage for anaerobes, considering the cutaneous focus. [person] is undergoing oral potassium replacement due to decreased serum levels. due to poor oral intake, on 06/06/2020, a nasoenteral tube was inserted to provide a laxative enteral diet. Last bowel movement on 04/06/2020. [person] showed improvement in blood pressure levels and improvement in general condition, and on 06/06/2020, a transfer to a bed in [organization] was proposed to complete antibiotic therapy and maintain potassium replacement. Clinical evaluation of [person] on the day of hospital discharge: anamnesis: I found [person] in an unmonitored bed. [person] accompanied by a friend of the family [person]. She is eupneic on room air, with no signs of respiratory distress. She is normotensive, with a blood pressure curve tending to the lower limit of normal, without using vasoactive drugs. [person] is sleepy, uncooperative with clinical evaluation, disoriented in time and space. She answers questions in a confused manner. She complains of pain in her right thigh. She denies other clinical complaints. [person] is not accepting oral nutrition and required 40 ml of 50% glucose solution yesterday to correct blood glucose levels. Last bowel movement reported in the medical record by the nursing staff on 06/04 {omitted}. [person] is unaware of further details of the clinical picture and reports that [person] will follow-up appointment in the afternoon and will be able to provide more detailed information about the picture. 95 high identification report: [person], 44 years old, from [address], high school graduate, electrician, right-handed, problem: guillainbarré syndrome semiology: weakness pain in limbs and back on 07/03/2020 suggestive clinical picture cerebrospinal fluid 07/05 with proteinocytological dissociation 2 cells / 333.9 prot egris score 4 orotracheal intubation on 07/05 due to respiratory fatigue cerebrospinal fluid 07/05: blood pressure 60 cmh2o, 2 cells, 1 red blood cells, 333.9 prot, glucose 58.8 diagnosis 230 cerebrospinal fluid 07/18: bp 20 / 2 cells / 0 red blood cells / 468 ptn / 71 glucose dextro 96. no additional procedures. therapy performed: igiv total dose 140g 06/07 11/07 history: patient diagnosed with guillainbarré syndrome with cerebrospinal fluid and compatible clinical condition, having been hospitalized in {organization} between 07 08/2020, presenting a high degree of dependence, tetraparetic, requiring bipap and feeding through a nasoenteric tube. transferred from the emergency care unit, with a report of treatment with levofloxacin for 7 days on 08/17 due to a urinary tract infection present in a neurology screening exam. in the 5 days prior to hospitalization, he presented episodes of visual and auditory hallucinations, progressing to drowsiness and a decline in general condition. after admission, he presented increased secretion in the tracheostomy, started empirical ceftriaxone on 09/02, with increasing oxygen needs with bipap. transferred to emergency room on 09/04, requiring vasoactive drug. transitioned from antibiotic therapy to meropenem and vancomycin. admitted to intensive care unit conscious, contacting, hemodynamically stable, on transport ventilator. personal history: denies previous pathologies. coronavirus june used astrazeneca 20 days ago third dose of coronavirus. denies relevant family diseases. home use: denies. relevant tests: cerebrospinal fluid 07/05: blood pressure 60 cmh2o, 2 cells, 1 red blood cells, 333.9 prot, glucose 58.8 diagnosis 230 cerebrospinal fluid 07/18: bp 20/ 2 cells/ 0 red blood cells/ 468 ptn/ 71 glucose dextrose 96. no additional procedures. General physical examination: regular general condition, weight loss, hydrated and normal-colored mucous membranes. Respiratory auscultation: bilateral vesicular breath sounds without adventitious sounds. Cardiac auscultation: normal heart sounds, regular heart rhythm in normal S1 and S2, without murmurs. Skin: no pressure injury or inflammatory signs. Neurological examination on discharge: conscious, Glasgow coma scale 11t o4 v1t m6, Isochoric and photoreactive pupils. Hirschberg: centered gaze. No nystagmus or abnormal movements. REM D/E: grade 1 upper limbs, grade 0 lower limbs. Indifferent cardiopulmonary resuscitation, no signs of meningeal irritation. No stigmata of crisis. Procedures: as discussed with [person], it was decided to suspend meropenem and vancomycin, considering [person] on day 8 of antibiotic therapy, already clinically improving. I discharge him from the hospital, considering [person] stable, afebrile, having follow-up appointmented to his previous baseline state. admission: 09/03/2020, discharge: 09/12/2020, stay: 08 d 20 h, location: ward payer: sus. High identification report: the patient, 44 years old, from [address], high school graduate, electrician, right-handed, problem: guillainbarré syndrome semiology: weakness pain in limbs and back on 07/03/2020 suggestive clinical picture cerebrospinal fluid 07/05 with proteinocytological dissociation 2 cells / 333.9 prot egris score 4 orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) on 07/05 due to respiratory fatigue cerebrospinal fluid 07/05: blood pressure 60 cmh2o, 2 cells, 1 red blood cells, 333.9 prot, glucose 58.8 diagnosis 230 cerebrospinal fluid 07/18: bp 20 / 2 cells / 0 red blood cells / 468 ptn / 71 glucose dextro 96. No additional procedures. Therapy performed: igiv total dose 140g 06/07 11/07 history: patient diagnosed with guillainbarré syndrome with cerebrospinal fluid and compatible clinical condition, having been hospitalized in the hospital between 07 08/2020, presenting a high degree of dependence, tetraparetic, requiring bipap and feeding through a nasoenteric tube. Transferred from the emergency care unit, with a report of treatment with levofloxacin for 7 days on 08/17 due to a urine infection (UTI) present in a neurology screening exam. In the 5 days prior to hospitalization, he presented episodes of visual and auditory hallucinations, progressing to drowsiness and a decline in general condition. After admission, he presented increased secretion in the breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy), started empirical ceftriaxone on 09/02, with increasing oxygen needs with bipap. Transferred to emergency room on 09/04, requiring vasoactive drug. Transitioned from infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) to meropenem and vancomycin. Admitted to ICU (serious care room) conscious, contacting, stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), on transport ventilator. Personal history: denies previous pathologies. Coronavirus june used astrazeneca 20 days ago third dose of coronavirus. Denies relevant family diseases. Home use: denies. Relevant tests: cerebrospinal fluid 07/05: blood pressure 60 cmh2o, 2 cells, 1 red blood cells, 333.9 prot, glucose 58.8 diagnosis 230 cerebrospinal fluid 07/18: bp 20/ 2 cells/ 0 red blood cells/ 468 ptn/ 71 glucose dextrose 96. No additional procedures. General physical examination: regular general condition, weight loss, hydrated and normal-colored mucous membranes. Respiratory auscultation: bilateral normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds) without adventitious sounds. Cardiac auscultation: normal heart sounds, regular heart rhythm in normal s1 and s2, without murmurs. Skin: no pressure injury or inflammatory signs. Neurological examination on sent home from hospital (discharge): conscious, glasgow coma scale 11t o4 v1t m6, isochoric and photoreactive pupils. Hirschberg: centered gaze. No nystagmus or abnormal movements. Rem d/e: grade 1 upper limbs, grade 0 lower limbs. Indifferent cardiopulmonary regovernment health scheme (sus)citation, no signs of meningeal irritation. No stigmata of crisis. Procedures: as discussed with the patient, it was decided to government health schemepend meropenem and vancomycin, considering the patient on day 8 of infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy), already clinically improving. I sent home from hospital him from the hospital, considering the patient stable, no fever (afebrile), having return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointmented to his previous baseline state. 96 sepsis of urinary focus. admission: 01/17/2020, discharge: 01/18/2020, stay: 20 h, bed location, ward, payer: sus. identification: [person], 60 years old, bricklayer, lives in [address] problem: acute symptomatic crisis secondary to sepsis? bacteremia? computed tomography admission: no changes history: report by a nurse at [organization] that the patient had started dental treatment 5 days ago with an implant, using clavulin. on 01/16, the patient began to have a fever of 38.6º, chills, asthenia, decreased level of consciousness, progressing to an epileptic seizure of unknown duration and nature at home, with postictal confusion. he sought care at the emergency care unit in [address], where the crisis recurred, presenting oxygen saturation desaturation of 78%. admitted to the {organization} with Glasgow coma scale 15, febrile, tachypneic, hypotensive, blood pressure 69x53, without neurological deficits, with complaints of dysuria and foul-smelling urine. personal history: left calcaneal pseudoarthrosis, smoker 2 packs/day for 40 years, exercise user denies: systemic arterial hypertension, diabetes mellitus denies: allergies, home use: denies use of medications, relevant tests: computed tomography of the skull: no alterations, verify cerebrospinal fluid report: volume 3 ml, clear appearance, colorless, cellularity 1.66, red blood cells 0.33, glucose 93.6, protein 37.6, chloride 124.0, lactate 2.9, cerebrospinal fluid without signs of infection [person] with probable tremors associated with bacteremia, lowering due to sepsis of urinary focus, referred with a diagnosis of seizure, ruled out after normal computed tomography of the skull and cerebrospinal fluid, furthermore [person] denies any history of loss of consciousness and states that in fact he was only experiencing tremors. urinalysis test suggestive of urinary tract infection and [person] septic, we maintain treatment with ceftriaxone 1g IV 12/12 h d1 01/17 and norepinephrine at 0.1 mcg/kg/min in weaning, we request a place in the intensive care unit {organization}. good general condition. computed tomography of the skull: no alterations verify cerebrospinal fluid report: volume 3ml, clear appearance, colorless cellularity 1.66 red blood cells 0.33 glucose 93.6 protein 37.6 chloride 124.0 lactate 2.9. cerebrospinal fluid puncture. transfer to the intensive care unit serrana. transfer to the intensive care unit {organization}. good general condition. Serious blood infection (sepsis) of urinary focus. 97 admission: 2020-12-07, discharge: 2020-12-13, stay: 06 d 03 h, location: ward, payer: sus. discharge report hematology date of admission 2020-07-07 discharge date: 2020-12-13 reason for admission: biopsy of a nodular lung lesion on 2020-07-12 and ii induction of chemotherapy remission. [person], 53 years old, from [address]. general services assistant. problem: acute myeloid leukemia with maturation, intermediate risk ii diagnosis: 27/10 initial blood count: hemoglobin: 7.5 hematocrit: 25 visceromegaly: 85 rdw: 17.7% gb: 89.8 thousand plaq: 37 thousand sp: 88% blasts, presence of auer rod, positive mpo. erythroblasts 17 in 100 cells counted myelogram: bl: 67%, pm: 1%, nb: 1%, ly: 6%, pl: 1%, erythroblasts: 24%. myeloperoxidase staining positive esterase staining negative. conclusion: compatible with acute myeloid leukemia with maturation. if mo 10/29: cd34, cd117, cd33, cd13, hladr, cd11b, cd19, cd38, cd123 dim, cd133, cd15, cd64/, cd300e, cd56, cd7/ karyotype 10/28/21: 46, xx [ 20 ] molecular biology 10/29: absence of mutation in npm1 and flt3 transthoracic echocardiography 10/29: no changes left ventricular ejection fraction 63% serologies 10/29: elisa and hemoagglutination for chagas positive other serologies negative chemotherapies released: i ir 3 7 d1: 10/30 control: 4% blasts drm 11/24: in processing ii ir 3 6 d1: 10/12 discussed during general visit on 12/09 start 2nd IR with alternative regimen with reduced dose adapted for the elderly given the presence of multiple complications during 2nd IR and borderline performance status. control: awaiting programming DRM: awaiting programming complications during treatment: during 1st IR 3 7 neutropenic colitis requiring total parenteral nutrition possible fungal pulmonary infection tomography 12/11 refractoriness to platelet transfusion gastrointestinal hemorrhage melena refractory septic shock on 11/19 of undefined focus during 2nd IR 3 6 at adjusted dose without complications so far regarding the left cervical mass: cervical tomography 11/03 verbal report: left lymph node with decreased dimensions compared to the previous exam, measuring 1.8 x 1.6 cm, with maintained point of necrosis. intense ipsilateral perilymph node inflammatory process, extending to the retroauricular region. left parotid gland enlargement, with heterogeneous pattern, without signs of lithiasis. without signs of abscess formation, collection, infectious involvement. [person] evaluated by [organization] on 11/04: since there are no signs of collection or abscess on tomography or physical examination, we do not consider there is an indication for surgical approach at this time. since the patient is already on antibiotic therapy, we recommend analgesia and clinically observe the evolution of the condition parotid infiltration due to acute myeloid leukemia. if signs of worsening of the condition or new imaging findings are observed, please contact the team again [organization] personal history denies comorbidities positive chagas serology, denies continuous use of medications denies allergies surgeries: cholecystectomy denies smoking and alcoholism family history: {omitted} died at age 53 due to complications from chagas disease, {omitted} died at age 84 of respiratory failure due to heart problem [person] {omitted}, 59 years old, lives in [address] no comorbidities alcoholic and smoker [person] unsure of full name, 62 years old, lives in {city} chagas disease 7 children, the youngest of which has epilepsy. hospitalization exams abdominal and pelvis tomography 11/08: exam showing signs of an infectious, inflammatory process in the proximal jejunum segment, compatible with enteritis chest tomography 11/08: discrete right lung opacity, which may indicate an initial inflammatory, infectious process. control is suggested. 11/08: sinus tomography without alterations. neck tomography with a mass originating in a lymph node and invading adjacent soft tissues verbal report 11/12: sinus tomography: without alterations chest tomography: presence of a nodule in the right lower lobe suggestive of invasive fungal lesion abdominal tomography: colitis without alterations when compared to the last exam chest tomography 11/29 verbal report: [person] maintains nodular lesion with no change in its dimensions in relation to the exam of 11/12. presents resolution of the ground-glass lesions present in the last tomography. abdominal ultrasound 11/29: no changes previous antibiotic therapy: polymyxin b 11/19 11/25 meropenem 11/07 11/25 vancomycin 11/12 11/20 amikacin 11/19 11/20 amphotericin 11/12 11/20. chemotherapy. biopsy result. procedures: 1. hospital discharge 2. maintain prophylaxis with voriconazole and acyclovir 3. maintain oral potassium replacement with slow k 4. prescribe filgrastim from 12/16 to 22 5. scheduled follow-up visits: outside the day 12/15 clinical evaluation and blood count check. reinforce [person] about starting filgrastim hla 12/17 clinical and laboratory evaluation. define next steps for treatment. check lung biopsy results from 12/07 transfusion room on 12/20 assess transfusion needs outside the day on 12/22/21 check blood count 6. advise immediate follow-up appointment for any signs of fever or bleeding. outside the day 12/15 clinical evaluation and blood count check. reinforce [person] about starting filgrastim hla 12/17 clinical and laboratory evaluation. define next treatment steps. check lung biopsy results from 12/07 transfusion room on 12/20 assess transfusion needs outside the day on 12/22 check blood count. stable. Admission: 2020-12-07, sent home from hospital (discharge): 2020-12-13, stay: 06 d 03 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Sent home from hospital (discharge) report hematology date of admission 2020-07-07 sent home from hospital (discharge) date: 2020-12-13 reason for admission: biopsy of a nodular lung lesion on 2020-07-12 and ii induction of chemotherapy remission. The patient, 53 years old, from [address]. General services assistant. Problem: acute myeloid leukemia with maturation, intermediate risk ii diagnosis: 27/10 initial blood count: blood count (Hb): 7.5 hematocrit: 25 visceromegaly: 85 rdw: 17.7% gb: 89.8 thousand plaq: 37 thousand sp: 88% blasts, presence of auer rod, positive mpo. Erythroblasts 17 in 100 cells counted myelogram: bl: 67%, pm: 1%, nb: 1%, ly: 6%, pl: 1%, erythroblasts: 24%. Myeloperoxidase staining positive esterase staining negative. Conclusion: compatible with acute myeloid leukemia with maturation. If mo 10/29: cd34, cd117, cd33, cd13, hladr, cd11b, cd19, cd38, cd123 dim, cd133, cd15, cd64/, cd300e, cd56, cd7/ karyotype 10/28/21: 46, xx [ 20 ] molecular biology 10/29: absence of mutation in npm1 and flt3 transthoracic echocardiography 10/29: no changes left ventricular ejection fraction 63% serologies 10/29: elisa and hemoagglutination for chagas positive other serologies negative chemotherapies released: i ir 3 7 d1: 10/30 control: 4% blasts drm 11/24: in processing ii ir 3 6 d1: 10/12 discussed during general visit on 12/09 start 2nd ir with alternative regimen with reduced dose adapted for the elderly given the presence of multiple complications during 2nd ir and borderline performance status. Control: awaiting programming drm: awaiting programming complications during treatment: during 1st ir 3 7 neutropenic colitis requiring total parenteral nutrition possible fungal pulmonary infection tomography 12/11 refractoriness to platelet transfusion gastrointestinal hemorrhage melena refractory severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) on 11/19 of undefined focus during 2nd ir 3 6 at adjusted dose without complications so far regarding the left cervical mass: cervical tomography 11/03 verbal report: left lymph node with decreased dimensions compared to the previous exam, measuring 1.8 x 1.6 cm, with maintained point of tissue death (necrosis). Intense ipsilateral perilymph node inflammatory process, extending to the retroauricular region. Left parotid gland enlargement, with heterogeneous pattern, without signs of lithiasis. Without signs of pus-filled swelling (abscess) formation, collection, infectious involvement. The patient evaluated by [organization] on 11/04: since there are no signs of collection or pus-filled swelling (abscess) on tomography or physical examination, we do not consider there is an indication for surgical approach at this time. Since the patient is already on infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy), we recommend analgesia and clinically observe the evolution of the condition parotid infiltration due to acute myeloid leukemia. If signs of worsening of the condition or new imaging findings are observed, please contact the team again [organization] personal history denies comorbidities positive chagas serology, denies continuous use of medications denies allergies surgeries: cholecystectomy denies smoking and alcoholism family history: died at age 53 due to complications from chagas disease, died at age 84 of lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) due to heart problem the patient , 59 years old, lives in [address] no comorbidities alcoholic and smoker the patient unsure of full name, 62 years old, lives in the city chagas disease 7 children, the youngest of which systemic arterial high BP seizure disorder (epilepsy). Presents resolution of the ground-glass lesions present in the last tomography. 98 [person], 37 years old, married, 01 child, born and raised in {city}. personal history: systemic arterial hypertension, grade iii obesity, previous weight: 134 kg, height: 1.72 m, bmi 45, gdm gestation 03 years ago, anxiety disorder depression. admission: 05/25/2020, discharge: 07/05/2020, stay: 01 m 10 d 08 h, location: ward, payer: sus. 1 severe acute respiratory syndrome, coronavirus. onset of symptoms: 05/14, worsening of symptoms: 05/19, orotracheal intubation mechanical ventilation: 05/24, 06/13 tracheostomy: 06/13, occluded on 06/29, metal cannula on 06/30, decannulation on 07/02, after fibroscopy critically ill polyneuromyopathy, corticosteroid therapy 05/25 06/06. 2 acute kidney injury kdigo iii resolved, baseline creatinine: 0.7, creatinine 05/25: 2.7, creatinine 07/04: 0.47. 3 fungemia due to Candida parapsilosis under treatment, blood culture 06/18: Candida parapsilosis, blood culture 06/21: negative, micafungin started on 06/21, transitioned to fluconazole on 06/30 according to fungigram d14/d14 fungemia protocol: fundus examination, transthoracic echocardiography and abdominal ultrasound without alterations. vital data: blood pressure: 160x100 mmHg, heart rate: 80 beats per minute, oxygen saturation: 92% room air, respiratory rate: 15 inspirations per minute. general: good general condition, pale 1/4, hydrated, acyanotic, anicteric, afebrile. nervous system: Glasgow coma scale 15, conscious and oriented, without focal deficits. Cardiac auscultation: Normal rhythmic heart sounds in normal S1 and S2, without murmurs. Peripheral pulses palpable and universally broad. Respiratory auscultation: Symmetrical vesicular breath sounds, reduced at the bases, without signs of respiratory distress. Symmetrically reduced expansibility. Abdomen: Globular, present bowel sounds, flaccid, painless. No masses or visceromegaly. Extremities: No edema, free calves, warm and well-perfused extremities. Fundus examination 06/22: Funduscopy within normal standards in both eyes. Echocardiogram 06/23: Cardiac chambers with normal dimensions, concentric hypertrophy of the left ventricle, preserved left ventricular systolic performance, ejection fraction 62% Simpson, normal segmental mobility, no vegetations visualized. Abdominal ultrasound 06/24: Marked hepatic steatosis. outpatient follow-up clinical medicine, speech therapy, laryngotracheal stenosis and tracheostomy. outpatient follow-up clinical medicine, speech therapy, laryngotracheal stenosis and tracheostomy. clm 03/08 speech therapy 08/07 laryngotracheal stenosis and tracheostomy 05/08. good general condition, good respiratory pattern in room air and no signs of active infectious process. 99 admission: 13/12/2020, discharge: 31/01/2021, stay: 01 m 17 d 21 h, location: ward, payer: sus. id: [person], 60 years old, from [address] weight: 74 kg height: 174 cm. problem: 1 left parieto-occipital hypodensity to be clarified, ischemic stroke pacs hce? last seen well on 12/10, initial nihss 25, previous rankin 0 recanalization therapy: contraindicated outside the window evolved decreased level of consciousness mental confusion electrographic status, cranial computed tomography 12/13: left parieto-occipital hypodensity, white matter edema up to the occipital horn of the lateral ventricle cranial computed tomography admission: subacute pattern hypodensity forming in apparently the territory of the inferior division of the left middle cerebral artery CT Angiography (CTA) of the skull and cervical vessels: absence of proximal occlusions 2 norse focal structural epilepsy with electrographic status captured on electroencephalogram on 12/16 new clinical event on 12/18 escape from crisis on 12/28 probably due to prominent cortical lesion electrographic evolution to superrefractory status resolved 3 sepsis of urinary focus 12/21: enterococcus faecalis ampicillin in 12/22 4 sepsis with cutaneous focus sacral lesion thrombophlebitis necrosis left upper limb due to phenytoin extravasation vancomycin 1/3 in use negative blood cultures 5 catheter-related bloodstream infection blood culture 1/9: acinetobacter baumannii 6 acute kidney injury kdigo iii sepsis? creatinine admission 12/30: 0.55 creat 1/16: 5.28 7 {omitted}, chest tube on 1/17 8 new septic shock of undetermined etiology in meropenem vancomycin on 1/30 gram negative bacilli growth on 2 blood culture amst. history of present illness: [person] initially admitted by [organization] with suspected meningitis. [person] lives with [person], [person] reports that she has a complaint of a new, holocranial, high-intensity headache 8/10, which started 10 days ago and has been to the emergency care unit several times for analgesia, with improvement. On 10/12, she reports a decrease in her level of consciousness with mental confusion. She presented with DXT 550 upon admission. She was last seen well on 10/12. She is taking ceftriaxone 2 g 12/12 due to suspected meningitis. I am receiving [person] in the basic unit, hemodynamically stable, without the use of vasoactive drugs, without oxygen, eupneic on room air. personal history: insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus, systemic arterial hypertension, alcoholism 34 cans/day denies smoking medications: losartan 50 mg 12/12 hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg fluoxetine 20 mg nph insulin 25 iu at 10 pm stroke protocol: computed tomography of the skull, admission 12/14: subacute pattern hypodensity forming in the territory of the lower division of the left middle cerebral artery, late control computed tomography of the skull 12/21: better definition of hypodensity, without signs of new ischemic events or hemorrhagic transformations CT Angiography (CTA) of the skull and cervical vessels 12/14: absence of proximal occlusions, mild atheromatosis of the cranial vessels ultrasound of the lower limbs: absence of deep vein thrombosis control computed tomography of the skull 12/15: hypodensity forming in the territory of the lower division of the left mca electrocardiogram: wellens pattern type b, sinus, transthoracic echocardiography: ef 65% / ae 60 / index 36 lipid profile: colt 168 / tri 111 / hdl 56 / ldl 89 vdrl: nr elisa for chagas: nr glycated hemoglobin: rheumatoid factor: 7.16 esv: 43 c3 dosage 2.26 elisa for human immunodeficiency virus nr of other tests electroencephalogram 12/15: subtle focal status epilepticus on the left electroencephalogram 12/16: no status. changes compatible with postictal. electroencephalogram 12/18: the findings are compatible with structural changes of a cortical, subcortical nature in the left cerebral hemisphere. the current test is maintained in relation to the previous one performed on 12/17. electroencephalogram 12/30: the findings are consistent with focal status epilepticus and structural cortico-subcortical alterations in the left cerebral hemisphere. electroencephalogram 12/31: the findings are consistent with focal status epilepticus and structural cortico-subcortical alterations in the left cerebral hemisphere. electroencephalogram 1/1: the background activity findings are compatible with asymmetric diffuse encephalopathy to the detriment of the left cerebral hemisphere. the acute morphology of the LPDs, in addition to the presence of electrographic seizures, correspond to an interictal continuum, which may occur in the context of structural lesions associated with epileptic seizures. electroencephalogram 1/2: the examination was abnormal due to a high burden of focal seizures involving the HCE, as described. the findings described above are consistent with subtle electroclinical status epilepticus with a focal basis, with isolated, subintrant seizures. Continuous electroencephalogram 02/01 03/01: the findings described above are consistent with focal electrographic status epilepticus and effects of sedative drugs, with no resolution of nonconvulsive status epilepticus after infusion of phenobarbital and midazolam. Electroencephalogram 04/01: the findings described above are consistent with diffuse encephalopathy of structural etiology, effects of sedative drugs and recording of four electrographic seizures not configuring status epilepticus. From 05/01 onwards, no status in serial electroencephalograms. Electroencephalogram 19/01: the examination revealed abnormal due to marked disorganization of the background activity, as described. The patient has been admitted to the emergency care unit for a long time. The patient lives with a new, holocranial, high-intensity headache, which started 10 days ago. She reports that she had a serious headache in her consciousness. She said that she was not able to get the symptoms of the headache. She also found that the patient's headache had a weakness in the head. She felt like she was being treated with vasoactive drugs, without oxygen, breathing normally (eupneic) on room air. The doctor said she was happy with the condition of the patient. She told her doctor that she is taking ceftriaxone 2 g 12/12 due to government health scheme (sus)pected brain membrane infection (meningitis). I am receiving the patient in the basic unit, stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), without the use of vasoleone, without any medicine, and even without the need for medicine. This is why she is using the medicines to keep the patient safely in the room air, especially when it's difficult to use it.</s> Personal history: insulin-dependent sugar disease (diabetes), systemic arterial high BP (hypertension), alcoholism 34 cans/day denies smoking medications: losartan 50 mg 12/12 hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg fluoxetine 20 mg nph insulin 25 iu at 10 pm stroke protocol: CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull, admission 12/14: subacute pattern hypodensity forming in the territory of the lower division of the left middle cerebral artery, late control CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 12/21: better definition of hypodensity, without signs of new reduced blood supply (ischemic) events or hemorrhagic transformations ct angiography (cta) of the skull and cervical vessels 12/14: absence of proximal occlusions, mild atheromatosis of the cranial vessels ultrasound of the lower limbs: absence of deep vein blood clot (thrombosis) control CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 12/15: hypodensity forming in the territory of the lower division of the left mca ECG/heart tracing (electrocardiogram): wellens pattern type b, sinus, transthoracic echocardiography: general hospital room (ward) 65% / ae 60 / index 36 lipid profile: colt 168 / tri 111 / hdl 56 / ldl 89 vdrl: nr elisa for chagas: nr glycated blood count (Hb): rheumatoid factor: 7.16 esv: 43 c3 dosage 2.26 elisa for human immunodeficiency virus nr of other tests electroencephalogram 12/15: subtle focal prolonged seizure (status epilepticus) on the left electroencephalogram 12/16: no status. Changes compatible with postictal. Electroencephalogram 12/18: the findings are compatible with structural changes of a cortical, subcortical nature in the left cerebral hemisphere. The current test is maintained in relation to the previous one performed on 12/17. Electroencephalogram 12/30: the findings are consistent with focal prolonged seizure (status epilepticus) and structural cortico-subcortical alterations in the left cerebral hemisphere. Electroencephalogram 12/31: the findings are consistent with focal prolonged seizure (status epilepticus) and structural cortico-subcortical alterations in the left cerebral hemisphere. Electroencephalogram 1/1: the background activity findings are compatible with asymmetric diffuse brain dysfunction (encephalopathy) to the detriment of the left cerebral hemisphere. The acute morphology of the lpds, in addition to the presence of electrographic seizures, correspond to an interictal continuum, which may occur in the context of structural lesions associated with epileptic seizures. Electroencephalogram 1/2: the examination was abnormal due to a high burden of focal seizures involving the hce, as described. The findings described above are consistent with subtle electroclinical prolonged seizure (status epilepticus) with a focal basis, with isolated, subintrant seizures. Continuous electroencephalogram 02/01 03/01: the findings described above are consistent with focal electrographic prolonged seizure (status epilepticus) and effects of sedative drugs, with no resolution of nonconvulsive prolonged seizure (status epilepticus) after infusion of phenobarbital and midazolam. Electroencephalogram 04/01: the findings described above are consistent with diffuse brain dysfunction (encephalopathy) of structural cause of disease (etiology), effects of sedative drugs and recording of four electrographic seizures not configuring prolonged seizure (status epilepticus). From 05/01 ongeneral hospital rooms, no status in serial electroencephalograms. Electroencephalogram 19/01: the examination revealed abnormal due to marked disorganization of the background activity, as described. The findings are consistent with marked diffuse brain dysfunction (encephalopathy), which may occur in the context of drug effects, metabolic disorders and structural alteration. chest tomography 09/10/2021: diffuse heterogeneity of the lung parenchyma, with ground-glass areas that may be related to alveolar hemorrhage. 1.2 arthritis in the ankles, ruled out ankle ultrasound 09/15/2021: absence of synovial hypertrophy or hyperemia on doppler study. small effusion in the recess of the talonavicular joint bilaterally. 1.3 bicytopenia secondary to disease activity? mds? azathioprine toxicity? methylprednisolone pulse therapy 1g first: 09/17 second: 09/18 third: 09/19 immunoglobulin 25 g/d for 5 d 25 09/29 recovery of thrombocytopenia from 10/07. admission: 14/09/2021, discharge: 30/09/2021, stay: 16 d 03 h, location: ward, payer: sus. reason for hospitalization: 1 cough recurrent hemoptysis dyspnea worsening for 4 days alveolar hemorrhage secondary to systemic lupus erythematosus? pulmonary thromboembolism? poor therapeutic adherence [person] without use of prednisone and chloroquine as prescribed, missed outpatient appointments, negative PCR for coronavirus 08/21/2021, history of current illness: [person] reports recurrent hemoptysis, with worsening for 4 days, larger volume, associated with worsening of dyspnea with mild exertion. no association with fever. no other complaints. history: 1 systemic lupus erythematosus diagnosis in {omitted} lupus nephritis recurrent alveolar hemorrhage antibodies: pil / beta2 / nucleosome / coombs / aca / low complement / proteinuria: 1.7 g / hematuria / leukocyturia / homogeneous FAN 1:400 and speckled 1:800 / antidna: 1:320 2 chronic kidney disease stage g5a3 diagnosis 3 years ago hemodialysis in {city}: Monday, Wednesday and Friday 5am - 9am 3 triple positive gestational saaf, aortic and mitral valve disease 2 spontaneous abortions 4 mild hap 5 systemic arterial hypertension 6 valvulopathy: mild mitral regurgitation catheterization 2019 7 previous cocaine use lifestyle: reports having stopped using cocaine a few months ago, cyclophosphamide pulse therapy methylprednisolone: first pulse: 09/18 second pulse: 10/18 third pulse: 11/18 developed acute hypertensive pulmonary edema orotracheal intubation improvement after hemodialysis fourth pulse: 01/19 lost follow-up recount pulse therapy scheduled for 12 cycles fifth pulse: 04/2020 sixth pulse 05/2020 scheduled pulse for June, [person] lost follow-up. Latest hospitalizations: 2019: decompensated valvular heart failure, profile B, May 2019: alveolar hemorrhage with restart of pulse therapy, new pulse scheduled in {omitted}, not performed, continuous medications: furosemide 40 mg, 110, reports use of 220, acetylsalicylic acid 100 mg, without use, prednisone 5 mg, reports regular use, losartan 580 mg, 12/12 hours, without use, carvedilol 25 mg, 12/12 hours, using 12.5 mg, 12/12 hours, propatilnitrate 30 mg, 8/8 hours due to headache with isosorbide, reports use of 30 mg, 12/12 hours, chloroquine 250 mg, 1x/d, Monday to Friday, without use for a few months, clonidine 150, 12/12 hours, maintains use although withdrawn from the last prescription, evolution during current hospitalization: [person] was admitted through the red room of {organization} due to possible coronavirus infection. RTPCR collected with negative result, and was later referred to an unmonitored bed, prednisone 1 mg/kg/day and chloroquine 250 mg/day were introduced and an evaluation by the [organization] team was requested. Hemodialysis was performed on 21/08 and 22/08 with UF 2000. It was decided to perform d-dimer given the diagnostic hypothesis of pulmonary thromboembolism with low-risk Wells score. d-dimer result was slightly elevated, however, CT Angiography (CTA) was not performed, given that [person] is poorly adherent, does not use the medications prescribed by [organization], has repeated episodes of alveolar hemorrhage and missed the last two appointments scheduled by the specialty, making it impossible to start anticoagulation in [person] without regular medical monitoring. Chest tomography without contrast was performed to evaluate possible alveolar hemorrhage, with no evidence of bleeding. [person] remained hemodynamically stable throughout hospitalization, with no need for vasoactive drugs, no recurrence of hemoptysis episodes, and no externalization of bleeding in other topographies. Hospital discharge was chosen with early outpatient follow-up for reevaluation by the attending physician. I spoke with [person] from [organization] who will schedule [person] appointment for the next {omitted} on 08/31 at the acol outpatient clinic. History of current illness: [person] reports pain and edema in bilateral ankles, taking prednisone 80 mg. Also reports a burning sensation in the anal region, soles of the feet, and vaginal region. Also complains of recurrent palatal ulcers, which sting and burn. Denies odynophagia and dysphagia. Today he had a coughing fit with hemoptysis. Complains of dyspnea and orthopnea. Denies fever, reports constipation, and residual diuresis without changes. Progress during hospitalization: [person] did not recur hemoptysis during hospitalization. Received pulse therapy with methylprednisolone at the beginning of hospitalization and maintained at 1 mg/kg/day, without satisfactory response of thrombocytopenia. Then, an infusion of human immunoglobulin was prescribed, which was completed on 09/29. [person] was discharged on 09/30, when she developed septic shock, with growth of Acinetobacter baumannii multir in blood culture, and was admitted to the intensive care unit. She was treated with polymyxin B meropenem for 10 days, with good response. Patient showed platelet recovery from 10/07, but required transfusion of 1 red blood cell concentrate on 10/10 due to hemoglobin 6.5. Physical examination: good general condition, pale mucosa 2/4, hydrated, anicteric and acyanotic. Lymph nodes cannot be palpated, afebrile to the touch. Oral cavity: no lesions. Respiratory auscultation: symmetrical expansibility, vesicular breath soundss present and well distributed, no adventitious sounds. Cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm, normal heart sounds, 3/6 systolic murmur in the mitral area. Abdomen: flaccid, bowel sounds present and normal, tympanic, painless on superficial and deep palpation, liver palpable 3 cm from the costal margin. Extremities: warm, broad and symmetrical pulses, capillary refill time less than 3 s, no edema, no signs of deep vein thrombosis. good general condition, hydrated, pale /4, anicteric, acyanotic, afebrile, glasgow 15, tvj at the level of the clavicle at 90 degrees, oxygen saturation 97%, heart rate 111 beats per minute pulmonary auscultation: universal vesicular breath sounds with discreet bibasilar crackles respiratory auscultation: regular heart rhythm, normal sound sounds, systolic murmur /6 pancardiac abdomen: protuberant due to adiposity, bowel sounds, flaccid, painless on palpation, no signs of peritonitis, no palpable masses or visceromegaly, lower limbs: no edema, no muscle swelling, pedal pulses present bilaterally. presence of functioning arteriovenous fistula in the right upper limb extremities: fine and symmetrical pedal pulse, without edema or cyanosis, capillary filling time 3s sound: no arthritis. relevant tests chest tomography 10/09: diffuse heterogeneity of the lung parenchyma, with ground-glass areas that may be related to alveolar hemorrhage. signs of asymmetric myocardial hypertrophy, with septal predominance. correlation with echocardiogram is suggested. pulmonary arteries and branches: normal calibers, without signs of filling failure. lung parenchyma: heterogeneous attenuation coefficient, with multiple areas of ground-glass attenuation, predominating in the lower lobes, associated with discrete smooth thickening of interlobular septa and some intralobular lines. sparse atelectatic parenchymal bands. ankle ultrasound 15/09: absence of synovial hypertrophy or hyperemia on Doppler study. small effusion in the recess of the talonavicular joint bilaterally. myelogram 09/24: bone marrow obtained by aspiration puncture in cipd, consisting of normocellular bone fragments for age, with a l:e ratio of 1.9:1. sv: normocellular, with preserved maturation. presence of hemoglobinization failure and basophilic stippling dyserythropoiesis in 10 to 50% of the cells. sb: normocellular, with preserved maturation. sm: megakaryocytes present in adequate numbers in the smear. differential count bl: 1%, pm: 1%, m: 6%, mm: 7%, nb: 19%, ns: 28%, ly: 3%, pl: 1%, erythroblasts: 34%. karyotype 09/24: inconclusive due to the absence of metaphases. no surgeries or procedures performed. As discussed and advised by [person]: hospital discharge with early return to the outpatient clinic of the attending clinic for reassessment of the condition. New case consultation at [organization] on 10/20 to check karyotype and BMO. Short outpatient return to check myelogram and bone marrow biopsy. Corticosteroid weaning. Rtpcr collected with negative result, and was later referred to an unmonitored bed, prednisone 1 mg/kg/day and chloroquine 250 mg/day were introduced and an evaluation by the [organization] team was requested. Kidney dialysis (hemodialysis) was performed on 21/08 and 22/08 with uf 2000. It was decided to perform d-dimer given the diagnostic hypothesis of pulmonary thromboblood clot blocking a vessel (embolism) with low-risk wells score. D-dimer result was slightly elevated, however, ct angiography (cta) was not performed, given that the patient is poorly adherent, does not use the medications prescribed by [organization], systemic arterial high BP (hypertension) repeated episodes of alveolar hemorrhage and missed the last two appointments scheduled by the specialty, making it impossible to start anticoagulation in the patient without regular medical monitoring. Chest tomography without contrast was performed to evaluate possible alveolar hemorrhage, with no evidence of bleeding. The patient remained stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable) throughout hospitalization, with no need for vasoactive drugs, no recurrence of hemoptysis episodes, and no externalization of bleeding in other topographies. Hospital sent home from hospital (discharge) was chosen with early outpatient return visit to doctor (follow-up) for reevaluation by the attending physician. I spoke with the patient from [organization] who will schedule the patient appointment for the next on 08/31 at the acol OPD (Out Patient Department). History of current illness: the patient reports pain and swelling (edema) in bilateral ankles, taking prednisone 80 mg. Also reports a burning sensation in the anal region, soles of the feet, and vaginal region. Also complains of recurrent palatal ulcers, which sting and burn. Denies odynophagia and dysphagia. Today he had a coughing fit with hemoptysis. Complains of difficulty in breathing (dyspnea) and orthopnea. Denies fever, reports constipation, and residual diuresis without changes. Progress during hospitalization: the patient did not recur hemoptysis during hospitalization. Received pulse therapy with methylprednisolone at the beginning of hospitalization and maintained at 1 mg/kg/day, without satisfactory response of low platelet count (thrombocytopenia). Then, an infusion of human immunoglobulin was prescribed, which was completed on 09/29. The patient was sent home from hospital (discharge)d on 09/30, when she developed severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock), with growth of acinetobacter baumannii multir in blood test to find infection (blood culture), and was admitted to the ICU (serious care room). She was treated with polymyxin b meropenem for 10 days, with good response. Patient showed platelet recovery from 10/07, but required transfusion of 1 red blood cell concentrate on 10/10 due to blood count (Hb) 6.5. Physical examination: good general condition, pale mucosa 2/4, hydrated, no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric) and no bluish discoloration (acyanotic). Lymph nodes cannot be palpated, no fever (afebrile) to the touch. Oral cavity: no lesions. Respiratory auscultation: symmetrical expansibility, normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds)s present and well distributed, no adventitious sounds. Cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm, normal heart sounds, 3/6 systolic murmur in the mitral area. Abdomen: flaccid, bowel sounds present and normal, tympanic, painless on superficial and deep palpation, liver palpable 3 cm from the costal margin. Extremities: warm, broad and symmetrical pulses, capillary refill time less than 3 s, no swelling (edema), no signs of deep vein blood clot (thrombosis). Good general condition, hydrated, pale /4, no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no fever (afebrile), glasgow 15, tvj at the level of the clavicle at 90 degrees, oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation) 97%, heart rate 111 beats per minute pulmonary auscultation: universal normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds) with discreet bibasilar crackles respiratory auscultation: regular heart rhythm, normal sound sounds, systolic murmur /6 pancardiac abdomen: protuberant due to adiposity, bowel sounds, flaccid, painless on palpation, no signs of infection inside the abdomen (peritonitis), no palpable masses or visceromegaly, lower limbs: no swelling (edema), no muscle swelling, pedal pulses present bilaterally. Presence of functioning dialysis access point on arm (arteriovenous fistula) in the right upper limb extremities: fine and symmetrical pedal pulse, without swelling (edema) or cyanosis, capillary filling time 3s sound: no arthritis. Relevant tests chest tomography 10/09: diffuse heterogeneity of the lung parenchyma, with ground-glass areas that may be related to alveolar hemorrhage. Signs of asymmetric myocardial hypertrophy, with septal predominance. Correlation with heart ultrasound (echocardiogram) is suggested. Pulmonary arteries and branches: normal calibers, without signs of filling failure. Lung parenchyma: heterogeneous attenuation coefficient, with multiple areas of ground-glass attenuation, predominating in the lower lobes, associated with discrete smooth thickening of interlobular septa and some intralobular lines. Sparse atelectatic parenchymal bands. Ankle ultrasound 15/09: absence of synovial hypertrophy or hyperemia on doppler study. Small effusion in the recess of the talonavicular joint bilaterally. Myelogram 09/24: bone marrow obtained by aspiration puncture in cipd, consisting of normocellular bone fragments for age, with a l:e ratio of 1.9:1. Sv: normocellular, with preserved maturation. Presence of blood count (Hb)ization failure and basophilic stippling dyserythropoiesis in 10 to 50% of the cells. Sb: normocellular, with preserved maturation. Sm: megakaryocytes present in adequate numbers in the smear. Differential count bl: 1%, pm: 1%, m: 6%, mm: 7%, nb: 19%, ns: 28%, ly: 3%, pl: 1%, erythroblasts: 34%. Karyotype 09/24: inconclusive due to the absence of metaphases. No surgeries or procedures performed. As discussed and advised by the patient: hospital sent home from hospital (discharge) with early return to the OPD (Out Patient Department) of the attending clinic for reassessment of the condition. New case consultation at [organization] on 10/20 to check karyotype and bmo. Short outpatient return to check myelogram and bone marrow biopsy. Corticosteroid weaning. Outpatient return scheduled at acol on 10/26, hospital sent home from hospital (discharge) with prednisone 30 mg/day, hydroxychloroquine, acetylsalicylic acid and antihypertensives. [organization] 10/05, the patient was in good condition, lucid and oriented in time and space. The patient had no recurrence of hemoptysis, no drop in blood count (Hb), no bleeding in other topographies. The doctor said that the patient is still in good health. The hospital has a good condition for sent home from hospital (discharge). The doctors said the patient has improved the condition of the hospital. They hoped to get better care for the patient. They will be able to get the best treatment. They also need to be careful and careful. They said that it's good to see if the patient's condition improves. They're in good care. They need better care and support for the hospital's patients. They don't have any problems. However, there aren't any problems with the patient, they'll be in need of care. This is a good time for the doctor's help. The hospital, welcoming the patient to see how well the doctor is treated, and we'll see if he'll continue to be in good [Other medical details: breathing normally (eupneic).] d688. other specified coagulation defects. m329. systemic lupus erythematosus, disseminated. r048. haemorrhage from other sites in airways. a419. septicemia, ne. z992. dependence on renal dialysis. septicemia, ne. r048. 0. haemorrhage from other sites in airways. i132. s. hypertensive heart and kidney disease with congestive heart failure and i. z911. 0. personal history of nonadherence to medical treatment or regimen. m321. s. systemic lupus erythematosus, disseminated. with involvement of other organs and. z992. dependence on renal dialysis. n18. chronic renal failure. {alive} 29.9 1.23

They reported progressive worsening of baseline dyspnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, and orthopnea. They took [person] to {organization} as soon as the symptoms began, and she was hospitalized for 4 days, where heart failure was diagnosed. They did not provide a transthoracic echocardiogram or discharge report, and that she needed home oxygen at 2 l/min 24 hours a day. After discharge, [person] continued to have dyspnea, which had worsened significantly for 2 days, and she remained bedridden. They took her back to [organization], where she was hospitalized, initially using an oxygen catheter. According to [person], on 11/23, around 8:00 p.m., [person] was wearing an oxygen mask with a reservoir and [person] witnessed 35% desaturation and a heart rate of 30 beats per minute. According to the doctor responsible for the removal, [person] went into cardiorespiratory arrest with asystole, which was reversed after 5 minutes with the administration of 1 ampoule of adrenaline. According to the physician, orotracheal intubation was performed at the time and [person] did not require vasoactive drugs after cardiorespiratory arrest. [person] denies any complaints of chest pain by [person]. Admission: 11/24/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 12/07/2020, stay: 13 d 11 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme (sus). Smoker patient, with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and previous asthma, icfep, admitted on 11/24 and referred to a zero bed at [organization], in a post-cardiorespiratory arrest condition. Upon admission, accompanied by the patient and the patient, who report that 1 year and a half ago the patient had a skin infection associated with confusional symptoms, having been admitted to [organization], where she was diagnosed with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. After this hospitalization, she continued to smoke, had difficulty in breathing (dyspnea) on exertion, but without limitations for daily activities. 20 days ago, they report having noticed hyperemia of the left lower limb, swelling (edema) of the lower limbs and increased abdominal volume, evolving with confusional symptoms, presenting more sleepiness. They reported progressive worsening of baseline difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), paroxysmal nocturnal difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), and orthopnea. They took the patient to the hospital as soon as the symptoms began, and she was hospitalized for 4 days, where heart failure was diagnosed. They did not provide a transthoracic heart ultrasound (echocardiogram) or sent home from hospital (discharge) report, and that she needed home oxygen at 2 l/min 24 hours a day. After sent home from hospital (discharge), the patient continued to have difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), which had worsened significantly for 2 days, and she remained bedridden. They took her back to [organization], where she was hospitalized, initially using an oxygen catheter. According to the patient, on 11/23, around 8:00 p.m., the patient was wearing an oxygen mask with a reservoir and the patient witnessed 35% deoxygen level in blood (saturation) and a heart rate of 30 beats per minute. According to the doctor responsible for the removal, the patient went into cardiorespiratory arrest with asystole, which was reversed after 5 minutes with the administration of 1 ampoule of adrenaline. According to the physician, orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) was performed at the time and the patient did not require vasoactive drugs after cardiorespiratory arrest. The patient denies any complaints of chest pain by the patient. The patient denies any recent history of fever, cough, odynophagia, anosmia, dyspepsia, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or previous contact with the patient. Gradual hemodynamic improvement with cessation of the vasoactive drug on 11/26. Presenting difficult ventilation, with blood gases showing a chronic retention pattern since admission. Extubated on 11/27, with installation of noninvasive ventilation aftergeneral hospital room (ward)s. Remained dependent on high fractions of oxygen. Admission: 11/24/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 12/07/2020, stay: 13 d 11 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme (sus). Smoker patient, with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and previous asthma, icfep, admitted on 11/24 and referred to a zero bed at [organization], in a post-cardiorespiratory arrest condition. Upon admission, accompanied by the patient and the patient, who report that 1 year and a half ago the patient had a skin infection associated with confusional symptoms, having been admitted to [organization], where she was diagnosed with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. After this hospitalization, she continued to smoke, had difficulty in breathing (dyspnea) on exertion, but without limitations for daily activities. They found that the patient had a weakness in the lower limb, swelling (edema) and increased abdominal volume, which caused confusion, and sleepiness. They reported progressive worsening of baseline difficulty in breathing (dyspnea). They took the patient to the hospital as soon as the symptoms began, and she was hospitalized for 4 days after the surgery. They also found that she had a heart attack, which had worsened the blood pressure, and the patient was not able to breathe properly. They said that she needed home oxygen at 2 l/min 24 hours a day. They didn't have enough blood pressure. They found it hard to see if the patient felt more likely to have a heart failure. They wanted to see whether the patient could be able to get the blood samples of the patient, which could help the patient recover from the disease, and if it had been worsened, they could have been able to make it clear that it's safe for the doctor to take care of the patients. The doctor said the patient needed a heart rate of 30 beats per minute, which was worsened significantly for 2 days, and that it could be a good time for the hospital to get it to the patient. The hospital's doctor also said that it was important to make sure that it doesn't change the patient's heart rate, and it is safe for everyone to see how well the patient is According to the physician, orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) was performed at the time and the patient did not require vasoactive drugs after cardiorespiratory arrest. The patient denies any complaints of chest pain by the patient. The patient denies any recent history of fever, cough, odynophagia, anosmia, dyspepsia, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or previous contact with the patient. 102 coronavirus infection. admission: 03/05/2020, discharge: 05/07/2020, stay: 02 m 01 d 17 h, location: ward, payer plan: sus. medical report [person]. admitted to {organization} on 03/05 with acute respiratory failure secondary to coronavirus, with a positive rtpcr test on 02/27/2020. on the same day of admission, she was intubated and transferred to {organization}. during her hospitalization, she presented several complications, such as acute kidney injury requiring hemodialysis from 04/13 to 04/20, septic shock secondary to pneumonia associated with mechanical ventilation, and high-response atrial fibrillation reversed chemically with amiodarone. she underwent tracheostomy on 03/24, without complications. She took ceftriaxone, clarithromycin, meropenem, vancomycin, gentamicin, acyclovir, polymyxin B, and ampicillin sulbactam. On 4/23, she was transferred to [organization] under the care of [organization] for motor rehabilitation, completion of antibiotic therapy, weaning from supplemental oxygen, and decannulation. She was evaluated by speech therapy and remained on exclusive enteral nutrition for most of her hospitalization due to severe dysphagia. She was decannulated on 5/5, and on 5/6, oral nutrition was introduced in combination with enteral nutrition. She underwent a transthoracic echocardiogram on 5/5, with the results noted below, and an electrocardiogram on 5/6, showing sinus rhythm and ventricular extrasystoles. At the moment, [person] is in good general condition, conscious and oriented, eupneic on room air, with the tracheostomy orifice occluded. requiring a wheelchair but with progressive improvement in motor strength. with good acceptance of oral diet. Transthoracic echocardiography 05/05: ejection fraction 60%, moderate concentric hypertrophy of the left ventricle, preserved systolic performance of the ventricles, normal segmental mobility of the left ventricle, mild mitral insufficiency. discharged from hospital [person] with scheduled follow-up appointment visit at [organization] on 05/18 for reevaluation and fine-tuning of medications for systemic arterial hypertension and diabetes mellitus. Scheduled follow-up appointment visit with the nutrition and speech therapy teams to progress the diet and remove the nasogastric tube. I recommend continuing physical therapy for motor rehabilitation. I recommend care for sacral pressure injury. I leave a prescription for continuous-use medications and request special medication to release Xarelto prophylactic dose for 30 days. age 05/18. Excellent general condition. Coronavirus infection. Admission: 03/05/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 05/07/2020, stay: 02 m 01 d 17 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer plan: government health scheme. Medical report the patient. Admitted to the hospital on 03/05 with acute lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) secondary to coronavirus, with a positive rtpcr test on 02/27/2020. On the same day of admission, she was intubated and transferred to the hospital. During her hospitalization, she presented several complications, such as acute kidney injury requiring kidney dialysis (hemodialysis) from 04/13 to 04/20, severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) secondary to lung infection (pneumonia) associated with machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation), and high-response irregular heartbeat (atrial fibrillation) reversed chemically with amiodarone. She underwent breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) on 03/24, without complications. She took ceftriaxone, clarithromycin, meropenem, vancomycin, gentamicin, acyclovir, polymyxin b, and ampicillin sulbactam. On 4/23, she was transferred to [organization] under the care of [organization] for motor rehabilitation, completion of infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy), weaning from supplemental oxygen, and decannulation. She was evaluated by speech therapy and remained on exclusive enteral nutrition for most of her hospitalization due to severe dysphagia. She was decannulated on 5/5, and on 5/6, oral nutrition was introduced in combination with enteral nutrition. She underwent a transthoracic heart ultrasound (echocardiogram) on 5/5, with the results noted below, and an ECG/heart tracing (electrocardiogram) on 5/6, showing sinus rhythm and ventricular extrasystoles. At the moment, the patient is in good general condition, conscious and oriented, breathing normally (eupneic) on room air, with the breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) orifice occluded. Requiring a wheelchair but with progressive improvement in motor strength. With good acceptance of oral diet. Transthoracic echocardiography 05/05: ejection fraction 60%, moderate concentric hypertrophy of the left ventricle, preserved systolic performance of the ventricles, normal segmental mobility of the left ventricle, mild mitral insufficiency. Sent home from hospital (discharge)d from hospital the patient with scheduled return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment visit at [organization] on 05/18 for reevaluation and fine-tuning of medications for systemic arterial high BP and sugar disease (diabetes). I recommend continuing physical therapy for motor rehabilitation. I recommend care for sacral pressure injury. I leave a prescription for continuous-use medications and request special medication to release xarelto prophylactic dose for 30 days. Age 05/18. Excellent general condition. Coronavirus infection. Admission: 03/05/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 05/07/2020, stay: 02 m 01 d 17 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer plan: government health scheme. Medical report the patient. Admitted to the hospital on 03/05 with acute lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) secondary to coronavirus, with a positive rtpcr test on 02/27/2020. On the same day of admission, she was intubated and transferred to the hospital. During her hospitalization, she presented several complications, such as acute kidney injury requiring kidney dialysis (hemodialysis) from 04/13 to 04/20, severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) secondary to lung infection (pneumonia) associated with machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation), and high-response irregular heartbeat (atrial fibrillation) reversed chemically with amiodarone. She underwent breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) on 03/24, without complications. She took ceftriaxone, clarithromycin, meropenem, vancomycin, gentamicin, acyclovir, polymyxin b, and ampicillin sulbactam. On 4/23, she was transferred to [organization] under the care of [organization] for motor rehabilitation, completion of infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy), weaning from supplemental oxygen, and decannulation. She was evaluated by speech therapy and remained on exclusive enteral nutrition for most of her hospitalization due to severe dysphagia. She was decannulated on 5/5, and on 5/6, oral nutrition was introduced in combination with enteral nutrition. She underwent a transthoracic heart ultrasound (echocardiogram) on 5/5, with the results noted below, and an ECG/heart tracing (electrocardiogram) on 5/6, showing sinus rhythm and ventricular extrasystoles. At the moment, the patient is in good general condition, conscious and oriented, breathing normally (eupneic) on room air, with the breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) orifice occluded. It's hard to see if the patient's condition is good enough to get the right balance. Transthoracic echocardiography 05/05: ejection fraction 60% of the ventricles are in good condition, but it doesn't work well with the right side of the body. This means that the patient needs to be careful of the left side of his body. I leave a prescription for the patient with scheduled return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment visit at [organization] on 05/18 for reevaluation and fine-tuning of medications for systemic arterial high BP (hypertension) and sugar disease (diabetes). I recommend a visit to the nutrition and speech therapy teams to progress the diet and remove the nasogastric tube for motor rehabilitation. I don't want to talk to anyone about it. I can't be sure if you need to be able to get it to the right place. I'll be sure that this means you need more care for your body and make sure you get the best possible treatment. I need a special medication to release xarelto prophylactic dose for 30 days.
