104 [person] with onset of flu-like symptoms on May 13, with positive RTPCR coronavirus. After 4 days, his condition worsened and he was hospitalized on May 20, at [organization], due to hematemesis and melena. He had been taking nimesulide for 1 day since the onset of symptoms and worsening of fatigue associated with dyspnea, respiratory rate 26 breaths per minute. Oxygen saturation 80% on room air. Hydration was performed, IBP was started, MR 10 L/min, ceftriaxone 2 g, dexamethasone 10 mg and tests were collected: HB 13.9 / HT 44 / GB 10,400 / rods 4% / platelets 175,000 / international normalized ratio 1.0 / CR 1.9 / UR 83 / chest x-ray: bilateral interstitial alveolar pattern obscuration, evolved with respiratory failure on the night of the 22nd, being intubated, without intercurrent events and transferred to the central emergency care unit, where he remained curarized, with maintenance of corticosteroid therapy, antibiotic therapy with ceftriaxone and prophylactic anticoagulation with enoxaparin, despite the already altered renal function. Report of not having had any more exteriorizations for approximately 5 days. [person] admitted to the red room, hemodynamically stable, not using vasoactive drugs, sedated with diluted midazolam fentanyl and curarized with rocuronium, well coupled to mechanical ventilation, but with significant desaturation 67% {omitted}. [person] maintaining desaturation until peep adjustment of 18 cmh20, reaching oxygen saturation of 78%. 1 severe acute respiratory syndrome due to coronavirus. onset of symptoms: 13/05/20 positive RTPCR: 05/18/20 worsening of symptoms, admission to the emergency care unit {organization}: 05/20 transfer to the central emergency care unit: 05/22 05/23 orotracheal intubation: 05/22 2 upper gastrointestinal hemorrhage 3 chronic kidney disease iiia acute hyperkalemia 4 septic shock from a pulmonary focus pneumonia associated with mechanical ventilation? meropenem d1 02/06 5 atrial fibrillation with high ventricular response resolved amiodarone 300 mg 6 thrombocytopenia to be clarified secondary to sepsis? 20/05 175,000 29/05: 111,000 31/05: 40,500 7 severe hypernatremia. [person] in serious general condition with multiple organ dysfunction cardiovascular: septic shock with use of norepinephrine, dopamine, and vasopressin respiratory: no response to protective and nonprotective ventilation strategies, maintaining saturation below 50%. renal: acute kidney injury requiring dialysis, but not stable enough to perform it hematological: thrombocytopenia due to the condition described, [person] developed cardiorespiratory arrest {omitted}. declared dead at {omitted}. admission: 06/01/2020, discharge: 06/03/2020, stay: 02 d 01 h, ward bed: red area payer: sus. After 4 days, his condition worsened and he was hospitalized on may 20, at [organization], due to hematemesis and melena. He had been taking nimesulide for 1 day since the onset of symptoms and worsening of fatigue associated with difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), respiratory rate 26 breaths per minute. Oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation) 80% on room air. Report of not having had any more exteriorizations for approximately 5 days. The patient admitted to the red room, stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), not using vasoactive drugs, sedated with diluted midazolam fentanyl and curarized with rocuronium, well coupled to machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation), but with significant deoxygen level in blood (saturation) 67% . 105 palliative care. admission: 04/05/2020, discharge: 05/01/2020, stay: 25 d 11 h, location: ward, payer: sus. history of current illness: patient transferred from [person] with a history of waking up well, without complaints. around 10:30 to 11:30 today 04/05 he began to present clonic movements of the right upper limb, systolic blood pressure of 200 mmhg. companion denies change in level or content of consciousness or generalization of seizure. denies other associated complaints. presents a history of 2 previous ischemic strokes in 2019, with sequelae of discrete erasure of the left nasolabial fold and weakness in the ipsilateral hand, with mild impairment of activities. approximately 3 weeks ago, he required a transfusion of 4 packed red blood cells due to anemia sic, without externalization of bleeding. According to [organization], a dose of diazepam was administered at approximately 11 am. [person] maintained spasms during transport, and a new dose of diazepam was administered at 1:10 pm. Upon admission, [person] was drowsy (Glasgow 13 to 4 / RM6 / RV 3), eupneic, and using a mask with a reservoir. He presented left hemiparesis and developed clonus in the left upper limb lasting approximately 1 minute and recurrence thereafter. Clonus was also observed in the right hand. General admission examination: regular general condition, pale / 4, hydrated, anicteric, acyanotic, afebrile. Cardiac auscultation: irregular heart rhythm, normal S1 and S2, normal sound sounds, no murmurs. Respiratory auscultation: universally audible vesicular breath sounds, without adventitious sounds. Eupneic on room air. Extremities: no edema, capillary refill time less than 3 seconds. Neurological: Glasgow 13, NIHSS 11 Previous drowsy deficit, easily awakened by verbal call, disoriented in time and space. Slowed speech secondary to benzodiazepine use?, mild dysarthria. Visual campimetry preserved by threat. Isochoric and photoreactive pupils, preserved extrinsic ocular motility without nystagmus or saccade alterations. Central facial paralysis on the left. Facial sensitivity preserved. Left hemiparesis, proportionate. Cardiopulmonary resuscitation in flexion on the right and indifferent on the left. Full sensitivity preserved in 4 limbs. Absence of ataxia. Computerized tomography of the skull Computerized tomography of the skull with old hypodense areas Parieto-occipital malacea on the left and fronto-parieto-occipital malacea on the right. Diffuse cortical atrophy. no new changes compared to the 2019 exam. atc calcified plaques in the aortic arch and internal carotid artery bilaterally. presence of calcifications in the carotid siphon on the left. presence of a stent in the internal carotid artery on the right. no new changes compared to the 2019 exam. electroencephalogram report 04/27/2020: marked baseline disorganization due to a burst-suppression pattern, with periods of suppression, which present a variable duration of 57 s. the bursts are characterized by monomorphic theta activity, asymmetrical with greater attenuation in the right cerebral hemisphere, occasionally with acute morphology, lasting between 25 s and a maximum amplitude of 50 mcv. no electroencephalographic reactivity to nociceptive stimuli was evidenced. death. Comfort-focused (not curative) care. Admission: 04/05/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 05/01/2020, stay: 25 d 11 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. 106 [person]. admission: 10/23/2020, discharge: 10/23/2020, stay: 3 h, location: ward, payer: SUS. problem 1 hemodynamic instability to be clarified, respiratory failure coronavirus 19? swab requested for coronavirus unlikely sepsis of abdominal focus? jaundice ascites abdominal pain, vomiting initiated vasoactive drugs volume expansion mechanical ventilation 2 acute kidney injury, anuric for 24 hours, presenting complaint, [person] came to [organization] complaining of increased abdominal volume, uncontrollable vomiting, jaundice and weakness and difficulty walking for 4 days. entered [organization] conscious and oriented, but developed respiratory failure, requiring orotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation. [person] was brought by [organization] on mechanical ventilation and using vasoactive drugs. [person] is poorly perfused, with cold extremities, poorly attached to mechanical ventilation, without effective venous access. [person] presenting jaundice and increased abdominal volume. Epigastric pain pancreatitis. Epigastric pain. Epigastric pain. Complications since admission [person] with increasing need for norepinephrine, already in new central venous access, with need for association of vasopressin and stabilization with 1.2 mcg/kg/min and vasopressin 0.03 iu/min and passage of 500 ml of Ringer, but maintaining tachycardia of 135. Electrocardiogram tracing with wide QRS tachycardia heart rate 158, external examinations without electrolyte disturbances, followed immediately by wide QRS bradycardia atrioventricular block? and cardiorespiratory arrest. Cardiopulmonary resuscitation was initiated at {omitted}, pulseless electrical activity with narrow QRS, with 250 ml of bicarbonate, 20 ml of calcium gluconate and 500 ml of Ringer lactate being administered. Spontaneous circulation follow-up appointmented after 10 minutes with wide QRS tachycardia, heart rate 153 and hypotension not measurable by noninvasive blood pressure. Synchronized electrical cardioversion of VT with pulse was chosen without success. Followed at {omitted} new cardiorespiratory arrest with VT without pulse, 2 defibrillations were applied and degeneration to new pulseless electrical activity, cardiopulmonary resuscitation of 16 min, resuscitation ceased due to resuscitation time, chronic disease and death was confirmed at {omitted}, absence of pulse, fixed middle pupils, without corneopalpebral reflex. Death. No pending issues. Death. The patient. 05/30 coldness in the left lower limb, evaluated by [organization] and instructed to keep the limb warm and heparin therapeutic dose, doppler with preserved flow, therapeutic heparin suspended on 09/01, 6. unclassifiable sacral pressure injury and blisters on the heels with necrotic cap with no indication for debridement by surgery on 09/02, conservative treatment, 7. neurological condition oct/20 coronavirus and ischemic stroke oct/2020 [organization] outpatient follow-up with team in post-covid rehabilitation protocol. small vessel disease with microangiopathy Fazekas 2 Follow-up with the neurology team May/20: Neurolues treated, neurological worsening with worsening of motor function, totally dependent for daily activities, without sphincter control, bedridden. 08/31/2020 CT scan of the skull: moderate microangiopathy, chronic ischemic lesions in the left cerebral hemisphere in high convexity affecting pre and post central gyri and chronic ischemic lesion in the right cerebral hemisphere in high convexity, subcortical, parietal on the right 09/01/2020: anisocoria maintained since the afternoon of 08/30 Cycloplegic eye drops used for evaluation, on the same day there was an evaluation by neurology with pupil asymmetry and ED ruled out, pharmacological blockade Ganglion lesion: tonic pupil of [person]? [person]? Third nerve lesion: no evidence of compression on imaging exam Microvascular lesion? 09/01/2020 electroencephalogram: diffuse encephalopathy of a nonspecific nature. history of current illness: this is a bedridden patient, totally dependent for instrumental and basic activities of daily living, modified Rankin scale five, who in October 2020 suffered an ischemic stroke, from then on restricted to bed but still fed by mouth, in May of this year admitted to {organization} for investigation of rapidly progressive dementia, where a vascular and possible neurolues component was found, which was treated adequately, readmitted to {organization} on 08/31/2020 from [organization] due to septic shock of urinary focus, intubated and intolerant to ventilatory weaning, making tracheostomy necessary. admitted to [organization] using bipap through the tracheostomy. During hospitalization, complete oxygen weaning was performed and ventilatory weaning was not tolerated by [person] aiming at macronebulization because in these attempts [person] presented significant tachypnea, respiratory rate 40 inspirations per minute with associated respiratory discomfort, therefore, [person] remains dependent on bipap. previous antibiotic therapy: meropenem d0 08/29 03/09 amikacin d0 08/31 03/09 ceftriaxone tazocim vancomycin d0 23 08/31/20 personal history: mrs 5 ischemic stroke neurolues dementia, probable rapidly progressive dementia, admitted by [organization] in may 2020, treated empirically with crystalline penicillin 14 days and 3 doses of benzathine penicillin after small vessel disease with microangiopathy fazekas 2 ischemic stroke oct/2020 tacs hce esus coronavirus, oct/2020 symptomatic focal epilepsy secondary to traumatic brain injury at age 20, systemic arterial hypertension, non-insulin dependent diabetes mellitus 4 previous abortions {omitted} denies smoking or alcoholism scheduled follow-ups: ancp: 10/06/2020 aned: 02/17/2021 adnv: 03/04/2021. [person]'s current condition glasgow coma scale 11t to 4 rv 1t rm 6, tetraparesis, communicates through gaze direction. moves left upper limb discreetly and nonspecifically. current prescription: 1 enteral nutrition 200 ml 6x/day free water 120 ml 8x/day 2 enoxaparin 40 mg/day 3 atorvastatin 20 mg/day 4 hypromellose 1g every 4h 5 omeprazole ev 40 mg/day 6 lactulose: 10 ml 8/8h 7 topical cold cream 1x/day 8 lamotrigine 50 mg in the morning and 25 mg at night 9 losartan 50 mg every 12h. Vital signs: heart rate: 110, respiratory rate: 26, oxygen saturation: 98% regular general condition, flushed, hydrated, anicteric, acyanotic, afebrile, anasarca. Respiratory auscultation: vesicular breath sounds present bilaterally. Diffuse rhonchi. Cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm in 2t, with normal sound sounds without murmurs. Abdomen: globular abdomen, wall edema, bowel sounds, no visceromegaly or palpable masses, no painful facies on palpation, no bladder. Extremities: warm and well perfused, 3/4 edema in upper and lower limbs, with present and symmetrical peripheral pulses. No calf swelling. Neurology: Isochoric and photoreactive pupils, Glasgow 11t to 4 RV 1t RM 6 directs gaze to the call. Responds yes or no with head movement. Moves left lower limb discreetly when requested. Spasticity in 4 limbs with no signs of pain or discomfort, no stigmata of crisis. Hemodynamically stable. Breathing comfortably via tracheostomy in macronebulization alternating with bipap. Presents gaze directing to the call and responds yes or no with head movement. Remains without verbal response. Makes nonspecific movements in lower limbs and left upper limb. Diuresis and stools present. Diet via nasoenteral tube with no fever spikes in the last 24h. Chest tomography 07/08: opacities suggestive of an infectious inflammatory process, compatible with the hypothesis of viral pneumonia by coronavirus with mild involvement, associated with opacities with a pattern of interstitial lung disease. loculated collections suggestive of abscesses in the right cervical region, with mediastinal extension and another similar collection in the left scapular musculature. excavated nodule in the left scapular region, lymphomegaly to be clarified. neck tomography 06/07: loculated collections suggestive of abscesses in the right cervical region, with extension to the cervicothoracic transition and upper third of the anterior mediastinum. contrast-enhanced examination is suggested for better evaluation. pansinusopathy with signs of exacerbation. neck tomography 08/07: loculated collections suggestive of abscesses in the right cervical region, with extension to the cervicothoracic transition and upper third of the anterior mediastinum, measuring together approximately 7.0 x 8.0 x 4.1 cm with a total estimated volume of 120 ml. the right jugular vein is partially collapsed in its middle third, distal due to the expansive effect of the adjacent right cervical collection, however without images suggestive of filling defects. Neck and chest tomography 07/07: obtained after an attempt to administer intravenous iodinated contrast medium to complement the examination on 06/07. However, retention and extravasation of the contrast medium was observed in the left cervical and supraclavicular regions, making it impossible to obtain adequate images during the contrast phase. Skull computed tomography 08/07: Inflammatory pansinusopathy with signs of exacerbation. Signs of incipient microangiopathy. Left upper limb ultrasound 14/07: Predominantly solid images in the left forearm and wrist. Collection with dense content. We cannot necessarily rule out solid lesions. Soft tissue edema, notably on the dorsum of the hand. Chest tomography 18/07: absence of pulmonary thromboembolism. Suspicious pleuropulmonary changes for edema, not ruling out an associated inflammatory process. Evolutionary control of regressing cervicomediastinal collections. Discrete tissue persists adjacent to the manubulum with mediastinal extension, probably inflammatory, but without defining collection. Left ankle ultrasound 07/19: synovitis and joint effusion in the anterior tibiotalar recess bilaterally, of nonspecific appearance. Subcutaneous lymphedema. Right ankle ultrasound 07/19: synovitis and joint effusion in the anterior tibiotalar recess bilaterally, of nonspecific appearance. Subcutaneous lymphedema. 111 1 acute pulmonary edema on 2020-01-26 volume overload? takotsubo syndrome? minoca? myocarditis? trop 1047 diffuse t-wave inversion catheterization with single coronary artery disease and discrete lesion in cd 30% 2 acute arterial obstruction left lower limb postoperative left suprapatellar amputation on 2020-01-25 previous anticoagulation in heparin pump 3 resolved septic shock of cutaneous focus? ceftriaxone and clindamycin hydrocortisone suspended on 2020-01-23 orotracheal intubation 2020-01-27 4 acute kidney injury secondary to rhabdomyolysis? supporting aggressors: septic shock contrast on 01/20/2020 hydration saline solution 0.9% 1000 ml 6/6 hours 5 epileptic seizures and decreased level of consciousness probable multifactorial cause previous structural alteration, poor adherence, circumstantial secondary to an infectious condition? phenytoin 100 mg 3x/day as [person] no evidence of new ischemic event or convulsive status epilepticus, no additional conduct by {organization} 6 desaturation tachypnea secretion meropenem 01/29 02/05. admission: 01/20/2020, discharge: 02/08/2020, stay: 18 d 23 h, location: ward: payer: sus. upon admission 02/20: report of alcoholic libation the night before admission. On the morning of January 20, there was a report of behavioral arrest evolving into a possible generalized tonic-clonic seizure. An indirect and poor description of the event. No companions were present. They reported that [person] commented on generalized clonus. The patient developed a decreased level of consciousness, with orotracheal intubation at the originating service. 1 ampoule of midazolam and 1 ampoule of fentanyl. There was also a report of hypertension at the origin, a priori 165/100. After that, 225/140. Admitted to [organization], mechanical ventilation, orotracheal intubation. He was hemodynamically unstable and shocked. Blood pressure on admission: 49x24. No vasoactive drugs. No sedation. Glasgow coma scale: 8t. Taken to {organization} for stabilization, where increasing doses of norepinephrine were started. Central venous access was performed in the left subclavian vein, but with an erratic path, it ascended through the left common jugular vein. Central venous access was successfully performed in the right subclavian vein. good general condition, pale 1/4, hydrated, anicteric, acyanotic, afebrile. respiratory auscultation: vesicular breath sounds in both hemithoraxes, no adventitious sounds, oxygen saturation: 94% in room air, no signs of respiratory distress, respiratory rate: 20 inspirations per minute. cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm, 2 normal sound sounds, no audible murmurs, good peripheral perfusion, heart rate: 100 beats per minute, blood pressure: 120x80 mmHg. abdomen: protuberant, normal bowel sounds, normotensive, painless on palpation, no palpable masses or visceromegaly, no pain on sudden decompression. Lower limbs: no edema or swelling in the right lower limb, surgical wound in the stump with a small peripheral hematoma, with stitches, mild to moderate local hyperemia, no secretion output. Neurology: Glasgow scale 14 4/4/6, Isochoric and photoreactive pupils, partially oriented in time, oriented in space and person, without stigmata of crisis. Computerized tomography of the skull (ADM) 20/01: old hemispheric hypodensity in the territory of the ACMD. Exvacuum hydrocephalus. Presence of clip in the ACMD area (M1D). CT Angiography (CTA) of the cervical vessels and skull (ADM): no acute occlusions or subocclusions. Chest x-ray: erratic path, central access inserted through the left subclavian vein. bronchovascular network apparently normal electrocardiogram: sinus tachycardia pathological q wave suggestive of previous acute myocardial infarction of the inferolateral wall transthoracic echocardiography 01/21/20: ejection fraction 31%. mild mitral regurgitation. cardiac chambers with normal dimensions. left ventricular systolic performance moderately to severely depressed. left ventricular segmental mobility: diffuse hypokinesia. right ventricular systolic performance preserved. presence of mild left ventricular diastolic dysfunction. hemodynamics: cardiac output 4.5 l/min. cardiac index 2.2 l/min/m2. Transthoracic echocardiography 01/26/2020: ae 33 ddfve 50 septum 10 pp 10 left ventricular ejection fraction 36 imve 90 im discrete cardiac chambers with normal dimensions msve diffuse hypokinesia with greater involvement of the apex and inferior wall. preserved dsvd. catheterization 02/02/2020: discrete obstructive lesion in cd 30% and other arteries without obstructive lesions. transthoracic echocardiography 02/04: normal cardiac chambers. left ventricular concentric remodeling. normal left ventricular and right ventricular systolic performance. there was improvement in ventricular function in relation to previous exams. suprapatellar left lower limb amputation 01/25/20. [organization]. clinically stable, with no signs of infection in surgical wound. transfer to [organization] to complete family care training and observe level of consciousness. 1 acute pulmonary swelling (edema) on 2020-01-26 volume overload? Takotsubo syndrome? Minoca? Myocarditis? Phenytoin 100 mg 3x/day as the patient no evidence of new reduced blood supply (ischemic) event or convulsive prolonged seizure (status epilepticus), no additional conduct by the hospital 6 deoxygen level in blood (saturation) tachypnea secretion meropenem 01/29 02/05. Admission: 01/20/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 02/08/2020, stay: 18 d 23 h, location: general hospital room (ward): payer: government health scheme. Upon admission 02/20: report of alcoholic libation the night before admission. On the morning of january 20, there was a report of behavioral arrest evolving into a possible generalized tonic-clonic seizure. An indirect and poor description of the event. No companions were present. They reported that the patient commented on generalized clonus. The patient developed a decreased alertness level, with orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) at the originating service. 1 ampoule of midazolam and 1 ampoule of fentanyl. There was also a report of high BP (hypertension) at the origin, a priori 165/100. After that, 225/140. Admitted to [organization], machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation), orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation). He was hemodynamically unstable and shocked. Blood pressure on admission: 49x24. No vasoactive drugs. No sedation. Glasgow coma scale: 8t. Taken to the hospital for stabilization, where increasing doses of norepinephrine were started. Central venous access was performed in the left subclavian vein, but with an erratic path, it ascended through the left common jugular vein. Central venous access was successfully performed in the right subclavian vein. Good general condition, pale 1/4, hydrated, no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no fever (afebrile). Respiratory auscultation: normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds) in both hemithoraxes, no adventitious sounds, oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation): 94% in room air, no signs of respiratory distress, respiratory rate: 20 inspirations per minute. Cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm, 2 normal sound sounds, no audible murmurs, good peripheral perfusion, heart rate: 100 beats per minute, blood pressure: 120x80 mmhg. Abdomen: protuberant, normal bowel sounds, normotensive, painless on palpation, no palpable masses or visceromegaly, no pain on sudden decompression. Lower limbs: no swelling (edema) or swelling in the right lower limb, surgical wound in the stump with a small peripheral hematoma, with stitches, mild to moderate local hyperemia, no secretion output. Neurology: glasgow scale 14 4/4/6, isochoric and photoreactive pupils, partially oriented in time, oriented in space and person, without stigmata of crisis. Computerized tomography of the skull (adm) 20/01: old hemispheric hypodensity in the territory of the acmd. Exvacuum hydrocephalus. Presence of clip in the acmd area (m1d). Ct angiography (cta) of the cervical vessels and skull (adm): no acute occlusions or subocclusions. Chest x-ray: erratic path, central access inserted through the left subclavian vein. Bronchovascular network apparently normal ECG/heart tracing (electrocardiogram): sinus fast heartbeat (tachycardia) pathological q wave suggestive of previous acute heart attack (myocardial infarction) of the inferolateral wall transthoracic echocardiography 01/21/20: ejection fraction 31%. Mild mitral regurgitation. Cardiac chambers with normal dimensions. Left ventricular systolic performance moderately to severely depressed. Left ventricular segmental mobility: diffuse hypokinesia. Right ventricular systolic performance preserved. Presence of mild left ventricular diastolic dysfunction. Hemodynamics: cardiac output 4.5 l/min. Cardiac index 2.2 l/min/m2. Transthoracic echocardiography 01/26/2020: ae 33 ddfve 50 septum 10 pp 10 left ventricular ejection fraction 36 imve 90 im discrete cardiac chambers with normal dimensions msve diffuse hypokinesia with greater involvement of the apex and inferior wall. Preserved dsvd. Catheterization 02/02/2020: discrete obstructive lesion in cd 30% and other arteries without obstructive lesions. Transthoracic echocardiography 02/04: normal cardiac chambers. Left ventricular concentric remodeling. Normal left ventricular and right ventricular systolic performance. There was improvement in ventricular function in relation to previous exams. Suprapatellar left lower limb amputation 01/25/20. [organization]. Clinically stable, with no signs of infection in surgical wound. Transfer to [organization] to complete family care training and observe alertness level (level of consciousness). 1 acute pulmonary swelling (edema) on 2020-01-26 volume overload? Takotsubo syndrome? Minoca? Myocarditis? folic acid deficiency? bacterial hyperproliferation? resolved positive sudam metronidazole 500 mg 8/8h 02/03 02/07 parenteral diet from 02/06 to 02/15 complete resolution of diarrhea after total parenteral nutrition 3 candidemia secondary to peripherally inserted central catheter in resolution seen blood culture with growth of yeast-like fungi candida parapsilosis micafungin d1 02/17 02/23 fluconazole in 02/24 transitioned to oral route on 03/04 normal fo, normal transthoracic echocardiography, awaiting abdominal ultrasound microbiological control 02/16: candida parapsilosis 02/18: negative 02/20: negative in 1 of the samples but candida parapsilosis in 2 blood samples and 1 catheter blood sample. 03/03: processing 4 liver cirrhosis due to autoimmune hepatitis type 1 diagnosis 2001 at {omitted} years of age liver biopsy 2002: presence of nodules, rosettes, inflammatory infiltrate compatible with antimuscular hai upper digestive endoscopy april: normal chc screening: ultrasound 2016: control of chronic liver disease, no evidence of suspicious focal lesions. alfafeto 2016: 1.38 5 diabetes mellitus 1 diagnosis at {omitted} 6 noonan syndrome?. admission: 01/02/2021, discharge: 10/03/2021, stay: 01 m 08 d 23 h, location: ward, payer: sus. History of current illness: [person] and [person] report significant weight loss 6 months ago, normal weight 52 kg, and 3 months ago, dark liquid diarrhea without mucus or blood, several episodes a day, hyporexia, headache in the temporal region almost daily, worse on the right. Denies fever, denies sweating, denies vomiting, coughing or other complaints. Reports that on 01/29 he fainted at home due to hypoglycemia and was taken to the emergency care unit [person] with low diet acceptance, but maintained daily insulin injections. Upon admission, he was a little confused, slowed down, with difficulty expressing symptoms and time of onset of each one. [person] reports that after losing [person] 40 days ago due to liver problems, [person] presented significant worsening of the hyporexia. Hepatomegaly, thinness. Microbiological control 02/16: Candida parapsilosis 02/18: negative 02/20: negative in 1 of the samples but Candida parapsilosis in 2 blood samples and 1 catheter blood sample. 03/03: processing. Parenteral nutrition. Laboratory tests to be collected on 03/20. 114 admission: 14/07/2020, discharge: 17/07/2020, stay: 02 d 17 h, location: ward, payer: sus. meeting [person] in a monitored bed, hemodynamically unstable, using high doses of a vasoactive drug, using sedative and analgesia coupled to mechanical ventilation. [person] admitted to the unit this morning due to hemodynamic instability that started yesterday afternoon. [person] was admitted to [organization] on 07/14 with a diagnostic hypothesis of coronavirus, with a history of a drawn-out cough, in addition to abdominal pain and diarrhea for 10 days. [person] positive for human immunodeficiency virus, vertical transmission, but with poor adherence to treatment, including recent hospitalization due to herpes zoster and skin abscesses. after ruling out coronavirus infection, [person] kept in an isolation ward bed to rule out tuberculosis. [person] had been evolving with hemodynamic stability and without respiratory distress, with control of the abdominal condition. However, yesterday [person] evolved with worsening abdominal pain and stopped eliminating feces, and an evaluation by the surgical team was requested for possible acute abdomen due to occlusion. A computed tomography of the abdomen was requested, which showed no signs of obstructive abdomen, and was evaluated by the surgical team, which also showed no signs of acute abdomen. During the afternoon, [person] evolved with hemodynamic instability refractory to volume expansion, in addition to tachydyspnea and desaturation, requiring orotracheal intubation and initiation of vasoactive drugs in increasing doses. Due to the need for a monitored isolation bed, [person] was referred to our sector. [person] evolving with pancytopenia, presenting bleeding from skin lesions, discharge of a small amount of bright red blood through a nasogastric tube, and a transfusion of 2 packed red blood cells was prescribed. [person] also presented severe acidosis on admission, blood gas analysis, requiring the start of bicarbonate infusion. Due to the progression of the condition with instability, antibiotic therapy was transitioned to meropenem and teicopalamina, in addition to fluconazole associated with a report of candidiasis in the oral cavity. At the moment, [person] is in a very serious general condition, hemodynamically unstable with norepinephrine at 0.8 mcg/kg/min, with a temperature of 39.9 ºC refractory to antipyretic medication, with livedo in the extremities, bleeding from the nasal and oral cavity, presenting signs of disseminated intravascular coagulation. [person] with a high possibility of evolution to death in the next few hours. Vital signs: blood pressure 68 x 42, heart rate 156, temperature 40.1, respiratory rate 28, saturation 90. General: very serious general condition, pale 3/4, dehydrated, anicteric, cyanotic, febrile. Cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm in 2t, tachycardic, heart rate 140 beats per minute. Blood pressure 102x48 66 mmHg. Perfusion preserved. symmetrical respiratory pulses: vesicular breath sounds present bilaterally, globally decreased, worse on the left and at the bases bilaterally, crackles at the left base abdomen: bowel sounds abolished, distended abdomen, depressive, liver 2 cm from the right costal margin, scar in the hypogastrium extremities presence of livedo reticularis in the extremities, cold extremities, non-palpable distal pulses neurology: mydriatic pupils, reactive on the right, non-reactive on the left, sedation agitation scale 5 presence of active bleeding in the nasal and oral cavity and in skin lesions. central venous blood gas analysis pH 7.13 / po2 58.1 / pco2 40.8 / hco3 13.1 / be 15.5 / s 76.2 blood gas analysis pH 7.16 / po2 98.7 / pco2 34.4 / hco3 11.9 / be 15.7 / s 94.3 / lac 5.6 / na 146 / k 4.1 / cai 1.09 / cl 111 / cr 2.07 / ur 86 hb 9.6 / ht 29 / plaq 42kil / gb 1900 / no possibility of cell counting, abdominal computed tomography 07/16: hydroareal distension of intestinal loops without evident mechanical obstructive point, splenic hypodensities to be clarified, ascites, hepatomegaly, bilateral non-obstructive nephrolithiasis. computed tomography of the abdomen, transfusion of platelets and packed red blood cells. investigation for tuberculosis. at {omitted}, [person] presents asystole on the monitor. Meeting the patient in a monitored bed, hemodynamically unstable, using high doses of a vasoactive drug, using sedative and analgesia coupled to machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation). The patient admitted to the unit this morning due to hemodynamic instability that started yesterday afternoon. The patient was admitted to [organization] on 07/14 with a diagnostic hypothesis of coronavirus, with a history of a drawn-out cough, in addition to abdominal pain and diarrhea for 10 days. The patient positive for human immunodeficiency virus, vertical transmission, but with poor adherence to treatment, including recent hospitalization due to herpes zoster and skin pus-filled swelling (abscess)es. After ruling out coronavirus infection, the patient kept in an isolation general hospital room bed to rule out TB (tuberculosis). The patient had been evolving with hemodynamic stability and without respiratory distress, with control of the abdominal condition. However, yesterday the patient evolved with worsening abdominal pain and stopped eliminating feces, and an evaluation by the surgical team was requested for possible acute abdomen due to occlusion. A CT scan (computed tomography) of the abdomen was requested, which showed no signs of obstructive abdomen, and was evaluated by the surgical team, which also showed no signs of acute abdomen. During the afternoon, the patient evolved with hemodynamic instability refractory to volume expansion, in addition to tachydifficulty in breathing (dyspnea) and deoxygen level in blood (saturation), requiring orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) and initiation of vasoactive drugs in increasing doses. Due to the need for a monitored isolation bed, the patient was referred to our sector. The patient evolving with pancytopenia, presenting bleeding from skin lesions, sent home from hospital (discharge) of a small amount of bright red blood through a nasogastric tube, and a transfusion of 2 packed red blood cells was prescribed. The patient also presented severe acidosis on admission, blood gas analysis, requiring the start of bicarbonate infusion. Due to the progression of the condition with instability, infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) was transitioned to meropenem and teicopalamina, in addition to fluconazole associated with a report of candidiasis in the oral cavity. At the moment, the patient is in a very serious general condition, hemodynamically unstable with norepinephrine at 0.8 mcg/kg/min, with a temperature of 39.9 ºc refractory to antipyretic medication, with livedo in the extremities, bleeding from the nasal and oral cavity, presenting signs of disseminated intravascular coagulation. The patient with a high possibility of evolution to death in the next few hours. General: very serious general condition, pale 3/4, dehydrated, no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), cyanotic, febrile. [person] in fair general condition, brought by samu, conscious, with slowed speech, hypotensive, poorly perfused, comfortable in room air. reports that for 01 month he has been presenting a decline in his general condition, associated with myalgia, adynamia and asthenia, progressive dyspnea with minimal exertion, nonspecific chest discomfort not related to exertion, dry cough that evolved to full cough without expectoration. also presents during the period, daily unmeasured fever, with chills, at no specific time, in addition to diffuse abdominal pain, of no specific nature, nausea, vomiting and daily diarrhea, with several bowel movements with liquid stools per day, without blood or mucus. reports dysuria and decreased urinary volume in the same period. reports that symptoms began after new interruption of treatment for human immunodeficiency virus. regular general condition, dependent on oxygen. bactrim 1 tablet 48/48 h adjusted for clearance < 30 ceftriaxone 1 g 12/12h d0 11/28 schedule until 12/08 clarithromycin 500 mg d1 11/28 schedule until 12/07 metronidazole 250 mg 3x/d d0 11/26 schedule until 12/06 vitamin b1 100 mg/day reduced on 12/05. scheduled outpatient evaluation in ne7 uremia 12/18, follow-up appointment of follow-up of ART, human immunodeficiency virus at {organization} upon presentation of a report written by me and scheduled to be delivered by [person] himself upon discharge to [person]: please adjust Bactrim for renal function according to the scheme discussed with [person] infectologist who follows [person]: Cl < 30: 1cp 48/48 h 30 < cl < 50: 1 cp/day 50: 2 cp/day. good general condition, not dependent on oxygen, no complaints. 116 Diagnostic hypotheses: 1. Sepsis of urinary focus; pyelonephritis; 2. Acute kidney injury; kdigo; 3. Secondary infectious condition? Proteinuria; Hematuria; Lower limb edema; Serositis; Pleural effusion; Photosensitivity; Autoimmune disease; Systemic lupus erythematosus; Awaiting antibodies in processing. admission: 13/02/2020, discharge: 28/02/2020, stay: 15 days 11 h, location: ward, payer: SUS. 15 days ago, dysuria and pollakiuria began. 3 days ago, the patient presented with lower back pain, unmeasured fever, and chills, with worsening of pain complaints radiating to the abdominal region, in addition to reduced urine volume and hematuria. The patient sought emergency care with worsening of her general condition. Upon being seen at the emergency care unit, [person] was septic, tachycardic, tachypneic, and hypotensive, with laboratory tests compatible with bacterial infection. She underwent expansion with saline solution and a dose of ceftriaxone, and was then referred to this unit. She denied hospitalization or recent use of antibiotics in the last year. She denied recurrent urinary tract infection or lithiasis. She was admitted to a monitored bed in a clinical stabilization room, awake and oriented, hemodynamically stable, tachypneic on room air, but with no signs of respiratory distress, with good peripheral perfusion. Evolution during hospitalization: initially, antibiotic therapy for sepsis of urinary focus with ceftriaxone was maintained, and a computed tomography scan of the abdomen showed an inflammatory process in the kidneys. She presented hematuria and proteinuria upon admission with a protein/creatinine ratio of 3.48 g. She required supportive renal therapy started on 02/14, with joint follow-up with nephrology during hospitalization, suggesting the need for a renal biopsy after resolution of the infectious condition. On 02/18 [person] presented a disproportionate increase in white blood cells, fever, abdominal pain, and antibiotic therapy was escalated to cefepime. A new abdominal computed tomography scan was performed on 02/18 to investigate a renal abscess, and the absence of an abscess and signs of a bilateral renal inflammatory process were found. After escalation of antibiotic therapy, the general condition improved, the patient remained afebrile and hemodynamically stable. Antibiotic therapy was suspended on 02/28. There was progressive recovery of renal function. Last hemodialysis on 02/21. In addition to urinary changes of hematuria and proteinuria, [person] presented pleural effusion and photosensitivity, and autoantibodies were requested to investigate autoimmune disease and systemic lupus erythematosus. Awaiting the results of tests in processing. Dialysis catheter removed on 02/28. urinalysis test repeated on 02/28. The urinary sediment improved. Previous changes due to an infectious process resolved. good general condition. total abdominal ultrasound 02/13 verbal report: hepatomegaly with liver disease with changes suggestive of fatty liver disease. small amount of free fluid in the abdominal cavity. abdominal computed tomography 02/14: signs of vascular congestion, with cardiomegaly, hepatomegaly, bilateral pleural effusion, free fluid in the cavity. pulmonary opacities on the left, which may correspond to congestion, not ruling out an associated infectious process. kidneys with slight loss of cortical differentiation, suggestive of an inflammatory process, with a hypodense area on the right that may correspond to an area of hypoperfusion. abdominal computed tomography 02/18: signs of bilateral renal inflammatory process. hepatomegaly. hepatic steatosis. small amount of intra-abdominal free fluid. bilateral pleural effusion. pulmonary consolidations. Chest computed tomography 20/02: bilateral pulmonary consolidations, which may represent atelectasis and/or an infectious inflammatory process. Bilateral pleural effusion stable in relation to the previous examination on 18/02. Central venous puncture and hemodialysis. Autoantibodies. Nephrofollow-up appointment. Good general condition. Diagnostic hypotheses: 1. Serious blood infection (sepsis) of urinary focus; pyelonephritis; 2. Acute kidney injury; kdigo; 3. Secondary infectious condition? Need for renal replacement therapy starting on 2/14, intermittently. Nephropathies under joint return visit to doctor (follow-up). Proteinuria; hematuria; lower limb swelling (edema); serositis; fluid around the lungs (pleural effusion); photosensitivity; autoimmune disease; systemic lupus erythematogovernment health scheme (sus); awaiting antibodies in processing. The patient sought emergency care with worsening of her general condition. Upon being seen at the emergency care unit, the patient was septic, tachycardic, tachypneic, and hypotensive, with laboratory tests compatible with bacterial infection. She underwent expansion with saline solution and a dose of ceftriaxone, and was then referred to this unit. She denied hospitalization or recent use of infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic)s in the last year. She denied recurrent urine infection (UTI) or lithiasis. She was admitted to a monitored bed in a clinical stabilization room, awake and oriented, stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), tachypneic on room air, but with no signs of respiratory distress, with good peripheral perfusion. She required supportive renal therapy started on 02/14, with joint return visit to doctor (follow-up) with nephrology during hospitalization, suggesting the need for a renal biopsy after resolution of the infectious condition. On 02/18 the patient presented a disproportionate increase in white blood cells, fever, abdominal pain, and infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) was escalated to cefepime. A new abdominal CT scan (computed tomography) scan was performed on 02/18 to investigate a renal pus-filled swelling (abscess), and the absence of an pus-filled swelling (abscess) and signs of a bilateral renal inflammatory process were found. After escalation of infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy), the general condition improved, the patient remained no fever (afebrile) and stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable). Infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) was government health schemepended on 02/28. There was progressive recovery of renal function. Last kidney dialysis (hemodialysis) on 02/21. Doctors said that the patient's condition was stable and there were no stable controls. z540. convalescence after surgery. a41. other septicemias. n17. acute renal failure. r578. other forms of shock. g400. epilepsies and idiopathic epilepsy syndromes defined by their location. n18. chronic renal failure. e10. insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus. b342. coronavirus infection, nes. c07. malignant neoplasm of parotid gland. r221. localized swelling, mass or tumor of neck. {alive} 34.1 1.12
118 problem: 1 ischemic stroke pacs hce ictus: 14:30 clinical: sudden aphasia decreased level of consciousness after cocaine use nihss admission 32 previous rankin 0 computed tomography of the skull admission: aspects 7 m2, ci, insula, hyperdense linear foci that may correspond to bifrontal hsa CT Angiography (CTA): occlusion of proximal left m1, linear image of filling defect in the left carotid bulb carotid web?, slight irregularity of contour of distal extracranial acis, nonspecific recanalization therapy: thrombolysis by frontal hsa contraindicated mechanical thrombectomy tici 2b, without stent recurrence of occlusion of left m1 ischemia throughout the territory of the occipital acme and due to compression uncal herniation decompressive craniectomy on 06/03 herpetic lesions in 2 dermatomes: Direct vasculitis by herpes virus? Negative pcr for varicella zoster Suspected diagnosis: Ischemic stroke due to focal arteriopathy induced by cocaine, carotid web? 2 Septic shock from soft tissue focus Abscess in the left cervical and paratracheal region Meropenem and vancomycin for 21 days. Current nihss: 21 Current rankin: 5 admission: 02/03/2020, discharge: 11/04/2020, stay: 01 m 09 d, location: ward, payer: sus. Evolution: 02/03: admission to ue, ischemic stroke pacs hce, in orotracheal intubation, and hypertensive peak. Nipride introduced. 02/03: tm performed, tici 2b later, nipride suspended 03/03: control computed tomography: area in m2 suggestive of hemorrhagic transformation or barrier breakdown, 03/03: admission to the intensive care unit, start of vasoactive drug. 03/03: passage of central venous catheter with pneumothorax after circulatory shock, to be clarified 04/03: pneumothorax drained 04/03: control computed tomography 05/03: infectious screening. 06/03: urinary tract infection? ceftriaxone started 03/06: recurrence of stroke in acme territory worsening of the left frontoparietal-occipital ischemic area decompressive craniectomy on 03/06 03/10: chest drain removed by the surgery team 03/10: tracheostomy head and neck surgery reports finding of significant cervical inflammatory process, without pus but much tissue infiltration 03/11: new infection of undefined focus, antibiotic changed to meropenem 03/11: bulging of the left cervical region thyroid abscess with fistula? awaiting final report 03/12: CT scans for new infection screening, suspicious image. in regular general cervical condition, fever plateau associated with empirical vancomycin 03/15: head and neck surgery considers abscess after orotracheal intubation trauma suggests computed tomography control for 03/16, according to the result possible need for approach in surgical center for drainage 03/16: extensive necrotic lesion 7 cm in skin of left forearm contrast extravasation previous peripheral access in left hand?? previous left radial parent vasospasm??. 03/18: herpes zoster in right dorsal region 01 dermatome. start of intravenous acyclovir 03/20: new infectious screening with computed tomography of the skull, sinuses, and neck 03/21: worsening of zoster lesions and start of intravenous acyclovir 03/23: reevaluated by the CLP with no indication for debridement of the left upper limb lesion 03/23: diarrhea for 48 hours, empirical treatment for pseudomembranous colitis with metronidazole via nasoenteral tube started 03/24: viral PCRs checked in cerebrospinal fluid, negative 03/24: check computed tomography 03/20 small image with hypodense center and parietal enhancement to contrast medium in the superficial cervical region, inferior to the tracheostomy path, associated with gas foci in between, measuring 1.3 cm³. another collection is seen in the left paratracheal region, inferior to the glottic region, measuring 0.6 cm³. 03/24: left jugular vein thrombosis visualized on computed tomography 03/20. 03/26: meropenem suspended after 14 days of use. 03/28: recurrence of fever, leukopenia with deviation and no defined focus ICC: endocarditis?? 03/28: meropenem and vancomycin restarted, new blood cultures 03/28: new cervical computed tomography, no abscess 03/29: head and neck surgery reevaluation, no abscess, no indication for approach 03/29: transthoracic echocardiography: no signs of endocarditis 04/06: metronidazole suspended for 14 full days 04/06 04/11: no complications. The patient remained afebrile, with low CRP, and uneventful. Physical examination upon discharge. Vital signs: blood pressure 125x82 mmHg, heart rate 105 beats per minute, oxygen saturation 96% on room air, respiratory rate 20 breaths per minute. In good general condition, flushed, acyanotic, and anicteric. Respiratory auscultation: well-distributed vesicular breath soundss, with the presence of sparse rhonchi. Cardiac auscultation: normal rhythmic heart sounds on 2 tones, without murmurs. Abdomen: flat, flaccid, and painless. Neurology: Glasgow Coma Scale 11 tones AO4, RV2T, RM5. Does not follow simple commands, does not understand, does not name. Emits incomprehensible sounds. Tracheostomy. Conjugated gaze deviation to the left that does not cross the midline with oculocephalic maneuver. left palpebral ptosis miosis cardiopulmonary resuscitation in extension to the right with exhaustible and indifferent clonus to the left. nih 21 1a 1 / 1b.2 / 1c.2 / 2.2 / 3.1 / 4.2 / 5a.0 / 5b.3 / 6b3 / 8.2 / 9.3 cephalic and abdominal fo in good appearance, without phlogistic signs. stroke protocol: computed tomography of the skull admission: aspects 7. presence of a thin layer of left temporoparietal hyperdensity hsa? angiotc of the skull and cervical vessels: proximal occlusion of m1 on the left collaterals grade 2 presence of ulceration in the left internal carotid artery. Cranial computed tomography 03/04: better delimitation of left frontal ischemia, hemorrhagic transformation ph1 and 3mm dlm; Cranial computed tomography 03/06: left frontotemporal ischemia with significant edema causing 13mm dlm and parafalcine and uncal herniation; control computed tomography 03/07: improvement in midline deviation, ischemia formed in vascular territories of frontal, temporal, parietal and occipital acm and acp associated with hemorrhagic transformation ih2 and subcutaneous hematoma in craniectomy topography. electrocardiogram: sinus rhythm, heart rate 75, with 1 mm supraventricular wall transthoracic echocardiography: ejection fraction 73% ae 20ml indexed dystolic performance and preserved biventricular segmental mobility holter: nr lipid profile: ct 85 tg 86 ldl 30 vdrl, human immunodeficiency virus and hepatitis serologies: negative elisa for chagas: negative glycated hemoglobin: 4.59 tsh 4.59 nuclear magnetic resonance brain: scheduled for 11/22 fan negative antib2 glyc negative aca: negative vitamin b12: 490 homocysteine: 7 benzoylecgonine 03/02/22: positive cerebrospinal fluid 03/21/22: 4 cells / 15 red blood cells / 70 ptn / glucose 68 dextro 108/ag viral PCR and culture electroencephalogram 03/22: asymmetric disorganization of the baseline activity, being discrete-moderate in the right hemisphere due to an increase in theta wave content, with a posterior wakefulness rhythm of up to 9 Hz being recorded. marked disorganization in the left hemisphere, due to a diffuse decrease in the amplitude of the rhythms in this hemisphere, absence of the posterior alpha rhythm and an increase in the delta wave content in the frontal regions. [person] presented reactivity to auditory stimuli, with discrete desynchronization of the rhythms and visual contact with [person]. conclusion and electroclinical correlation: the findings are consistent with diffuse encephalopathy. amplitude asymmetry and attenuation of the rhythms in the left hemisphere may be related to structural injury and/or extensive craniectomy in this hemisphere. decompressive craniectomy tracheostomy. Under the care of the clinical team at [organization] Maintain the following medications: Acetylsalicylic acid 100 mg/day Thiamine 100 mg/day orally Baclofen 5 mg 12/12 h Spasticity Methadone 5 mg 8/8 h Pain control follow-up appointment to [organization] on April 25 Cranioplasty evaluation. follow-up appointment to the ADNV neurology outpatient clinic on July 15 Perform magnetic resonance imaging of the skull with sedation On November 22 Fast for 8 hours Request lupus anticoagulant as an outpatient. Conditions for discharge [person] is discharged in good clinical condition, hemodynamically stable, without the use of vasoactive drugs. Alert, makes spontaneous eye contact and when called, does not understand and does not follow simple commands. Spontaneous ventilation under occluded tracheostomy in room air. No signs of respiratory distress. Afebrile. Low CRP. No use of antibiotics. Diaper diuresis. feeding through nasogastric tube. last bowel movement on 04/09. controlled blood glucose levels. no bleeding. Problem: 1 reduced blood supply (ischemic) stroke pacs hce ictus: 14:30 clinical: sudden aphasia decreased alertness level (level of consciousness) after cocaine use nihss admission 32 previous rankin 0 CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull admission: aspects 7 m2, ci, insula, hyperdense linear foci that may correspond to bifrontal hsa ct angiography (cta): occlusion of proximal left m1, linear image of filling defect in the left carotid bulb carotid web?, slight irregularity of contour of distal extracranial acis, nonspecific recanalization therapy: thrombolysis by frontal hsa contraindicated mechanical thrombectomy tici 2b, without stent recurrence of occlusion of left m1 ischemia throughout the territory of the occipital acme and due to compression uncal herniation decompressive craniectomy on 06/03 herpetic lesions in 2 dermatomes: direct vasculitis by herpes virus? Negative pcr for varicella zoster government health scheme (sus)pected diagnosis: reduced blood supply (ischemic) stroke due to focal arteriopathy induced by cocaine, carotid web? 2 severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) from soft tissue focus pus-filled swelling (abscess) in the left cervical and paratracheal region meropenem and vancomycin for 21 days. Current nihss: 21 current rankin: 5 admission: 02/03/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 11/04/2020, stay: 01 m 09 d, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Evolution: 02/03: admission to ue, reduced blood supply (ischemic) stroke pacs hce, in orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation), and hypertensive peak. Nipride introduced. 02/03: tm performed, tici 2b later, nipride government health schemepended 03/03: control CT scan (computed tomography): area in m2 suggestive of hemorrhagic transformation or barrier breakdown, 03/03: admission to the ICU (serious care room), start of vasoactive drug. 03/03: passage of central venous catheter with air trapped in chest (pneumothorax) after circulatory shock, to be clarified 04/03: air trapped in chest (pneumothorax) drained 04/03: control CT scan (computed tomography) 05/03: infectious screening. 06/03: urine infection (UTI)? Respiratory auscultation: well-distributed normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds)s, with the presence of sparse rhonchi. Cardiac auscultation: normal rhythmic heart sounds on 2 tones, without murmurs. Abdomen: flat, flaccid, and painless. Neurology: glasgow coma scale 11 tones ao4, rv2t, rm5. ECG/heart tracing (electrocardiogram): sinus rhythm, heart rate 75, with 1 mm supraventricular wall transthoracic echocardiography: ejection fraction 73% ae 20ml indexed dystolic performance and preserved biventricular segmental mobility holter: nr lipid profile: ct 85 tg 86 ldl 30 vdrl, human immunodeficiency virus and hepatitis serologies: negative elisa for chagas: negative glycated blood count (Hb): 4.59 tsh 4.59 nuclear MRI scan brain: scheduled for 11/22 fan negative antib2 glyc negative aca: negative vitamin b12: 490 homocysteine: 7 benzoylecgonine 03/02/22: positive cerebrospinal fluid 03/21/22: 4 cells / 15 red blood cells / 70 ptn / glucose 68 dextro 108/ag viral pcr and culture electroencephalogram 03/22: asymmetric disorganization of the baseline activity, being discrete-moderate in the right hemisphere due to an increase in theta wave content, with a posterior wakefulness rhythm of up to 9 hz being recorded. Marked disorganization in the left hemisphere, due to a diffuse decrease in the amplitude of the rhythms in this hemisphere, absence of the posterior alpha rhythm and an increase in the delta wave content in the frontal regions. The patient presented reactivity to auditory stimuli, with discrete desynchronization of the rhythms and visual contact with the patient. Conclusion and electroclinical correlation: the findings are consistent with diffuse brain dysfunction (encephalopathy). Amplitude asymmetry and attenuation of the rhythms in the left hemisphere may be related to structural injury and/or extensive craniectomy in this hemisphere. Decompressive craniectomy breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy). 119 1 septic shock on 06/05 abdominal focus? tazocin: 06/05 06/09 intravenous vancomycin: 06/08 06/09 oxacillin: 06/09 06/18 blood culture 06/05: staphylococcus aureus 2 pseudomembranous colitis toxins a/b positive on 27/05/2020 metronidazole d0 on 05/28/2020 06/05 oral vancomycin: 06/05 06/18 3 disease activity ulcerative colitis? prednisone 40 mg/day 4 urinary tract infection urine culture 05/29/2020: candida glabrata 90,000 colonies fluconazole d1: 06/01/20 discussed indication with ccih. admission: 05/26/2020, discharge: 06/18/2020, stay: 22 d 21 h, location: ward, payer plan: sus. [person] with ulcerative colitis, was admitted in early May with abdominal pain and diarrhea with mucus, blood and pus, and was discharged after treatment with corticosteroids and antibiotics. She was discharged and after 2 days, on 05/27, she follow-up appointmented to this hospital reporting follow-up appointment of the same symptoms, initially suspecting exacerbation of colitis and requesting colonoscopy and tests to exclude other causes. After a colonoscopy, with the presence of ulcerative pancolitis and pseudomembranes, and positive a and b toxins results, clostridium difficile infection was suspected as the cause of the condition, although it was not possible to rule out disease activity. After starting metronidazole, [person] initially improved, however, after 8 days of hospitalization, [person] developed fever, abdominal pain, diarrhea and hypotension, and antibiotic therapy was changed from tazocin to oral vancomycin for treatment of pseudomembranous colitis, intravenous vancomycin and vasoactive drug were started due to septic shock and a place in an intensive care unit was requested. [person] evolved well after these measures, being weaned off the vasoactive drug in 1 day and being discharged from the intensive care unit after 2 days. After the result of a blood culture with oxacillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus, antibiotic therapy was transitioned from vancomycin to intravenous oxacillin. [person] evolved clinically and hemodynamically stable after these measures and remained hospitalized until the end of antibiotic therapy, completing d10 of oxacillin on 06/18 and d13 of oral vancomycin on 06/18. [person] in good general condition, lucid and oriented, pale 2/4, hydrated, afebrile to the touch heart rate: 84 beats per minute blood pressure: 150x80 mmHg respiratory rate: 20 inspirations per minute respiratory auscultation: well distributed vesicular breath soundss, without adventitious sounds. no signs of respiratory distress. cardiac auscultation: arrhythmic and normal-phonetic heart sounds in normal S1 and S2, without murmurs. abdomen: protuberant, bowel sounds present, tympanic, flaccid, with mild pain on palpation of iliac fossae. no signs of peritonitis. I do not palpate visceromegaly. extremities: well perfused, capillary refill time 3 sec. presence of edema in the left ankle, without hyperemia, heat or redness, painless. free calves. blood culture 06/05: staphylococcus aureus toxins a/b positive on 27/05/2020. colonoscopy 06/01/2020: intense ulcerative pancolitis pseudomembranous colitis the ulcerative process cannot be attributed to UC activity given the presence of active pseudomembranous colitis. no pending exams. take medications as prescribed by a doctor. follow-up appointment for an outpatient appointment at [organization] on 07/07. [person] is discharged in good general condition, clinically and hemodynamically stable, afebrile, reporting only 1 bowel movement per day, with well-formed stools and no blood, mucus or pus, denying other complaints. 1 severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) on 06/05 abdominal focus? 120 severe coronavirus, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, chronic tracheostomy. admission: 2020-02-10, discharge: 2020-03-18, stay: 01 m 08 d 10 h, location: ward, payer: sus. [person] admitted on 02/09 due to respiratory failure due to coronavirus. remained in intensive care until 03/09, where he had complications from sepsis of pulmonary focus treated on 02/26 klebsiella pneumoniae, serratia, severe refractory bronchospasm presumed chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, critically ill polyneuromyopathy, myoclonus of undetermined cause, used valproic acid, with no recurrence after its discontinuation. In the ward, he showed progressive improvement, with total withdrawal of supplemental oxygen, care for sacral pressure injury, physiotherapy, release of oral diet, exchange of tracheostomy cannula for metal, being discharged on 03/18. Tracheostomy in good general appearance, with metal cannula, absence of wheezing, no respiratory distress, grade iii sacral pressure injury debrided. growth of klebsiella pneumoniae and serratia on 02/26, hypernatremia and acute kidney injury due to dehydration on 02/14. central venous access, indwelling urinary catheter, leaves with need for maintenance due to urinary retention prostate carcinoma. comorbidity control exams for outpatient consultation at a basic health unit and ager. glycemic control control of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease with long-acting bronchodilator, care with long-standing tracheostomy 20 years without need for follow-up by [organization], discussed with specialist follow-up and therapeutic planning of prostate carcinoma, urinary retention with urology follow-up of thoracic nodule with thoracic surgery. ager 06/22 urology 05/11 counter-referral for clinical follow-up in basic health unit and physiotherapy. good general condition breathing in room air, with metal tracheostomy, grade III sacral lesion debrided on 03/15. Severe coronavirus, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, chronic breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy). Admission: 2020-02-10, sent home from hospital (discharge): 2020-03-18, stay: 01 m 08 d 10 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. 123 problem: 1 aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage at the top of the basilar artery fisher 4 hunthess 5 ictus: 01/26 at 04:30 clinical picture: unprecedented crisis tonic posture of the upper limbs lower limbs headache projectile vomiting amaurosis decreased level of consciousness. orotracheal intubation at [organization] on 01/26 upon admission: glasgow coma scale 3t. left and right anisocoria. CT Angiography (CTA) on admission: subarachnoid hemorrhage with hemoventricular and slight supratentorial ventricular dilation. hypodensity in the trunk. suspicious image for aneurysm at the top of the basilar artery, approximately 1 cm. 01/27: diagnostic arteriography performed, which confirmed the aneurysm at the top of the basilar artery. embolization of the aneurysm was performed, without complications. complications hydrocephalus, hemoventricular using ventriculoperitoneal shunt angiospasm started milrinone ev on 02/08 suspended on 02/23 after reassuring dtc delayed ischemia formation of bilateral hypodense image in thalamus, greater on the right ischemia in pica territory cerebral salt wasting syndrome? polyuria, mild hyponatremia max 132 fludrocortisone between 02/14 and 02/08 epilepsy secondary to bleeding using levotiracetam 1 g 12/12h as secondary prophylaxis without new crises 2 pneumonia associated with mechanical ventilation being treated with polymyxin acinetobacter polymyxin d1 on 03/04 3 CSF infection by pseudomonas resolved 4 cephalic vein thrombophlebitis ultrasound 02/20 without measurements 5 pleural effusion on the right drained on 02/20 380 ml exudate not complicated by Light's criteria 6 tracheostomy on 02/02 03/05 first day off mechanical ventilation currently spends the day with an occluded ostium, without need for supplemental oxygen or signs of respiratory distress 7 bronchoaspiration pneumonia with new orotracheal intubation on 03/15. admission: 01/26/2020, discharge: 04/08/2020, stay: 02 m 13 d 10 h, location: ward, payer: sus. history of hospitalization patient admitted with subarachnoid hemorrhage due to rupture of a basilar artery aneurysm. fisher 4. hunt hess: 5 presented some complications associated with the condition: vasospasm, hic, delayed ischemia of the thalamus and cerebellum, and epilepsy. it was necessary to perform a ventriculoperitoneal shunt. presented as intercurrences, pneumonia associated with mechanical ventilation, treated with polymyxin guided by tracheal aspirate culture with acinetobacter. showed significant improvement in complications, and tracheostomy was performed with subsequent decannulation. at the moment, [person] is clinically stable, but with significant neurological sequelae, requiring intensive care for basic activities of living. He is fed orally, with assisted diet. Pasty consistency and free liquids offered through a straw. No trunk control. Breathes in room air. Has sphincter control. Presents possible picture of cortical blindness. CT scan without possibility of identifying lesion by angioembolization atefacts. Good general condition, alert, little contact, anicteric, acyanotic, afebrile, hydrated, pale /4 respiratory rate: 16 inspirations per minute, symmetrical vesicular breath sounds without adventitious sounds. oxygen saturation: 98%, no signs of respiratory distress cardiac auscultation: 96 beats per minute 2 normal rhythmic heart sounds without murmurs, blood pressure: 130x80 mmhg normotensive abdomen, painless to palpation, bowel sounds absence of slurring in the lower limbs, warm and well perfused extremities glasgow coma scale: 13 ao: 3 rv: 4 rm: 6, slightly anisochoric pupils left right, right and left reactant, [person] mobilizes the 4 limbs on demand but presents hemiparesis on the left. cortical blindness with confabulation. exams: computed tomography of the skull 01/26: subarachnoid hemorrhage with fisher iv hemoventricular. slight supratentorial ventricular dilation. diffuse hypodensity in the pons and in the inferior aspect of the midbrain. Hyperdensity in the posterior aspects of the vitreous cavities, suspicious for bleeding. Rounded formation, measuring approximately 0.9 cm in diameter in the interpeduncular cistern Aneurysm?. Skull CT angiography of cervical vessels 01/26: contrast of a rounded formation, at the top of the basilar artery, with an approximate caliber of 1.0 cm, suspicious for aneurysm. Diagnostic therapeutic arteriography 01/27: Saccular aneurysm, located at the top of the basilar artery, measuring 10.0 x 9.8 mm, with a 5 mm neck, oriented superiorly. Embolization of aneurysm at the top of the basilar artery, with balloon remodeling technique, uneventful. Computerized tomography of the skull 01/27: definite hypodensity in the right thalamus. Questionable hypodensity in the pons and midbrain. Mild hydrocephalus, with bilateral hemoventricle. presence of DVE and embolization artifact. computed tomography of the skull 01/28: better definition of hypodensity in the right thalamus and brainstem. new hypodensity forming in the left thalamus. worsening of hydrocephalus. maintenance of the hemoventricular system. computed tomography of the skull 01/29: better definition of hypodensities in the thalamus, bilaterally larger on the right and brainstem. hydrocephalus, hemoventricular system and brain edema maintained. cranial CT angiography of the cervical vessels 01/29: narrowing of the basilar artery, but without determining severe vasospasm. computed tomography angiography of the skull, cervical vessels 01/29: narrowing of the basilar artery, but without determining severe vasospasm. computed tomography of the skull 3/03: almost total absorption of the pneumocephalus, hydrocephalus with better definition of sulci and slight reduction of cortical edema. computed tomography of the skull 4/03: almost total absorption of the pneumocephalus remains in the right frontal horn, reduction of hydrocephalus and cortical edema with better definition of sulci. malaceous right and left medial thalamic ischemia well defined. control tomography 12/03 comparative evaluation with previous tomography: improvement of hydrocephalus, bilateral thalamic infarctions, infarction in convexity d tcd: 27/01: normal flow velocity. no vasospasm was seen. 28/01: normal flow velocity. no vasospasm was seen. absence of signs of intracranial hypertension. 29/01: CT Angiography (CTA) performed. 30/01: normal flow velocity. no vasospasm was seen. 01/31: no vasospasm was observed in the insomniac vessels. increased acme velocity, but [person] presented fever spikes. 02/03: probable vasospasm in right acp1, but without absolute criteria for this. 02/04: probable vasospasm in right acp1, but without absolute criteria for this. electroencephalogram 01/28: a. marked symmetrical disorganization of the basal activity due to a burst-suppression pattern with synchronous and asynchronous bursts, composed of rhythms of all frequency ranges, with variable duration, between 3 to 6 seconds, with the periods of suppression lasting up to 6 seconds. there is an absence of physiological graphoelements of sleep and of clinical or electroencephalographic reactivity to painful stimuli. conclusion and electroclinical correlation: the electroencephalogram was abnormal. The findings are consistent with the effect of central nervous system depressant drugs, and do not rule out associated encephalopathy. Electroencephalogram 03/14: moderate disorganization of the basal activity: excessive slow theta/delta rhythms during wakefulness. Later wakefulness rhythm with a maximum frequency of 6 Hz, 50 microvolts. The electroencephalogram was abnormal. The findings are consistent with moderate to severe diffuse encephalopathy of a nonspecific nature. Angioembolization tracheostomy. take medications as prescribed: amlodipine 10 mg/day losartan 50 mg/day spironolactone 25 mg/day hydralazine 100 mg 8/8h levetiracetam 1000 mg 12/12h prednisone 5 mg/day wean from 10 mg 5 mg/day on 04/08 maintain weaning from corticosteroids soft diet and liquids through a straw follow-up appointment to [organization] adnv on 10/07 at [organization] follow-up with [person] via cer report issued by physical therapy upon follow-up appointment to the outpatient clinic, evaluate the possibility of performing outpatient nuclear magnetic resonance to delimit a possible occipital lesion. highly alert, communicative conditions. breathing comfortably in room air, with good oxygen saturation. hemodynamically stable, with controlled pressure levels. eating orally, with liquids without thickeners, with good acceptance. last bowel movement on 04/08. spontaneous diuresis. stable renal function. sacral ulcer with chemical debridement papain 6%.
