Admission: 01/26/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 04/08/2020, stay: 02 m 13 d 10 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. History of hospitalization patient admitted with subarachnoid hemorrhage due to rupture of a basilar artery aneurysm. Fisher 4. Hunt hess: 5 presented some complications associated with the condition: vasospasm, hic, delayed ischemia of the thalamus and cerebellum, and seizure disorder (epilepsy). It was necessary to perform a ventriculoperitoneal shunt. Presented as intercurrences, lung infection (pneumonia) associated with machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation), treated with polymyxin guided by tracheal aspirate culture with acinetobacter. Showed significant improvement in complications, and breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) was performed with subsequent decannulation. At the moment, the patient is clinically stable, but with significant neurological sequelae, requiring intensive care for basic activities of living. He is fed by mouth (orally), with assisted diet. Pasty consistency and free liquids offered through a straw. No trunk control. Breathes in room air. Systemic arterial high BP sphincter control. Presents possible picture of cortical blindness. Ct scan without possibility of identifying lesion by angioembolization atefacts. Good general condition, alert, little contact, no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no fever (afebrile), hydrated, pale /4 respiratory rate: 16 inspirations per minute, symmetrical normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds) without adventitious sounds. Oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation): 98%, no signs of respiratory distress cardiac auscultation: 96 beats per minute 2 normal rhythmic heart sounds without murmurs, blood pressure: 130x80 mmhg normotensive abdomen, painless to palpation, bowel sounds absence of slurring in the lower limbs, warm and well perfused extremities glasgow coma scale: 13 ao: 3 rv: 4 rm: 6, slightly anisochoric pupils left right, right and left reactant, the patient mobilizes the 4 limbs on demand but presents hemiparesis on the left. Cortical blindness with confabulation. Exams: CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 01/26: subarachnoid hemorrhage with fisher iv hemoventricular. Slight supratentorial ventricular dilation. Diffuse hypodensity in the pons and in the inferior aspect of the midbrain. Hyperdensity in the posterior aspects of the vitreous cavities, government health schemepicious for bleeding. Rounded formation, measuring approximately 0.9 cm in diameter in the interpeduncular cistern aneurysm?. Skull ct angiography of cervical vessels 01/26: contrast of a rounded formation, at the top of the basilar artery, with an approximate caliber of 1.0 cm, government health schemepicious for aneurysm. Diagnostic therapeutic arteriography 01/27: saccular aneurysm, located at the top of the basilar artery, measuring 10.0 x 9.8 mm, with a 5 mm neck, oriented superiorly. Embolization of aneurysm at the top of the basilar artery, with balloon remodeling technique, uneventful. Computerized tomography of the skull 01/27: definite hypodensity in the right thalamus. Questionable hypodensity in the pons and midbrain. Mild hydrocephalus, with bilateral hemoventricle. Presence of dve and embolization artifact. CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 01/28: better definition of hypodensity in the right thalamus and brainstem. New hypodensity forming in the left thalamus. Worsening of hydrocephalus. Maintenance of the hemoventricular system. CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 01/29: better definition of hypodensities in the thalamus, bilaterally larger on the right and brainstem. Hydrocephalus, hemoventricular system and brain swelling (edema) maintained. Cranial ct angiography of the cervical vessels 01/29: narrowing of the basilar artery, but without determining severe vasospasm. CT scan (computed tomography) angiography of the skull, cervical vessels 01/29: narrowing of the basilar artery, but without determining severe vasospasm. CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 3/03: almost total absorption of the pneumocephalus, hydrocephalus with better definition of sulci and slight reduction of cortical swelling (edema). CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 4/03: almost total absorption of the pneumocephalus remains in the right frontal horn, reduction of hydrocephalus and cortical swelling (edema) with better definition of sulci. Malaceous right and left medial thalamic ischemia well defined. Control tomography 12/03 comparative evaluation with previous tomography: improvement of hydrocephalus, bilateral thalamic infarctions, infarction in convexity d tcd: 27/01: normal flow velocity. No vasospasm was seen. 28/01: normal flow velocity. No vasospasm was seen. Absence of signs of intracranial high BP (hypertension). 29/01: ct angiography (cta) performed. 30/01: normal flow velocity. No vasospasm was seen. 01/31: no vasospasm was observed in the insomniac vessels. Increased acme velocity, but the patient presented fever spikes. 02/03: probable vasospasm in right acp1, but without absolute criteria for this. 02/04: probable vasospasm in right acp1, but without absolute criteria for this. Electroencephalogram 01/28: a. Marked symmetrical disorganization of the basal activity due to a burst-suppression pattern with synchronous and asynchronous bursts, composed of rhythms of all frequency ranges, with variable duration, between 3 to 6 seconds, with the periods of suppression lasting up to 6 seconds. There is an absence of physiological graphoelements of sleep and of clinical or electroencephalographic reactivity to painful stimuli. Conclusion and electroclinical correlation: the electroencephalogram was abnormal. The findings are consistent with the effect of central nervous system depressant drugs, and do not rule out associated brain dysfunction (encephalopathy). Electroencephalogram 03/14: moderate disorganization of the basal activity: excessive slow theta/delta rhythms during wakefulness. Later wakefulness rhythm with a maximum frequency of 6 hz, 50 microvolts. The electroencephalogram was abnormal. The findings are consistent with moderate to severe diffuse brain dysfunction (encephalopathy) of a nonspecific nature. Angioembolization breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy). Take medications as prescribed: amlodipine 10 mg/day losartan 50 mg/day spironolactone 25 mg/day hydralazine 100 mg 8/8h levetiracetam 1000 mg 12/12h prednisone 5 mg/day wean from 10 mg 5 mg/day on 04/08 maintain weaning from corticosteroids soft diet and liquids through a straw return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment to [organization] adnv on 10/07 at [organization] return visit to doctor (follow-up) with the patient via cer report issued by physical therapy upon return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment to the OPD (Out Patient Department), evaluate the possibility of performing outpatient nuclear MRI scan (magnetic resonance) to delimit a possible occipital lesion. Highly alert, communicative conditions. Breathing comfortably in room air, with good oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation). Stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), with controlled pressure levels. Eating by mouth (orally), with liquids without thickeners, with good acceptance. Last bowel movement on 04/08. Spontaneous diuresis. Stable renal function. Sacral ulcer with chemical surgical wound cleaning (debridement) papain 6%. Problem: 1 aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage at the top of the basilar artery fisher 4 hunthess 5 ictus: 01/26 at 04:30 clinical picture: unprecedented crisis tonic posture of the upper limbs lower limbs headache projectile vomiting amaurosis decreased alertness level (level of consciousness). Orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) at [organization] on 01/26 upon admission: glasgow coma scale 3t. Left and right anisocoria. Ct angiography (cta) on admission: subarachnoid hemorrhage with hemoventricular and slight supratentorial ventricular dilation. Hypodensity in the trunk. Government health schemepicious image for aneurysm at the top of the basilar artery, approximately 1 cm. 01/27: diagnostic arteriography performed, which confirmed the aneurysm at the top of the basilar artery. Embolization of the aneurysm was performed, without complications. Complications hydrocephalus, hemoventricular using ventriculoperitoneal shunt angiospasm started milrinone ev on 02/08 government health schemepended on 02/23 after reassuring dtc delayed ischemia formation of bilateral hypodense image in thalamus, greater on the right ischemia in pica territory cerebral salt wasting syndrome? Polyuria, mild hyponatremia max 132 fludrocortisone between 02/14 and 02/08 seizure disorder (epilepsy) secondary to bleeding using levotiracetam 1 g 12/12h as secondary prophylaxis without new crises 2 lung infection (pneumonia) associated with machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation) being treated with polymyxin acinetobacter polymyxin d1 on 03/04 3 csf infection by pseudomonas resolved 4 cephalic vein thrombophlebitis ultrasound 02/20 without measurements 5 fluid around the lungs (pleural effusion) on the right drained on 02/20 380 ml exudate not complicated by light's criteria 6 breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) on 02/02 03/05 first day off machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation) currently spends the day with an occluded ostium, without need for supplemental oxygen or signs of respiratory distress 7 broncholung infection from inhaling food/liquid (aspiration lung infection (pneumonia)) with new orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) on 03/15. 124 1 retropectoral abscess 2 acute kidney injury 3 hydrocele. admission: 10/19/2020, discharge: 10/31/2020, stay: 11 d 11 h, location: ward, payer: sus. [person] attends [organization] on 10/19 complaining of progressive pain in the left chest region associated with local hyperemia of a few days; evolution, with 1 fever peak of 39ºc at the onset of the condition. during the period, he evolved with dyspnea, ventilatory-dependent pain, and jaundice. upon admission to the su, he was tachycardic, tachypneic, with borderline capillary refill time, pain on palpation of the left chest region, and mild local edema. Ceftriaxone and oxacillin were started considering the hypothesis of sepsis of cutaneous focus, confirmed by chest tomography, which showed the presence of an extensive collection in the lateral wall of the chest on the left, in addition to bilateral pulmonary consolidations. The presence of a possible abdominal focus was excluded by diagnostic paracentesis performed on 10/22, without criteria for spontaneous bacterial peritonitis. The collection was addressed by surgery on 10/21, without complications. It presents abundant secretion progressively decreasing in surgical wound. The surgical team followed up together. The presence of Staphylococcus aureus was observed in 02 pairs of blood cultures. A transthoracic echocardiogram was performed, which excluded endocarditis. During hospitalization, [person] presented acute kidney injury kdigo 3. Due to the underlying liver disease, expansion with albumin 1 g/kg was performed for 48 hours for differential diagnosis of hepatorenal syndrome. There was an improvement in renal function, although the baseline creatinine level was not 0.69, and the hypothesis of hepatorenal syndrome was excluded. [person] maintains good urinary output. Hemodynamically stable, eupneic on room air. Abdominal ultrasound 10/19: splenomegaly 21 cm in largest diameter, chronic liver disease follow-up with no evidence of focal lesions, minimal amount of free intra-abdominal fluid in the pelvic region. Soft tissue ultrasound 10/19: collection in the left hemithorax below the pectoralis major muscle, 5.3 x 2.0 x 3.4 cm, with an estimated volume of 18.7 ml, approximately 2 cm from the skin. Hematoma? Inflammatory, infectious process? Chest tomography 10/20 provisional report: heterogeneous collection of poorly defined borders on the left lateral chest wall, whose irregular morphology prevents volume estimation, extending superiorly from the infraclavicular region deep to the pectoralis muscle in its anterior aspect and posteriorly deep to the subscapularis muscle to inferiorly deep to the serratus muscle. Thickness of the collection of up to 2.8 cm. Bilateral pulmonary consolidations and pleural effusion, greater on the left. Surgical drainage of retropectoral hematoma on 10/21. Treatment for staphylococcemia with oxacillin until 11/04, guided by culture. Blood cultures on 10/28 and 10/30 were negative. Secondary prophylaxis for spontaneous bacterial peritonitis for an indefinite period. Surgical wound healing by secondary intention, with good evolution. General surgery recommends washing with soap and water at least twice a day and changing the dressing whenever saturated. maintains serum secretion output, without signs of infection. monitoring of renal function, starting furosemide spironolactone on 10/28 due to signs of hypovolemia and poorly distributed blood volume. non-punctureable ascites. hemodynamically stable, eupneic on room air. 1 retropectoral pus-filled swelling (abscess) 2 acute kidney injury 3 hydrocele. Admission: 10/19/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 10/31/2020, stay: 11 d 11 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. The patient attends [organization] on 10/19 complaining of progressive pain in the left chest region associated with local hyperemia of a few days; evolution, with 1 fever peak of 39ºc at the onset of the condition. During the period, he evolved with difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), ventilatory-dependent pain, and jaundice. Upon admission to the su, he was tachycardic, tachypneic, with borderline capillary refill time, pain on palpation of the left chest region, and mild local swelling (edema). Ceftriaxone and oxacillin were started considering the hypothesis of serious blood infection (sepsis) of cutaneous focus, confirmed by chest tomography, which showed the presence of an extensive collection in the lateral wall of the chest on the left, in addition to bilateral pulmonary consolidations. The presence of a possible abdominal focus was excluded by diagnostic paracentesis performed on 10/22, without criteria for spontaneous bacterial infection inside the abdomen (peritonitis). The collection was addressed by surgery on 10/21, without complications. It presents abundant secretion progressively decreasing in surgical wound. The surgical team followed up together. The presence of staphylococcus aureus was observed in 02 pairs of blood test to find infection (blood culture)s. A transthoracic heart ultrasound (echocardiogram) was performed, which excluded infection of heart valves (endocarditis). During hospitalization, the patient presented acute kidney injury kdigo 3. Due to the underlying liver disease, expansion with albumin 1 g/kg was performed for 48 hours for differential diagnosis of hepatorenal syndrome. There was an improvement in renal function, although the baseline kidney function marker level was not 0.69, and the hypothesis of hepatorenal syndrome was excluded. The patient maintains good urinary output. Stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), breathing normally (eupneic) on room air. Abdominal ultrasound 10/19: splenomegaly 21 cm in largest diameter, chronic liver disease return visit to doctor (follow-up) with no evidence of focal lesions, minimal amount of free intra-abdominal fluid in the pelvic region. Soft tissue ultrasound 10/19: collection in the left hemithorax below the pectoralis major muscle, 5.3 x 2.0 x 3.4 cm, with an estimated volume of 18.7 ml, approximately 2 cm from the skin. Hematoma? Inflammatory, infectious process? Chest tomography 10/20 provisional report: heterogeneous collection of poorly defined borders on the left lateral chest wall, whose irregular morphology prevents volume estimation, extending superiorly from the infraclavicular region deep to the pectoralis muscle in its anterior aspect and posteriorly deep to the subscapularis muscle to inferiorly deep to the serratus muscle. Thickness of the collection of up to 2.8 cm. Bilateral pulmonary consolidations and fluid around the lungs (pleural effusion), greater on the left. Surgical drainage of retropectoral hematoma on 10/21. Treatment for staphylococcemia with oxacillin until 11/04, guided by culture. Blood test to find infection (blood culture)s on 10/28 and 10/30 were negative. Secondary prophylaxis for spontaneous bacterial infection inside the abdomen (peritonitis) for an indefinite period. Surgical wound healing by secondary intention, with good evolution. General surgery recommends washing with soap and water at least twice a day and changing the dressing whenever saturated. Maintains serum secretion output, without signs of infection. Monitoring of renal function, starting furosemide spironolactone on 10/28 due to signs of hypovolemia and poorly distributed blood volume. Non-punctureable fluid buildup in abdomen (ascites). Stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), breathing normally (eupneic) on room air. 1 retropectoral pus-filled swelling (abscess) 2 acute kidney injury 3 hydrocele. Thickness of the collection of up to 2.8 cm. Bilateral pulmonary consolidations and fluid around the lungs (pleural effusion), greater on the left. Surgical drainage of retropectoral hematoma on 10/21. Treatment for staphylococcemia with oxacillin until 11/04, guided by culture. Blood test to find infection (blood culture)s on 10/28 and 10/30 were negative. Secondary prophylaxis for spontaneous bacterial infection inside the abdomen (peritonitis) for an indefinite period. Surgical wound healing by secondary intention, with good evolution. General surgery recommends washing with soap and water at least twice a day and changing the dressing whenever saturated. Maintains serum secretion output, without signs of infection. Monitoring of renal function, starting furosemide spironolactone on 10/28 due to signs of hypovolemia and poorly distributed blood volume. Non-punctureable fluid buildup in abdomen (ascites). 126 id: [person], 50 years old, from {city}, born in {city}, works with recycling, divorced problem: 1 waldenström's macroglobulinemia in disease progression diagnosis 07/01/2020 symptoms at diagnosis: weight loss, pleural effusion, and ascites external gastric biopsy 05/18/2020: small lymphocytes, with a high n/c ratio, with oval nuclei, containing dense chromatin, without visible nucleoli. cd20, bcl2 positivity, and negativity for cd5, cd10, cd23, cyclin d1. ki67 lower than 10%. the complementary immunohistochemical study cd38, cd138, kappa, lambda, and cd56 did not show a monoclonal population of plasma cells in the sample evaluated. gastric btx 05/21/2020: atypical and monomorphic lymphoid proliferation, predominantly of small cells, with scarce cytoplasm and round nuclei with dense chromatin, without evident nucleoli. strong and diffuse positivity for cd20 and bcl2, without staining for cd5, cd10, cd23 and cyclin d1. pleural fluid immunophenotyping 05/12/2020: pleural fluid demonstrates a population of b cells and monoclonal plasma cells. presence of plasma cells with monoclonal immunoglobulin expression characterized by: cd38, cd138, cd45, cd19, cd56, igk, igl, cd27, cd81, cd117. differentiation profile highly related to lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma. Bone marrow biopsy: 2020-06-10: bone marrow focally infiltrated by mature B lymphocyte neoplasm. Echocardiogram 2020-06-16: ejection fraction 56%. Minimal pericardial effusion. Preserved LV segmental mobility. Serologies 2020-10-26: negative. Prophylaxis: viral and bacterial, scheduled up to 3 months after chemotherapy. Last on 03-03-22 Echocardiogram: 0 [person] Initially diagnosed with diffuse small cell B lymphoma in the marginal zone associated with the mucosa. Id: the patient, 50 years old, from the city, born in the city, works with recycling, divorced problem: 1 waldenström's macroglobulinemia in disease progression diagnosis 07/01/2020 symptoms at diagnosis: weight loss, fluid around the lungs (pleural effusion), and fluid buildup in abdomen (ascites) external gastric biopsy 05/18/2020: small lymphocytes, with a high n/c ratio, with oval nuclei, containing dense chromatin, without visible nucleoli. Cd20, bcl2 positivity, and negativity for cd5, cd10, cd23, cyclin d1. Ki67 lower than 10%. The complementary immunohistochemical study cd38, cd138, kappa, lambda, and cd56 did not show a monoclonal population of plasma cells in the sample evaluated. Gastric btx 05/21/2020: atypical and monomorphic lymphoid proliferation, predominantly of small cells, with scarce cytoplasm and round nuclei with dense chromatin, without evident nucleoli. Strong and diffuse positivity for cd20 and bcl2, without staining for cd5, cd10, cd23 and cyclin d1. Pleural fluid immunophenotyping 05/12/2020: pleural fluid demonstrates a population of b cells and monoclonal plasma cells. Presence of plasma cells with monoclonal immunoglobulin expression characterized by: cd38, cd138, cd45, cd19, cd56, igk, igl, cd27, cd81, cd117. Differentiation profile highly related to lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma. Bone marrow biopsy: 2020-06-10: bone marrow focally infiltrated by mature b lymphocyte neoplasm. Heart ultrasound (echocardiogram) 2020-06-16: ejection fraction 56%. Minimal pericardial effusion. Preserved lv segmental mobility. Serologies 2020-10-26: negative. Prophylaxis: viral and bacterial, scheduled up to 3 months after chemotherapy. Last on 03-03-22 heart ultrasound (echocardiogram): 0 the patient initially diagnosed with diffuse small cell b lymphoma in the marginal zone associated with the mucosa. However, during return visit to doctor (follow-up), infiltration of the lymphoma, cavitary effusions, lymphoplasmacytic cells in peripheral blood, monoclonal peak in protein electrophoresis at the expense of igm, and elevated kappa. Discussed in a slide meeting on 01/07/2020 that bone marrow and gastric biopsies present the same pattern, and it is not possible to differentiate by appearance or immunohistochemical markers between marginal zone lymphoma associated with the mucosa and lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma. Considering, however, clinical presentation, lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma/waldenstrom's macroglobulinemia was defined. Previous history of intraparenchymal hemorrhage fracture, requiring bmo by interventional radiology. 128 loss of nephrostomy with local infection 01/28: approach by the nose, start of ceftriaxone 01/28: sudden dyspnea 01/29: start of hemodialysis. 01/29: clinical worsening syncope, vomiting, decreased level of consciousness and hypotension 01/30: shock. 01/31: progressive worsening 01/31: klebsiella pneumoniae carbapenemase in urine: start polymyxin meropenem 01/30 02/02: transfer to intensive care unit 02/02: approach by general surgery, cystostomy without urine output, 02/04: discussed with {person} about comfort measures with [person] from clinical oncology personal history: squamous cell carcinoma of the urinary bladder 09/03, neurogenic overactive bladder secondary to botox meningomyelocele 2015, left renal agenesis, post-operative nephrostomy on the right 02/15/2020, recurrent urinary tract infection and osteomyelitis of the right thigh treated on 07/19. admission: 01/27/2021, discharge: 02/08/2021, stay: 11 d 16 h, location: ward, payer: SUS. Admitted since 01/26 for treatment of nephrostomy repositioning, pus discharge through surgical wound, lower back pain. Evaluated and exchanged for RINT on 01/28. Cultures were collected and antibiotic therapy with ceftriaxone was started. In the afternoon of 01/28, he began with dyspnea, and a significant alteration in renal function with metabolic acidosis was found. On the morning of 01/29, he presented with syncope with postural hypotension and loss of consciousness. On 01/29, he was evaluated by nephrology and hemodialysis was started during this admission. On 01/30, antibiotic therapy was changed to meropenem, but the condition worsened progressively, with vomiting, prostration, tachycardia, and hypotension unresponsive to fluid administration and worsening dyspnea. Klebsiella pneumoniae carbapenemase was identified in the urine and polymyxin was associated with meropenem. He was undergoing daily hemodialysis due to refractory hyperkalemia, requiring increasing doses of vasoactive drugs to maintain target blood pressure. He arrived at the intensive care unit {omitted} accompanied by a medical and nursing team, on spontaneous ventilation with an oxygen catheter, awake, conscious, and oriented, calm, using low-dose vasoactive drugs (norepinephrine) and receiving scheduled antibiotic therapy. Baseline [person] is wheelchair-bound due to myelomeningocele, with a history of neurogenic bladder requiring intermittent bladder catheterization (botox 2015), and has a single kidney with a history of recurrent urinary tract infection. squamous cell carcinoma of the bladder {omitted}, nephrostomy and cystostomy {omitted}, osteomyelitis of the right thigh treated for 3 weeks with meropenem linezolid in 07/19, also had events of cellulitis of the lower limbs and erysipelas. recurrent hematuria. nephrostomy {omitted} and cystotomy {omitted} were indicated. tests were performed and an intravesical vegetative mass was found. ultrasound: oncological follow-up was chosen, which was initiated in {omitted} with a diagnosis of locally invasive urothelial carcinoma of the bladder. underwent chemotherapy and radiotherapy, and had complications of febrile neutropenia. localized metastasis in t12, underwent 6 sessions of radiotherapy. proposed stabilization of the infectious state and reversal of sepsis. intensive care unit discharge was issued. poor general condition, pale. anicteric, sedated. sedation agitation scale 4 glasgow 13 regular heart rhythm in two beats normal heart sounds with systolic murmur decreased vesicular breath sounds at the bases. on noninvasive ventilation mask. respiratory rate: 18 oxygen saturation: 98% somewhat tense abdomen, with cystostomy. and ureterostomy to the right alteration of lower limbs due to atrophy. cultures: 01/28: urine culture klebsiella pneumoniae carbapenemase multir creatinine material: blood entry: 02/06 available: 02/06 released: 02/07 method: jaffe reaction element result reference unit value. 3.70 vn: 0.7 1.6 mg/dl etfg. 19.41 ml/min/1.73 m² the etfg estimate of the glomerular filtration rate is the result of a calculation formula ckdepi obtained from the measurement of serum creatinine, the [person's] age, gender and ethnicity and applies only to individuals 18 years of age or older. although the etfg is only an estimate of the glomerular filtration rate, it provides the clinician with a better assessment of renal function than serum creatinine alone. reference values ​​and staging, according to kdigo 2013, for adults: normal: 90 ml/min/1.73 m² stage g1, slight reduction: 89 60 ml/min/1.73 m² stage g2, slight-moderate reduction: 59 45 ml/min/1.73 m² stage g3a, moderate-severe reduction: 44 30 vn: 150,000 450,000 103/mm3 gb white blood cells. 18.7 vn: 3,500 10,500 103/mm3. nephrostomy repositioning pus discharge from surgical wound low back pain. evaluated and exchanged for rint on 01/28. 01/29: start of hemodialysis. 01/29: clinical worsening syncope, vomiting, decreased level of consciousness and hypotension 02/02: transfer to the intensive care unit 02/02: approach by general surgery cystostomy. proposal for stabilization of the infectious state and reversal of sepsis. evolved with refractory septic shock and respiratory failure. Loss of nephrostomy with local infection 01/28: approach by the nose, start of ceftriaxone 01/28: sudden difficulty in breathing (dyspnea) 01/29: start of kidney dialysis (hemodialysis). 01/29: clinical worsening syncope, vomiting, decreased alertness level (level of consciousness) and low BP (hypotension) 01/30: shock. 01/31: progressive worsening 01/31: klebsiella lung infection (pneumonia)e carbapenemase in urine: start polymyxin meropenem 01/30 02/02: transfer to ICU (serious care room) 02/02: approach by general surgery, cystostomy without urine output, 02/04: discussed with {person} about comfort measures with the patient from clinical oncology personal history: squamous cell carcinoma of the urinary bladder 09/03, neurogenic overactive bladder secondary to botox meningomyelocele 2015, left renal agenesis, post-operative nephrostomy on the right 02/15/2020, recurrent urine infection (UTI) and bone infection (osteomyelitis) of the right thigh treated on 07/19. Admission: 01/27/2021, sent home from hospital (discharge): 02/08/2021, stay: 11 d 16 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme (sus). Admitted since 01/26 for treatment of nephrostomy repositioning, pus sent home from hospital (discharge) through surgical wound, lower back pain. Evaluated and exchanged for rint on 01/28. Cultures were collected and infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) with ceftriaxone was started. In the afternoon of 01/28, he began with difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), and a significant alteration in renal function with metabolic acidosis was found. On the morning of 01/29, he presented with syncope with postural low BP (hypotension) and loss of consciousness. On 01/29, he was evaluated by nephrology and kidney dialysis (hemodialysis) was started during this admission. On 01/30, infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) was changed to meropenem, but the condition worsened progressively, with vomiting, prostration, fast heartbeat (tachycardia), and low BP (hypotension) unresponsive to fluid administration and worsening difficulty in breathing (dyspnea). Klebsiella lung infection (pneumonia)e carbapenemase was identified in the urine and polymyxin was associated with meropenem. He was undergoing daily kidney dialysis (hemodialysis) due to refractory hyperkalemia, requiring increasing doses of vasoactive drugs to maintain target blood pressure. He arrived at the ICU (serious care room) accompanied by a medical and nursing team, on spontaneous ventilation with an oxygen catheter, awake, conscious, and oriented, calm, using low-dose vasoactive drugs (norepinephrine) and receiving scheduled infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy). Baseline the patient is wheelchair-bound due to myelomeningocele, with a history of neurogenic bladder requiring intermittent bladder catheterization (botox 2015), and systemic arterial high BP (hypertension) a single kidney with a history of recurrent urine infection (UTI). Squamous cell carcinoma of the bladder , nephrostomy and cystostomy , bone infection (osteomyelitis) of the right thigh treated for 3 weeks with meropenem linezolid in 07/19, also had events of skin infection (cellulitis) of the lower limbs and erysipelas. Recurrent hematuria. Nephrostomy and cystotomy were indicated. Tests were performed and an intravesical vegetative mass was found. Ultrasound: oncological return visit to doctor (follow-up) was chosen, which was initiated in with a diagnosis of locally invasive urothelial carcinoma of the bladder. Underwent chemotherapy and radiotherapy, and had complications of febrile neutropenia. Localized metastasis in t12, underwent 6 sessions of radiotherapy. Proposed stabilization of the infectious state and reversal of serious blood infection (sepsis). ICU (serious care room) sent home from hospital was issued. Poor general condition, pale. No yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), sedated. Sedation agitation scale 4 glasgow 13 regular heart rhythm in two beats normal heart sounds with systolic murmur decreased normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds) at the bases. On noninvasive ventilation mask. Respiratory rate: 18 oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation): 98% somewhat tense abdomen, with cystostomy. And ureterostomy to the right alteration of lower limbs due to atrophy. Cultures: 01/28: urine culture klebsiella lung infection (pneumonia)e carbapenemase multir kidney function marker (creatinine) material: blood entry: 02/06 available: 02/06 released: 02/07 method: jaffe reaction element result reference unit value. 3.70 vn: 0.7 1.6 mg/dl etfg. 19.41 ml/min/1.73 m² the etfg estimate of the glomerular filtration rate is the result of a calculation formula ckdepi obtained from the measurement of serum kidney function marker (creatinine), the [person's] age, gender and ethnicity and applies only to individuals 18 years of age or older. Although the etfg is only an estimate of the glomerular filtration rate, it provides the clinician with a better assessment of renal function than serum kidney function marker (creatinine) alone. Reference values ​​and staging, according to kdigo 2013, for adults: normal: 90 ml/min/1.73 m² stage g1, slight reduction: 89 60 ml/min/1.73 m² stage g2, slight-moderate reduction: 59 45 ml/min/1.73 m² stage g3a, moderate-severe reduction: 44 30 ml/min/1.73 m² stage g3b, severe reduction: 29 15 ml/min/1.73 m² stage g4, kidney failure (renal failure): <15 ml/min/1.73 m² stage g5. 129 traumatic brain injury while on anticoagulation. admission: 10/26/2020, discharge: 11/01/2020, stay: 05 d 22 h, location: ward, sus. [person] patient with metastatic prostate cancer, undergoing palliative treatment for several years, with stable disease. has multiple recent hospitalizations for decompensated heart failure or infectious conditions. [person] admitted on 10/26/20 due to traumatic brain injury while on full anticoagulation with apixaban. non-contrast cranial computed tomography performed on admission and 24 hours later, showing the presence of subacute infarction in the territory of the acpd with probable hemorrhagic transformation. From a clinical point of view, [person] lucid, conscious and oriented, without focal motor deficits, Glasgow 15, photoreactive isochoric pupils, on admission and throughout hospitalization. We discussed with [person] and [person] the risks and benefits of follow-up appointmenting anticoagulation at this time and chose not to reintroduce it during this hospitalization, risk of increased hemorrhagic transformation seen on computed tomography of the skull and risk of further falls and traumatic brain injury. [person] and [person] understand and agree with the decision. Good general condition, pale 2/4, anicteric, dehydrated 1/4, awake. Respiratory auscultation: universally audible vesicular breath sounds with reduced vesicular breath sounds in the left base, eupneic on room air. Oxygen saturation 95% on room air, no respiratory effort. Cardiac auscultation: irregular heart rhythm, muffled heart sounds, presence of 2/6 systolic murmur in the aortic and tricuspid areas and 1/6 systolic murmur in the mitral area, without radiation. Heart rate: 94 beats per minute, good peripheral capillary perfusion. Abdomen: bowel sounds, globular, flaccid, painless to palpation. Hernia in the right iliac fossa, no phlogistic signs. Ostomy without phlogistic signs. Scar at the site of a fistula on the right flank, no secretion output and no local phlogistic signs. Presence of a large inguinal hernia, urine collected in a bag, clear and with lumps. Lower limbs: pulse present, free calves, no edema. Computerized tomography of the skull on admission and 24 hours after traumatic brain injury. suspended apixaban after discussion with [person] and [person]. suspended apixaban after discussion with [person] and [person]. follow-up appointmented 02/11 at [organization]. follow-up appointmented 02/11 at [organization]. [person] in good general condition, no complaints Traumatic brain injury while on anticoagulation. Ostomy without phlogistic signs. Scar at the site of a fistula on the right flank, no secretion output and no local phlogistic signs. Presence of a large inguinal hernia, urine collected in a bag, clear and with lumps. Lower limbs: pulse present, free calves, no swelling (edema). Computerized tomography of the skull on admission and 24 hours after traumatic brain injury. Government health schemepended apixaban after discussion with the patient and the patient. 130 urinary tract infection. admission: 2020-08-11, discharge: 2020-08-15, stay: 03 d 22 h, location: ward, payer: sus. clinical history collected from [person], [person] and via regulation form. [person] reports that since discharge from this service by {organization} on {omitted}, after starting antiepileptic medications clobazam 10 mg every 12 hours and lmg 25 mg with dose progression up to 200 mg/day, [person] became progressively more sleepy and confused. sought medical attention on 08/08 due to worsening of her general condition, unmeasured fever, diarrhea, and dyspnea. according to [person], clobazam was discontinued upon admission to the emergency care unit, with improvement in mental confusion over the days. With tests collected at the emergency care unit, urinary tract infection was confirmed and ceftriaxone was started on 08/08, with the need to start norepinephrine the following day. Admitted via basic transport without norepinephrine, not monitored with blood pressure 80x50 too much according to physical examination. Immediately referred to the emergency room. Vital signs: saturation 99%, heart rate 88 beats per minute. General: regular general condition, flushed, hydrated, acyanotic, anicteric, afebrile. Neurology: Glasgow 15, slowed speech, without motor or focal deficits. Isochoric and photoreactive pupils. Cardiac auscultation: irregular heart rhythm, in normal S1 and S2, normal sound sounds, good peripheral capillary perfusion, heart rate 84 beats per minute. Difficult auscultation due to adipose tissue. respiratory auscultation: bilateral vesicular breath sounds, no adventitious sounds, no signs of respiratory effort. difficult auscultation, adipose tissue seen. abdomen: apron-shaped, flaccid, peristaltic, painless to palpation, no signs of peritoneal irritation. no masses or visceromegaly palpated. lower limbs: warm and well perfused, capillary refill time 2 s. edema in the left lower limb, /4, no signs of deep vein thrombosis, painless to palpation bilaterally. none. completion of antibiotic therapy by d14. completion of antibiotic therapy by d14, with ceftriaxone, to be carried out at [organization]. referrals for physical therapy, nutrition and psychology. [person] in good general condition, no urinary symptoms or signs of shock. asymptomatic from a urinary point of view, with no decrease in the level of consciousness. Urine infection (UTI). Admission: 2020-08-11, sent home from hospital (discharge): 2020-08-15, stay: 03 d 22 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Clinical history collected from the patient, the patient and via regulation form. The patient reports that since sent home from hospital (discharge) from this service by the hospital on , after starting antiepileptic medications clobazam 10 mg every 12 hours and lmg 25 mg with dose progression up to 200 mg/day, the patient became progressively more sleepy and confused. Sought medical attention on 08/08 due to worsening of her general condition, unmeasured fever, diarrhea, and difficulty in breathing (dyspnea). According to the patient, clobazam was discontinued upon admission to the emergency care unit, with improvement in mental confusion over the days. With tests collected at the emergency care unit, urine infection (UTI) was confirmed and ceftriaxone was started on 08/08, with the need to start norepinephrine the following day. Admitted via basic transport without norepinephrine, not monitored with blood pressure 80x50 too much according to physical examination. Immediately referred to the emergency room. Vital signs: oxygen level in blood (saturation) 99%, heart rate 88 beats per minute. General: regular general condition, flushed, hydrated, no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no fever (afebrile). Neurology: glasgow 15, slowed speech, without motor or focal deficits. Isochoric and photoreactive pupils. Cardiac auscultation: irregular heart rhythm, in normal s1 and s2, normal sound sounds, good peripheral capillary perfusion, heart rate 84 beats per minute. Difficult auscultation due to adipose tissue. Respiratory auscultation: bilateral normal breathing sounds, no adventitious sounds, no signs of respiratory effort. Difficult auscultation, adipose tissue seen. Abdomen: apron-shaped, flaccid, peristaltic, painless to palpation, no signs of peritoneal irritation. No masses or visceromegaly palpated. Lower limbs: warm and well perfused, capillary refill time 2 s. Swelling (edema) in the left lower limb, /4, no signs of deep vein blood clot (thrombosis), painless to palpation bilaterally. None. Completion of infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) by d14. Completion of infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) by d14, with ceftriaxone, to be carried out at [organization]. Referrals for physical therapy, nutrition and psychology. The patient in good general condition, no urinary symptoms or signs of shock. Asymptomatic from a urinary point of view, with no decrease in the alertness level (level of consciousness). Urine infection (UTI): 2020-08-11, sent home from hospital (discharge)d from the hospital ongeneral hospital room (ward)s. The patient's condition is very poor. It's hard to see if the patient has been able to get the treatment. The doctor said that after the surgery, after starting antiepileptic medications clobazam 10 mg every 12 hours and lmg 25 mg with dose progression up to 200 mg/day, the patient became progressively more sleepy and confused. They also found that the patient had a lot of problems with her general condition, unmeasured fever, diarrhea, and difficulty in breathing (dyspnea). According to the doctor, the doctor was discontinued upon admission to the emergency care unit, with improvement in mental health, mental health and physical health. However, the hospital didn't have enough tests to find out whether the patient was able to take the medication. This means that the doctor could be able to make sure the patient is able to manage the infection, and if needed, it can be difficult to take care of the patient. Difficult auscultation due to adipose tissue. Respiratory auscultation: bilateral normal breathing sounds, no adventitious sounds, no signs of respiratory effort. Difficult auscultation, adipose tissue seen. Abdomen: apron-shaped, flaccid, peristaltic, painless to palpation, no signs of peritoneal irritation. No masses or visceromegaly palpated. Lower limbs: warm and well perfused, capillary refill time 2 s. Swelling (edema) in the left lower limb, /4, no signs of deep vein blood clot (thrombosis), painless to palpation bilaterally. None. Asymptomatic from a urinary point of view, with no decrease in the alertness level (level of consciousness). [person] with chronic kidney disease on dialysis currently using a permcath in the left subclavian artery, with a new dialysis scheduled for today, but the procedure was unsuccessful due to the lack of reflux in the component. distension at 45º respiratory auscultation: vesicular breath sounds present, no adventitious sounds, absence of use of accessory respiratory muscles abdomen: flaccid, painless, bowel sounds present 134 1. colon tumor 2. bacterial peritonitis 3. abdominal sepsis 4. refractory septic shock. admission: 04/05/2020, discharge: 04/25/2020, stay: 19 days 16 hours, location: ward, payer: SUS. problem: 1 postoperative abdominal septic shock 04/16: metabolic acidosis decreased level of consciousness need for vasoactive drug antibiotic therapy: tazocin in 04/15 escalated 04/16 meropenem vancomycin due to severity of condition meropenem gentamicin 04/20 culture-guided escalated 04/23 meropenem gentamicin polymyxin vancomycin refractory septic shock 04/24 micafungin fungemia abdominal collection culture 04/17: klebsiella pneumoniae carbapenemase blood culture urine culture 04/20: yeast-like fungus 2 acute kidney injury kdigo 3 nta secondary to abdominal septic shock baseline creatinine 0.4 3 indicated start of renal replacement therapy on 04/19 without tolerance to hemodialysis due to hemodynamic instability. unresponsive to calls and painful stimuli Glasgow coma scale 3 rm 1 rv 1 to 1. [person] presents without central carotid and femoral pulses and peripheral radial, popliteal and pedal pulses, with absence of respiratory incursions, inaudible heart sounds on auscultation, medium-fixed and non-photoreactive pupils, absent doll's eye reflex and absence of corneal-palpebral reflex as well as cough reflex. presence of cold extremities. unresponsive to calls and painful stimuli Glasgow coma scale 3 rm 1 rv 1 to 1. surgeries: postoperative 12/04 extended left colectomy transverse sigmoid manual tt anastomosis bilateral oophorectomy partial omentectomy protective ileostomy. uneventful procedure. findings of a large tumor in the descending colon, with adhesions to the left ovary. small bowel loops, other colonic segments, uterus and abdominal cavity without lesions ureters with double j without lesions bladder without lesions postoperative 04/16: exploratory laparotomy drainage of intracavitary abscess. findings moderate intra-abdominal purulent collection in pelvis minimal dehiscence of the transverse sigmoid anastomosis, with air output during the tire repair maneuver corrected [person] was extubated after surgery, second approach but evolved with lowered level of consciousness and worsening respiratory pattern. metabolic acidosis seen pH 7.13 bicarbonate prescribed, opted for reintubation with need to start vasoactive drug. death. 1. Colon tumor 2. Bacterial infection inside the abdomen (peritonitis) 3. Abdominal serious blood infection (sepsis) 4. Refractory severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock). Admission: 04/05/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 04/25/2020, stay: 19 days 16 hours, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme (sus). Problem: 1 postoperative abdominal severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) 04/16: metabolic acidosis decreased alertness level (level of consciousness) need for vasoactive drug infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy): tazocin in 04/15 escalated 04/16 meropenem vancomycin due to severity of condition meropenem gentamicin 04/20 culture-guided escalated 04/23 meropenem gentamicin polymyxin vancomycin refractory severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) 04/24 micafungin fungemia abdominal collection culture 04/17: klebsiella lung infection (pneumonia)e carbapenemase blood test to find infection (blood culture) urine culture 04/20: yeast-like fungus 2 acute kidney injury kdigo 3 nta secondary to abdominal severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) baseline kidney function marker 0.4 3 indicated start of renal replacement therapy on 04/19 without tolerance to kidney dialysis (hemodialysis) due to hemodynamic instability. Unresponsive to calls and painful stimuli glasgow coma scale 3 rm 1 rv 1 to 1. The patient presents without central carotid and femoral pulses and peripheral radial, popliteal and pedal pulses, with absence of respiratory incursions, inaudible heart sounds on auscultation, medium-fixed and non-photoreactive pupils, absent doll's eye reflex and absence of corneal-palpebral reflex as well as cough reflex. Presence of cold extremities. Unresponsive to calls and painful stimuli glasgow coma scale 3 rm 1 rv 1 to 1. Surgeries: postoperative 12/04 extended left colectomy transverse sigmoid manual tt surgical joining of bowel ends bilateral oophorectomy partial omentectomy protective ileostomy. Uneventful procedure. Findings of a large tumor in the descending colon, with adhesions to the left ovary. Small bowel loops, other colonic segments, uterus and abdominal cavity without lesions ureters with double j without lesions bladder without lesions postoperative 04/16: exploratory laparotomy drainage of intracavitary pus-filled swelling (abscess). Findings moderate intra-abdominal purulent collection in pelvis minimal dehiscence of the transverse sigmoid surgical joining of bowel ends (anastomosis), with air output during the tire repair maneuver corrected the patient was extubated after surgery, second approach but evolved with lowered alertness level (level of consciousness) and worsening respiratory pattern. Metabolic acidosis seen ph 7.13 bicarbonate prescribed, opted for rebreathing tube placed in throat (intubation) with need to start vasoactive drug. Death. 1. Colon tumor 2. Bacterial infection inside the abdomen (peritonitis) 3. Abdominal serious blood infection (sepsis) 4. Refractory severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock). Unresponsive to calls and painful stimuli glasgow coma scale 3 rm 1 rv 1 to 1. The patient presents without central carotid and femoral pulses and peripheral radial, popliteal and pedal pulses, with absence of respiratory incursions, inaudible heart sounds on auscultation, medium-fixed and non-photoreactive pupils, absent doll's eye reflex and absence of corneal-palpebral reflex as well as cough reflex. Presence of cold extremities. Unresponsive to calls and painful stimuli glasgow coma scale 3 rm 1 rv 1 to 1. Surgeries: postoperative 12/04 extended left colectomy transverse sigmoid manual tt surgical joining of bowel ends bilateral oophorectomy partial omentectomy protective ileostomy. Uneventful procedure. Findings of a large tumor in the descending colon, with adhesions to the left ovary. Small bowel loops, other colonic segments, uterus and abdominal cavity without lesions ureters with double j without lesions bladder without lesions postoperative 04/16: exploratory laparotomy drainage of intracavitary pus-filled swelling (abscess). Findings moderate intra-abdominal purulent collection in pelvis minimal dehiscence of the transverse sigmoid surgical joining of bowel ends (anastomosis), with air output during the tire repair maneuver corrected the patient was extubated after surgery, second approach but evolved with lowered alertness level (level of consciousness) and worsening respiratory pattern. 135 pyelonephritis, empyema, evisceration, hypokalemia, septic shock. admission: 04/07/2020, discharge: 05/10/2020, stay: 01 m 03 d 14 h, location: ward, payer: sus. [person], 22 years old, personal history: sequelae of myelomeningocele, spastic tetraparesis, admitted to {organization} for renal abscess and pleural empyema. underwent left nephrectomy for xanthugranulomatous pyelonephritis in the excluded kidney on 04/11/2020 and drainage of pleural empyema on 04/13/2020, drain maintained until 04/16. in outpatient service, started using tazocin on 03/27, which was maintained until 04/16 in our service. Patient with prolonged postoperative evolution, with poor diet acceptance, nausea and vomiting refractory to medication, requiring the insertion of a nasogastric tube. Central venous access was inserted on 04/19 to begin parenteral nutrition. There was worsening of the ventilatory pattern and chest x-ray on 04/19. Meropenem 1 g 8/8h was started on 04/20. Discussion with [person] from [organization] was held if clinical worsening continued, and they advised to associate vancomycin 700 mg 12/12h. The patient evolved with the need for orotracheal intubation. Diagnostic hypothesis: septic shock of pulmonary focus. During hospitalization in the intensive care unit, he used the following antibiotics: piperaccillinatazobactam 03/27 04/16 meropenem 04/20 04/25 vancomycin 04/20 04/27 polymyxin b 25/040 05/6 amikacin 04/25 05/06 He showed improvement in his respiratory pattern and was discharged from the intensive care unit. After transfer to [organization], he progressed well and was discharged on 05/10/2020. Open nephrectomy. Collect tests for follow-up. follow-up appointment in 7 days at the urce. Good. Pyelonephritis, empyema, evisceration, hypokalemia, severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock). staphylococcus aureus oxar, sensitive to vancomycin parent catheter tip 04/17: negative. discussed with [organization] [person] yesterday, who noted clinical improvement in [person], advised the end of antibiotic therapy after 72 hours without fever, with capped tracheostomy and no need for oxygen support, concluded today, thus I discharge the patient from the hospital I prescribe medications previously used for home use gabapentin, venlafaxine, omeprazole and simvastatin I prescribe NPH insulin, according to the current scheme 1010010, in addition to prescribing a glucometer, glucometer strips and insulin syringes, and I advise the correct use of the same outpatient reassessment regarding the need to continue using insulin therapy or follow-up appointment to oral hypoglycemic agents at the family's request, I provide a report of the request for materials for home care of [person] diapers, bath chair and dressing materials I make a referral to CER for follow-up with physical therapy I refer to [organization] for outpatient follow-up of comorbidities I schedule a follow-up appointment visit to the outpatient clinic infectology on 05/21, I request tests for outpatient follow-up. [person] with 2 previous strokes, referred to {organization} due to escape seizure associated with urinary tract infection and poor therapeutic adherence. evolves with septic shock with cutaneous focus on admission due to infected sacral ulcer and acute renal failure requiring dialysis. surgically debrided on a few occasions during admission, without conditions for complete cleaning of infected content due to great depth of wound and associated osteomyelitis. on extended antimicrobial regimen due to osteomyelitis. [person] maintaining a marked decrease in the level of consciousness throughout hospitalization, as well as dependence on mechanical ventilation. palliative care was initiated given the refractory nature of the condition during hospitalization and the reserved prognosis. [person] progressed unfavorably, with death being confirmed at {omitted}. discharged death. Admission: 08/28/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 10/14/2020, stay: 01 m 15 d 11 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. The patient with 2 previous strokes, referred to the hospital due to escape seizure associated with urine infection (UTI) and poor therapeutic adherence. Evolves with severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) with cutaneous focus on admission due to infected sacral ulcer and sudden kidney failure (acute kidney failure (renal failure)) requiring dialysis. Surgically debrided on a few occasions during admission, without conditions for complete cleaning of infected content due to great depth of wound and associated bone infection (osteomyelitis). On extended antimicrobial regimen due to bone infection (osteomyelitis). The patient maintaining a marked decrease in the alertness level (level of consciousness) throughout hospitalization, as well as dependence on machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation). Comfort-focused (not curative) care was initiated given the refractory nature of the condition during hospitalization and the reserved expected outcome (prognosis). The patient progressed unfavorably, with death being confirmed at . Sent home from hospital (discharge)d death. The patient, who had a heart attack, was sent home from hospital (discharge)d from the hospital. The doctor said that the patient was suffering from bone infection (osteomyelitis). The patient had a lot of pain in the hospital due to the seizure associated with urine infection (UTI) and poor therapeutic adherence. It's hard to see if the patient's condition was worse than the patient. The condition of the patient has been worsened due to a few occasions during admissions. However, the patient progressed unfavorably due to great depth of wounds and associated health problems. The hospital's focus was on improving the condition during hospitalization, as well as dependence on machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation). Comfort-focused (not curative) care was initiated to ensure that patients don't get a chance of getting the treatment. This means that patients are more likely to be treated at the hospital, especially after the surgery. This is because of the lack of care for patients who have been treated for the disease. This has led to a surge in the number of patients and the patient staying in hospitalization. This could lead to better treatments for the patient being confirmed at the earliest. This suggests that the hospital should focus on the hospitalization and ensure that patient progresses more care for the treatment, especially if it doesn't work well. Given the importance of treatment, the doctor's advice, the [Other medical details: general hospital room (ward), severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock), sudden kidney failure (acute renal failure), expected outcome (prognosis).] z515. palliative care. n180. end-stage renal disease. a41. other septicaemia. n17. acute renal failure. l89. pressure ulcer. i678. other specified cerebrovascular diseases. i110. hypertensive heart disease with congestive heart failure. e118. non-insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus with complications n. n179. acute renal failure n. e875. hyperkalemia. g410. grand mal status epilepticus. i64. stroke, unspecified as haemorrhagic or ischaemic. g40. epilepsy. death 45.4 1.89
138 [person], 68 years old problem: high-grade glioma brain tumor. tumor in the central nervous system. admission: 09/10/2020, discharge: 10/26/2020, stay: 01 m 16 d 09 h, location: ward, payer: SUS. history of current illness: [person] with a history of having been found unconscious by [person] on 09/08, regaining consciousness when he arrived at the emergency care unit. the next day, [person] had a generalized tonic-clonic seizure witnessed by family members, who were unable to report signs of lateralization. they said that the seizure lasted between 5 and 10 minutes, and that he was drowsy for about 10 minutes afterwards, before having a new event. he was taken to the emergency room again. The transport team reports that [person] did not respond to diazepam, so 20 mg/kg of phenytoin was administered, but [person] still had seizures, which was then supplemented with another 10 mg/kg. Then [person] stopped having seizures, but did not regain consciousness, requiring orotracheal intubation. A computed tomography scan of the skull showed an expansive frontal lesion. The lesion was excised on 09/21/2020. The pathological examination of the tumor was high-grade glioma. He had several infections during hospitalization. used multiple antibiotics ceftriaxone 09/09 09/23, clindamycin 09 09/22, vancomycin 09/30 10/01, tazocin: 10/03 10/07, fluconazole: 09/25 to 10/10, vancomycin 10/09 10/16, polymyxin: 10/07 10/16. due to multiple treatments for multidrug-resistant bacteria, [person] is in contact isolation. in the last urine culture on 10/22, pseudomonas and yeast-like fungi were isolated discussed with the [organization], considered asymptomatic bacteriuria and expectant management, given clinical stability and [person] afebrile. [person] in tracheostomy under macronebulization. without effective awakening. has already undergone electroencephalogram that did not show convulsive status. head tomography does not justify the non-awakening of [person]. [person] in tracheostomy under macronebulization. using phenytoin 50 mg 12/12h, metformin 1000 mg 12/12h, nph insulin 363234, rapid insulin 181818. [person] in metal tracheostomy is careful. has already undergone an electroencephalogram which did not show convulsive status. neurological: glasgow coma scale: 9t m4 o 4 v1 pupils: isochoric and photoreactive. rot: 2 cardiopulmonary resuscitation: indifferent. glasgow coma scale: 14t m6 o 4 v4 pupils: isochoric and photoreactive. rot: 2 cardiopulmonary resuscitation: indifferent. cranio computed tomography frontal lesion. biopsy high-grade glioma. tumor. exeresis of the lesion on 09/21/2020. exeresis. consultation with oncology at [organization] on 11/16/2020, consultation with neurosurgery at the emergency unit on 11/18/2020, [person] needs a backup bed for family training. no further medical issues to be resolved at the emergency unit. consultation with oncology. good general condition. change and antibiotic de-escalation suspended polymyxin tigecycline, meropenem started 09/02: sacral eschar with necrotic cap, chemical debridement started 09/03: new sedation weaning 09/04: vasoactive drug suspended 09/07: electroencephalogram: asymmetric diffuse EDI 09/07: weaning from PHT 09/09: start of weaning from mechanical ventilation 09/13: reassessment of orthopedics due to fracture of the body of T11, maintain movement in block. 10/06: debridement of sacral pressure injury. exams: imaging: computed tomography of the skull 08/20: partial reabsorption of contusions, without new collection that suggests an intracranial infectious process, computed tomography of the skull 09/05: significant reabsorption of contusions and subarachnoid hemorrhage with resolution of General surgery assessment: bilateral chest drainage due to right-sided hemothorax and left-sided air trapped in chest (pneumothorax). Neuroprotection measures initiated. Clavulin was started for prophylaxis due to hemonasinus. 08/14: transferred to ICU (serious care room), oral cavity bleeding, fracture, increased intracranial pressure requiring drug intervention immediately after transfer. Control ct scan of skull. 08/16: small bifrontal hygroma with thicker layer on the left measuring 6 mm. Better definition of right orbitofrontal, left inferior parietal, and bilateral anterior temporopolar contusions. Exams: imaging: CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 08/20: partial reabsorption of contusions, subarachnoid hemorrhage and hemoventricular, however, there is still significant diffuse swelling (edema) and multiple intracranial lesions that favor the unsatisfactory awakening of the patient, even considering the current picture of serious blood infection (sepsis) with a focus to be clarified, which may even correspond to a central focus given the misaligned skull fracture. Examination performed without contrast, however apparently without new collection that suggests an intracranial infectious process, CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 09/05: significant reabsorption of contusions and subarachnoid hemorrhage with resolution of the hemoventricular electroencephalogram: 08/20: compatible with asymmetric diffuse brain dysfunction (encephalopathy), of a marked degree, worse in the left cerebral hemisphere. 09/07: findings consistent with brain dysfunction (encephalopathy) of asymmetric involvement and may occur in the context of post-traumatic brain injury. Chest trauma with multiple bilateral rib fractures, left clavicle fracture, spine, body of t11. Neurosurgery assessment: trepanation indicated for passage of catheter for intracranial pressure monitoring. General surgery assessment: bilateral chest drainage due to right-sided hemothorax and left-sided air trapped in chest (pneumothorax). Neuroprotection measures initiated. Clavulin was started for prophylaxis due to hemonasinus. 08/14: transferred to ICU (serious care room), oral cavity bleeding, fracture, increased intracranial pressure requiring drug intervention immediately after transfer. Control ct scan of skull. 08/16: small bifrontal hygroma with thicker layer on the left measuring 6 mm. Better definition of right orbitofrontal, left inferior parietal, and bilateral anterior temporopolar contusions. Partial reabsorption of traumatic subarachnoid hemorrhage and left nucleocapsular hematoma. Other findings maintained. Centered midline. 08/20: fever plateau, skull bone fracture brain membrane infection (meningitis)? 08/23: circulatory shock with infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) change to meropenem vancomycin 08/23: severe worsening of ventilation, rel paraf < 70 contraindicated for proning due to unstable chest 08/23: transthoracic echocardiography: no pulmonary thromboblood clot blocking a vessel (embolism) 08/24: infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) change to polymyxin tigecycline vancomycin with significant improvement 08/26: shock reversal 08/30: breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) 08/31: sedation government health scheme (sus)pended, return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointmented due to asynchrony and unstable chest 09/01: fever plateau, device change and infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) de-escalation government health scheme (sus)pended polymyxin tigecycline, meropenem started 09/02: sacral eschar with necrotic cap, chemical surgical wound cleaning (debridement) started 09/03: new sedation weaning 09/04: vasoactive drug government health scheme (sus)pended 09/07: electroencephalogram: asymmetric diffuse edi 09/07: weaning from pht 09/09: start of weaning from machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation) 09/13: reassessment of orthopedics due to fracture of the body of t11, maintain movement in block. 10/06: surgical wound cleaning (debridement) of sacral pressure injury. Exams: imaging: CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 08/20: partial reabsorption of contusions, subarachnoid hemorrhage and hemoventricular, however, there is still significant diffuse swelling (edema) and multiple intracranial lesions that favor the unsatisfactory awakening of the patient, even considering the current picture of serious blood infection (sepsis) with a focus to be clarified, which may even correspond to a central focus given the misaligned skull fracture. Examination performed without contrast, however apparently without new collection that suggests an intracranial infectious process, CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 09/05: significant reabsorption of contusions and subarachnoid hemorrhage with resolution of the hemoventricular electroencephalogram: 08/20: compatible with asymmetric diffuse brain dysfunction (encephalopathy), of a marked degree, worse in the left cerebral hemisphere. 09/07: findings consistent with brain dysfunction (encephalopathy) of asymmetric involvement and may occur in the context of post-traumatic brain injury. Cerebrospinal fluid 08/21: 3.3 cell 94 prot 171 glucose negative culture. Surgical wound cleaning (debridement) of pressure injury without complications. Rear bed. Management of pressure injury with action on injuries. Good general condition. Good general condition. n17. acute renal failure. z930. tracheostomy. tracheostomy. j158. other bacterial pneumonias. a419. septicemia ns. s420. fracture of clavicle. fracture of clavicle. r579. shock ns. s21. chest wound. s272. traumatic hemopneumothorax. s06. intracranial trauma. m999. biomechanical injury ns. alive {transfer} 14.7 1.15
140 hemodialysis catheter-related bloodstream infection, with septic shock secondary adrenal insufficiency. admission: 04/22/2020, discharge: 04/25/2020, stay: 02 d 23 h, location: ward, payer: sus. [person] with a history of insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus, 53 years old, recently admitted to {omitted} due to septic shock with an abdominal focus and decreased level of consciousness, which required orotracheal intubation and central nervous system exams with an image suggestive of pituitary macroadenoma. during this same hospitalization he required hemodialysis due to probable acute on chronic kidney disease. After discharge, she was on hemodialysis at {omitted} and on 04/22 she presented an episode of intradialytic hypotension, fever and bacteremia, with the removal of femoral cdl and the initiation of antibiotic therapy with ceftazidime vancomycin. [person] remained refractory to volume expansion, requiring vasoactive drugs for a short time and the initiation of intravenous hydrocortisone due to the hypothesis of secondary adrenal insufficiency. She remained in the ICU for less than 24 hours, progressing well, and was transferred to the ward, where she progressed satisfactorily, with no recurrence of fever and no dialysis emergencies. Cultures were checked with evidence of growth of Pseudomonas aeruginosa multis, with ceftazidime remaining as antibiotic therapy. Furthermore, prednisone was introduced on 04/24 with the aim of weaning her off hydrocortisone, the last dose of which is scheduled to be given tomorrow. Since she is stable, I will proceed with her hospital discharge, and the post-discharge schedule is detailed below. parameters of the last 24 hours systolic blood pressure 120 134 mmHg diastolic blood pressure 64 80 mmHg body temperature afebrile diuresis: not measured physical examination upon discharge good general condition, lucid oriented in time and space, eupneic, afebrile, pale 1/4, hydrated, anicteric, acyanotic, capillary refill time 3 sec, in room air vesicular breath sounds both hemithorax without adventitious sounds regular heart rhythm in two beats muffled heart sounds without murmurs evaluation impaired by breast size protuberant abdomen, flaccid, painless, without masses or visceromegaly lower limbs with slight edema 1/4, flaccid calves. growth of pseudomonas aeruginosa in blood culture. awaiting the results of control blood culture from 24 and 25/04. [person] progressing stably, without recurrence of fever, without signs or symptoms of uremia or refractory hypervolemia despite worsening of azotemia, being in a condition to be discharged from hospital and undergo outpatient follow-up with the schedule below. 1. Due to the growth of gram-negative bacteria in blood culture, I am suspending vancomycin as [person] has a history of poor therapeutic adherence, and he/she should come frequently to the dialysis unit to evaluate kidney damage. I choose to maintain antibiotic therapy with ceftazidime 2 g on the days he/she comes for evaluation. Treatment duration is 10 to 14 days according to clinical evolution. Control blood culture 48 hours after starting antibiotic therapy. Negative to date. 2. I am maintaining prednisone 5 mg/day, and should perform a 50 mg dose of hydrocortisone tomorrow. I will suspend the treatment and maintain only oral corticosteroids. I will follow-up appointment to the endocrinology outpatient clinic on 05/15. 3. [person] with acute kidney injury, DRCA, without signs or symptoms of dialysis urgency, with good diuresis. I have increased the dose of furosemide to 80 mg/day orally, and I advise him/her to seek the nearest emergency room. 4. I will follow-up appointment tomorrow to the acute dialysis unit with tests to collect in the laboratory to evaluate the indication for dialysis. 5. I provide guidance on signs of clinical worsening and the need to immediately seek the nearest emergency room. I provide guidance on diet and fluid intake. 6. As the patient's blood sugar levels are controlled without the use of insulin and [person] does not know the dose of the medication, I continue to use no other medications. I will follow-up appointment to [organization] on 05/15. I will follow-up appointment tomorrow to the acute dialysis unit, with tests to be collected in the laboratory to assess the indication for dialysis. in good general condition, afebrile, stable. The patient remained refractory to volume expansion, requiring vasoactive drugs for a short time and the initiation of given through a vein (drip/IV) hydrocortisone due to the hypothesis of secondary adrenal insufficiency. 141 liver abscess. admission: 03/31/2020, discharge: 04/20/2020, stay: 20 d 10 h, location: ward payer: sus. 1 sepsis: hepatic focus? pulmonary focus? high fever for 7 days, decreased level of consciousness, emesis. reports consumption of spoiled cream, white blood cells 19,900 and external examination with 10% rods. tests: computed tomography of the abdomen suggesting liver abscess 51 ml and bronchopneumonia. antibiotic therapy: ceftriaxone started on 03/29 suspended 04/03/2020 metronidazole 500 mg 8/8 hours on 03/31 pipetazo on 04/03 ceftriaxone follow-up appointment on 04/11 guided by liver abscess culture: klebsiella rint reapproach on 04/17, with drainage of 5 ml of purulent content presented hypotension on 03/29 requiring vasoactive drug, with improvement after 24 h, weaning from [organization]: [person] has been followed up with [person]. reports that on 03/24 she started a fever of 38.5 with large amounts of emesis, abdominal pain. [person] sought care at [organization], where they performed laboratory tests on 03/24: hemoglobin 12.6, hematocrit 37, leukocytes 5,400, rods 10%, platelets 185,000, creatinine 1.05, dengue igm negative, dengue ns1 nonreactive, urine with 8 leukocytes per field, negative nitrite. After being released, she had an episode of postural hypotension and was unconscious for 5-10 minutes, seeking care at [organization], where she performed intravenous hydration. The medical team advised her of the possibility of dengue, prescribed hydration and symptomatic home care with a follow-up appointment on Monday for new tests. On Saturday, she maintained a fever of 40º C and asthenia. She used symptomatic dipyrone and paracetamol, remaining afebrile during that period. On Tuesday 03/28, when she stopped taking the symptomatic care, her fever follow-up appointmented to 39º C, drowsiness, and asthenia. On Wednesday 3/29/20, she arrived at the emergency room with blood pressure of 60x40 mmHg, emesis, fever 40.3, and mental confusion. She was started on hydration, symptomatic medications, and antibiotic therapy with ceftriaxone. On 3/31/20, [person] with no headache, no emesis, no abdominal pain, no asthenia, and no complaints. She went to the {organization} with a referral for a diagnostic hypothesis of meningitis and encephalitis. Observation: [person] reports that [person] used expired cream on 3/18/20 in a stroganoff dish with diarrhea and abdominal pain. Personal history: diabetes mellitus and systemic arterial hypertension. Ultrasound 3/31: heterogeneous area in segments II and III. Post-cholecystectomy scar? abdominal tomography 03/04: expansive hepatic formation in the left lobe, possibly representing an abscess, in the clinical context of infection. mild hepatomegaly. chest tomography 03/04: pulmonary opacities in the left upper lobe suggestive of an infectious, inflammatory process with a bronchopneumonia pattern. small bilateral pleural effusion, larger on the left. cardiomegaly. echocardiogram 05/04: left atrium with dimensions at the upper limit of normal, preserved systolic performance of the ventricles, normal segmental mobility of the ventricles. drainage of hepatic abscess. follow-up appointment visit 02/05. stable, improving. Liver pus-filled swelling (abscess).
