growth after 55h contaminant? urine culture: 09/21: negative 09/28: yeast fungi 09/30: yeast fungi 10/05: negative urine mycology 10/29: candida albicans sensitive to fluconazole 10/02: candida albicans sensitive to fluconazole tracheal aspirate 09/28: negative dialysis catheter tip 10/02: negative central venous catheter tip 10/03: negative 10/14: 15 cfu of acinetobacter multir sacral pressure injury culture: 10/05: klebsiella pneumoniae carbapenemase multir pattern klebsiella pneumoniae carbapenemase renal abscess puncture culture: 10/05: acinetobacter multir pattern klebsiella pneumoniae carbapenemase, history of present illness: case from {organization}. history collected from [person] who accompanied the transport: reports that 1 week ago [person] started complaining of diffuse abdominal pain associated with constipation, sought care where a fleet enema was performed and was discharged. sought care again where he was diagnosed with urinary tract infection and started taking ciprofloxacin. 2 days ago on 9/19, his condition worsened, presenting mental confusion, polydipsia, dyspnea, nausea and worsening abdominal pain. denies polyuria, denies weight loss, denies using new medications. sought emergency care again where the hypothesis of diabetic ketoacidosis was raised for [organization]. [person] also reports that [person] underwent recent routine exams that did not diagnose comorbidities. [person] admitted on 09/21 to the clinical stabilization room, with a Glasgow coma scale of 7, attributed to CAD, sepsis of urinary or cutaneous focus?, orotracheal intubation was performed on admission, vasoactive drugs were started and she was transferred to the intensive care unit [organization] the next day. She developed acute kidney injury kdigo 3 secondary to sepsis? nephrotoxicity?, starting renal replacement therapy on 09/23. Extubated on 09/30. Admitted to the ward of [organization] on 10/02 during the night, she developed a fever and in the afternoon of 10/03 she presented worsening of the respiratory pattern, point of care ultrasound was performed by [person]: no signs of hypervolemia. Possible consolidation on the left. Preserved cardiac function. Case discussed with ICC and transferred to the red room to exclude coronavirus. Personal history: denies comorbidities; denies drug allergies. Medications in use: denies use of medications. Tests: Abdominal ultrasound: verbal report: liver enlarged to the upper limit of normal, gallbladder and bile ducts without signs of obstruction, pancreas without signs of pancreatitis. Chest computed tomography without contrast on 10/05: peripheral pulmonary consolidations, nonspecific. Inflammatory, infectious process? Pulmonary infarction?. Left kidney of increased size and heterogeneous with an image that may correspond to a subcapsular collection. Complicated pyelonephritis?. Skull computed tomography without contrast on 10/05: hyperdense nodular lesion in the foramen of Monroe. Consider the hypothesis of a colloid cyst. CT Angiography (CTA) on 10/06: signs of pulmonary thromboembolism in arteries to the lower segmental branches bilaterally and the left lower lobe. pulmonary consolidations suggestive of infarction, and associated pneumonia could not be ruled out. contrast-enhanced abdominal computed tomography scan on 10/6: enlarged left kidney presenting subcapsular and parenchymal collections compatible with empyema. hospitalization summary [person] admitted due to primary diabetic decompensation (severe diabetic ketoacidosis), requiring hospitalization and orotracheal intubation, in addition to the use of high doses of vasopressors. developed necrosis of the second left toe and left hallux, with a proposal for outpatient amputation by [organization]. presented multiple complications during the current hospitalization, such as acute kidney injury (DKIGO III) requiring renal replacement therapy, with the last hemodialysis session on 09/30. successfully extubated on 09/30, but presenting with critical patient myopathy and dysphonia. presented asymmetric muscle weakness on 10/05, and a new computed tomography scan of the skull was requested, which showed a hyperdense lesion located in the foramen of Monroe, suggestive of a colloid cyst. A magnetic resonance imaging scan of the skull was recommended to complement the investigation, with [person] admitted due to risk of acute non-communicating hydrocephalus. [person] maintained a fever during hospitalization and showed signs of infection, and a computed tomography scan of the abdomen was performed, revealing a left perirenal abscess, and drainage was performed by the [organization] team guided by computed tomography. [person] used meropenem d20, d10, gentamicin d14, fluconazole, with no new signs of infection at the time. presented a finding of subsegmental pulmonary thromboembolism on computed tomography angiography requested due to dyspnea at hospitalization, and is currently undergoing oral anticoagulation with verfarin, with supratherapeutic prothrombin time being adjusted. remaining stable during hospitalization. good general condition, lucid, oriented in time and space, flushed, hydrated, anicteric, acyanotic, afebrile, euvolemic. debridement of pressure injury. post-discharge schedule follow-up with endocrinology cad: new case scheduled at paena for 11/04 I submit a request for outpatient exams I advise [person] regarding the follow-up appointment visit with neurology: electromyography scheduled for 10/29, follow-up with otorhinolaryngology: scheduled follow-up for dysphonia at the lvo outpatient clinic at [organization], on 11/04, I write home prescriptions for insulin therapy, prescription for glucometer and strips for home glycemic control by neurosurgery as instructed by {omitted}: follow-up appointment to [organization] for neurosurgical procedure. updated contacts in [person]'s record. prescription for compression stockings written and delivered to [person] by surgery: debridement of pressure injury and removal of areas of slough, with instructions for home care of the injury as instructed by [person]: I write a report describing MRI findings and risk of acute complications, instruct [person] on neurological warning signs, instruct emergency follow-up appointment with [organization] at [organization] in case of neurological warning signs, programming, walking with walker. Patient admitted for management of diabetic ketoacidosis. Admission: 09/21/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 10/22/2020, stay: 01 m 01 d 12 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Risk of acute hydrocephalus planning of approach by [organization], after decontamination patient colonized with multir germs urine infection (UTI): candida albicans, vaginal candidiasis, admitted with whitish sent home from hospital (discharge) fluconazole discussed with ccih resolved, tetraparesis g2 strength by neurology: myopathy of the critically ill patient improving after withdrawal of corticosteroid and start of motor physiotherapy, renal pus-filled swelling (abscess) 2 collections in left kidney drainage by [organization] on 10/07 culture of material: acinetobacter multir without clinical worsening decided not to cover at the moment in case of new infectious worsening: re-discuss coverage with ccih devices: current: valproic acid previous: tot: 09/21 09/30 indwelling urinary catheter: 09/21 03/10 central venous catheter: right internal jugular vein 09/21 03/10 central venous catheter: left subclavian vein 03/10 14/10 cdl: right femoral 23/09 26/09 cdl: left femoral 26/09 02/10 invasive arterial line: ard 22/09? Infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy): current: none previous: meropenem 1g 8/8h d0 09/22 10/15 fluconazole 400 mg 1x/day d0 10/02 10/16 gentamicin d0 10/03 10/15 ciprofloxacin: d0 09/19 09/20 ceftriaxone 1 g 12/12h d1: 09/20 09/21 vancomycin d0 09/22 10/10 cultures: blood test to find infection (blood culture): 09/21: negative 09/28: negative 10/03: negative 10/05: klebsiella standard klebsiella lung infection (pneumonia)e carbapenemase multir staphylococcus capitis 10/06: one sample with growth after 55h contaminant? Urine culture: 09/21: negative 09/28: yeast fungi 09/30: yeast fungi 10/05: negative urine mycology 10/29: candida albicans sensitive to fluconazole 10/02: candida albicans sensitive to fluconazole tracheal aspirate 09/28: negative dialysis catheter tip 10/02: negative central venous catheter tip 10/03: negative 10/14: 15 cfu of acinetobacter multir sacral pressure injury culture: 10/05: klebsiella lung infection (pneumonia)e carbapenemase multir pattern klebsiella lung infection (pneumonia)e carbapenemase renal pus-filled swelling (abscess) puncture culture: 10/05: acinetobacter multir pattern klebsiella lung infection (pneumonia)e carbapenemase, history of present illness: case from the hospital. History collected from the patient who accompanied the transport: reports that 1 week ago the patient started complaining of diffuse abdominal pain associated with constipation, sought care where a fleet enema was performed and was sent home from hospital (discharge)d. Sought care again where he was diagnosed with urine infection (UTI) and started taking ciprofloxacin. 2 days ago on 9/19, his condition worsened, presenting mental confusion, polydipsia, difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), nausea and worsening abdominal pain. Denies polyuria, denies weight loss, denies using new medications. Sought emergency care again where the hypothesis of diabetic ketoacidosis was raised for [organization]. The patient also reports that the patient underwent recent routine exams that did not diagnose comorbidities. The patient admitted on 09/21 to the clinical stabilization room, with a glasgow coma scale of 7, attributed to cad, serious blood infection (sepsis) of urinary or cutaneous focus?, orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) was performed on admission, vasoactive drugs were started and she was transferred to the ICU (serious care room) [organization] the next day. She developed acute kidney injury kdigo 3 secondary to serious blood infection (sepsis)? Nephrotoxicity?, starting renal replacement therapy on 09/23. Extubated on 09/30. Admitted to the general hospital room (ward) of [organization] on 10/02 during the night, she developed a fever and in the afternoon of 10/03 she presented worsening of the respiratory pattern, point of care ultrasound was performed by the patient: no signs of hypervolemia. Possible consolidation on the left. Preserved cardiac function. Case discussed with heart failure and transferred to the red room to exclude coronavirus. Personal history: denies comorbidities; denies drug allergies. Medications in use: denies use of medications. Tests: abdominal ultrasound: verbal report: liver enlarged to the upper limit of normal, gallbladder and bile ducts without signs of obstruction, pancreas without signs of pancreatitis. Chest CT scan (computed tomography) without contrast on 10/05: peripheral pulmonary consolidations, nonspecific. Inflammatory, infectious process? Pulmonary infarction?. Left kidney of increased size and heterogeneous with an image that may correspond to a subcapsular collection. Complicated pyelonephritis?. Skull CT scan (computed tomography) without contrast on 10/05: hyperdense nodular lesion in the foramen of monroe. Consider the hypothesis of a colloid cyst. Ct angiography (cta) on 10/06: signs of pulmonary thromboblood clot blocking a vessel (embolism) in arteries to the lower segmental branches bilaterally and the left lower lobe. Pulmonary consolidations suggestive of infarction, and associated lung infection (pneumonia) could not be ruled out. Contrast-enhanced abdominal CT scan (computed tomography) scan on 10/6: enlarged left kidney presenting subcapsular and parenchymal collections compatible with empyema. Hospitalization summary the patient admitted due to primary diabetic decompensation (severe diabetic ketoacidosis), requiring hospitalization and orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation), in addition to the use of high doses of medicines to raise blood pressure (vasopressors). Developed tissue death (necrosis) of the second left toe and left hallux, with a proposal for outpatient amputation by [organization]. Presented multiple complications during the current hospitalization, such as acute kidney injury (dkigo iii) requiring renal replacement therapy, with the last kidney dialysis (hemodialysis) session on 09/30. Successfully extubated on 09/30, but presenting with critical patient myopathy and dysphonia. Presented asymmetric muscle weakness on 10/05, and a new CT scan (computed tomography) scan of the skull was requested, which showed a hyperdense lesion located in the foramen of monroe, suggestive of a colloid cyst. A MRI scan (magnetic resonance) imaging scan of the skull was recommended to complement the investigation, with the patient admitted due to risk of acute non-communicating hydrocephalus. The patient maintained a fever during hospitalization and showed signs of infection, and a CT scan (computed tomography) scan of the abdomen was performed, revealing a left perirenal pus-filled swelling (abscess), and drainage was performed by the [organization] team guided by CT scan (computed tomography). The patient used meropenem d20, d10, gentamicin d14, fluconazole, with no new signs of infection at the time. Presented a finding of subsegmental pulmonary thromboblood clot blocking a vessel (embolism) on CT scan (computed tomography) angiography requested due to difficulty in breathing (dyspnea) at hospitalization, and is currently undergoing oral anticoagulation with verfarin, with supratherapeutic prothrombin time being adjusted. Remaining stable during hospitalization. Good general condition, lucid, oriented in time and space, flushed, hydrated, no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no fever (afebrile), euvolemic. Surgical wound cleaning (debridement) of pressure injury. Post-sent home from hospital (discharge) schedule return visit to doctor (follow-up) with endocrinology cad: new case scheduled at paena for 11/04 i submit a request for outpatient exams i advise the patient regarding the return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment visit with neurology: electromyography scheduled for 10/29, return visit to doctor (follow-up) with otorhinolaryngology: scheduled return visit to doctor (follow-up) for dysphonia at the lvo OPD (Out Patient Department) at [organization], on 11/04, i write home prescriptions for insulin therapy, prescription for glucometer and strips for home glycemic control by neurosurgery as instructed by : return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment to [organization] for neurosurgical procedure. Updated contacts in the patient's record. Prescription for compression stockings written and delivered to the patient by surgery: surgical wound cleaning (debridement) of pressure injury and removal of areas of slough, with instructions for home care of the injury as instructed by the patient: i write a report describing mri findings and risk of acute complications, instruct the patient on neurological warning signs, instruct emergency return visit to doctor appointment with [organization] at [organization] in case of neurological warning signs, programming, walking with walker. Patient admitted for a diabetic ketoacidosis. Admission: 09/21/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 10/22/2020. Stay: 01 m 01 d 12 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. As per the rules, the doctor has to visit the hospital. The patient has to pay for treatment. 143 septic shock focus to be clarified polyarthritis to be clarified acute kidney injury kdigo 3 disseminated intravascular coagulation hyperkalemia metabolic acidosis. admission: 07/05/2020, discharge: 07/06/2020, stay: 16 h, location: ward, payer: sus. [person], 41 years old. problem: 1 septic shock focus to be clarified cutaneous? abdominal? 2 polyarthritis etiology to be clarified infectious? immune complex? embolic? synovial fluid arthrocentesis of left knee: 400 cells 58.8 % lymphocytes 3 acute kidney injury kdigo 3 sec sepsis? external cr 04/07/20: 0.7 cr admission: 3.15 4 disseminated intravascular coagulation isth: 7 history of current illness: [person] referred from [organization] in vacancy zero today 05/07, due to arthritis in the left wrist associated with oliguria and hyperbilirubinemia at the expense of direct. [organization] reports that [person] had been admitted 1 day ago with arthritis in the left wrist, progressing to oliguria and edema of the right wrist, knees and ankles. had choluria and hyperbilirubinemia at the expense of direct. at the service of origin, ceftriaxone was administered and an uncertain report of starting clindamycin. admitted to this service at approximately {omitted}, presenting with tachydyspnea, saturation 96% on non-rebreathing mask 12 l/min, with mild respiratory distress and auscultation, with hypotension, mean arterial pressure ranging from 60 to 65, tachycardia, heart rate 145, somewhat confused and slightly agitated, [address]. reports that 7 days ago he began to present with pain, edema and redness in the left wrist, which evolved with edema and less significant pain in the knees, ankles and right wrist. he also began to present oliguria and choluria a few days after being seen at the service of origin. he denied fever, urinary symptoms (denied dysuria, pollakiuria or hematuria), respiratory symptoms (denied cough, expectoration, dyspnea or abdominal pain throughout the evolution). denies change in bowel habits or skin lesions. personal history: necrosis of the head of the right femur in 2018 according to information in the medical records. denies other comorbidities. Previous surgeries: ulcerorrhaphy in this service in 2019, due to perforated ulcer. Habits: history of substance use disorder, reports being abstinent for some years. Reports recent unprotected sex, has no steady partner. Medications of continuous use: denies. Hemodynamically stable due to high doses of norepinephrine and vasopressin, febrile tachycardic, maximum temperature 39.5 °, poorly adapted to mechanical ventilation, requiring neuromuscular blocker for better coupling. No measured diuresis. at {omitted} min I found [person] in asystole in bed, without pulse. Cardiac arrest in asystole. 6 cycles of cardiopulmonary resuscitation. Infused 2 ampoules of epinephrine. Infused 250 ml to correct metabolic acidosis. Infused 20 ml of empirical calcium gluconate. Before the result of the k dosage, there was reversal of cardiorespiratory arrest. Electrocardiogram tracing with prolonged QRS. Infused 20 ml of calcium gluconate. Total arrest time: 12 min. at {omitted} New cardiorespiratory arrest in asystole: 1 cycle of cardiopulmonary resuscitation was performed with follow-up appointment of spontaneous circulation. Total arrest time: 2 min. at {omitted} New cardiorespiratory arrest in asystole: It was decided not to perform cardiopulmonary resuscitation due to refractoriness. exams: gsv 08:00: ph 6.839, pco2 80, po2 26.3, hco3 13.3, k 8.6, na 133. continuous infusion drugs: nor: 1 mcg/kg/min, vas: 0.04 u/min, mdz 2 ml/h, fnt 2 ml/h, cis 4.5 ml/h. sedation agitation scale 5. asystole, inaudible blood pressure without adventitious sounds, innocent abdomen, limbs in passive warming. [person] pulseless with inaudible blood pressure on examination: [person] in reactive and apperceptive coma, Glasgow scale 3. mydriatic and reactive pupils, inaudible blood pressure in four limbs, I cannot palpate central and peripheral pulses, I cannot auscultate heart sounds in the precordium. [person] pulseless with inaudible blood pressure on examination: [person] in a reactive and apperceptive coma glasgow coma scale 3 mydriatic and reactive pupils inaudible blood pressure in all four limbs I cannot palpate central or peripheral pulses I cannot auscultate heart sounds in the precordium. death certificate {omitted}. Severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) focus to be clarified polyarthritis to be clarified acute kidney injury kdigo 3 disseminated intravascular coagulation hyperkalemia metabolic acidosis. Admitted to this service at approximately , presenting with tachydifficulty in breathing (dyspnea), oxygen level in blood (saturation) 96% on non-rebreathing mask 12 l/min, with mild respiratory distress and auscultation, with low BP (hypotension), mean arterial pressure ranging from 60 to 65, fast heartbeat (tachycardia), heart rate 145, somewhat confused and slightly agitated, [address]. Reports that 7 days ago he began to present with pain, swelling (edema) and redness in the left wrist, which evolved with swelling (edema) and less significant pain in the knees, ankles and right wrist. He also began to present oliguria and choluria a few days after being seen at the service of origin. He denied fever, urinary symptoms (denied dysuria, pollakiuria or hematuria), respiratory symptoms (denied cough, expectoration, difficulty in breathing (dyspnea) or abdominal pain throughout the evolution). Denies change in bowel habits or skin lesions. Personal history: tissue death (necrosis) of the head of the right femur in 2018 according to information in the medical records. Denies other comorbidities. Previous surgeries: ulcerorrhaphy in this service in 2019, due to perforated ulcer. Habits: history of substance use disorder, reports being abstinent for some years. No measured diuresis. At min i found the patient in asystole in bed, without pulse. Heart stopped beating (cardiac arrest) in asystole. 6 cycles of cardiopulmonary regovernment health scheme (sus)citation. Infused 2 ampoules of epinephrine. Infused 250 ml to correct metabolic acidosis. Infused 20 ml of empirical calcium gluconate. Before the result of the k dosage, there was reversal of cardiorespiratory arrest. ECG/heart tracing (electrocardiogram) tracing with prolonged qrs. Infused 20 ml of calcium gluconate. Total arrest time: 12 min. At new cardiorespiratory arrest in asystole: 1 cycle of cardiopulmonary regovernment health scheme (sus)citation was performed with return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment of spontaneous circulation. Total arrest time: 2 min. At new cardiorespiratory arrest in asystole: it was decided not to perform cardiopulmonary regovernment health scheme (sus)citation due to refractoriness. 144 sepsis of urinary focus. admission: 03/03/2020, discharge: 03/13/2020, stay: 09 d 14 h, location: ward, payer: sus. discharge report: sepsis of urinary focus personal history: prostate carcinoma undergoing chemotherapy with clinical oncology 4 4 t3bn1m1 bone metastasis zoladex weekly street june/2019 intermittent indwelling urinary catheter since 01/20 in the service: patient admitted stable, with reduced urinary output one day after admission. indwelling urinary catheter changed with output of 1.5 l of hematuric urine. ceftriaxone started 03/03. on 03/04 he presented septic shock requiring vasoactive drug, with admission to the intensive care unit from 03/04/2020 to 03/10/2020 vasoactive drug turned off on 03/08, with transition from antibiotic therapy to meropenem. he evolved well, being discharged to the ward on 03/10, he finished the 7 days of antibiotic therapy with meropenem, without complications, with improvement in clinical and laboratory parameters and maintenance of clear and abundant diuresis through an indwelling urinary catheter. bladder. cultures: blood culture 04/03: klebsiella pneumoniae esbl sensitive to amikacin, imipenem, meropenem and ertapenem urine culture 24/02: escherichia coli multis 04/03: escherichia coli esbl sensitive to meropenem additional tests: ultrasound 04/03: test within normal parameters absence of pyelocaliceal dilation or images suggestive of stones. ureters not visible. empty bladder with indwelling urinary catheter cuff. maintain indwelling urinary catheter every 21 days. follow-up appointment to the urology outpatient clinic [organization] 10/05. asymptomatic, with no changes in physical examination. Findings: presence of intense adhesion between the lung parenchyma and chest wall in the lung apex region with presence of clots retained in this region, presence of a small, moderate amount of gelatinous secretion in the posterior region of the thoracic cavity. 3 severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) with a focus to be clarified pulmonary? 12/18 4 refeeding syndrome 5 pulmonary and pleural infection by non-TB (tuberculosis) mycobacterium possible pulmonary TB (tuberculosis), awaiting culture d0 ripe clarithromycin 12/29. 6 severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) of pulmonary focus. 12/28 breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) aspirate 12/18: {klebsiella lung infection (pneumonia)}, multiresistant pseudomonas torgena polymyxin d0 12/28. 7 hemodynamic instability intermediate blood marker for infection (procalcitonin), severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) of focus to be clarified bloodstream infection associated with device? 12/01 vancomycin d0 12/01 central venous catheter changed on 15/01 infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy): current: torgena in 28/12 polymyxin b in 28/12 ripe in 29/12 clarithromycin d0 28/12 vancomycin d0 12/01 micafungin in 15/01 previous: meropenem vancomycin d0 18/12 28/12 invasive devices: current: invasive arterial line 12/01 right internal jugular vein 15/01 indwelling urinary catheter 15/01 gtt 07/01 previous: central venous catheter right subclavian vein 16/12 15/01 indwelling urinary catheter 17/12 15/01 culture: blood test to find infection (blood culture) 28/11: negative urine culture 28/11: negative pleural fluid culture 11/29: negative blood test to find infection (blood culture) 12/15: negative pleural lavage 12/16: staphylococcus haemolyticus tracheal aspirate 12/18: pseudomonas aeruginosa multir sensitive to torgena, klebsiella lung infection (pneumonia)e multir, urine culture 12/18: negative blood test to find infection (blood culture) 12/19: negative blood test to find infection (blood culture) 01/13: negative urine culture in progress 01/13: negative tracheal aspirate in progress 01/13: negative blood test to find infection (blood culture) in progress 01/15: urine culture collected 01/15: catheter tip culture collected 01/15: collected. History of present illness referred from [organization] with report of abdominal wound, admitted in severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock), abdominal focus, pulmonary focus. Started on tazocin, performed chest drainage. Awaiting clinical compensation for surgical correction. Personal history: laryngeal neoplasia, operated in 2004 in the city, supraglottic laryngectomy, preserved ventricular and arytenoid bands, absence of epiglottis. Adjuvant radiotherapy in 2004, esophageal substenosis after radiotherapy: last dilation aug/13 hypothyroidism after radiotherapy: puran t4 100 mcg/day. Postoperative 08/17/13 of breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) due to ira rebreathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) in jul/19 due to lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) government health scheme (sus)pected of second primary tumor of the cervical esophagus with laryngeal invasion? 2 negative biopsies for malignancy postoperative 2019 of total laryngectomy due to dysfunctional larynx pathological anatomy: high-grade dysplasia in the epiglottis. No signs of neoplasia, developed coronavirus during hospitalization, with good recovery, presented pharyngocutaneous fistula, with an admission of approximately 3 x 3 cm from the pharynx to the skin and need for a portex cannula due to secretion, post-procedure stenosis? Return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment of food through the nose during speech evaluation, new digestive endoscopy requested. Last upper digestive endoscopy in december 2020: fibrotic stenosis with a diameter of approximately 0.6 cm. Presence of friable ulcers in the same topography, the largest with 2 cm in diameter. Biopsy performed. Scar stenosis after laryngectomy in the main esophagus. Pathological anatomy: esophageal mucosa with focus of ulceration covered by granulation tissue, without signs of malignancy or specificity. Postoperative procedure exploratory laparotomy with lysis of adhesions creation of gastrostomy 04/14. Mesenteric lymph node biopsy: inflammation. No signs of malignancy. Admission: 11/28/2021, sent home from hospital (discharge): 01/19/2022, stay: 01 m 21 d 18 h, location: general hospital room (ward) payer: government health scheme. Sent home from hospital (discharge) death the patient without palpable central pulses, straight line on the monitor, absence of respiratory movements, non-reactive pupils, without corneal-palpebral reflex. 147 coronavirus acute respiratory distress syndrome. admission: 08/08/2020, discharge: 09/16/2020, stay: 01 m 08 d 02 h, location: ward, payer: sus. id: [person], 50 years old. anthropometry: reported weight: 120 kg. problem: 1 coronavirus onset of symptoms: 07/28 worsening of symptoms dyspnea: 08/07 positive external rtpcr 08/06 orotracheal intubation 08/09 2 acute kidney injury kdigo iii prerenal component resolved 3 shock to be clarified resolved septic with hypovolemic component? Urine culture in three samples with Escherichia coli Esbl Meropenem started on 08/23 Tracheal aspirate with Acinetobacter baumannii, but with improved ventilation and x-ray without signs of pneumonic process Thick secretion Tracheitis? Polymyxin B started Vancomycin started empirically due to hemodynamic instability on 08/23, fever improved and procalcitonin of 0.6 Antibiotic therapy suspended 4 Edema Paste Hematoma in the right upper limb Ultrasound 08/24: collection on the medial side of the right arm, with discreet internal flow on Doppler study, pseudoaneurysm cannot be excluded. Ultrasound 08/25 Small collection on the medial side of the left arm, with stable dimensions and no flow on color Doppler study. Hematoma on the medial side of the left arm stable. Ultrasound 09/09: small collection on the medial side of the left arm, with stable dimensions, but with adjacent inflammatory signs through the vascular: no indication for surgical approach for hematoma evacuation at the moment, given signs of regression topical use of local hirudoid in the area of ​​ecchymosis, hematoma without contraindication to the follow-up appointment of anticoagulation hematoma in regression, stable hemoglobin 5 new renal lesion kdigo II prerenal? intrinsic due to rhabdomyolysis? also resolved, personal history: history of thrombophilia to be clarified that due to multiple episodes of previous deep vein thrombosis and an episode of pulmonary embolism, perennial anticoagulation was indicated since 06/17. investigation for saaf was negative. use of warfarin, with extreme values ​​with the same dose, being questioned about poor adherence. 8 deep vein thrombosis in the right leg and 1 deep vein thrombosis in the left lower limb. episode of pulmonary thromboembolism after appendectomy. 2 abortions 23 years ago, right saphenectomy 14 years ago, left saphenectomy 12 years ago. Varicose veins, chronic venous insufficiency, gestational hypothyroidism investigated, resolved after pregnancy, migraine, has already had adenotonsillectomy, appendectomy, tubal ligation, allergies: penicillin, pineapple and fragrances. lifestyle habits: denies smoking or alcoholism continuous medications: warfarin half a tablet Monday, Wednesday, Friday, one tablet more days antibiotic therapy: current: no previous use: ceftriaxone 08/07 service of origin tigecycline 08/21 08/24 meropenem 08/24 08/30 vancomycin 08/24 08/25 polymyxin 08/21 09/02 devices: current: no previous use: tot 8.5 08/09 08/26 peripherally inserted central catheter right upper limb 08/19 08/21 invasive arterial line pediosa d 08/16 08/24 indwelling urinary catheter 08/17 08/24 indwelling urinary catheter 08/24 08/29 central venous catheter right internal jugular vein 08/09 08/19 central venous catheter vein left internal jugular 21/08? invasive arterial line afd 24 08/31 central venous catheter right internal jugular vein ev 1000 08/24 08/31 central venous catheter left internal jugular vein ev 1000 08/31 09/13 nasoenteral tube cultures: blood culture: 08/15: collected 08/19: 1 sample staphylococcus capitis oxa r 08/24: 1 sample with staphylococcus epidermidis oxa r 09/01: negative 09/06: negative urine culture: 08/15, 08/18, and 08/20: escherichia coli esbl 08/24: negative 09/06 klebsiella pneumoniae 50,000 colonies tracheal aspirate: 08/16: acinetobacter 08/24: staphylococcus aureus oxas catheter culture: 09/01: negative 09/13: negative mycology 09/07: negative right upper limb collection culture: 09/10: negative corticosteroid therapy: hydrocortisone suspended day? methylprednisolone 125mg/day in 08/0823/08 dexamethasone 08/07 service of origin first dose astrazeneca vaccine in may, [person] was admitted due to severe acute respiratory syndrome due to coronavirus 2 on 08/08/2020, required orotracheal intubation and had complications with nosocomial infections already treated. In addition, there was suspected pseudoaneurysm formation, significant hematomas in the right upper limb with regression and evaluation of vascular surgery, without additional procedures. She had worsening renal function with follow-up appointment to baseline. Currently, eupneic in room air, without fever. She only needs adjustment of oral anticoagulation, due to thrombophilia of undetermined etiology, with indication of perennial anticoagulation, which is why she will be transferred to {organization}. Eupneic in room air. RT PCR for coronavirus 2 positive. Schedule: regarding the infectious condition [person] developed episodic fever, but normal leukocytes, stable PCR, procalcitonin 0.6 borderline value. Therefore, it was decided to suspend polymyxin on 09/02. After suspension, he has been presenting subfebrile symptoms with slight improvement and a falling CRP of 0.4. Afebrile since 09/10. Urine culture on 09/06 with Klebsiella pneumoniae, but only 50,000 colonies and urinalysis from the previous day normal, suggesting only colonization. Regarding the hematoma of the right upper limb with apparent reduction, awaiting the report of the ultrasound of the right upper limb on 09/09, but, according to the provisional report, there is no description of pseudoaneurysm and there is a reduction in the collection. Rint punctured the collection on 09/10: negative regarding anticoagulation: [person] with hematoma in regression, stable hemoglobin and absence of pseudoaneurysm in the last ultrasound. Full anticoagulation with enoxaparin 100 mg 12/12h was started on 09/13, I prescribe Marevan 5 mg. assess throughout the week reintroduction and oral anticoagulation as previously used: 0.5 tablet Monday, Wednesday, Friday and 1 tablet Tuesday, Thursday, Saturday, Sunday. assessed by hematology with guidance to refer [person] to [organization] where he was previously followed up other Czech culture of central catheter tip: negative [person] with itchy genital lesion improving in the diaper region, suggestive of diaper dermatitis with a fungal component, I maintain topical hydrocortisone and cotoconazole. discontinue fluconazole 300 mg/day from 09/13 to 09/15 [person] in the post-coronavirus rehabilitation process, requiring full anticoagulation due to a history of thrombophilia with no defined diagnosis and with a hematoma in the right upper limb in the process of regression. started full anticoagulation on 09/13 with no new evidence of bleeding. already with optimized oral medications, without invasive accesses and with the only pending adjustment of the international normalized ratio. I request a vacancy for transfer to {organizaton}. {omitted}. discharge schedule follow-up appointment [organization] on 10/14 follow-up appointment speech therapy 10/11 anticoagulation follow-up in {organization} upon follow-up appointment with [organization] evaluate follow-up appointment with physiotherapy. eupneic in room air, afebrile. Coronavirus acute respiratory distress syndrome. Intrinsic due to rhabdomyolysis? Also resolved, personal history: history of thrombophilia to be clarified that due to multiple episodes of previous deep vein blood clot (thrombosis) and an episode of pulmonary blood clot blocking a vessel (embolism), perennial anticoagulation was indicated since 06/17. [person] admitted to [organization] on 09/07 after having started feeling unwell, nausea, and reduced appetite 10 days ago. sought emergency care on 09/13, where he was evaluated and diagnosed with an infected diabetic foot lesion. He remained hospitalized during the week receiving ampicillin and metronidazole. On Saturday, his general condition and respiratory pattern worsened, along with mental confusion. On Sunday, he continued with orotracheal intubation, initiation of vasoactive drugs, and escalation of antibiotic therapy to ceftriaxone and vancomycin. [person] also developed oliguria and significant worsening of renal function, in addition to hyperkalemia. According to the companion, a tomography scan of the chest and skull was performed at the origin, without alterations. [person] was admitted to [organization] on mechanical ventilation via orotracheal tube, hemodynamically stable with norepinephrine 35 ml/h. [person] was sedated with midazolam, fentanyl, and blocked at the origin. Collection bag with 25 ml of clear urine. At [organization], he was diagnosed with septic shock of pulmonary focus. It was decided to perform an infrapatellar amputation on the left lower limb and the right hallux on the same day, in addition to antibiotic therapy with meropenem. The procedure was said to be uneventful. Upon admission, acute kidney injury kdigo 3 was also evidenced, requiring renal replacement therapy. [person] had been evolving stably until the onset of hematuria on 09/26. An indwelling urinary catheter was performed, with persistent hematuria and oliguria after catheter removal, requiring a repeat catheterization on 09/27. On 09/28, [person] presented a new episode of shock, with a probable mixed hypovolemic component after hemodialysis, septic from a urinary and cutaneous focus, and antibiotic therapy was escalated to meropenem and vanciomycin. From then on, he was refractory to the measures, with progressive worsening of the infectious condition, without recovery of renal function, with progressive worsening of the level of consciousness and general condition. family meeting held with the care team to clarify the clinical picture, measures instituted and refractoriness to such measures, {omitted}. [person] died on {omitted}. left infrapatellar amputation [person] on the right 07/09. death. The procedure was said to be uneventful. Upon admission, acute kidney injury kdigo 3 was also evidenced, requiring renal replacement therapy. The patient had been evolving stably until the onset of hematuria on 09/26. On 09/28, the patient presented a new episode of shock, with a probable mixed hypovolemic component after kidney dialysis (hemodialysis), septic from a urinary and cutaneous focus, and infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) was escalated to meropenem and vanciomycin. From then on, he was refractory to the measures, with progressive worsening of the infectious condition, without recovery of renal function, with progressive worsening of the alertness level (level of consciousness) and general condition. 149 1. febrile neutropenia with pulmonary focus 2. dengue group d acute hepatitis ns1 positive dengue encephalitis 3. herpes encephalitis ulcers in the oral cavity changes in the level of consciousness suggestive of encephalitis 4. cutaneous abscess in the right upper limb approached by orthopedics on 02/27/2020 vancomycin 02/27/2020 03/05/2020 5. nosocomial pneumonia radiological worsening respiratory distress fever hypotension during hemodialysis. admission: 02/08/2020, discharge: 03/17/2020, stay: 01 m 09 d 13 h, location: ward, payer: sus. [person] referred from the emergency care unit in {city} in usb, accompanied by [person]. presenting with a fever of 39 °C for 5 days throughout the day without recurrence, associated with cough with expectoration, without signs of respiratory distress, subjective complaints impaired by cognitive deficit and loss of appetite. In the dialysis service, oral antibiotic therapy was started without improvement. He sought emergency care on 02/06 due to persistent symptoms, and was administered ceftriaxone 2 g intravenously and regulated for this service. {omitted}. Antibiotic therapy with clavulin and azithromycin was started. [person] was admitted conscious and oriented, initially hemodynamically stable without vasoactive drugs, eupneic saturation 92% with oxygen catheter at 3 l/min, without spontaneous complaints. He denied dyspnea, cough, expectoration, chest or abdominal pain. Afebrile and presenting with usual diuresis and evacuation without pathological products. During hospitalization, [person] became unstable and needed to start norepinephrine 0.12 mcg/kg/min. General tests were requested and [person] was neutropenic. A chest tomography showed posterior consolidations and she was considered to have septic shock with a pulmonary focus and febrile neutropenia. Antibiotic therapy with cefepime and vancomycin was started due to a dialysis catheter. In addition, she started with abdominal pain 1 day ago and was diagnosed with acute hepatitis, and among the possible causes, she had a positive igm for epstein bar virus, and then ganciclovir was started, discussed with hemato. However, due to the history of fever for 5 days and loss of appetite, ns1 was requested, which came back positive. Therefore, dengue was considered the cause of the acute hepatitis and ganciclovir was suspended, discussed with [person]. She was transferred to [organization] on 02/08/2020, and the next day, she developed ulcers in the oral cavity, in addition to psychomotor agitation, with herpetic encephalopathy being suspected and acyclovir being started. Due to persistent oral cavity bleeding and decreased level of consciousness, she was intubated on 02/10/2020. She continued to have oral cavity bleeding and thrombocytopenia. The orotracheal tube was replaced due to the presence of a mucus plug on 02/18/2020. A tracheotomy was performed on 02/20/2020 due to failed extubation with Tobin 200 and cleaning of the nasal and oral cavity with a hemostatic tampon with gelfuan in the nasal cavity and peritracheostomy. On 02/22, she restarted medications to control systemic arterial hypertension and heart failure with enalapril and bisoprolol, with improved ventilatory weaning and she was able to remain on macro since 02/25/2020 8 hours. After admission to a bed in the semi-intensive care unit of [organization], [person] presented a fever which was considered secondary to an abscess in the right upper limb. It was decided to start empirical treatment with vancomycin. The abscess was drained by the orthopedics team on 2/27/2020, with good progress and discharge by the team on 3/4/2020. On 2/28/2020, [person] presented fever and hypotension during the hemodialysis session. Contacted [person] from [organization] and decided to start meropenem for coverage of the pulmonary focus due to the appearance of a new radiopacity in the right hemithorax associated with respiratory distress. On 03/02/2020, [person] presented new fever spikes, associated with clinical worsening and new consolidation in the right hemithorax. Tracheal aspirate culture checked showing meropenem-resistant acinetobacter, discussed with [organization] and then decided to start polymyxin B and gentamicin. The next day, he maintained fever spike and blood culture showed growth of gran-negative bacillus in 8 h acinetobacter, requested the nephrology team to remove permcath. Two days later, a new dialysis catheter was inserted in the right femoral vein on 03/05 without complications. During her stay in semi-intensive care, [person] presented episodes of epistaxis and otorrhea on the right, which were evaluated by the otorhinolaryngologist and showed good progress. [person] was transferred to the [organization] ward on 3/10, where she remained well, under frequent monitoring with speech therapy, progression of diet to oral route, physical therapy, weaning from bipap, tolerating macronebulization well, and managing to keep the tracheostomy occluded since 3/11. The following day, with the help of the head and neck surgery team, the portex was exchanged for a metal cannula, also without complications. She progressed well, accepting an oral diet but still with a nasoenteral tube. She remained on dialysis with intermittent renal replacement therapy, every 2 days, without complications during the sessions. The last session was held in the early hours of 3/17, and she was fit for hospital discharge after finishing 14 days of gentamicin and polymyxin B. laboratory results on 03/17: hb: 7.2 ht: 22% 4,400 leukocytes 106,000 platelets bicarbonate: 27.4 ph: 7.44 creatinine: 2.14 urea: 35. tracheostomy 02/20 drainage of abscess in right upper limb 02/27. procedures: maintenance of hemodialysis as prior to hospitalization, at hospital [address]: Mondays, Wednesdays and Fridays follow-up with speech therapy and evaluation of the possibility of removing the nasoenteral tube follow-up appointment to the outpatient clinics of: pre-transplant nephrology: 03/19/2020, total parenteral nutrition, hematology 03/19/2020, adult epilepsy neurology: 06/01/2020, cardiology pericardiopathies and cardiomyopathies: 08/19/2020, prescriptions at discharge 1 topiramate 50 mg 12/12h 2 cyclobenzaprine 5 mg/night 3 gabapentin 300 mg/d 4 ​​zinc gluconate alternate days 5 folic acid complex b alternate days 6 ascorbic acid 5 gts/night 7 alfapoietin 4000 3x/week 8 sevelamer 2 2 2 9 sodium bicarbonate 2 caps/d 10 bisoprolol 5 mg 1x/d 11 enalapril 5mg 12/12h on alternate days 12 danazol 200mg 1x/d 13 doxazosin 2 mg/night 14 levothyroxine 25 mcg/d. with nasoenteral tube, metal tracheostomy, in the process of transitioning to an oral diet. 1. The procedures for surgery are like surgery. It's important to see if kidney dialysis (hemodialysis) is done in right limb. The surgery is done at hospital [address]: mondays, wednesdays and fridays. Pre-transplant nephrology: 03/19/2020, total parenteral nutrition, hematology, and cardiomyopathies: 08/19,2020, prescriptions at sent home from hospital (discharge) 1 topiramate 50 mg 12/12h 2 cyclobenzaprine 5 mg /night 3 gabapentin 300 mg/d 4 zinc gluconate alternate days 5 folic acid complexes 6 ascorbic acid 5 gts/night 7 alfapoietin 3x/week 8 sevelamer 2 2 9 sodium bicarbonate 2 caps/d 10 bisoprolol 3x4 mg 1x/d 11 enalapril 5mg/mcg/dr.mc [Other medical details: kidney function marker (creatinine).] n939. abnormal bleeding from uterus or vagina, ne. d539. nutritional anemia ne. h660. acute suppurative otitis media. h921. otorrhea. j159. bacterial pneumonia ne. a91. hemorrhagic fever due to dengue virus. a419. septicemia ne. j960. acute respiratory failure. n18. chronic renal failure. n19. renal failure ne. d469. myelodysplastic syndrome, ne. z315. genetic counseling. d619. aplastic anemia ne. i509. heart failure ne. j180. bronchopneumonia ne. z992. renal dialysis dependence. alive 37.3 1.28
150 admission: 05/27/2021, discharge: 06/03/2021, stay: 07 d 07 h, location: ward, payer: sus. case summary: id: [person], female, 82 years old, housewife, from [postal code] problem: this is a patient admitted due to an episode of unresponsiveness and right foot clonus on 05/27, at 6:45 pm, preceded by worsening of long-standing dizziness and headache. initially transported to the emergency care unit, evolving with partial recovery of the level of consciousness, according to {person}, without presenting dimidiated deficits and dysarthria, but maintaining tonic gaze deviation to the left. admitted to [organization] with a stroke in the Glasgow coma scale window 6 t ao1 / rv 1t / rm 4, using nipride 5 ml / h and midazolam. Upon admission, anisocoria was described and the patient was normal, with 183,000 platelets, dextrose 178, and blood pressure 150/90 mmHg. Upon admission, computed tomography of the skull showed aspect 10, with no signs of early ischemia, with CT Angiography (CTA) showing occlusion at the origin of the left vertebral column, with reinhabitation in distal V2, without other stenoses or occlusions. It was decided not to thrombolyze due to the clinical picture not suggestive of ischemic stroke and no images that corroborated this diagnosis. Diagnostic hypothesis of convulsive crisis, with a decrease in the level of consciousness in the postictal period, evolving with the need for orotracheal intubation. In the initial infectious-metabolic screening, a urinary tract infection was found and treatment with ceftriaxone was initiated. The patient evolved with rapid recovery of the level of consciousness with weaning from sedation and was extubated, without complications, on 05/29. During hospitalization, the patient presented an episode of deep vein thrombosis in the right upper limb. Treatment was started with enoxaparin 0.75 mg/kg/day, which subsequently evolved to cellulitis in the right upper limb. Treatment was started with oxacillin. He had an episode of multifocal atrial tachycardia that was reversed with amiodarone. The following hypotheses led to hospitalization and discharge: 1. Seizure with decreased level of consciousness in the postictal period. Circumstantial seizure secondary to infection? Epilepsy? Hypertensive encephalopathy? Second episode of seizure, the first in 2017, secondary to hypertensive encephalopathy, without previous use of AEDs. Outpatient treatment. 2. Stroke? TIA? Discarded and referred as a stroke in the window, with no image forming on control tomography and an inconsistent clinical picture. 3. urinary tract infection, cellulitis in the right upper limb ceftriaxone 05/28 06/02 oxacillin 05/30 06/02 amoxicillin clavulanate d0 06/02 4. deep vein thrombosis in the right upper limb on 05/28 used enoxaparin 0.75 mg/kg/day transitioned to rivaroxaban 15mg/day 5. multifocal atrial tachycardia on 05/28 reversed after amiodarone. documented in electrocardiogram in the file advised by cardiologist to maintain use of amiodarone and rivaroxaban. personal history: depression, stroke in 2018 report of discharge without sequelae sic chronic kidney disease stage iv ckdepi 27 ml/min/1.73 m2 05/23/19 tachyarrhythmia for 3 years heart rate: 19normal S1 and S2 per minute with 3 electrical cardioversions? systemic arterial hypertension 40 years old hypothyroidism antitype osteoporosis rankin 2 for orthopedic reasons transtrochanteric fracture of the right femur consolidated and fixed with synthesis material with satisfactory bone alignment. 06/07 prosthesis reapproach in 07/17 with right hip prosthesis. medications in use: sertraline 50 mg 200 atenolol 25 mg 100 omeprazole 20 mg 100 acetylsalicylic acid 100 mg 010 calcium carbonate 500 mg d3 400 iu: 1 tablet 12x12h atorvastatin 40mg/day levothyroxine 50 mcg 100 sodium alendronate 70 mg: 1 tablet/week hospitalization exams: computed tomography of the skull 05/26: microangiopathy fazekas 3 discrete diffuse atrophy bilateral parenchymal calcifications CT Angiography (CTA) 05/26: occlusion at the origin of the left vertebral with reinhabitation in distal v2. no other stenoses or occlusions computed tomography control 05/27: no hypodensity in formation computed tomography chest 05/27: signs of incipient fibrosing lung disease, ectasia of the ascending aorta. 05/27 electroencephalogram nonspecific diffuse encephalopathy examination performed under sedation ultrasound right upper limb 05/28: focal venous thrombosis of the distal third of one of the brachial veins and focal thrombophlebitis of the cephalic vein lumbar puncture 05/28: clear and colorless: 1.3 cells / 450 red blood cells / 35 protein / glucose 99 / chloride 114 / negative VDRL. Stroke protocol: VDRL neg/ Chagas NR/ HBA1C 5.35/ CT 137/ TRIG 111/ HDL 45/ LDL 69 Transthoracic echocardiography: Left ventricular ejection fraction 66%, AE 105 ml, indexed 66. Discharge schedule: recovered from medical records a previous episode of circumstantial seizure secondary to hypertensive encephalopathy. Considering this, therefore, a second episode of circumstantial seizure secondary to urinary tract infection? New hypertensive encephalopathy? [person] admitted using nipride 5 ml/h, I prescribe lamotrigine for home use due to the interaction of phenytoin with rivaroxaban, which will need to be used. We continue using phenytoin 100 mg 12/12h until the dose of lamotrigine is increased. I request a new outpatient electroencephalogram. Discussed with cardiologist about anticoagulation and heart rate control: [person] presented on monitor, with confirmation by electrocardiogram documented in file, an episode of multifocal atrial tachycardia, reversed with amiodarone. There is an indication to continue the use of amiodarone and anticoagulation, since it is not possible to rule out episodes of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation and the patient presents significant atrial dilation indexed at 66. I prescribe amiodarone 200 mg 12/12h for 3 weeks and then 200 mg/day. Discussed with ICC: I chose to continue treatment for urinary tract infection and cellulitis of the right upper limb with oral amoxicillin clavulanate. I prescribe for use until completing a total of 10 days of antibiotics. I reintroduce hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg/day. Current CLCR of 46 HD of ischemic stroke. Anticoagulation with rivaroxaban. I spoke with [person] who accepted treatment, dose adjusted for renal function 15 mg/day indicated due to deep vein thrombosis in the right upper limb and by cardio. I discontinue acetylsalicylic acid. keep following up on [organization], [organization] and [organization] follow-up appointment on acln 06/24. Admission: 05/27/2021, sent home from hospital (discharge): 06/03/2021, stay: 07 d 07 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Case summary: id: the patient, female, 82 years old, housewife, from [postal code] problem: this is a patient admitted due to an episode of unresponsiveness and right foot clonus on 05/27, at 6:45 pm, preceded by worsening of long-standing dizziness and headache. Initially transported to the emergency care unit, evolving with partial recovery of the alertness level (level of consciousness), according to {person}, without presenting dimidiated deficits and dysarthria, but maintaining tonic gaze deviation to the left. Admitted to [organization] with a stroke in the glasgow coma scale window 6 t ao1 / rv 1t / rm 4, using nipride 5 ml / h and midazolam. Upon admission, anisocoria was described and the patient was normal, with 183,000 platelets, dextrose 178, and blood pressure 150/90 mmhg. Upon admission, CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull showed aspect 10, with no signs of early ischemia, with ct angiography (cta) showing occlusion at the origin of the left vertebral column, with reinhabitation in distal v2, without other stenoses or occlusions. It was decided not to thrombolyze due to the clinical picture not suggestive of reduced blood supply (ischemic) stroke and no images that corroborated this diagnosis. Diagnostic hypothesis of convulsive crisis, with a decrease in the alertness level (level of consciousness) in the postictal period, evolving with the need for orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation). In the initial infectious-metabolic screening, a urine infection (UTI) was found and treatment with ceftriaxone was initiated. The patient evolved with rapid recovery of the alertness level (level of consciousness) with weaning from sedation and was extubated, without complications, on 05/29. During hospitalization, the patient presented an episode of deep vein blood clot (thrombosis) in the right upper limb. Treatment was started with enoxaparin 0.75 mg/kg/day, which subsequently evolved to skin infection (cellulitis) in the right upper limb. Treatment was started with oxacillin. He had an episode of multifocal atrial fast heartbeat (tachycardia) that was reversed with amiodarone. The following hypotheses led to hospitalization and sent home from hospital (discharge): 1. Seizure with decreased alertness level (level of consciousness) in the postictal period. Circumstantial seizure secondary to infection? Seizure disorder (epilepsy)? Hypertensive brain dysfunction (encephalopathy)? Second episode of seizure, the first in 2017, secondary to hypertensive brain dysfunction (encephalopathy), without previous use of aeds. Outpatient treatment. 2. Stroke? Tia? Discarded and referred as a stroke in the window, with no image forming on control tomography and an inconsistent clinical picture. 3. Urine infection (UTI), skin infection (cellulitis) in the right upper limb ceftriaxone 05/28 06/02 oxacillin 05/30 06/02 amoxicillin clavulanate d0 06/02 4. Deep vein blood clot (thrombosis) in the right upper limb on 05/28 used enoxaparin 0.75 mg/kg/day transitioned to rivaroxaban 15mg/day 5. Multifocal atrial fast heartbeat (tachycardia) on 05/28 reversed after amiodarone. Documented in ECG/heart tracing (electrocardiogram) in the file advised by cardiologist to maintain use of amiodarone and rivaroxaban. Personal history: depression, stroke in 2018 report of sent home from hospital (discharge) without sequelae sic chronic kidney problem (kidney disease) stage iv ckdepi 27 ml/min/1.73 m2 05/23/19 tachyarrhythmia for 3 years heart rate: 19normal s1 and s2 per minute with 3 electrical cardioversions? Systemic arterial high BP (hypertension) 40 years old hypothyroidism antitype osteoporosis rankin 2 for orthopedic reasons transtrochanteric fracture of the right femur consolidated and fixed with synthesis material with satisfactory bone alignment. 06/07 prosthesis reapproach in 07/17 with right intraparenchymal hemorrhage prosthesis. Medications in use: sertraline 50 mg 200 atenolol 25 mg 100 omeprazole 20 mg 100 acetylsalicylic acid 100 mg 010 calcium carbonate 500 mg d3 400 iu: 1 tablet 12x12h atorvastatin 40mg/day levothyroxine 50 mcg 100 sodium alendronate 70 mg: 1 tablet/week hospitalization exams: CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 05/26: microangiopathy fazekas 3 discrete diffuse atrophy bilateral parenchymal calcifications ct angiography (cta) 05/26: occlusion at the origin of the left vertebral with reinhabitation in distal v2. No other stenoses or occlusions CT scan (computed tomography) control 05/27: no hypodensity in formation CT scan (computed tomography) chest 05/27: signs of incipient fibrosing lung disease, ectasia of the ascending aorta. 05/27 electroencephalogram nonspecific diffuse brain dysfunction examination performed under sedation ultrasound right upper limb 05/28: focal venous blood clot (thrombosis) of the distal third of one of the brachial veins and focal thrombophlebitis of the cephalic vein lumbar puncture 05/28: clear and colorless: 1.3 cells / 450 red blood cells / 35 protein / glucose 99 / chloride 114 / negative vdrl. Stroke protocol: vdrl neg/ chagas nr/ hba1c 5.35/ ct 137/ trig 111/ hdl 45/ ldl 69 transthoracic echocardiography: left ventricular ejection fraction 66%, ae 105 ml, indexed 66. Sent home from hospital (discharge) schedule: recovered from medical records a previous episode of circumstantial seizure secondary to hypertensive brain dysfunction (encephalopathy). Considering this, therefore, a second episode of circumstantial seizure secondary to urine infection (UTI)? New hypertensive brain dysfunction (encephalopathy)? The patient admitted using nipride 5 ml/h, i prescribe lamotrigine for home use due to the interaction of phenytoin with rivaroxaban, which will need to be used. We continue using phenytoin 100 mg 12/12h until the dose of lamotrigine is increased. I request a new outpatient electroencephalogram. Discussed with cardiologist about anticoagulation and heart rate control: the patient presented on monitor, with confirmation by ECG/heart tracing (electrocardiogram) documented in file, an episode of multifocal atrial fast heartbeat, reversed with amiodarone. There is an indication to continue the use of amiodarone and anticoagulation, since it is not possible to rule out episodes of paroxysmal irregular heartbeat (atrial fibrillation) and the patient presents significant atrial dilation indexed at 66. I prescribe amiodarone 200 mg 12/12h for 3 weeks and then 200 mg/day. Discussed with heart failure: i chose to continue treatment for urine infection (UTI) and skin infection (cellulitis) of the right upper limb with oral amoxicillin clavulanate. I prescribe for use until completing a total of 10 days of infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic)s. I reintroduce hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg/day. Current clcr of 46 hd of reduced blood supply (ischemic) stroke. Anticoagulation with rivaroxaban. I spoke with the patient who accepted treatment, dose adjusted for renal function 15 mg/day indicated due to deep vein blood clot (thrombosis) in the right upper limb and by cardio. I discontinue acetylsalicylic acid. Keep following up on [organization], [organization] and [organization] return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment on acln 06/24. symptoms: 08/06 cough worsening of symptoms: 08/14 confirmatory rtpcr: 08/17 on 08/17 paraf 59 on 08/17 under 15 l reservoir mask orotracheal intubation 08/17 08/23 2 associated bacterial pneumonia, treated with meropenem 08/17 08/28 3 venous thromboembolism of solear veins in left lower limb 08/26 start of full anticoagulation 4 acute respiratory failure, pulmonary thromboembolism low embolic load 08/27 need for new orotracheal intubation 08/27 09/03 tracheostomy 09/03 current need for proning position 08/28 at 02:15 am CT Angiography (CTA) 08/27 with pulmonary opacities with an infectious, inflammatory aspect, pulmonary thromboembolism with low embolic load on the left, without signs of cor pulmonale. emphysema. small sliding gastric hiatal hernia. already using full anticoagulation with enoxa since 08/26, seen chest computed tomography with involvement greater than 50%, mosaic pattern, started on methylpred 2mg/kg on 08/27, prophylaxis with nitazoxanide 08/28 09/02, transthoracic echocardiography: ejection fraction 71%, cardiac chambers with normal dimensions, systolic performance and segmental mobility of the ventricles normal. 5 ventilator-associated pneumonia with acinetobacter in tracheal aspirate started with amikacin 09/03 started with meropenem 09/07 suspended 09/11 associated with colistin on 09/11 associated with vancomycin on 09/16 6 hypoxemia, hypercapnia refractory to mechanical ventilation 7 septic shock refractory to vasoactive amines 8 palliative care, received: levofloxacin: 08/06 08/12 ceftriaxone: 08/16 azithromycin: 08/16 08/17 meropenem d0 08/17 08/28 09/07 09/11 amikacin: 09/03 current colistin: 09/11 current dexamethasone: 08/17 08/27 vancomycin: 09/16 methylprednisolone: ​​08/27 current weaning methylprednisolone reintroduced at a therapeutic dose on 08/17/20, for treatment of pulmonary fibrosis. history: diabetes mellitus reports allergy to amoxicillin denies recent hospitalizations denies alcoholism ex-smoker stopped 25 years ago med previous continuous use of metformin 1 1 1 case summary registered by: [person] on 09/24/2020 evolution: nocturnal medical evolution intensive care center {organization} covid 4, patient with multiple organ and system dysfunction, under clinical refractoriness. patient under analgesia and sedation with ketamine and midazolam, agitation sedation scale 5, under continuous curarization with cisatracurium. ventilated synchronously in vcac afebrile for the last 14 h. using norepinephrine at 0.9 mcg/kg/min and vasopressin at 2 u/h to maintain mean arterial pressure of 65 mmHg. mild sinus cardiac output now, heart rate 98 beats per minute. had diuresis of 560 ml/12h with positive BH of 31. fasting due to shock. blood glucose levels fluctuated from 180 to 404 mg/dl. had bowel movements once today. [person] on high doses of norepinephrine and vasopressin progressed to refractory shock and death. absence of vital signs. confirmed death at {omitted}. discharged from hospital. The patient, 62 years old, born in the city, from [address] weight: 74 kg height: 153 cm body mass index: 31.61 kg/m2 predicted weight 46 kg, 1 acute respiratory syndrome secondary to coronavirus, onset of symptoms: 08/06 cough worsening of symptoms: 08/14 confirmatory rtpcr: 08/17 on 08/17 paraf 59 on 08/17 under 15 l reservoir mask orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) 08/17 08/23 2 associated bacterial lung infection (pneumonia), treated with meropenem 08/17 08/28 3 venous thromboblood clot blocking a vessel (embolism) of solear veins in left lower limb 08/26 start of full anticoagulation 4 acute lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure), pulmonary thromboblood clot blocking a vessel (embolism) low embolic load 08/27 need for new orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) 08/27 09/03 breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) 09/03 current need for proning position 08/28 at 02:15 am ct angiography (cta) 08/27 with pulmonary opacities with an infectious, inflammatory aspect, pulmonary thromboblood clot blocking a vessel (embolism) with low embolic load on the left, without signs of cor pulmonale. Emphysema. Small sliding gastric hiatal hernia. Already using full anticoagulation with enoxa since 08/26, seen chest CT scan (computed tomography) with involvement greater than 50%, mosaic pattern, started on methylpred 2mg/kg on 08/27, prophylaxis with nitazoxanide 08/28 09/02, transthoracic echocardiography: ejection fraction 71%, cardiac chambers with normal dimensions, systolic performance and segmental mobility of the ventricles normal. 5 ventilator-associated lung infection (pneumonia) with acinetobacter in tracheal aspirate started with amikacin 09/03 started with meropenem 09/07 government health schemepended 09/11 associated with colistin on 09/11 associated with vancomycin on 09/16 6 hypoxemia, hypercapnia refractory to machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation) 7 severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) refractory to vasoactive amines 8 comfort-focused (not curative) care, received: levofloxacin: 08/06 08/12 ceftriaxone: 08/16 azithromycin: 08/16 08/17 meropenem d0 08/17 08/28 09/07 09/11 amikacin: 09/03 current colistin: 09/11 current dexamethasone: 08/17 08/27 vancomycin: 09/16 methylprednisolone: ​​08/27 current weaning methylprednisolone reintroduced at a therapeutic dose on 08/17/20, for treatment of pulmonary fibrosis. Patient under analgesia and sedation with ketamine and midazolam, agitation sedation scale 5, under continuous curarization with cisatracurium. Ventilated synchronously in vcac no fever (afebrile) for the last 14 h. Using norepinephrine at 0.9 mcg/kg/min and vasopressin at 2 u/h to maintain mean arterial pressure of 65 mmhg. Mild sinus cardiac output now, heart rate 98 beats per minute. Had diuresis of 560 ml/12h with positive bh of 31. Fasting due to shock. Blood glucose levels fluctuated from 180 to 404 mg/dl. Had bowel movements once today. The patient on high doses of norepinephrine and vasopressin progressed to refractory shock and death. Absence of vital signs. Confirmed death at . Sent home from hospital (discharge)d from hospital. The patient, who was born in the city, had a sickness, and had a heart attack. The patient's body mass index: 31.61 kg/m2 predicted weight 46 kg, 1 acute respiratory syndrome secondary to coronavirus, and the symptoms: 08/17.11 p.m. The doctor said that it's a good idea to see if the patient had a cough worsening orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation), but it didn't work well with the patient. He said that the patient was more likely to have a heart disease, and he had a problem with the disease. He also said that he had no symptoms of the disease and had no problems with it. He added that the condition of the patient might be worsening if he had an infectious disease, but he had to be careful about it. However, the doctor's findings showed that the disease had a serious complication with the virus, and it could be a serious problem for people with pulmonary thromboblood clot blocking a vessel (embolism). The study suggests that it should be done in left lower limb 08/26 start of full anticoagulation to prevent heart failure, as well as heart failures. The report says that it might be a good way to deal with the problem with it, but there's no need for new treatments to be Already using full anticoagulation with enoxa since 08/26, seen chest CT scan (computed tomography) with involvement greater than 50%, mosaic pattern, started on methylpred 2mg/kg on 08/27, prophylaxis with nitazoxanide 08/28 09/02, transthoracic echocardiography: ejection fraction 71%, cardiac chambers with normal dimensions, systolic performance and segmental mobility of the ventricles normal. 5 ventilator-associated lung infection (pneumonia) with acinetobacter in tracheal aspirate started with amikacin 09/03 started with meropenem 09/07 government health schemepended 09/11 associated with colistin on 09/11 associated with vancomycin on 09/16 6 hypoxemia, hypercapnia refractory to machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation) 7 severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) refractory to vasoactive amines 8 comfort-focused (not curative) care, received: levofloxacin: 08/06 08/12 ceftriaxone: 08/16 azithromycin: 08/16 08/17 meropenem d0 08/17 08/28 09/07 09/11 amikacin: 09/03 current colistin: 09/11 current dexamethasone: 08/17 08/27 vancomycin: 09/16 methylprednisolone: ​​08/27 current weaning methylprednisolone reintroduced at a therapeutic dose on 08/17/20, for treatment of pulmonary fibrosis. History: sugar disease (diabetes) reports allergy to amoxicillin denies recent hospitalizations denies alcoholism ex-smoker stopped 25 years ago med previous continuous use of metformin 1 1 1 case summary registered by: the patient on 09/24/2020 evolution: nocturnal medical evolution intensive care center the hospital coronavirus 4, patient with multiple organ and system dysfunction, under clinical refractoriness. Patient under analgesia and sedation with ketamine and midazolam, agitation sedation scale 5, under continuous curarization with cisatracurium. Ventilated synchronously in vcac no fever (afebrile) for the last 14 h. Using norepinephrine at 0.9 mcg/kg/min and vasopressin at 2 u/h to maintain mean arterial pressure of 65 mmhg. Mild sinus cardiac output now, heart rate 98 beats per minute. Had diuresis of 560 ml/12h with positive bh of 31. Fasting due to shock. Blood glucose levels fluctuated from 180 to 404 mg/dl. Had bowel movements once today. The patient on high doses of norepinephrine and vasopressin progressed to refractory shock and death. Absence of vital signs. Confirmed death at . Sent home from hospital (discharge)d from hospital. [Other medical details: general hospital room (ward), breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy).] n17. acute renal failure. i828. embolism and thrombosis of other specified veins. k590. constipation. a419. septicemia ne. j189. pneumonia ne. j80. adult respiratory distress syndrome. r579. shock ne. u071. novel coronavirus infection. b342. coronavirus infection, ne. e119. uncomplicated non-insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus death 37.5 1.22
