153 admission: 2021-06-26, discharge: 2021-07-10, stay: 13 d 23 h, location: ward, payer: sus. [person], 39 years old, from [address] patient with primary mediastinal non-Hodgkin's lymphoma diagnosed in 2018 and heart failure with reduced ejection fraction secondary to the use of anthracyclines was admitted to [organization] on 26/07 referred from the emergency care unit due to respiratory failure requiring orotracheal intubation and report of acute pulmonary edema at the origin in addition to a respiratory infection, where 8 ampoules of furosemide, 1 ampoule of terbutaline and 2 g of ceftriaxin were administered. Upon admission, the patient did not present with pulmonary congestion, and the patient was opted for volume expansion, combination of clarithromycin, initiation of sedation with fentanyl and midazolam, transfer to the intensive care unit and introduction of low-dose dobutamine and norepinephrine, which was weaned on 06/27. On 07/29, the patient presented a new clinical worsening with worsening of the volume and appearance of tracheal secretion and hypotension requiring reintroduction of norepinephrine, and the patient was opted to change antibiotic therapy to meropenem. On 07/01, [person] was clinically improving and was weaned from norepinephrine and dobutamine, sedation was started and medications previously used for heart failure were reintroduced. On the same day, tachycardia of approximately 140 beats per minute began, and it was decided to begin amiodarone impregnation, which was discontinued on 04/07, as [person] had not presented any documented episodes of atrial fibrillation, and it was decided to control the rate with bisoprolol. On 03/07, [person] was already scheduled for extubation and evolved well after intermittent noninvasive ventilation and without respiratory distress. On 04/07, [person] was transferred to [organization] to complete antibiotic therapy and progress to heart failure drugs. At [organization], [person] evolved well, remaining hemodynamically stable, afebrile, and without episodes of hypotension or worsening of renal function with progression to drugs. Now taking bisoprolol, enalapril, digoxin, and fusosemide at the doses previously used. The use of ivabradine will be discussed again in the outpatient clinic, as directed by the [organization] cardiology team. Today, 10 days of antibiotic therapy with meropenem have been completed. Personal history: Primary mediastinal large B-cell non-Hodgkin's lymphoma, Iax staging, diagnosis 06/03/2020. Superior vena cava syndrome. Right and left subclavian vein thrombosis. Cavernous sinus thrombosis. Angioresonance in 2018. Anticoagulated until 08/22. MCP heart failure secondary to anthracyclines. Left ventricular ejection fraction: 60% - 18%. Used doxurubicin, vincristine/etoposide. Hospitalization in 2018 for heart failure and mediastinal infection. In the 2018 discharge report: subclavian and jugular vein access contraindicated due to superior vena cava and subclavian vein thrombosis. Difficult femoral venous access. medications in use: bisoprolol 10 mg 1x/day enalapril 5 mg in the morning and 10 mg at night furosemide 40 mg 110 ivabradine 7.5 mg 12/12h digoxin 2.5 mg/day previous tests: transthoracic echocardiography 2018: preserved systolic function, ejection fraction 60% pre-treatment transthoracic echocardiography january 2019: ef: 18% ldfd: 60, moderate im moderate pulmonary hypertension. transthoracic echocardiography 4/19/18: ae 31 ldfd 49 ef 35% with diffuse hypo qtx performed since 06/2016. restaging 2020: computed tomography of the neck: absence of adenomegaly schedule: hospital discharge cardiology follow-up appointment 07/19 hematology follow-up 08/20. Admission: 2021-06-26, sent home from hospital (discharge): 2021-07-10, stay: 13 d 23 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. The patient, 39 years old, from [address] patient with primary mediastinal non-hodgkin's lymphoma diagnosed in 2018 and heart failure with reduced ejection fraction secondary to the use of anthracyclines was admitted to [organization] on 26/07 referred from the emergency care unit due to lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) requiring orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) and report of acute pulmonary swelling (edema) at the origin in addition to a respiratory infection, where 8 ampoules of furosemide, 1 ampoule of terbutaline and 2 g of ceftriaxin were give (administer)ed. Upon admission, the patient did not present with pulmonary congestion, and the patient was opted for volume expansion, combination of clarithromycin, initiation of sedation with fentanyl and midazolam, transfer to the ICU (serious care room) and introduction of low-dose dobutamine and norepinephrine, which was weaned on 06/27. On 07/29, the patient presented a new clinical worsening with worsening of the volume and appearance of tracheal secretion and low BP (hypotension) requiring reintroduction of norepinephrine, and the patient was opted to change infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) to meropenem. On 07/01, the patient was clinically improving and was weaned from norepinephrine and dobutamine, sedation was started and medications previously used for heart failure were reintroduced. On the same day, fast heartbeat (tachycardia) of approximately 140 beats per minute began, and it was decided to begin amiodarone impregnation, which was discontinued on 04/07, as the patient had not presented any documented episodes of irregular heartbeat (atrial fibrillation), and it was decided to control the rate with bisoprolol. On 03/07, the patient was already scheduled for breathing tube removed (extubation) and evolved well after intermittent noninvasive ventilation and without respiratory distress. On 04/07, the patient was transferred to [organization] to complete infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) and progress to heart failure drugs. At [organization], the patient evolved well, remaining stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), no fever (afebrile), and without episodes of low BP (hypotension) or worsening of renal function with progression to drugs. Now taking bisoprolol, enalapril, digoxin, and fusosemide at the doses previously used. The use of ivabradine will be discussed again in the OPD (Out Patient Department), as directed by the [organization] cardiology team. Today, 10 days of infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) with meropenem have been completed. Personal history: primary mediastinal large b-cell non-hodgkin's lymphoma, iax staging, diagnosis 06/03/2020. Superior vena cava syndrome. Right and left subclavian vein blood clot (thrombosis). Cavernous sinus blood clot (thrombosis). Angioresonance in 2018. Anticoagulated until 08/22. Mcp heart failure secondary to anthracyclines. Left ventricular ejection fraction: 60% - 18%. Used doxurubicin, vincristine/etoposide. Hospitalization in 2018 for heart failure and mediastinal infection. In the 2018 sent home from hospital (discharge) report: subclavian and jugular vein access contraindicated due to superior vena cava and subclavian vein blood clot (thrombosis). Difficult femoral venous access. Medications in use: bisoprolol 10 mg 1x/day enalapril 5 mg in the morning and 10 mg at night furosemide 40 mg 110 ivabradine 7.5 mg 12/12h digoxin 2.5 mg/day previous tests: transthoracic echocardiography 2018: preserved systolic function, ejection fraction 60% pre-treatment transthoracic echocardiography january 2019: general hospital room (ward): 18% ldfd: 60, moderate im moderate pulmonary high BP (hypertension). Transthoracic echocardiography 4/19/18: ae 31 ldfd 49 general hospital room (ward) 35% with diffuse hypo qtx performed since 06/2016. Restaging 2020: CT scan (computed tomography) of the neck: absence of adenomegaly schedule: hospital sent home from hospital (discharge) cardiology return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment 07/19 hematology return visit to doctor (follow-up) 08/20. Admission: 2021-06-26, sent home from hospital (discharge): 2021-07-10, stay: 13 d 23 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. 154 [person], 56 years old, born and raised in [address] admitted: 26/05, anthropometry: weight: 78 kg height: 159 cm bmi 30.8. problem: 1 acute respiratory distress syndrome, secondary to coronavirus, onset of symptoms: 05/25 worsening of symptoms: 05/29 pcr on 05/27 orotracheal intubation on 05/30 tracheostomy 06/21 proning: 15 proning last on 07/12, comorbidities 1 gallbladder neoplasm, right-sided abdominal pain, report of fecal acholia 2 weeks after admission, videolaparoscopic cholecystectomy on 05/24, personal history: high-grade poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinoma grade 3, with lymphatic and blood vascular invasion, focally compromised radial margin, rare foci of moderately differentiated tubular adenocarcinoma less than 10%, staging pt3 pn1, 2 pneumonia associated with mechanical ventilation treated, respiratory worsening on 05/29 CT Angiography (CTA) 05/30: no signs of thromboembolism pulmonary, staphylococcus aureus growth in aspirate, 3 septic shock from pulmonary focus 06/30 treated with meropenem gentamicin 06/30, 4 sacral pressure injury undergoing mechanical debridement treatment on 07/15, 5 right orbital proptosis treated already evaluated by ophthalmology, which recommended ciprofloxacin eye drops, occlusion, hypromellose and regencel, 6 pneumonia associated with mechanical ventilation, acinetobacter tracheobronchitis treated with meropenem and tigecycline guided by culture. admission: 05/26/2020, discharge: 08/11/2020, stay: 02 m 15 d 20 h, location: ward, payer: sus. [person] with prolonged hospitalization due to coronavirus infection with respiratory distress syndrome. [person] required intensive care, with orotracheal intubation, mechanical ventilation, proning positioning, and vasoactive drugs. She presented bacterial complications secondary to her respiratory condition. She was admitted to [organization] on July 31 to complete antibiotic therapy and rehabilitation. [person] showed progressive improvement in her ventilatory status with oxygen weaning and tracheostomy decannulation. She was undergoing continuous physical therapy due to critical patient polyneuropathy. She was investigated for chronic stroke due to an asymmetric pattern of loss of strength. Tests did not indicate vascular changes, and outpatient investigation for possible brachial plexus injury or deep fibular nerve {omitted} was indicated. Sacral pressure injury with progressive improvement with the use of papain. Given that oral intake was limited due to the absence of a dental prosthesis, [person] needed to maintain a nasoenteric tube diet for better caloric intake. She had no new bacterial complications. At the time of hospital discharge, [person] was hemodynamically stable, without vasoactive drugs. On room air, eupneic and comfortable. Normal and stable vital signs. Diet by mouth and nasoenteral tube. Without tracheostomy, with ostium occluded by dressing. Eliminations in diapers. Oriented in time and space. Regular general condition, pale 2 /4, dehydrated /4, acyanotic, anicteric, afebrile. Oriented in space and time vital signs: blood pressure 108x74 saturation 94% room air heart rate 80 respiratory rate 20 cardiac auscultation: regular rhythm, 2t, normal sound sounds, no murmurs respiratory auscultation vesicular breath sounds, crackles at the bases. without use of accessory muscles, abdomen: protuberant, depressive, painless, without peritonism, bowel sounds, I do not palpate masses or visceromegaly. warm and well-perfused extremities, without edema, no signs of deep vein thrombosis. sacral pressure injury g3: in right and left buttocks residual necrotic cap with almost complete detachment, hyperemia in edges, no purulent secretion improvement in macroscopic appearance and granulation tissue pressure injury in left trochanter region g2 with fibrin crusted lesions in bilateral tibias in scarring process neurological: glasgow coma scale 15. reduced strength in limbs, more important in lower limbs and on the left. sarcopenia, right ocular proptosis. preserved extrinsic movement. blood culture: 05/26: negative. 05/30: negative. 06/03: negative. 06/12: staphylococcus aureus oxa r 1 sample 06/12 catheter blood: negative 06/22: negative 06/29 negative 07/05: negative 07/06 catheter blood: negative 07/20: staphylococcus hominis 07/25: negative so far urine culture: 05/18: negative 05/26: negative. 05/30: negative. 06/03: candida albicans. 06/07: candida albicans and tropicalis. 06/12: pseudomonas s. 06/22: candida tropicalis 06/30: candida tropicalis 07/05: presence of fungi 80,000 cfu/ml 07/20: negative tracheal aspirate: 05/30: staphylococcus aureus oxar. 06/12: staphylococcus aureus oxar. 06/24 negative 07/05: multidrug-resistant acinetobacter baumani 07/21: multidrug-resistant acinetobacter sensitive to tigecycline and polymyxin catheter: 06/12: negative 06/22 negative 06/24 negative 06/29 negative 06/30 negative 06/06 negative 06/07 negative 07/25 in progress. tracheostomy 06/21 08/09. 1 post-covid coronavirus infection outside respiratory isolation decannulated on 08/09, 2 critically ill polyneuropathy under follow-up with physiotherapy and physiatry asymmetrical strength reduction under follow-up with neurology awaiting definitive report of head tomography and cranial and carotid CT Angiography (CTA) brachial plexus injury? deep fibular? 3 sacral pressure injury g3 improving dressing with 10% and 2% papain surgical debridement on 07/15, 4 anemia to be clarified multifactorial iron deficiency? chronic disease? bleeding? hypothyroidism? patient with long hospitalization gallbladder carcinoma with videolaparoscopy cholecystectomy on 05/24 lower oral acceptance, tsh 55 evaluate as an outpatient. 1 hospital discharge [person] come at 10 am as agreed. receive guidance from social services, nutrition and nursing. I talk to family members and give final instructions and deliver documents. rivaroxaban 10 mg/day 1 month is given to family members at the time of discharge, 2 outpatient follow-up appointments aminc: 09/23 speech: 10/06 physiatry: 09/13 head and neck surgery: 08/19 gastro surgery: 09/03 neurology: 09/15 nutrition will evaluate [person] on the day of follow-up appointment with aminc 09/23 3 reports delivered speech therapy dentistry sad basic health unit management of systemic arterial hypertension, diabetes mellitus, arrhythmia and hypothyroidism purchase of diapers ophthalmologist to monitor ocular proptosis 4 prescriptions levothyroxine 50 cmg/morning amiodarone 200 mg/morning simvastatin 20 mg/night paco 500 30 mg 1 tablet every 6/6h if pain bromopride 10 mg, 1 tablet every 8/8h if nausea or vomiting rivaroxaban 10mg/day, use for 1 month papain 2% 20 mg/g tube 60 grams, 1 tube per month papain 10% 100 mg/g tube 60 grams, 1 tube per month. 5 I request exams for follow-up appointment to the AMINC 6 Upon follow-up appointment to the AMINC, evaluate anemia and report of tomography and CT Angiography (CTA) of the skull and carotid arteries. At the time of hospital discharge, [person] [person] hemodynamically, without vasoactive drugs. on room air, eupneic and comfortable. normal and stable vital signs. diet by mouth and nasoenteral tube. without tracheostomy, with ostium occluded by dressing. eliminations in diapers. oriented in time and space. The patient, 56 years old, born and raised in [address] admitted: 26/05, anthropometry: weight: 78 kg height: 159 cm bmi 30.8. Problem: 1 acute respiratory distress syndrome, secondary to coronavirus, onset of symptoms: 05/25 worsening of symptoms: 05/29 pcr on 05/27 orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) on 05/30 breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) 06/21 proning: 15 proning last on 07/12, comorbidities 1 gallbladder neoplasm, right-sided abdominal pain, report of fecal acholia 2 weeks after admission, videolaparoscopic cholecystectomy on 05/24, personal history: high-grade poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinoma grade 3, with lymphatic and blood vascular invasion, focally compromised radial margin, rare foci of moderately differentiated tubular adenocarcinoma less than 10%, staging pt3 pn1, 2 lung infection (pneumonia) associated with machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation) treated, respiratory worsening on 05/29 ct angiography (cta) 05/30: no signs of thromboblood clot blocking a vessel (embolism) pulmonary, staphylococcus aureus growth in aspirate, 3 severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) from pulmonary focus 06/30 treated with meropenem gentamicin 06/30, 4 sacral pressure injury undergoing mechanical surgical wound cleaning (debridement) treatment on 07/15, 5 right orbital proptosis treated already evaluated by ophthalmology, which recommended ciprofloxacin eye drops, occlusion, hypromellose and regencel, 6 lung infection (pneumonia) associated with machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation), acinetobacter tracheobronchitis treated with meropenem and tigecycline guided by culture. Admission: 05/26/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 08/11/2020, stay: 02 m 15 d 20 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. The patient with prolonged hospitalization due to coronavirus infection with respiratory distress syndrome. The patient required intensive care, with orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation), machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation), proning positioning, and vasoactive drugs. She presented bacterial complications secondary to her respiratory condition. She was admitted to [organization] on july 31 to complete infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) and rehabilitation. The patient showed progressive improvement in her ventilatory status with oxygen weaning and breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) decannulation. She was undergoing continuous physical therapy due to critical patient polyneuropathy. She was investigated for chronic stroke due to an asymmetric pattern of loss of strength. Tests did not indicate vascular changes, and outpatient investigation for possible brachial plexus injury or deep fibular nerve was indicated. Sacral pressure injury with progressive improvement with the use of papain. Given that oral intake was limited due to the absence of a dental prosthesis, the patient needed to maintain a nasoenteric tube diet for better caloric intake. She had no new bacterial complications. At the time of hospital sent home from hospital (discharge), the patient was stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), without vasoactive drugs. On room air, breathing normally (eupneic) and comfortable. Normal and stable vital signs. Diet by mouth and nasoenteral tube. Without breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy), with ostium occluded by dressing. Eliminations in diapers. Oriented in time and space. Regular general condition, pale 2 /4, dehydrated /4, no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no fever (afebrile). Oriented in space and time vital signs: blood pressure 108x74 oxygen level in blood (saturation) 94% room air heart rate 80 respiratory rate 20 cardiac auscultation: regular rhythm, 2t, normal sound sounds, no murmurs respiratory auscultation normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds), crackles at the bases. Without use of accessory muscles, abdomen: protuberant, depressive, painless, without peritonism, bowel sounds, i do not palpate masses or visceromegaly. Warm and well-perfused extremities, without swelling (edema), no signs of deep vein blood clot (thrombosis). Sacral pressure injury g3: in right and left buttocks residual necrotic cap with almost complete detachment, hyperemia in edges, no purulent secretion improvement in macroscopic appearance and granulation tissue pressure injury in left trochanter region g2 with fibrin crusted lesions in bilateral tibias in scarring process neurological: glasgow coma scale 15. Reduced strength in limbs, more important in lower limbs and on the left. Sarcopenia, right ocular proptosis. Preserved extrinsic movement. Problem: 1 acute respiratory distress syndrome, secondary to coronavirus, onset of symptoms: 05/25 worsening of symptoms: 05/29 pcr on 05/27 orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) on 05/30 breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) 06/21 proning: 15 proning last on 07/12, comorbidities 1 gallbladder neoplasm, right-sided abdominal pain, report of fecal acholia 2 weeks after admission, videolaparoscopic cholecystectomy on 05/24, personal history: high-grade poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinoma grade 3, with lymphatic and blood vascular invasion, focally compromised radial margin, rare foci of moderately differentiated tubular adenocarcinoma less than 10%, staging pt3 pn1, 2 lung infection (pneumonia) associated with machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation) treated, respiratory worsening on 05/29 ct angiography (cta) 05/30: no signs of thromboblood clot blocking a vessel (embolism) pulmonary, staphylococcus aureus growth in aspirate, 3 severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) from pulmonary focus 06/30 treated with meropenem gentamicin 06/30, 4 sacral pressure injury undergoing mechanical surgical wound cleaning (debridement) treatment on 07/15, 5 right orbital proptosis treated already evaluated by ophthalmology, which recommended ciprofloxacin eye drops, occlusion, hypromellose and regencel, 6 lung infection (pneumonia) associated with machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation), acinetobacter tracheobronchitis treated with meropenem and tigecycline guided by culture. Admission: 05/26/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 08/11/2020, stay: 02 m 15 d 20 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. The patient with prolonged hospitalization due to coronavirus infection with respiratory distress syndrome. The patient required intensive care, with orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation), machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation), proning positioning, and vasoactive drugs. She presented bacterial complications secondary to her respiratory condition. She was admitted to [organization] on july 31 to complete infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) and rehabilitation. The patient showed progressive improvement in her ventilatory status with oxygen weaning and breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) decannulation. She was undergoing continuous physical therapy due to critical patient polyneuropathy. She was investigated for chronic stroke due to an asymmetric pattern of loss of strength. Tests did not indicate vascular changes, and outpatient investigation for possible brachial plexus injury or deep fibular nerve was indicated. Sacral pressure injury with progressive improvement with the use of papain. Given that oral intake was limited due to the absence of a dental prosthesis, the patient needed to maintain a nasoenteric tube diet for better caloric intake. She had no new bacterial complications. At the time of hospital sent home from hospital (discharge), the patient was stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), without vasoactive drugs. On room air, breathing normally (eupneic) and comfortable. Normal and stable vital signs. Diet by mouth and nasoenteral tube. Without breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy), with ostium occluded by dressing. Eliminations in diapers. Oriented in time and space. Regular general condition, pale 2 /4, dehydrated /4, no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no fever (afebrile). Oriented in space and time vital signs: blood pressure 108x74 oxygen level in blood (saturation) 94% room air heart rate 80 respiratory rate 20 cardiac auscultation: regular rhythm, 2t, normal sound sounds, no murmurs respiratory auscultation normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds), crackles at the bases. Without use of accessory muscles, abdomen: protuberant, depressive, painless, without peritonism, bowel sounds, i do not palpate masses or visceromegaly. Warm and well-perfused extremities, without swelling (edema), no signs of deep vein blood clot (thrombosis). Sacral pressure injury g3: in right and left buttocks residual necrotic cap with almost complete detachment, hyperemia in edges, no purulent secretion improvement in macroscopic appearance and granulation tissue pressure injury in left trochanter region g2 with fibrin crusted lesions in bilateral tibias in scarring process neurological: glasgow coma scale 15. Reduced strength in limbs, more important in lower limbs and on the left. Sarcopenia, right ocular proptosis. Preserved extrinsic movement. 155 identification: [person], 70 years old, born in {city} and coming from {city}, retired. admission: 03/05/2020, discharge: 09/05/2020, stay: 06 d 02 h, location: ward, payer: sus. patient being followed up in the hemodialysis room, with a diagnosis of disease associated with igg4, having appeared on 05/30 with a report of fever that started 3 days ago, measured at 38 39.5 °c, 1 daily peak at night, which quickly ceases after the administration of an oral antipyretic, associated with nausea and bilious vomiting 1 episode per day. patient reports left lower back pain, nephrostomy closed on 04/30. denied cough and dyspnea during evaluation, patient with productive cough. denied dysuria and abdominal pain. A collection was performed to test for coronavirus, which was negative, and a new infectious screening was requested, with the start of antibiotic therapy with meropenem and vancomycin, guided by previous cultures. As [person] needed daily antibiotic therapy and had difficulty in getting around, we opted for hospitalization for better treatment. Afterwards, a urine culture on 4/30 was positive for multiresistant Klebsiella and Pseudomonas, discussed with the ICC, and it was decided to start amikacin corrected for renal function on 5/5. [person] improved in his general condition, with no new episodes of fever. He is on hemodialysis due to the lack of improvement in renal function. He will undergo hemodialysis on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays, with an evaluation schedule for improvement in renal function. Tests: negative serologies, non-contrast abdominal tomography on 3/14: cardiomegaly with probable pericardial effusion with a thickness of up to 1.8 cm near the free edge of the VC. presence of expansive periaortic mass that bilaterally encompasses ureters, with suspicion of malignancy (lymphoma)? retroperitoneal fibrosis? bilateral pleural effusion, greater on the right. pulmonary nodule at the left base, suspicious. [person] 03/18: ao asc: 43 ae: 22 ddfve: 46 septum: 13 left ventricular ejection fraction: 35%, ms: diffuse hypokinesia. dsvd. heve. mild/moderate iao, mild dilatation of the aortic root and moderate of the ascending aorta, moderate pericardial effusion measuring up to 18 mm, anechoic, circumferential, more adjacent to the right chambers, without signs of increased intrapericardial pressure. tricuspid flow variation 34%, mitral 15%, absence of chamber collapse, [person] 03/23: no evidence of worsening of the effusion or signs of hemodynamic repercussions, however, as a patient with left ventricular systolic dysfunction not explained by other causes [person] 04/01: moderate to severe effusion measuring up to 21 mm, anechoic, circumferential, with incipient signs of increased intrapericardial pressure, protodiastolic collapse of the RV, variation of transvalvular flows may be altered by ventricular dysfunction tricuspid flow variation 29%, variation of flow in the LVOT 14%, RV less than 10%, variation of mitral flow 7%, RV less than 25%. LVEF 36%. pericardial fluid 03/04 also negative trm mycology negative cultures: urine culture 04/30: in processing blood culture 04/30: in processing suggestive of growth of microorganisms urine culture 03/26: negative urine culture 03/14: negative urine culture 04/07: 100,000 cfu/ml yeast-like fungi blood culture 03/26: klebsiella esbl sensitive to meropenem blood culture 04/07: enterococcus faecium s epidermidis. sens to vancomycin urine culture 04/30: klebsiella and pseudomonas amikacin d0 05/05 pericardial fluid 04/03: tb trm negative abar negative mycology culture for bacteria: in process cells 2400 / pmn 2% / mnm 24% / mesothelium 60% / atypical cells 14% prominent vacuole, nucleus with intermediate chromatin / ldh 215 / ptn 4.39 / gli 94 lab: 05/03: hb 10.2 / ht 30% / leuko 11,200 / plaq 182k / cr 2.21 gfr 22 / u 47 / na 136 / k 3.74 05/08: hb 8.6 / ht 25% / leuko 10,900 / plaq 223k / cr 3.66 tfg 12 / u 115 / k 3.45 / na 142 pre-hemodialysis. 12/04: left nephrostomy by rint without complications. will perform hemodialysis on Mondays, Wednesdays and Fridays will continue amikacin after hemodialysis. [person] in good general condition, conscious and oriented, hemodynamically stable, comfortable in room air. 156 pacemaker pocket infection. admission: 12/27/2021, discharge: 01/29/2022, stay: 01 m 01 d 18 h, location: ward, payer: sus. [person], 56 years old, male, from {city}, problem: 1st wall steviocardia killip iv without known delta t electrocardiogram 12/21: sinus rhythm, 1st degree avb, rbbr, zei inferior wall with residual st elevation icp 12/21: severe lesions in ada with closed dg1 atcp of ada with 2 successful drug-eluting stents, without complications. 2 bloodstream infection, endocarditis related to the pacemaker device, mixed septic cardiogenic shock blood cultures 12/08 and 12/11: staphylococcus aureus oxas extraction of pacemaker system on the right temporary pacemaker passage 12/17 temporary pacemaker removed 12/22 used oxacicillin d0 12/17 01/23 6 weeks infected pocket hematoma? cavity cleaning on 01/04, 3 myotonic dystrophy exacerbation due to rhabdomyolysis in the context of clinical complications, 4 bronchoaspiration pneumonia meropenem 01/22 to 02/01, history of current illness: [person] reports that about 4 days ago [person] presented disorientation, hyporexia, asthenia, decline in general condition and fever measured at 38º. sought care where symptomatic medications and guidance were administered. evolved with worsening of disorientation associated with hypotension and worsening of general condition. received [person] referral to zero vacancy. at the moment, he is hemodynamically unstable using norepinephrine, afebrile, disoriented in time and space and complaining of pain in the lower limbs. last febrile episode on 12/06 and last hemodialysis session on 12/07, personal history: 1 non-alcoholic fatty liver disease probable liver cirrhosis? splenomegaly with thrombocytopenia resolved ascites hypervolemic component? biopsy: not performed. [person] 2016: difficult to perform due to adipose tissue. [person] 2019: erosive esophagitis los angeles a, severe hypertensive gastropathy, bulboduodenitis. Differential diagnosis: viral hepatitis: negative, immune for Hep B Auto antibodies: FAN non-reactive, anti-mitochondria and anti-LKM negative Iron: normal ferritin, low serum iron Copper: not tested Alcoholism: deny, 2nd congestive liver disease Increased cannular enzymes secondary to volume congestion Personal history: 1. Diabetes mellitus for 25 years, diabetic nephropathy, chronic kidney disease V CKDEPI 15 ml/min Follow-up with nephrology, non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy, diabetic neuropathy 2. Stable angina CCSIII Follow-up with cardiology Angioplasty 11/18: SNF in proximal segment of PV / SNF to mid segment of CX / SNF to proximal branch of MG Decided not to approach distal / SNF to mid segment of anterior descending branch Moderate to severe mitral regurgitation Probable secondary etiology 3. Systemic arterial hypertension, dyslipidemia, obesity 4. Hyperuricemia, gout 5. hyperkalemia with ACE inhibitors 6. symptomatic cholelithiasis 7. dyspepsia 8. pacemaker in 2019: due to syncope / HV 70 ms, tests current hospitalization abdominal ultrasound 12/08: signs of chronic liver disease splenomegaly ultrasound of the kidneys and urinary tract 12/08: signs of chronic nephropathy, discussed head tomography 12/08 with radiology: no signs of bleeding awaiting official report, transthoracic echocardiography 12/09: left ventricular ejection fraction 59%. moderate tricuspid regurgitation, PSAP 41 mmHg, mild atrial dilation AE indexed 36 ml/m², AD indexed 36 ml/m², hyperrefrigerant image in right chambers suggestive of intracardiac implantable device cable. Change in segmental mobility of the left ventricle: septal dyssynergia. Hypokinesia of the basal inferior septum, basal anterior septum, basal inferior, basal anterolateral and middle segments. Other segments with normal mobility. Ultrasound of the right upper limb 20/12: absence of thrombosis in the studied segments. Blood culture 08/12: Staphylococcus aureus oxa s 1 sample after 14h, another after 25h. Blood culture 11/12: Staphylococcus aureus oxa s in 2 samples at 3 and 4 pm Blood culture 05/01: Staphylococcus capitis in 1 sample from HD catheter tip 14/12: Klebsiella pneumoniae carbapenemase sensitive to torgena Culture from MP store 14/01: Proteus mirabilis MS opted for surveillance, medications previously used: Atorvastatin 40 mg at night Carvedilol 25 mg 12/12h Furosemide 80 mg 12/12h Insulin NPH 1212012 Insulin, allopurinol 100 mg 1x/day Clopidogrel 75 mg/day Bic sodium calcium carbonate 500 mg 220 Suspended by Nephro after starting hemodialysis Acetylsalicylic acid 100 mg after lunch Suspended 11/19 due to nasal bleeding clonidine 150 mcg 212 suspended by nephro after starting hemodialysis. Last febrile episode on 12/06 and last hemodialysis session on 12/07, cardiac surgery to replace epicardial pacemaker, exploratory thoracotomy. Death. Pacemaker pocket infection. Admission: 12/27/2021, sent home from hospital (discharge): 01/29/2022, stay: 01 m 01 d 18 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. The patient, 56 years old, male, from the city, problem: 1st wall steviocardia killip iv without known delta t ECG/heart tracing (electrocardiogram) 12/21: sinus rhythm, 1st degree avb, rbbr, zei inferior wall with residual st elevation icp 12/21: severe lesions in ada with closed dg1 atcp of ada with 2 successful drug-eluting stents, without complications. 2 bloodstream infection, infection of heart valves (endocarditis) related to the pacemaker device, mixed septic cardiogenic shock blood test to find infection (blood culture)s 12/08 and 12/11: staphylococcus aureus oxas extraction of pacemaker system on the right temporary pacemaker passage 12/17 temporary pacemaker removed 12/22 used oxacicillin d0 12/17 01/23 6 weeks infected pocket hematoma? Sought care where symptomatic medications and guidance were give (administer)ed. Evolved with worsening of disorientation associated with low BP (hypotension) and worsening of general condition. They also found that the virus was spreading quickly, and the virus had spread to the heart of the body. This is why we need to make sure that we don't get the virus out of the blood test to find infection (blood culture). We can't wait for the virus to stop the virus. We'll be able to find out if the virus is spreading in the blood from the heart, but we're using the virus and it'll continue to be used in the future. We need more information about how to use it. This new technology to make it harder for people to understand how we can be used for the disease. 157 [person]. {date of birth}. admission: 22/12/2021. stay: 10 d 21 h, location: ward, payer: sus. follow-up appointment. emergency. home care. admission to the emergency unit: 22/12/2021 reason: urinary complaints fluctuations in level of consciousness 1 pyelonephritis sofa 3 alterations in creatinine and cognitive function already present onset of symptoms on 17/12/2021: urine with foul odor pollakiuria fluctuations in level of consciousness urine culture: 23/12 instalado stuartii antibiotic therapy: discussed with {organization} meropenem 22/12 01/01, ultrasound of kidneys and urinary tract 22/12/2021: pyelocaliceal dilatation with worsening in relation to the previous appearance. presence of urinary sediment in renal calyx pus?. computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis 12/23: marked diffuse dilatation of colic loops, secondary to a large fecal mass in the rectosigmoid. It exerts a significant locoregional mass effect, with displacement of adjacent structures and determining ureteropelvic dilatation of the transplanted kidney in the left iliac fossa. chronic kidney disease. atheromatous arterial disease evidence of a large fecal mass of approximately 12 cm with anterior displacement of the bladder and compression of the ureter causing pyelocaliceal dilatation in the transplanted kidney located in the left iliac fossa. presence of severe aortic atheromatosis. 2 chronic kidney disease stage 3b without exacerbation at the moment 3 resolved hyperactive delirium. evolution: [person] demented, institutionalized, with frequent hospitalizations for urinary tract infection, last 90 days ago. Regulated via cross-check of the emergency care unit with report of decline in general condition for approximately 5 days associated with periods of agitation and urinary changes (fetid odor and pollakiuria). Denies fever. I evaluate [person] at reception, drowsy, speaking incomprehensible words and not answering questions. At origin, only nitrofurantoin was started. He was admitted, observed in computed tomography a fecal mass that had a locoregional mass effect, displacing the transplanted kidney and presenting pyelocaliceal dilation. A general surgery evaluation was requested, which performed a digital rectal examination to break up the fecal mass, indicating Fleet and oral laxatives. [person] showed significant improvement and was able to evacuate approximately 23 times/day. In addition, meropenem was started on 12/22 guided by urine culture, finishing on 01/01. [person] evolved with significant clinical improvement, follow-up appointmenting to his previous baseline neurological status. He was discharged without complications. medications in use: mf 360 mg 2 tablets valproic acid: 500 mg 10 ml 101 clonazepam 2 mg/night if necessary quetiapine 50 mg 203 olanzapine 5 mg 001/2 prednisone 5 mg/day atorvastatin 80 mg amlodipine 5 mg night omeprazole 20 mg 100 phosphomycin 1x every 15 days vitamin d 50000 once a month solifenacin? lactulose 15 ml 8/8h. clinical discharge. follow-up appointments follow-up appointment on 12/01 for kidney ultrasound and laboratory tests on 29/01 follow-up appointment to the ntx outpatient clinic. 158 coronavirus 19. admission: 08/27/2020, discharge: 09/28/2020, stay: 01 m 01 d 11 h, location: ward, payer: sus. [person] reports that in june he presented flu-like symptoms with myalgia, fever and cough lasting 15 days without the need for hospitalization, positive coronavirus pcr 06/18 and complete improvement of symptoms. 9 days ago [person] started dry cough and fever again, with progressive worsening. reports orthopnea and worsening of symptoms at night. denies expectoration, syncope, chest pain, lower limb edema or urinary changes. sought emergency care on 08/26 with desaturation and tachydyspnea and was referred to this service requiring high-flow oxygen. I admit [person], conscious and oriented, hemodynamically stable without vasoactive drugs, tachypneic and with discomfort during physical exertion, saturation 95% with reservoir mask 10 l/min at rest. evolution during hospitalization: 08/27: orotracheal intubation in the red room without complications 08/27: admitted to the intensive care unit at {omitted} proning at 8:30 PM 08/28: rhabdomyolysis creatine phosphokinase 7430 with preserved renal function 08/29: new proning at 00:00 AM 08/30: new proning at 3:30 PM 08/31: rel paraf over 150 creatine phosphokinase falling, attention 08/01: not proning rel paraf: 180 09/02: new proning due to worsening ventilatory mechanics 09/08: ventilator-associated pneumonia diagnosed, meropenem vancomycin started 09/10: tracheostomy 09/16: new worsening of infection, cultures collected and devices changed. escalated antibiotic therapy to gentamicin. 09/18: fungi identified in urine, in presence of fever, fluconazole started 09/22: portex tracheostomy occluded 09/23: [person] transferred to the ward 09/24: decannulation performed. meropenem and vancomycin discontinued 09/25: evaluation of surgery for mechanical debridement of sacral ulcer proning: first proning: 08/27 at 8:30 PM. second proning: 08/29 at 12:00 AM. third proning 08/30 at 3:30 PM. fourth proning: 09/02 at 10:00 PM supine at 8:00 PM on 09/03. fifth proning: 09/04 at 10:00 PM. preproning paraf 120. proning: 246. supine: 05/09 18h paraf 148. proning friday: 07/09 at 02h. post-proning paraf: 224. [person] in good general condition, alert, conscious, normal complexion, hydrated, afebrile, anicteric, acyanotic respiratory auscultation vesicular breath soundss present, symmetrical, decreased at the bases bilaterally, no adventitious sounds cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm in two beats, normal phonetic sounds, no murmurs abdomen: obese, bowel sounds, flaccid, no signs of bladder mass limbs: capillary refill time less than 2 s, full distal pulses, thighs and calves without swelling, edema 1/4 of lower limbs at ankles neurological: glasgow 15, isochoric and Isochoric and photoreactive pupils clc evaluation: sacral region: pressure injury measuring approximately 15x20 cm, no phlogistic signs or secretion discharge, no foul odor. presence of devitalized tissue occupying the entire lesion, softened, with well-defined edges. debridement of sacral ulcer. maintain gentamicin until d14 and fluconazole until d21. good general condition. Coronavirus 19. Admission: 08/27/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 09/28/2020, stay: 01 m 01 d 11 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. The patient reports that in june he presented flu-like symptoms with myalgia, fever and cough lasting 15 days without the need for hospitalization, positive coronavirus pcr 06/18 and complete improvement of symptoms. 9 days ago the patient started dry cough and fever again, with progressive worsening. Reports orthopnea and worsening of symptoms at night. Denies expectoration, syncope, chest pain, lower limb swelling (edema) or urinary changes. Sought emergency care on 08/26 with deoxygen level in blood (saturation) and tachydifficulty in breathing (dyspnea) and was referred to this service requiring high-flow oxygen. I admit the patient, conscious and oriented, stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable) without vasoactive drugs, tachypneic and with discomfort during physical exertion, oxygen level in blood (saturation) 95% with reservoir mask 10 l/min at rest. Evolution during hospitalization: 08/27: orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) in the red room without complications 08/27: admitted to the ICU (serious care room) at proning at 8:30 pm 08/28: rhabdomyolysis creatine phosphokinase 7430 with preserved renal function 08/29: new proning at 00:00 am 08/30: new proning at 3:30 pm 08/31: rel paraf over 150 creatine phosphokinase falling, attention 08/01: not proning rel paraf: 180 09/02: new proning due to worsening ventilatory mechanics 09/08: ventilator-associated lung infection (pneumonia) diagnosed, meropenem vancomycin started 09/10: breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) 09/16: new worsening of infection, cultures collected and devices changed. Escalated infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) to gentamicin. 09/18: fungi identified in urine, in presence of fever, fluconazole started 09/22: portex breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) occluded 09/23: the patient transferred to the general hospital room (ward) 09/24: decannulation performed. Meropenem and vancomycin discontinued 09/25: evaluation of surgery for mechanical surgical wound cleaning (debridement) of sacral ulcer proning: first proning: 08/27 at 8:30 pm. Second proning: 08/29 at 12:00 am. Third proning 08/30 at 3:30 pm. Fourth proning: 09/02 at 10:00 pm supine at 8:00 pm on 09/03. Fifth proning: 09/04 at 10:00 pm. Preproning paraf 120. Proning: 246. Supine: 05/09 18h paraf 148. Proning friday: 07/09 at 02h. Post-proning paraf: 224. The patient in good general condition, alert, conscious, normal complexion, hydrated, no fever (afebrile), no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no bluish discoloration (acyanotic) respiratory auscultation normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds)s present, symmetrical, decreased at the bases bilaterally, no adventitious sounds cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm in two beats, normal phonetic sounds, no murmurs abdomen: obese, bowel sounds, flaccid, no signs of bladder mass limbs: capillary refill time less than 2 s, full distal pulses, thighs and calves without swelling, swelling (edema) 1/4 of lower limbs at ankles neurological: glasgow 15, isochoric and isochoric and photoreactive pupils clc evaluation: sacral region: pressure injury measuring approximately 15x20 cm, no phlogistic signs or secretion sent home from hospital (discharge), no foul odor. Presence of devitalized tissue occupying the entire lesion, softened, with well-defined edges. Surgical wound cleaning (debridement) of sacral ulcer. Maintain gentamicin until d14 and fluconazole until d21. Good general condition. Coronavirus 19: admission: 08/27/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 09/28/2020. Stay: 01 m 01 d 11 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. The patient had a flu-like symptoms with myalgia, fever and cough lasting 15 days. The doctor said that the patient started dry cough and fever again, with progressive worsening of symptoms. He said that he had a lot of problems with the symptoms in the morning, but he was not able to get the symptoms. They said that it's hard to see if the patient's symptoms are worse. They were more likely to have symptoms at night, especially when he was in the hospital. They also found that the doctor didn't have enough medicines to treat the patient. They found that it was difficult to keep the patient safe and be able to take care of the patient, especially if he was stable without vasoactive drugs, tachypneic and with discomfort during physical exertion, oxygen level in blood (saturation) 95% with reservoir mask 10 l/min at rest.</s> Evolution during hospitalization: 08/27: orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) in the red room without complications 08/27: admitted to the ICU (serious care room) at proning at 8:30 pm 08/28: rhabdomyolysis creatine phosphokinase 7430 with preserved renal function 08/29: new proning at 00:00 am 08/30: new proning at 3:30 pm 08/31: rel paraf over 150 creatine phosphokinase falling, attention 08/01: not proning rel paraf: 180 09/02: new proning due to worsening ventilatory mechanics 09/08: ventilator-associated lung infection (pneumonia) diagnosed, meropenem vancomycin started 09/10: breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) 09/16: new worsening of infection, cultures collected and devices changed. Presence of devitalized tissue occupying the entire lesion, softened, with well-defined edges. Surgical wound cleaning (debridement) of sacral ulcer. Maintain gentamicin until d14 and fluconazole until d21. Good general condition. a419. septicemia ne. j80. adult respiratory distress syndrome. u04. severe acute respiratory syndrome [sars]. u049. severe acute respiratory syndrome [sars], unspecified. b342. coronavirus infection, ne. e66. obesity. j189. pneumonia ne. u071. novel coronavirus infection (covid-19). alive 36.3 1.22
159 decompensation of heart failure abandonment of treatment for systemic lupus erythematosus, problem: 1 sepsis of urinary focus, pulmonary 2 adrenal insufficiency suggestive symptoms abrupt discontinuation of corticosteroids 3 acute kidney injury kdigo i nta sec a sepsis? prerenal?. admission: 12/21/2020, discharge: 01/09/2021, stay: 19 d 06 h, location: ward, payer: sus. hospitalization summary: [person] with prolonged hospitalization for sepsis of urinary focus, antibiotic therapy with third-generation cephalosporin completed. during hospitalization, she was diagnosed with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, treated during hospitalization for decompensated heart failure profile b, and is currently undergoing medication optimization. has a diagnosis of dissected aortic aneurysm, evaluated by vascular surgery, with proposal of heart rate and blood pressure control, and outpatient follow-up initially conservative, with no current surgical proposal. presented acute kidney injury when trying to optimize drugs for heart failure. [person] reports worsening of dyspnea for 10 days, in addition to symptoms compatible with postural hypotension, dizziness and visual impairment when standing upright. denies syncope. also reports that, in the last few days, he has presented nausea and vomiting, as well as hyporexia and postprandial bloating. reports that, on the morning of admission, he presented visual impairment and dizziness, when he measured his blood pressure and it was 50 x 30, seeking medical care. [person] denies paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, denies orthopnea, is already dependent for instrumental activities and partially dependent for basic activities. presents difficulty walking. denies dysuria, denies diarrhea, denies skin lesions, denies odynophagia, denies change in cough pattern. denies measured fever. Denies craving for salt. [person] reports that the [person] who was responsible for administering the medications. However, 1 month ago, [person] gave up doing so and, since then, [person] has been without the medications. Reports that he is only taking psychotropic drugs. During hospitalization, he progressed with improvement of symptoms, improvement of infection, with isolation in urine culture and blood culture of Escherichia coli multis. Clarithromycin was administered for 5 days, ceftriaxone maintained until day 10. During hospitalization, an electrocardiogram was performed showing sinus rhythm with occasional atrial fibrillation. Investigation of anemia as a chronic disease. Escherichia coli and Stanford A aortic aneurysm were evidenced, with no surgical proposal, under clinical treatment with double control. Awaiting transfer to a ward bed in {organization}, drug optimization with double control. Reduced mobility. vital signs within normal limits regular general condition, conscious and oriented, glasgow 15, hemodynamically stable, pale 2/4, hydrated, acyanotic, anicteric, peripheral capillary perfusion less than 3 seconds respiratory auscultation vesicular breath sounds present bilaterally, no adventitious sounds, no signs of respiratory effort cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm in 2t, normal sound sounds, no murmur, slight TJP in 1/3 less than 45° abdomen: flat, flaccid, painless on superficial and deep palpation, no signs of peritoneal irritation, peristalsis lower limbs: no edema, no signs of deep vein thrombosis, well perfused. Admission tests: chest tomography 12/21: chronic-looking Stanford A aortic dissection, probable retrograde route, signs of interstitial lung disease, non-recent bilateral costal arch fractures. Previous antibiotic therapy: ceftriaxone D1: 12/21 12/30; metronidazole 12/21 12/21; clarithromycin 12/21 12/27. Pending issues, goals: completion of antibiotic therapy; adjustment of medications; optimization of heart failure drugs. 1 regarding septic condition of probable pulmonary focus: antibiotic therapy already performed; 2 regarding probable adrenal insufficiency; chronic user of corticosteroids with abrupt discontinuation of use. I maintain prednisone 10 mg once a day; observe intestinal condition associated with vomiting for need to adjust corticosteroid therapy; 3 [person] defining therapeutic goals. palliative care not defined so far due to social issues family members do not attend [person] with lupus, AR, Marfan, stopped taking medication 3 months ago because she depended on [person]'s help. She was admitted to the emergency unit with septic shock from urinary focus and adrenal insufficiency secondary to discontinuation of prednisone. During admission, due to tachypnea and ventilatory effort, she requested that orotracheal intubation not be performed, the decision being respected and morphine being started. [person] progressed with improvement in the infection and shock, weaning from oxygen and having morphine withdrawn. Discussed goals with [person] and she is reconsidering the decision, at the moment without a defined opinion. Checked the medical records of previous hospitalization: [person] already evaluated by palliative care. Talked again with [person] on 12/22 and 12/23. Reported that she spent most of the time lying in bed. Had great difficulty moving, could only walk short distances alone and had a lot of dyspnea when walking such distances. Reported that clinical worsening and greater dependence for care since last hospitalization. If [person] develops new clinical worsening and need for invasive interventions, reconsider defining therapeutic goals depending on [person]'s clinical condition. 4 regarding aortic aneurysm: discussion of images with [person] and [person] and [person] images compatible with stanford aortic aneurysm a with onset after emergency of left subclavian artery. we requested vascular surgery evaluation as [person] stable, without worsening clinical dissection, without radiating pain, uncontrolled blood pressure or heart rate, we opted for outpatient follow-up, with early follow-up appointment as soon as discharged. as soon as [person] is in condition to discharge, page general surgery, vascular to schedule early follow-up appointment, requested myocardial necrosis markers troponin and ckmb negative, suspended performance of transesophageal echocardiogram as discussed with [person]. follow-up appointment to aimu rheumatology 01/29 follow-up appointment to cvp cardiology 03/31 vascular follow-up appointment 01/26. transfer to {organization}. discharged in good general condition. case discussed with [person]. Denies syncope. Also reports that, in the last few days, he systemic arterial high BP (hypertension) presented nausea and vomiting, as well as hyporexia and postprandial bloating. However, 1 month ago, the patient gave up doing so and, since then, the patient systemic arterial high BP been without the medications. Reports that he is only taking psychotropic drugs. During hospitalization, he progressed with improvement of symptoms, improvement of infection, with isolation in urine culture and blood test to find infection (blood culture) of escherichia coli multis. Clarithromycin was give (administer)ed for 5 days, ceftriaxone maintained until day 10. During hospitalization, an ECG/heart tracing (electrocardiogram) was performed showing sinus rhythm with occasional irregular heartbeat (atrial fibrillation). Investigation of low blood (anemia) as a chronic disease. Escherichia coli and stanford a aortic aneurysm were evidenced, with no surgical proposal, under clinical treatment with double control. Awaiting transfer to a general hospital room (ward) bed in the hospital, drug optimization with double control. Reduced mobility. Admission tests: chest tomography 12/21: chronic-looking stanford a aortic dissection, probable retrograde route, signs of interstitial lung disease, non-recent bilateral costal arch fractures. Comfort-focused (not curative) care not defined so far due to social issues family members do not attend the patient with lupus, ar, marfan, stopped taking medication 3 months ago because she depended on the patient's help. She was admitted to the emergency unit with severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) from urinary focus and adrenal insufficiency secondary to discontinuation of prednisone. During admission, due to tachypnea and ventilatory effort, she requested that orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) not be performed, the decision being respected and morphine being started. The patient progressed with improvement in the infection and shock, weaning from oxygen and having morphine withdrawn. Discussed goals with the patient and she is reconsidering the decision, at the moment without a defined opinion. Checked the medical records of previous hospitalization: the patient already evaluated by comfort-focused (not curative) care. Talked again with the patient on 12/22 and 12/23. Reported that she spent most of the time lying in bed. Had great difficulty moving, could only walk short distances alone and had a lot of difficulty in breathing (dyspnea) when walking such distances. 160 1 spondylodiscitis: fever for 2 months low back pain worsening of renal function postoperative valproic acid t9l1 unilateral on the left decompression t10t12 11/08/2020 spondylodiscitis t 10t11 postoperative 11/29/20: debridement lavage dressing in the surgical center wound culture 11/29: multi-resistant klebsiella multi-resistant pseudomonas but we considered spondylodiscitis secondary to endocarditis and transitioned to maintenance treatment with amoxicillin, blood culture enterococcus faecalis, 2 infective endocarditis enterococcus faecalis multis in two blood cultures from 11/04 surgical wound 11/07: normal transthoracic echocardiography 11/09: left ventricular ejection fraction 60% mitral valve with thickened leaflets and Filamentary image adhered to the atrial face of the posterior leaflet suggestive of vegetation. Valvular regurgitation apparently through the posterior leaflet suggestive of valve perforation. Diffusely preserved systolic performance. Transthoracic echocardiography 16/11: left ventricular ejection fraction 54% mild dilation of the left cardiac chambers. Concentric hypertrophy of the left ventricle preserved systolic performance of the ventricles. Normal segmental mobility of the left ventricle. Marked mitral insufficiency secondary to lesions suggestive of endocarditis. Moderate dilation of the aortic root and mild dilation of the ascending aorta. No images suggestive of intracardiac thrombus were seen. antibiotic therapy: tazocin 11/04 11/09, empirical ceftriaxone ampicillin 11/09 12/07, guided vancomycin 12/07 12/14/22, started due to clinical worsening blood culture 12/07: negative we consider infective endocarditis treated by cardiac surgery 12/15: surgery contraindicated patient with multiple comorbidities, tracheostomized and dependent on mechanical ventilation, evolving with multiple complications after spinal surgical approaches 3 acute kidney injury kdigo 3 resolved infectious? drug toxicity? 4 upper gastrointestinal bleeding? episode of melena on 12/01 without recurrence did not perform upper gastrointestinal endoscopy. admission: 11/03/2020, discharge: 01/06/2021, stay: 02 m 02 d 23 h, location: ward, payer: sus. previously hypertensive, pre-diabetic patient, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, with ischemic stroke in 2020 and previous psychiatric follow-up for panic syndrome. was being followed up with a gastrosurgery team for choledocholithiasis with a videolaparoscopic cholecystectomy approach in 2019 and PRE in 2020. admitted to {organization} on 11/3/2020 for investigation of fever for 2 months associated with low back pain, weight loss and worsening renal function. in initial investigation exams, spondylodiscitis of t10t11 was evidenced by lumbar spine resonance on 11/4/20, being approached for decompression on 11/8/2020 and re-approached for lavage and debridement on 11/29/20. in infectious screening, blood culture showed on 11/04/20 with Enterococus faecalis and transthoracic echocardiography on 11/09/20 with vegetation in the mitral valve, confirmed by transthoracic echocardiography on 11/16/20. Antibiotic therapy with empirical tazocim was started on 11/04/20, being transitioned to ceftriaxone and ampicillin on 11/09/20 guided by blood culture on 11/04 and escalated to vancomycin, polymyxin and tigecycline on 12/07/20 due to clinical worsening, guided by surgical site culture on 11/29 with growth of Klebisiella multir Pseudomonas multir. underwent treatment until 12/14/20, with the last blood culture on 12/07/20 negative, being considered treated infective endocarditis. seen spondylodiscitis considered secondary to endocarditis, currently on maintenance treatment with oral amoxicillin 10ml 8/8h starting on 12/14/20. [person] after the first surgical approach, he evolved with the need for mechanical ventilation in a monitored bed, being extubated on 11/21/20 with creation of a tracheostomy and exchange for a portex on 12/20/20, being decannulated on 01/02/21, without the need for supplemental oxygen support. admitted to a ward bed on 12/06, remaining afebrile, hemodynamically stable. discharged on 01/06 with a new ultrasound scheduled for 01/18 and outpatient follow-up visits with the infectious disease, thoracic surgery, and orthopedics teams. fracture of vertebrae t10t12. chest tomography 11/04 evaluation compromised by the absence of contrast: presence of mediastinal lymph node in chain 4d 2.1 x 1.2 and other prominent lymph nodes in unusual chains engorged by congestion? enlarged cardiac area. parenchyma: diffuse ground-glass opacity, post-coronavirus mosaic pattern?. abdominal and pelvic tomography 11/04 evaluation compromised by the absence of contrast: bilateral renal cysts, with no evidence of obstruction. absence of collections and/or signs of intra-abdominal inflammatory processes. musculoskeletal evaluation: fracture of l1. destructive process centered on the disc with involvement of the bodies of t10 and t11 spondylodiscitis?. Magnetic resonance imaging of the spine 04/11: extensive pattern of edema in the bone marrow of the vertebral bodies of t10 and t11, accompanied by disorganization of the intervertebral disc and blurring of the contours of the vertebral plates at t10t11. Enhancement of the areas of edema after intravenous contrast, with a non-enhancing area in the central region of the vertebral body of t11, suspicious for necrosis and/or devitalized tissue. Signs of a paravertebral inflammatory process in the region, with marked enhancement of soft tissues and epidural post-contrast. There is also edema in the posterior elements of t10. There is a deformity with increased kyphosis at the level of t10t11 with signs of associated spinal cord compression. There is also an alteration that may correspond to a small pre-spinal fluid collection. Deformity suggesting sequelae of a compressive fracture in the vertebral body l1. Transthoracic echocardiography 12/20: mitral valve with thickened, hyperrefringent leaflets and heterogeneous, mobile masses, irregular contours located on the edge of the anterior leaflet, measuring 9x6 mm, protruding into the left atrium during systole. Filamentary and mobile image adhered to the subvalvular apparatus measuring 7 mm, leading to severe insufficiency. Mild dilation of the left atrium. Preserved ventricular systolic performance. Postoperative valproic acid t9l1 unilateral on the left decompression t10t12 11/08 central venous access. Maintain antibiotic therapy with amoxicillin until outpatient follow-up appointment with [organization]. Maintain continuous use of medications against referral to CAPS where anxiety continued. 01/12 orthopedics outpatient spine surgery wound assessment, lumbar and thoracic spine x-ray. 01/27 thoracic surgery outpatient clinic. 02/02 collection of laboratory tests on 01/27 new case cdee 02/24. walking with assistance, eating orally, in physical rehabilitation. 1 spine bone infection (spondylodiscitis): fever for 2 months low back pain worsening of renal function postoperative valproic acid t9l1 unilateral on the left decompression t10t12 11/08/2020 spine bone infection (spondylodiscitis) t 10t11 postoperative 11/29/20: surgical wound cleaning (debridement) lavage dressing in the surgical center wound culture 11/29: multi-resistant klebsiella multi-resistant pseudomonas but we considered spine bone infection (spondylodiscitis) secondary to infection of heart valves (endocarditis) and transitioned to maintenance treatment with amoxicillin, blood test to find infection (blood culture) enterococcus faecalis, 2 infective infection of heart valves (endocarditis) enterococcus faecalis multis in two blood test to find infection (blood culture)s from 11/04 surgical wound 11/07: normal transthoracic echocardiography 11/09: left ventricular ejection fraction 60% mitral valve with thickened leaflets and filamentary image adhered to the atrial face of the posterior leaflet suggestive of vegetation. Valvular regurgitation apparently through the posterior leaflet suggestive of valve perforation. Diffusely preserved systolic performance. Transthoracic echocardiography 16/11: left ventricular ejection fraction 54% mild dilation of the left cardiac chambers. Concentric hypertrophy of the left ventricle preserved systolic performance of the ventricles. Normal segmental mobility of the left ventricle. Marked mitral insufficiency secondary to lesions suggestive of infection of heart valves (endocarditis). Moderate dilation of the aortic root and mild dilation of the ascending aorta. No images suggestive of intracardiac thrombus were seen. Infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy): tazocin 11/04 11/09, empirical ceftriaxone ampicillin 11/09 12/07, guided vancomycin 12/07 12/14/22, started due to clinical worsening blood test to find infection (blood culture) 12/07: negative we consider infective infection of heart valves (endocarditis) treated by cardiac surgery 12/15: surgery contraindicated patient with multiple comorbidities, tracheostomized and dependent on machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation), evolving with multiple complications after spinal surgical approaches 3 acute kidney injury kdigo 3 resolved infectious? Drug toxicity? 4 upper gastrointestinal bleeding? Episode of melena on 12/01 without recurrence did not perform upper gastrointestinal endoscopy. Admission: 11/03/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 01/06/2021, stay: 02 m 02 d 23 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Previously hypertensive, pre-diabetic patient, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, with reduced blood supply (ischemic) stroke in 2020 and previous psychiatric return visit to doctor (follow-up) for panic syndrome. Was being followed up with a gastrosurgery team for choledocholithiasis with a videolaparoscopic cholecystectomy approach in 2019 and pre in 2020. Admitted to the hospital on 11/3/2020 for investigation of fever for 2 months associated with low back pain, weight loss and worsening renal function. In initial investigation exams, spine bone infection (spondylodiscitis) of t10t11 was evidenced by lumbar spine resonance on 11/4/20, being approached for decompression on 11/8/2020 and re-approached for lavage and surgical wound cleaning (debridement) on 11/29/20. In infectious screening, blood test to find infection (blood culture) showed on 11/04/20 with enterococus faecalis and transthoracic echocardiography on 11/09/20 with vegetation in the mitral valve, confirmed by transthoracic echocardiography on 11/16/20. Infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) with empirical tazocim was started on 11/04/20, being transitioned to ceftriaxone and ampicillin on 11/09/20 guided by blood test to find infection (blood culture) on 11/04 and escalated to vancomycin, polymyxin and tigecycline on 12/07/20 due to clinical worsening, guided by surgical site culture on 11/29 with growth of klebisiella multir pseudomonas multir. Underwent treatment until 12/14/20, with the last blood test to find infection (blood culture) on 12/07/20 negative, being considered treated infective infection of heart valves (endocarditis). Seen spine bone infection (spondylodiscitis) considered secondary to infection of heart valves (endocarditis), currently on maintenance treatment with oral amoxicillin 10ml 8/8h starting on 12/14/20. The patient after the first surgical approach, he evolved with the need for machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation) in a monitored bed, being extubated on 11/21/20 with creation of a breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) and exchange for a portex on 12/20/20, being decannulated on 01/02/21, without the need for supplemental oxygen support. Admitted to a general hospital room (ward) bed on 12/06, remaining no fever (afebrile), stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable). Sent home from hospital (discharge)d on 01/06 with a new ultrasound scheduled for 01/18 and outpatient return visit to doctor visits with the infectious disease, thoracic surgery, and orthopedics teams. Fracture of vertebrae t10t12. Chest tomography 11/04 evaluation compromised by the absence of contrast: presence of mediastinal lymph node in chain 4d 2.1 x 1.2 and other prominent lymph nodes in unusual chains engorged by congestion? Enlarged cardiac area. Parenchyma: diffuse ground-glass opacity, post-coronavirus mosaic pattern?. Abdominal and pelvic tomography 11/04 evaluation compromised by the absence of contrast: bilateral renal cysts, with no evidence of obstruction. Absence of collections and/or signs of intra-abdominal inflammatory processes. Musculoskeletal evaluation: fracture of l1. Destructive process centered on the disc with involvement of the bodies of t10 and t11 spine bone infection (spondylodiscitis)?. MRI scan (magnetic resonance) imaging of the spine 04/11: extensive pattern of swelling (edema) in the bone marrow of the vertebral bodies of t10 and t11, accompanied by disorganization of the intervertebral disc and blurring of the contours of the vertebral plates at t10t11. Enhancement of the areas of swelling (edema) after given through a vein (drip/IV) contrast, with a non-enhancing area in the central region of the vertebral body of t11, government health schemepicious for tissue death (necrosis) and/or devitalized tissue. Signs of a paravertebral inflammatory process in the region, with marked enhancement of soft tissues and epidural post-contrast. There is also swelling (edema) in the posterior elements of t10. There is a deformity with increased kyphosis at the level of t10t11 with signs of associated spinal cord compression. There is also an alteration that may correspond to a small pre-spinal fluid collection. Deformity suggesting sequelae of a compressive fracture in the vertebral body l1. Transthoracic echocardiography 12/20: mitral valve with thickened, hyperrefringent leaflets and heterogeneous, mobile masses, irregular contours located on the edge of the anterior leaflet, measuring 9x6 mm, protruding into the left atrium during systole. Filamentary and mobile image adhered to the subvalvular apparatus measuring 7 mm, leading to severe insufficiency. Mild dilation of the left atrium. Preserved ventricular systolic performance. Postoperative valproic acid t9l1 unilateral on the left decompression t10t12 11/08 central venous access. Maintain infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) with amoxicillin until outpatient return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment with [organization]. Valvular regurgitation apparently through the posterior leaflet suggestive of valve perforation. Diffusely preserved systolic performance. Transthoracic echocardiography 16/11: left ventricular ejection fraction 54% mild dilation of the left cardiac chambers. Concentric hypertrophy of the left ventricle preserved systolic performance of the ventricles. Normal segmental mobility of the left ventricle. Marked mitral insufficiency secondary to lesions suggestive of infection of heart valves (endocarditis). Moderate dilation of the aortic root and mild dilation of the ascending aorta. No images suggestive of intracardiac thrombus were seen. Drug toxicity? 4 upper gastrointestinal bleeding? Episode of melena on 12/01 without recurrence did not perform upper gastrointestinal endoscopy. Admission: 11/03/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 01/06/2021, stay: 02 m 02 d 23 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Previously hypertensive, pre-diabetic patient, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, with reduced blood supply (ischemic) stroke in 2020 and previous psychiatric return visit to doctor (follow-up) for panic syndrome. Was being followed up with a gastrosurgery team for choledocholithiasis with a videolaparoscopic cholecystectomy approach in 2019 and pre in 2020. Admitted to the hospital on 11/3/2020 for investigation of fever for 2 months associated with low back pain, weight loss and worsening renal function. In initial investigation exams, spine bone infection (spondylodiscitis) of t10t11 was evidenced by lumbar spine resonance on 11/4/20, being approached for decompression on 11/8/2020 and re-approached for lavage and surgical wound cleaning (debridement) on 11/29/20. In infectious screening, blood test to find infection (blood culture) showed on 11/04/20 with enterococus faecalis and transthoracic echocardiography on 11/09/20 with vegetation in the mitral valve, confirmed by transthoracic echocardiography on 11/16/20. Infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) with empirical tazocim was started on 11/04/20, being transitioned to ceftriaxone and ampicillin on 11/09/20 guided by blood test to find infection (blood culture) on 11/04 and escalated to vancomycin, polymyxin and tigecycline on 12/07/20 due to clinical worsening, guided by surgical site culture on 11/29 with growth of klebisiella multir pseudomonas multir. Underwent treatment until 12/14/20, with the last blood test to find infection (blood culture) on 12/07/20 negative, being considered treated infective infection of heart valves (endocarditis). Seen spine bone infection (spondylodiscitis) considered secondary to infection of heart valves (endocarditis), currently on maintenance treatment with oral amoxicillin 10ml 8/8h starting on 12/14/20. The patient after the first surgical approach, he evolved with the need for machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation) in a monitored bed, being extubated on 11/21/20 with creation of a breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) and exchange for a portex on 12/20/20, being decannulated on 01/02/21, without the need for supplemental oxygen support. Admitted to a general hospital room (ward) bed on 12/06, remaining no fever (afebrile), stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable). Sent home from hospital (discharge)d on 01/06 with a new ultrasound scheduled for 01/18 and outpatient return visit to doctor visits with the infectious disease, thoracic surgery, and orthopedics teams. Fracture of vertebrae t10t12. Abdominal and pelvic tomography 11/04 evaluation compromised by the absence of contrast: bilateral renal cysts, with no evidence of obstruction. Absence of collections and/or signs of intra-abdominal inflammatory processes. Musculoskeletal evaluation: fracture of l1.
