161 Hemorrhagic stroke. admission: 2020-06-05, discharge: 2020-07-09, stay: 01 m 03 d 22 h, location: ward, payer: SUS. [person] 50 years old, previously hypertensive, taking losartan. Upon arriving home from work at around 6:30 pm on 06/05, he began vomiting associated with mental confusion with subsequent hemiparesis. Brought by the SAMU team to [organization] with a windowed ischemic stroke. Upon admission to [organization], [person] was hypertensive, with blood pressure 220x120 mmHg, intarparenchymal hemorrhage, and 43 ml of thalamocapsular intraparenchymal hemorrhage. Nipride was started to control blood pressure. Underwent craniotomy to drain intraparenchymal hemorrhage. Extubated on 06/07. On 06/07, losartan 25 mg 12/12h and hydrochlorothiazide were started, but hypertensive requiring nipride. The patient developed a decrease in consciousness on 06/09 and was reintubated. [person] underwent decompressive craniectomy with a bone flap placed in the abdomen on 06/10. Blood pressure was controlled with nipride. The patient presented with fever on 06/12 and a new infectious screening was performed. Seizure on 06/13, controlled with diazepam and phenytoin attack. Treatment for ventilator-associated pneumonia was started on 06/16, guided by tracheal aspirate culture with growth of Klebsiella pneumoniae sensitive to meropenem. On 06/18, a central nervous system infection was suspected and vancomycin was started, in addition to adjusting the dose of meropenem for the central nervous system. Furthermore [person] remained febrile and with recurrent seizures. CSF was collected on 6/22 with: xanthochromic fluid, 20 cells, 968 red blood cells, 92% lymphocytes, glucose 86, protein 161, chloride 113. Lactate 2.7 [person] with recurrent use of nipride for blood pressure control, but it was observed that vasoactive drugs are only necessary after seizures with rapid weaning. Sedation was maintained since 6/18 due to recurrent seizures. On 6/25, phenobarbital was started and on 6/26, antibiotic therapy with polymyxin B and amikacin was started to treat Acinetobacter with growth in blood cultures. Sedation was suspended on 7/1 with [person] maintaining a Glasgow coma scale of 3t. [person] presenting with daily fever spikes and requested new infectious screening on 03/07 presented recurrence of tonic seizure with scanning gaze on 04/07, witnessed by the medical team with improvement after 1 ampoule of diazepam. [person] evolves maintaining daily fever spikes and suspected infectious worsening. due to worsening with suspected bloodstream infection, associated with vancomycin in the treatment, [person] evolves with acute kidney injury associated with septic condition use of nephrotoxic medications. performed electroencephalogram on 08/07: findings consistent with asymmetric encephalopathy, which may occur in the context of right hemisphere stroke; on 09/07 spoke with family members about the severity of the case with the assisting team. on the same day, [organization] spoke with family members and opted for palliative care. in the evening [person] evolves with septic shock, evolving to death at {omitted}. I contact {organization}. The on-call physician [person] advises me to fill out the death certificate. General physical examination: poor general condition, pale /4, hydrated, anicteric, acyanotic, afebrile. Cardiovascular: 2 rhythmic normal heart sounds without murmurs. Peripheral pulses present and symmetrical, normal amplitude. Capillary refill time < 3 sec. Pulmonary: vesicular breath sounds, but decreased at the bases, the decrease being greater on the right. Comfortable in macronebulizer. Abdomen: flat, bowel sounds but decreased, flaccid, tympanic, without masses and without visceromegaly. Sudden decompression negative. Extremities: no edema and no signs of deep vein thrombosis. Edema of the right upper limb. Neurological: unconscious. agitation scale sedation 5. glasgow coma scale: 3t rm1 ro1 rv1 pupils: Isochoric and photoreactive pupils rot upper limbs: brachial 2/4 triceps 2/4. lower limbs patellar 2/4 achilles 2/4 cardiopulmonary resuscitation: indifferent abdominal surgical wound: mild erythema with no other inflammatory signs. clean dressing. cranial surgical wound: good appearance. tomography showing intraparenchymal hemorrhage. craniotomy 06/06 and craniectomy 10/06. death. 09/12: methylprednisolone 90 mg/day 1 mg/kg salbutamol spray and terbutaline sc standard resistive schedule started on mechanical ventilation maintained sedation/analgesia and neuromuscular blockade 11/12: tracheostomy scheduling, dressing review for 12/12 tcle ok, 11/12 ccih: linezolid, tigecycline and meropenem for pneumonia associated with mechanical ventilation and skin infection, empirical 11/12: maintenance of shock, leukocytosis, new radiographic image. negative aspirate culture 12/12: fasting for reoperation 12/12: {omitted} 12/13: tracheostomy, debridement in operating room 12/13: hyponatremia 12/14: withdrawal of curarization, weaning of norepinephrine, increase in vancomycin dose from 1 g every 12 h to every 8 h vancocinemia 12/14 8.3 12/16: seizure about 30 s reintroduction of anti-seizure medications: valproic acid, carbamazepine, phenobarbital, reintroduction of anti-depressant medications: diazepam, quetiapine, levopromazine 12/17: new seizures lasting about 10 s {omitted} 12/18: catheter-associated bloodstream infection? device exchange, blood cultures, sending tip for culture 12/18: candida vulvovaginitis? start of fluconazole 12/19: loss of central venous access in left subclavian vein and puncture of right subclavian vein 12/19: dressing performed at bedside by {omitted} of [organization] [person] 12/21: loss of vacuum in dressing redone at bedside by [organization] with effective vacuum. 23/12: hemodynamic worsening, need for increased vasoactive drug and associated vasopressin 26/12: re-approach to surgical wound by plastic surgery 27/12: meropenem and vancomycin suspended, tigecycline maintained 27/12: phenobarbital transitioned to enteral route 28/12: fluconazole suspended 29/12: polyneuropathy of critically ill patient, failure to wean from ventilation in PSV, muscle weakness 29/12: ventilatory worsening due to bronchospasm 30/12: infectious surveillance 02/01 debridement and grafting in surgical wound in left inguinal region 03/01: fluid leak in surgical wound 03/01: indwelling urinary catheter changed 03/01: neurology evaluation requested 04/01: abdominal CT performed, awaiting report, dressing on bed [organization], with serous secretion, good appearance of the wound bed 01/05: discussed with [organization] start of polymyxin b for tracheobronchitis, cs? 01/16: start albumin furosemide 01/22: maintained shock secondary to central venous catheter, central venous catheter exchange, invasive arterial line. collection of tip cultures, blood cultures, start of empirical vancomycin 01/23: exchange of previous tracheostomy cannula 12/13 01/25: growth of pseudomonas in tracheal aspirate culture 01/23 provide uro 01/22 associated meropenem 01/25: follow-up appointmented to mechanical ventilation as {omitted} due to ventilatory fatigue, respiratory effort, with use of abdominal muscles. 01/29: ccih completion of polymyxin b and vancomycin. meropenem maintained for urinary tract infection and tracheitis. 01/30: start of fluconazole urinary tract infection 02/02: maintenance of need for mechanical ventilation 02/03: transfusion of 2 red blood cell concentrates, with transfusion reaction at the end tachycardia and hypertension 02/04: start of fludrocortisone due to suspected adrenal insufficiency 02/05: discontinued meropenem 02/06: macronebulization for 8 h 02/07: closure of the surgical wound in the left inguinal region with a left thigh flap 02/08: discontinued norepinephrine 02/08: macronebulization for 10 h 02/09: macronebulization for 12 h 02/11: peripherally inserted central catheter passed 02/11: macronebulization 02/12: follow-up appointment to mechanical ventilation at {omitted} 02/13: discontinued fluconazole 02/13: dressing of surgical wound. redone by plastic surgery 02/13: hydrocortisone decreased to 50 mg/day 02/15: runny nose: influenza and coronavirus collected. CCIH guidance: closed aspiration system, negative result 02/17: urinary tract infection? bloodstream infection secondary to central venous catheter? growth of providence at the tip of the central venous catheter 02/11, recurrence of fever, increased PCR start of cefepime on 02/17 and gentamicin on 02/18 replacement of indwelling urinary catheter due to lumps. presence of intense leukocyturia in urinalysis. awaiting urine culture in processing. 02/18: follow-up appointment to mechanical ventilation 02/19: dressing released to nursing team 02/20: severe runny nose 02/23: maintaining dependence on mechanical ventilation 02/25: cefepime and gentamicin suspended 02/27: follow-up appointment to progressive weaning from mechanical ventilation starting 4 h with 2 h increase each day 02/27: decrease fludrocortisone to 0.1 mg/day 02/28: macronebulization for 6 h 02/29: macronebulization for 8 h 03/01: macronebulization for 2 h 03/02: increase dose of bronchodilators and associated oral corticosteroid prednisone 20 mg/d 03/02: macronebulization for 10 h 03/03: macronebulization schedule for 12 h 03/04: tracheostomy change 04/03: did not tolerate macronebulization, cough after tracheostomy replacement. weaning from oral corticosteroid prednisone 10 mg/d 03/04: discharge requested to semi-intensive care unit of [organization] 03/05: subfebrile 1 fever leukocytosis: recultured and indwelling urinary catheter changed 03/05: macronebulization for 14 h 03/06: subfebrile 3 fevers, gram-negative bacillus in urine culture, start of tazocin 03/06: macronebulization for 11 h 03/07: isolation of tazocin-resistant pseudomonas in tracheal aspirate, klebsiella in urine culture: tazocin changed to cefepime 03/07: macronebulization for 9.2 h 03/08: macronebulization 11:30 h 03/15 03/15: cefepime weaning from bipab 03/17: hypotension after discontinuation of prednisone 5 mg. Patient , the patient 48 years old, weight 89 kg, height 1.57 m, admission 12/2, problem: rapidly progressing necrotizing fasciitis in the left inguinal region, serious blood infection (sepsis) with cutaneous focus, evolution: pus-filled swelling (abscess) in the left inguinal region, fever and purulent secretion, hemodynamic instability and decreased alertness level (level of consciousness), 12/2: wound surgical wound cleaning (debridement) 12/3: shock 12/4: admitted to the ICU (serious care room) 12/5: large amount of purulent secretion sent home from hospital (discharge) from surgical wound 12/5: reoperation indicated, but postponed due to acute pulmonary swelling (edema) and worsening ventilatory status. 12/7: surgical wound cleaning (debridement) of surgical wound in the surgical center, improving 12/8: severe extrapulmonary respiratory distress syndrome secondary to cutaneous infection? 09/12: methylprednisolone 90 mg/day 1 mg/kg salbutamol spray and terbutaline sc standard resistive schedule started on machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation) maintained sedation/analgesia and neuromuscular blockade 11/12: breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) scheduling, dressing review for 12/12 tcle ok, 11/12 ccih: linezolid, tigecycline and meropenem for lung infection (pneumonia) associated with machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation) and skin infection, empirical 11/12: maintenance of shock, high white blood cell count (leukocytosis), new radiographic image. 164 acute diverticulitis and hyponatremia. admission: 2020-06-28, discharge: 2020-08-05, stay: 01 m 08 d 08 h, location: ward, payer: sus. problem: acute abdomen with abdominal sepsis, taking ceftriaxone and metronidazole since 2020-06-28. acute abdominal pain and fever and diarrhea for 2 days. attempted videolaparoscopy, but adhesions and distension made the video procedure unfeasible. converted to exploratory laparotomy. 2020-06-29: peritoneostomy 01/07: peritoneostomy review, cavity lavage and creation of a new peritoneostomy, follow-up appointment from the surgical center with orotracheal intubation and sedation 02/07: evaluation by nutrology requested and total parenteral nutrition started. 03/07: peritoneostomy review surgical finding: clean cavity, without contamination presence of tumor in rectosigmoid transition topography adhered to the pelvis edematous intestinal loops, without suffering other organs without alterations performed rectosigmoidectomy stapled colon-rectal anastomosis creation of protective colostomy in transverse colon loop procedure performed without complications. 04/07: start of meropenem 06/07: fever plateau 07/07: start of vancomycin, central venous catheter change 08/07: inserted nasogastric tube, start of oral medication 09/07: discussed with [person], instructed to clamp nasogastric tube and observe abdomen 10/07: abdominal distension with regurgitation of gastric contents, opened nasogastric tube and left in drainage 11/07: extubated at 14:50 and reintubated at 23:30 07/12: tracheostomy requested 12/07: abdominal tomography, slow progression, significant inflammatory signs 13/07: pelvic collection punctured by [organization], awaiting cultures. tigecycline started 15/07: nasogastric tube clamped. good tolerance 07/15: desaturation and radiographic image in the left base; improvement in chest x-ray on 07/16; atelectasis. tracheal aspirate requested 07/16: trophic diet started 07/16: macronebulization at 08:15 hours 07/18: salt-wasting syndrome. fludrocortisone started 07/19: recultured and discharge from the intensive care unit requested. physical examination: good general condition, pale 4, hydrated, acyanotic, anicteric, afebrile; heart rate 78 beats per minute; respiratory rate 18 inspirations per minute; capillary refill time less than 3 seconds; oxygen saturation 95% on room air; vesicular breath soundss present and symmetrical, without adventitious sounds, without respiratory distress. regular heart rhythm in normal S1 and S2. I do not auscultate murmurs. abdomen: protuberant, flaccid, bowel sounds and normal activity. functioning ostomy for gas and feces. dry and clean surgical wound. extremities: warm, capillary refill time less than 3 seconds. lower limbs: free calves, without edema or signs of deep vein thrombosis. approaches: postoperative peritoneostomy on 06/29 postoperative peritoneostomy revision cavity washing and creation of a new peritoneostomy on 07/01 postoperative peritoneostomy revision rectosigmoidectomy stapled colorectal anastomosis protective transversostomy punctured pelvic collection by [organization] on 07/13/2020: awaiting cultures. started. 1. SIADH: moderate hyponatremia under investigation: probable etiology: associated with medication 2. hypocalcemia 3. postoperative recovery from peritonostomy for complicated diverticulitis. general impression: [person] maintaining sodium levels above 130 in a stable manner, with no neurological worsening, and with significant clinical improvement. [person] in condition for discharge with guidance given regarding warning signs and with outpatient segment. sodium check scheduled in a follow-up appointment with endocrinology as already discussed with the team aware team. in this appointment also scheduled to evaluate [person] regarding calcium metabolism with laboratory tests. [person] no follow-up appointment of fever spikes, with drop in CRP, no worsening of leukogram, no new complaints. also indicating conditions for discharge. thus: conducts: according to the endocrinological picture: outpatient follow-up appointment with endocrinology on 08/17/2020 at [organization] reassessment sodium calcium metabolism we maintain 2 calcium carbonate tablets per day vitamin d 7000 iu/day starting on 07/18/2020 post-surgical segment and multidisciplinary follow-up scheduled follow-up appointment at [organization] with nutrology on 10/06/2020. psychiatric follow-up already with referral to [organization], as carried out by [organization] as discussed with speech therapist, [person] has a segment schedule with sad. scheduled follow-up appointment at the speech therapist swallowing outpatient clinic on 08/30/2020. for now maintain previous diet as instructed. maintain fluid intake with cervical flexion maneuver and proposed exercises, 3x/day, according to the last speech evaluation. As discussed in conjunction with physical therapy, [person] with conditions for tracheostomy decannulation, we performed decannulation of [person] in conjunction with physical therapy and provided guidance on how to perform dressings in the cervical region after decannulation for better healing. I request an evaluation by the nursing team regarding the care provided to the sacral wound that was debrided yesterday without complications and regarding the colostomy bag, assessing the need for replacement and reinsertion of [person] and [person] regarding management and care. Guidance was provided regarding warning signs and the need to seek medical attention if CLC beeps appear upon discharge to schedule an outpatient follow-up. [person]. Acute diverticulitis and hyponatremia. Admission: 2020-06-28, sent home from hospital (discharge): 2020-08-05, stay: 01 m 08 d 08 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Problem: acute abdomen with abdominal serious blood infection (sepsis), taking ceftriaxone and metronidazole since 2020-06-28. Acute abdominal pain and fever and diarrhea for 2 days. Attempted videolaparoscopy, but adhesions and distension made the video procedure unfeasible. Converted to exploratory laparotomy. 2020-06-29: peritoneostomy 01/07: peritoneostomy review, cavity lavage and creation of a new peritoneostomy, return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment from the surgical center with orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) and sedation 02/07: evaluation by nutrology requested and total parenteral nutrition started. 03/07: peritoneostomy review surgical finding: clean cavity, without contamination presence of tumor in rectosigmoid transition topography adhered to the pelvis swellingtous intestinal loops, without suffering other organs without alterations performed rectosigmoidectomy stapled colon-rectal surgical joining of bowel ends (anastomosis) creation of protective colostomy in transverse colon loop procedure performed without complications. 04/07: start of meropenem 06/07: fever plateau 07/07: start of vancomycin, central venous catheter change 08/07: inserted nasogastric tube, start of oral medication 09/07: discussed with the patient, instructed to clamp nasogastric tube and observe abdomen 10/07: abdominal distension with regurgitation of gastric contents, opened nasogastric tube and left in drainage 11/07: extubated at 14:50 and reintubated at 23:30 07/12: breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) requested 12/07: abdominal tomography, slow progression, significant inflammatory signs 13/07: pelvic collection punctured by [organization], awaiting cultures. Tigecycline started 15/07: nasogastric tube clamped. Good tolerance 07/15: deoxygen level in blood (saturation) and radiographic image in the left base; improvement in chest x-ray on 07/16; atelectasis. Tracheal aspirate requested 07/16: trophic diet started 07/16: macronebulization at 08:15 hours 07/18: salt-wasting syndrome. Fludrocortisone started 07/19: recultured and sent home from hospital (discharge) from the ICU (serious care room) requested. Physical examination: good general condition, pale 4, hydrated, no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no fever (afebrile); heart rate 78 beats per minute; respiratory rate 18 inspirations per minute; capillary refill time less than 3 seconds; oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation) 95% on room air; normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds)s present and symmetrical, without adventitious sounds, without respiratory distress. Regular heart rhythm in normal s1 and s2. I do not auscultate murmurs. Abdomen: protuberant, flaccid, bowel sounds and normal activity. Functioning ostomy for gas and feces. Dry and clean surgical wound. Extremities: warm, capillary refill time less than 3 seconds. Lower limbs: free calves, without swelling (edema) or signs of deep vein blood clot (thrombosis). Approaches: postoperative peritoneostomy on 06/29 postoperative peritoneostomy revision cavity washing and creation of a new peritoneostomy on 07/01 postoperative peritoneostomy revision rectosigmoidectomy stapled colorectal surgical joining of bowel ends (anastomosis) protective transversostomy punctured pelvic collection by [organization] on 07/13/2020: awaiting cultures. Started. 1. Siadh: moderate hyponatremia under investigation: probable cause of disease (etiology): associated with medication 2. Hypocalcemia 3. Postoperative recovery from peritonostomy for complicated diverticulitis. General impression: the patient maintaining sodium levels above 130 in a stable manner, with no neurological worsening, and with significant clinical improvement. The patient in condition for sent home from hospital (discharge) with guidance given regarding warning signs and with outpatient segment. Sodium check scheduled in a return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment with endocrinology as already discussed with the team aware team. In this appointment also scheduled to evaluate the patient regarding calcium metabolism with laboratory tests. The patient no return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment of fever spikes, with drop in crp, no worsening of leukogram, no new complaints. Also indicating conditions for sent home from hospital (discharge). Thus: conducts: according to the endocrinological picture: outpatient return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment with endocrinology on 08/17/2020 at [organization] reassessment sodium calcium metabolism we maintain 2 calcium carbonate tablets per day vitamin d 7000 iu/day starting on 07/18/2020 post-surgical segment and multidisciplinary return visit to doctor (follow-up) scheduled return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment at [organization] with nutrology on 10/06/2020. Psychiatric return visit to doctor (follow-up) already with referral to [organization], as carried out by [organization] as discussed with speech therapist, the patient systemic arterial high BP a segment schedule with sad. Scheduled return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment at the speech therapist swallowing OPD (Out Patient Department) on 08/30/2020. For now maintain previous diet as instructed. Maintain fluid intake with cervical flexion maneuver and proposed exercises, 3x/day, according to the last speech evaluation. As discussed in conjunction with physical therapy, the patient with conditions for breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) decannulation, we performed decannulation of the patient in conjunction with physical therapy and provided guidance on how to perform dressings in the cervical region after decannulation for better healing. I request an evaluation by the nursing team regarding the care provided to the sacral wound that was debrided yesterday without complications and regarding the colostomy bag, assessing the need for replacement and reinsertion of the patient and the patient regarding management and care. Guidance was provided regarding warning signs and the need to seek medical attention if clc beeps appear upon sent home from hospital (discharge) to schedule an outpatient return visit to doctor (follow-up). The patient. e87. other disorders of fluid, electrolyte and acid-base balance. l984. chronic skin ulcer, not elsewhere classified. e871. hyposmolarity and hyponatraemia. f321. moderate depressive episode. d51. vitamin b12 deficiency anaemia. d52. folate deficiency anaemia. e50. vitamin a deficiency. e539. vitamin b deficiency, unspecified. e55. vitamin d deficiency. e780. pure hypercholesterolaemia. e832. zinc metabolism disorders. e833. phosphorus metabolism disorders. e835. calcium metabolism disorders. r19. other symptoms and signs relating to digestive system and abdomen. a419. septicaemia ne. j189. pneumonia ne. k57. diverticular disease of intestine. r100. acute abdomen. alive 12.6 1.18
165 admission: 07/11/2020, discharge: 08/02/2020, stay: 21 d 19 h, location: ward, payer: sus. current: [person] was admitted to this service on 07/10, complaining of pain in the right thoracic region anteriorly and bulging in the cervical region, in addition to fever and chills. was admitted on 06/15, due to veno-occlusive crisis, remaining hospitalized until 06/28. during admission, an infectious focus search was performed, with blood culture showing 06/15: staphylococcus lentus oxar, sensitive to tigecycline, teicoplanin, linezolid, rifampicin, and vancomycin; ceftriaxone and vancomycin were performed for 10 days, d0 06/18. Identified on tomography image compatible with peritonsillar and thoracic abscess, being started empirically with vancomycin and meropenem until culture results, based on previous blood culture. Abscess drained by [organization] on 07/12 without complications. Lung abscess drained on 07/17 with drainage of 01 ml of secretion. Cultures of secretions showed no growth of germs, I maintain under the guidance of [organization] maintenance of antibiotic therapy, since it has already been started, although germ demonstrated in blood culture is not considered an agent of infection. During hospitalization, extensive pityriasis versicolosis was identified, which was treated with 500 mg of fluconazole orally on 07/23. Shows progressive improvement of hypochromic spots. [person] evolved in good general condition, with good response to antibiotic therapy, being re-discussed tomography after 03 weeks. During the discussion, the hypothesis of Lemierre's syndrome was raised, in which the causative agent responds well to narrow-spectrum antibiotics. Antibiotic therapy was deescalated to meropenem and vancomycin on August 1, and the patient was observed for one day. He was discharged today in good general condition, afebrile, with no complaints of pain, cough or cervical swelling. He denied other complaints. We prescribed the medication at the day hospital, he was discharged with the medications for home use, and follow-up appointmented next {omitted} for an outpatient reevaluation. problem: sickle cell anemia with poor adherence, had not taken medication for 30 days, recurrent vaso-occlusive crises in {omitted} 2012 {omitted} 2017 bilateral avascular necrosis of the femoral head 2012, pityriasis versicolor treated, cholecystolithiasis choledocholithiasis treated with preoperative cardiac arrest in {omitted} 2018 by general surgery, medications at home, folic acid benzathine penicillin 21/21d discontinued use on own hydrea: 4 tablets/day increased dose on 7/11/14 32.2 mg/kg/day, relevant tests chest x-ray 7/10: no evidence of infiltrate or pulmonary condensation, neck tomography 7/11: check definitive neck tomography report: extensive pharyngeal inflammatory process extending inferiorly to the supraglottic region, highlighting image compatible with Right tonsillar abscess. Reactive cervical lymph nodes, including retropharyngeal ones. Opacity in the upper lobe of the right lung, new in relation to the previous examination on 06/19 and with a central necrotic aspect, therefore being suspicious for necrotizing pneumonia, abscessed. No images suggestive of thrombophlebitis of cervical vessels were identified. Other findings described above. Transthoracic echocardiography 07/13: good quality examination, with good window. Moderate dilation of left chambers and mild dilation of right chambers. Normal systolic performance and segmental mobility of the left ventricle. Preserved systolic performance of the right ventricle. Mild insufficiency of the mitral and tricuspid valves. Absence of images suggestive of vegetation in the present examination. Complete blood count 07/10: with leukocytosis and left shift. Blood culture 06/15 Previous hospitalization: with growth of Staphylococcus lentus oxar, abdominal ultrasound 06/16 Presence of biliary lithiasis, no signs of dilatation of the intra and extrahepatic biliary tract, transthoracic echocardiography 06/21 Moderate dilatation of the left chambers with indexed left atrium of 44 ml/m2 and ejection fraction of 66%, medications currently in use Meropenem Vancomycin in 07/11 Ended 07/01 Clindamycin 600 mg 8/8h Ciprofloxacin 500 mg 12/12h in 08/01 Scheduled for 14 days Scheduled Clindamycin 600 mg 88h Ciprofloxacin 500 mg 12/12h for 13 days. follow-up appointment on the next two Wednesdays for reactivation, immediate follow-up appointment if necessary. Admission: 07/11/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 08/02/2020, stay: 21 d 19 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Current: the patient was admitted to this service on 07/10, complaining of pain in the right thoracic region anteriorly and bulging in the cervical region, in addition to fever and chills. Was admitted on 06/15, due to veno-occlusive crisis, remaining hospitalized until 06/28. During admission, an infectious focus search was performed, with blood test to find infection (blood culture) showing 06/15: staphylococcus lentus oxar, sensitive to tigecycline, teicoplanin, linezolid, rifampicin, and vancomycin; ceftriaxone and vancomycin were performed for 10 days, d0 06/18. Identified on tomography image compatible with peritonsillar and thoracic pus-filled swelling (abscess), being started empirically with vancomycin and meropenem until culture results, based on previous blood test to find infection (blood culture). Pus-filled swelling (abscess) drained by [organization] on 07/12 without complications. Lung pus-filled swelling (abscess) drained on 07/17 with drainage of 01 ml of secretion. Cultures of secretions showed no growth of germs, i maintain under the guidance of [organization] maintenance of infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy), since it systemic arterial high BP (hypertension) already been started, although germ demonstrated in blood test to find infection (blood culture) is not considered an agent of infection. During hospitalization, extensive pityriasis versicolosis was identified, which was treated with 500 mg of fluconazole by mouth (orally) on 07/23. Shows progressive improvement of hypochromic spots. The patient evolved in good general condition, with good response to infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy), being re-discussed tomography after 03 weeks. During the discussion, the hypothesis of lemierre's syndrome was raised, in which the causative agent responds well to narrow-spectrum infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic)s. Infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) was deescalated to meropenem and vancomycin on august 1, and the patient was observed for one day. He was sent home from hospitald today in good general condition, no fever (afebrile), with no complaints of pain, cough or cervical swelling. He denied other complaints. We prescribed the medication at the day hospital, he was sent home from hospitald with the medications for home use, and return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointmented next for an outpatient reevaluation. Problem: sickle cell low blood (anemia) with poor adherence, had not taken medication for 30 days, recurrent vaso-occlusive crises in 2012 2017 bilateral avascular tissue death (necrosis) of the femoral head 2012, pityriasis versicolor treated, cholecystolithiasis choledocholithiasis treated with preoperative heart stopped beating (cardiac arrest) in 2018 by general surgery, medications at home, folic acid benzathine penicillin 21/21d discontinued use on own hydrea: 4 tablets/day increased dose on 7/11/14 32.2 mg/kg/day, relevant tests chest x-ray 7/10: no evidence of infiltrate or pulmonary condensation, neck tomography 7/11: check definitive neck tomography report: extensive pharyngeal inflammatory process extending inferiorly to the supraglottic region, highlighting image compatible with right tonsillar pus-filled swelling (abscess). Reactive cervical lymph nodes, including retropharyngeal ones. Opacity in the upper lobe of the right lung, new in relation to the previous examination on 06/19 and with a central necrotic aspect, therefore being government health scheme (sus)picious for necrotizing lung infection (pneumonia), pus-filled swelling (abscess)ed. No images suggestive of thrombophlebitis of cervical vessels were identified. Other findings described above. Transthoracic echocardiography 07/13: good quality examination, with good window. Moderate dilation of left chambers and mild dilation of right chambers. Normal systolic performance and segmental mobility of the left ventricle. Preserved systolic performance of the right ventricle. Mild insufficiency of the mitral and tricuspid valves. Absence of images suggestive of vegetation in the present examination. Complete blood count 07/10: with high white blood cell count (leukocytosis) and left shift. Blood test to find infection (blood culture) 06/15 previous hospitalization: with growth of staphylococcus lentus oxar, abdominal ultrasound 06/16 presence of biliary lithiasis, no signs of dilatation of the intra and extrahepatic biliary tract, transthoracic echocardiography 06/21 moderate dilatation of the left chambers with indexed left atrium of 44 ml/m2 and ejection fraction of 66%, medications currently in use meropenem vancomycin in 07/11 ended 07/01 clindamycin 600 mg 8/8h ciprofloxacin 500 mg 12/12h in 08/01 scheduled for 14 days scheduled clindamycin 600 mg 88h ciprofloxacin 500 mg 12/12h for 13 days. Return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment on the next two wednesdays for reactivation, immediate return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment if necessary. 169 [person], 67 years old, retired, from {city} problem: 1. sepsis of urinary focus? dysuria pollakiuria 1 week ago treatment with acriflavine methenamine methylthioninium atropa belladonna ceftriaxone d1 07/30/2020 urine culture: negative. blood culture negative to date urine 1: could not be evaluated 2. bre sca? old? tropo 269 138 considered secondary to sepsis acetylsalicylic acid clopidogrel enoxa at the origin discussed with cardiology on 07/30 echocardiogram 08/03: cardiac chambers of normal dimensions. systolic performance at the lower limit of normal. segmental mobility of the left ventricle: septal dyssynergia preserved systolic performance of the right ventricle mild dilation of the aortic root and its ascending and transverse portions history of current illness: I admit the patient, hemodynamically stable without the use of vasoactive drugs, sedated with midazolam and fentanyl, in orotracheal intubation. diuresis 100 ml present in an indwelling urinary catheter, greenish in color. personal history: comorbidities: systemic arterial hypertension, hydrocele, continuous-use medications: losartan 50 mg 12/12 h / atenolol 25 mg 12/12h, habits: smoker 2 packs a day for 52 years, alcoholic 2 doses of cachaça a day for 2 years allergies: denies previous surgeries: orthopedic {person} cannot specify dates, antibiotic therapy: ceftriaxone daily 07/30 complementary exams: echocardiogram 08/03: cardiac chambers of normal dimensions. systolic performance at the lower limit of normal. segmental mobility of the left ventricle: septal dyssynergia preserved systolic performance of the right ventricle mild dilation of the aortic root and its ascending and transverse portions ultrasound of the kidneys and pathways 07/30: sonographically normal kidneys. admission: 07/30/2020, discharge: 08/03/2020, stay: 04 d 05 h, location: ward, payer: sus. history of current illness: [person] reports that he started experiencing dysuria and pollakiuria, in addition to abdominal pain, associated with fever; he does not know how to report the temperature or how many episodes. initially he did not seek medical help and was using the urinary tract antiseptic cystex acriflavine methenamine methylthioninium atropa belladonna. yesterday, his condition worsened significantly and he sought medical help. in a medical unit, he developed hypotension, sweating, and a decrease in the level of consciousness at the origin, in addition to dyspnea, requiring orotracheal intubation and initiation of vasoactive drugs. electrocardiogram tracing at the origin, which showed left bundle branch block. acetylsalicylic acid, clopiogrel, and enoxaparin were administered. A loading dose of ceftriaxone, 2 g, was also administered at 4:00 a.m. on July 30. [person] reports that when he transported [person], he was taking norepinephrine 5 ml/h, but with a mean blood pressure of 95. The vasoactive drug was withdrawn before transport. A pocus was performed: B lines were present in the lung bases. The heart was difficult to assess, but the left ventricle appeared to be hypocontractile. The patient was in good general condition, flushed, hydrated, anicteric, acyanotic, and afebrile. Respiratory auscultation revealed bilateral vesicular breath soundss without adventitious sounds. Eupneic on room air. Cardiac auscultation revealed normal rhythmic heart sounds in two beats, without murmurs. Abdomen: globular, bowel sounds, slightly painful on deep palpation. No visceromegaly. Negative Giordano test. Extremities: no edema in the lower limbs, no signs of deep vein thrombosis. Systolic performance at the lower limit of normal. Segmental mobility of the left ventricle: septal dyssynergia preserved systolic performance of the right ventricle mild dilation of the aortic root and its ascending and transverse portions history of current illness: i admit the patient, stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable) without the use of vasoactive drugs, sedated with midazolam and fentanyl, in orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation). Diuresis 100 ml present in an indwelling urinary catheter, greenish in color. Personal history: comorbidities: systemic arterial high BP (hypertension), hydrocele, continuous-use medications: losartan 50 mg 12/12 h / atenolol 25 mg 12/12h, habits: smoker 2 packs a day for 52 years, alcoholic 2 doses of cachaça a day for 2 years allergies: denies previous surgeries: orthopedic {person} cannot specify dates, infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy): ceftriaxone daily 07/30 complementary exams: heart ultrasound (echocardiogram) 08/03: cardiac chambers of normal dimensions. Segmental mobility of the left ventricle: septal dyssynergia preserved systolic performance of the right ventricle mild dilation of the aortic root and its ascending and transverse portions ultrasound of the kidneys and pathways 07/30: sonographically normal kidneys. Admission: 07/30/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 08/03/2020, stay: 04 d 05 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. History of current illness: the patient reports that he started experiencing dysuria and pollakiuria, in addition to abdominal pain, associated with fever; he does not know how to report the temperature or how many episodes. Initially he did not seek medical help and was using the urinary tract antiseptic cystex acriflavine methenamine methylthioninium atropa belladonna. Yesterday, his condition worsened significantly and he sought medical help. In a medical unit, he developed low BP (hypotension), sweating, and a decrease in the alertness level (level of consciousness) at the origin, in addition to difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), requiring orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) and initiation of vasoactive drugs. ECG/heart tracing (electrocardiogram) tracing at the origin, which showed left bundle branch block. Acetylsalicylic acid, clopiogrel, and enoxaparin were give (administer)ed. A loading dose of ceftriaxone, 2 g, was also give (administer)ed at 4:00 a.m. On july 30. The patient reports that when he transported the patient, he was taking norepinephrine 5 ml/h, but with a mean blood pressure of 95. The vasoactive drug was withdrawn before transport. A pocus was performed: b lines were present in the lung bases. The heart was difficult to assess, but the left ventricle appeared to be hypocontractile. The patient was in good general condition, flushed, hydrated, no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), and no fever (afebrile). Respiratory auscultation revealed bilateral normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds)s without adventitious sounds. Breathing normally (eupneic) on room air. Cardiac auscultation revealed normal rhythmic heart sounds in two beats, without murmurs. Abdomen: globular, bowel sounds, slightly painful on deep palpation. No visceromegaly. Negative giordano test. Extremities: no swelling (edema) in the lower limbs, no signs of deep vein blood clot (thrombosis). Full and symmetrical pulses. Negative urine culture. Negative blood test to find infection (blood culture) so far. Renal: the patient continues to improve renal function with spontaneous diuresis after removal of indwelling urinary catheter. 170 [person], 65 years old, from [address] anthropometry height: 164 cm weight: 106 kg predicted weight: 56.1 kg bmi 39. admission: 08/12/2020, discharge: 09/28/2020, stay: 01 m 15 d 20 h, location: ward, payer: sus. problem 1 severe acute respiratory syndrome due to coronavirus, onset of symptoms: 08/05 dry cough, odynophagia, myalgia, diarrhea, anosmia worsening of symptoms: 08/12 dyspnea pain in the right hemithorax po2/fio2 ratio on admission: 61.8 orotracheal intubation 08/15 paraf pre orotracheal intubation 75.7 first proning 08/15 for 16 hours paraf 150 220 second proning on 08/29 third proning on 08/21 11:10 pre: 147 during: 323 fourth proning on 09/03 paraf: 150 300 extubated on 09/09 oxygen turned off on 09/18 at 10:00 am, 2 pulmonary thromboembolism ruled out CT Angiography (CTA) 08/19: no evidence of pulmonary thromboembolism, lower limb ultrasound without deep vein thrombosis, 3 corrected hyperglycemia in critically ill patients, critically ill coronavirus, use of corticosteroids, previous use of insulin pump, 4 resolved septic shock with cutaneous pulmonary focus, new catheter infection by klebsiella pneumoniae carbapenemase multir, growth in tracheal aspirate and catheter tip, 5 primary hyperparathyroidism vitamin d deficiency in replacement: corrected hypercalcemia secondary to primary hyperparathyroidism administered zoledronic acid 4 mg intravenously on 09/11/2020. Hyperparathyroidism secondary to vitamin D deficiency was ruled out, since in these cases the elevation of PTH is concomitant with normocalcemia, which evolved with severe hypophosphatemia and required intravenous replacement. It was decided to correct vitamin D deficiency with an attack of 50,000 IU/week for 8 weeks. D1 on 9/21. Schedule: outpatient follow-up with the endocrinologist to investigate primary hyperparathyroidism and vitamin D at a maintenance dose of 1000 IU/day after the end of the attack for correction. 7 Areflexic flaccid tetraparesis Polyneuromyopathy of the critically ill patient. Skull tomography 9/17: Neurovascular causes ruled out. by neurology on 09/18: scheduled outpatient electromyography and awaiting electroencephalogram during hospitalization, [person] undergoing motor physiotherapy for rehabilitation, 8 urinary tract infection by multiresistant germ in treatment, urine culture 09/18/2020: multiresistant klebisiella pneumoniae, torgena amikacin d0 09/20 scheduled until 09/27 last fever peak on 09/21/2020, 9 probable edm started sertraline on 09/17 with schedule to increase to 50 mg/day on 09/27, 10 anemia of chronic disease vitamin b12 and folic acid in processing, corticotherapy: dexamethasone 6 mg/day 12 08/19 methylprednisolone 08/23 09/14 prednisone 40 mg 09/15 started weaning on 09/15, with a schedule to reduce the dose by 50% every 7 days. By the endocrinologist on 09/14: [person] with a diagnosis of primary hyperparathyroidism. Hyperparathyroidism secondary to vitamin D deficiency was ruled out since in these cases the elevation of PTH is concomitant with normocalcemia and not hypercalcemia. Vitamin D deficiency is probably secondary to excessive activation of the renal enzyme 1alphahydroxylase, which converts 25 ohvitd to 125 oh vitamin D. reduction of vitamin D dose to 1000 iu/day, 20 drops maintenance dose, zoledronic acid at a dose of 4 mg intravenously on 09/11/2020. 7 areflexic flaccid tetraparesis polyneuromyopathy of [person]? as [person] presented bradyarrhythmia and hemodynamic instability, a head tomography was requested to rule out neurovascular causes. cranial computed tomography 09/17: no changes by [organization] awaiting final report by [organization] on 09/18: diagnostic hypothesis: polyneuromyopathy of the critically ill patient, metabolic encephalopathy to be clarified? previous low cognitive reserve in the context of prolonged hospitalization? subclinical status epilepticus? outpatient electroneuromyography scheduled, [organization] will contact you to schedule, due to the pandemic. regular general condition, pale 1/4, hydrated, acyanotic, afebrile neurology: conscious and oriented in time and space respiratory auscultation symmetrical vesicular breath sounds, without adventitious sounds, symmetrical chest expansion, no signs of respiratory distress. oxygen saturation: 96% in room air. cardiac auscultation: regular rhythm in two beats, normal phonetic sounds without murmurs. heart rate: 98 beats per minute. blood pressure 120x80 mmhg globular abdomen, normal bowel sounds, flaccid, painless on superficial and deep palpation, no masses or visceromegaly palpable tympanic on percussion. extremities: lower limbs without edema, no signs of deep vein thrombosis, pedal pulses present, broad and symmetrical. grade 2 strength in upper and lower limbs. [person] with metabolic condition AE, presenting hypophosphatemia, hypomagnesemia and hypokalemia, in addition to hypovitaminosis D, during hospitalization. with need to investigate causes of hypovitaminosis D. in slight improvement of DHE. scheduled follow-up appointment with endocrinology for investigation. requested investigation to be checked in outpatient: PTN/CR ratio, GSA, TSH, urinary CA, urinary phosphorus, electrolytes and renal function requested tests for outpatient collection of primary hypoparathyroidism. perform ultrasound of parathyroid and urinary tract in outpatient before cn with ena ultrasound of kidneys and outpatient DMO in outpatient already scheduled next dose of vitamin D 5000 IU today, I prescribe. I prescribe 5000 IU on Mondays, for 6 weeks, as directed by the endocrinologist. Regarding corticosteroid therapy: I continue to taper off prednisone at 10 mg/day, then reduce to 5 mg/day on 10/3. After that, from 10/4 to 10/4, I continue to taper off at 5 mg/day. Then, from 10/4 to 10/16, I stop taking it at 2.5 mg/day. On 10/17, I stop taking it. I continue to take omeprazole at 40 mg once a day. Anemia due to chronic disease is prophylaxis due to corticosteroid therapy until the end of CTC. I continue to take oral potassium replacement therapy with 6% KCl, 10ml every 8 hours for 3 days. Regarding diabetes mellitus: glycosylated HB 6.33. I am writing a report for monitoring diabetes mellitus at a basic health unit. I am discharged without insulin therapy. I am evaluating oral antidiabetics. Regarding deep vein thrombosis prophylaxis, improve 5: high risk, deep vein thrombosis prophylaxis indicated. I prescribe rivaroxaban 10 mg/day for 30 days. Other: I continue taking sertraline 50 mg as directed. Regarding the urinary tract infection: I finished antibiotic therapy yesterday with amikacin, [person] has been afebrile since 9/21, with clinical and laboratory improvement. We opted for hospital discharge today. Upon discharge: [person] with improve score 5, therefore high risk of thrombosis. Upon discharge, we suggest prophylactic anticoagulation after coronavirus with one of the following options: rivaroxaban 10 mg/day for 30 days or enoxaparin 40 mg/day for 30 days or unfractionated heparin 5000 IU 8/8h for 30 days. Follow-up appointments: outpatient follow-up with neurology for electroneuromyography. We will contact you. follow-up appointment to Aminc for lab tests on 12/11; follow-up appointment to endocrinology on 17/11. Regarding the urinary tract infection: antibiotic therapy ended yesterday [person], patient has been afebrile since 21/09, with clinical and laboratory improvement. Hyperparathyroidism secondary to vitamin d deficiency was ruled out, since in these cases the elevation of pth is concomitant with normocalcemia, which evolved with severe hypophosphatemia and required given through a vein (drip/IV) replacement. It was decided to correct vitamin d deficiency with an attack of 50,000 iu/week for 8 weeks. D1 on 9/21. 20 mg 101 metformin 1 tablet/day. By the endocrinologist on 09/14: the patient with a diagnosis of primary hyperparathyroidism. Hyperparathyroidism secondary to vitamin d deficiency was ruled out since in these cases the elevation of pth is concomitant with normocalcemia and not hypercalcemia. Vitamin d deficiency is probably secondary to excessive activation of the renal enzyme 1alphahydroxylase, which converts 25 ohvitd to 125 oh vitamin d. Reduction of vitamin d dose to 1000 iu/day, 20 drops maintenance dose, zoledronic acid at a dose of 4 mg given through a vein (drip/IV)ly on 09/11/2020. 7 areflexic flaccid tetraparesis polyneuromyopathy of the patient? Regular general condition, pale 1/4, hydrated, no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no fever (afebrile) neurology: conscious and oriented in time and space respiratory auscultation symmetrical normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds), without adventitious sounds, symmetrical chest expansion, no signs of respiratory distress. Oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation): 96% in room air. Cardiac auscultation: regular rhythm in two beats, normal phonetic sounds without murmurs. Heart rate: 98 beats per minute. Blood pressure 120x80 mmhg globular abdomen, normal bowel sounds, flaccid, painless on superficial and deep palpation, no masses or visceromegaly palpable tympanic on percussion. Extremities: lower limbs without swelling (edema), no signs of deep vein blood clot (thrombosis), pedal pulses present, broad and symmetrical. Grade 2 strength in upper and lower limbs. The patient with metabolic condition ae, presenting hypophosphatemia, hypomagnesemia and hypokalemia, in addition to hypovitaminosis d, during hospitalization. With need to investigate causes of hypovitaminosis d. In slight improvement of dhe. Scheduled return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment with endocrinology for investigation. I continue to take oral potassium replacement therapy with 6% kcl, 10ml every 8 hours for 3 days. Regarding sugar disease (diabetes): glycosylated hb 6.33. I am writing a report for monitoring sugar disease (diabetes) at a basic health unit. I am sent home from hospital (discharge)d without insulin therapy. I am evaluating oral antidiabetics. 171 [person], 59 years old. companion: [person] admission 07/07/2020 [organization] discharge from ward 07/27/2020 proportionate palliative care defined 02/25, 03/14, 07/11 limited therapeutic support avoidance of obstinate measures {omitted}. problem 1 purulent collection in hypogastrium bladder diverticulum? abdominal tomography 07/09: large expansile cystic formation in hypogastrium and right iliac fossa, with fine internal septations, measuring 8.5x8.4x5.3 cm, with an estimated volume of 192 ml. extensive contact with the anterior wall of the bladder, with no identification of evident areas of communication between the two structures. contact with the wall of the sigmoid and ascending colon, without determining stenosis of their lumens. puncture [organization] on 07/12 120 ml of citrine fluid and 80 ml of purulent fluid immediate post-puncture control with ultrasound: minimal residual collection, insufficient volume for drain insertion. awaiting culture and cytology abdominal mass culture 07/12: psudomonas sensitive to meropenem. control abdominal ultrasound 07/19: recollection of approximately 200 ml in previous hypogastric collection infected urachal cyst? bladder diverticulum? colonic diverticulum? cystic neoplasm, abscessed? new puncture by [organization] on 07/22 with placement of abdominal drain. culture 07/22/2020: negative creatinine 1.00, urea 72 of the punctured fluid methylene blue test through indwelling urinary catheter by surgery 07/24: positive abdominal drain removal on 07/27/2020 without complications. 2 aspiration pneumonia only chronic bronchoaspiration? urinary tract infection, chronic colonization? resolved elevated pcr leukocytosis without deviation bilateral consolidation chest computed tomography urine culture 07/07: pseudomonas aeruginosa multi s. meropenem d0 09/07 25/07: 7-day treatment for pulmonary focus and 14 days for complicated lower urinary tract infection infected bladder diverticulum 3 anemia to be clarified without externalization of bleeding May: hb 12.4 09/07: hb 5.4. deficiency? of inflammation? tx 2 ch on 09/07 iron profile / b12 and b9 dosage performed after transfusion tx 2 packed red blood cells, on 22/07: hb 6.8 10.4. admission: 07/07/2020, discharge: 07/27/2020, stay: 19 d 20 h, location: ward, payer: sus. [person] admitted on 07/07/2020 for investigation of an abdominal mass in the hypogastrium associated with a decline in general condition, and antibiotic therapy with meropenem was started, guided by a previous urine culture from 05/22/2020 with klebisiella pneumoniae. abdominal tomography performed with evidence of a collection in contact with the bladder, images suggestive of aspiration bronchopneumonia, and findings suggestive of secondary neoplastic involvement. regarding bronchopneumonia: started [person] on 07/09/2020 and treatment was carried out for 7 days as directed by [organization], with clinical improvement thereafter. regarding neoplastic screening: [person] with a proportional palliative proposal. defined together with [organization], family and [person] himself not to follow up with neoplastic screening, seeing [person] not performing well to undergo eventual oncological treatment. regarding the abdominal collection: puncture performed by rint on 07/12/2020 with the output of 80 ml of purulent secretion, with growth of pseudomonas sensitive to [person]. performed abdominal ultrasound on 07/19/2020 for control with evidence of recollection in previous topography, discussed with [organization] and opted for a new puncture and placement of a drain, as it is considered beneficial as a way of controlling the infectious focus in accordance with goals defined by palliative care. in joint follow-up with general surgery, methylene blue test performed, with findings suggestive of the collection being an infected bladder diverticulum, with adequate antibiotic therapy coverage with meropenem for 14 days, considering complicated lower urinary tract infection. suspended meropenem on 07/25/2020. in images taken after abdominal drainage, findings suggestive of a bladder fistula into the abdominal cavity were seen, with initial indication for surgery. {omitted}. thus, seeing [person] hemodynamically stable, afebrile, without respiratory distress, with excellent family support, we opted, together with [person] and [person], for outpatient follow-up of the condition with the neurogenic bladder team at {organization} and management of an indwelling urinary catheter. general cultures i material: abdominal mass collected: 22/07/2020 made available: 25/07/2020 released: 25/07/2020 sample element a result. absence of growth after 48 hours of incubation validated by.: [person]. collection puncture in hypogastrium by [organization] on 12/07/2020 and 22/07/2020. hospital discharge procedures I maintain analgesia with tramadol 50 mg 8/8h for better analgesia control. I monitor the evacuation pattern. I maintain measures for abdominal distension optimized for [address] simethicone and domperidone. I advise maintenance of other medications previously used. I advise care with an indwelling urinary catheter. I advise care with an occlusive dressing in the site of a previous abdominal drain. I advise warning signs and seeking emergency care if necessary. outpatient follow-up outpatient follow-up [person] aware on 08/12/2020. Companion: the patient admission 07/07/2020 [organization] sent home from hospital (discharge) from general hospital room (ward) 07/27/2020 proportionate comfort-focused (not curative) care defined 02/25, 03/14, 07/11 limited therapeutic support avoidance of obstinate measures . Problem 1 purulent collection in hypogastrium bladder diverticulum? Abdominal tomography 07/09: large expansile cystic formation in hypogastrium and right iliac fossa, with fine internal septations, measuring 8.5x8.4x5.3 cm, with an estimated volume of 192 ml. Extensive contact with the anterior wall of the bladder, with no identification of evident areas of communication between the two structures. Contact with the wall of the sigmoid and ascending colon, without determining stenosis of their lumens. Puncture [organization] on 07/12 120 ml of citrine fluid and 80 ml of purulent fluid immediate post-puncture control with ultrasound: minimal residual collection, insufficient volume for drain insertion. Awaiting culture and cytology abdominal mass culture 07/12: psudomonas sensitive to meropenem. Control abdominal ultrasound 07/19: recollection of approximately 200 ml in previous hypogastric collection infected urachal cyst? Bladder diverticulum? Colonic diverticulum? Cystic neoplasm, pus-filled swelling (abscess)ed? New puncture by [organization] on 07/22 with placement of abdominal drain. Culture 07/22/2020: negative kidney function marker (creatinine) 1.00, urea 72 of the punctured fluid methylene blue test through indwelling urinary catheter by surgery 07/24: positive abdominal drain removal on 07/27/2020 without complications. 2 lung infection from inhaling food/liquid (aspiration lung infection (pneumonia)) only chronic bronchoaspiration? Urine infection (UTI), chronic colonization? Resolved elevated pcr high white blood cell count without deviation bilateral consolidation chest CT scan (computed tomography) urine culture 07/07: pseudomonas aeruginosa multi s. Meropenem d0 09/07 25/07: 7-day treatment for pulmonary focus and 14 days for complicated lower urine infection (UTI) infected bladder diverticulum 3 low blood (anemia) to be clarified without externalization of bleeding may: hb 12.4 09/07: hb 5.4. Deficiency? Of inflammation? Tx 2 ch on 09/07 iron profile / b12 and b9 dosage performed after transfusion tx 2 packed red blood cells, on 22/07: hb 6.8 10.4. Admission: 07/07/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 07/27/2020, stay: 19 d 20 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. The patient admitted on 07/07/2020 for investigation of an abdominal mass in the hypogastrium associated with a decline in general condition, and infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) with meropenem was started, guided by a previous urine culture from 05/22/2020 with klebisiella lung infection (pneumonia)e. Abdominal tomography performed with evidence of a collection in contact with the bladder, images suggestive of aspiration broncholung infection (pneumonia), and findings suggestive of secondary neoplastic involvement. Regarding broncholung infection (pneumonia): started the patient on 07/09/2020 and treatment was carried out for 7 days as directed by [organization], with clinical improvement thereafter. Regarding neoplastic screening: the patient with a proportional comfort-focused (not curative) proposal. Defined together with [organization], family and the patient himself not to follow up with neoplastic screening, seeing the patient not performing well to undergo eventual oncological treatment. Regarding the abdominal collection: puncture performed by rint on 07/12/2020 with the output of 80 ml of purulent secretion, with growth of pseudomonas sensitive to the patient. Performed abdominal ultrasound on 07/19/2020 for control with evidence of recollection in previous topography, discussed with [organization] and opted for a new puncture and placement of a drain, as it is considered beneficial as a way of controlling the infectious focus in accordance with goals defined by comfort-focused (not curative) care. In joint return visit to doctor (follow-up) with general surgery, methylene blue test performed, with findings suggestive of the collection being an infected bladder diverticulum, with adequate infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) coverage with meropenem for 14 days, considering complicated lower urine infection (UTI). Government health scheme (sus)pended meropenem on 07/25/2020. In images taken after abdominal drainage, findings suggestive of a bladder fistula into the abdominal cavity were seen, with initial indication for surgery. . Thus, seeing the patient stable blood pressure and heart rate, no fever (afebrile), without respiratory distress, with excellent family support, we opted, together with the patient and the patient, for outpatient return visit to doctor (follow-up) of the condition with the neurogenic bladder team at the hospital and management of an indwelling urinary catheter. General cultures i material: abdominal mass collected: 22/07/2020 made available: 25/07/2020 released: 25/07/2020 sample element a result. Absence of growth after 48 hours of incubation validated by.: the patient. Collection puncture in hypogastrium by [organization] on 12/07/2020 and 22/07/2020. I maintain measures for abdominal distension optimized for [address] simethicone and domperidone. I advise maintenance of other medications previously used. I advise care with an indwelling urinary catheter. I advise care with an occlusive dressing in the site of a previous abdominal drain. I advise warning signs and seeking emergency care if necessary. 174 1 severe acute respiratory syndrome, coronavirus negative community-acquired pneumonia? chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, decompensated by infection? 2 septic shock of pulmonary focus? cardiogenic?. admission: 05/17/2020, discharge: 05/20/2020, stay: 03 d 04 h, ward bed, payer: sus. rhinorrhea for 4 days, evolved with an episode of fever, sudden dyspnea. admitted to the emergency care unit, orotracheal intubation performed and norepinephrine started due to hypothetical septic shock of pulmonary focus. admitted to this service intubated, using norepinephrine, in isolation due to possibility of coronavirus, ruled out on 05/18. started ceftriaxone, clindamycin and oseltamivir on 05/17, the latter suspended on 05/19. Transthoracic echocardiography was performed with an ejection fraction of 24%, and the most likely cardiogenic shock was discussed and considered to be of cardiogenic etiology, considering changes in the echocardiogram, benign blood count, low CRP, no fever, and it was decided to change antibiotic therapy to clavulin 1 g every 8 hours intravenously, considering a lower risk of pseudomembranous colitis and selective pressure. High treponemal test MI 3 Ischemic stroke 3 years ago Mild motor sequelae on the left and use of an orthosis 4 Depressive disorder with psychotic symptoms 5 Hypertensive cardiomyopathy Transthoracic echocardiography 2014 Ejection fraction 76%, mild left ventricular diastolic dysfunction Mild mitral and aortic insufficiency, preserved MS. 6 pre-diabetic 7 chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, active smoking, pulmonary nodule 8 dyspeptic syndrome 9 mixed urinary incontinence in follow-up in agriculture 10 osteoporosis bone densitometry 2016: osteoporosis 3.7 lumbar 2.3 2.1 11 myalgia with statin 12 previous contact with hepatitis B 13 low social support, low medication adherence. monitor: heart rate 94 beats per minute, noninvasive blood pressure: 119 x 47 64 mmHg, respiratory rate 25 breaths per minute, saturation 94% general: serious general condition, flushed, hydrated, anicteric, acyanotic, afebrile respiratory auscultation: vesicular breath soundss present and well distributed bilaterally, without adventitious sounds cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm in 2t, normal sound sounds, I do not auscultate murmurs or extra sounds, without TVJ abdomen: protuberant, bowel sounds present and hypoactive, flaccid, depressed, I do not palpate masses or visceromegaly extremities: well perfused, warm, capillary refill time less than 3s, edema 2/4 in upper limbs, without edema lower limbs, no signs of deep vein thrombosis neurology: agitation scale sedation 5, left pupil dyschoric, photoreagents. cultures: blood culture: negative so far; urine culture: in progress; complementary tests: electrocardiogram 2020: showing left bundle branch block and axis deviation to the left. transthoracic echocardiography 2020: at 30.5 ae 39 ddfve 62 s 8 ppve 9 fe 24%. marked dilation of the left chambers, eccentric left ventricular hypertrophy, markedly depressed left ventricular systolic performance, diffuse hypokinesia, moderate left ventricular diastolic dysfunction, preserved right ventricular systolic performance, moderate aortic insufficiency, mild to moderate mitral insufficiency, mild tricuspid insufficiency, elevated pulmonary artery pressure. Transthoracic echocardiography 2016: mild left atrial dilation, volume 59 ml, ind 39, eccentric left ventricular hypertrophy, normal systolic performance and segmental mobility of the ventricles, ejection fraction 63%, mild aortic insufficiency. daily laboratory control tests. transfer to intensive care unit due to need for intensive care, hemodynamic monitoring and management of cardiogenic shock, probable nephrology evaluation, weaning from norepinephrine. hemodynamically compensated with drugs, requiring intensive care. 1 severe acute respiratory syndrome, coronavirus negative community-acquired lung infection (pneumonia)? Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, decompensated by infection? 2 severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) of pulmonary focus? Cardiogenic?. Admission: 05/17/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 05/20/2020, stay: 03 d 04 h, general hospital room (ward) bed, payer: government health scheme. Rhinorrhea for 4 days, evolved with an episode of fever, sudden difficulty in breathing (dyspnea). Admitted to the emergency care unit, orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) performed and norepinephrine started due to hypothetical severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) of pulmonary focus. Admitted to this service intubated, using norepinephrine, in isolation due to possibility of coronavirus, ruled out on 05/18. Started ceftriaxone, clindamycin and oseltamivir on 05/17, the latter government health schemepended on 05/19. Transthoracic echocardiography was performed with an ejection fraction of 24%, and the most likely cardiogenic shock was discussed and considered to be of cardiogenic cause of disease (etiology), considering changes in the heart ultrasound (echocardiogram), benign blood count, low crp, no fever, and it was decided to change infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) to clavulin 1 g every 8 hours given through a vein (drip/IV)ly, considering a lower risk of pseudomembranous colitis and selective pressure. Monitor: heart rate 94 beats per minute, noninvasive blood pressure: 119 x 47 64 mmhg, respiratory rate 25 breaths per minute, oxygen level in blood (saturation) 94% general: serious general condition, flushed, hydrated, no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no fever (afebrile) respiratory auscultation: normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds)s present and well distributed bilaterally, without adventitious sounds cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm in 2t, normal sound sounds, i do not auscultate murmurs or extra sounds, without tvj abdomen: protuberant, bowel sounds present and hypoactive, flaccid, depressed, i do not palpate masses or visceromegaly extremities: well perfused, warm, capillary refill time less than 3s, swelling (edema) 2/4 in upper limbs, without swelling (edema) lower limbs, no signs of deep vein blood clot (thrombosis) neurology: agitation scale sedation 5, left pupil dyschoric, photoreagents. Cultures: blood test to find infection (blood culture): negative so far; urine culture: in progress; complementary tests: ECG/heart tracing (electrocardiogram) 2020: showing left bundle branch block and axis deviation to the left. Transthoracic echocardiography 2020: at 30.5 ae 39 ddfve 62 s 8 ppve 9 fe 24%. Marked dilation of the left chambers, eccentric left ventricular hypertrophy, markedly depressed left ventricular systolic performance, diffuse hypokinesia, moderate left ventricular diastolic dysfunction, preserved right ventricular systolic performance, moderate aortic insufficiency, mild to moderate mitral insufficiency, mild tricuspid insufficiency, elevated pulmonary artery pressure. Transthoracic echocardiography 2016: mild left atrial dilation, volume 59 ml, ind 39, eccentric left ventricular hypertrophy, normal systolic performance and segmental mobility of the ventricles, ejection fraction 63%, mild aortic insufficiency. 175 [person], 75 years old, from [address] companion: [person] problem: 1 infective endocarditis: mitral vegetation 2.8 cm 2 positive blood cultures for streptococcus agalactiae glomerulonephritis janeway lesions 2 acute kidney injury kdigo 3 prerenal glomerulonephritis 3 ischemic stroke: decreased level of consciousness: computed tomography of the skull 04/18: formation of new hypodensity in the left thalamus region. atrophy and multiple small infarcts. previous cerebellar lesion encephalomalacia 4 resolved septic shock 5 fibrillation, atrial flutter 6 thrombocytopenia widened international normalized ratio sec sepsis? 7 nodular lesion in the right gluteus without phlogosis: sebaceous cyst? history: systemic arterial hypertension diabetes mellitus hip prosthesis approximately 8 years ago previous gastrectomy approximately 25 years ago gastric ulcer? reports stomach injuries that perforated and the stomach needed to be partially removed denies previous alcoholism or smoking denies allergies history of seizures cysticercosis [person] reports a history of seizures when young using phenobarbital, but without use or seizures for many years medications taken at home: acetylsalicylic acid 100 mg/day simvastatin 20 mg/day atenolol 25 mg/day. admission: 04/13/2020, discharge: 05/07/2020, stay: 24 d 06 h, location: ward, payer: SUS. history of current illness: [person] reports that the patient was fully functional and lived alone. Two days before admission, he fell from the bed approximately 60 cm after postural imbalance, without loss of consciousness or traumatic brain injury. He was taken to the emergency room in his hometown and received only symptomatic treatment. [person] slipped and fell to the floor 24 hours later, with trauma to the occipital region, without loss of consciousness, tonic-clonic jerks or other signs. Shortly after the trauma, [person] was found by his family with dysarthria, understanding what was said to him, but with difficulty responding. He presented progressive worsening of the level of consciousness and onset of a Cheynestokes breathing pattern. The neurology and surgery teams ruled out intracranial lesions and/or acute bleeding that could justify an alteration in the level of consciousness, and then the case was assumed by [organization] based on evidence from laboratory tests showing infection, acute kidney injury, poor perfusion and high-response atrial fibrillation. clinical evolution: 04/13: ceftriaxone 2 g started 12/12h clindamycin 600 mg 8/8h acyclovir 10 mg/kg given the possibility of aspiration pneumonia, urinary tract infection and herpetic meningoencephalitis. [person] had neck stiffness that improved after starting antibiotics underwent orotracheal intubation to protect the airway started on norepinephrine and later dobutamine after visualization of diffuse left ventricular hypokinesia 04/14: elevated troponin and ckmb, in addition to increased inactive area on electrocardiogram v1 to v4: loading dose of acetylsalicylic acid and clopidogrel administered. later re-discussed and interpreted as myocardial involvement by sepsis, possible type 2 acute myocardial infarction, vegetation visualized on transthoracic echocardiography, and oxacycline 2 g every 4 hours was started due to positive blood culture for gram-positive cocci, sedation and analgesia with midazolam and fentanyl were suspended, around 5 pm, amiodarone 900 mg/24 hours was started to control heart rate, atrial flutter on electrocardiogram 04/15: positive blood culture for streptococcus agalactiae, norepinephrine and dobutamine were suspended, and VMA was maintained in spontaneous and TOT mode since [person] was not at a safe level of consciousness for extubation 04/18: positive blood culture for streptococcus agalactiae and staphylococcus capitis, antibiotic adjustments as discussed with [organization] without adequate awakening. regular general condition, pale 1/4, hydrated, acyanotic, anicteric edema of upper limbs 2/4 oxygen saturation 95% with 4 l of oxygen. blood pressure 140/50 cardiovascular: heart rate: 71 beats per minute, regular heart rhythm, in normal S1 and S2 with fixed splitting of b2, hypophonetic heart sounds, no murmurs respiratory: respiratory rate: 15, vesicular breath sounds present diffusely, with coarse crackles at the bases. abdomen: protuberant abdomen, I do not palpate masses or visceromegaly, diffuse bowel sounds present lower limbs: no signs of deep vein thrombosis glasgow: 7t. exams:Transthoracic echocardiography 04/14: thickened mitral leaflets with hyperrefringent, filamentary image, measuring 2.8 cm suggestive of vegetation. Mild mitral and pulmonary insufficiency. Mild to moderate aortic and tricuspid insufficiency. AE dilation. LV concentric hypertrophy. Normal LV and RV systolic performance - subjective assessment. Ultrasound of kidneys and airways: sonographically normal kidneys with nonspecific echogenic point in the middle third of the right kidney. Computerized tomography of the skull without murmurs 04/13 and 04/14: left cerebellar encephalomalacia in pica territory. Signs of small vessel vascular disease, with microangiopathy and lacunar infarcts. Reduced brain volume. No changes compared to the examination of 04/13. Ultrasound of soft tissues - nodule in the gluteal region: nodular image with signs suggestive of calcifications. I retrieved radiographs of the pelvis and hip from 2014, showing the presence of a calcified lesion in the right gluteal region, without an increase in volume, suggestive of calcification from a local infectious process. I also saw diffuse calcifications in the lower limbs, compatible with cysticercosis. I discussed with radiology previous computed tomography of the skull, with no evidence of lesions compatible with neurocysticercosis. Electroencephalogram 04/14: a moderate diffuse disorganization of the background activity due to the presence of a pattern similar to a suppression burst, with bursts characterized by rhythms of varying frequencies, with a predominance of monomorphic rhythms in the theta range, interspersed with sections of global attenuation of the rhythms. The duration of the bursts and interburst intervals varied between 1 and 3 seconds. After nociceptive stimulation, there was no significant reactivity of the tracing. b presence of rare slow waves in the delta range with projection in the frontotemporal regions of the right hemisphere. electroclinical correlation: the findings are nonspecific, with signs suggestive of drug sedation antimicrobial regimen: current: ceftriaxone d0: 04/13 previous: clindamycin: 04/13 04/14 oxacillin: 04/14 04/18 vancomycin 04/18 04/21 acyclovir 04/13 04/18 cultures: blood cultures 04/13: 2 samples positive for streptococcus agalactiae sensitive to penicillin blood cultures 04/15: 3/4 samples positive for streptococcus agalactiae ms and staphylococcus capitis resistant to oxacillin catheter tip culture 04/18: pseudomonas multis growth 15 cfu blood culture 04/20: negative blood culture 04/23: staphylococcus hominis gluteal nodule: klebsiela pneumoniae sensitive to meropenem, esbl, pseudomonas sensitive to meropenem. tracheostomy. [person] in palliative care as already defined with the cardiology team and [organization] during hospitalization at [organization] {omitted} ceftriaxone maintained for treatment of infective endocarditis d0: 13/05 to complete 6 weeks tracheostomy management guided in report. referral to [organization]. regular general condition. Admission: 04/13/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 05/07/2020, stay: 24 d 06 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme (sus). History of current illness: the patient reports that the patient was fully functional and lived alone. Two days before admission, he fell from the bed approximately 60 cm after postural imbalance, without loss of consciousness or traumatic brain injury. He was taken to the emergency room in his hometown and received only symptomatic treatment. The patient slipped and fell to the floor 24 hours later, with trauma to the occipital region, without loss of consciousness, tonic-clonic jerks or other signs. Shortly after the trauma, the patient was found by his family with dysarthria, understanding what was said to him, but with difficulty responding. He presented progressive worsening of the alertness level (level of consciousness) and onset of a cheynestokes breathing pattern. The neurology and surgery teams ruled out intracranial lesions and/or acute bleeding that could justify an alteration in the alertness level (level of consciousness), and then the case was assumed by [organization] based on evidence from laboratory tests showing infection, acute kidney injury, poor perfusion and high-response irregular heartbeat (atrial fibrillation). Clinical evolution: 04/13: ceftriaxone 2 g started 12/12h clindamycin 600 mg 8/8h acyclovir 10 mg/kg given the possibility of lung infection from inhaling food/liquid (aspiration lung infection (pneumonia)), urine infection (UTI) and herpetic meningoencephalitis. The patient had neck stiffness that improved after starting infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic)s underwent orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) to protect the airway started on norepinephrine and later dobutamine after visualization of diffuse left ventricular hypokinesia 04/14: elevated troponin and ckmb, in addition to increased inactive area on ECG/heart tracing (electrocardiogram) v1 to v4: loading dose of acetylsalicylic acid and clopidogrel give (administer)ed.
