Regular general condition, pale 1/4, hydrated, no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric) swelling (edema) of upper limbs 2/4 oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation) 95% with 4 l of oxygen. Blood pressure 140/50 cardiovascular: heart rate: 71 beats per minute, regular heart rhythm, in normal s1 and s2 with fixed splitting of b2, hypophonetic heart sounds, no murmurs respiratory: respiratory rate: 15, normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds) present diffusely, with coarse crackles at the bases. Abdomen: protuberant abdomen, i do not palpate masses or visceromegaly, diffuse bowel sounds present lower limbs: no signs of deep vein blood clot (thrombosis) glasgow: 7t. Exams:transthoracic echocardiography 04/14: thickened mitral leaflets with hyperrefringent, filamentary image, measuring 2.8 cm suggestive of vegetation. Mild mitral and pulmonary insufficiency. Mild to moderate aortic and tricuspid insufficiency. Ae dilation. Lv concentric hypertrophy. Normal lv and rv systolic performance - subjective assessment. Ultrasound of kidneys and airways: sonographically normal kidneys with nonspecific echogenic point in the middle third of the right kidney. Computerized tomography of the skull without murmurs 04/13 and 04/14: left cerebellar encephalomalacia in pica territory. Signs of small vessel vascular disease, with microangiopathy and lacunar infarcts. Reduced brain volume. No changes compared to the examination of 04/13. Ultrasound of soft tissues - nodule in the gluteal region: nodular image with signs suggestive of calcifications. I retrieved radiographs of the pelvis and intraparenchymal hemorrhage from 2014, showing the presence of a calcified lesion in the right gluteal region, without an increase in volume, suggestive of calcification from a local infectious process. I also saw diffuse calcifications in the lower limbs, compatible with cysticercosis. I discussed with radiology previous CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull, with no evidence of lesions compatible with neurocysticercosis. Electroencephalogram 04/14: a moderate diffuse disorganization of the background activity due to the presence of a pattern similar to a suppression burst, with bursts characterized by rhythms of varying frequencies, with a predominance of monomorphic rhythms in the theta range, interspersed with sections of global attenuation of the rhythms. The duration of the bursts and interburst intervals varied between 1 and 3 seconds. After nociceptive stimulation, there was no significant reactivity of the tracing. Electroclinical correlation: the findings are nonspecific, with signs suggestive of drug sedation antimicrobial regimen: current: ceftriaxone d0: 04/13 previous: clindamycin: 04/13 04/14 oxacillin: 04/14 04/18 vancomycin 04/18 04/21 acyclovir 04/13 04/18 cultures: blood test to find infection (blood culture)s 04/13: 2 samples positive for streptococcus agalactiae sensitive to penicillin blood test to find infection (blood culture)s 04/15: 3/4 samples positive for streptococcus agalactiae ms and staphylococcus capitis resistant to oxacillin catheter tip culture 04/18: pseudomonas multis growth 15 cfu blood test to find infection (blood culture) 04/20: negative blood test to find infection (blood culture) 04/23: staphylococcus hominis gluteal nodule: klebsiela lung infection (pneumonia)e sensitive to meropenem, esbl, pseudomonas sensitive to meropenem. Breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy). The patient in comfort-focused (not curative) care as already defined with the cardiology team and [organization] during hospitalization at [organization] ceftriaxone maintained for treatment of infective infection of heart valves (endocarditis) d0: 13/05 to complete 6 weeks breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) management guided in report. Referral to [organization]. Regular general condition. i33. acute and subacute endocarditis. i64. stroke, unspecified as hemorrhagic or ischemic. i38. valvular endocarditis, unspecified. m160. bilateral primary coxarthrosis. m170. bilateral primary gonarthrosis. n17. acute renal failure. a419. septicemia nes. b004. herpes virus encephalitis. r410. disorientation nes. alive {transfer} 16.8 1.16
176 [person], 56 years old, obese, previously hypertensive. admission: 08/01/2020, discharge: 07/02/2020, stay: 29 d 10 h, location: ward, payer: sus. patient admitted to this service on 08/01 with a 7-day history of initial lesion with painful perianal nodulation and local hyperemia associated with fever. evolution to a picture compatible with fournier's syndrome requiring surgical approach. evolution to acute respiratory failure requiring orotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation, also evolution to cardiorespiratory arrest in ventricular tachycardia rhythm with successful reversal after 8 minutes. there was a need for electrical cardioversion and impregnation with amiodarone. was admitted to the intensive care unit on 09/01. evolution to refractory septic shock and multiple organ dysfunction. evolution to renal failure requiring hemodialysis. also evolved with disseminated intravascular coagulation. initially used the following antibiotics: ceftriaxone 08/01 oxacillin and metronidazole 09/01. after which there was escalation to piperacillinatazobactam and vancomycin 12/01. and after clinical deterioration and culture findings there was a new escalation to polymyxin B and tigecycline 23/01 torgena and vancomycin 26/01 micafungin 02/02. necrotic tissue in the perineum and left buttock, with pockets with purulent secretion extending from the base of the penis to the topography of the anus on the left. surgical debridement on 16/01: presence of improvement in the appearance of the wound, without worsening of the subcutaneous pockets and without progression of the necrotic involvement communication of the infected pocket with the scrotum delimited by the dartos fascia, without invasion. rectal walls intact to the touch. evidence of maintenance of the hemorrhoidal disease aspect previously seen. thrombosis without necrosis, presence of grade II pressure injury in bilateral sacral and gluteal topography, in progression surgical debridement on 01/21: extensive sacral pressure injury with necrotic tissue, without signs of infection, without purulent secretion, without foul odor deep pocket in left gluteus without communication with rectum, rectal walls intact upon rectal examination evidence of maintenance of the hemorrhoidal disease aspect previously seen. thrombosis without necrosis perineal wound with purulent secretion cultures: blood cultures: 09/01: enterococcus faecalis 1 sample aged 30 h mults 20/01: staphylococcus capitis oxa r 22/01: negative after 7 days culture of material from surgical wound lesion of fournier's syndrome 09/01: enterococcus faecalis mults escherichia coli mults 21/01: klebsiella mr s a torgena urine culture 11/01: negative in 48 h 20/01: klebsiella pneumoniae mr 80,000 cfu/ml tracheal aspirate culture 18/01: acinetobacter mr sensitive to polymyxin and tigecycline. 09/01: surgical excision with finding of necrotic tissue in the perineum and left buttock, with pockets with purulent secretion extending from the base of the penis to the topography of the anus on the left; apparently intact testicles, urethra and rectum, with involvement only of the subcutaneous and muscular plane. need for multiple re-approaches of the surgical wound for Fornier syndrome on 11/01, 13/01, 16/01, 21/01, 25/01 and 31/01. Tracheostomy performed on 25/01. Evolution with refractory septic shock and multiple organ dysfunction. presented cardiorespiratory arrest in asystole and death confirmed at {omitted}. The patient, 56 years old, obese, previously hypertensive. Admission: 08/01/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 07/02/2020, stay: 29 d 10 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Patient admitted to this service on 08/01 with a 7-day history of initial lesion with painful perianal nodulation and local hyperemia associated with fever. Evolution to a picture compatible with fournier's syndrome requiring surgical approach. Evolution to acute lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) requiring orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) and machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation), also evolution to cardiorespiratory arrest in ventricular fast heartbeat (tachycardia) rhythm with successful reversal after 8 minutes. There was a need for electrical cardioversion and impregnation with amiodarone. Was admitted to the ICU (serious care room) on 09/01. Evolution to refractory severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) and multiple organ dysfunction. Evolution to kidney failure (renal failure) requiring kidney dialysis (hemodialysis). Also evolved with disseminated intravascular coagulation. Initially used the following infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic)s: ceftriaxone 08/01 oxacillin and metronidazole 09/01. After which there was escalation to piperacillinatazobactam and vancomycin 12/01. And after clinical deterioration and culture findings there was a new escalation to polymyxin b and tigecycline 23/01 torgena and vancomycin 26/01 micafungin 02/02. Necrotic tissue in the perineum and left buttock, with pockets with purulent secretion extending from the base of the penis to the topography of the anus on the left. Surgical surgical wound cleaning (debridement) on 16/01: presence of improvement in the appearance of the wound, without worsening of the subcutaneous pockets and without progression of the necrotic involvement communication of the infected pocket with the scrotum delimited by the dartos fascia, without invasion. Rectal walls intact to the touch. Evidence of maintenance of the hemorrhoidal disease aspect previously seen. Blood clot (thrombosis) without tissue death (necrosis), presence of grade ii pressure injury in bilateral sacral and gluteal topography, in progression surgical surgical wound cleaning (debridement) on 01/21: extensive sacral pressure injury with necrotic tissue, without signs of infection, without purulent secretion, without foul odor deep pocket in left gluteus without communication with rectum, rectal walls intact upon rectal examination evidence of maintenance of the hemorrhoidal disease aspect previously seen. Blood clot (thrombosis) without tissue death (necrosis) perineal wound with purulent secretion cultures: blood test to find infection (blood culture)s: 09/01: enterococcus faecalis 1 sample aged 30 h mults 20/01: staphylococcus capitis oxa r 22/01: negative after 7 days culture of material from surgical wound lesion of fournier's syndrome 09/01: enterococcus faecalis mults escherichia coli mults 21/01: klebsiella mr s a torgena urine culture 11/01: negative in 48 h 20/01: klebsiella lung infection (pneumonia)e mr 80,000 cfu/ml tracheal aspirate culture 18/01: acinetobacter mr sensitive to polymyxin and tigecycline. 09/01: surgical excision with finding of necrotic tissue in the perineum and left buttock, with pockets with purulent secretion extending from the base of the penis to the topography of the anus on the left; apparently intact testicles, urethra and rectum, with involvement only of the subcutaneous and muscular plane. Need for multiple re-approaches of the surgical wound for fornier syndrome on 11/01, 13/01, 16/01, 21/01, 25/01 and 31/01. Breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) performed on 25/01. Evolution with refractory severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) and multiple organ dysfunction. Presented cardiorespiratory arrest in asystole and death confirmed at . The patient, 56 years old, obese, previously hypertensive. Admission: 08/01/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 07/02/2020, stay: 29 d 10 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Evolution to a picture compatible with fournier's syndrome requiring surgical approach. Evolution to acute lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) requiring orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) and machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation), also evolution to cardiorespiratory arrest in ventricular fast heartbeat (tachycardia) rhythm with successful reversal after 8 minutes. There was a need for electrical cardioversion and impregnation with amiodarone. Was admitted to the ICU (serious care room) on 09/01. Evolution to refractory severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) and multiple organ dysfunction. Evolution to kidney failure (renal failure) requiring kidney dialysis (hemodialysis). Also evolved with disseminated intravascular coagulation. Initially used the following infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic)s: ceftriaxone 08/01 oxacillin and metronidazole 09/01. After which there was escalation to piperacillinatazobactam and vancomycin 12/01. And after clinical deterioration and culture findings there was a new escalation to polymyxin b and tigecycline 23/01 torgena and vancomycin 26/01 micafungin 02/02. Necrotic tissue in the perineum and left buttock, with pockets with purulent secretion extending from the base of the penis to the topography of the anus on the left. Surgical surgical wound cleaning (debridement) on 16/01: presence of improvement in the appearance of the wound, without worsening of the subcutaneous pockets and without progression of the necrotic involvement communication of the infected pocket with the scrotum delimited by the dartos fascia, without invasion. Rectal walls intact to the touch. Evidence of maintenance of the hemorrhoidal disease aspect previously seen. Blood clot (thrombosis) without tissue death (necrosis), presence of grade ii pressure injury in bilateral sacral and gluteal topography, in progression surgical surgical wound cleaning (debridement) on 01/21: extensive sacral pressure injury with necrotic tissue, without signs of infection, without purulent secretion, without foul odor deep pocket in left gluteus without communication with rectum, rectal walls intact upon rectal examination evidence of maintenance of the hemorrhoidal disease aspect previously seen. Blood clot (thrombosis) without tissue death (necrosis) perineal wound with purulent secretion cultures: blood test to find infection (blood culture)s: 09/01: enterococcus faecalis 1 sample aged 30 h mults 20/01: staphylococcus capitis oxa r 22/01: negative after 7 days culture of material from surgical wound lesion of fournier's syndrome 09/01: enterococcus faecalis mults escherichia coli mults 21/01: klebsiella mr s a torgena urine culture 11/01: negative in 48 h 20/01: klebsiella lung infection (pneumonia)e mr 80,000 cfu/ml tracheal aspirate culture 18/01: acinetobacter mr sensitive to polymyxin and tigecycline. 09/01: surgical excision with finding of necrotic tissue in the perineum and left buttock, with pockets with purulent secretion extending from the base of the penis to the topography of the anus on the left; apparently intact testicles, urethra and rectum, with involvement only of the subcutaneous and muscular plane. Need for multiple re-approaches of the surgical wound for fornier syndrome on 11/01, 13/01, 16/01, 21/01, 25/01 and 31/01. Breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) performed on 25/01. Evolution with refractory severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) and multiple organ dysfunction. Presented cardiorespiratory arrest in asystole and death confirmed at . a419. septicemia ne. n179. acute renal failure ne. r579. shock ne. j960. acute respiratory failure. d65. disseminated intravascular coagulation [defibrination syndrome]. i460. cardiac arrest with successful resuscitation. i472. ventricular tachycardia. i48. atrial flutter and fibrillation. n498. inflammatory disorders of other specified male genital organs. e66. obesity. i10. primary essential hypertension. death 18.9 1.14
177 coronavirus 19. admission: 27/07/2020, discharge: 21/08/2020, stay: 24 d 16 h, location: ward, payer: sus. [person] with dyspnea for 15 days after admission with progressive worsening, onset of secretory cough 7 days after admission. sought care at [organization] on 22/07, was diagnosed with pneumonia and started treatment with levofloxacin for 3 days without improvement. presented significant acute worsening of dyspnea on 27/07 in the morning associated with chest pain in the left anterior region, with a pleuritic appearance. admitted to [organization] with oxygen saturation of 73% in room air and 90% with a mask with a reservoir at 15 l/min. admitted to the [organization] hemodynamically stable, conscious and oriented, with oxygen saturation 88-90% on a reservoir mask, at 15 l/min, tachydyspneic respiratory rate 40 inspirations per minute, with significant respiratory distress. orotracheal intubation was performed due to acute respiratory failure on admission. remained intubated until 08/12 and developed bloodstream infection associated with a central catheter on 08/06, with growth of multidrug-resistant Klebsiella. at that time, meropenem vancomycin polymyxin was started. after identification of an agent already described, vancomycin was discontinued. after sensitivity testing, meropenem was discontinued and torgena to the regimen was started. polymyxin was discontinued on 08/13. presented transient hemodynamic instability on 08/13, without additional procedures. admitted to the ward for continued therapy and correction of hydroelectrolytic disturbances hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia. Vital signs: heart rate 100 beats per minute, blood pressure 120x70 mmHg, respiratory rate 20 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation 96% regular general condition, anicteric, acyanotic, pale /4, edematous in the limbs, afebrile to the touch cardiovascular: regular heart rhythm, normal S1 and S2, normal sound sounds, no murmurs, capillary refill time 3 seconds, palpable peripheral pulses respiratory: reduced vesicular breath sounds at the bases, no adventitious sounds. no signs of respiratory distress abdomen: globular, bowel sounds present. no faces of pain on palpation, no visceromegaly or palpable masses edematous upper and lower limbs /4, calves without signs of swelling neurological: Glasgow Coma Scale 15 ao4/rv5/rm6 oriented and localized in space, partial orientation to time. person-oriented. moderate dysarthria, pupils with previous cataract 1 mm difference, photoreactive. preserved amplitude of the moe. preserved facial sensitivity. preserved mimicry, without asymmetry of smile. symmetrical elevation of the palate, bilateral gag reflex present. trophic tongue, with preserved movements. reduced strength overall and improving. indifferent cutaneous-plantar reflex bilaterally. negative Hoffmann and Troemner tests. untested ataxia due to strength deficit. schedule: rehabilitation: motor and respiratory physiotherapy, prednisone 20 mg, dose reduced on 08/16 with schedule for further reduction on 08/22, forwarding speech therapy report. [person] in fair general condition. Coronavirus 19. Admission: 27/07/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 21/08/2020, stay: 24 d 16 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. The patient with difficulty in breathing (dyspnea) for 15 days after admission with progressive worsening, onset of secretory cough 7 days after admission. Sought care at [organization] on 22/07, was diagnosed with lung infection (pneumonia) and started treatment with levofloxacin for 3 days without improvement. Presented significant acute worsening of difficulty in breathing (dyspnea) on 27/07 in the morning associated with chest pain in the left anterior region, with a pleuritic appearance. Admitted to [organization] with oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation) of 73% in room air and 90% with a mask with a reservoir at 15 l/min. Admitted to the [organization] stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), conscious and oriented, with oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation) 88-90% on a reservoir mask, at 15 l/min, tachydyspneic respiratory rate 40 inspirations per minute, with significant respiratory distress. Orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) was performed due to acute lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) on admission. Remained intubated until 08/12 and developed bloodstream infection associated with a central catheter on 08/06, with growth of multidrug-resistant klebsiella. At that time, meropenem vancomycin polymyxin was started. After identification of an agent already described, vancomycin was discontinued. After sensitivity testing, meropenem was discontinued and torgena to the regimen was started. Polymyxin was discontinued on 08/13. Presented transient hemodynamic instability on 08/13, without additional procedures. Admitted to the general hospital room (ward) for continued therapy and correction of hydroelectrolytic disturbances hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia. Vital signs: heart rate 100 beats per minute, blood pressure 120x70 mmhg, respiratory rate 20 breaths per minute, oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation) 96% regular general condition, no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), pale /4, swelling (edema)tous in the limbs, no fever (afebrile) to the touch cardiovascular: regular heart rhythm, normal s1 and s2, normal sound sounds, no murmurs, capillary refill time 3 seconds, palpable peripheral pulses respiratory: reduced normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds) at the bases, no adventitious sounds. No signs of respiratory distress abdomen: globular, bowel sounds present. No faces of pain on palpation, no visceromegaly or palpable masses swellingtous upper and lower limbs /4, calves without signs of swelling neurological: glasgow coma scale 15 ao4/rv5/rm6 oriented and localized in space, partial orientation to time. Person-oriented. Moderate dysarthria, pupils with previous cataract 1 mm difference, photoreactive. Preserved amplitude of the moe. Preserved facial sensitivity. Preserved mimicry, without asymmetry of smile. Symmetrical elevation of the palate, bilateral gag reflex present. Trophic tongue, with preserved movements. Reduced strength overall and improving. Indifferent cutaneous-plantar reflex bilaterally. Negative hoffmann and troemner tests. Untested ataxia due to strength deficit. Schedule: rehabilitation: motor and respiratory physiotherapy, prednisone 20 mg, dose reduced on 08/16 with schedule for further reduction on 08/22, forgeneral hospital rooming speech therapy report. 178 sepsis with cutaneous focus, acute pulmonary edema. admission: 2020-09-27, discharge: 2020-10-31, stay: 01 m 03 d 13 h, location: ward, payer: sus. 1 sepsis with cutaneous focus: sacral pressure injury, orbit, nasopharynx: postoperative osteomyelitis 2020-09-29, debridement of sacral ulcer, drainage of extensive abscess in the dorsal region. sacral pressure injury with exposure of coccyx with probable osteomyelitis, extensive pockets of purulent secretion in the dorsal region 2 renal dysfunction initiated renal replacement therapy by nephrology on 3020-09-30. last hemodialysis on 1020-10-01 3 desquamation secondary to edema process, onset of lesions 1020-1, according to [person] under follow-up with dermatology. [person] unresponsive, absence of central pulse, mydriatic pupils. current imaging tests: computed tomography of the skull 09/27: evolutionary control of invasive fungal sinusitis, with enucleation of the left eyeball, partial resection of the paranasal sinuses. amorphous content with discrete post-contrast enhancement in the left orbital cavity, associated with changes in the ethmoid cavity, nasal cavity and left maxilla, suspected recurrence of mucormycosis. computed tomography of the abdomen 09/27: sacrococcygeal ulcer with osteomyelitis and paravertebral abscess extending to the height of t9 t10 on the right. small volume ascites. anasarca. nasofibroscopy 10/01/2020 otorhinolaryngology: good-looking nasopharynx with no signs of recurrence. [person] evolves with cardiorespiratory arrest in asystole, cardiopulmonary resuscitation started immediately, communicated by the [organization] team. orotracheal intubation was performed after airway aspiration. adrenaline was administered every 3 minutes as per protocol. death as {omitted}. 179 [person], 81 years old. admission: 10/20/2020, discharge: 10/30/2020, stay: 09 d 14 h, location: ward, payer: SUS. [person] reports that 1 month ago the patient choked on bread, losing consciousness for a few minutes, with recovery after airway clearance in the emergency room. After the episode, he reports that his mother continued to snore and presented with slowness and dependence for activities, in addition to being able to ingest only soft foods with difficulty. 2 days after admission, the mother presented worsening fatigue and 1 day after admission she presented a new episode of choking at lunch, with worsening dyspnea, noisy auscultation, and a decline in her general condition. She was taken to the emergency room, where she was intubated despite corticosteroid and bronchodilator measures. Furthermore, he reports that in the last few months [person] had been undergoing treatment for recurrent urinary tract infection with nitrofurantoin, having finished treatment 1 week after admission. Upon admission to [organization], [person] had severe hypernatremia that resolved during hospitalization. Furthermore, due to suspected aspiration pneumonia, antibiotic therapy with ceftriaxone and clindamycin was started. During hospitalization, [person] presented hemodynamic instability, requiring norepinephrine. Furthermore, due to clinical worsening and suspected septic shock of pulmonary focus, antibiotic therapy was escalated on 10/26 with meropenem and vancomycin. He also developed worsening renal function, requiring dialysis support started on 10/26. Regarding functionality: [person] reports that [person] had a certain degree of dementia, but presented coherent speech. He presented forgetfulness of some information. He walked with a walker, performed personal hygiene and ate alone, requiring only supervision. had a significant decline in functionality after an episode of choking 1 month after admission. vital signs: heart rate 130 respiratory rate 31 blood pressure: 126x109 117 mmhg oxygen saturation: 87% poor general condition, pale 2/4, hydrated, anicteric, acyanotic, afebrile, tachypneic. neurology: sedation agitation scale 5. miotic and poorly reactive pupils. respiratory auscultation present and symmetrical vesicular breath sounds, reduced overall, without adventitious sounds. with signs of respiratory effort. cardiac auscultation: rhythmic, normal-sounding sounds, in 2t, without murmurs, without pathological TVJ. abdomen: globular, without distension, hypoactive bowel sounds, without visceromegaly or palpable masses. no bladder mass. extremities: cold with filling time 5 seconds, no edema of lower limbs, edema 3/4 upper limbs, symmetrical, pittable, without hyperemia. absence of signs of deep vein thrombosis. bruising in upper limbs. physical examination death: absence of central pulses for more than 10 seconds. absence of heartbeat on auscultation. absence of vesicular breath sounds. absence of corneopalpebral trunk reflexes, cough, photomotor. right subclavian cardiac auscultation 20/10 tot no. 7 rhyme 23 20/10 indwelling urinary catheter 20/10 right chest drain 22/10 tot no. 8 25/10 cdl right femoral dialysis 26/10. discharge death. After the episode, he reports that his mother continued to snore and presented with slowness and dependence for activities, in addition to being able to ingest only soft foods with difficulty. 2 days after admission, the mother presented worsening fatigue and 1 day after admission she presented a new episode of choking at lunch, with worsening difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), noisy auscultation, and a decline in her general condition. She was taken to the emergency room, where she was intubated despite corticosteroid and bronchodilator measures. [person] had already authorized a tracheostomy. 2 disseminated intravascular coagulation? score 6 points: exams on 05/17 prothrombin time: 1.89 apt_pt: 2.29 fibrinogen: 316 d-dimers: 3.82 platelets: 40000 3 chronic liver disease of probable alcoholic etiology clinical signs of liver disease on admission and signs on ultrasound admission procedures 04/20: thiamine vitamin k lactulose history of systemic arterial hypertension, anxiety, depression significant alcoholism distilled spirits polyneuropathy secondary to alcoholism, b12 deficiency in 2018. admission: 04/20/2020, discharge: 05/20/2020, stay: 01 m 09 h, location: ward, payer: sus. [person] admitted transported by [organization], with 15 l oxygen in a non-rebreather mask, with a history of dyspnea and cough probably for 2 days. He was admitted to [organization] 1 day ago with a report of pleural effusion in the right hemithorax, and was started on ceftriaxone 2 g. Today, he developed mental confusion and worsening dyspnea, requiring oxygen. He denies the use of vasoactive drugs. [person] reports alcohol abuse and use of medications to sleep and control depression and anxiety for many years, in addition to the report of blood in the stool. He was admitted to an isolation bed due to the report of suspected tuberculosis, 2 negative tests for coronavirus. Tuberculosis was ruled out during hospitalization. [person] developed septic shock of pulmonary focus, with the presence of multir microorganisms: klebsiella pneumoniae in tracheal aspirate, without response to treatment with ceftazidime, avibactam, vancomycin, tigecycline, and micafungin. He started renal replacement therapy on 05/13, which was not tolerated due to hemodynamic instability. developed anasarca and worsening hemodynamic instability requiring high doses of vesopressors. On 05/20, I was called by the nursing team with a report of asystole on the monitor. I evaluated [person] in a monitored bed, with the presence of a straight line on the monitor, maintained in other leads, absence of central pulses. Cardiac arrest was confirmed. {omitted}. Precordial auscultation was performed, showing absence of heartbeat, absence of respiratory movements, presence of fixed mydriasis without brainstem reflexes. Death was confirmed {omitted}. cultures: tracheal aspirate 04/29: pseudomonas aeruginosa ms tracheal aspirate 05/05: polymyxin-resistant klebisiella pneumoniae tests: computed tomography of the skull 04/22: slight diffuse reduction in brain and cerebellar volume chest tomography 04/22: pulmonary alterations suggestive of an inflammatory, infectious process, with a small pleural effusion on the left ultrasound of the right lower limb 04/21: no signs of thrombosis regarding the computed tomography angiography: discussed about waiting to perform the test, since pulmonary thromboembolism is not the main hypothesis for the cause of pulmonary failure at the moment ultrasound of the right lower limb without signs of deep vein thrombosis electroencephalogram 04/25: conclusion and clinical correlation: the electroencephalogram was abnormal, the findings are consistent with marked encephalopathy, possibly associated with diffuse cortical involvement. transthoracic echocardiography 04/26: ejection fraction 57% and 48 to 33.5, mild dilation of the left atrium, preserved ventricular systolic performance, normal left ventricular segmental mobility, negative serologies for hepatitis and human immunodeficiency virus. Chest ultrasound 04/28: pleural effusion of 2.7 cm on the left and less than 2 cm on the right, stable in relation to the other exams. presence of a large amount of ascitic fluid. death. 1 lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) secondary to serious blood infection (sepsis) of pulmonary focus severe community-acquired lung infection (pneumonia) admission status: fluid around the lungs (pleural effusion) htd mental confusion worsening of difficulty in breathing (dyspnea) admitted with non-rebreathing mask 15 l/min blood count 04/21: hb 9.8 gb 14,000 bast 6% segm 88% plaq 72,000 admission procedures 04/20: monitored isolation bed coronavirus negative 04/19 ringer lactate infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) ceftriaxone given through a vein (drip/IV) clarithromycin d0 04/20 attempted noninvasive ventilation the patient uncooperative, in mental confusion orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation) continuous sedation with midazolam fentanyl signs of bronchospasm scheduled salbutamol methylprednisone associated with ketamine propofol for sedation and analgesia in the context of severe bronchospasm chest tomography 04/22: pulmonary changes suggestive of an inflammatory, infectious process, with a small fluid around the lungs (pleural effusion) on the left the patient with a high risk of unfavorable progression. Admission: 04/20/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 05/20/2020, stay: 01 m 09 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. The patient admitted transported by [organization], with 15 l oxygen in a non-rebreather mask, with a history of difficulty in breathing (dyspnea) and cough probably for 2 days. He was admitted to [organization] 1 day ago with a report of fluid around the lungs (pleural effusion) in the right hemithorax, and was started on ceftriaxone 2 g. Today, he developed mental confusion and worsening difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), requiring oxygen. He denies the use of vasoactive drugs. The patient reports alcohol abuse and use of medications to sleep and control depression and anxiety for many years, in addition to the report of blood in the stool. He was admitted to an isolation bed due to the report of government health scheme (sus)pected TB (tuberculosis), 2 negative tests for coronavirus. TB (tuberculosis) was ruled out during hospitalization. The patient developed severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) of pulmonary focus, with the presence of multir microorganisms: klebsiella lung infection (pneumonia)e in tracheal aspirate, without response to treatment with ceftazidime, avibactam, vancomycin, tigecycline, and micafungin. He started renal replacement therapy on 05/13, which was not tolerated due to hemodynamic instability. Developed anasarca and worsening hemodynamic instability requiring high doses of vesopressors. On 05/20, i was called by the nursing team with a report of asystole on the monitor. I evaluated the patient in a monitored bed, with the presence of a straight line on the monitor, maintained in other leads, absence of central pulses. Heart stopped beating (cardiac arrest) was confirmed. . Precordial auscultation was performed, showing absence of heartbeat, absence of respiratory movements, presence of fixed mydriasis without brainstem reflexes. Death was confirmed . Transthoracic echocardiography 04/26: ejection fraction 57% and 48 to 33.5, mild dilation of the left atrium, preserved ventricular systolic performance, normal left ventricular segmental mobility, negative serologies for hepatitis and human immunodeficiency virus. Chest ultrasound 04/28: fluid around the lungs (pleural effusion) of 2.7 cm on the left and less than 2 cm on the right, stable in relation to the other exams. Presence of a large amount of ascitic fluid. TB (tuberculosis) was ruled out during hospitalization. The patient developed severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock), with the presence of multir microorganisms called klebsiella lung infection (pneumonia)e in tracheal aspirate, without response to treatment with ceftazidime, avibactam, vancomycin, tigecycline, and micafungin. 181 id: [person], 59 years old, from {city}, patient with systemic arterial hypertension, chronic kidney disease on hemodialysis, at {organization} Monday, Wednesday, Friday, heart patient with a pacemaker. On June 14th, he started with erythema at the dialysis access site in the left femoral vein. On Friday, June 18th, on hemodialysis, [organization] evaluated the catheter and recommended hospitalization at {organization} for replacement and investigation of lower back pain with magnetic resonance imaging and sciatica. During hospitalization, he did not present hemodynamic instability or other complications. He was discharged from {organization} on June 22nd with a schedule to receive a dose of 1000 mg of vancomycin on dialysis. At home, he remained well until Wednesday night, June 23rd, when {person} suddenly noticed unresponsiveness and drowsiness, which prompted him to call emergency medical services. The patient was admitted to the emergency care unit with significant hypotension and blood pressure of 60/40. High doses of norepinephrine were administered. Admission to the emergency unit on June 25 [person] resulted in a decrease in the level of consciousness and hemodynamic instability, requiring high doses of vasoactive drugs, norepinephrine and vasopressin. Orotracheal intubation was required. Meropenem and vancomycin were started. Blood culture collected on admission showed multidrug-resistant Klebsiella. Antibiotics were maintained due to gravity. A computed tomography scan showed a larger bilateral psoas abscess on the left and spondylodiscitis in l2 and l3, contiguous to the psoas abscesses. The abscess was punctured on the left by radiointervention, with growth of multidrug-resistant Escherichia coli. The patient developed fever and blood culture showed multidrug-resistant Acinetobacter. Amikacin was started on July 5. Transthoracic transesophageal echocardiography was performed, without vegetations. [person] showed clinical improvement, and extubation was performed on 07/06. Evaluated by orthopedics, who did not indicate surgical approaches. A new computed tomography scan was performed on 08/02, with puncture of psoas collections bilaterally, with the appearance of hematoma. Control culture of these collections showed no bacterial growth, and control computed tomography scan on 08/04 showed improvement, which was discussed with [person] from [organization], opting to deescalate antibiotic therapy to cefuroxime, given the sensitivity of the isolated Escherichia coli. On 08/06, a Permcath implant was performed for hemodialysis, with precordialgia and dyspnea in the first 30 minutes of dialysis. A chest pain protocol was performed, with no changes in the electrocardiogram, chest x-ray, or myocardial enzyme levels. [person] began to become hemodynamically unstable and hypotensive at {omitted} 08/07. as [person] with end-stage chronic renal disease, already evaluated post-operatively [organization], which defined proportionate palliative care, {omitted}. on the morning of 08/07, [person] was found unresponsive, with non-palpable carotid pulse, in apnea, cardiac and pulmonary auscultation abolished, mydriatic and non-photoreactive pupils, without oculocephalic or corneopalpebral reflexes. death certificate at {omitted}. admission: 2020-06-25, discharge: 2020-08-07, stay: 01 m 12 d 20 h, location: ward, payer: sus. death certificate at {omitted} Id: the patient, 59 years old, from the city, patient with systemic arterial high BP (hypertension), chronic kidney problem (kidney disease) on kidney dialysis (hemodialysis), at the hospital monday, wednesday, friday, heart patient with a pacemaker. On june 14th, he started with erythema at the dialysis access site in the left femoral vein. On friday, june 18th, on kidney dialysis (hemodialysis), [organization] evaluated the catheter and recommended hospitalization at the hospital for replacement and investigation of lower back pain with MRI scan (magnetic resonance) imaging and sciatica. During hospitalization, he did not present hemodynamic instability or other complications. He was sent home from hospital (discharge)d from the hospital on june 22nd with a schedule to receive a dose of 1000 mg of vancomycin on dialysis. At home, he remained well until wednesday night, june 23rd, when {person} suddenly noticed unresponsiveness and drowsiness, which prompted him to call emergency medical services. The patient was admitted to the emergency care unit with significant low BP (hypotension) and blood pressure of 60/40. High doses of norepinephrine were give (administer)ed. Admission to the emergency unit on june 25 the patient resulted in a decrease in the alertness level (level of consciousness) and hemodynamic instability, requiring high doses of vasoactive drugs, norepinephrine and vasopressin. Orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) was required. Meropenem and vancomycin were started. Blood test to find infection (blood culture) collected on admission showed multidrug-resistant klebsiella. Infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic)s were maintained due to gravity. A CT scan (computed tomography) scan showed a larger bilateral psoas pus-filled swelling (abscess) on the left and spine bone infection (spondylodiscitis) in l2 and l3, contiguous to the psoas pus-filled swelling (abscess)es. The pus-filled swelling (abscess) was punctured on the left by radiointervention, with growth of multidrug-resistant escherichia coli. The patient developed fever and blood test to find infection (blood culture) showed multidrug-resistant acinetobacter. Amikacin was started on july 5. Transthoracic transesophageal echocardiography was performed, without vegetations. The patient showed clinical improvement, and breathing tube removed (extubation) was performed on 07/06. Evaluated by orthopedics, who did not indicate surgical approaches. A new CT scan (computed tomography) scan was performed on 08/02, with puncture of psoas collections bilaterally, with the appearance of hematoma. Control culture of these collections showed no bacterial growth, and control CT scan (computed tomography) scan on 08/04 showed improvement, which was discussed with the patient from [organization], opting to deescalate infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) to cefuroxime, given the sensitivity of the isolated escherichia coli. On 08/06, a permcath implant was performed for kidney dialysis (hemodialysis), with precordialgia and difficulty in breathing (dyspnea) in the first 30 minutes of dialysis. A chest pain protocol was performed, with no changes in the ECG/heart tracing (electrocardiogram), chest x-ray, or myocardial enzyme levels. The patient began to become hemodynamically unstable and hypotensive at 08/07. As the patient with end-stage chronic renal disease, already evaluated post-operatively [organization], which defined proportionate comfort-focused (not curative) care, . On the morning of 08/07, the patient was found unresponsive, with non-palpable carotid pulse, in apnea, cardiac and pulmonary auscultation abolished, mydriatic and non-photoreactive pupils, without oculocephalic or corneopalpebral reflexes. Death certificate at . Admission: 2020-06-25, sent home from hospital (discharge): 2020-08-07, stay: 01 m 12 d 20 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Death certificate at The patient, 59 years old, was diagnosed with chronic kidney problem (kidney disease) in the hospital. He had a heart attack, and had to be treated at the hospital after kidney dialysis (hemodialysis). He started with erythema at the doctor's access site in the left femoral vein. On friday, june 18th, the doctor called him to call emergency medical services. The patient was admitted to the emergency care unit with significant low BP (hypotension) and blood pressure. He was sent home from hospital (discharge)d from the hospital on june 22nd with a schedule to receive a dose of 1000 mg of vancomycin on dialysis. At home, he didn't have enough time to get the patient to go to the hospital for treatment. This was the first time that the patient had been treated in a hospital in the city. The hospital had a specialized hospital, where he had no problem with the patient. The doctor said he had to wait for the patient's blood pressure and hemodynamic instability. However, the patient could not get enough blood pressure, and the hospital was not able to get it to be able to take care of the patients. This is why he was given a lot of medicines, especially if he had not been able to make sure that he was able to use the medicines for the treatment of the surgery. It's important to see if the Blood test to find infection (blood culture) collected on admission showed multidrug-resistant klebsiella. Infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic)s were maintained due to gravity. A CT scan (computed tomography) scan showed a larger bilateral psoas pus-filled swelling (abscess) on the left and spine bone infection (spondylodiscitis) in l2 and l3, contiguous to the psoas pus-filled swelling (abscess)es. The pus-filled swelling (abscess) was punctured on the left by radiointervention, with growth of multidrug-resistant escherichia coli. The patient developed fever and blood test to find infection (blood culture) showed multidrug-resistant acinetobacter. Amikacin was started on july 5. Transthoracic transesophageal echocardiography was performed, without vegetations. The patient showed clinical improvement, and breathing tube removed (extubation) was performed on 07/06. Evaluated by orthopedics, who did not indicate surgical approaches. A new CT scan (computed tomography) scan was performed on 08/02, with puncture of psoas collections bilaterally, with the appearance of hematoma. Control culture of these collections showed no bacterial growth, and control CT scan (computed tomography) scan on 08/04 showed improvement, which was discussed with the patient from [organization], opting to deescalate infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) to cefuroxime, given the sensitivity of the isolated escherichia coli. On 08/06, a permcath implant was performed for kidney dialysis (hemodialysis), with precordialgia and difficulty in breathing (dyspnea) in the first 30 minutes of dialysis. A chest pain protocol was performed, with no changes in the ECG/heart tracing (electrocardiogram), chest x-ray, or myocardial enzyme levels. The patient began to become hemodynamically unstable and hypotensive at 08/07. As the patient with end-stage chronic renal disease, already evaluated post-operatively [organization], which defined proportionate comfort-focused (not curative) care, . On the morning of 08/07, the patient was found unresponsive, with non-palpable carotid pulse, in apnea, cardiac and pulmonary auscultation abolished, mydriatic and non-photoreactive pupils, without oculocephalic or corneopalpebral reflexes. Death certificate at . Admission: 2020-06-25, sent home from hospital (discharge): 2020-08-07, stay: 01 m 12 d 20 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Severe acute respiratory syndrome by coronavirus 19, onset of symptoms: 05/20 dry cough, difficulty in breathing (dyspnea) worsening of symptoms: 05/29 hospitalization: 05/29 difficulty in breathing (dyspnea) and deoxygen level in blood (saturation), positive external swab: 05/24, machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation) initiated: 05/30, proning first 06/06 5pm 06/07 11am: 71.7116 monday 06/08 10:15pm: pre 147 149 third 06/09 at 11:30pm to 06/10 at 5:30pm wednesday 06/11 9:15pm: paraf pre 113 91.2 supine 12:35am thursday 06/13 8pm total supine at 8:15pm on 06/14 friday 06/15 10h total for pre-146 post-175 2. Acute kidney injury kdigo iii secondary to pulmonary serious blood infection (sepsis), coronavirus, cr 10.99 and ur 298 03/05 refractory hyperkalemia, uremia, kidney dialysis (hemodialysis) started on 03/06, cdl in right femoral vein ultrasound of kidneys and urinary tract 03/06: no changes, personal history: obesity, no report of allergies, devices: orotracheal tube 8.5 30/05 indwelling urinary catheter 30/05 right internal jugular vein 10/06 cdl right femoral vein 03/06 nasoenteral tube, right femoral vein catheter 03/06 17/06, nasoenteral tube shilley 17/06, infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy): tigecycline 06/06 18/06 22/06 gentamicin 06/06 18/06 22/06 meropenem 17/06 previous: rocefin, azithromycin and cefepime fluconazole 16/06 23/06 vancomycin 16/06 22/06 corticotherapy methylpred 08/06 cultures: blood test to find infection (blood culture) 03/06: negative 07/06: 1 sample gram positive coccus bacillus sp 14/06: growth of gram negative bacillus. Urine culture 03/06: negative 06/06: 100000 iu candida tropicalis 14/06: candida tropicalis tracheal aspirate 03/06: acinetobacter baumanii sensitive to genta and amikacin 07/06: acinetobacter baumanii sensitive to genta and amikacin. Admission: 03/06/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 24/06/2020, stay: 21 d 11 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. The patient diagnosed with coronavirus 19, with onset of symptoms on 20/05/2020. Evolved with the need for orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) and admission to an ICU (serious care room) bed. Presented with ventilator-associated hospital-acquired (nosocomial) infection by acinetobacter and bloodstream infection by pseudomonas, evolving to refractory shock and death in . Tachypnea, deoxygen level in blood (saturation), lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) requiring orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) on 30/05/2020. 185 admission: 23/02/2020, discharge: 09/03/2020, stay: 14 d, location: ward, payer: sus. [person], 65 years old, born and raised in [address], widower. problem: asthenia, worsening of basal dyspnea, drowsiness, desaturation, bladder indwelling urinary catheter with pyuria, hypotension and worsening of perfusion diagnostic hypotheses: 1. mixed shock: septic of urinary focus, weaning from norepinephrine on 25/02, cardiogenic, heart failure, due to decompensated alcoholic cardiomyopathy profile, weaning from dobutamine on 03/03 started tridil 26/02 and oral vasodilators on 27/02/2020. 2. acute kidney injury kdigo 3 creatinine: 6.57 urea: 348: acute ischemic tubular necrosis secondary to infection, SCR type 1 on renal replacement therapy since 02/23/2020. cultures: blood culture 02/23/2020: carbapenem-resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae in 2 samples at 22 and 42 h. urine culture 02/23/2020: carbapenem-resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae. history: systemic arterial hypertension, hypothyroidism? chronic liver disease, active significant alcoholism, active smoking 110 pack-years, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, presumed pulmonology evaluation on 08/18. Evolution: despite the culture showing Klebsiella pneumoniae carbapenemase, [person] showed a good clinical response to meropenem, thus concluding the use of meropenem 1000 mg 1x/day 500 mg post renal replacement therapy for 14 days dated 02/23/2020. He evolved with improvement in infectious parameters, afebrile, with no genitourinary or cardiorespiratory complaints. Latest laboratory tests: Hb: 12.4 ht: 37 platelets: 123 gb: 7.5 without deviation to the left. Undergoing follow-up with nephrology, daily hemodialysis was performed. Schedule: with completion of antibiotic therapy and maintenance of improvement in clinical parameters, discharged from [organization] on 03/09/2020. Scheduled by nephrology for follow-up on acute dialysis on Mondays, Wednesdays and Fridays, in the first shift. Admission: 23/02/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 09/03/2020, stay: 14 d, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. The patient, 65 years old, born and raised in [address], widower. Problem: asthenia, worsening of basal difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), drowsiness, deoxygen level in blood (saturation), bladder indwelling urinary catheter with pyuria, low BP (hypotension) and worsening of perfusion diagnostic hypotheses: 1. Mixed shock: septic of urinary focus, weaning from norepinephrine on 25/02, cardiogenic, heart failure, due to decompensated alcoholic cardiomyopathy profile, weaning from dobutamine on 03/03 started tridil 26/02 and oral vasodilators on 27/02/2020. 2. Acute kidney injury kdigo 3 kidney function marker (creatinine): 6.57 urea: 348: acute reduced blood supply (ischemic) tubular tissue death (necrosis) secondary to infection, scr type 1 on renal replacement therapy since 02/23/2020. Cultures: blood test to find infection (blood culture) 02/23/2020: carbapenem-resistant klebsiella lung infection (pneumonia)e in 2 samples at 22 and 42 h. Urine culture 02/23/2020: carbapenem-resistant klebsiella lung infection (pneumonia)e. History: systemic arterial high BP (hypertension), hypothyroidism? Chronic liver disease, active significant alcoholism, active smoking 110 pack-years, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, presumed pulmonology evaluation on 08/18. Evolution: despite the culture showing klebsiella lung infection (pneumonia)e carbapenemase, the patient showed a good clinical response to meropenem, thus concluding the use of meropenem 1000 mg 1x/day 500 mg post renal replacement therapy for 14 days dated 02/23/2020. He evolved with improvement in infectious parameters, no fever (afebrile), with no genitourinary or cardiorespiratory complaints. Latest laboratory tests: hb: 12.4 ht: 37 platelets: 123 gb: 7.5 without deviation to the left. Undergoing return visit to doctor (follow-up) with nephrology, daily kidney dialysis (hemodialysis) was performed. Schedule: with completion of infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) and maintenance of improvement in clinical parameters, sent home from hospital (discharge)d from [organization] on 03/09/2020. 186 abdominal septic shock, bloodstream septic shock. admission: 03/15/2020, discharge: 03/29/2020, stay: 14 days 02 hours, location: ward: payer: sus. 1 septic shock abdominal focus decline in general condition diarrhea decreased level of consciousness onset of symptoms 03/14 orotracheal intubation on admission 03/15 due to decreased level of consciousness antibiotic therapy: meropenem din 03/15 2 atrial fibrillation [person] with irregular rhythm on admission despite no record of the comorbidity in the medical record, {person} of [person] confirms previous diagnosis of atrial fibrillation, electrocardiogram admission 03/15: atrial fibrillation 3 septic shock bloodstream 03/22: worsening of hemodynamics worsening of parameters in blood culture tests 03/22/2020: klebsiella pneumoniae. presenting complaint: decreased level of consciousness, respiratory failure, diarrhea, chest pain, history collected from [person], [person] and [person]: [person] observed a decreased level of consciousness this morning, with [person] aphasic, and presenting worsening of the basal hemiplegia. She evolved with respiratory distress, and the [organization] was called to provide assistance. at the scene, the [organization] team found blood pressure of 60x0 mmhg, non-measurable oxygen saturation, capillary refill time greater than 3 s, poorly perfused extremities, glasgow 6. 1000 ml of ringer's lactate and 500 ml of sf 0.9% were infused during transport and oxygen was administered 10 l/min via mask with reservoir. Norepinephrine 20 ml/h was started while still in the ambulance shortly before being admitted to the {organization}. [person] reports that [person] has had a progressive decline in her general health and diarrhea since yesterday, with 4 voluminous, watery bowel movements, without blood or pus, after 5 days of constipation. [person] also reports that [person] complained of chest pain this morning, but was unable to detail the characteristics of the pain. They deny fever, abdominal pain, dyspnea, cough, dysuria, nausea, vomiting, or other symptoms. [person] was admitted to the lower emergency unit, glasgow 9 3 1 5, with evident respiratory distress, pulse oxygen saturation not measurable, poorly perfused, capillary refill time greater than 3 s. Orotracheal intubation was performed on admission, {omitted}. Sedation with fentanyl and midazolam was initiated. Norepinephrine was maintained at 25 ml/h 0.382 mcg/kg/min. personal history: 1 systemic arterial hypertension 2 epilepsy in AEPA follow-up 3 nucleocapsular hemorrhagic stroke ich1 / sich1 on 07/01 etiology: hypertensive, clinical: right hemiparesis, deviation of the labial rhyme to the left, confusion nihss admission 17 1b.2 / 4.2 / 5d.3 / 5e.1 / 6d.3 / 6e.2 / 8.1 / 9.1 / 10.2 previous rankin: 0 computed tomography of the skull admission: intraparenchymal thalamocapsular hemorrhage 7.7 ml CT Angiography (CTA): no stenosis or significant vascular changes identified during previous hospitalization 07/01, left hemothorax with atelectasis due to fractures of the ribs t6t11 trauma prior to hospitalization resolved with thoracic drainage fracture of the left transverse process of t7 and fractures due to demineralization from t6t11 continuous use medications: enalapril 10 mg 12/12h amlodipine 10 mg hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg carbamazepine 700 mg/day phenobarbital 100 mg/day propranolol 40 mg [person] brought, but does not know how to inform the dose, laboratory tests: 03/16 hb 12.5 ht 38 leuco 22,000 plt 282,000 na 129 k 4.6 ca 1.04 cr 1.4 lact 3.7 urine routine nitrite leuco 20,000 red blood cells 3540 granular casts 03/18: hb 11.2 ht 34 leuco 15300 plt 150000 na 134 k 3.3 cr 0.88 ur 82 lactate 2.4 pcr 23.75 ldh 821 cultures: blood culture 03/15: no growth. urine culture 03/15: no growth. tracheal aspirate 03/16: no growth. blood culture 03/22: klebsiella pneumoniae. antibiotics on admission meropenem 03/15 03/16 ceftriaxone metronidazole 03/16 03/21 meropenam vancomycin 03/22 03/27 ceftazidime avibactam 03/28 03/29 imaging tests: transthoracic echocardiogram 03/17: moderate dilation of the left atrium and mild dilation of the left atrium and ventricle. markedly depressed systolic performance of the ventricles. alterations in segmental mobility of the left ventricle. moderate mitral valve insufficiency. mild pulmonary valve insufficiency. in time, late in the afternoon, I am called to evaluate [person] in a semi-intensive bed. I find [person] monitored with orotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation with the following vital signs on the monitor: heart rate: 0 beats per minute, no electrical activity identified by the monitor and no invasive blood pressure curve on the monitor. no signal also on pulse oximetry. I perform a physical examination with absent central pulses. I perform a physical examination with absent central pulses. I proceed with a physical examination of the trunk reflex with absence of oculocephalic reflexes, absence of corneal-palpal reflex and fixed medium pupils that do not react to light, without consensual reflex. I certify death at {omitted}. surgical tracheostomy 03/27. death. Abdominal severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock), bloodstream severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock). Admission: 03/15/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 03/29/2020, stay: 14 days 02 hours, location: general hospital room (ward): payer: government health scheme. 1 severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) abdominal focus decline in general condition diarrhea decreased alertness level (level of consciousness) onset of symptoms 03/14 orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) on admission 03/15 due to decreased alertness level (level of consciousness) infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy): meropenem din 03/15 2 irregular heartbeat (atrial fibrillation) the patient with irregular rhythm on admission despite no record of the other existing health condition (comorbidity) in the medical record, {person} of the patient confirms previous diagnosis of irregular heartbeat (atrial fibrillation), ECG/heart tracing (electrocardiogram) admission 03/15: irregular heartbeat (atrial fibrillation) 3 severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) bloodstream 03/22: worsening of hemodynamics worsening of parameters in blood test to find infection (blood culture) tests 03/22/2020: klebsiella lung infection (pneumonia)e. Presenting complaint: decreased alertness level (level of consciousness), lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure), diarrhea, chest pain, history collected from the patient, the patient and the patient: the patient observed a decreased alertness level (level of consciousness) this morning, with the patient aphasic, and presenting worsening of the basal hemiplegia. She evolved with respiratory distress, and the [organization] was called to provide assistance. At the scene, the [organization] team found blood pressure of 60x0 mmhg, non-measurable oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation), capillary refill time greater than 3 s, poorly perfused extremities, glasgow 6. 1000 ml of ringer's lactate and 500 ml of saline solution 0.9% were infused during transport and oxygen was give (administer)ed 10 l/min via mask with reservoir. Norepinephrine 20 ml/h was started while still in the ambulance shortly before being admitted to the the hospital. The patient reports that the patient systemic arterial high BP had a progressive decline in her general health and diarrhea since yesterday, with 4 voluminous, watery bowel movements, without blood or pus, after 5 days of constipation. The patient also reports that the patient complained of chest pain this morning, but was unable to detail the characteristics of the pain. They deny fever, abdominal pain, difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), cough, dysuria, nausea, vomiting, or other symptoms. The patient was admitted to the lower emergency unit, glasgow 9 3 1 5, with evident respiratory distress, pulse oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation) not measurable, poorly perfused, capillary refill time greater than 3 s. Orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) was performed on admission, . Sedation with fentanyl and midazolam was initiated. Norepinephrine was maintained at 25 ml/h 0.382 mcg/kg/min. Personal history: 1 systemic arterial high BP 2 seizure disorder (epilepsy) in aepa return visit to doctor (follow-up) 3 nucleocapsular hemorrhagic stroke ich1 / sich1 on 07/01 cause of disease (etiology): hypertensive, clinical: right hemiparesis, deviation of the labial rhyme to the left, confusion nihss admission 17 1b.2 / 4.2 / 5d.3 / 5e.1 / 6d.3 / 6e.2 / 8.1 / 9.1 / 10.2 previous rankin: 0 CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull admission: intraparenchymal thalamocapsular hemorrhage 7.7 ml ct angiography (cta): no stenosis or significant vascular changes identified during previous hospitalization 07/01, left hemothorax with atelectasis due to fractures of the ribs t6t11 trauma prior to hospitalization resolved with thoracic drainage fracture of the left transverse process of t7 and fractures due to demineralization from t6t11 continuous use medications: enalapril 10 mg 12/12h amlodipine 10 mg hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg carbamazepine 700 mg/day phenobarbital 100 mg/day propranolol 40 mg the patient brought, but does not know how to inform the dose, laboratory tests: 03/16 hb 12.5 ht 38 leuco 22,000 plt 282,000 na 129 k 4.6 ca 1.04 cr 1.4 lact 3.7 urine routine nitrite leuco 20,000 red blood cells 3540 granular casts 03/18: hb 11.2 ht 34 leuco 15300 plt 150000 na 134 k 3.3 cr 0.88 ur 82 lactate 2.4 pcr 23.75 ldh 821 cultures: blood test to find infection (blood culture) 03/15: no growth. Urine culture 03/15: no growth. Tracheal aspirate 03/16: no growth. Blood test to find infection (blood culture) 03/22: klebsiella lung infection (pneumonia)e. Infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic)s on admission meropenem 03/15 03/16 ceftriaxone metronidazole 03/16 03/21 meropenam vancomycin 03/22 03/27 ceftazidime avibactam 03/28 03/29 imaging tests: transthoracic heart ultrasound (echocardiogram) 03/17: moderate dilation of the left atrium and mild dilation of the left atrium and ventricle. Markedly depressed systolic performance of the ventricles. Alterations in segmental mobility of the left ventricle. Moderate mitral valve insufficiency. Mild pulmonary valve insufficiency. In time, late in the afternoon, i am called to evaluate the patient in a semi-intensive bed. I find the patient monitored with orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) and machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation) with the following vital signs on the monitor: heart rate: 0 beats per minute, no electrical activity identified by the monitor and no invasive blood pressure curve on the monitor. No signal also on pulse oximetry. I perform a physical examination with absent central pulses. I perform a physical examination with absent central pulses. I proceed with a physical examination of the trunk reflex with absence of oculocephalic reflexes, absence of corneal-palpal reflex and fixed medium pupils that do not react to light, without consensual reflex. I certify death at . Surgical breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) 03/27. Death. The patient was hospitalized at the hc complex from 6/13 to 7/13 due to severe acute respiratory distress syndrome due to coronavirus infection. She required orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) and machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation) and remained in the ICU (serious care room) between . The patient presented hospital-acquired (nosocomial) infections during hospitalization, requiring broad-spectrum infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy). Was transferred to a general hospital room (ward) bed to wean off oxygen therapy and end infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) on 07/05. Responded well to infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic)s and systemic arterial high BP (hypertension) been on room air since 07/11, with good oxygen level in blood (saturation) and no respiratory symptoms. Due to prolonged hospital stay and need for machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation) with neuromuscular blockers, the patient developed critical patient polyneuromyopathy. The patient was sent home from hospital (discharge)d on 07/13, with scheduled motor physical therapy at [organization]. Followed up at a basic health unit for physical therapy. Systemic arterial high BP (hypertension) a return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment appointment scheduled at [organization] on 07/22. Systemic arterial high BP (hypertension) a return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment appointment scheduled at [organization] on 09/03 for tests. 188 1. coronavirus infection onset of symptoms 02/17 orotracheal intubation 02/24 extubation 03/08 2. septic shock from pulmonary focus requiring vasoactive drug resolved: antibiotic therapy: ceftriaxone 02/23 03/02 clarithromycin 04/24 03/02 corticosteroid therapy: 02/24 03/09 3. critically ill polyneuropathy grade 2 strength upper limbs and lower limbs 4. pressure injury left lower limb grade i 5. your appointment scheduled with gynecology. on 03/24 for investigation. admission: 02/24/2020, discharge: 03/19/2020, stay: 22 d 21 h, location: ward, payer: sus. current: [person] with severe acute respiratory syndrome due to coronavirus, onset of symptoms on 02/17, requiring orotracheal intubation due to respiratory failure on 02/24, due to the possibility of associated bacterial pneumonia, antibiotic therapy with ceftriaxone and clarithromycin was performed. [person] presented satisfactory clinical evolution, and extubation was performed on 03/08, without complications. Gradual weaning of oxygen therapy was performed, and oxygen was withdrawn on 03/17, at the time comfortable in room air. Due to prolonged use of neuromuscular blocker to manage mechanical ventilation, [person] developed critically ill polyneuropathy, currently with grade II motor strength in the upper and lower limbs. He also has a pressure injury in the left lower limb, which is improving, with no signs of infection. Insulin therapy was started to manage hyperglycemia, with weaning after optimization of oral metformin. It is worth mentioning that during hospitalization [person] presented abnormal vaginal bleeding, and an appointment with gynecology was scheduled for 03/24 for investigation. Good general condition, hydrated, normal color, acyanotic, anicteric, afebrile; respiratory auscultation: vesicular breath sounds with crackles on a residual basis, without respiratory distress; cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm in two beats; normal sound sounds without murmurs; abdomen: innocent; extremities without edema, free calves, capillary refill time less than 3 s, ulcer on the left lower limb, grade I. therapeutic plan: I speak with [person] about the importance of performing pulmonary and motor physiotherapy to manage polyneuropathy in critically ill patients. He states that he will pay for home physiotherapy. He is referred to a basic health unit: management of hypertension and diabetes. Dressing of ulcer in the left lower limb region. I prescribe a dressing with AGE. Guidance from the nutrition team regarding the home diet. I follow-up appointment to the GEAD outpatient clinic on 04/26 for clinical reassessment. I follow-up appointment to the speech therapy outpatient clinic on 05/05. I schedule a pelvic ultrasound for 03/22 and a surgical gynecology outpatient clinic for 03/24. I investigate uterine bleeding. against reference [organization]. Good general condition, maintaining polyneuropathy in critically ill patients, with improvement in motor strength grade II. 1.
