190 1. pneumonia, pulmonary metastasis? negative external rt pcr on 06/21 verified by surveillance on 06/23 2. syncope to be clarified neurocardiogenic? investigation suspended 3. consumptive syndrome due to duodenal papilla neoplasia. admission: 06/23/2020, discharge: 06/30/2020, stay: 07 d 05 h, location: ward, payer: sus. history of current illness admission: [person] referred from the emergency care unit, where he was hospitalized for 4 days. reports that 3 days ago he had a sudden episode of loss of consciousness, not preceded by prodromes, with follow-up appointment after a few seconds. level of consciousness without dysfunctions afterwards, without release of sphincters. reports only significant asthenia and fatigue associated. had 2 of these episodes on this day. had already had another similar syncope 20 days ago. He had several episodes of vomiting, which started 5 days ago and improved 2 days ago. His family called [organization] due to his condition. During the evaluation by the transport team, [person] reported an episode of fever (38.3 °C) and dry cough since 06/10. During hospitalization at [organization], he was given antibiotic therapy and referred [person] to our services. His syncope did not recur. He reported diarrhea for 15 days and improvement for 3 days, which is why his chemotherapy cycle was suspended. Upon admission, [person] was hemodynamically stable, breathing with a mask with a reservoir at 10 L/min, without respiratory distress, and denying any complaints at the moment. He is afebrile. Edema of the lower limbs, crackles, and reduced vesicular breath sounds on the left. [person] was pale and emaciated. No relevant results. Pleural fluid puncture and chest drain. Pneumonia, no pending tests. Antibiotic therapy: cefepime and clarithromycin. [person] evolves into death. 1. Lung infection (pneumonia), pulmonary metastasis? Negative external rt pcr on 06/21 verified by surveillance on 06/23 2. Syncope to be clarified neurocardiogenic? Investigation government health schemepended 3. Consumptive syndrome due to duodenal papilla neoplasia. Admission: 06/23/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 06/30/2020, stay: 07 d 05 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. History of current illness admission: the patient referred from the emergency care unit, where he was hospitalized for 4 days. Reports that 3 days ago he had a sudden episode of loss of consciousness, not preceded by prodromes, with return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment after a few seconds. Alertness level (level of consciousness) without dysfunctions aftergeneral hospital rooms, without release of sphincters. Reports only significant asthenia and fatigue associated. Had 2 of these episodes on this day. Had already had another similar syncope 20 days ago. He had several episodes of vomiting, which started 5 days ago and improved 2 days ago. His family called [organization] due to his condition. During the evaluation by the transport team, the patient reported an episode of fever (38.3 °c) and dry cough since 06/10. During hospitalization at [organization], he was given infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) and referred the patient to our services. His syncope did not recur. He reported diarrhea for 15 days and improvement for 3 days, which is why his chemotherapy cycle was government health schemepended. Upon admission, the patient was stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), breathing with a mask with a reservoir at 10 l/min, without respiratory distress, and denying any complaints at the moment. He is no fever (afebrile). Swelling (edema) of the lower limbs, crackles, and reduced normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds) on the left. The patient was pale and emaciated. No relevant results. Pleural fluid puncture and chest drain. Lung infection (pneumonia), no pending tests. Infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy): cefepime and clarithromycin. The patient evolves into death. 1. Lung infection (pneumonia), pulmonary metastasis? Negative external rt pcr on 06/21 verified by surveillance on 06/23 2. Syncope to be clarified neurocardiogenic? Investigation government health schemepended 3. Consumptive syndrome due to duodenal papilla neoplasia. Admission: 06/23/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 06/30/2020, stay: 07 d 05 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. History of current illness admission: the patient referred from the emergency care unit, where he was hospitalized for 4 days. Reports that 3 days ago he had a sudden episode of loss of consciousness, not preceded by prodromes, with return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment after a few seconds. Alertness level (level of consciousness) without dysfunctions aftergeneral hospital rooms, without release of sphincters. Reports only significant asthenia and fatigue associated. Had 2 of these episodes on this day. Had already had another similar syncope 20 days ago. He had several episodes of vomiting, which started 5 days ago and improved 2 days ago. His family called [organization] due to his condition. During the evaluation by the transport team, the patient reported an episode of fever (38.3 °c) and dry cough since 06/10. During hospitalization at [organization], he was given infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) and referred the patient to our services. His syncope did not recur. The patient was suffering from diarrhea for 15 days and was being treated for 3 days, which is why his chemotherapy cycle was government health schemepended. After the surgery, he was able to breathe well, breathing with a mask with a reservoir at 10 l/min, without respiratory distress, and denying any complaints at the moment. The patient's condition worsened with the lower limbs, crackles, and reduced normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds) on the left and emaciated. The doctor said that the patient had no problems with the treatment. The doctors said that he needed more tests to find out if he had a problem with the patient. They found that he had no symptoms of the disease, and he had to go to the hospital to see if the patient could get the treatment needed. They said that it's important to see how the patient has been treated properly and how the treatment is being done. This means that the treatment can help the patient recover quickly and recover from the disease. Therapy: cefepime and clarithromycin. It helps the patient evolves into death. [Other medical details: stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), no fever (afebrile), swelling (edema).] a419. septicemia ns. c241. malignant neoplasm of ampulla of vater. e872. acidosis. n17. acute renal failure. j159. bacterial pneumonia ns. j939. ​​pneumothorax ns. c258. malignant neoplasm with invasive lesion of pancreas. d377. neoplasm of uncertain or unknown behavior of other digestive organs. j15. bacterial pneumonia not classified elsewhere. n18. chronic renal failure. death 46.3 1.51
191 1 severe acute respiratory syndrome, due to coronavirus. 2 new septic shock 01/14, fever hypotension, 3 acneiform eruption secondary to corticosteroid therapy, 4 pneumomediastinum airway injury? subcutaneous emphysema after depronation worsening ventilatory status, 5 new septic shock on 01/24 start of vasoactive drug, leukocytosis, 6 reactive adrenal insufficiency? hyperkalemia hyponatremia shock. admission: 01/06/2020, discharge: 03/03/2020, stay: 01 m 24 d 18 h, location: ward, payer: sus. clinical history [person] reports that he started with headache, myalgia and dry cough on 12/28, persisting with the same until 12/31, when he sought care and was prescribed prednisone, azithromycin, zinc and symptomatics. [person] started taking prescribed medications and on 02/01 he started with a fever, having performed a PCR for coronavirus with no result yet. On 03/01 he presented respiratory discomfort, having sought care on 04/01, when antibiotic therapy was changed to intravenous; the family member does not know which medication, however, there was no indication for hospital admission. [person] persisted with respiratory discomfort, having been hospitalized in the early hours of the same day. Emergency care unit report of oxygen saturation of 88% on room air, progressively worsening. He was admitted to the emergency unit on 06/01 with severe pulmonary involvement on chest x-ray, and was continued on antibiotic therapy initiated in the emergency care unit, ceftriaxone daily on 05/01 and prophylactic dexamethasone and enoxaparin initiated. He was admitted to [organization] on 07/01, requiring orotracheal intubation and central venous catheterization. He presented septic shock on 14/01, and his antibiotic regimen was changed. He remained in bed at [organization]. He developed new septic shock on 24/01, and began using fluconazole for fungal coverage. He developed pneumomediastinum on 26/01 due to airway injury, and underwent conservative treatment. He underwent tracheostomy on 29/01. He presented reactive adrenal insufficiency and acneiform rash related to corticosteroid therapy, in addition to diarrhea treated empirically with the hypothesis of pseudomembranous colitis. Acinetobacter baumanii was isolated in the tracheal aspirate, but was not treated due to the lack of clinical repercussion. He presented progressive clinical improvement and was transferred to [organization], where he remained stable. He underwent fibroscopy and was evaluated by speech therapy on 02/03, with no residual airway injury identified and a soft diet was released. He was decannulated on 03/03, remaining eupneic and in condition to be discharged. Good general condition, alert, acyanotic, anicteric, afebrile, Glasgow 15, oxygen saturation 94% in room air with capped tracheostomy, respiratory rate 28 inspirations per minute, muscle strength grade 4 in upper and lower limbs, regular heart rhythm in two beats, normal sound sounds without murmurs. Heart rate 76, vesicular breath sounds present, symmetrical, without adventitious sounds, without use of accessory muscles. Flaccid, flat abdomen, painless to palpation, without masses or visceromegaly. Extremities: palpable peripheral pulses, without edema, absence of swelling of the calves. Presence of pressure ulcer in the left lower limb, without signs of infection. Tracheostomy fibroscopy. I advise [person] on outpatient follow-up visits for: head and neck surgery on 03/15 speech therapy on 03/25 reassessment of diet physiatry to be scheduled I advise [person] on home care for tracheostomy orifice, diet, exercise and care. I prescribe symptomatic medications. Aminc, head and neck surgery, speech therapy, physiatry. Good general condition, slim, conscious and oriented, moving all 4 limbs actively with grade 4 muscle strength, still not able to walk alone. Tracheostomy orifice occluded with dressing and eating a soft diet. No pain complaints. 1 severe acute respiratory syndrome, due to coronavirus. 2 new severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) 01/14, fever low BP (hypotension), 3 acneiform eruption secondary to corticosteroid therapy, 4 pneumomediastinum airway injury? Subcutaneous emphysema after depronation worsening ventilatory status, 5 new severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) on 01/24 start of vasoactive drug, high white blood cell count (leukocytosis), 6 reactive adrenal insufficiency? Hyperkalemia hyponatremia shock. Admission: 01/06/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 03/03/2020, stay: 01 m 24 d 18 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Clinical history the patient reports that he started with headache, myalgia and dry cough on 12/28, persisting with the same until 12/31, when he sought care and was prescribed prednisone, azithromycin, zinc and symptomatics. The patient started taking prescribed medications and on 02/01 he started with a fever, having performed a pcr for coronavirus with no result yet. On 03/01 he presented respiratory discomfort, having sought care on 04/01, when infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) was changed to given through a vein (drip/IV); the family member does not know which medication, however, there was no indication for hospital admission. The patient persisted with respiratory discomfort, having been hospitalized in the early hours of the same day. Emergency care unit report of oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation) of 88% on room air, progressively worsening. He was admitted to the emergency unit on 06/01 with severe pulmonary involvement on chest x-ray, and was continued on infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) initiated in the emergency care unit, ceftriaxone daily on 05/01 and prophylactic dexamethasone and enoxaparin initiated. He was admitted to [organization] on 07/01, requiring orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) and central venous catheterization. He presented severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) on 14/01, and his infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) regimen was changed. He remained in bed at [organization]. He developed new severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) on 24/01, and began using fluconazole for fungal coverage. He developed pneumomediastinum on 26/01 due to airway injury, and underwent conservative treatment. He underwent breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) on 29/01. He presented reactive adrenal insufficiency and acneiform rash related to corticosteroid therapy, in addition to diarrhea treated empirically with the hypothesis of pseudomembranous colitis. Acinetobacter baumanii was isolated in the tracheal aspirate, but was not treated due to the lack of clinical repercussion. He presented progressive clinical improvement and was transferred to [organization], where he remained stable. He underwent fibroscopy and was evaluated by speech therapy on 02/03, with no residual airway injury identified and a soft diet was released. He was decannulated on 03/03, remaining breathing normally (eupneic) and in condition to be sent home from hospital (discharge)d. Good general condition, alert, no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), no fever (afebrile), glasgow 15, oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation) 94% in room air with capped breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy), respiratory rate 28 inspirations per minute, muscle strength grade 4 in upper and lower limbs, regular heart rhythm in two beats, normal sound sounds without murmurs. Heart rate 76, normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds) present, symmetrical, without adventitious sounds, without use of accessory muscles. Flaccid, flat abdomen, painless to palpation, without masses or visceromegaly. Extremities: palpable peripheral pulses, without swelling (edema), absence of swelling of the calves. Presence of pressure ulcer in the left lower limb, without signs of infection. Breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) fibroscopy. I advise the patient on outpatient return visit to doctor visits for: head and neck surgery on 03/15 speech therapy on 03/25 reassessment of diet physiatry to be scheduled i advise the patient on home care for breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) orifice, diet, exercise and care. I prescribe symptomatic medications. Aminc, head and neck surgery, speech therapy, physiatry. Good general condition, slim, conscious and oriented, moving all 4 limbs actively with grade 4 muscle strength, still not able to walk alone. Breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) orifice occluded with dressing and eating a soft diet. 192 admission: 09/01/2020, discharge: 09/17/2020, stay: 16 d 13 h, location: ward, payer: SUS. A 67-year-old patient with a history of overweight, smoking, and major depression was admitted on 09/01 with a perforative acute abdomen, diagnosed after examinations. On the evening of 08/31, she sought care with abdominal pain, general malaise, and dyspnea. She had undergone hysterectomy on 08/30. On 09/01, she underwent exploratory laparotomy with a finding of intestinal perforation, with a suture of the lesion and placement of a peritoneostomy. She developed severe circulatory shock, probably due to peritonitis from contamination of the abdominal cavity, and antibiotic treatment was promptly started. She was re-addressed on 09/06, where an appendectomy and abdominal wall closure were performed. On 09/07, the patient's ventilatory status began to worsen, and a chest tomography scan was performed on 09/08, revealing low-load pulmonary embolism (5%) in addition to pneumonia. A multiresistant germ was isolated in a lung culture, with specific treatment directed at the patient. Given the clinical progression, a tracheostomy was indicated due to the need for prolonged mechanical ventilation. On 09/15, {omitted} prolonged cardiopulmonary arrest was reversed after approximately 20 minutes. The patient follow-up appointmented to the intensive care unit after the procedure, with mydriatic pupils and absence of reflexes. On 09/17, a computed tomography scan of the skull was performed, revealing anoxic-ischemic encephalopathy. Tests were performed to confirm brain death, which was confirmed on {omitted}. Perforating acute abdomen. 09/01: computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis: pneumoperitoneum, ascites, signs of peritonitis, parietal hyperalces and edema of the loops 09/01: computed tomography of the chest, pulmonary congestion, cardiomegaly, bilateral pleural effusion, opacities suggestive of an inflammatory or infectious process 09/08: computed tomography of the chest, pulmonary thromboembolism with 5% embolic burden 09/17: computed tomography of the skull. 09/01: exploratory laparotomy, sigmoid suture, creation of peritoneostomy 09/06: review, touch-ups on previous surgery with appendectomy and closure of the abdominal wall 09/15: tracheostomy 09/15: bilateral chest drainage. death. Admission: 09/01/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 09/17/2020, stay: 16 d 13 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme (sus). A 67-year-old patient with a history of overweight, smoking, and major depression was admitted on 09/01 with a perforative acute abdomen, diagnosed after examinations. On the evening of 08/31, she sought care with abdominal pain, general malaise, and difficulty in breathing (dyspnea). She had undergone hysterectomy on 08/30. On 09/01, she underwent exploratory laparotomy with a finding of intestinal perforation, with a suture of the lesion and placement of a peritoneostomy. She developed severe circulatory shock, probably due to infection inside the abdomen (peritonitis) from contamination of the abdominal cavity, and infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment was promptly started. She was re-addressed on 09/06, where an appendectomy and abdominal wall closure were performed. On 09/07, the patient's ventilatory status began to worsen, and a chest tomography scan was performed on 09/08, revealing low-load pulmonary blood clot blocking a vessel (embolism) (5%) in addition to lung infection (pneumonia). A multiresistant germ was isolated in a lung culture, with specific treatment directed at the patient. Given the clinical progression, a breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) was indicated due to the need for prolonged machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation). On 09/15, prolonged cardiopulmonary arrest was reversed after approximately 20 minutes. The patient return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointmented to the ICU (serious care room) after the procedure, with mydriatic pupils and absence of reflexes. On 09/17, a CT scan (computed tomography) scan of the skull was performed, revealing anoxic-reduced blood supply (ischemic) brain dysfunction (encephalopathy). Tests were performed to confirm brain death, which was confirmed on . Perforating acute abdomen. 09/01: CT scan (computed tomography) of the abdomen and pelvis: pneumoperitoneum, fluid buildup in abdomen (ascites), signs of infection inside the abdomen (peritonitis), parietal hyperalces and swelling (edema) of the loops 09/01: CT scan (computed tomography) of the chest, pulmonary congestion, cardiomegaly, bilateral fluid around the lungs, opacities suggestive of an inflammatory or infectious process 09/08: CT scan (computed tomography) of the chest, pulmonary thromboblood clot blocking a vessel (embolism) with 5% embolic burden 09/17: CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull. 09/01: exploratory laparotomy, sigmoid suture, creation of peritoneostomy 09/06: review, touch-ups on previous surgery with appendectomy and closure of the abdominal wall 09/15: breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) 09/15: bilateral chest drainage. Death. Admission: 09/01/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 09/17/2020, stay: 16 d 13 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme (sus). A 67-year-old patient with a history of overweight, smoking, and major depression was admitted on 09/01 with a perforative acute abdomen, diagnosed after examinations. On the evening of 08/31, she sought care with abdominal pain, general malaise, and difficulty in breathing (dyspnea). She had undergone hysterectomy on 08/30. On 09/01, she underwent exploratory laparotomy with a finding of intestinal perforation, with a suture of the lesion and placement of a peritoneostomy. She developed severe circulatory shock, probably due to infection inside the abdomen (peritonitis) from contamination of the abdominal cavity, and infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment was promptly started. She was re-addressed on 09/06, where an appendectomy and abdominal wall closure were performed. On 09/07, the patient's ventilatory status began to worsen, and a chest tomography scan was performed on 09/08, revealing low-load pulmonary blood clot blocking a vessel (embolism) (5%) in addition to lung infection (pneumonia). A multiresistant germ was isolated in a lung culture, with specific treatment directed at the patient. Given the clinical progression, a breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) was indicated due to the need for prolonged machine-assisted breathing (mechanical ventilation). On 09/15, prolonged cardiopulmonary arrest was reversed after approximately 20 minutes. The patient return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointmented to the ICU (serious care room) after the procedure, with mydriatic pupils and absence of reflexes. On 09/17, a CT scan (computed tomography) scan of the skull was performed, revealing anoxic-reduced blood supply (ischemic) brain dysfunction (encephalopathy). Tests were performed to confirm brain death, which was confirmed on . Perforating acute abdomen. 09/01: CT scan (computed tomography) of the abdomen and pelvis: pneumoperitoneum, fluid buildup in abdomen (ascites), signs of infection inside the abdomen (peritonitis), parietal hyperalces and swelling (edema) of the loops 09/01: CT scan (computed tomography) of the chest, pulmonary congestion, cardiomegaly, bilateral fluid around the lungs, opacities suggestive of an inflammatory or infectious process 09/08: CT scan (computed tomography) of the chest, pulmonary thromboblood clot blocking a vessel (embolism) with 5% embolic burden 09/17: CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull. 09/01: exploratory laparotomy, sigmoid suture, creation of peritoneostomy 09/06: review, touch-ups on previous surgery with appendectomy and closure of the abdominal wall 09/15: breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) 09/15: bilateral chest drainage. Death. r100. acute abdomen. r579. shock na. z540. convalescence after surgery. i26. pulmonary embolism. i460. cardiac arrest with successful resuscitation. j189. pneumonia n.e. death 19.2 1.22

Admission: 11/08/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 12/06/2020, stay: 28 d 04 h, location: general hospital room (ward) payer: government health scheme (sus). 14 days ago, he started to have a blackened appearance and lesions on his right lower limb. Until then he was walking. Associated with pain without improvement with analgesics, but he did not seek the patient. 2 days ago, he presented a bad smell, lesions on his right lower limb and a fall from his own height in the bathroom, without traumatic brain injury, without loss of consciousness, at which time he sought the patient on 11/06, since then awaiting transfer as an inpatient. He reports that he was agitated and contactful upon admission at the origin, but after medication he became more drowsy. Hu: concentrated diuresis in an indwelling urinary catheter. Hi: companion was unable to provide information. Upon admission, he was stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), without vasoactive drugs. Oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation) 100% room air. Respiratory rate 24, heart rate 60, blood pressure 110x60, temperature 37.3. Drowsy, barely contacting glasgow 13 at 3 / rv 4 / rm 6. With passive warming of the right lower limb. The patient underwent amputation of the right lower limb, developed acute kidney injury and several infectious complications during hospitalization, and was finally given comfort-focused (not curative) care . The patient died on at . Absent pulses, unresponsive with mydriatic pupils, cold extremities, with inaudible heart sounds. Death. 1 peripheral arterial obstructive disease critical ischemia / oaa? Rhabdomyolysis? Prerenal? New image on chest x-ray on 12/04, with increased secretion aspiration and decreased alertness level (level of consciousness) measures started to reduce secretion. Admission: 11/08/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 12/06/2020, stay: 28 d 04 h, location: general hospital room (ward) payer: government health scheme (sus). 14 days ago, he started to have a blackened appearance and lesions on his right lower limb. Until then he was walking. Associated with pain without improvement with analgesics, but he did not seek the patient. 2 days ago, he presented a bad smell, lesions on his right lower limb and a fall from his own height in the bathroom, without traumatic brain injury, without loss of consciousness, at which time he sought the patient on 11/06, since then awaiting transfer as an inpatient. He reports that he was agitated and contactful upon admission at the origin, but after medication he became more drowsy. Hu: concentrated diuresis in an indwelling urinary catheter. Hi: companion was unable to provide information. Upon admission, he was stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), without vasoactive drugs. Oxygen oxygen level in blood (saturation) 100% room air. Respiratory rate 24, heart rate 60, blood pressure 110x60, temperature 37.3. Drowsy, barely contacting glasgow 13 at 3 / rv 4 / rm 6. With passive warming of the right lower limb. The patient underwent amputation of the right lower limb, developed acute kidney injury and several infectious complications during hospitalization, and was finally given comfort-focused (not curative) care . The patient died on at . Absent pulses, unresponsive with mydriatic pupils, cold extremities, with inaudible heart sounds, and heart beats. The patient's condition worsened during the hospitalization. It's hard to see if the patient had a heart attack. It might be a big problem for the patient to learn about his death. This article talks about the death of the patient in the hospital about his heart. Read more →</s> n170. acute renal failure with tubular necrosis. n17. acute renal failure. a419. septicemia ns. c61. malignant neoplasm of prostate. i702. atherosclerosis of arteries of extremities. i739. peripheral vascular diseases ns. i74. arterial embolism and thrombosis death 25.1 1.30
194 date of admission: 10/04 on d11 of i mcbad and d5 of filgrastim d1 on 09/24 date of discharge: 11/03 reason for admission: treatment of bacterial and fungal endocarditis. admission: 10/04/2022, discharge: 11/04/2022, stay: 01 m 23 h, location: ward, payer: sus. case report: refractory multiple myeloma iga lambda a vi vcd iss without b2mgb/ds iiia diagnosis {omitted} relapse: anemia elevated iga and lambda pleural effusion with cytology 41% atypical plasma cells extramedullary plasmacytoma abdomen and pleura pleural biopsy 04/22: cells with scant eosinophilic cytoplasm and small, round, hyperchromatic nuclei at the cell periphery. ihq: strong and demarcated expression of cd 56, cd 138, and lambda plasmacytoid differentiation. kappa. ki67: 95%. diagnosis: plasmacytoma, multiple myeloma. abdominal biopsy 04/22: plasma cells infiltrating skeletal muscle tissue. frequent mitotic figures. no necrosis. ihq: cd138, cd56, lambda, kappa. pmo 04/16/21: diluted in sp. sv hypocellular. sb normocellular with preserved maturation. sm megakaryocytes absent in smear bmo 04/16: 35% cellularity. no neoplastic infiltration in the sample. tomography myeloma protocol 02/15/2022: awaiting report transthoracic echocardiography 04/22: ejection fraction 65% mild tricuspid regurgitation, psap 47. 2020 serologies: negative ecog: 1 previous chemotherapies: i vtd pace 07/31/2020 vi vtd d1 10/07/2020 to d1 03/10/2021 while awaiting krd disease progression previous therapeutic schedule krd total of xviii cycles [person]: performed v cycles, with disease progression emergence of plasmacytomas new treatment schedule, as discussed with [person] on 10/23: performance of mcbad for 2 to 2 cycles, while defining a new therapy. d1 i mcbad 09/24. examinations carried out during hospitalization: head tomography 05/10 done in the context of lowered level of consciousness, thrombocytopenia, bleeding ruled out. transthoracic echocardiography 10/08: preserved LVSD subjective s 8 pp 8 LVDD 45 it mild psap 50 mmhg rectification of the septum in the shape of a d extracardiac structure compressing the left atrium moderately depressed RVSD fac 20% transthoracic echocardiography 10/19: fe 62% LVDD 46 s 9.5 pp 9 filamentous images adhered to the atrial face measuring the largest 4 mm suggestive of mild IM vegetation, cultures: blood culture 10/04: staphylococcus mitis 2 samples blood culture 10/08: candida tropicalis sensitive to ketoconazole and amphotericin B micafungin and caspofungin not tested, blood culture 10/12: staphylococcus epidermidis 1 sample after 43 hours considered as a contaminant, antibiotic therapy used: cefepime: 10/04 10/05. escalated to 11/05 due to growth of streptococcus mittis in blood culture on 11/04 meropenem 10/05 10/20 vancomycin 10/05 11/03 micafungin 10/10 evaluation of other specialties: in medical records. I performed reassessment tests for the disease on the day of hospital discharge. I prescribe SLOK K 1 tablet every 12 hours. Please, whoever evaluates her at the blood center, checks the need to maintain replacement, advises warning signs and to contact the [organization] team if necessary. I request a purchase order for vorizonazole for secondary prophylaxis as instructed by the [organization] team. I schedule a transesophageal echocardiogram for 11/24. I leave pending tests to monitor eosinophilia and GGT changes, atrial fibrillation that, together with the rash presented after vancomycin on 10/31, raised the diagnostic hypothesis of DRESS syndrome by the dermatologist. Despite this hypothesis, [person] showed complete improvement of the rash and a decrease in the eosinophil count. Therefore, we opted for discharge due to low clinical suspicion at the time. I performed reassessment tests for the disease on the day of hospital discharge. hospitalization summary: [person] admitted on 10/04 on d11 of i mcbad d5 of [person] from [organization]. attended a consultation for clinical and laboratory evaluation after hospitalization 09/21 - 10/01. While waiting to be called for a consultation, she developed tremors and an increase in body temperature (pre-consultation temperature 35.6 during tremors 37.3). Due to neutropneic, she was admitted for treatment of febrile neutropenia with immediate initiation of cefepime. She was hospitalized from 09/21 to 10/01 due to dyspnea, when an extensive right lung lesion was identified, with extrinsic compression of the superior vena cava and right atrium. On September 24, the MCBAD regimen was initiated, but during the night of that date, he developed acute pulmonary edema (fluid overload), which resolved after noninvasive ventilation and furosemide. He remained in the intensive care unit for 3 days. He was discharged to the ward and, from there, to [address]. He underwent a control tomography on September 29, which showed complete resolution of compression and reduction of lung injury. After one day of hospitalization, on 10/05, she presented septic shock with need for vasoactive drugs and decreased level of consciousness, being referred to [organization], where she was hospitalized until 10/12. On 10/10, cultures with growth of yeast-like fungi were seen in blood culture 1 sample after 7:00 p.m. Candida tropicalis. Contacted [organization] and started on micafungin 100 mg 1x/day, as well as fungemia protocol, removal of central access and PIA devices, implantation of peripherally inserted central catheter and collection of catheter tip. In addition to abdominal and fundus ultrasound. The transthoracic echocardiogram showed no evidence of vegetations on examination. Therefore, transthoracic echocardiography was performed on 10/19 with the presence of filamentous images in the mitral valve suggestive of infectious vegetation, associated with mild mitral insufficiency, confirming the diagnostic hypothesis of bacterial and fungal endocarditis. Discussed with [person] from [organization] on 10/18, who advised to maintain micafungin because most Candida tropicalis are sensitive. Chest tomography was repeated on 10/18, showing control of multiple myeloma with extensive bone and right pleural disease, with disease stability in relation to 09/29 and pulmonary opacities including nodules, with the main suspicion of infectious alteration being septic embolism or fungal disease. In discussion with the [organization] team and rediscussed with [organization], we were advised to maintain antibiotics for 4 weeks from the start of treatment and not from the culture on 10/05/21 and antifungal for 6 weeks from 10/10/21. After the 6 weeks, repeat the echocardiogram and continue oral voriconazole for an indefinite period of time. Candida tropicalis is resistant to fluco and intermediate to voriconazole, given that there is no surgical plan for removal of vegetation of possible fungal etiology. On 10/31, the patient presented a skin rash with no hemodynamic repercussions and no warning signs. The hypothesis of Dress syndrome was raised, but [person] showed improvement on 11/4 with improvement in eosinophilia. discharged on 11/04/21, hemodynamically stable, eupneic, afebrile since 10/15, after finishing intravenous vancomycin with a schedule to follow-up appointment daily for infusion of micafungin 100 mg/day at the hospital on the scheduled end date of 11/21, as well as a scheduled follow-up appointment at [organization] for 11/08 to re-discuss the therapeutic schedule and arrange for the next lines of daratunumab chemotherapy. Date of admission: 10/04 on d11 of i mcbad and d5 of filgrastim d1 on 09/24 date of sent home from hospital (discharge): 11/03 reason for admission: treatment of bacterial and fungal infection of heart valves (endocarditis). Admission: 10/04/2022, sent home from hospital (discharge): 11/04/2022, stay: 01 m 23 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Case report: refractory multiple myeloma iga lambda a vi vcd iss without b2mgb/ds iiia diagnosis relapse: low blood (anemia) elevated iga and lambda fluid around the lungs (pleural effusion) with cytology 41% atypical plasma cells extramedullary plasmacytoma abdomen and pleura pleural biopsy 04/22: cells with scant eosinophilic cytoplasm and small, round, hyperchromatic nuclei at the cell periphery. Ihq: strong and demarcated expression of cd 56, cd 138, and lambda plasmacytoid differentiation. Kappa. Ki67: 95%. Diagnosis: plasmacytoma, multiple myeloma. Abdominal biopsy 04/22: plasma cells infiltrating skeletal muscle tissue. Frequent mitotic figures. No tissue death (necrosis). Ihq: cd138, cd56, lambda, kappa. Pmo 04/16/21: diluted in sp. Sv hypocellular. Sb normocellular with preserved maturation. Sm megakaryocytes absent in smear bmo 04/16: 35% cellularity. No neoplastic infiltration in the sample. Tomography myeloma protocol 02/15/2022: awaiting report transthoracic echocardiography 04/22: ejection fraction 65% mild tricuspid regurgitation, psap 47. 2020 serologies: negative ecog: 1 previous chemotherapies: i vtd pace 07/31/2020 vi vtd d1 10/07/2020 to d1 03/10/2021 while awaiting krd disease progression previous therapeutic schedule krd total of xviii cycles the patient: performed v cycles, with disease progression emergence of plasmacytomas new treatment schedule, as discussed with the patient on 10/23: performance of mcbad for 2 to 2 cycles, while defining a new therapy. D1 i mcbad 09/24. Examinations carried out during hospitalization: head tomography 05/10 done in the context of lowered alertness level, low platelet count (thrombocytopenia), bleeding ruled out. Transthoracic echocardiography 10/08: preserved lvsd subjective s 8 pp 8 lvdd 45 it mild psap 50 mmhg rectification of the septum in the shape of a d extracardiac structure compressing the left atrium moderately depressed rvsd fac 20% transthoracic echocardiography 10/19: fe 62% lvdd 46 s 9.5 pp 9 filamentous images adhered to the atrial face measuring the largest 4 mm suggestive of mild im vegetation, cultures: blood test to find infection (blood culture) 10/04: staphylococcus mitis 2 samples blood test to find infection (blood culture) 10/08: candida tropicalis sensitive to ketoconazole and amphotericin b micafungin and caspofungin not tested, blood test to find infection (blood culture) 10/12: staphylococcus epidermidis 1 sample after 43 hours considered as a contaminant, infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) used: cefepime: 10/04 10/05. Escalated to 11/05 due to growth of streptococcus mittis in blood test to find infection (blood culture) on 11/04 meropenem 10/05 10/20 vancomycin 10/05 11/03 micafungin 10/10 evaluation of other specialties: in medical records. 195 admission: 02/06/2020, discharge: 12/06/2020, stay: 09 d 10 h, location: ward, payer: sus. discharge report [person], 31 years old, born and raised in [address], married, {omitted} at [organization]. date of admission to emergency unit 02/06, hospitalization summary: patient with no known comorbidities or addictions, with severe dry cough, pain in the left hemithorax and decline in general condition that started on 05/20, progressively worsening. sought medical help at the emergency care unit on 01/05 and 02/05 and was released with symptoms. due to persistent complaints, sought consultation with [person], when desaturation and signs of cor pulmonale were evidenced upon pocus according to information from [person]. The patient was referred to our service in poor general condition, hypotensive, with respiratory failure and using a 15 l/min non-rebreathing mask. Orotracheal intubation and intensive care were performed. Pulmonary thromboembolism was ruled out and bilateral pulmonary opacities suggestive of an infectious process were identified. Ceftriaxone, clindamycin, and clarithromycin were started on 6/3 and transitioned to piperacillin, tazobactam, and vancomycin on 6/5 due to septic shock, maintained until 6/12. Vasoactive drugs and neuromuscular blockade were maintained until 6/4. Sedation was discontinued on 6/7, when he was extubated without complications. The patient showed significant clinical, laboratory, and radiological improvements. He was discharged from hospital on 6/12, with guidance on warning signs and symptoms, prescription of symptomatic medications, and referral for follow-up at a primary health care unit. cultures: 04/06 negative urine culture 05/06/23 negative blood culture ongoing antibiotic therapy: ceftriaxone 03/06 to 05/06 clindamycin 03/06 to 05/06 clarithromycin 03/06 to 05/06 piperacillin tazobactam 05/06 to 12/06 vancomycin 05/06 to 12/06 previous devices: orotracheal tube 03 to 07/06 central venous catheter left internal jugular vein 03 to 11/06 intensive care unit: vasoactive drug 03 to 04/06 orotracheal intubation 03 to 07/06 neuromuscular blocker 03 to 04/06 sedation 03 to 07/06 personal history: denies known comorbidities denies known allergies habits: social drinker denies smoking denies drug use of illicit drugs medications in continuous use: ø. complementary exams: chest tomography 03/06 without images of pulmonary artery filling failure. bilateral consolidative opacities with air bronchograms and angiograms between pleural cavities: without effusions or thickening. warning signs and symptoms: intense pain high fever shortness of breath, rapid breathing, shortness of breath, fatigue intense cough with phlegm guidelines after discharge: follow-up in [organization], where to consider a new chest tomography for follow-up vaccination update symptomatic medications if necessary. Admission: 02/06/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 12/06/2020, stay: 09 d 10 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Sent home from hospital (discharge) report the patient, 31 years old, born and raised in [address], married, at [organization]. Date of admission to emergency unit 02/06, hospitalization summary: patient with no known comorbidities or addictions, with severe dry cough, pain in the left hemithorax and decline in general condition that started on 05/20, progressively worsening. Sought medical help at the emergency care unit on 01/05 and 02/05 and was released with symptoms. Due to persistent complaints, sought consultation with the patient, when deoxygen level in blood (saturation) and signs of cor pulmonale were evidenced upon pocus according to information from the patient. The patient was referred to our service in poor general condition, hypotensive, with lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) and using a 15 l/min non-rebreathing mask. Orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) and intensive care were performed. Pulmonary thromboblood clot blocking a vessel (embolism) was ruled out and bilateral pulmonary opacities suggestive of an infectious process were identified. Ceftriaxone, clindamycin, and clarithromycin were started on 6/3 and transitioned to piperacillin, tazobactam, and vancomycin on 6/5 due to severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock), maintained until 6/12. Vasoactive drugs and neuromuscular blockade were maintained until 6/4. Sedation was discontinued on 6/7, when he was extubated without complications. The patient showed significant clinical, laboratory, and radiological improvements. He was sent home from hospitald from hospital on 6/12, with guidance on warning signs and symptoms, prescription of symptomatic medications, and referral for return visit to doctor (follow-up) at a primary health care unit. Cultures: 04/06 negative urine culture 05/06/23 negative blood test to find infection (blood culture) ongoing infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy): ceftriaxone 03/06 to 05/06 clindamycin 03/06 to 05/06 clarithromycin 03/06 to 05/06 piperacillin tazobactam 05/06 to 12/06 vancomycin 05/06 to 12/06 previous devices: orotracheal tube 03 to 07/06 central venous catheter left internal jugular vein 03 to 11/06 ICU (serious care room): vasoactive drug 03 to 04/06 orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) 03 to 07/06 neuromuscular blocker 03 to 04/06 sedation 03 to 07/06 personal history: denies known comorbidities denies known allergies habits: social drinker denies smoking denies drug use of illicit drugs medications in continuous use: ø. Complementary exams: chest tomography 03/06 without images of pulmonary artery filling failure. Bilateral consolidative opacities with air bronchograms and angiograms between pleural cavities: without effusions or thickening. Warning signs and symptoms: intense pain high fever shortness of breath, rapid breathing, shortness of breath, fatigue intense cough with phlegm guidelines after sent home from hospital (discharge): return visit to doctor (follow-up) in [organization], where to consider a new chest tomography for return visit to doctor (follow-up) vaccination update symptomatic medications if necessary. Admission: 02/06/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 12/06/2020, stay: 09 d 10 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Sent home from hospital (discharge) report the patient, 31 years old, born and raised in [address], married, at [organization]. Date of admission to emergency unit 02/06, hospitalization summary: patient with no known comorbidities or addictions, with severe dry cough, pain in the left hemithorax and decline in general condition that started on 05/20, progressively worsening. Sought medical help at the emergency care unit on 01/05 and 02/05 and was released with symptoms. Due to persistent complaints, sought consultation with the patient, when deoxygen level in blood (saturation) and signs of cor pulmonale were evidenced upon pocus according to information from the patient. The patient was referred to our service in poor general condition, hypotensive, with lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure) and using a 15 l/min non-rebreathing mask. Orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) and intensive care were performed. Pulmonary thromboblood clot blocking a vessel (embolism) was ruled out and bilateral pulmonary opacities suggestive of an infectious process were identified. Ceftriaxone, clindamycin, and clarithromycin were started on 6/3 and transitioned to piperacillin, tazobactam, and vancomycin on 6/5 due to severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock), maintained until 6/12. Vasoactive drugs and neuromuscular blockade were maintained until 6/4. Sedation was discontinued on 6/7, when he was extubated without complications. The patient showed significant clinical, laboratory, and radiological improvements. He was sent home from hospitald from hospital on 6/12, with guidance on warning signs and symptoms, prescription of symptomatic medications, and referral for return visit to doctor (follow-up) at a primary health care unit. Cultures: 04/06 negative urine culture 05/06/23 negative blood test to find infection (blood culture) ongoing infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy): ceftriaxone 03/06 to 05/06 clindamycin 03/06 to 05/06 clarithromycin 03/06 to 05/06 piperacillin tazobactam 05/06 to 12/06 vancomycin 05/06 to 12/06 previous devices: orotracheal tube 03 to 07/06 central venous catheter left internal jugular vein 03 to 11/06 ICU (serious care room): vasoactive drug 03 to 04/06 orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) 03 to 07/06 neuromuscular blocker 03 to 04/06 sedation 03 to 07/06 personal history: denies known comorbidities denies known allergies habits: social drinker denies smoking denies drug use of illicit drugs medications in continuous use: ø. Complementary exams: chest tomography 03/06 without images of pulmonary artery filling failure. Bilateral consolidative opacities with air bronchograms and angiograms between pleural cavities: without effusions or thickening. Warning signs and symptoms: intense pain high fever shortness of breath, rapid breathing, shortness of breath, fatigue intense cough with phlegm guidelines after sent home from hospital (discharge): return visit to doctor (follow-up) in [organization], where to consider a new chest tomography for return visit to doctor (follow-up) vaccination update symptomatic medications if necessary. a419. septicemia ne. n17. acute renal failure. j180. bronchopneumonia ne. alive 20.0 1.19
196 coronavirus . admission: 2020-03-15, discharge: 2020-05-11, stay: 01 m 25 d 17 h, location: ward, payer: sus. id: [person], 59 years old, from [address] patient admitted on 2020-03-15 to the emergency unit of [organization] due to severe acute respiratory syndrome caused by coronavirus. patient in need of orotracheal intubation since 2020-03-17 with progression to tracheostomy on 2020-04-08. evolved with improvement in respiratory parameters being decannulated on 2020-05-08, remaining without tracheostomy, without respiratory discomfort, only with occlusive dressing. presented several complications during hospitalization, such as: 1 low-embolic load pulmonary thromboembolism, examination described below; 2 pulmonary septic shock with carbapenem-sensitive Acinetobacter baumannii, being treated with meropnem and polymyxin for 10 days and subsequently using tigecycline for 14 days, with improvement in the condition; 3 acute kidney injury, code 3, without the need for dialysis with subsequent reversal; 4 primary bloodstream infection secondary to Burkholderia cepacia, sensitive to ceftazidime and Bactrim. evolved with rehabilitation with motor and respiratory physiotherapy, with good tolerance and improved functionality during hospitalization. [person] without the need for oxygen therapy for more than 48 hours, with the need to consolidate treatment for bloodstream infection with oral Bactrim. opted for hospital discharge after a long period of hospitalization with improvement in the coronavirus condition and hospital complications. described below is the schedule for ending treatment for [person] after discharge. good general condition, pale 2/4, hydrated, oriented in time and space, acyanotic, anicteric, afebrile, eupneic heart rate: 70 oxygen saturation: 98% in room air cardiac auscultation: heart rhythm in normal S1 and S2, no murmurs, normal heart sounds, preserved capillary refill time, absence of jugular distension at 90º respiratory auscultation: vesicular breath sounds present, no adventitious sounds, absence of use of accessory respiratory muscles abdomen: flaccid, painless, bowel sounds present and normal, tympanic to percussion, no palpable masses or palpable visceromegaly extremities: palpable peripheral pulses, well perfused extremities, absence of edema in the lower limbs, absence of signs of deep vein thrombosis neurology: Glasgow Coma Scale 15, presence of tremor postural in upper limbs. coronavirus pulmonary thromboembolism. schedule: anticoagulation for 6 months counting the start of treatment from 03/31/2020 ending on 09/31/2020 at the moment there is a need to use rivaroxaban 15 mg 12/12 h for another 7 days after discharge and subsequently use of a dose of rivaroxaban 20 mg/day until 05/07/2020. end of treatment for bloodstream infection with bactrin 2 tablets 8/8 hours for another 5 days after hospital discharge requested maintenance of respiratory and motor physiotherapy in [person's] city of origin. hospitalization exams: chest CT Angiography (CTA) 03/31: acute pulmonary thromboembolism with low embolic burden, without cor pulmonale. opacities compatible with coronavirus, severe involvement 50 - 75% with a component of organizing pneumonia and fibroreaction. cardiomegaly. Transthoracic echocardiography 07/05: ejection fraction 57%, mild dilation of the left atrium, preserved systolic performance of the right and left ventricles, without images suggestive of vegetation. discharge with follow-up in primary care. good conditions for hospital discharge. Coronavirus . Admission: 2020-03-15, sent home from hospital (discharge): 2020-05-11, stay: 01 m 25 d 17 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Id: the patient, 59 years old, from [address] patient admitted on 2020-03-15 to the emergency unit of [organization] due to severe acute respiratory syndrome caused by coronavirus. Patient in need of orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) since 2020-03-17 with progression to breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy) on 2020-04-08. Evolved with improvement in respiratory parameters being decannulated on 2020-05-08, remaining without breathing tube in neck (tracheostomy), without respiratory discomfort, only with occlusive dressing. Presented several complications during hospitalization, such as: 1 low-embolic load pulmonary thromboblood clot blocking a vessel (embolism), examination described below; 2 pulmonary severe infection causing dangerously low BP (septic shock) with carbapenem-sensitive acinetobacter baumannii, being treated with meropnem and polymyxin for 10 days and subsequently using tigecycline for 14 days, with improvement in the condition; 3 acute kidney injury, code 3, without the need for dialysis with subsequent reversal; 4 primary bloodstream infection secondary to burkholderia cepacia, sensitive to ceftazidime and bactrim. Evolved with rehabilitation with motor and respiratory physiotherapy, with good tolerance and improved functionality during hospitalization. The patient without the need for oxygen therapy for more than 48 hours, with the need to consolidate treatment for bloodstream infection with oral bactrim. Coronavirus pulmonary thromboblood clot blocking a vessel (embolism). Opacities compatible with coronavirus, severe involvement 50 - 75% with a component of organizing lung infection (pneumonia) and fibroreaction. Cardiomegaly. Transthoracic echocardiography 07/05: ejection fraction 57%, mild dilation of the left atrium, preserved systolic performance of the right and left ventricles, without images suggestive of vegetation. [person] with a history of productive cough with whitish secretion, edema in the lower limbs, and dyspnea on moderate exertion for approximately 34 months, evolving with progressive worsening of dyspnea on mild exertion and a decline in general condition for approximately 2 weeks associated with odynophagia. 1 week ago [person] began to experience edema, hyperemia, heat, and pain in the genital region extending to the lower abdomen. He was taken to the emergency care unit and started on ceftriaxone 2 g/day d1 08/06 and later referred to [organization], where he was intubated due to acute respiratory failure. He was transferred to {organization} via advanced life support ambulance, intubated, hemodynamically stable, using dobutamine 20 ml/h and sedative and analgesia with fentanyl, midazolam and atracurium. No history of fever, rhinorrhea, sneezing, fever or other complaints. Serious general condition, flushed, hydrated, acyanotic, anicteric, feverish RAS4 pupils isomiotic respiratory auscultation vesicular breath sounds, reduced globally, with crackles in bases cardiac auscultation: 2 arrhythmic and normophonetic sounds in 2t without murmurs globular abdomen, normal bowel sounds, normotensive presence of sacral pressure injury. Heat in lower limbs legs bilaterally associated with edema. no signs of deep vein thrombosis genitalia with edema in penis and scrotum, associated with hyperemia and ulcer of approximately 1.5 cm in the foreskin of the bottom pustular lesion on the back, scaly cold and poorly perfused extremities. no surgeries performed. no pending exams. [person] evolved to death. discharged death. 1 decompensated ic bc profile? Decompensated pulmonary high BP (hypertension)? Liver injury? Chronic liver disease? 198 admission: 05/28/2020, discharge: 06/06/2020, stay: 09 d 04 h, location: ward, payer: sus. identification: [person], 80 years old, from [address], social worker, retired, married. problem: 1 sepsis of pulmonary focus treated with ceftriaxone d10 clarithromycin d8 pulmonary focus: productive cough flu-like symptoms fever dyspnea desaturation 6 days ago acute kidney injury kdigo 3 creat 2.14 / basal 0.68 discharged from nephrology on 06/05. hypotension requiring norepinephrine. 2 atrial fibrillation with high ventricular response 05/28 reversed amiodarone chads2vas 4. 3 urinary tract infection candida treated with fluconazole. History of current illness: Patient with previous chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, under treatment with anoro umeclidino vilanterol, with a history of smoking, discontinued in 1988 53 pack-years. Reports that 6 days ago he started having a productive cough, associated with runny nose, nasal obstruction and shortness of breath, as well as a fever of 38 ºC in the first 3 days of symptoms. 3 days ago, he had an appointment with a pulmonologist, and was started on oseltamivir every 12 hours, but for the past 1 day he has been developing worsening shortness of breath, ventilatory-dependent pain in the back and lung bases, as well as a decline in general condition. Reports having taken tests for coronavirus and influenza, both negative. Denies gastrointestinal and genitourinary complaints. No other complaints. Personal history: 1. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (IPD)? Aspiration?? Spirometry 2019: obstruction with FEV1 31.59%, FVC 63.9%, no response to bronchodilator. Chest computed tomography 2021: emphysema, reticular infiltrate without honeycombing in the right base. Aspiration? 2. Systemic arterial hypertension 3. Diabetes mellitus 4. Hypothyroidism 5. Depressive disorder under current treatment. 6. Hearing impairment under treatment with a hearing aid. Continuous-use medications: Anoro, Metformin 1000 mg/day, Dapagliflozin 10 mg, Escitalopram 10 mg, Puran T 125 mcg, Rosuvastatin 5 mg, Calcium carbonate 1000 mg/day, Vit D3 1000 IU/day. Allergies: Denies. Surgeries: Denies. habits and addictions: denies alcoholism, smoking 52 pack-years; quit in 1988. antibiotic therapy ceftriaxone 1g 12/12h 05/28 06/06 clarithromycin 500 mg 12/12h 05/28 05/06 fluconazole 05/31 06/06. tests: transthoracic echocardiography 05/30/2020: mild left atrial dilation. concentric left ventricular remodeling, preserved systolic performance of the ventricles. normal left ventricular segmental mobility. mild mac mitral annular calcification. mild mitral and ai. clinical evolution [organization] finds [person] in a ward bed accompanied by [person]. hemodynamically stable, using a central venous catheter, no vasoactive drugs, and an indwelling urinary catheter. [person] reports improvement in respiratory condition, with reduction in fatigue and inspiratory pain and improvement in cough in appearance and volume, but reports that dyspnea persists with minimal effort such as taking a bath. Reports regular use of alenia, without use of supplemental oxygen, eupneic in room air, presents improvement in respiratory pattern. Reports anal pain, with presence of prolapsed hemorrhoid. Reports daily bowel movements, of liquid pasty consistency, without pain or effort. Reports bedsore in gluteal region without pain symptoms, reported by nursing and {person}. Discussed during visit with [person]: [person] with pneumococcus intermediate to ceftriaxone, however, given clinical and laboratory improvement, weaning from oxygen, maintaining cough, improving, we recommend hospital discharge. I keep [person] aware that in the event of further clinical worsening, he should seek emergency unit for reevaluation. [person] did not present recurrence of atrial fibrillation with high ventricular response; however, due to CHADs2 of 4, with a high risk of recurrence, seen in transthoracic echocardiography with atrial enlargement, we maintained rhythm control with amiodarone 200 mg 12/12h and started anticoagulation with rivaroxaban 20 mg/day. In addition, during hospitalization, he presented worsening of hemorrhoidal disease, now prolapsed, on local conservative therapy. Upon discharge, we requested a control electrocardiogram, laboratory tests for follow-up, scheduled to be performed on 06/27 with teleconsultation on 06/28. prescription at discharge: amiodarone 200 mg 12/12h rivaroxaban 20 mg/day amlodipine 5 mg 12/12h levothyroxine 125 mcg/day rosuvastatin 5 mg/day escitalopram 10 mg/day metformin xr 1g/day dapagliflozin 10 mg/day anoro 1 spray per day salbutamol sn proctyl ointment. follow-up: teleconsultation with geriatrics on 06/28 in the afternoon. good general condition, conscious, oriented, eupneic in room air, no pain complaints, with clinical improvement and recovery of renal function.
