Reports having taken tests for coronavirus and influenza, both negative. Denies gastrointestinal and genitourinary complaints. No other complaints. Personal history: 1. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (ipd)? Aspiration?? Spirometry 2019: obstruction with fev1 31.59%, fvc 63.9%, no response to bronchodilator. Chest CT scan (computed tomography) 2021: emphysema, reticular infiltrate without honeycombing in the right base. Aspiration? 2. Systemic arterial high BP 3. Sugar disease (diabetes) 4. Hypothyroidism 5. Depressive disorder under current treatment. 6. Hearing impairment under treatment with a hearing aid. Continuous-use medications: anoro, metformin 1000 mg/day, dapagliflozin 10 mg, escitalopram 10 mg, puran t 125 mcg, rosuvastatin 5 mg, calcium carbonate 1000 mg/day, vit d3 1000 iu/day. Allergies: denies. Surgeries: denies. Habits and addictions: denies alcoholism, smoking 52 pack-years; quit in 1988. Infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) ceftriaxone 1g 12/12h 05/28 06/06 clarithromycin 500 mg 12/12h 05/28 05/06 fluconazole 05/31 06/06. Tests: transthoracic echocardiography 05/30/2020: mild left atrial dilation. Concentric left ventricular remodeling, preserved systolic performance of the ventricles. Normal left ventricular segmental mobility. Mild mac mitral annular calcification. Mild mitral and ai. Clinical evolution [organization] finds the patient in a general hospital room (ward) bed accompanied by the patient. Stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), using a central venous catheter, no vasoactive drugs, and an indwelling urinary catheter. The patient reports improvement in respiratory condition, with reduction in fatigue and inspiratory pain and improvement in cough in appearance and volume, but reports that difficulty in breathing (dyspnea) persists with minimal effort such as taking a bath. Reports regular use of alenia, without use of supplemental oxygen, breathing normally (eupneic) in room air, presents improvement in respiratory pattern. Reports anal pain, with presence of prolapsed hemorrhoid. Reports daily bowel movements, of liquid pasty consistency, without pain or effort. Reports bedsore in gluteal region without pain symptoms, reported by nursing and {person}. Discussed during visit with the patient: the patient with pneumococcus intermediate to ceftriaxone, however, given clinical and laboratory improvement, weaning from oxygen, maintaining cough, improving, we recommend hospital sent home from hospital (discharge). I keep the patient aware that in the event of further clinical worsening, he should seek emergency unit for reevaluation. The patient did not present recurrence of irregular heartbeat (atrial fibrillation) with high ventricular response; however, due to chads2 of 4, with a high risk of recurrence, seen in transthoracic echocardiography with atrial enlargement, we maintained rhythm control with amiodarone 200 mg 12/12h and started anticoagulation with rivaroxaban 20 mg/day. In addition, during hospitalization, he presented worsening of hemorrhoidal disease, now prolapsed, on local conservative therapy. Upon sent home from hospital (discharge), we requested a control ECG/heart tracing (electrocardiogram), laboratory tests for return visit to doctor (follow-up), scheduled to be performed on 06/27 with teleconsultation on 06/28. Prescription at sent home from hospital (discharge): amiodarone 200 mg 12/12h rivaroxaban 20 mg/day amlodipine 5 mg 12/12h levothyroxine 125 mcg/day rosuvastatin 5 mg/day escitalopram 10 mg/day metformin xr 1g/day dapagliflozin 10 mg/day anoro 1 spray per day salbutamol sn proctyl ointment. Return visit to doctor (follow-up): teleconsultation with geriatrics on 06/28 in the afternoon. Good general condition, conscious, oriented, breathing normally (eupneic) in room air, no pain complaints, with clinical improvement and recovery of renal function. Admission: 05/28/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 06/06/2020, stay: 09 d 04 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. Identification: the patient, 80 years old, from [address], social worker, retired, married. Problem: 1 serious blood infection (sepsis) of pulmonary focus treated with ceftriaxone d10 clarithromycin d8 pulmonary focus: productive cough flu-like symptoms fever difficulty in breathing (dyspnea) deoxygen level in blood (saturation) 6 days ago acute kidney injury kdigo 3 creat 2.14 / basal 0.68 sent home from hospital (discharge)d from nephrology on 06/05. Low BP (hypotension) requiring norepinephrine. 2 irregular heartbeat (atrial fibrillation) with high ventricular response 05/28 reversed amiodarone chads2vas 4. 3 urine infection (UTI) candida treated with fluconazole. History of current illness: patient with previous chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, under treatment with anoro umeclidino vilanterol, with a history of smoking, discontinued in 1988 53 pack-years. Reports that 6 days ago he started having a productive cough, associated with runny nose, nasal obstruction and shortness of breath, as well as a fever of 38 ºc in the first 3 days of symptoms. 3 days ago, he had an appointment with a pulmonologist, and was started on oseltamivir every 12 hours, but for the past 1 day he systemic arterial high BP (hypertension) been developing worsening shortness of breath, ventilatory-dependent pain in the back and lung bases, as well as a decline in general condition. Reports having taken tests for coronavirus and influenza, both negative. Denies gastrointestinal and genitourinary complaints. No other complaints. Personal history: 1. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (ipd)? Aspiration?? Spirometry 2019: obstruction with fev1 31.59%, fvc 63.9%, no response to bronchodilator. Chest CT scan (computed tomography) 2021: emphysema, reticular infiltrate without honeycombing in the right base. Aspiration? 2. Systemic arterial high BP 3. Sugar disease (diabetes) 4. Hypothyroidism 5. Depressive disorder under current treatment. 6. Hearing impairment under treatment with a hearing aid. Continuous-use medications: anoro, metformin 1000 mg/day, dapagliflozin 10 mg, escitalopram 10 mg, puran t 125 mcg, rosuvastatin 5 mg, calcium carbonate 1000 mg/day, vit d3 1000 iu/day. Allergies: denies. Surgeries: denies. Habits and addictions: denies alcoholism, smoking 52 pack-years; quit in 1988. Infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) ceftriaxone 1g 12/12h 05/28 06/06 clarithromycin 500 mg 12/12h 05/28 05/06 fluconazole 05/31 06/06. Tests: transthoracic echocardiography 05/30/2020: mild left atrial dilation. Concentric left ventricular remodeling, preserved systolic performance of the ventricles. Normal left ventricular segmental mobility. Mild mac mitral annular calcification. Mild mitral and ai. Clinical evolution [organization] finds the patient in a general hospital room (ward) bed accompanied by the patient. Stable blood pressure and heart rate (hemodynamically stable), using a central venous catheter, no vasoactive drugs, and an indwelling urinary catheter. The patient reports improvement in respiratory condition, with reduction in fatigue and inspiratory pain and improvement in cough in appearance and volume, but reports that difficulty in breathing (dyspnea) persists with minimal effort such as taking a bath. Reports regular use of alenia, without use of supplemental oxygen, breathing normally (eupneic) in room air, presents improvement in respiratory pattern. Reports anal pain, with presence of prolapsed hemorrhoid. Reports daily bowel movements, of liquid pasty consistency, without pain or effort. Reports bedsore in gluteal region without pain symptoms, reported by nursing and {person}. Discussed during visit with the patient: the patient with pneumococcus intermediate to ceftriaxone, however, given clinical and laboratory improvement, weaning from oxygen, maintaining cough, improving, we recommend hospital sent home from hospital (discharge). I keep the patient aware that in the event of further clinical worsening, he should seek emergency unit for reevaluation. The patient did not present recurrence of irregular heartbeat (atrial fibrillation) with high ventricular response; however, due to chads2 of 4, with a high risk of recurrence, seen in transthoracic echocardiography with atrial enlargement, we maintained rhythm control with amiodarone 200 mg 12/12h and started anticoagulation with rivaroxaban 20 mg/day. In addition, during hospitalization, he presented worsening of hemorrhoidal disease, now prolapsed, on local conservative therapy. Upon sent home from hospital (discharge), we requested a control ECG/heart tracing (electrocardiogram), laboratory tests for return visit to doctor (follow-up), scheduled to be performed on 06/27 with teleconsultation on 06/28. Prescription at sent home from hospital (discharge): amiodarone 200 mg 12/12h rivaroxaban 20 mg/day amlodipine 5 mg 12/12h levothyroxine 125 mcg/day rosuvastatin 5 mg/day escitalopram 10 mg/day metformin xr 1g/day dapagliflozin 10 mg/day anoro 1 spray per day salbutamol sn proctyl ointment. Return visit to doctor (follow-up): teleconsultation with geriatrics on 06/28 in the afternoon. Good general condition, conscious, oriented, breathing normally (eupneic) in room air, no pain complaints, with clinical improvement and recovery of renal function. j441. chronic obstructive pulmonary disease with acute exacerbation n. f411. generalized anxiety disorder. e119. non-insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus without complications. e039. hypothyroidism, unspecified. i10. primary essential hypertension. n17. acute renal failure. e14. diabetes mellitus n. e66. obesity. a41. other septicemias. a419. septicemia n. alive 25.2 1.13
199 admission: 12/11/2020, discharge: 01/23/2021, stay: 01 m 11 d 10 h, location: ward, payer: sus [person], 28 years old, from [address] 12/14 weight: 102 kg height: 162 cm. problem: 1 Status epilepticus secondary to pulmonary infection Phenytoin attack performed and maintenance dose of PHT increased Pb dosage maintained Dosage of other drugs LTG, TPM and CLB 2 Sepsis pulmonary focus 3 Deep vein thrombosis Right lower limb Full dose of enoxaparin started on 12/23 4 Pseudomembranous colitis Metronidazole started 12/30 01/10 5 Adrenal insufficiency secondary to sepsis Hydrocortisone 50 mg every 12h started on 12/31 Weaning started on 01/10 Previous functionality Oral diet Walked without assistance Dependent for some instrumental activities of daily living Bathing, physiological eliminations. Tests: Electroencephalogram 12/12: Mild to moderate disorganization of baseline activity due to increased slow wave content in the theta and delta bands, but with the presence of normal sleep graphoelements. There was no clinical or electrographic reactivity to nociceptive stimulation. b Record of electrographic seizures of variable duration (4 to 9 seconds), characterized by rhythmic activity in the beta range, evolving to alpha and theta ranges, associated with spike-type epileptiform paroxysms, with generalized projection, without associated clinical phenomena. There was a record of a seizure with rhythmic evolution to the delta range and of longer duration (18 seconds). Rare spike-type paroxysms were recorded, occurring isolatedly in the left frontocentral region. Clinical correlation: The findings are consistent with focal epilepsy with electrographic seizures, but do not characterize status epilepticus. electroencephalogram 12/19: marked symmetrical disorganization of the background activity due to persistent rhythmic, semi-rhythmic bifrontal slowing in the delta band, at approximately 1.5 Hz, of medium amplitude, only temporarily mitigated by nociceptive stimulation; furthermore, lack and poor definition of physiological graphoelements of sleep. Upon nociceptive stimulation, there is also a triggering of generalized rhythmic epileptiform activity in the beta band, of low voltage, which evolves into an outbreak of frank epileptiform paroxysms of the spike type followed by a slow wave, also of diffuse expression, and with bifrontal accentuation of such stimulus-induced discharges, suppression of the clinical tracing follows; [person] presents only with painful facies. b Occasionally, associated with the slow waves described above, bisynchronous epileptiform paroxysms of the sharp wave type are observed, involving the anterior regions of the scalp. Conclusion and electroclinical correlation: the electroencephalogram was abnormal. The findings are compatible with severe diffuse encephalopathy of a nonspecific nature, associated with focal and generalized interictal epileptogenesis, the latter being stimulus-induced. Electroencephalogram 12/22: a moderate symmetrical disorganization of the background activity due to an increase in the content of slow waves in the theta and delta bands, in addition to a lack and poor definition of the physiological graphoelements of sleep. However, good electroencephalographic reactivity is noted in response to nociceptive stimuli, with an association with a dominant posterior rhythmic outline in the alpha band. b bursts of generalized rhythmic delta activity (grda) with bifrontal accentuation, occurring intermittently throughout the tracing during sleep, consisting of stereotyped waves in the 2.0 to 2.5 Hz band. Conclusion and electroclinical correlation: the electroencephalogram was abnormal. The findings are compatible with moderate diffuse encephalopathy, of a nonspecific nature in relation to the previous tracing; significant improvement is noted, with mitigation of grda and suppression of stimulus-induced generalized epileptogenesis. electroencephalogram 02/01: slight symmetrical disorganization of the baseline activity due to the slowing of the dominant posterior rhythm of wakefulness, recorded at a frequency of up to 6.5 Hz, however with the presence of typical physiological graphoelements of sleep. b recording of epileptiform paroxysms of the spike and sharp wave type, with generalized projection, with anterior accentuation, occurring in rhythmic bursts of short duration of 3 to 4 seconds. the paroxysms are followed by delta waves and, occasionally, rhythmic activity in the beta range of short duration of less than 1 second, which also occurred preceding the bursts of paroxysms, as well as in an isolated episode during drowsiness. c outbreaks of generalized delta activity, with bifrontal accentuation, the same projections as the paroxysms, also occurring exclusively during sleep. note: [person] presented stereotyped cephalic lateralization and oromandibular movements during wakefulness, however, without electrographic correlation with ictal patterns. conclusion and electroclinical correlation: the electroencephalogram was abnormal. the findings are compatible with diffuse encephalopathy and generalized epilepsy. computed tomography of the skull 11/12: no relevant alterations; computed tomography of the chest 11/12: no signs of an infectious process; cerebrospinal fluid 13/12: 1 cell; 0 red blood cells; glucose: 96; protein: non-reactive; chloride: 125; lactate: 1.91; transthoracic echocardiography 23/12: no signs of vegetation. no other alterations. cultures: tracheal aspirate: 12/27: enterobacter cloacae complex sensitive to meropenem 12/19: negative 12/12: staphylococcus aureus oxas resistant to clindamycin 01/07: staphylococcus epidermididis oxar blood culture: 12/27: negative so far 12/19: staphylococcus hominis 12/13: negative 01/07: 01/16: pseudomonas aeroginosas sensitive to ciprofloxacin, cephalosporin and aminoglycoside urine culture: 12/27: negative 01/07: negative venous catheter: 12/27: negative 12/19: corynebacterium sensitive to vancomycin and teicoplanin 01/09: staphylococcus epidermidis oxar cerebrospinal fluid: 12/13: micrococcus sp antibiotic therapy: current: ceftazidime d0 01/19 pseudomonas bloodstream infection. AEDC follow-up appointments: 02/02 schedule to complete antibiotic therapy directed at pseudomonas bloodstream infection: using ceftazidime d3, maintain for 7-10 days: currently clinically stable, cpr falling, last 2.5 AEDC: 02/02/22 maintain current antiepileptic drugs until follow-up appointment: phenytoin 100 mg 12/12h phenobarbital 100 mg 12/12h topiramate 100 100 200 clobazam 20 mg 8/8h lamotrigine 100 100 200 I renew prescriptions from [organization] regarding deep vein thrombosis: I prescribe rivaroxaban 20 mg/d, I provide a prescription for 6 months. I request follow-up in the network. Follow-up by physiotherapy at the CER: difficulty walking, associated with equine feet. Wheelchair, bath and diaper report: already delivered. Treatment for scabies with ivermectin was carried out on 01/17, repeat in 7 days. Regular general condition, acyanotic, anicteric, hydrated, afebrile. Cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm in 2t, normal sound sounds, no audible murmurs. Respiratory auscultation: vesicular breath sounds in both hemithorax, no adventitious sounds. Globular abdomen, bowel sounds, depressed and no signs of defense. Warm extremities, capillary refill time less than 3s, free calves Glasgow 14, isochoric and photoreactive pupils, no pupillary hippus, no stigmata of crisis. Can raise all 4 limbs and hold them against gravity. Understands and obeys commands. Equine feet bilaterally. Admission: 12/11/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 01/23/2021, stay: 01 m 11 d 10 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme the patient, 28 years old, from [address] 12/14 weight: 102 kg height: 162 cm. Problem: 1 prolonged seizure (status epilepticus) secondary to pulmonary infection phenytoin attack performed and maintenance dose of pht increased pb dosage maintained dosage of other drugs ltg, tpm and clb 2 serious blood infection (sepsis) pulmonary focus 3 deep vein blood clot (thrombosis) right lower limb full dose of enoxaparin started on 12/23 4 pseudomembranous colitis metronidazole started 12/30 01/10 5 adrenal insufficiency secondary to serious blood infection (sepsis) hydrocortisone 50 mg every 12h started on 12/31 weaning started on 01/10 previous functionality oral diet walked without assistance dependent for some instrumental activities of daily living bathing, physiological eliminations. Tests: electroencephalogram 12/12: mild to moderate disorganization of baseline activity due to increased slow wave content in the theta and delta bands, but with the presence of normal sleep graphoelements. There was no clinical or electrographic reactivity to nociceptive stimulation. B record of electrographic seizures of variable duration (4 to 9 seconds), characterized by rhythmic activity in the beta range, evolving to alpha and theta ranges, associated with spike-type epileptiform paroxysms, with generalized projection, without associated clinical phenomena. There was a record of a seizure with rhythmic evolution to the delta range and of longer duration (18 seconds). Rare spike-type paroxysms were recorded, occurring isolatedly in the left frontocentral region. Clinical correlation: the findings are consistent with focal seizure disorder (epilepsy) with electrographic seizures, but do not characterize prolonged seizure (status epilepticus). Electroencephalogram 12/19: marked symmetrical disorganization of the background activity due to persistent rhythmic, semi-rhythmic bifrontal slowing in the delta band, at approximately 1.5 hz, of medium amplitude, only temporarily mitigated by nociceptive stimulation; furthermore, lack and poor definition of physiological graphoelements of sleep. Upon nociceptive stimulation, there is also a triggering of generalized rhythmic epileptiform activity in the beta band, of low voltage, which evolves into an outbreak of frank epileptiform paroxysms of the spike type followed by a slow wave, also of diffuse expression, and with bifrontal accentuation of such stimulus-induced sent home from hospital (discharge)s, suppression of the clinical tracing follows; the patient presents only with painful facies. B occasionally, associated with the slow waves described above, bisynchronous epileptiform paroxysms of the sharp wave type are observed, involving the anterior regions of the scalp. Conclusion and electroclinical correlation: the electroencephalogram was abnormal. The findings are compatible with severe diffuse brain dysfunction (encephalopathy) of a nonspecific nature, associated with focal and generalized interictal epileptogenesis, the latter being stimulus-induced. Electroencephalogram 12/22: a moderate symmetrical disorganization of the background activity due to an increase in the content of slow waves in the theta and delta bands, in addition to a lack and poor definition of the physiological graphoelements of sleep. However, good electroencephalographic reactivity is noted in response to nociceptive stimuli, with an association with a dominant posterior rhythmic outline in the alpha band. B bursts of generalized rhythmic delta activity (grda) with bifrontal accentuation, occurring intermittently throughout the tracing during sleep, consisting of stereotyped waves in the 2.0 to 2.5 hz band. Conclusion and electroclinical correlation: the electroencephalogram was abnormal. The findings are compatible with moderate diffuse brain dysfunction, of a nonspecific nature in relation to the previous tracing; significant improvement is noted, with mitigation of grda and suppression of stimulus-induced generalized epileptogenesis. Electroencephalogram 02/01: slight symmetrical disorganization of the baseline activity due to the slowing of the dominant posterior rhythm of wakefulness, recorded at a frequency of up to 6.5 hz, however with the presence of typical physiological graphoelements of sleep. B recording of epileptiform paroxysms of the spike and sharp wave type, with generalized projection, with anterior accentuation, occurring in rhythmic bursts of short duration of 3 to 4 seconds. The paroxysms are followed by delta waves and, occasionally, rhythmic activity in the beta range of short duration of less than 1 second, which also occurred preceding the bursts of paroxysms, as well as in an isolated episode during drowsiness. C outbreaks of generalized delta activity, with bifrontal accentuation, the same projections as the paroxysms, also occurring exclusively during sleep. Note: the patient presented stereotyped cephalic lateralization and oromandibular movements during wakefulness, however, without electrographic correlation with ictal patterns. Conclusion and electroclinical correlation: the electroencephalogram was abnormal. The findings are compatible with diffuse brain dysfunction (encephalopathy) and generalized seizure disorder (epilepsy). CT scan (computed tomography) of the skull 11/12: no relevant alterations; CT scan (computed tomography) of the chest 11/12: no signs of an infectious process; cerebrospinal fluid 13/12: 1 cell; 0 red blood cells; glucose: 96; protein: non-reactive; chloride: 125; lactate: 1.91; transthoracic echocardiography 23/12: no signs of vegetation. No other alterations. Cultures: tracheal aspirate: 12/27: enterobacter cloacae complex sensitive to meropenem 12/19: negative 12/12: staphylococcus aureus oxas resistant to clindamycin 01/07: staphylococcus epidermididis oxar blood test to find infection (blood culture): 12/27: negative so far 12/19: staphylococcus hominis 12/13: negative 01/07: 01/16: pseudomonas aeroginosas sensitive to ciprofloxacin, cephalosporin and aminoglycoside urine culture: 12/27: negative 01/07: negative venous catheter: 12/27: negative 12/19: corynebacterium sensitive to vancomycin and teicoplanin 01/09: staphylococcus epidermidis oxar cerebrospinal fluid: 12/13: micrococcus sp infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy): current: ceftazidime d0 01/19 pseudomonas bloodstream infection. Aedc return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointments: 02/02 schedule to complete infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) treatment (infection-fighting medicine (antibiotic) therapy) directed at pseudomonas bloodstream infection: using ceftazidime d3, maintain for 7-10 days: currently clinically stable, cpr falling, last 2.5 aedc: 02/02/22 maintain current antiepileptic drugs until return visit to doctor (follow-up) appointment: phenytoin 100 mg 12/12h phenobarbital 100 mg 12/12h topiramate 100 100 200 clobazam 20 mg 8/8h lamotrigine 100 100 200 i renew prescriptions from [organization] regarding deep vein blood clot (thrombosis): i prescribe rivaroxaban 20 mg/d, i provide a prescription for 6 months. I request return visit to doctor (follow-up) in the network. Return visit to doctor (follow-up) by physiotherapy at the cer: difficulty walking, associated with equine feet. Wheelchair, bath and diaper report: already delivered. Treatment for scabies with ivermectin was carried out on 01/17, repeat in 7 days. Regular general condition, no bluish discoloration (acyanotic), no yellowing of skin/eyes (anicteric), hydrated, no fever (afebrile). Cardiac auscultation: regular heart rhythm in 2t, normal sound sounds, no audible murmurs. Respiratory auscultation: normal breathing sounds (vesicular breath sounds) in both hemithorax, no adventitious sounds. Globular abdomen, bowel sounds, depressed and no signs of defense. Warm extremities, capillary refill time less than 3s, free calves glasgow 14, isochoric and photoreactive pupils, no pupillary hippus, no stigmata of crisis. Can raise all 4 limbs and hold them against gravity. Understands and obeys commands. Equine feet bilaterally. [person] with a recent diagnosis of human immunodeficiency virus, was admitted to [organization] on 10/15 complaining of dyspnea, fatigue, fever, and cough. He began treatment for pneumocystosis and with clarithromycin and ceftriaxone for pneumonia. On 10/16, he was referred to [organization], where he continued to worsen his respiratory condition, having started treatment with ganciclovir for cytomegalovirus and with a new need for antimicrobial escalation. Even so, [person] evolved with worsening, being referred to the intensive care unit on 10/28. On 10/29, [person] developed hypotension and respiratory failure, having undergone orotracheal intubation since then. On 11/04, he presented tachyarrhythmia, and impregnation with amiodarone was started, and RIP was started due to the presence of TBTRM in the bronchial lavage with positive traces of tuberculosis. [person] continued to use vasoactive drugs, having shown no improvement even after new antimicrobial escalations and continued to present progressive clinical worsening, beginning to require hemodialysis. on 11/20 [person] began to present significant worsening of hemodynamic shock, having evolved with the need for vasoactive drugs at maximum doses, not even having tolerated the first hemodialysis session that would be performed on 11/21. despite the use of vasoactive drugs at maximum doses, [person] evolved with progressive hypotension, until on 11/23 he evolved to death. absence of cardiac electrical activity on monitor absence of pulse curve in invasive blood pressure absence of respiratory incursions absent central pulses absence of response to painful stimulus. absent corneopalpebral and photomotor reflexes. absence of cardiac electrical activity on monitor absence of pulse curve in invasive blood pressure absence of respiratory incursions absent central pulses absence of response to painful stimulus. absent corneopalpebral and photomotor reflexes. coronavirus test 10/18: negative tbtrm 11/03: traces. coronavirus test 10/18: negative tbtrm 11/03: traces. no surgeries or procedures were performed. no pending tests. no therapeutic plan or education to be carried out. Catheter?. Admission: 10/15/2020, sent home from hospital (discharge): 11/23/2020, stay: 01 m 07 d 22 h, location: general hospital room (ward), payer: government health scheme. The patient with a recent diagnosis of human immunodeficiency virus, was admitted to [organization] on 10/15 complaining of difficulty in breathing (dyspnea), fatigue, fever, and cough. He began treatment for pneumocystosis and with clarithromycin and ceftriaxone for lung infection (pneumonia). On 10/16, he was referred to [organization], where he continued to worsen his respiratory condition, having started treatment with ganciclovir for cytomegalovirus and with a new need for antimicrobial escalation. Even so, the patient evolved with worsening, being referred to the ICU (serious care room) on 10/28. On 10/29, the patient developed low BP (hypotension) and lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure), having undergone orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) since then. On 11/04, he presented tachyarrhythmia, and impregnation with amiodarone was started, and rip was started due to the presence of tbtrm in the bronchial lavage with positive traces of TB (tuberculosis). The patient continued to use vasoactive drugs, having shown no improvement even after new antimicrobial escalations and continued to present progressive clinical worsening, beginning to require kidney dialysis (hemodialysis). On 11/20 the patient began to present significant worsening of hemodynamic shock, having evolved with the need for vasoactive drugs at maximum doses, not even having tolerated the first kidney dialysis (hemodialysis) session that would be performed on 11/21. Despite the use of vasoactive drugs at maximum doses, the patient evolved with progressive low BP (hypotension), until on 11/23 he evolved to death. Absence of cardiac electrical activity on monitor absence of pulse curve in invasive blood pressure absence of respiratory incursions absent central pulses absence of response to painful stimulus. Absent corneopalpebral and photomotor reflexes. Absence of cardiac electrical activity on monitor absence of pulse curve in invasive blood pressure absence of respiratory incursions absent central pulses absence of response to painful stimulus. Absent corneopalpebral and photomotor reflexes. Coronavirus test 10/18: negative tbtrm 11/03: traces. Coronavirus test 10/18: negative tbtrm 11/03: traces. No surgeries or procedures were performed. No pending tests. No therapeutic plan or education to be carried out. The patient evolved to death. After the surgery, he developed low BP (hypotension) and lungs unable to work properly (respiratory failure), having undergone orotracheal breathing tube placed in throat (intubation) since then. This was the first time that he was given tachyarrhythmia, and the patient started treatment with ganciclovir for cytomegalovirus and with a new way of treating it. This time, the patient has been able to get a better treatment for the disease. It's important to see how the patient's condition improves, as well as he has a better understanding of how to treat it and how to use it, and how much it can be used to improve the condition. This is why he's able to use vasovirin in the hospital to make sure that the patient doesn't get the best treatment. Despite the use of vasoactive drugs at maximum doses, the patient evolved with progressive low BP (hypotension), until on 11/23 he evolved to death.
